topics.title: Operating Point of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operating Point of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Point (Q-Point)**: The **Quiescent Point** (Q-point) of a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) is the specific pair of DC coordinates $(I_C, V_{CE})$ — or equivalently the set $(I_B, I_C, I_E, V_{BE}, V_{CE})$ — that defines the transistor's steady-state, no-signal operating condition on its **output characteristics (DC load line)**.

The Q-point is established by the **DC biasing network** connected to the transistor and is independent of any AC input signal. Once set, the small AC signal causes the instantaneous operating point to *swing* about this Q-point along the **AC load line**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Quiescent"?** The word *quiescent* literally means "quiet" or "at rest." With zero input signal, the transistor sits silently at this fixed DC coordinate, ready to amplify an incoming AC waveform without distortion.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise-Control Car

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. Before you start the journey:

- **Engine RPM (Revolutions Per Minute)** ↔ Collector current $I_C$
- **Gear Position / Speed** ↔ Collector–Emitter voltage $V_{CE}$
- **Gas Pedal (Throttle)** ↔ Base current $I_B$ (controlled by bias resistors)
- **Cruise Control Set Button** ↔ DC biasing network

When you set cruise control to **80 km/h**, the car's electronics hold the engine at a specific RPM and the transmission at a specific gear — this is your **Q-point**. Now when hills come (AC signal), the system auto-adjusts *around* this set point. If the set point is wrong (too low/high), the car either stalls or redlines — just like a transistor clipping or saturating.

## 1.3 Why the Q-Point Must Be Set Correctly

A properly chosen Q-point ensures:

| Criterion | Required Condition | Consequence of Violation |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Amplification** | Q-point lies in the **Active Region** | Distortion / clipping |
| **Maximum Symmetrical Swing** | Q-point at **center of DC load line** | Unequal positive & negative half-cycles |
| **Thermal Stability** | $I_C$ insensitive to $\beta$ and temperature | **Thermal Runaway** → device destruction |
| **Bias Point Independence** | Stable against $V_{BE}$ drift, $\beta$ variation | Q-point drift with temperature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Silicon BJT at Room Temperature (T = 300 K)**:
> - $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx \mathbf{0.7\ V}$
> - $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx \mathbf{0.2\ V}$
> - $V_{CC}$ is typically **$\pm 12\ \text{V}$ or $\pm 15\ \text{V}$** in laboratory work
> - Thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$ at 300 K

## 1.4 The Four Operating Regions of a BJT

```
          Cut-off  │  Active         │  Saturation   │  Reverse-Active
        ───────────┼─────────────────┼───────────────┼──────────────────
 V_BE   < 0.5 V    │  ≈ 0.7 V        │  ≈ 0.8 V      │  V_BC forward
 I_B    ≈ 0        │  small (μA–mA)  │  large        │  small
 I_C    ≈ 0        │  I_C = β·I_B    │  I_C ≠ β·I_B  │  I_C = -β_R·I_B
 V_CE   ≈ V_CC     │  0.2 < V_CE<V_CC│  ≈ 0.2 V      │  V_CE > V_CC
 Use    Switch OFF │  Amplifier ✔    │  Switch ON    │  Rarely used
```

For a transistor to function as a **linear amplifier** in the KTU syllabus, the Q-point **must** be biased firmly in the **Active Region**.


<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The DC Load Line — Backbone of Q-Point Analysis

The **DC load line** is the graphical locus of all possible $(I_C, V_{CE})$ pairs permitted by the **external DC biasing circuit** alone, assuming the transistor were an *ideal* variable resistor.

**Derivation of the KVL Loop (Collector–Emitter Side):**

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the output loop:
$$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0$$

Rearranging to express $I_C$ as a function of $V_{CE}$:
$$I_C = -\frac{1}{R_C} V_{CE} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

This is a **straight-line equation** $y = mx + c$ on the $I_C$ vs. $V_{CE}$ plane with:
- **Slope** $m = -\dfrac{1}{R_C}$ (always negative)
- **Y-intercept** (when $V_{CE}=0$): $I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$
- **X-intercept** (when $I_C=0$): $V_{CE(\text{cut-off})} = V_{CC}$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Operating Point is the intersection of the DC load line with the transistor's output $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ characteristic curve corresponding to the DC base current $I_{BQ}$ set by the input bias loop.**

## 2.2 The AC Load Line — For Signal Analysis

When an AC signal $v_s$ is applied, the **instantaneous** operating point moves along the **AC load line**, which generally has a *steeper* slope (because $R_{ac} < R_{DC}$ typically, since coupling/bypass capacitors short emitter resistors at signal frequency).

$$i_c = -\frac{v_{ce}}{R_{ac}} + I_{CQ}$$

where $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$ for a CE amplifier with collector load $R_L$.

## 2.3 Stability of the Q-Point — A Major KTU Topic

A real BJT's parameters drift due to:
1. **Temperature variation** of $I_{CO}$ (reverse saturation current, doubles every $10\ ^\circ\text{C}$)
2. **Temperature variation** of $V_{BE}$ (decreases by $\approx 2\ \text{mV}/^\circ\text{C}$)
3. **Unit-to-unit variation** of $\beta$ (50 → 200 for the same part number)
4. **Aging** effects

**Stability Factors (S, S', S''):** These quantify *how much* $I_C$ changes for a unit change in the offending parameter.

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\rvert_{V_{BE},\beta}}, \quad S' = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} \bigg\rvert_{I_{CO},\beta}, \quad S'' = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta} \bigg\rvert_{I_{CO},V_{BE}}$$

The total incremental change in $I_C$ is:
$$\Delta I_C = S \, \Delta I_{CO} + S' \, \Delta V_{BE} + S'' \, \Delta \beta$$

**Ideal Stability:** $S = 1$ (perfect), but practically we want $S$ as close to 1 as possible. For a fixed-bias circuit, $S = \beta + 1$ (terrible!).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the structure, not just the result.**

### Master Formula Table

| # | Parameter | Expression | Notes / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | KVL Output Loop | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | Always valid for CE with single $V_{CC}$ |
| 2 | Saturation Current | $I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ | When $V_{CE} = 0$ |
| 3 | Cut-off Voltage | $V_{CE(\text{off})} = V_{CC}$ | When $I_C = 0$ |
| 4 | Active Region | $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$ | Includes leakage term |
| 5 | Base–Emitter Diode | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ (Si) | $0.3\ \text{V}$ for Ge |
| 6 | Stability Composite | $S + S' \cdot \dfrac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial I_C} + S'' \cdot \dfrac{\partial \beta}{\partial I_C} = 1$ | — |
| 7 | Thermal Runaway Cond. | $\dfrac{\partial P_C}{\partial T_j} < \dfrac{1}{\theta_{JA}}$ | Must hold to avoid destruction |
| 8 | Fixed Bias $I_B$ | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$ | Independent of $\beta$ at DC |
| 9 | Fixed Bias $I_C$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | $I_C$ depends on $\beta$ |
| 10 | Fixed Bias Stability | $S = \beta + 1$ | **Worst stability** |
| 11 | Emitter Bias $I_B$ | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | Self-bias with split supplies |
| 12 | Emitter Bias $S$ | $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_B + R_E)}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | Better than fixed bias |
| 13 | Voltage Divider $V_{TH}$ | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Thevenin at base |
| 14 | Voltage Divider $R_{TH}$ | $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ | Thevenin resistance |
| 15 | Voltage Divider $I_B$ | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | KTU most-tested topology |
| 16 | Voltage Divider $S$ | $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$ | Best stability if $R_E \gg R_{TH}/\beta$ |
| 17 | Voltage Divider Approx. | $I_C \approx \dfrac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E}$ | Valid if $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ |
| 18 | Emitter Voltage (approx.) | $V_E = I_E R_E \approx I_C R_E$ | Approx. bias design rule |
| 19 | Collector Voltage | $V_C = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | DC node voltage |
| 20 | V_CE Q-point | $V_{CE} = V_C - V_E = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ | Final design equation |

> **Engineering Utility of Q-Point Analysis:** Every analog IC you have ever used — op-amps, audio amplifiers, RF mixers, oscillators — was designed using these exact biasing equations. Without a stable Q-point, your smartphone audio amplifier would distort, your Wi-Fi mixer would have unpredictable gain, and your pacemaker's analog frontend could fail catastrophically. **Biasing is not theory — it is the silent foundation of every working electronic system.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — DC Analysis of Fixed Bias (Base Bias) Circuit

### Circuit Description
A single resistor $R_B$ connects $V_{CC}$ to the base. The collector resistor $R_C$ connects $V_{CC}$ to the collector. The emitter is grounded. This is the simplest — and the **worst** — biasing topology.

### Step 1: Write KVL Around the Input (Base–Emitter) Loop
Starting from $V_{CC}$, going through $R_B$, into the base, and out the emitter to ground:

$$V_{CC} - I_B R_B - V_{BE} = 0$$

### Step 2: Solve for $I_B$
$$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

> For silicon BJT, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$. If $V_{CC} \gg 0.7\ \text{V}$, we may approximate $I_B \approx V_{CC}/R_B$.

### Step 3: Apply Transistor Equation in Active Region
The collector current is controlled by the base current through the current gain $\beta$ (ignoring the small leakage term for first-order analysis):

$$I_C = \beta I_B = \beta \cdot \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}$$

### Step 4: Write KVL Around the Output (Collector–Emitter) Loop
$$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0$$

### Step 5: Solve for $V_{CE}$ (the Q-point voltage)
$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = V_{CC} - \beta \cdot \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \cdot R_C$$

### Step 6: Derivation of Stability Factor S
The definition: $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}}\Big\rvert_{V_{BE},\beta}$.

We begin from $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$.

Substituting the input loop equation, where $I_B = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E}{R_B}$ (with $R_E = 0$ for fixed bias):

$$I_C = \beta \left( \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} \right) + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$$

Differentiating with respect to $I_{CO}$ (treating $\beta$ and $V_{BE}$ as constants):

$$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \beta + 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion for Fixed Bias:** $S = \beta + 1 \gg 1$. If $\beta = 100$, then $S = 101$. This means for every 1 nA increase in $I_{CO}$, $I_C$ increases by 101 nA. This is **catastrophically unstable** and is the reason this topology is virtually never used in production designs — it appears in KTU exams purely as a *contrasting example*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DC Analysis of Voltage Divider Bias (VDB)

### Circuit Description
Two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a voltage divider across $V_{CC}$ to set the base voltage. $R_C$ is the collector resistor. $R_E$ is the emitter resistor (often bypassed by a capacitor $C_E$ at signal frequency, but for **DC analysis**, $C_E$ is an open circuit and $R_E$ is fully effective). This is the **industry standard** biasing topology.

### Step 1: Thevenize the Base Network
Replace the $V_{CC}$–$R_1$–$R_2$ network as seen from the base terminal by its Thevenin equivalent.

**Thevenin Voltage** (open-circuit voltage at base):
$$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Thevenin Resistance** (set $V_{CC} = 0$, look into the divider):
$$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Step 2: Write KVL Around the New Input Loop
$$V_{TH} - I_B R_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$$

Since $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B$:

$$V_{TH} - I_B R_{TH} - V_{BE} - (\beta+1) I_B R_E = 0$$

### Step 3: Solve for $I_B$
$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $I_C$
$$I_C = \beta I_B = \frac{\beta (V_{TH} - V_{BE})}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

### Step 5: Solve for $V_{CE}$ (Q-Point Voltage)
Apply KVL to the output loop (note: $R_E$ is in the loop too, since emitter is not grounded):
$$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} - I_E R_E = 0$$

Using $I_E \approx I_C$:
$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)$$

### Step 6: Derive the Stability Factor S for VDB
Starting from the fundamental $I_C$ expression:
$$I_C = \frac{\beta (V_{TH} - V_{BE})}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} + \frac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} \cdot I_{CO}$$

Taking the partial derivative with respect to $I_{CO}$ (holding $\beta$ and $V_{BE}$ constant):

$$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

### Step 7: Design Rule of Thumb
If we choose $R_E$ such that $(\beta+1) R_E \gg R_{TH}$ (typically $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$), then:

$$S \approx \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{(\beta+1) R_E} = 1$$

> This is the **ideal stability limit** — and it is achievable in VDB but impossible in fixed bias. This is precisely why VDB is used in **99%** of all BJT amplifier designs.

### Approximate Design Equations (used heavily in KTU problems)
When $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$:

$$I_C \approx I_E = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E} = \frac{V_E}{R_E}$$

This makes the Q-point essentially **independent of $\beta$**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — AC Load Line and Maximum Symmetrical Swing

For a CE amplifier with load $R_L$ AC-coupled at the collector, the AC resistance seen at the collector is:

$$R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$

The AC load line passes through the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ with slope $-\dfrac{1}{R_{ac}}$.

**Maximum unclipped (symmetrical) swing condition:** Place Q-point at the *center* of the AC load line:

$$V_{CEQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{2} + \frac{I_{CQ} R_{ac}}{2}$$

For most designs where $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \ll V_{CC}$:
$$V_{CEQ} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{2} + \frac{I_{CQ} R_{ac}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peak-to-peak output voltage swing** before clipping: $V_{o(\text{pp})} = 2 \cdot \min(V_{CEQ} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}, I_{CQ} \cdot R_{ac})$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Full Solution)

> **Given**: VDB circuit with $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, silicon transistor.

**Step 1 — Thevenin voltage:**
$$V_{TH} = 12 \cdot \frac{5}{10 + 5} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 4\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Thevenin resistance:**
$$R_{TH} = \frac{10 \cdot 5}{10 + 5} = \frac{50}{15} \approx 3.333\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Check design validity:**
$$\beta R_E = 100 \cdot 1 = 100\ \text{k}\Omega \quad \text{vs.} \quad 10 R_{TH} = 33.33\ \text{k}\Omega$$

Since $100 \geq 33.33$, the **approximate method is valid**.

**Step 4 — Approximate $I_C$:**
$$I_C \approx \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E} = \frac{4 - 0.7}{1} = 3.3\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Exact $I_C$ for verification:**
$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{4 - 0.7}{3.333 + 101 \cdot 1} = \frac{3.3}{104.333} \approx 31.63\ \mu\text{A}$$

$$I_C = 100 \cdot 31.63\ \mu\text{A} = 3.163\ \text{mA}$$

(Approximate method gave 3.3 mA — error of $\approx 4.2\%$, which is acceptable.)

**Step 6 — Q-point voltage $V_{CE}$:**
$$V_{CE} = 12 - 3.163 \cdot (2 + 1) = 12 - 9.489 = 2.511\ \text{V}$$

> **Final Q-point coordinates: $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (3.16\ \text{mA}, 2.51\ \text{V})$**

**Step 7 — Stability factor:**
$$S = \frac{101 \cdot (3.333 + 1)}{3.333 + 101 \cdot 1} = \frac{101 \cdot 4.333}{104.333} = \frac{437.6}{104.333} \approx 4.19$$

Compare to fixed bias where $S = 101$ — we have improved stability by a factor of $\approx 24\times$.


<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Q-Point Establishment Flow

This block diagram captures the *cause-and-effect* chain that establishes the Q-point in any BJT biasing circuit.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VCC DC Source] --> B[Bias Network: RB or R1 R2 RE]
    B --> C[IBQ Base Current]
    C --> D[BJT Active Region Equation]
    D --> E[ICQ equals beta times IBQ]
    B --> F[Output Loop KVL]
    E --> F
    F --> G[VCEQ Quiescent Voltage]
    E --> H[DC Load Line]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Q Point Coordinates ICQ VCEQ]
    J[Temperature Drift] --> K[ICO VBE Beta Variation]
    K --> L[Stability Factor S]
    L --> I
    M[AC Input Signal vs] --> N[AC Load Line through Q Point]
    I --> N
    N --> O[Amplified Output vo]
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style I fill:#ffe066,stroke:#806600
    style O fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style J fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style L fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
```

## 4.2 Fixed-Bias Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[VCC] --> RB[RB Base Resistor]
    RB --> BASE[Base Node B]
    BASE --> BJT[(NPN BJT)]
    V1 --> RC[RC Collector Resistor]
    RC --> COLLECTOR[Collector Node C]
    COLLECTOR --> BJT
    BJT --> EMITTER[Emitter Node E]
    EMITTER --> GND[Ground]
    BASE -. VBE 0.7V .-> EMITTER
    COLLECTOR -. VCE .-> EMITTER
    IB[IB Base Current] --> BASE
    IC[IC Collector Current] --> COLLECTOR
    style V1 fill:#cce5ff
    style GND fill:#d4edda
    style BJT fill:#ffe066
    style IB fill:#fff3cd
    style IC fill:#fff3cd
```

## 4.3 Voltage Divider Bias — Complete Schematic Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VCC[VCC Supply Rail] --> R1[R1 Upper Divider]
    R1 --> BASENODE[Base Node B]
    VCC --> R2[R2 Lower Divider]
    R2 --> GND1[Ground]
    BASENODE --> R2
    BASENODE --> BJTIN[Base of NPN BJT]
    BJTIN --> BJTDEVICE[(NPN BJT)]
    VCC --> RC[RC Collector Resistor]
    RC --> COLLECTORNODE[Collector Node C]
    COLLECTORNODE --> BJTDEVICE
    BJTDEVICE --> EMITTERNODE[Emitter Node E]
    EMITTERNODE --> RE[RE Emitter Resistor]
    RE --> GND2[Ground]
    BYPASS[(CE Bypass Capacitor AC Only)] -. parallel to RE .-> RE
    COLLECTORNODE --> COUPOUT[Coupling Capacitor CC out]
    COUPOUT --> OUTPUT[AC Output vo]
    INPUT[AC Input vs] --> COUPIN[Coupling Capacitor CC in]
    COUPIN --> BASENODE
    style VCC fill:#cce5ff
    style BJTDEVICE fill:#ffe066
    style GND1 fill:#d4edda
    style GND2 fill:#d4edda
    style BYPASS fill:#e2e3e5
    style INPUT fill:#f8d7da
    style OUTPUT fill:#d4edda
```

## 4.4 DC Load Line with Q-Point (Graphical Coordinate System)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph VCE_AXIS[Horizontal Axis VCE]
        X0[Origin 0V]
        XEND[VCC volts at Cut off]
    end
    subgraph IC_AXIS[Vertical Axis IC]
        Y0[Origin 0mA]
        YEND[VCC divided by RC at Saturation]
    end
    X0 --- Y0
    XEND --- Y0

    XEND --- YEND
    SAT[Saturation Point IC sat 0V] --> X0
    SAT --> YEND

    CUT --> Y0
    DCLOAD[DC Load Line slope minus 1 over RC] --- SAT
    DCLOAD --- CUT
    QPT[Q Point ICQ VCEQ] --- DCLOAD
    QPT --> ICQ[ICQ on vertical axis]
    QPT --> VCEQ[VCEQ on horizontal axis]
    ACTIVE[Active Region Operating Zone] -. enclosed by .-> DCLOAD

```

## 4.5 Comparison Flow — Which Bias to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Need a BJT Bias Network] --> Q1{Is temperature stability critical?}
    Q1 -- YES --> VDB[Use Voltage Divider Bias: S approaches 1]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is simplicity more important?}
    Q2 -- YES --> FB[Fixed Bias: Simpler but S equals beta plus 1]
    Q2 -- NO --> CB[Collector to Base Bias: Moderate S]
    VDB --> D1[Industrial Standard for Amplifiers]
    FB --> D2[Educational Only NOT used in production]
    CB --> D3[Occasional use in simple switching circuits]
    style START fill:#cce5ff
    style VDB fill:#d4edda
    style FB fill:#f8d7da
    style CB fill:#fff3cd
    style D1 fill:#d4edda
    style D2 fill:#f8d7da
    style D3 fill:#fff3cd
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the operating point of a BJT and explain why it must be set in the active region for amplifier applications.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **operating point (Q-point)** of a BJT is the DC quiescent point — the values of collector current $I_C$ and collector–emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ that exist in the transistor when no AC input signal is applied. It is fixed by the external biasing network.
>
> For amplifier applications, the Q-point **must be located in the active region** because:
> 1. Only in the active region does $I_C = \beta I_B$ hold linearly, ensuring faithful (undistorted) reproduction of the input signal.
> 2. In saturation, $V_{CE} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ is too small to allow negative output swing.
> 3. In cut-off, $I_C = 0$ prevents positive output swing.
> 4. The Q-point should preferably be at the **midpoint of the load line** to obtain maximum symmetrical (undistorted) output swing on both half-cycles of the AC signal. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the stability factor S? Why is it desirable to have S close to unity?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The stability factor $S$ is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current $I_{CO}$, with $V_{BE}$ and $\beta$ held constant:
> $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \bigg\rvert_{V_{BE}, \beta}$$
>
> It is desirable to have $S$ close to unity because $I_{CO}$ is **strongly temperature-dependent** — it approximately doubles for every $10^\circ\text{C}$ rise in junction temperature. A large $S$ means even a small increase in $I_{CO}$ causes a large change in $I_C$, shifting the Q-point and causing **thermal runaway**, which can destroy the transistor. When $S \to 1$, the collector current becomes essentially independent of temperature-induced leakage variations, ensuring a **stable, predictable Q-point**. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — 14 Marks

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **voltage divider bias** BJT configuration and derive expressions for the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ using the Thevenin equivalent method. State the design condition under which $I_C$ becomes independent of $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the voltage divider bias circuit shown, calculate the Q-point and the stability factor $S$.
Given: $V_{CC} = 20\ \text{V}$, $R_1 = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 2.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 120$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$. Also find the operating point if $\beta$ doubles to 240 and comment on stability. **(7 marks)**

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Circuit Diagram** — The voltage divider bias circuit consists of $R_1$ from $V_{CC}$ to base, $R_2$ from base to ground, $R_C$ from $V_{CC}$ to collector, and $R_E$ from emitter to ground.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Thevenin Equivalent at the Base**
$$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
$$R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): KVL at Input Loop**
$$V_{TH} - I_B R_{TH} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E = 0$$

With $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B$:
$$I_B = \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$
$$I_{CQ} = \beta I_B = \frac{\beta (V_{TH} - V_{BE})}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): KVL at Output Loop**
$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_{CQ} (R_C + R_E)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Design Condition for $\beta$-Independence**
If $\beta R_E \gg R_{TH}$ (practically $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$), then $I_{CQ} \approx (V_{TH} - V_{BE})/R_E$, which is **independent of $\beta$**. **[Total: 7 marks]**

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Compute $V_{TH}$**
$$V_{TH} = 20 \cdot \frac{20}{50 + 20} = 20 \cdot \frac{20}{70} = \frac{400}{70} = 5.714\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark): Compute $R_{TH}$**
$$R_{TH} = \frac{50 \cdot 20}{50 + 20} = \frac{1000}{70} = 14.286\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 (1 mark): Compute $I_B$ and $I_{CQ}$ for $\beta = 120$**
$$I_B = \frac{5.714 - 0.7}{14.286 + 121 \cdot 2.2} = \frac{5.014}{14.286 + 266.2} = \frac{5.014}{280.486} = 17.88\ \mu\text{A}$$
$$I_{CQ} = 120 \cdot 17.88\ \mu\text{A} = 2.146\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Compute $V_{CEQ}$**
$$V_{CEQ} = 20 - 2.146 \cdot (4.7 + 2.2) = 20 - 2.146 \cdot 6.9 = 20 - 14.81 = 5.19\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark): Stability Factor**
$$S = \frac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E} = \frac{121 \cdot (14.286 + 2.2)}{14.286 + 121 \cdot 2.2} = \frac{121 \cdot 16.486}{14.286 + 266.2} = \frac{1994.8}{280.486} = 7.11$$

**Step 6 (1 mark): Repeat for $\beta = 240$**
$$I_B' = \frac{5.014}{14.286 + 241 \cdot 2.2} = \frac{5.014}{14.286 + 530.2} = \frac{5.014}{544.486} = 9.21\ \mu\text{A}$$
$$I_{CQ}' = 240 \cdot 9.21\ \mu\text{A} = 2.21\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark): Comment on Stability**
The Q-point moved only from $2.146\ \text{mA}$ to $2.21\ \text{mA}$ — a change of $\approx 3\%$ when $\beta$ doubled from 120 to 240. This demonstrates **excellent $\beta$-independence** because $\beta R_E = 120 \cdot 2.2 = 264\ \text{k}\Omega$ which is $\gg 10 R_{TH} = 142.86\ \text{k}\Omega$. The voltage divider bias provides a **stable operating point** with $S \approx 7$ (compared to $S = 121$ for fixed bias with same $\beta$). **[Total: 7 marks]**

---

### ⭐ Question B [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Derive the equation of the **DC load line** for a common-emitter BJT amplifier with collector resistor $R_C$ and supply $V_{CC}$. Mark the **Q-point, saturation point, and cut-off point** on the load line. Explain why placing the Q-point at the midpoint of the load line is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fixed-bias BJT circuit has $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_B = 240\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, and silicon transistor. Determine:
  - (i) The Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$
  - (ii) The DC load line equation
  - (iii) The stability factor $S$
  - (iv) The new Q-point if $I_{CO}$ increases by $5\ \mu\text{A}$ (assume $\beta$ constant) **(7 marks)**

#### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — DC Load Line Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): KVL on Output Loop**
For a common-emitter amplifier with collector resistor $R_C$ and DC supply $V_{CC}$:

$$V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} = 0$$

Rearranged:
$$I_C = -\frac{V_{CE}}{R_C} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Identify Saturation and Cut-off Points**
- When $V_{CE} = 0$: $I_{C(\text{sat})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ — **Saturation point** on the $I_C$ axis.
- When $I_C = 0$: $V_{CE(\text{off})} = V_{CC}$ — **Cut-off point** on the $V_{CE}$ axis.

The straight line joining these two points is the **DC load line**, with slope $-\dfrac{1}{R_C}$.

**Step 3 (1 mark): Q-point Location**
The **Q-point** is the intersection of this load line with the transistor's $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ output characteristic curve corresponding to the DC base current $I_{BQ}$ set by the bias network.

**Step 4 (2 marks): Why Midpoint is Preferred**
Placing the Q-point at the **midpoint of the load line** ensures:
1. **Maximum symmetrical swing** — the AC output can swing equally in both positive and negative directions without hitting saturation or cut-off limits.
2. **Minimum distortion** — equal positive and negative half-cycles of the input signal are amplified without clipping.
3. **Maximum peak-to-peak output** — given by $V_{o(\text{pp})} = 2 \cdot \min(I_{CQ} R_{ac}, V_{CEQ} - V_{CE(\text{sat})})$.
4. If the Q-point is too close to saturation, the negative half-cycle clips; if too close to cut-off, the positive half-cycle clips. **[Total: 7 marks]**

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_B = 240\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$.

**(i) Q-point calculation (2 marks):**
$$I_{BQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{240\ \text{k}\Omega} = \frac{11.3}{240} = 47.08\ \mu\text{A}$$

$$I_{CQ} = \beta \cdot I_{BQ} = 100 \cdot 47.08\ \mu\text{A} = 4.708\ \text{mA}$$

$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_C = 12 - 4.708 \cdot 1.2 = 12 - 5.65 = 6.35\ \text{V}$$

**Q-point: $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ}) = (4.71\ \text{mA}, 6.35\ \text{V})$**

**(ii) DC Load Line equation (1 mark):**
$$I_C = -\frac{V_{CE}}{1.2\ \text{k}\Omega} + \frac{12\ \text{V}}{1.2\ \text{k}\Omega} = -\frac{V_{CE}}{1200} + 10\ \text{mA}$$
- Saturation point: $(0\ \text{V}, 10\ \text{mA})$
- Cut-off point: $(12\ \text{V}, 0\ \text{mA})$

**(iii) Stability Factor (2 marks):**
For fixed bias:
$$S = \beta + 1 = 100 + 1 = 101$$

**(iv) New Q-point when $\Delta I_{CO} = 5\ \mu\text{A}$ (2 marks):**
Using the active region equation with leakage term:
$$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CO}$$
$$\Delta I_C = S \cdot \Delta I_{CO} = 101 \cdot 5\ \mu\text{A} = 505\ \mu\text{A} = 0.505\ \text{mA}$$

New $I_{CQ}' = 4.708 + 0.505 = 5.213\ \text{mA}$

$$V_{CEQ}' = 12 - 5.213 \cdot 1.2 = 12 - 6.256 = 5.744\ \text{V}$$

**New Q-point: $(5.21\ \text{mA}, 5.74\ \text{V})$** — a significant shift, demonstrating the **poor stability** of fixed bias. **[Total: 7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the leakage term**: When asked about stability or $I_C$ at elevated temperature, many students compute $I_C = \beta I_B$ only. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for omitting the $(\beta+1) I_{CO}$ term.
>
> 2. **Confusing $R_E$ bypass action**: At **DC analysis**, $R_E$ is **fully active**. At **AC analysis**, the bypass capacitor $C_E$ makes $R_E$ *invisible* to the signal. Mixing these is a classic 2-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Wrong Thevenin sign convention**: Always remember that $R_{TH} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ — students often write $R_{TH} = R_1 + R_2$ (which is the *wrong* topology — that would be a series connection).
>
> 4. **Approximating $I_E \approx I_C$**: For $\beta = 100$, $I_E = 101/100 \cdot I_C = 1.01 I_C$. The 1% error is usually acceptable in KTU, but if the problem says *"calculate exactly"*, you must use $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B$.
>
> 5. **Units in stability factor calculation**: $S$ is dimensionless — but $R_{TH}$, $R_B$, $R_E$ must be in the **same unit** (all kΩ or all Ω). Mismatched units = zero marks for that step.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to check design validity** in VDB: Always verify $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$ before using the approximate formula. KTU examiners explicitly test this.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This 30 Minutes Before the Exam**

- [x] **Q-point** = DC operating coordinates $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ with **no input signal** applied.
- [x] Q-point is fixed by the **biasing network** (resistors + $V_{CC}$).
- [x] The Q-point must lie in the **active region** for linear amplification.
- [x] **DC load line** equation: $I_C = -\dfrac{V_{CE}}{R_C} + \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$ — a straight line on the output characteristics.
- [x] Endpoints: **Saturation** at $(0\ \text{V}, V_{CC}/R_C)$ and **Cut-off** at $(V_{CC}, 0\ \text{mA})$.
- [x] **Midpoint Q-point** → maximum symmetrical AC swing, minimum distortion.
- [x] **Fixed bias** → simplest, but $S = \beta + 1$ → terrible stability. Not used in practice.
- [x] **Voltage divider bias (VDB)** → industry standard. $S = \dfrac{(\beta+1)(R_{TH} + R_E)}{R_{TH} + (\beta+1) R_E}$.
- [x] **Design rule for VDB**: if $\beta R_E \geq 10 R_{TH}$, then $I_C \approx (V_{TH} - V_{BE})/R_E$ (independent of $\beta$).
- [x] **Stability factors**: $S$ (vs $I_{CO}$), $S'$ (vs $V_{BE}$), $S''$ (vs $\beta$). Sum rule: $S + S'(\partial V_{BE}/\partial I_C) + S''(\partial \beta/\partial I_C) = 1$.
- [x] **Thermal runaway** condition: $\dfrac{\partial P_C}{\partial T_j} < \dfrac{1}{\theta_{JA}}$ must hold — else the device self-destructs.
- [x] **$I_{CO}$ doubles every $10^\circ\text{C}$** — primary cause of Q-point drift.
- [x] **$V_{BE}$ decreases by $\approx 2\ \text{mV}/^\circ\text{C}$** — secondary cause.
- [x] At AC signal frequency, $C_E$ bypasses $R_E$ → emitter is effectively at AC ground.
- [x] Standard Si values: $V_{BE} = 0.7\ \text{V}$, $V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 0.2\ \text{V}$, $V_T = 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K.
- [x] **Always** write $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B$, not $I_E = I_C$ (the latter is an approximation, often acceptable but not exact).
- [x] The Q-point in VDB is approximately at the **center of the load line** when $V_{TH} \approx V_{CC}/2 + V_{BE}$ and $R_C = R_E$ (a common design choice).
- [x] **Order of attack in KTU numerical problems**: (1) $V_{TH}$, (2) $R_{TH}$, (3) $I_B$, (4) $I_{CQ}$, (5) $V_{CEQ}$, (6) $S$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: BJT as Amplifier (CE Configuration) - Input Characteristics, Output Characteristics, Current Amplification Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** BJT as Amplifier (CE Configuration) - Input Characteristics, Output Characteristics, Current Amplification Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a BJT in Common Emitter (CE) Configuration?

A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** is a three-terminal, current-controlled semiconductor device used for amplification and switching. When operated as a **linear amplifier**, the BJT is biased in the **active region** (also called the forward-active region) of its characteristic curves.

The **Common Emitter (CE) configuration** is the most widely used transistor amplifier topology in analog electronics. In this arrangement:
- The **emitter (E)** terminal is common to both the input loop and the output loop (typically connected to ground or via a bypass capacitor).
- The **input signal** is applied between the **base (B)** and the **emitter (E)**.
- The **amplified output signal** is taken between the **collector (C)** and the **emitter (E)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *In the CE configuration, the input is applied between Base and Emitter, and the output is taken between Collector and Emitter. The Emitter is common to both input and output ports, making it the reference terminal for the amplifier.*

### 1.2 Why CE is the Most Popular Configuration

The CE configuration provides:
- **High current gain** ($\beta$ typically between **20 and 500** for silicon BJTs).
- **High voltage gain** (multiplied by the load resistor).
- **High power gain** (product of current and voltage gain).
- **Moderate input impedance** (typically **1 kΩ to 5 kΩ**).
- **Moderate output impedance** (typically **50 kΩ to 500 kΩ**).
- **180° phase reversal** between input and output (a key identifying feature).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CE amplifier introduces a **180° phase shift** between input and output. When the input voltage increases, the output voltage decreases. This inversion is a hallmark of CE operation and frequently appears in KTU numerical problems.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Faucet & Pressure" Model

Imagine a **water faucet (tap)** connected to a high-pressure main pipe. The small knob you turn is like the **base** of the transistor, the high-pressure main pipe is the **collector supply**, and the output water flow is the **collector current**.

- A **tiny turn of the knob** (small change in base current $I_B$) results in a **massive surge of water** (large change in collector current $I_C$).
- The water pressure regulator (collector voltage $V_{CE}$) sets the operating point so the faucet works efficiently without leaking (saturation) or shutting off (cutoff).
- The "current amplification factor" $\beta$ is simply the **ratio of output water flow to knob rotation effort**.

In the CE configuration, the small AC signal at the base is like "tweaking the knob rhythmically," and the large amplified current swing at the collector is the "rhythmic water surge" that can drive a loudspeaker or any downstream load.

### 1.4 Input Characteristics, Output Characteristics, and Current Amplification Factor — Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Input Characteristics** of a CE BJT describe the functional relationship between the **input base current ($I_B$)** and the **input base-to-emitter voltage ($V_{BE}$)** at a fixed collector-to-emitter voltage ($V_{CE}$). The curve resembles a **forward-biased p-n junction diode** characteristic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Output Characteristics** of a CE BJT describe the functional relationship between the **output collector current ($I_C$)** and the **output collector-to-emitter voltage ($V_{CE}$)** for various fixed values of the **input base current ($I_B$)**. The family of curves divides into three operating regions: **Active**, **Saturation**, and **Cutoff**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Current Amplification Factor ($\beta$)** is defined as the ratio of the change (or small-signal increment) in collector current to the corresponding change in base current, with $V_{CE}$ held constant. Mathematically, $\beta = \Delta I_C \ / \ \Delta I_B$ at constant $V_{CE}$. It is also called the **large-signal current gain** when referring to the ratio $I_C \ / \ I_B$ in the forward-active region.

---

## 1.5 Circuit Topology and Biasing Conventions

In a practical CE amplifier, the BJT is biased using **two DC supply sources**:
- $V_{BB}$ (or derived from $V_{CC}$ via a resistor network) provides forward bias to the **base-emitter junction**.
- $V_{CC}$ provides reverse bias to the **base-collector junction**.

The **Q-point (Quiescent point)** or operating point is set such that the transistor operates in the **active region**, allowing the input AC signal to be amplified linearly without distortion.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU exam problems, you will often be given numerical values of $I_B$, $I_C$, and $V_{CE}$ and asked to compute $\beta$, $\alpha$, $I_E$, transconductance $g_m$, or input/output resistance. Memorize the standard relationships listed in the formula sheet of **SECTION 2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the BJT as an Amplifier

A BJT amplifies because the **collector current $I_C$** is **directly proportional** to the **base current $I_B$**, controlled by the **transconductance characteristic** of the device. The base-emitter junction, forward-biased, modulates the minority carrier injection across the base region. These carriers are swept into the collector by the reverse-biased collector-base junction, producing a much larger current at the collector.

The amplification chain is:
1. A small **AC signal voltage** $v_{be}$ is applied at the base.
2. This modulates the **base current** $i_b$ (small AC component).
3. The base current modulates a **larger collector current** $i_c = \beta \cdot i_b$.
4. The collector current passes through a **load resistor** $R_C$, producing a much larger **AC output voltage** $v_{ce} = -i_c \cdot R_C$.
5. The voltage gain $A_V = v_{ce} \ / \ v_{be}$ is typically large (e.g., 100 to 1000).

The negative sign in step 4 indicates the **180° phase inversion** characteristic of CE amplifiers.

## 2.2 Input Characteristics of CE Configuration

The **input characteristics** are obtained by plotting the **input current $I_B$** (y-axis) against the **input voltage $V_{BE}$** (x-axis) while keeping the **output voltage $V_{CE}$** at a constant value.

**Procedure to plot:**
- Set $V_{CE} = 0 \, \text{V}$ and vary $V_{BE}$ from 0 to a few volts, recording the corresponding $I_B$.
- Repeat for $V_{CE} = 1 \, \text{V}$, then $V_{CE} = 5 \, \text{V}$, etc.
- The resulting curves are the input characteristics.

**Key observations from the curves:**

- For $V_{CE} = 0$, the transistor behaves essentially like a **forward-biased diode** between B and E with the C-B junction also forward biased. The curve has a low threshold voltage (~0.5 V to 0.7 V for silicon).
- As $V_{CE}$ **increases**, the curves shift slightly to the **right** — that is, for a given $V_{BE}$, the base current $I_B$ **decreases**. This phenomenon is called the **Early Effect** (base-width modulation).
- Beyond a certain $V_{CE}$ (typically 1 V or more), the curves almost coincide. This means the input characteristics become nearly **independent of $V_{CE}$** in the active region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The input characteristics of a CE BJT resemble a forward-biased p-n junction diode. The threshold (cut-in) voltage is approximately **0.7 V for silicon** and **0.3 V for germanium** at room temperature (300 K).

## 2.3 Output Characteristics of CE Configuration

The **output characteristics** form the most important graph in transistor analysis. They are obtained by plotting the **output collector current $I_C$** (y-axis) against the **output collector-to-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$** (x-axis), with the **input base current $I_B$** held constant for each curve.

**Procedure to plot:**
- Fix $I_B = 0$ (cutoff condition). Vary $V_{CE}$ from 0 to a maximum value and record $I_C$.
- Increase $I_B$ to a new value, say $I_B = 20 \, \mu A$, and repeat.
- Continue for several values of $I_B$ (e.g., 0, 20 µA, 40 µA, 60 µA, 80 µA, 100 µA).

The resulting family of curves has three clearly identifiable regions:

### 2.3.1 Active (Forward-Active) Region
- The **base-emitter junction is forward-biased**, and the **base-collector junction is reverse-biased**.
- $I_C$ is **nearly constant** for a wide range of $V_{CE}$ (a slight upward slope exists due to the **Early Effect**).
- The transistor behaves as a **linear current amplifier** with $I_C = \beta I_B$.
- This is the **amplification region** used in linear amplifiers.

### 2.3.2 Saturation Region
- Both the **base-emitter and base-collector junctions are forward-biased**.
- $I_C$ **decreases** as $V_{CE}$ decreases below the saturation voltage $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ (typically **0.2 V**).
- $I_C$ is **no longer controlled** by $I_B$; instead, it is limited by the external collector resistor $R_C$.
- The transistor behaves as a **closed switch** (ON state).

### 2.3.3 Cutoff Region
- Both the **base-emitter and base-collector junctions are reverse-biased**.
- $I_B = 0$ and $I_C \approx I_{CEO}$ (collector-emitter leakage current, very small).
- The transistor behaves as an **open switch** (OFF state).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to identify regions in output characteristics, explicitly mention the **junction biasing conditions**:
> - Active: BE → Forward, BC → Reverse
> - Saturation: BE → Forward, BC → Forward
> - Cutoff: BE → Reverse, BC → Reverse

## 2.4 Current Amplification Factor — The Heart of BJT Amplification

There are two commonly used current amplification factors in a BJT:

### 2.4.1 Common-Emitter Current Gain ($\beta$)

Defined as the **small-signal ratio** of the change in collector current to the change in base current, with $V_{CE}$ held constant:

$$
\beta_{ac} = \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}\bigg|_{V_{CE} = \text{constant}}
$$

The **DC (large-signal)** version is:

$$
\beta_{DC} = \frac{I_C}{I_B}\bigg|_{Q\text{-point}}
$$

For most engineering analysis, we assume $\beta_{ac} \approx \beta_{DC} = \beta$.

> [!NOTE]
> The current amplification factor $\beta$ is also called $h_{fe}$ in **hybrid-parameter (h-parameter) models** of the transistor. The "f" stands for "forward," "e" for "common emitter." This is a high-yield topic in KTU's analog electronics module.

### 2.4.2 Common-Base Current Gain ($\alpha$)

Defined as the ratio of collector current to emitter current:

$$
\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E}
$$

The value of $\alpha$ is always **less than unity** (typically **0.95 to 0.99**) because $I_C < I_E$.

### 2.4.3 Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\beta$

Using Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor: $I_E = I_B + I_C$, we obtain:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}
$$

This is one of the **most-tested formulas** in KTU exams.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes all critical formulas for the BJT CE amplifier, with strict adherence to the no-vertical-pipe rule.

| **Parameter / Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Value / Unit** | **Operating Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Common-Emitter current gain | $\beta$ | $\beta = I_C \ / \ I_B$ | 20 to 500 (dimensionless) | Active region |
| Common-Base current gain | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = I_C \ / \ I_E$ | 0.95 to 0.99 (dimensionless) | Active region |
| Alpha-Beta relationship | $\alpha, \beta$ | $\beta = \alpha \ / \ (1 - \alpha)$ | — | Active region |
| Alpha-Beta relationship (alt.) | $\alpha, \beta$ | $\alpha = \beta \ / \ (1 + \beta)$ | — | Active region |
| Emitter current | $I_E$ | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | mA range | All regions |
| Collector current (active) | $I_C$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | mA range | Active region |
| Collector current (general) | $I_C$ | $I_C = \alpha I_E$ | mA range | Active region |
| Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | $V_T = kT \ / \ q$ | $\approx 26 \, \text{mV}$ at 300 K | — |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $g_m = I_C \ / \ V_T$ | $\approx 38.5 \cdot I_C$ (S, with $I_C$ in A) | Active region |
| Relation: $g_m$ to $\beta$ | $g_m, \beta$ | $g_m = \beta \ / \ r_\pi$ | — | Small-signal |
| Input resistance (small-signal) | $r_\pi$ | $r_\pi = \beta V_T \ / \ I_C = \beta \ / \ g_m$ | $\approx 1 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ to } 5 \text{ k}\Omega$ | Small-signal |
| Output resistance (with Early effect) | $r_o$ | $r_o = (V_A + V_{CE}) \ / \ I_C$ | $\approx 50 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ to } 500 \text{ k}\Omega$ | Active region |
| Base-emitter voltage (silicon) | $V_{BE}$ | — | $\approx 0.7 \, \text{V}$ | Active region |
| Saturation voltage | $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | — | $\approx 0.2 \, \text{V}$ | Saturation |
| Leakage current | $I_{CEO}$ | $I_{CEO} = (1 + \beta) I_{CBO}$ | nA to µA | Cutoff |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The CE amplifier is the **workhorse of analog integrated circuits**. It forms the basic gain stage in operational amplifiers (op-amps), audio pre-amplifiers, RF amplifiers, and switching power supplies. Understanding its characteristics is essential for designing bias networks, predicting gain, and analyzing frequency response in real circuits.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\beta$

We begin from two fundamental KCL relationships in a BJT operating in the active region.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):**

The total emitter current is the sum of base and collector currents:

$$
I_E = I_B + I_C
$$

**Step 2 — Express collector current in terms of base current:**

By the definition of the common-emitter current gain:

$$
I_C = \beta \cdot I_B
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KCL equation:**

Replace $I_C$ in the KCL equation with $\beta I_B$:

$$
I_E = I_B + \beta I_B = I_B (1 + \beta)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of $\alpha$:**

The common-base current gain is defined as the ratio of $I_C$ to $I_E$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions for $I_C$ and $I_E$:**

Substitute $I_C = \beta I_B$ and $I_E = I_B (1 + \beta)$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta I_B}{I_B (1 + \beta)}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify by canceling $I_B$:**

Since $I_B \neq 0$ in the active region, we can divide numerator and denominator by $I_B$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

**Step 7 — Invert to get $\beta$ in terms of $\alpha$:**

Start with $\alpha (1 + \beta) = \beta$:

$$
\alpha + \alpha \beta = \beta
$$

**Step 8 — Group the $\beta$ terms on one side:**

$$
\alpha = \beta - \alpha \beta = \beta (1 - \alpha)
$$

**Step 9 — Solve for $\beta$:**

Divide both sides by $(1 - \alpha)$:

$$
\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}
$$

This completes the derivation of the fundamental $\alpha$–$\beta$ relationship.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: DC Analysis of a Simple CE Circuit

Consider a BJT in CE configuration with a base bias resistor $R_B$ connected to $V_{CC}$ and a collector resistor $R_C$. The emitter is at ground.

**Step 1 — Write KVL around the base-emitter loop:**

$$
V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $I_B$:**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
$$

For silicon, $V_{BE} \approx 0.7 \, \text{V}$. For germanium, $V_{BE} \approx 0.3 \, \text{V}$.

**Step 3 — Apply the active-region collector current relation:**

$$
I_C = \beta I_B = \beta \cdot \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
$$

**Step 4 — Write KVL around the collector-emitter loop:**

$$
V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $V_{CE}$:**

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C
$$

**Step 6 — Verify the active-region condition:**

The transistor is in the active region if $V_{CE} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ (typically $> 0.2 \, \text{V}$). If not, the BJT is in saturation, and the above formula for $I_C$ is invalid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Each step above directly applies Ohm's Law or Kirchhoff's Voltage Law. The collector current is found by multiplying $I_B$ (from the input loop) by $\beta$ (the amplification factor). The output voltage $V_{CE}$ is then the supply voltage minus the drop across $R_C$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Small-Signal Transconductance $g_m$

The transconductance $g_m$ describes how effectively the input voltage $V_{BE}$ controls the output current $I_C$ in the small-signal model.

**Step 1 — Start from the Ebers-Moll equation for collector current:**

The collector current in the active region is given by:

$$
I_C = I_S \exp\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)
$$

where $I_S$ is the saturation current (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-15} \, \text{A}$), and $V_T$ is the thermal voltage ($\approx 26 \, \text{mV}$ at 300 K).

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $V_{BE}$:**

$$
\frac{dI_C}{dV_{BE}} = I_S \cdot \frac{1}{V_T} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right) = \frac{I_S}{V_T} \exp\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that the exponential term equals $I_C \ / \ I_S$:**

$$
\frac{dI_C}{dV_{BE}} = \frac{I_S}{V_T} \cdot \frac{I_C}{I_S} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the transconductance $g_m$:**

$$
g_m = \frac{dI_C}{dV_{BE}} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerically, at room temperature:**

With $V_T \approx 26 \, \text{mV}$:

$$
g_m \approx \frac{I_C}{26 \, \text{mV}} \approx 38.46 \cdot I_C \quad (\text{where } I_C \text{ is in amperes})
$$

For example, if $I_C = 1 \, \text{mA}$, then $g_m \approx 38.46 \, \text{mS}$ (millisiemens).

**Step 6 — Relate $g_m$ to $\beta$ and $r_\pi$:**

The small-signal base resistance is:

$$
r_\pi = \frac{V_T}{I_B} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \frac{\beta}{g_m}
$$

Thus:

$$
g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** The transconductance $g_m$ arises from the exponential I-V characteristic of the base-emitter diode. The factor $1 / V_T$ comes from differentiating the exponential. At room temperature, $V_T$ is constant, so $g_m$ is directly proportional to the bias current $I_C$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Input Resistance of the CE Stage (with Emitter Resistor)

Consider a CE stage with an unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$ (no bypass capacitor).

**Step 1 — Write the small-signal input loop equation:**

The base current $i_b$ flows into the base, and the emitter current is $i_e = (1 + \beta) i_b$.

**Step 2 — Voltage drop across the base-emitter junction and $R_E$:**

$$
v_{be} = i_b \cdot r_\pi + i_e \cdot R_E = i_b \cdot r_\pi + (1 + \beta) i_b \cdot R_E
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $i_b$:**

$$
v_{be} = i_b \left[ r_\pi + (1 + \beta) R_E \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Define the input resistance $R_{in}$:**

$$
R_{in} = \frac{v_{be}}{i_b} = r_\pi + (1 + \beta) R_E
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate for large $\beta$:**

If $\beta \gg 1$ and $\beta R_E \gg r_\pi$:

$$
R_{in} \approx (1 + \beta) R_E \approx \beta R_E
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU problems:** The unbypassed emitter resistor $R_E$ **linearizes** the amplifier (improves stability) but **reduces voltage gain** by a factor of approximately $(1 + \beta) R_E / R_C$ (in the bypassed case, the gain is approximately $g_m R_C$). A common exam question asks the student to compare gain with and without the bypass capacitor.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (3-mark style):** A BJT in CE configuration has $I_B = 50 \, \mu A$ and $I_C = 3 \, \text{mA}$ at the Q-point. Calculate $\alpha$ and $I_E$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 60
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_E$ using KCL:**

$$
I_E = I_B + I_C = 50 \, \mu A + 3 \, \text{mA} = 0.05 \, \text{mA} + 3 \, \text{mA} = 3.05 \, \text{mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\alpha$:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \frac{3 \, \text{mA}}{3.05 \, \text{mA}} = 0.9836
$$

**Step 4 — Verify using the $\alpha$–$\beta$ formula:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \frac{60}{61} = 0.9836 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of BJT Configurations

| **Parameter** | **Common Base (CB)** | **Common Emitter (CE)** | **Common Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input resistance | Very low ($20 \, \Omega$ to $100 \, \Omega$) | Moderate ($1 \, \text{k}\Omega$ to $5 \, \text{k}\Omega$) | High ($150 \, \text{k}\Omega$ to $600 \, \text{k}\Omega$) |
| Output resistance | Very high ($1 \, \text{M}\Omega$) | High ($50 \, \text{k}\Omega$ to $500 \, \text{k}\Omega$) | Low ($25 \, \Omega$ to $50 \, \Omega$) |
| Current gain ($\alpha$ or $\beta$) | $< 1$ ($\alpha \approx 0.99$) | High ($\beta \approx 100$) | High ($\beta \approx 100$) |
| Voltage gain | High | High | $< 1$ (unity, follow) |
| Power gain | Moderate | **Highest** | Moderate |
| Phase shift | 0° (in-phase) | **180° (inverted)** | 0° (in-phase) |
| Typical application | High-frequency RF input | General-purpose amplifier | Impedance buffer, output stage |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Heuristic:** Whenever a question asks "which configuration has highest power gain?" the answer is **CE**. Whenever it asks "which has voltage gain $< 1$?" the answer is **CC (emitter follower)**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Block-Level Architecture of the CE Amplifier

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of signals and bias networks in a CE amplifier, from the input AC source to the amplified output across the load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[("AC Input Source v_in")] --> cb["Coupling Capacitor C_in"]
    cb --> baseNode(("Base Terminal B"))
    baseNode --> vbe["V_BE Forward Biased ~0.7V"]
    vbe --> bjtsym{{"BJT in CE Configuration"}}
    bjtsym --> biasV["V_CC Supply through R_C"]
    biasV --> cout["Coupling Capacitor C_out"]
    cout --> rload[("Load R_L")]
    rload --> outNode[("Output v_out")]
    bjtsym --> emitterNode(("Emitter to Ground via R_E"))
    emitterNode --> gnd[("Ground")]
    bjtsym --> ctrlCur["I_C = beta times I_B"]
    ctrlCur --> gainSym[("Voltage Gain A_V approx -g_m times R_C")]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The AC input enters through a **coupling capacitor** $C_{in}$ (blocks DC, passes AC).
- The signal modulates the **base-emitter voltage** $V_{BE}$.
- The BJT amplifies the input current by a factor of $\beta$ at the collector.
- The amplified current passes through $R_C$, producing the output voltage $v_{out}$.
- The emitter is connected to ground (directly or via a bypass capacitor for AC).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Output Characteristics Family of Curves (Schematic Block View)

This block diagram represents the three operating regions of the CE output characteristics and the Q-point placement.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph axis["Output Characteristic Plot"]
        xaxis["X axis: V_CE (0 to V_CC_max)"] --- yaxis["Y axis: I_C (0 to I_C_max)"]
    end
    subgraph regions["Operating Regions on the I_C vs V_CE Plane"]
        cutoff["Cutoff Region: I_B = 0, I_C approx I_CEO (very small)"]
        active["Active Region: BJT as Linear Amplifier, I_C = beta I_B, slight upward slope"]
        sat["Saturation Region: Both Junctions Forward Biased, V_CE less than 0.2V"]
    end
    subgraph qpoint["Q-Point Placement"]
        qid["Q-Point Coordinates: V_CEQ, I_CQ chosen at center of active region"]
    end
    cutoff --> active
    active --> sat
    qid --> active
```

**Interpretation of the regions:**
- **Cutoff region** lies along the x-axis (lowest $I_C$).
- **Saturation region** lies along the y-axis (lowest $V_{CE}$).
- **Active region** is the broad area between them, where the curves are nearly horizontal and equally spaced for equal increments of $I_B$.
- The **Q-point** is chosen centrally within the active region to allow maximum undistorted AC swing.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Functional Flow from Input to Output in CE Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Input AC Signal v_in Applied at Base"] --> step2["Step 2: Base Emitter Junction Forward Biased"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Small Base Current i_b Generated"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Collector Current i_c = beta times i_b is Much Larger"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: i_c Flows Through R_C Creating Voltage Drop"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Output Voltage v_out = V_CC minus i_c times R_C"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: Net Result - Amplified and Inverted AC Signal at Output"]
```

This sequence captures the **causal chain** of amplification: a small input voltage causes a small base current, which the transistor multiplies by $\beta$ at the collector, and the resulting current swing through $R_C$ produces a large, inverted output voltage.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Conceptual Transistor as a Current-Controlled Current Source

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ib(("I_B in")) --> cccs{{"BJT as Current Controlled Current Source"}}
    betaLabel["Multiplier beta"] --> cccs
    cccs --> ic(("I_C out = beta times I_B"))
    cccs --> earlyLabel["Output Resistance r_o due to Early Effect"]
    earlyLabel --> ic
```

**Engineering note:** In the small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ model, the BJT is represented as a **voltage-controlled current source (VCCS)** with $g_m \cdot v_{be}$, but the **DC behavior** is a current-controlled current source (CCCS) with $\beta \cdot i_b$. The output resistance $r_o$ models the Early Effect.

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above use **block-level representations** rather than detailed circuit schematics, as Mermaid is best suited for flowchart-style functional representations. In an examination, you are expected to draw the **actual circuit diagram** of the CE amplifier with all components ($R_B$, $R_C$, $R_E$, $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, bypass capacitor $C_E$, and DC supplies).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the current amplification factor in CE configuration and explain its significance. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The **current amplification factor** in the common-emitter (CE) configuration is defined as the ratio of the change in collector current to the change in base current, with the collector-to-emitter voltage held constant:

$$
\beta = \left.\frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_B}\right|_{V_{CE} = \text{constant}}
$$

It is also denoted as $h_{fe}$ in the hybrid-parameter model. The significance of $\beta$ is that it determines the **DC current gain** of the transistor in the active region: $I_C = \beta I_B$. A small base current controls a much larger collector current, which is the fundamental principle of BJT amplification. Typical values of $\beta$ for silicon BJTs range from **20 to 500**, making CE the configuration with the **highest current and power gain** among the three basic configurations.

### Question 2: Sketch and label the input characteristics of a BJT in CE configuration. [3 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **input characteristics** of a BJT in CE configuration are obtained by plotting the **input base current $I_B$** (y-axis) against the **input base-to-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$** (x-axis) at a fixed $V_{CE}$. The family of curves is drawn for several values of $V_{CE}$ (e.g., 0 V, 1 V, 5 V, 10 V).

**Key features to be labeled on the sketch:**
- The curve resembles a **forward-biased silicon p-n junction diode**.
- Threshold (cut-in) voltage $\approx \mathbf{0.7 \, V}$ for silicon.
- As $V_{CE}$ increases, the curve shifts slightly to the right (Early Effect), and the input current $I_B$ for a given $V_{BE}$ decreases.
- The curves nearly coincide for $V_{CE} \geq 1 \, \text{V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> In a 3-mark KTU answer, the student should include: (i) axes labels, (ii) the shape of the curve, (iii) the threshold voltage value, and (iv) the effect of increasing $V_{CE}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: BJT in CE Configuration — Comprehensive Analysis [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the input and output characteristics of a BJT in CE configuration. Identify and describe the three operating regions in the output characteristics. [**7 Marks**]

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (description for the answer sheet):**

A BJT with emitter at common ground, input applied between base and emitter through $V_{BB}$ (or base-bias resistor $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$), and output taken between collector and emitter. The collector is connected to $V_{CC}$ through $R_C$. Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$ and $C_{out}$ are used for AC signal injection and extraction.

**Input Characteristics:**
- **Plot:** $I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$ at constant $V_{CE}$.
- **Nature:** Resembles a forward-biased p-n junction diode.
- **Cut-in voltage:** $\approx 0.7 \, \text{V}$ (Si) or $0.3 \, \text{V}$ (Ge).
- **Effect of $V_{CE}$:** Increasing $V_{CE}$ shifts the curve slightly to the right (Early Effect), causing a small reduction in $I_B$ for a given $V_{BE}$. This is because a larger $V_{CE}$ widens the depletion region at the base-collector junction, effectively reducing the base width and hence the recombination in the base, requiring slightly less base current to sustain the same collector current.

**Output Characteristics:**
- **Plot:** $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ at constant $I_B$ for several values of $I_B$.
- **Three regions:**

| **Region** | **BE Junction** | **BC Junction** | **$V_{CE}$ range** | **$I_C$ behavior** | **Transistor mode** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cutoff | Reverse | Reverse | $V_{CE} \geq 0$ | $I_C \approx I_{CEO}$ (very small, $\mu$A or nA) | OFF switch |
| Active | Forward | Reverse | $V_{CE} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ (nearly constant, slight slope) | Linear amplifier |
| Saturation | Forward | Forward | $V_{CE} < V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2 \, \text{V}$ | $I_C < \beta I_B$ (limited by $R_C$) | ON switch |

- The active region is the **amplification region** used in linear amplifiers. The slight upward slope of the active-region curves is due to the **Early Effect**, characterized by the **Early voltage $V_A$**.

**(b)** For a BJT in CE configuration, the following measurements were taken at the Q-point: $I_C = 5 \, \text{mA}$, $I_B = 50 \, \mu A$, $V_{CE} = 10 \, \text{V}$. Calculate (i) $\beta$, (ii) $\alpha$, (iii) $I_E$, (iv) transconductance $g_m$ at $300 \, \text{K}$, (v) small-signal input resistance $r_\pi$, and (vi) comment on the type of amplifier configuration and its distinguishing feature. [**7 Marks**]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Current gain $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 100
$$

**(ii) Common-base current gain $\alpha$:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.9901
$$

**(iii) Emitter current $I_E$:**

$$
I_E = I_B + I_C = 50 \, \mu A + 5 \, \text{mA} = 5.05 \, \text{mA}
$$

**(iv) Transconductance $g_m$:**

At $T = 300 \, \text{K}$, the thermal voltage is:

$$
V_T = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 25.875 \, \text{mV} \approx 26 \, \text{mV}
$$

Then:

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.1923 \, \text{S} = 192.3 \, \text{mS}
$$

**(v) Small-signal input resistance $r_\pi$:**

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.1923} \approx 520 \, \Omega
$$

Alternatively:

$$
r_\pi = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C} = \frac{100 \times 26 \, \text{mV}}{5 \, \text{mA}} = 520 \, \Omega
$$

**(vi) Configuration comment:**

This is a **common-emitter (CE) configuration**. Its distinguishing feature is the **180° phase reversal** between input and output, along with **high current gain ($\beta$)**, **high voltage gain**, and **high power gain**, making it the most widely used BJT amplifier topology.

---

### Question B: BJT Biasing and Current Amplification [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the current amplification factors $\alpha$ and $\beta$ of a BJT. Derive the relationship between them. [**7 Marks**]

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of $\alpha$ (Common-Base Current Gain):**

The common-base current gain is the ratio of the output collector current to the input emitter current:

$$
\alpha = \left.\frac{I_C}{I_E}\right|_{\text{active region}}
$$

Since $I_C < I_E$ (a small portion of the emitter current is lost as base current), $\alpha$ is always **less than unity**, typically between **0.95 and 0.99**.

**Definition of $\beta$ (Common-Emitter Current Gain):**

The common-emitter current gain is the ratio of the output collector current to the input base current:

$$
\beta = \left.\frac{I_C}{I_B}\right|_{V_{CE} = \text{constant, active region}}
$$

It is also called $h_{fe}$ in the hybrid-parameter model. Typical values range from **20 to 500**.

**Derivation of the $\alpha$–$\beta$ relationship:**

Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the BJT terminals:

$$
I_E = I_B + I_C \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

From the definition of $\beta$:

$$
I_C = \beta I_B \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

Substituting equation (2) into equation (1):

$$
I_E = I_B + \beta I_B = I_B (1 + \beta)
$$

By definition of $\alpha$:

$$
\alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} = \frac{\beta I_B}{I_B (1 + \beta)} = \frac{\beta}{1 + \beta}
$$

Rearranging for $\beta$:

$$
\alpha (1 + \beta) = \beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha + \alpha \beta = \beta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \beta (1 - \alpha)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** A small change in $\alpha$ (e.g., from 0.99 to 0.98) produces a large change in $\beta$ (e.g., from 100 to 50). This explains why CE amplifiers are **more sensitive to device variations** and **less temperature-stable** than CB amplifiers, and why **emitter-degeneration resistors** are commonly used for bias stabilization.

**(b)** A silicon BJT in CE configuration has $V_{CC} = 12 \, \text{V}$, $R_B = 600 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2 \, \text{k}\Omega$, and $\beta = 100$. Calculate (i) $I_B$, (ii) $I_C$, (iii) $V_{CE}$, and (iv) the region of operation. Take $V_{BE} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$ for silicon. [**7 Marks**]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Base current $I_B$:**

Apply KVL around the base-emitter loop:

$$
V_{CC} = I_B R_B + V_{BE}
$$

Solve for $I_B$:

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B} = \frac{12 - 0.7}{600 \times 10^3} = \frac{11.3}{600 \times 10^3} \approx 18.83 \, \mu A
$$

**(ii) Collector current $I_C$:**

Assume the transistor is in the **active region**. Then:

$$
I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 18.83 \, \mu A \approx 1.883 \, \text{mA}
$$

**(iii) Collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$:**

Apply KVL around the collector-emitter loop:

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = 12 - (1.883 \times 10^{-3})(2 \times 10^3) = 12 - 3.766 = 8.234 \, \text{V}
$$

**(iv) Region of operation:**

Since $V_{CE} = 8.234 \, \text{V} > V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2 \, \text{V}$, the transistor is in the **active region (forward-active region)**. The BJT operates as a **linear amplifier** in this state. The assumption made in step (ii) is therefore validated.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using the wrong current gain formula:** Students often confuse $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Remember: $\alpha$ is the **CB gain** ($I_C / I_E < 1$), and $\beta$ is the **CE gain** ($I_C / I_B \gg 1$). Marks are lost when $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are interchanged in derivations.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the KCL equation:** In numerical problems, some students compute only $I_C$ and $I_B$ but forget that $I_E = I_B + I_C$. This is a standard 1-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Not verifying the active-region assumption:** In part (b) of Q.A/B, students must verify that $V_{CE} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$. If this is not checked, the answer may be marked as "incomplete" even if the numerical value is correct.
>
> 4. **Using wrong units for $V_T$:** Some students use $V_T = 26 \, \text{mV}$ but substitute it incorrectly when computing $g_m$. The correct form is $g_m = I_C / V_T$ with both quantities in compatible units (e.g., mA / mV = S, or A / V = S).
>
> 5. **Confusing input and output characteristics:** Input characteristics plot $I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$; output characteristics plot $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$. Swapping the axes is a frequent error.
>
> 6. **Skipping the sketch of characteristic curves:** In a 7-mark question, merely describing the curves without a labeled sketch costs at least 1 to 2 marks. Always include a neatly labeled diagram.
>
> 7. **Missing the 180° phase inversion:** When asked about the "distinguishing feature" of the CE configuration, always mention the **180° phase reversal** between input and output. This is a high-frequency KTU question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Fire Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam:**

- [x] **CE Configuration:** Input at B-E, output at C-E, Emitter is common. Provides the **highest power gain** of all three configurations.
- [x] **Current Gain $\beta$:** Defined as $\beta = \Delta I_C \ / \ \Delta I_B$ at constant $V_{CE}$. Range: **20 to 500** for silicon BJTs.
- [x] **Current Gain $\alpha$:** Defined as $\alpha = I_C \ / \ I_E$, always **less than 1** (typically 0.95 to 0.99).
- [x] **Key Relationship:** $\beta = \alpha \ / \ (1 - \alpha)$ and $\alpha = \beta \ / \ (1 + \beta)$.
- [x] **Kirchhoff's Law:** $I_E = I_B + I_C$ (always, in all regions of operation).
- [x] **Active Region Equation:** $I_C = \beta I_B$ (valid only when the BJT is in forward-active mode).
- [x] **Input Characteristics:** Plot $I_B$ vs $V_{BE}$ at constant $V_{CE}$. Resembles a forward-biased diode. Cut-in voltage $\approx \mathbf{0.7 \, V}$ for silicon.
- [x] **Output Characteristics:** Plot $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ at constant $I_B$. Three regions — **Cutoff, Active, Saturation**.
- [x] **Active Region Biasing:** BE junction **forward-biased**, BC junction **reverse-biased**.
- [x] **Saturation Region Biasing:** Both junctions **forward-biased**. $V_{CE} < V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2 \, \text{V}$.
- [x] **Cutoff Region Biasing:** Both junctions **reverse-biased**. $I_B = 0$ and $I_C \approx I_{CEO}$.
- [x] **Phase Inversion:** CE amplifier produces **180° phase shift** between input and output. This is its **hallmark**.
- [x] **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T \approx 26 \, \text{mV}$ at 300 K. Used in $g_m = I_C / V_T$.
- [x] **Transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T \approx 38.46 \cdot I_C$ (S, with $I_C$ in amperes). Higher $I_C$ → higher $g_m$ → higher gain.
- [x] **Input Resistance:** $r_\pi = \beta / g_m = \beta V_T / I_C$. Increases as $I_C$ decreases.
- [x] **Early Effect:** Output characteristics have a **slight upward slope** in the active region due to base-width modulation, characterized by **Early voltage $V_A$**.
- [x] **Comparison Quick-Facts:**
  - **CE:** Highest power gain, 180° phase shift, moderate input/output impedance.
  - **CB:** High-frequency, low input impedance, no phase shift.
  - **CC (Emitter Follower):** Unity voltage gain, no phase shift, high input impedance, low output impedance — used as a **buffer**.
- [x] **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
  - Do not use $V_{BE} = 0$ (always use 0.7 V for Si or 0.3 V for Ge in active region).
  - Do not compute $\beta$ as $I_C / I_E$ — that would be $\alpha$.
  - Always state the **region of operation** after computing $V_{CE}$ in a numerical problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For a 7-mark BJT CE question, structure your answer as: (1) labeled circuit diagram, (2) input characteristics sketch with explanation, (3) output characteristics sketch with the three regions labeled, (4) the key formulas, and (5) a worked numerical example. This covers the entire marking scheme in a single coherent answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifier (Using Transistor): Class A, Class B, Class AB, Class C (Working, Characteristics, Power and Efficiency Calculation for Class A & B, Comparison of Power Amplifiers)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Amplifier (Using Transistor): Class A, Class B, Class AB, Class C (Working, Characteristics, Power and Efficiency Calculation for Class A & B, Comparison of Power Amplifiers)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Power Amplifiers: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal electronic amplifier designed to deliver appreciable power to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, or motor) with a high power-conversion efficiency, while maintaining acceptable levels of signal distortion. Unlike voltage amplifiers whose primary objective is to boost the signal voltage with high input impedance and low output impedance, power amplifiers are characterized by their ability to handle large voltage and current swings simultaneously.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PBMRT304 – Analog Electronics), power amplifiers are classified based on the **conduction angle (θ)** of the transistor, which is the portion of the input cycle during which the transistor remains in the active (ON) region. This conduction angle determines the class of operation — **Class A, Class B, Class AB, or Class C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):** Students are expected to understand the working principle, derive the efficiency expressions, draw the relevant waveforms, and compare all four classes of power amplifiers. Numerical problems frequently ask for maximum theoretical efficiency and output power.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a water pump (the **transistor**) that pushes water through a hose (the **load**) to fill a tank (the **output**). A **voltage amplifier** is like a pump that produces a strong water *pressure* (voltage) but a weak *flow* (current) — useful for signaling. A **power amplifier** is like a high-capacity pump that delivers both strong pressure and heavy flow — useful to actually do work, like spinning a turbine (the loudspeaker cone).

Now, the **class of operation** describes *when* the pump is allowed to push water:

- **Class A**: The pump runs continuously at half-throttle. It never stops. Excellent fidelity, but wastes enormous energy as heat.
- **Class B**: Two pumps work in shifts. Pump-1 pushes during the first half-cycle; Pump-2 pushes during the second half. Highly efficient because the pumps rest half the time, but there's a "dead zone" at the handover (crossover distortion).
- **Class AB**: A compromise — each pump idles a little during the other's shift to eliminate the dead zone. Almost as efficient as Class B with negligible distortion.
- **Class C**: The pump only pushes during less than half the cycle, in sharp bursts. Extremely efficient but produces a terribly distorted output — only useful when followed by a tuned resonant filter (e.g., in RF transmitters).

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Terminology

- **Conduction Angle (θ)**: Expressed in degrees or radians. $\theta = 360°$ for Class A, $\theta = 180°$ for Class B, $180° < \theta < 360°$ for Class AB, and $\theta < 180°$ for Class C.
- **Power Conversion Efficiency (η)**: Defined as $\eta = \dfrac{P_{AC(out)}}{P_{DC(in)}} \times 100\%$.
- **Figure of Merit**: The ideal theoretical maximum efficiency is **$\eta_{max}$**, and is **50%** for transformer-coupled Class A, and **$\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.6\%$** for Class B push-pull.
- **Harmonic Distortion (D)**: The ratio of harmonic power to fundamental power, expressed in dB or as a percentage.
- **Heat Sink Rating ($P_D$)**: The maximum continuous power the transistor can dissipate without exceeding its junction temperature, typically with the case at **25°C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Conduction Angle:**
> The conduction angle $\theta$ is the angular portion of the input signal cycle (in degrees or radians) during which the collector current of the transistor is non-zero. It is the single most important parameter that defines the class of a power amplifier and is the primary determinant of its efficiency and distortion characteristics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Are Power Amplifiers Classified? The Underlying Physics

The classification of power amplifiers arises from a fundamental engineering trade-off known as the **Efficiency–Linearity Trade-off**. The amount of time a transistor conducts (i.e., the conduction angle $\theta$) is directly linked to:

1. **Efficiency (η):** Longer rest periods → less DC power wasted as heat → higher efficiency.
2. **Linearity (Low Distortion):** Shorter rest periods → more faithful reproduction of the input signal → lower distortion.

The DC operating point (Q-point) of the transistor, set by the biasing network, physically determines $\theta$. By moving the Q-point closer to cutoff (below the threshold of conduction), the conduction angle shrinks and efficiency rises — but at the cost of introducing signal distortion.

## 2.2 Class-by-Class Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Class A Power Amplifier

**Biasing Condition:** The Q-point is set at the **exact center of the DC load line** so that the transistor conducts over the entire $360°$ of the input cycle.

**Operational Logic:**

- The transistor never turns OFF, regardless of the input signal amplitude.
- The collector current $i_C(t)$ is a faithful, undistorted sinusoid: $i_C(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin(\omega t)$.
- Maximum undistorted swing occurs when $I_m = I_{CQ}$, allowing the collector current to swing from **0 to $2I_{CQ}$** and the collector-emitter voltage to swing from **0 to $2V_{CC}$** (in transformer-coupled configuration).
- Two common topologies exist: **Series-Fed Class A** (low efficiency, $\eta_{max} = 25\%$) and **Transformer-Coupled Class A** (higher efficiency, $\eta_{max} = 50\%$).

**Characteristics:**

- Conduction angle: $\theta = 360°$
- Theoretical maximum efficiency: **50%** (transformer-coupled), **25%** (series-fed)
- Distortion: **Very Low** (highest fidelity)
- Power transistor utilization: **Poor** (always dissipating power)

### 2.2.2 Class B Power Amplifier
