**Biasing Condition:** The Q-point is set **exactly at cutoff** ($I_{CQ} = 0$, $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$). The transistor is biased to conduct only when the input signal exceeds the base-emitter threshold voltage ($V_{BE} \approx 0.7V$).

**Operational Logic:**

- Each transistor conducts for exactly **half** the input cycle ($180°$).
- A **push-pull configuration** uses two complementary transistors (NPN + PNP, or two NPNs with input transformer) — one handles the positive half-cycle, the other handles the negative half-cycle.
- The output is reconstructed at the load by combining both half-cycles.
- A critical drawback: **Crossover Distortion** occurs near the zero-crossing of the input because neither transistor conducts for input voltages between $-0.7V$ and $+0.7V$.

**Characteristics:**

- Conduction angle: $\theta = 180°$
- Theoretical maximum efficiency: **$\pi/4 \approx 78.6\%$**
- Distortion: **Moderate** (crossover distortion present)
- Power transistor utilization: **Good**

### 2.2.3 Class AB Power Amplifier

**Biasing Condition:** The Q-point is set **slightly above cutoff**, with a small quiescent collector current $I_{CQ} > 0$ (typically a few mA). This small bias is sufficient to keep both transistors in the active region during the zero-crossing of the input.

**Operational Logic:**

- Each transistor conducts for slightly **more than half** the cycle ($180° < \theta < 360°$).
- The small bias current eliminates the dead zone that causes crossover distortion in Class B.
- Efficiency is marginally lower than Class B but significantly higher than Class A.

**Characteristics:**

- Conduction angle: $180° < \theta < 360°$
- Theoretical maximum efficiency: Between **50% and 78.6%** (typically ~70%)
- Distortion: **Low** (crossover distortion eliminated)
- This is the **most widely used class** in audio amplifier applications (e.g., op-amp output stages, audio power amplifiers in Hi-Fi systems).

### 2.2.4 Class C Power Amplifier

**Biasing Condition:** The Q-point is set **below cutoff** ($V_{BQ} < V_{BE(on)}$). The transistor is reverse-biased at the base-emitter junction, ensuring it remains OFF for most of the input cycle.

**Operational Logic:**

- The transistor conducts for **less than half** the input cycle ($\theta < 180°$), producing narrow current pulses.
- The output is heavily distorted and **cannot be used directly** to drive a load.
- A **parallel resonant LC tank circuit** at the output filters the current pulses into a near-sinusoidal output voltage at the resonant frequency.
- Class C amplifiers are exclusively used in **RF (Radio Frequency) applications** such as transmitters, where the LC tank restores the sinusoidal shape.

**Characteristics:**

- Conduction angle: $\theta < 180°$
- Theoretical maximum efficiency: **> 78.6%**, can approach **90–100%** for small conduction angles
- Distortion: **Very High** (unusable without resonant filtering)
- Power transistor utilization: **Excellent**

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** In the table below, all instances of absolute value or magnitude use `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve valid markdown table syntax. Do NOT use the vertical pipe character `|` inside any cell.

| **Parameter** | **Class A (Series-Fed)** | **Class A (Transformer-Coupled)** | **Class B (Push-Pull)** | **Class C (Tuned)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction Angle $\theta$ | $360°$ | $360°$ | $180°$ | $\theta < 180°$ |
| Q-Point Location | Center of load line | Center of AC load line | At cutoff ($I_{CQ} = 0$) | Below cutoff |
| Max. AC Output Power $P_{out(max)}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{4 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | $\dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ (after filtering) |
| DC Input Power $P_{DC}$ | $2 V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $2 V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $\dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$ | Varies with $\theta$ |
| Max. Theoretical Efficiency $\eta_{max}$ | **25%** | **50%** | **$\pi/4 \approx 78.6\%$** | **> 78.6%** |
| Crossover Distortion | None | None | Present (severe) | Not applicable |
| Typical Application | Small-signal audio pre-amp | Audio output stage | Audio push-pull output | RF transmitters, IF stages |
| Harmonic Distortion | Very Low | Very Low | Moderate | Very High (unfiltered) |

**Universal Formulas (Applicable to All Classes):**

- **AC Output Power:** $P_{out} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L}{2} = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{2}$
- **DC Input Power:** $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(avg)}$
- **Power Conversion Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$
- **Power Dissipated by Transistor(s):** $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out}$
- **Maximum Power Dissipation Condition (Class A):** Occurs when $V_m = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$ (i.e., $\approx 63.7\%$ of $V_{CC}$)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Class A**: Used in high-fidelity audiophile equipment and precision instrumentation where signal purity is paramount and power consumption is not a constraint. Example: Input stages of oscilloscopes, pre-amplifiers in studio mixing consoles.
- **Class B**: Found in early transistor radio output stages and battery-powered devices where moderate fidelity is acceptable and battery life is critical.
- **Class AB**: The **dominant class** in commercial audio amplification — found in car stereos, home theater systems, public address (PA) systems, and the output stages of operational amplifiers. The LM386, TDA2003, and LM3886 are classic examples.
- **Class C**: Indispensable in **RF power amplification** for AM/FM transmitters, radio broadcasting equipment, and high-frequency communication systems. The resonant tank circuit simultaneously provides impedance matching and harmonic filtering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Efficiency of Transformer-Coupled Class A Power Amplifier

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup & Assumptions

Consider a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier with:

- Supply voltage: $V_{CC}$
- Load resistance (reflected to primary): $R_L$
- Quiescent collector current: $I_{CQ}$
- Quiescent collector-emitter voltage: $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ (transformer primary has zero DC resistance)
- Assume ideal transformer (no losses) and ideal transistor (no saturation voltage).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the collector current waveform.**

The collector current is a sinusoidal variation about the quiescent point:

$$i_C(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

For maximum undistorted operation, the amplitude $I_m$ cannot exceed $I_{CQ}$ (otherwise the transistor would cutoff). Therefore, at the **limit of maximum swing**, $I_m = I_{CQ}$, giving:

$$i_C(t) = I_{CQ} \left[ 1 + \sin(\omega t) \right]$$

**Step 2: Express the output voltage waveform.**

The output voltage across the load is also sinusoidal, swinging about the Q-point voltage:

$$v_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} - V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

For maximum swing, $V_m = V_{CC}$ (the collector voltage can swing down to 0V on one extreme and up to $2V_{CC}$ on the other, due to the back-EMF of the transformer).

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum AC output power.**

The AC power delivered to the load is the average of the instantaneous power over one cycle:

$$P_{out} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$$

(Using $V_m = V_{CC}$ and $I_m = I_{CQ}$ for the maximum case.)

**Step 4: Calculate the DC input power drawn from the supply.**

The DC power is the product of the supply voltage and the **average** collector current. Since $i_C(t)$ is a sinusoid centered at $I_{CQ}$, its average over a full cycle is simply $I_{CQ}$:

$$I_{C(avg)} = I_{CQ}$$

Therefore, the DC input power is:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(avg)} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the maximum power conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\% \text{ (Transformer-Coupled Class A)}}$$

**Step 6: Verify the power dissipation condition.**

The power dissipated by the transistor is:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{V_m I_m}{2}$$

For the general case where $V_m \neq V_{CC}$:

$$P_D = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L}$$

This is a **quadratic function** of $V_m$ that is maximized (using calculus: $\dfrac{dP_D}{dV_m} = 0$) when:

$$V_m = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \, V_{CC}$$

The maximum transistor dissipation in Class A is:

$$P_{D(max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} = 2 \times P_{out(max)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** The transistor in a Class A amplifier must be rated to handle at least **twice the maximum AC output power** as continuous dissipation. This is why Class A amplifiers require massive heat sinks.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Power Amplifier

### 3.2.1 Circuit Setup & Assumptions

Consider a complementary-symmetry Class B push-pull amplifier with:

- Supply voltage: $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ (dual supply) or $V_{CC}$ and ground (single supply with coupling capacitor)
- Load resistance: $R_L$
- Two transistors: $Q_1$ (NPN, handles positive half-cycle) and $Q_2$ (PNP, handles negative half-cycle)
- Q-point: Both transistors are biased exactly at cutoff ($I_{CQ} = 0$)
- Assume ideal transistors with zero saturation voltage ($V_{CE(sat)} = 0$).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the output voltage and current waveforms.**

The output voltage across the load is sinusoidal:

$$v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The corresponding load current is:

$$i_L(t) = \frac{V_m \sin(\omega t)}{R_L} = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

where $I_m = \dfrac{V_m}{R_L}$ is the peak load current.

**Step 2: Determine the current drawn from the supply by each transistor.**

Transistor $Q_1$ (NPN) conducts during the **positive half-cycle** ($0 \le \omega t \le \pi$):

$$i_{C1}(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for } 0 \le \omega t \le \pi$$

Transistor $Q_2$ (PNP) conducts during the **negative half-cycle** ($\pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$):

$$i_{C2}(t) = -I_m \sin(\omega t) \quad \text{for } \pi \le \omega t \le 2\pi$$

(Note: For the PNP transistor, the collector current is negative relative to the defined positive direction, but it draws current from the negative supply rail, which is equivalent in magnitude.)

**Step 3: Calculate the average current drawn from the supply.**

For transistor $Q_1$, the average collector current over the **full** $2\pi$ cycle is:

$$\bar{I}_{C1} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\bar{I}_{C1} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\omega t) \right]_{0}^{\pi} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(0) \right]$$

$$\bar{I}_{C1} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[ -(-1) + (1) \right] = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{I_m}{\pi}$$

By symmetry, the average current drawn from the negative supply by $Q_2$ is also $\dfrac{I_m}{\pi}$. Since the two supplies are typically of equal magnitude, the **total DC input power** is:

$$P_{DC} = 2 \cdot V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the AC output power delivered to the load.**

$$P_{out} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{I_m^2 R_L}{2}$$

**Step 5: Calculate the power conversion efficiency.**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\dfrac{V_m I_m}{2}}{\dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}} = \frac{\pi V_m}{4 V_{CC}}$$

$$\eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_{CC}}$$

**Step 6: Determine the maximum efficiency.**

For maximum efficiency, the output voltage swing must be as large as possible. In the ideal case, $V_m \to V_{CC}$ (the peak output voltage can approach the supply voltage, limited only by $V_{CE(sat)}$ which we have assumed to be zero):

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7857 \approx 78.6\% \text{ (Class B Push-Pull)}}$$

**Step 7: Calculate the maximum output power at peak efficiency.**

At maximum swing, $V_m = V_{CC}$ and $I_m = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L}$:

$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

The corresponding DC input power is:

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$$

The power dissipated by both transistors combined is:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L} \left( \frac{2}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L} \cdot \frac{4 - \pi}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** The Class B amplifier's maximum transistor dissipation does **not** occur at maximum output power. It occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi}$ and equals approximately $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$. Beyond this point, as $V_m$ increases toward $V_{CC}$, the transistor dissipation actually **decreases**. This makes Class B far more thermally robust than Class A.

## 3.3 Comparative Summary Matrix

| **Property** | **Class A** | **Class B** | **Class AB** | **Class C** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\theta$ | $360°$ | $180°$ | $180° < \theta < 360°$ | $\theta < 180°$ |
| $\eta_{max}$ | 25% / 50% | 78.6% | 50% – 78.6% | $\to$ 100% |
| Linearity | Excellent | Poor (crossover) | Very Good | Very Poor |
| Heat Dissipation | High | Low | Moderate | Very Low |
| Power Supply Current | Continuous | Pulsed (half-wave) | Mostly Pulsed | Narrow Pulses |
| Output Coupling | Transformer or Direct | Transformer or Capacitor | Transformer or Capacitor | Resonant LC Tank |
| Typical Use | Hi-Fi, Instrumentation | Radio, Low-power audio | Audio power amplifiers | RF transmitters |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved keyword conflicts. Labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric text with no embedded markdown formatting.

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Class A Transformer-Coupled Power Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    input1["INPUT SIGNAL VIN"] --> bias1["DC BIAS NETWORK SETS Q POINT AT CENTER OF LOAD LINE"]
    bias1 --> transistor1["TRANSISTOR Q1 CONDUCTS 360 DEGREES"]
    vcc1["DC SUPPLY VCC"] --> transistor1
    transistor1 --> transformer1["OUTPUT TRANSFORMER IMPEDANCE MATCHING"]
    transformer1 --> load1["LOAD RESISTANCE RL"]
    load1 --> output1["AC OUTPUT POWER POUT TO SPEAKER"]
    transistor1 --> heatsink1["HEAT SINK DISSIPATES PDC MINUS POUT"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Class B Push-Pull Power Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    input2["INPUT SIGNAL VIN"] --> splitter2["PHASE SPLITTER OR COMPLEMENTARY DRIVER"]
    splitter2 --> q1path["Q1 NPN CONDUCTS POSITIVE HALF CYCLE 0 TO PI"]
    splitter2 --> q2path["Q2 PNP CONDUCTS NEGATIVE HALF CYCLE PI TO 2PI"]
    vccpos["POSITIVE SUPPLY VCC"] --> q1path
    vccneg["NEGATIVE SUPPLY MINUS VCC"] --> q2path
    q1path --> junction2["OUTPUT JUNCTION COMBINES BOTH HALF CYCLES"]
    q2path --> junction2
    junction2 --> load2["LOAD RESISTANCE RL"]
    load2 --> output2["RECONSTRUCTED SINE WAVE OUTPUT POUT"]
    junction2 --> biascheck["CROSSOVER DISTORTION ZONE NEAR ZERO CROSSING"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Class AB Power Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    input3["INPUT SIGNAL VIN"] --> biasdiode3["DIODE BIAS NETWORK PROVIDES SMALL VBE OFFSET"]
    biasdiode3 --> q1ab["Q1 NPN CONDUCTS SLIGHTLY MORE THAN 180 DEGREES"]
    biasdiode3 --> q2ab["Q2 PNP CONDUCTS SLIGHTLY MORE THAN 180 DEGREES"]
    q1ab --> overlap3["OVERLAP REGION ELIMINATES CROSSOVER DISTORTION"]
    q2ab --> overlap3
    overlap3 --> load3["LOAD RESISTANCE RL"]
    load3 --> output3["HIGH FIDELITY AC OUTPUT"]
    biasdiode3 --> quiescent3["SMALL QUIESCENT CURRENT ICQ FEW MILLIAMPERES"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Class C RF Power Amplifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    input4["RF INPUT SIGNAL"] --> bias4["REVERSE BIAS KEEPS TRANSISTOR BELOW CUTOFF"]
    bias4 --> q4["TRANSISTOR Q CONDUCTS LESS THAN 180 DEGREES NARROW PULSES"]
    vcc4["DC SUPPLY VCC"] --> q4
    q4 --> lctank4["PARALLEL LC RESONANT TANK CIRCUIT"]
    lctank4 --> filter4["TANK FILTERS HARMONICS TO FUNDAMENTAL FREQUENCY"]
    filter4 --> antenna4["ANTENNA LOAD RL"]
    antenna4 --> output4["CLEAN RF SINE WAVE OUTPUT"]
    lctank4 --> impedance4["TANK ALSO PROVIDES IMPEDANCE MATCHING TO ANTENNA"]
```

## 4.5 Conduction Angle Comparison Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    classa["CLASS A THETA 360 DEGREES"] -->|MOVE Q POINT TOWARD CUTOFF| classb["CLASS B THETA 180 DEGREES"]
    classb -->|ADD SMALL FORWARD BIAS| classab["CLASS AB THETA BETWEEN 180 AND 360"]
    classb -->|REVERSE BIASE FURTHER| classc["CLASS C THETA LESS THAN 180 DEGREES"]
    classa -->|EFFICIENCY INCREASES| effnote["LINEARITY DECREASES AS EFFICIENCY INCREASES"]
    classb --> effnote
    classab --> effnote
    classc --> effnote
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the conduction angle of a power amplifier. How is it used to classify power amplifiers into different classes?**

**Model Answer:**

The **conduction angle ($\theta$)** is defined as the angular portion (in degrees or radians) of the complete input signal cycle during which the collector current of the transistor in a power amplifier is non-zero (i.e., the transistor is in the ON state).

**Classification based on $\theta$:**

- **Class A:** $\theta = 360°$ (transistor conducts for the full cycle)
- **Class B:** $\theta = 180°$ (transistor conducts for exactly half the cycle)
- **Class AB:** $180° < \theta < 360°$ (transistor conducts for more than half but less than the full cycle)
- **Class C:** $\theta < 180°$ (transistor conducts for less than half the cycle)

The conduction angle is the single most important parameter that determines the **efficiency**, **distortion**, and **application** of a power amplifier class. As $\theta$ decreases, efficiency increases but linearity worsens.

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is crossover distortion? In which class of power amplifier does it occur, and how can it be eliminated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Crossover distortion** is a type of non-linear distortion that appears in the output waveform of a power amplifier near the **zero-crossing** of the input signal. It manifests as a flat "notch" or dead zone in the output waveform as it transitions from positive to negative (or vice versa).

**Occurrence:** It occurs in **Class B push-pull power amplifiers** because the transistors are biased exactly at cutoff ($I_{CQ} = 0$). For input voltages in the range $-V_{BE(on)} \le v_{in} \le +V_{BE(on)}$ (approximately $\pm 0.7V$ for silicon transistors), neither transistor conducts, resulting in zero output.

**Elimination:** Crossover distortion is eliminated in **Class AB** amplifiers by providing a small forward bias ($V_{BB} \approx V_{BE(on)}$) to both transistors using a **diode bias network** or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier circuit. This ensures that both transistors are on the verge of conduction and immediately enter the active region as the input signal crosses zero, eliminating the dead zone.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. Draw the relevant waveforms and derive an expression for its maximum power conversion efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier operates from a 20V supply and drives a 100Ω load through an ideal transformer. Calculate: **(i)** the maximum AC output power, **(ii)** the DC input power at maximum output, **(iii)** the maximum efficiency, and **(iv)** the power dissipated by the transistor at maximum output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier uses a step-down (or step-up) output transformer to couple the amplified signal from the collector of the transistor to the load resistance $R_L$. The transformer serves two purposes: (1) DC isolation — it prevents the DC collector voltage from appearing across the load, and (2) Impedance matching — it transforms the speaker's low impedance (e.g., 8Ω) to the optimal collector load required for maximum power transfer.

**Working Principle:**

The transistor is biased by the base bias network such that the Q-point is at the **center of the DC load line** ($V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$, $I_{CQ}$ at midpoint). When an AC input signal $v_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied, the collector current varies sinusoidally about $I_{CQ}$:

$$i_C(t) = I_{CQ} + I_{CQ} \sin(\omega t) = I_{CQ}[1 + \sin(\omega t)]$$

This produces a sinusoidal voltage across the primary of the transformer, which is stepped down and delivered to the load as $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Waveforms** (standard sinusoidal input, output, and collector current centered on Q-point — to be drawn on answer script).

**Efficiency Derivation:**

$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}$$

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 20V$, $R_L = 100\Omega$ (reflected to primary), ideal transformer.

**(i) Maximum AC Output Power:**

At maximum swing, $V_m = V_{CC} = 20V$ and $I_m = \dfrac{V_m}{R_L} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.2A$

$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 100} = \frac{400}{200} = 2W$$

**(ii) DC Input Power at Maximum Output:**

The quiescent collector current must equal the peak AC current: $I_{CQ} = I_m = 0.2A$

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 20 \times 0.2 = 4W$$

**(iii) Maximum Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

**(iv) Power Dissipated by the Transistor at Maximum Output:**

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out(max)} = 4 - 2 = 2W$$

**Final Answers:** $P_{out(max)} = 2W$, $P_{DC} = 4W$, $\eta_{max} = 50\%$, $P_D = 2W$.

---

### Question B1 (Choice B): **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**a)** Explain the working of a Class B push-pull power amplifier with the help of a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for its maximum power conversion efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A Class B push-pull amplifier operates from $\pm 15V$ supplies and drives a 10Ω load. Calculate: **(i)** the maximum output power, **(ii)** the DC input power at maximum output, **(iii)** the maximum efficiency, and **(iv)** the power dissipated by both transistors at maximum output. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

A **Class B push-pull amplifier** uses two complementary transistors ($Q_1$: NPN, $Q_2$: PNP) connected in series between the positive ($+V_{CC}$) and negative ($-V_{CC}$) supply rails. The load $R_L$ is connected between the junction of the two collectors and ground. Both transistors are biased exactly at cutoff.

**Working Principle:**

- During the **positive half-cycle** of the input signal, $Q_1$ (NPN) turns ON and $Q_2$ (PNP) remains OFF. The current flows from $+V_{CC}$ through $Q_1$ to the load.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, $Q_2$ (PNP) turns ON and $Q_1$ (NPN) remains OFF. The current flows from ground through the load and into $Q_2$ toward $-V_{CC}$.
- The output is reconstructed as a full sine wave at the load.

**Efficiency Derivation:**

Average current drawn from each supply: $I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_m}{\pi}$

$$P_{DC} = 2 V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi}$$

$$P_{out} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} I_m}{2} \text{ (at } V_m = V_{CC}\text{)}$$

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.6\%$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 15V$, $R_L = 10\Omega$

**(i) Maximum Output Power:**

At maximum swing, $V_m = V_{CC} = 15V$:

$$P_{out(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{(15)^2}{2 \times 10} = \frac{225}{20} = 11.25W$$

**(ii) DC Input Power at Maximum Output:**

Peak load current: $I_m = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L} = \dfrac{15}{10} = 1.5A$

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_m}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 15 \times 1.5}{\pi} = \frac{45}{\pi} \approx 14.32W$$

**(iii) Maximum Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.7857 \approx 78.6\%$$

**(iv) Power Dissipated by Both Transistors at Maximum Output:**

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{out(max)} = 14.32 - 11.25 = 3.07W$$

**Alternative verification:**

$$P_D = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{R_L} \left( \frac{2}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{225}{10} \times (0.6366 - 0.5) = 22.5 \times 0.1366 = 3.07W$$

**Final Answers:** $P_{out(max)} = 11.25W$, $P_{DC} \approx 14.32W$, $\eta_{max} = 78.6\%$, $P_D \approx 3.07W$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the configuration:** Always state whether your Class A derivation is for *series-fed* ($\eta_{max} = 25\%$) or *transformer-coupled* ($\eta_{max} = 50\%$). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for ambiguity.
> 2. **Averaging error in Class B:** A very common mistake is using $I_{avg} = I_m / 2$ instead of the correct $I_{avg} = I_m / \pi$. Remember that each transistor conducts for only half the cycle, and the average must be computed over the **full** $2\pi$ cycle, not just the conduction half.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_{CE(sat)}$:** In practical problems, $V_{CE(sat)}$ is sometimes given (typically 0.2V or 1V). Students forget to subtract it from $V_{CC}$ to get the actual peak swing, leading to an inflated output power calculation. The correct peak swing is $V_m = V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}$.
> 4. **Confusing power dissipated by ONE transistor vs. BOTH:** In Class B push-pull, the question may ask for dissipation by one transistor (divide $P_D$ by 2) or both combined. Read the question carefully.
> 5. **Crossover distortion identification:** Many students incorrectly attribute crossover distortion to Class A. It is **exclusively** a Class B phenomenon.
> 6. **Class AB is NOT a separate amplifier topology:** It is a biasing variation of Class B. Do not draw a fundamentally different circuit — only the bias network differs.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1 Power Amplifiers**

- **Conduction Angle ($\theta$)** is THE defining parameter: Class A ($360°$), Class B ($180°$), Class AB ($180° < \theta < 360°$), Class C ($\theta < 180°$).
- **Efficiency–Linearity Trade-off:** As $\theta$ decreases, $\eta$ increases but distortion increases. This is the most fundamental concept in power amplifier design.
- **Class A $\eta_{max}$:** **25% (series-fed)** and **50% (transformer-coupled)**. Always specify the configuration in your answer.
- **Class B $\eta_{max}$:** **$\pi/4 \approx 78.6\%$** — derived by averaging the half-wave collector current over the full $2\pi$ cycle.
- **Class C $\eta_{max}$:** Can approach **100%** for very small $\theta$, but requires a **resonant LC tank** to reconstruct a sinusoidal output. Used ONLY in RF applications.
- **Crossover Distortion:** Occurs in Class B near the zero-crossing; eliminated by the small forward bias in Class AB using diode biasing or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier.
- **Maximum Power Dissipation in Class A** occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi} \approx 0.637 V_{CC}$, **not** at maximum output. The transistor must be rated for $P_{D(max)} = 2 \times P_{out(max)}$.
- **Maximum Power Dissipation in Class B** occurs at $V_m = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi}$, and is $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$. Above this, dissipation **decreases** as output increases — a key thermal advantage.
- **Practical $V_{CE(sat)}$:** When given, the true peak swing is $V_m = V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}$, not $V_m = V_{CC}$.
- **Formulas to memorize cold (KTU 2024 high-yield):**
  - $P_{out} = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2R_L}$
  - $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{C(avg)}$
  - $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$
  - Class A (transformer): $P_{out(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L}$, $\eta_{max} = 50\%$
  - Class B: $P_{out(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2R_L}$, $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.6\%$
- **Application Matching:** Class A → Hi-Fi; Class B → Early radios; Class AB → Modern audio (LM386, TDA series); Class C → RF transmitters.
- **Examiner Pet Topics:** Efficiency derivations (both Class A and Class B), crossover distortion explanation, comparison tables, and numerical problems involving $P_{out}$, $P_{DC}$, $\eta$, and $P_D$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FET: Construction and Characteristics of JFET & MOSFET - Enhancement & Depletion Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FET: Construction and Characteristics of JFET & MOSFET - Enhancement & Depletion Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Field Effect Transistor (FET)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** A **Field Effect Transistor (FET)** is a unipolar, voltage-controlled semiconductor device in which the current between the source and drain terminals is controlled by an electric field established by the voltage applied to the **gate** terminal. Unlike the bipolar junction transistor (BJT), the FET uses *only majority carriers* for conduction, hence the term **unipolar**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a water pipeline carrying water from a tank to your home.

- The **water flow** between source and drain is analogous to **drain current ($I_D$)**.
- The **gate terminal** acts like a **screw/valve knob** that squeezes the pipe. You don't let any water touch the knob; you just *squeeze* the pipe from the outside.
- The **voltage you apply to the gate ($V_{GS}$)** is the pressure you put on the knob. More pressure (more negative $V_{GS}$ for an n-channel JFET) means the pipe squeezes more, and less water flows.
- The key insight: **No current flows into the gate** — the control is purely by an electric field, just like the knob never gets wet!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board High-Yield Point:** In a FET, the input current at the gate is practically **zero (≈ 0)** because the gate is reverse-biased (JFET) or insulated (MOSFET). This gives the FET an extremely high input impedance (typically $10^9$ to $10^{15}\ \Omega$), which is its single biggest advantage over the BJT.

### 1.3 The Three FET Families

The FET family tree is divided into two major branches:

1. **Junction FET (JFET)** — Gate is a reverse-biased p-n junction.
2. **Metal Oxide Semiconductor FET (MOSFET)** — Gate is insulated by a thin layer of $SiO_2$ (oxide).
   - **Depletion-type MOSFET (D-MOSFET)** — Conduction channel exists at $V_{GS} = 0$.
   - **Enhancement-type MOSFET (E-MOSFET)** — No channel at $V_{GS} = 0$; channel must be *induced*.

### 1.4 Why FETs Matter in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The FET has an input resistance in the range of **$10^9$ to $10^{15}\ \Omega$** and a power consumption of roughly **$1\ mW$** in small-signal operation, making it the cornerstone of modern integrated circuits (ICs), CMOS logic, op-amp input stages, and analog switches.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 JFET — Construction and Operating Principle

**Construction (n-channel JFET):**
- A bar of **n-type** semiconductor (the channel) with two heavily-doped **p-regions** diffused on either side. These p-regions are tied together to form the **Gate (G)**.
- Ohmic contacts at the two ends of the n-bar form the **Source (S)** and **Drain (D)**.
- The channel is normally-on (conduction exists at $V_{GS} = 0$).

**Operating Principle:**
- A reverse-bias voltage $V_{GS} < 0$ widens the depletion regions around the two p-n junctions, pinching the conducting channel.
- At a critical voltage $V_{GS} = V_P$ (the **pinch-off voltage**), the channel is completely closed and $I_D = 0$.

### 2.2 JFET — Characteristic Equations (Shockley's Equation)

The drain current of a JFET is governed by **Shockley's Equation**:

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2$$

where:
- $I_{DSS}$ = Drain current with the gate shorted to the source ($V_{GS} = 0$)
- $V_P$ = Pinch-off voltage (negative for n-channel, positive for p-channel)
- $V_{GS}$ = Gate-to-source voltage

**Mutual Transconductance (Transconductance, $g_m$):**

$$g_m = g_{m0} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)$$

where $g_{m0}$ is the transconductance at $V_{GS} = 0$, given by:

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 \cdot I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$$

### 2.3 MOSFET — Depletion Type (D-MOSFET)

- An n-channel is physically diffused between source and drain at the time of fabrication.
- The gate is separated from the channel by a thin insulating layer of $SiO_2$.
- The device conducts at $V_{GS} = 0$ because the channel already exists.
- Applying a negative $V_{GS}$ **depletes** (reduces) the channel carriers — this is *depletion mode*.
- Applying a positive $V_{GS}$ **enhances** (increases) the channel carriers — this is *enhancement mode* in the same device.
- Threshold voltage is denoted as $V_{GS(th)}$.

### 2.4 MOSFET — Enhancement Type (E-MOSFET)

- **No physical channel** exists between source and drain at the time of fabrication.
- A positive gate voltage $V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$ **induces** an n-channel by repelling holes and attracting electrons toward the oxide interface.
- For $V_{GS} \leq V_{GS(th)}$ → $I_D = 0$ (device is OFF).
- This is the workhorse of digital CMOS logic.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Formula Master Table** — Memorize these for ESE 2024.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | JFET Drain Current (Shockley) | $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ | $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$ (n-ch) | A |
| 2 | JFET Cut-off | $I_D = 0$ | $V_{GS} \le V_P$ | A |
| 3 | JFET Saturation Drain Current | $I_D = I_{DSS}$ | $V_{GS} = 0$, $V_{DS} \ge \vert V_P \vert$ | A |
| 4 | Transconductance (JFET) | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | — | S (Siemens) |
| 5 | D-MOSFET Drain Current | $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_{GS(th)}}\right)^2$ | $V_{GS(th)}$ can be ± | A |
| 6 | E-MOSFET Drain Current | $I_D = k\left(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}\right)^2$ | $V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$ | A |
| 7 | Conduction Parameter | $k = \dfrac{I_{D(on)}}{\left(V_{GS(on)} - V_{GS(th)}\right)^2}$ | — | A/V² |
| 8 | Output Resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{\Delta V_{DS}}{\Delta I_D}\bigg\vert_{V_{GS}=const}$ | — | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Amplification Factor | $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ | — | dimensionless |
| 10 | Pinch-off Voltage Identity | $V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$ | n-channel JFET | V |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JFET inputs** in oscilloscopes, electrometers, and audio preamplifiers because of ultra-high $Z_{in}$.
- **D-MOSFETs** in RF amplifiers and constant-current sources.
- **E-MOSFETs** form the **CMOS inverter** — the fundamental building block of every micro-processor and digital IC.
- **Power MOSFETs** are used in switching power supplies, motor drivers, and Class-D audio amplifiers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shockley's Equation for JFET Drain Current

The JFET operates in the **saturation (pinch-off) region** when $V_{DS} \ge V_{GS} - V_P$. In this region, the drain current becomes nearly independent of $V_{DS}$ and is a strong function of $V_{GS}$.

**Step 1 — Define the channel conductance proportionality.**
The channel conductance is proportional to the cross-sectional area of the conducting channel, which in turn is proportional to the *available* channel width after depletion encroachment.

$$\text{Channel width available} \;\propto\; \left( 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Drain current is proportional to the available channel area.**
Since $I_D$ flows through the conducting channel, it is proportional to the available channel cross-section:

$$I_D \;\propto\; \left( 1 - \sqrt{\frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}} \right)$$

Wait — this is the *gradual channel* model. We will re-cast it in a more standard form.

**Step 3 — Use the boundary conditions to fix the proportionality constant.**
At $V_{GS} = 0$, the drain current is maximum and equals $I_{DSS}$. Substituting this into the proportionality:

$$I_{DSS} = K \cdot (1 - 0) \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = I_{DSS}$$

**Step 4 — Standard form using empirical square-law fit.**
The experimentally observed relation is the **square-law**:

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2$$

**Verification of boundary conditions:**
- At $V_{GS} = 0$: $I_D = I_{DSS} (1 - 0)^2 = I_{DSS}$ ✓
- At $V_{GS} = V_P$: $I_D = I_{DSS} (1 - 1)^2 = 0$ ✓ (Pinch-off)

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent in Shockley's equation is **2** (square law), not 1. This is because the channel conductance depends on the *cross-sectional area*, which in turn depends on the *width* of the depletion region, which is proportional to $\sqrt{V_{GS}}$. The square of the square-root recovers the linear dependence inside the parentheses.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mutual Transconductance $g_m$

The **transconductance** is defined as the rate of change of drain current with respect to gate-source voltage, with $V_{DS}$ held constant.

**Step 1 — Definition.**

$$g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS} = \text{const}}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate Shockley's equation with respect to $V_{GS}$.**

Starting from:

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2$$

Apply the chain rule $\dfrac{d}{dV_{GS}}\left[u^2\right] = 2u \cdot \dfrac{du}{dV_{GS}}$ where $u = \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$:

$$\frac{dI_D}{dV_{GS}} = I_{DSS} \cdot 2 \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right) \cdot \left( - \frac{1}{V_P} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$g_m = -\frac{2 \cdot I_{DSS}}{V_P} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Since $V_P$ is negative for an n-channel JFET, absorb the minus sign by writing $\vert V_P \vert$:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 \cdot I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the coefficient as $g_{m0}$:**

$$\boxed{\,g_m = g_{m0} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right), \quad \text{where} \quad g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}\,}$$

**Verification:** At $V_{GS} = 0$: $g_m = g_{m0}$. At $V_{GS} = V_P$: $g_m = 0$ (device cut off). ✓

### 3.3 Derivation: E-MOSFET Conduction Parameter $k$

The E-MOSFET drain current in saturation is given by:

$$I_D = k \left( V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2$$

The constant $k$ is found from a known bias point $(I_{D(on)}, V_{GS(on)})$ provided on the datasheet.

**Step 1 — Substitute the known operating point.**

$$I_{D(on)} = k \left( V_{GS(on)} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $k$.**

$$\boxed{\,k = \frac{I_{D(on)}}{\left( V_{GS(on)} - V_{GS(th)} \right)^2}\,}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Given: $I_{D(on)} = 10\ mA$ at $V_{GS(on)} = 8\ V$ and $V_{GS(th)} = 2\ V$ for an n-channel E-MOSFET.

$$k = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{(8 - 2)^2} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{36} = 2.778 \times 10^{-4}\ A/V^2$$

$$k = 277.8\ \mu A/V^2$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: n-Channel JFET Construction & Biasing

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Source Terminal S] --> chn((N-Channel Bar))
    drn[Drain Terminal D] --> chn
    gate[Gate Terminal G] --> pleft[P+ Region Left]
    gate --> pright[P+ Region Right]
    pleft -.Reverse Biased Depletion.-> chn
    pright -.Reverse Biased Depletion.-> chn
    vgs[VGS Voltage Source] --> gate
    vds[VDS Voltage Source] --> drn
    src --> gnd[Ground]
    chn --> id[ID Current Flow S to D]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Three Operating Regions of a JFET

```mermaid
graph LR
    a[Start: Apply VDS] --> b{VGS compared to VP}
    b -- VGS more negative than VP --> c[Cut-off Region: ID equals 0]
    b -- VP less than VGS less than 0 --> d[Ohmic/Triode Region: ID rises with VDS]
    d --> e{VDS reaches VGS minus VP}
    e -- Yes --> f[Saturation/Pinch-off Region: ID constant]
    e -- No --> d
    f --> g[Constant Current Source Behavior]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Functional Map: JFET vs D-MOSFET vs E-MOSFET

```mermaid
graph TD
    fet[Field Effect Transistor Family] --> jfet[Junction FET JFET]
    fet --> mosfet[MOSFET]
    jfet --> njfet[N-Channel JFET Conducts at VGS 0]
    jfet --> pjfet[P-Channel JFET Conducts at VGS 0]
    mosfet --> dmos[D-MOSFET Depletion Type]
    mosfet --> emos[E-MOSFET Enhancement Type]
    dmos --> dmos1[Physical channel exists at VGS 0]
    dmos --> dmos2[Works in both depletion and enhancement]
    emos --> emos1[No channel at VGS 0]
    emos --> emos2[Channel induced when VGS exceeds VGS th]
    emos --> emos3[Used in CMOS digital logic]
```

### 4.4 Transfer Characteristic Curve Map (n-Channel Devices)

```mermaid
graph TD
    axis[Coordinate System: VGS on X axis, ID on Y axis] --> jfcurve[JFET Transfer Curve]
    axis --> dmoscurve[D-MOSFET Transfer Curve]
    axis --> emoscurve[E-MOSFET Transfer Curve]
    jfcurve --> jf1[Parabola opening downward from IDSS at VGS 0 to 0 at VGS VP]
    dmoscurve --> dm1[Parabola with minimum at VGS off negative and maximum positive enhancement]
    emoscurve --> em1[ID is 0 for VGS less than VGS th]
    emoscurve --> em2[Parabola starts at VGS th and increases as square law]
```

### 4.5 Symbol Reference Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    s1[JFET Symbol] --> s2[Arrow on gate points inward for N-channel]
    s1 --> s3[Arrow on gate points outward for P-channel]
    s4[D-MOSFET Symbol] --> s5[Broken line channel indicates D-mode]
    s4 --> s6[Substrate arrow indicates type]
    s7[E-MOSFET Symbol] --> s8[Broken line channel separated by gap]
    s7 --> s9[No conduction until VGS exceeds threshold]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Memory Tip for KTU Boards:** A **continuous line** between Source and Drain = D-MOSFET or JFET (channel exists). A **broken/dashed line** with a gap = E-MOSFET (no physical channel until induced).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with reference to a JFET: (i) Pinch-off voltage $V_P$, (ii) Drain current at zero gate voltage $I_{DSS}$, (iii) Transconductance $g_m$.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Pinch-off voltage ($V_P$):** It is the value of the reverse gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ at which the drain current $I_D$ becomes zero. For an n-channel JFET, $V_P$ is negative.

(ii) **$I_{DSS}$:** It is the drain current that flows when the gate is short-circuited to the source ($V_{GS} = 0$) and $V_{DS} \ge \vert V_P \vert$. It is the maximum possible drain current of the device.

(iii) **Transconductance ($g_m$):** It is the ratio of the change in drain current $\Delta I_D$ to the change in gate-source voltage $\Delta V_{GS}$ that caused it, with $V_{DS}$ held constant. It has units of Siemens (S) or millisiemens (mS).

$$g_m = \left. \frac{\Delta I_D}{\Delta V_{GS}} \right|_{V_{DS}}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare Enhancement-type and Depletion-type MOSFETs in terms of (i) channel existence at $V_{GS} = 0$ and (ii) threshold voltage polarity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | D-MOSFET | E-MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| Channel at $V_{GS} = 0$ | Pre-existing physical channel conducts | No channel; $I_D = 0$ |
| Threshold voltage | Can be negative (n-ch) or positive | Must have correct polarity (positive for n-ch) to turn ON |
| Conduction at $V_{GS} = 0$ | Yes, $I_D = I_{DSS}$ | No, $I_D = 0$ |
| Operating regions | Depletion and enhancement | Only enhancement |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]****

**(a)** With a neat construction diagram, explain the working principle of an n-channel JFET. Define pinch-off voltage and drain current $I_{DSS}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The n-channel JFET consists of a bar of n-type silicon with two heavily-doped p-regions diffused on opposite sides. The two p-regions are internally connected to form the gate. Ohmic contacts at the two ends of the n-bar provide the source and drain terminals.

When a voltage $V_{DS}$ is applied (drain positive with respect to source) and $V_{GS} = 0$, a current $I_{DSS}$ flows from D to S through the n-channel. The p-n junctions are reverse-biased by the drain voltage, so depletion regions form around them and intrude into the channel.

**Pinch-off voltage ($V_P$):** It is the value of $V_{GS}$ (negative for n-channel) at which the depletion regions from both sides meet, choking the channel completely, and $I_D = 0$.

**$I_{DSS}$:** It is the drain-to-source current with the gate shorted to the source and the device in saturation.

**(b)** For an n-channel JFET, $I_{DSS} = 10\ mA$ and $V_P = -4\ V$. Calculate (i) $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = -2\ V$, (ii) $g_m$ at $V_{GS} = -2\ V$, and (iii) $g_{m0}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $I_{DSS} = 10\ mA = 10 \times 10^{-3}\ A$, $V_P = -4\ V$, $V_{GS} = -2\ V$.

**(i) Drain current using Shockley's equation:**

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right)^2$$

Substitute values:

$$I_D = 10\ mA \times \left( 1 - \frac{-2}{-4} \right)^2 = 10\ mA \times \left( 1 - 0.5 \right)^2$$

$$I_D = 10\ mA \times (0.5)^2 = 10\ mA \times 0.25 = 2.5\ mA$$

**(ii) Transconductance $g_{m0}$:**

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 \cdot I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ S = 5\ mS$$

**(iii) Transconductance $g_m$ at $V_{GS} = -2\ V$:**

$$g_m = g_{m0} \left( 1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P} \right) = 5\ mS \times \left( 1 - \frac{-2}{-4} \right) = 5\ mS \times 0.5 = 2.5\ mS$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]****

**(a)** Draw the constructional features of an n-channel Enhancement MOSFET and explain its principle of operation. Why is it called an enhancement device? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

An n-channel E-MOSFET is built on a p-type substrate. Two heavily-doped n+ regions (Source and Drain) are diffused into the substrate, separated by a region called the *channel region*. A thin insulating layer of $SiO_2$ is grown over the channel region, and a metallic gate electrode is deposited on top of the oxide.

**Operation:** When $V_{GS} = 0$, no conducting channel exists between S and D, so $I_D = 0$. When a positive voltage $V_{GS}$ is applied to the gate, it creates an electric field that repels holes from the p-substrate surface and attracts electrons. When $V_{GS}$ exceeds the threshold voltage $V_{GS(th)}$, a thin layer of electrons (an *inverted n-layer*) forms just below the oxide, creating an n-channel that connects S and D. As $V_{GS}$ increases further, this channel is **enhanced** — hence the name. The drain current is given by:

$$I_D = k (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2$$

**(b)** For an n-channel E-MOSFET, $V_{GS(th)} = 2\ V$, $I_{D(on)} = 8\ mA$ at $V_{GS(on)} = 6\ V$. Determine the conduction parameter $k$ and find $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = 5\ V$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the conduction parameter $k$:**

$$k = \frac{I_{D(on)}}{(V_{GS(on)} - V_{GS(th)})^2} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{(6 - 2)^2} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{16}$$

$$k = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\ A/V^2 = 0.5\ mA/V^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_D$ at $V_{GS} = 5\ V$:**

$$I_D = k (V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)})^2 = 0.5\ mA/V^2 \times (5 - 2)^2 = 0.5\ mA/V^2 \times 9$$

$$I_D = 4.5\ mA$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Shockley's Equation:** Students often plug $V_{GS} = -2\ V$ and $V_P = -4\ V$ into the equation *without* writing the ratio as a positive quantity. Always write $\dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}$ carefully: $\dfrac{-2}{-4} = +0.5$, and remember that the result must be **less than 1** for the device to conduct.
> 2. **Threshold Voltage Confusion:** For n-channel E-MOSFET, $V_{GS(th)}$ is **positive** (≈ 1–4 V). For p-channel, it is **negative**. Mixing up the sign will give a negative or undefined drain current.
> 3. **E-MOSFET at $V_{GS} = 0$:** Students incorrectly write $I_D = I_{DSS}$ for an E-MOSFET. Remember: $I_D = 0$ at $V_{GS} = 0$ for an E-MOSFET — it is *normally-off*.
> 4. **Forgetting the Square:** The exponent in Shockley's equation is **2**, not 1. KTU examiners award only 1 of 3 marks if you write a linear relation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FET is unipolar & voltage-controlled** — conduction is by *majority carriers only* (electrons in n-channel, holes in p-channel).
- **JFET** has a reverse-biased p-n gate; **MOSFET** has an insulated $SiO_2$ gate.
- **Shockley's Equation:** $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ — valid for JFET and D-MOSFET.
- **E-MOSFET equation:** $I_D = k\left(V_{GS} - V_{GS(th)}\right)^2$ — no conduction until $V_{GS} > V_{GS(th)}$.
- **$g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$** — a key formula tested every KTU cycle.
- **D-MOSFET** conducts at $V_{GS} = 0$ and can operate in both depletion and enhancement modes.
- **E-MOSFET** does not conduct at $V_{GS} = 0$ and is the building block of CMOS digital circuits.
- **Symbols:** Continuous channel line → D-mode; broken channel line with a gap → E-mode.
- **Input impedance:** JFET ≈ $10^9\ \Omega$; MOSFET ≈ $10^{15}\ \Omega$.
- **Pinch-off condition:** $V_{DS(sat)} = V_{GS} - V_P$ — beyond this, $I_D$ saturates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of BJT & FET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of BJT & FET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)**
A **BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)** is a three-terminal, current-controlled semiconductor device in which conduction is carried out by **both majority and minority charge carriers** (electrons and holes). It consists of two PN junctions formed by sandwiching either a P-type or N-type semiconductor between two layers of the opposite type, giving rise to NPN and PNP configurations. The three terminals are designated as **Emitter (E)**, **Base (B)**, and **Collector (C)**.

**FET (Field Effect Transistor)**
A **FET (Field Effect Transistor)** is a three-terminal, voltage-controlled semiconductor device in which conduction is performed solely by **majority charge carriers** (either electrons or holes, but not both). The current flow between the Source and Drain terminals is controlled by an electric field established by the voltage applied to the **Gate (G)** terminal. The other two terminals are the **Source (S)** and the **Drain (D)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:**
> BJT = **Bipolar** (two carrier types) + **Current-Controlled**.
> FET = **Unipolar** (one carrier type) + **Voltage-Controlled**.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two different kinds of **water faucets (taps)** controlling a flow pipeline:

1. **BJT as a "Pull/Push Lever Tap"**: Think of a tap that requires you to *physically push or pull a lever* to adjust water flow. The harder you push (more base current $I_B$), the more water flows out of the collector. The control parameter is the **force/pressure you apply** (analogous to current). Even a small amount of dirt or debris (thermal noise) can disturb its operation because it is highly sensitive to the input current.

2. **FET as a "Knob-Controlled Electronic Valve"**: Think of an automatic solenoid valve that is opened or closed by simply turning a *magnetic knob* (voltage). You don't need to push water *into* the control port — you only apply an electric field (voltage at the Gate). This makes the FET extremely **input-efficient**, requiring virtually no current to operate, much like a magnetic knob that turns effortlessly with no flow-back into the controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The BJT consumes a *measurable input current* to amplify, while the FET is controlled purely by an *electric field* with negligible input current. This single distinction governs almost every practical difference between them.

---

### 1.3 Symbol & Terminal Identification

**BJT Symbols:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NPN BJT:} \quad & \text{Arrow on Emitter points OUTWARD (Not Pointing iN)} \\
\text{PNP BJT:} \quad & \text{Arrow on Emitter points INWARD (Pointing iN Permanently)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**FET Symbols (JFET example):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{N-Channel JFET:} \quad & \text{Arrow on Source points INWARD} \\
\text{P-Channel JFET:} \quad & \text{Arrow on Source points OUTWARD}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 board exams, students are expected to draw the symbols from memory with correct arrow directions, terminal labels, and identify each as N-type or P-type. Marks are frequently lost for missing the arrow on the Emitter (BJT) or Source (FET).

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Thermal Voltage at room temperature (T = 300 K):** $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Electronic Charge:** $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration in Silicon at 300 K:** $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$
- **BJT Input Resistance Range:** $\mathbf{1\ k\Omega\ to\ 5\ k\Omega}$ (low)
- **FET Input Resistance Range:** $\mathbf{10^9\ \Omega\ to\ 10^{15}\ \Omega}$ (very high)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Construction — Why Are They Different?

| Structural Aspect | BJT | FET |
|---|---|---|
| Number of PN junctions | **Two** (EBJ and CBJ) | **One** (Gate-to-Channel junction) |
| Conduction Mechanism | Injection of minority carriers across forward-biased EBJ | Drift of majority carriers in a channel under electric field |
| Carrier Types | Both electrons & holes (hence *bipolar*) | Either electrons *or* holes (hence *unipolar*) |
| Control Variable | Base Current $I_B$ | Gate-Source Voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| Output Variable | Collector Current $I_C$ | Drain Current $I_D$ |
| Input Terminal Resistance | Low (forward-biased junction) | Very high (reverse-biased junction) |
| Physical Size | Larger, with thick base region | Smaller, can be densely packed |
| Thermal Stability | Poor (high sensitivity to temperature) | Excellent (negligible input current) |

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

**BJT Operation Logic:**
1. The **Emitter-Base Junction (EBJ)** is *forward biased* by $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ (silicon).
2. Minority carriers (electrons in NPN) are injected from Emitter into the thin Base.
3. A small fraction of these carriers recombine in the base, forming $I_B$.
4. The vast majority are swept into the Collector region by the reverse-biased **Collector-Base Junction (CBJ)**, forming $I_C$.
5. The fundamental relation is $I_C = \beta I_B$, where $\beta$ is the **common-emitter current gain**.

**FET Operation Logic:**
1. A voltage $V_{GS}$ is applied between Gate and Source, creating an electric field across the depletion region.
2. This field modulates the **width of the conducting channel** between Source and Drain.
3. As $V_{GS}$ becomes more negative (for N-channel JFET), the channel narrows, reducing $I_D$.
4. At **pinch-off voltage $V_P$**, the channel is fully depleted and $I_D \approx 0$.
5. The fundamental relation is **Shockley's Equation**: $I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the BJT is faster in switching:** The BJT has lower internal capacitances and minority carrier storage, allowing faster turn-off. The FET, while having very high input impedance, suffers from **Miller capacitance** issues that can slow it down at high frequencies — except for specific RF-optimized structures.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Relation | BJT Formula | FET Formula |
|---|---|---|
| Control variable | $I_B$ (Base current) | $V_{GS}$ (Gate-Source voltage) |
| Output current | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | $I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$ |
| Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | $g_m = \dfrac{2\,I_{DSS}}{\,\vert V_P\,\vert}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ |
| Input Resistance | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | $R_{in} \geq 10^{9}\ \Omega$ |
| Output Resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ (finite) | $r_d = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ (very high ideally) |
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 26\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K | Same definition applies |
| Voltage Gain (CS/CE) | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ (formally similar form) |
| Noise Figure | Higher (due to base current shot noise) | Lower (negligible input current noise) |
| Temperature Coefficient of $I_C$ / $I_D$ | Increases with temperature (runaway risk) | Decreases with temperature (self-stabilizing) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **BJT Applications:** Low-noise audio pre-amplifiers, current drivers, high-speed digital logic (TTL), analog integrated circuits, oscillators, and high-current switching regulators.
- **FET Applications:** Input stages of oscilloscopes, electrometers, RF amplifiers, analog switches, mixers, computer memory (MOSFET-based RAM/ROM), and high-impedance buffer amplifiers.
- **Modern CMOS technology** (the backbone of all microprocessors) uses complementary MOSFETs (a type of FET) due to their **near-zero static power consumption**, which the BJT simply cannot match.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The FET is the fundamental building block of every modern integrated circuit (IC). The BJT, while still used in precision analog circuits and power electronics, has largely been replaced by MOSFETs in digital ICs due to scalability and power efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of BJT Transconductance $g_m$

**Starting Definition:**
The transconductance is defined as the rate of change of output (collector) current with respect to input (base-emitter) voltage at constant collector-emitter voltage.

$$
g_m = \left.\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}\right|_{V_{CE}=\text{const.}}
$$

**Step 1:** The collector current follows the Ebers-Moll exponential law for the forward-active region:

$$
I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}
$$

where $I_S$ is the reverse saturation current of the CBJ (typically $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-15}$ A).

**Step 2:** Differentiate $I_C$ with respect to $V_{BE}$:

$$
\frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = I_S \cdot \frac{1}{V_T} \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the original expression for $I_C$ back:

$$
g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}
$$

**Step 4:** At room temperature ($T = 300$ K, $V_T \approx 26$ mV), for $I_C = 1$ mA:

$$
g_m = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{26} \approx 0.0385\ \text{S} = 38.5\ \text{mS}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{g_m^{\,\text{BJT}} = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{q\,I_C}{kT}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of JFET Transconductance $g_m$

**Starting Definition (Shockley's Square Law):**

$$
I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$ to find the transconductance:

$$
g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = I_{DSS} \cdot 2\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{V_P}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the negative sign:

$$
g_m = -\frac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $g_{m0} = -\dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P}$ is the **maximum transconductance** (at $V_{GS} = 0$):

$$
\boxed{g_m^{\,\text{FET}} = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify at the pinch-off point: When $V_{GS} = V_P$, then $g_m = 0$ (channel is pinched off), which is consistent with $I_D = 0$.

**Step 5:** Numerical example: Given $I_{DSS} = 10$ mA, $V_P = -4$ V, $V_{GS} = -1$ V:

$$
g_{m0} = -\frac{2(10\ \text{mA})}{-4\ \text{V}} = 5\ \text{mS}
$$

$$
g_m = 5\ \text{mS} \times \!\left(1 - \frac{-1}{-4}\right) = 5 \times 0.75 = 3.75\ \text{mS}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Side-by-Side

| S.No. | Parameter | BJT | FET |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Device type** | Bipolar, current-controlled | Unipolar, voltage-controlled |
| 2 | **Terminals** | Emitter, Base, Collector | Source, Gate, Drain |
| 3 | **PN junctions** | 2 (EBJ + CBJ) | 1 (Gate-Channel) |
| 4 | **Input resistance $R_{in}$** | Low (1 kΩ – 5 kΩ) | Very high (10⁹ Ω – 10¹⁵ Ω) |
| 5 | **Input control** | Base current $I_B$ | Gate voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| 6 | **Input current** | Non-zero (forward-biased) | Practically zero (reverse-biased) |
| 7 | **Output resistance** | Moderate (10 kΩ – 100 kΩ) | Very high (100 kΩ – 1 MΩ) |
| 8 | **Current gain** | $\beta = I_C/I_B$ (50 – 300) | $\mu = g_m \cdot r_d$ (no direct current gain) |
| 9 | **Voltage gain** | $A_v = -g_m R_C$ | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ |
| 10 | **Switching speed** | Fast (low storage time) | Slower (Miller capacitance) |
| 11 | **Noise** | Higher (shot noise in $I_B$) | Lower (no input current) |
| 12 | **Thermal stability** | Poor (runaway possible) | Good (negative temp. coefficient) |
| 13 | **Power consumption** | Higher (bias currents needed) | Lower (voltage-controlled) |
| 14 | **Fabrication in ICs** | Difficult (vertical structure) | Easy (planar structure — MOSFET) |
| 15 | **Cost & size** | Larger, more expensive | Smaller, cheaper (mass-produced) |
| 16 | **Biasing complexity** | Higher (requires $I_B$ source) | Simple (voltage divider for $V_{GS}$) |
| 17 | **Reverse breakdown voltage** | Lower (BV_CEO) | Higher |
| 18 | **Radiation hardness** | Lower | Higher (used in space electronics) |

---

### 3.4 Why the FET is Preferred in Modern ICs — Engineering Insight

The defining advantage is the **input resistance**. A high $R_{in}$ means:

- The FET draws negligible DC current from the previous stage → no loading effect.
- The FET is essentially an **ideal voltage amplifier input**.
- The FET is naturally suited to **CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor)** technology, where two complementary MOSFETs (NMOS + PMOS) draw zero static current in the steady state.

This is why microprocessors, DSPs, and memory chips all use MOSFETs. The BJT, despite its linearity and high transconductance, is now largely restricted to **precision analog, RF, and high-power** applications.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Comparison Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Input Signal Source] --> B[Control Signal Type Decision]
    B --> C{Is the signal current or voltage?}
    C -->|Current I_B| D[BJT Amplifier Stage]
    C -->|Voltage V_GS| E[FET Amplifier Stage]
    D --> F[Output: I_C = beta times I_B]
    E --> G[Output: I_D via Shockley Law]
    F --> H[Low Input Impedance Stage]
    G --> I[High Input Impedance Stage]
    H --> J[Application: Audio, Power, Switching]
    I --> K[Application: ICs, RF, Instrumentation]
    D --> L[Both Electrons and Holes Move]
    E --> M[Only Majority Carriers Move]
    L --> N[Bipolar Device]
    M --> O[Unipolar Device]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Biasing & Control Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BJT_Path
        S1[Signal Source V_sig] --> R1[Series Resistor]
        R1 --> B1[Base Terminal]
        B1 --> Q1[EBJ Forward Biased]
        Q1 --> E1[Emitter Grounded]
        Q1 --> C1[Collector Output]
        C1 --> RL1[Load Resistor R_C]
    end
    subgraph FET_Path
        S2[Signal Source V_sig] --> R2[Gate Bias Resistor]
        R2 --> G1[Gate Terminal]
        G1 --> J1[Reverse Biased PN Junction]
        J1 --> S2term[Source Terminal]
        J1 --> D1[Drain Output]
        D1 --> RL2[Load Resistor R_D]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Internal Construction Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BJT_Structure
        E2[Emitter Region Heavily Doped] --> B2[Base Region Thin and Lightly Doped]
        B2 --> C2[Collector Region Moderately Doped]
        B2 -.->|Minority Carrier Injection| E2
        C2 -.->|Carrier Collection| B2
    end
    subgraph FET_Structure
        S3[Source Terminal] --> CH[Conduction Channel]
        CH --> D3[Drain Terminal]
        G3[Gate Terminal] -->|Electric Field Controls| CH
        DR[Depletion Region] -->|Modulated by V_GS| CH
    end
```

### 4.4 Operational Flowchart — Choosing BJT vs FET

```mermaid
graph TB
    Q1[Need: Amplifier Design] --> Q2{What is the priority?}
    Q2 -->|High transconductance and linearity| Q3[Choose BJT]
    Q2 -->|High input impedance and low power| Q4[Choose FET/MOSFET]
    Q2 -->|High-frequency switching| Q5[Choose BJT for moderate freq]
    Q2 -->|Microwave RF operation| Q6[Choose GaAs FET or specialized RF BJT]
    Q3 --> R1[Use in: Audio preamps, Current drivers]
    Q4 --> R2[Use in: ICs, CMOS logic, Oscilloscope inputs]
    Q5 --> R3[Use in: TTL logic, Switching regulators]
    Q6 --> R4[Use in: Satellite comms, Radar systems]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams are block-level functional architecture flows. They are designed to clearly show the **conceptual flow** of input control → internal operation → output for each device, which is what KTU examiners expect when students are asked to "explain the operational difference" in a 7-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between BJT and FET on the basis of (i) control parameter, (ii) input impedance, and (iii) switching speed.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **(i) Control Parameter:** A BJT is a **current-controlled device** in which the output collector current $I_C$ is controlled by the input base current $I_B$ via the relation $I_C = \beta I_B$. A FET is a **voltage-controlled device** in which the output drain current $I_D$ is controlled by the input gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ via Shockley's equation $I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - V_{GS}/V_P\right)^{2}$.

2. **(ii) Input Impedance:** The BJT has a **low input impedance** of the order of 1 kΩ to 5 kΩ because the input junction is forward-biased. The FET has a **very high input impedance** of the order of $10^9$ to $10^{15}$ Ω because the gate-channel junction is reverse-biased.

3. **(iii) Switching Speed:** The BJT is **faster** in switching applications due to lower minority-carrier storage time. The FET is **slower** at high frequencies because of the Miller effect from the gate-drain capacitance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is the BJT called a bipolar device and the FET a unipolar device? Give one application where FET is preferred over BJT.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BJT — Bipolar:** The name "bipolar" comes from the fact that **both types of charge carriers (electrons and holes)** participate in the conduction process. For example, in an NPN BJT, electrons are injected from the emitter into the base, while holes are injected from the base into the emitter. Both carriers contribute to the current flow. **(1 mark)**

- **FET — Unipolar:** The FET is called "unipolar" because conduction is carried out by **only one type of charge carrier** — the majority carriers. In an N-channel JFET, only electrons conduct from source to drain; holes play no role. **(1 mark)**

- **Application where FET is preferred:** FETs are preferred as the **input stage of oscilloscopes and electrometers** because of their extremely high input impedance (≈ 10¹² Ω), which prevents loading of the signal source. Alternatively, MOSFETs are preferred in **digital ICs (CMOS logic)** because they consume almost zero static power. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Module 1 questions typically carry 14 marks with internal choice. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. Solutions must include neat diagrams, derivations, and comparison tables.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the construction and working principle of an N-channel JFET. Derive the expression for drain current using Shockley's equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Diagram & Construction (2 marks):**
- Draw the N-channel JFET structure: an N-type semiconductor bar with two P-type regions diffused on opposite sides, forming a PN junction. The top of the bar is the **Drain (D)**, the bottom is the **Source (S)**, and the two P-regions are connected to the **Gate (G)** terminal.
- Show the depletion regions around the two PN junctions, which intrude into the N-channel from both sides.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- When $V_{GS} = 0$ and a small $V_{DS}$ is applied, current flows from D to S through the N-channel.
- When a **reverse bias** $V_{GS} < 0$ is applied, the depletion region widens, narrowing the conducting channel.
- At $V_{GS} = V_P$ (pinch-off voltage), the channel is completely pinched off, and $I_D = 0$.
- For $V_{DS}$ beyond pinch-off, the drain current saturates and becomes nearly constant, controlled by $V_{GS}$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
- The empirical relationship between $I_D$ and $V_{GS}$ is given by Shockley's equation:

$$
I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}
$$

- This is derived by applying the gradual channel approximation and integrating the channel conductance over the channel length, accounting for the gate-voltage-dependent channel thickness. The square-law dependence arises from the parabolic potential distribution in the channel.

---

**(b)** Compare BJT and FET on any **seven** key parameters in a tabular form. Justify why FETs are preferred in CMOS digital ICs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Tabular Comparison (5 marks — 0.7 mark per row, deduct 0.5 for missing key):**

| S.No. | Parameter | BJT | FET |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Type | Bipolar, current-controlled | Unipolar, voltage-controlled |
| 2 | Input resistance | Low (1–5 kΩ) | Very high (10⁹–10¹⁵ Ω) |
| 3 | Output resistance | Moderate | High |
| 4 | Switching speed | Fast | Slower (Miller effect) |
| 5 | Thermal stability | Poor | Good |
| 6 | Noise | Higher | Lower |
| 7 | Power consumption | Higher | Negligible (CMOS) |
| 8 | IC fabrication | Difficult | Easy (planar MOSFET) |

**Justification for CMOS preference (2 marks):**
- In CMOS, an NMOS and a PMOS transistor are connected in series. In steady state, **one of them is always OFF**, drawing **virtually zero static current** from the supply.
- This makes CMOS logic gates consume power **only during switching transitions**, giving an extremely low static power dissipation — ideal for battery-operated and high-density VLSI circuits.
- The BJT-based TTL logic family, in contrast, draws a continuous supply current even in the static state, making it unsuitable for modern low-power applications.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction, symbol, and working of an NPN BJT in the **active region** with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship $I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Construction & Symbol (2 marks):**
- A BJT consists of three semiconductor regions: **Emitter (N+)**, **Base (P, thin)**, and **Collector (N)** sandwiched together.
- Draw the symbol: a vertical line for the base with two angled lines for collector and emitter; the arrow on the emitter points **outward** (for NPN).
- Label all three terminals and the two junctions (EBJ forward biased, CBJ reverse biased in active region).

**Working in Active Region (3 marks):**
- The **EBJ is forward biased** ($V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V) → electrons are injected from emitter into the thin base.
- The **CBJ is reverse biased** ($V_{CE} > 0.5$ V) → electrons arriving at the depletion edge are swept into the collector.
- A small fraction of electrons recombine with holes in the base, giving rise to $I_B$.
- The relation $I_E = I_B + I_C$ holds by KCL.

**Derivation of $I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}$ (2 marks):**
- The common-base current gain is $\alpha = I_C / I_E$. So $I_C = \alpha I_E = \alpha(I_B + I_C)$.
- Solving: $I_C(1 - \alpha) = \alpha I_B$ → $I_C = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} I_B = \beta I_B$.
- However, due to thermally generated carriers at the CBJ, a small leakage current $I_{CBO}$ flows even when $I_E = 0$. This adds a term to give:

$$
I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}
$$

where $I_{CEO} = (\beta + 1)\,I_{CBO}$ is the collector-emitter leakage current with base open.

---

**(b)** With the help of a table, list **seven advantages** of FETs over BJTs. Justify why BJT cannot be used as a high-impedance input stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Seven Advantages of FET (5 marks — 0.7 mark per row):**

| S.No. | Advantage of FET over BJT |
|---|---|
| 1 | Very high input impedance (10⁹ to 10¹⁵ Ω) |
| 2 | Voltage-controlled device — no continuous input current required |
| 3 | Lower power consumption (near-zero gate current) |
| 4 | Better thermal stability (negative temperature coefficient of $I_D$) |
| 5 | Lower noise figure (no shot noise from input current) |
| 6 | Smaller in size and cheaper in IC fabrication (planar MOSFET) |
| 7 | Radiation tolerant — preferred in space and military electronics |

**Why BJT cannot be used as a high-impedance input stage (2 marks):**
- The BJT's input is a forward-biased EBJ, which inherently has **low dynamic resistance** (typically 1–5 kΩ).
- A low input resistance means the BJT **loads down** the previous stage, drawing significant current and attenuating the input signal.
- This causes signal loss, distortion, and reduces the effective voltage gain of the overall amplifier. The FET's reverse-biased gate junction eliminates this loading effect, making it the natural choice for high-impedance applications.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the arrow direction** in symbols (BJT emitter vs. FET source). Examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for this in 7-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing the biasing of junctions:** A very common error is to say the EBJ is reverse-biased in the active region — it is **forward-biased**; only the CBJ is reverse-biased.
> 3. **Writing $I_C = \beta I_B$** without mentioning the leakage term $I_{CEO}$ — for 7-mark derivations, the complete expression $I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}$ is required.
> 4. **Memorizing the comparison without reasoning** — KTU 2024 examiners award partial credit only when *justifications* (not just statements) are given.
> 5. **Ignoring the role of Miller capacitance** in FET switching speed comparison — this is a frequently-asked sub-question worth 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Using $|$ in comparison tables** — this breaks the markdown table rendering. Always write $V_P$ as $V_P$ (not $\vert V_P \vert$ inside a table cell).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BJT = Bipolar Junction Transistor**, current-controlled, two PN junctions, low input impedance, faster switching.
- **FET = Field Effect Transistor**, voltage-controlled, one PN junction, very high input impedance, slower switching (Miller effect).
- **BJT input terminals** are Emitter, Base, Collector. **FET input terminals** are Source, Gate, Drain.
- **BJT carriers** are both electrons and holes. **FET carriers** are only majority carriers.
- **BJT current equation:** $I_C = \beta I_B + I_{CEO}$.
- **FET current equation (JFET):** $I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - V_{GS}/V_P\right)^{2}$.
- **BJT transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T \approx 38.5 I_C(\text{mA})\ \text{mS}$.
- **FET transconductance:** $g_m = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - V_{GS}/V_P\right)$.
- **Thermal voltage** at 300 K: $V_T = 26$ mV (must be remembered for all BJT numericals).
- **At pinch-off** ($V_{GS} = V_P$ for JFET), the drain current is zero.
- **FET is the heart of modern CMOS ICs** due to its near-zero static power consumption.
- **BJT is preferred in low-noise audio and high-power applications** due to its high $g_m$ and current-driving capability.
- **BJT switching** is faster; **FET switching** is slower because of gate-drain Miller capacitance.
- For KTU 2024 exams: **draw the symbol, mention arrow direction, give formulas, justify the comparison with a one-line engineering reason** — this ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UJT: Construction, Working and Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** UJT: Construction, Working and Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Unijunction Transistor (UJT)** is a three-terminal, single-junction, voltage-controlled solid-state semiconductor device that exhibits a stable **negative resistance characteristic** in its operating region. It consists of a lightly doped N-type silicon bar (the base) with a heavily doped P-type emitter alloyed into one side, closer to terminal **Base 2 (B2)**. The two ends of the bar form **Base 1 (B1)** and **Base 2 (B2)**, while the P-region is the **Emitter (E)** terminal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The UJT is classified as a *single-PN-junction, three-terminal device* and **NOT** a transistor in the conventional sense (BJT/FET), despite its misleading historical name. KTU examiners frequently test this conceptual distinction.

**Key Device Identifiers (Memorize for Exams):**
- **Original name:** Double-Base Diode (DBD)
- **Alternate trade name:** *Barristor*
- **Internal Resistance (Interbase Resistance, R_BB):** Typically **4 kΩ to 12 kΩ**
- **Standard JEDEC Nomenclature:** 2N2646, 2N2647, 2N3980 (μA742 family)
- **Symbol Identifier:** Single emitter arrow with a bent/curved base bar (NOT the same as BJT/FET symbols)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Pressure Analogy — Plain English Explanation**

Imagine a **horizontal water pipe** with two pressure gauges at each end (B1 and B2), and a small **side-injection valve** (the Emitter) near the right end. When you slightly open the side valve, water trickles in but the pipe resists the flow. Suddenly, at a critical pressure (the **Peak Point**), the pipe's internal state changes — the water surges through dramatically, and the pressure between the ends *drops* (this is the **Negative Resistance Region**). Eventually, the system stabilizes at a high-flow, low-pressure state (the **Valley Point**).

This sudden change is exactly what a UJT does electrically:
- **B1 ↔ B2** = the horizontal pipe (the N-type bar)
- **Emitter (E)** = the side-injection valve (P-type region)
- **Peak Point** = the critical triggering pressure
- **Negative Resistance** = the surprising "more current, less voltage" behavior
- **Valley Point** = the stabilization point
