For a B.Tech student, the UJT is essentially a **triggering device** — it stays "off" until a precise voltage threshold is reached, then it "fires" sharply. This makes it ideal for generating sharp pulses, firing TRIACs, and building relaxation oscillators.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Range / Value | KTU Exam Significance |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Intrinsic Stand-off Ratio | $\eta$ (eta) | **0.51 to 0.82** | ⭐ HIGH-YIELD |
| Interbase Resistance | $R_{BB}$ | **4 kΩ – 12 kΩ** | Critical for biasing |
| Peak Point Voltage | $V_P$ | Function of $V_{BB}, \eta$ | ⭐ HIGH-YIELD |
| Valley Point Voltage | $V_V$ | **1 V – 3 V** (typical) | Important for cutoff |
| Peak Point Current | $I_P$ | **~1 μA to 25 μA** | Very small (high impedance off-state) |
| Valley Point Current | $I_V$ | **~1 mA to 25 mA** | Much larger (low impedance on-state) |

---

### 1.4 Pinout Identification (Reference Diagram in Section 4)

The emitter is located **closer to B2** than to B1 (asymmetric placement), which is the geometric reason why $R_{B1} < R_{B2}$ when the emitter is unbiased.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Breakdown (Step-by-Step Logic)

The UJT is constructed through the following physical and electrical stages:

1. **Base Substrate:** A lightly doped **N-type silicon bar** of uniform cross-section forms the foundation. The bar has two ohmic contacts at its extremes — **B1 (Base 1)** and **B2 (Base 2)**.
2. **Emitter Junction:** A small, **heavily doped P-type region** is alloyed into the bar at a position physically closer to B2 (typically ~70% of the distance from B1).
3. **Single PN Junction:** This P-N alloy junction constitutes the *only* active junction in the device — hence the name *Unijunction*.
4. **Ohmic (Non-Rectifying) Base Contacts:** The B1 and B2 terminals are non-rectifying ohmic contacts, making the N-bar behave as a simple **voltage divider** when $V_{BB}$ is applied.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only ONE junction?** Unlike a BJT (which has two PN junctions: EB and CB), the UJT has a *single* PN junction between E and the N-bar. The two base terminals are just ohmic taps on the same bar.

---

### 2.2 Equivalent Circuit & The Intrinsic Stand-off Ratio

When analyzing the UJT, the N-bar is split into two internal resistances viewed from the emitter:

- $R_{B1}$ = Resistance of N-bar between **E** and **B1** (variable, depends on emitter current)
- $R_{B2}$ = Resistance of N-bar between **E** and **B2** (essentially constant)
- $R_{BB} = R_{B1} + R_{B2}$ (the total interbase resistance)

The **Intrinsic Stand-off Ratio ($\eta$)** is the most critical UJT parameter:

$$
\eta = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{B1} + R_{B2}} = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}}
$$

**Physical Meaning:** $\eta$ represents the *fraction of the interbase voltage that would appear at the emitter if the emitter were open-circuited*. It is a *geometric constant* for a given UJT (set at manufacturing), typically between **0.51 and 0.82**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The parameter is denoted by Greek letter **$\eta$ (eta)** OR **$\sigma$ (sigma)** in different textbooks. The 2N2646 standard datasheet uses $\eta$. Be consistent in your KTU answer scripts.

---

### 2.3 Working Principle — Qualitative Analysis

**Stage 1: Emitter Reverse-Biased (Cutoff Region)**
- When $V_E < V_P$, the emitter PN junction is reverse-biased.
- Only a tiny reverse leakage current flows from E into the bar.
- The UJT is in the **OFF state**, behaving like a high-impedance open switch.

**Stage 2: Peak Point Reached**
- As $V_E$ increases and reaches $V_P$, the emitter junction becomes forward-biased.
- Holes are injected from the P-emitter into the N-bar.
- These holes recombine with electrons, drawing more electrons from B1 to neutralize them.
- This **modulates the conductivity** of the $R_{B1}$ region — it *decreases*.

**Stage 3: Negative Resistance Region**
- As $R_{B1}$ decreases, the voltage at the emitter *also* decreases (because $V_E = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$ depends on the local potential).
- Result: $V_E$ drops while $I_E$ increases — a classic **negative differential resistance**.

**Stage 4: Valley Point & Saturation**
- At $I_E = I_V$, the device reaches its lowest voltage point $V_V$.
- Beyond this, $R_{B1}}$ saturates and the V-I curve behaves like a forward-biased diode.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 — Memorize All.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}}$ | Intrinsic stand-off ratio (geometric constant) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $V_{BB} = I_{B2} \cdot R_{BB}$ | Interbase voltage from supply | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $V_P = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_D$ | **Peak point voltage** (where UJT fires) | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $V_V \approx \eta \cdot V_{BB}$ | Approx. valley voltage (ignoring $V_D$) | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $R_{B1(\text{initial})} = \eta \cdot R_{BB}$ | Initial R_B1 when emitter is unbiased | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | $I_P = \dfrac{V_S - V_P}{R_E}$ | Peak current through external emitter resistor | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $T = R_E \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)$ | **Relaxation oscillator period** | Seconds (s) |
| 8 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Oscillation frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | $V_D \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \text{ V}$ | Equivalent emitter diode forward drop | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Symbol Reminder:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values in any table extension. Never use raw $\vert$ symbols in KTU answer sheets inside tables, as the markdown parser breaks.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Specific Use Case |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| **Power Electronics** | Triggering circuits for **TRIACs and SCRs** in AC phase-control circuits (lamp dimmers, heater controls) |
| **Timing Circuits** | **Relaxation oscillators** for generating sawtooth and pulse waveforms |
| **Oscillator Design** | Simple, low-cost **UJT-based oscillators** for industrial timing |
| **Thyristor Firing** | Generating synchronized **gate trigger pulses** for power converters |
| **Surge Protection** | Over-voltage crowbar circuits |
| **Educational Labs** | Demonstrating negative resistance in undergraduate electronics experiments |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Peak Point Voltage $V_P$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation #1** — This is asked almost every cycle. Master it completely.

**Given:** A UJT with intrinsic stand-off ratio $\eta$ is biased by an interbase supply $V_{BB}$ across B1-B2, and emitter supply $V_S$ through a series resistor $R_E$.

**Step 1 — Voltage Divider Analysis (Emitter Open-Circuited):**

When the emitter is open, the N-bar acts as a pure resistive voltage divider. The voltage at the point just below the emitter junction (let's call it point X) is:

$$
V_X = V_{B1} + I_{B2} \cdot R_{B1} = 0 + \frac{V_{BB}}{R_{BB}} \cdot R_{B1} = V_{BB} \cdot \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Definition of $\eta$:**

Since $\eta = R_{B1} / R_{BB}$, we substitute:

$$
V_X = \eta \cdot V_{BB}
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for Peak Point (Emitter Junction Just Forward-Biased):**

The emitter PN junction becomes forward-biased when the emitter voltage $V_E$ exceeds the local bar voltage $V_X$ by the diode drop $V_D$:

$$
V_E = V_X + V_D
$$

**Step 4 — Define Peak Point Voltage:**

At the peak point, $V_E = V_P$ (the critical firing voltage). Therefore:

$$
V_P = \eta \cdot V_{BB} + V_D
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $V_P$ is the minimum emitter voltage required to forward-bias the single PN junction. It is a **linear function of $V_{BB}$** with slope $\eta$ and offset $V_D$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Relaxation Oscillator Period

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Derivation #2** — Universally tested in Part B (14 marks).

**Circuit:** A capacitor $C$ charges through resistor $R_E$ from supply $V_S$. When $V_C = V_P$, the UJT fires and rapidly discharges the capacitor through $R_{B1}}$. Once $V_C$ falls to $V_V$, the UJT turns off and the cycle repeats.

**Step 1 — Charging Equation of RC Circuit:**

The capacitor charges from initial voltage $V_V$ toward $V_S$ with time constant $\tau = R_E C$:

$$
V_C(t) = V_S - (V_S - V_V) \cdot e^{-t / R_E C}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Condition at Peak Point:**

At time $t = T$ (the period), the capacitor voltage reaches $V_P$:

$$
V_P = V_S - (V_S - V_V) \cdot e^{-T / R_E C}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Exponential Term:**

Rearranging:

$$
V_S - V_P = (V_S - V_V) \cdot e^{-T / R_E C}
$$

$$
e^{-T / R_E C} = \frac{V_S - V_P}{V_S - V_V}
$$

**Step 4 — Take Natural Logarithm of Both Sides:**

$$
-\frac{T}{R_E C} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_S - V_P}{V_S - V_V}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{T}{R_E C} = \ln\!\left(\frac{V_S - V_V}{V_S - V_P}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Standard Approximation ($V_S \gg V_V$):**

Since $V_V$ is typically only 1-3 V and $V_S$ is usually 10-30 V, we can neglect $V_V$ in the numerator:

$$
T \approx R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_S}{V_S - V_P}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$ and Neglect $V_D$:**

For $V_S = V_{BB}$ (common configuration where single supply is used):

$$
T = R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_S}{V_S - \eta V_S}\right) = R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_S}{V_S(1 - \eta)}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Final Simplification:**

$$
T = R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{T = R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)}$

**Frequency:** $\boxed{f = \dfrac{1}{R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)}}$

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example: UJT Relaxation Oscillator Design

**Problem:** Design a UJT relaxation oscillator using a 2N2646 ($\eta = 0.65$) to generate a frequency of **1 kHz** from a $V_S = 20$ V supply. Choose $C = 0.1 \mu F$ and find $R_E$.

**Step 1 — Use the Frequency Formula:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Logarithm:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.65}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.35}\right) = \ln(2.857)
$$

$$
\ln(2.857) \approx 1.0498
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $R_E$:**

$$
R_E = \frac{1}{f \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)}
$$

$$
R_E = \frac{1}{1000 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.0498}
$$

$$
R_E = \frac{1}{1.0498 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
R_E \approx 9525 \;\Omega \approx 9.53 \;\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Peak Point Voltage:**

$$
V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D = 0.65 \times 20 + 0.6 = 13.6 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 5 — Check the Validity of Approximations:**

Since $V_S = 20$ V $\gg V_V \approx 1$ V, the approximation in Step 5 of the derivation is justified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $R_E \approx 9.53$ kΩ (use a standard 10 kΩ potentiometer for fine adjustment).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: UJT vs BJT vs FET

| Parameter | UJT | BJT | FET |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Number of PN Junctions | **1** | 2 | 0 (or 1 in JFET) |
| Control Terminal | Emitter (voltage) | Base (current) | Gate (voltage) |
| Input Impedance | Medium (~kΩ) | Low (~kΩ) | Very High (~MΩ) |
| Negative Resistance | **Yes** (key feature) | No | No |
| Primary Use | Triggering, Oscillators | Amplification, Switching | Amplification, Switching |
| Output Type | Pulse / Sawtooth | Analog / Digital | Analog / Digital |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 UJT Physical Construction (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph N_BAR["N-Type Silicon Bar (Lightly Doped)"]
        B1_TERMINAL["B1 - Base 1 Terminal (Ohmic Contact)"]
        RB1_REGION["Variable Resistance R_B1 Region (Modulated by Emitter Current)"]
        RB2_REGION["Constant Resistance R_B2 Region"]
        B2_TERMINAL["B2 - Base 2 Terminal (Ohmic Contact)"]
    end

    P_EMITTER["P-Type Emitter Region (Heavily Doped, Alloyed)"]
    E_TERMINAL["E - Emitter Terminal"]
    PN_JUNCTION["Single PN Junction (E to N-Bar)"]

    B1_TERMINAL --- RB1_REGION
    RB1_REGION --- PN_JUNCTION
    PN_JUNCTION --- RB2_REGION
    RB2_REGION --- B2_TERMINAL

    P_EMITTER --- PN_JUNCTION
    P_EMITTER --- E_TERMINAL

    style N_BAR fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px
    style P_EMITTER fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,stroke-width:2px
    style PN_JUNCTION fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:3px
    style RB1_REGION fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style RB2_REGION fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **green region** ($R_{B1}$) shrinks in size as the UJT fires — this is the visual metaphor for the modulation effect.
- The **red region** ($R_{B2}$) stays constant.
- The **yellow PN junction** is the *only* active junction in the device.

---

### 4.2 UJT Circuit Symbol & Equivalent Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYMBOL["UJT Circuit Symbol (Schematic Representation)"]
        E_SYM["E - Emitter (P-type)"]
        B1_SYM["B1 - Base 1"]
        B2_SYM["B2 - Base 2"]
        BAR_SYM["N-Type Base Bar (Slanted, with arrow)"]
        E_SYM --- BAR_SYM
        B1_SYM --- BAR_SYM
        B2_SYM --- BAR_SYM
    end

    subgraph EQUIV["Equivalent Circuit Model"]
        D_MODEL["D - Emitter Diode (Forward Drop V_D)"]
        RB1_VAR["R_B1 - Variable Resistor (Emitter to B1)"]
        RB2_FIX["R_B2 - Fixed Resistor (Emitter to B2)"]
        VBB_SRC["V_BB - Interbase Supply Source"]
        E_NODE["Node E (Emitter Terminal)"]
        B1_NODE["Node B1"]
        B2_NODE["Node B2"]

        E_NODE --- D_MODEL
        D_MODEL --- INT_X["Internal Node X"]
        INT_X --- RB1_VAR
        INT_X --- RB2_FIX
        RB1_VAR --- B1_NODE
        RB2_FIX --- B2_NODE
        VBB_SRC --- B1_NODE
        VBB_SRC --- B2_NODE
    end

    style SYMBOL fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px
    style EQUIV fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px
    style INT_X fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px
```

---

### 4.3 Static V-I Characteristics of UJT (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CUTOFF["Region 1: Cutoff (OFF State)"]
        REG1_DESC["V_E less than V_P: Reverse-biased junction. Only tiny reverse leakage current I_E flows (~ micro A). High impedance. UJT acts as OPEN switch."]
    end

    subgraph NEGRES["Region 2: Negative Resistance Region"]
        REG2_DESC["V_E between V_P and V_V: Conductivity modulation. As I_E increases, V_E DECREASES. Classic NDR behavior. UJT transitions from OFF to ON."]
    end

    subgraph SAT["Region 3: Saturation (ON State)"]
        REG3_DESC["V_E greater than V_V: R_B1 fully modulated, UJT behaves like forward-biased diode. Low voltage drop, high current flow. UJT acts as CLOSED switch."]
    end

    subgraph BREAK["Region 4: Breakdown"]
        REG4_DESC["V_E exceeds breakdown limit: Device damage. AVOID in design. Protected by external clamping."]
    end

    P1["Peak Point (V_P, I_P)"] --- CUTOFF
    P1 --- NEGRES
    P2["Valley Point (V_V, I_V)"] --- NEGRES
    P2 --- SAT
    P2 --- BREAK

    style CUTOFF fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style NEGRES fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:3px
    style SAT fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style BREAK fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4A148C
    style P1 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#FFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.4 UJT Relaxation Oscillator Circuit (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VSRC["V_S Supply (DC Source)"]
    RE_RES["R_E - Emitter Timing Resistor"]
    CAP_C["C - Timing Capacitor"]
    UJT_DEV["UJT Device (with η, R_BB)"]
    RB1_OUT["R_B1 - Output Resistor (Pulse Takeoff)"]
    OUT_PULSE["Output Pulse (Sharp Negative Spike)"]
    B2_GRND["B2 Terminal (Grounded)"]

    VSRC --- RE_RES
    RE_RES --- CAP_C
    CAP_C --- E_TERM["E (Emitter)"]
    E_TERM --- UJT_DEV
    UJT_DEV --- B1_TERM["B1 (Base 1)"]
    B1_TERM --- RB1_OUT
    RB1_OUT --- OUT_PULSE
    B2_GRND --- UJT_DEV

    style VSRC fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    style RE_RES fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style CAP_C fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C
    style UJT_DEV fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:3px
    style OUT_PULSE fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
```

**Operating Sequence:**
1. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R_E$ toward $V_S$.
2. When $V_C$ reaches $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$, the UJT fires (snap action).
3. Capacitor rapidly discharges through the UJT and $R_{B1}$.
4. When $V_C$ falls to $V_V$, the UJT turns off, and the cycle restarts.
5. A **sharp negative-going pulse** appears across $R_{B1}$ at each firing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1: Short-Answer Conceptual** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define the term "Intrinsic Stand-off Ratio" of a UJT. What is its typical range?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Intrinsic Stand-off Ratio ($\eta$)** is defined as the ratio of the resistance of the N-type bar between the emitter and Base 1 ($R_{B1}$) to the total interbase resistance ($R_{BB}$):

$$
\eta = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{B1} + R_{B2}} = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}}
$$

It is a **dimensionless, geometric constant** determined at the time of manufacturing. It represents the fraction of the interbase voltage that appears at the emitter junction when the emitter is open-circuited.

**Typical Range:** $\eta$ lies between **0.51 and 0.82** for commercial UJTs (e.g., 2N2646 has $\eta = 0.65$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Definition = 2 marks; Typical Range = 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A2: Short-Answer Definitional** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is a UJT called a "negative resistance" device? Identify the region in the V-I characteristics where this behavior is observed.**

**Model Answer:**

A UJT is termed a **negative resistance device** because, in a specific region of its static V-I characteristics, the **emitter voltage $V_E$ decreases as the emitter current $I_E$ increases**. The differential resistance $dV_E / dI_E$ is therefore **negative** in this region.

This behavior is observed in the region **between the Peak Point (V_P, I_P) and the Valley Point (V_V, I_V)**. Physically, this occurs because the injected carriers from the emitter modulate the conductivity of the $R_{B1}$ region, reducing its resistance and hence the local bar voltage, which in turn reduces the forward bias on the emitter junction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation:** Negative resistance definition = 1.5 marks; Region identification = 1 mark; Physical reason = 0.5 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternative questions provided (OR-style internal choice).

---

#### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working of a UJT**. Draw its **equivalent circuit** and derive the expression for **Peak Point Voltage ($V_P$)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A UJT has the following parameters: $R_{B1} = 4.5$ kΩ, $R_{B2} = 3.5$ kΩ, and $V_{BB} = 20$ V. Calculate:
1. Intrinsic stand-off ratio $\eta$
2. Peak point voltage $V_P$ (assume $V_D = 0.6$ V)
3. Approximate valley voltage $V_V$

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
Refer to Section 4.1 and 4.2 diagrams. The UJT consists of:
- A **lightly doped N-type silicon bar** with two ohmic contacts at the ends (B1 and B2).
- A **heavily doped P-type emitter** alloyed into the bar, located closer to B2.
- A **single PN junction** at the P-N interface.
- Total interbase resistance $R_{BB} = R_{B1} + R_{B2}$ typically 4-12 kΩ.

**Working (2 marks):**
- When $V_E < V_P$: Junction is reverse-biased, only leakage current flows (cutoff region).
- When $V_E = V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$: Junction becomes forward-biased, holes are injected.
- Hole injection modulates the conductivity of $R_{B1}$, reducing it.
- This creates the **negative resistance region** (V decreases as I increases).
- At $I_E = I_V$: Valley point reached; UJT saturates and behaves as a forward-biased diode.

**Equivalent Circuit (1 mark):**
Diode $D$ (with drop $V_D$) in series with $R_{B1}$ and $R_{B2}$ acting as a voltage divider, with $V_{BB}$ across B1-B2.

**Derivation of $V_P$ (1 mark):**
As derived in Section 3.1:
$$
V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D
$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate $\eta$:**

$$
R_{BB} = R_{B1} + R_{B2} = 4.5 \;\text{k}\Omega + 3.5 \;\text{k}\Omega = 8 \;\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{R_{B1}}{R_{BB}} = \frac{4.5}{8} = 0.5625
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $V_P$:**

$$
V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D = 0.5625 \times 20 + 0.6 = 11.25 + 0.6 = 11.85 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Approximate $V_V$:**

$$
V_V \approx \eta V_{BB} = 0.5625 \times 20 = 11.25 \;\text{V}
$$

*(Note: This is the upper-bound approximation; the actual $V_V$ is typically 1-3 V and depends on the device's I-V characteristics at high $I_E$. For KTU numerical problems, use this formula unless specified otherwise.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:** $\eta = 0.5625$, $V_P = 11.85$ V, $V_V \approx 11.25$ V.

---

#### **Question B2 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **static V-I characteristics of a UJT** and label all critical points and regions. Explain the operation in each region. Define **peak point**, **valley point**, and **negative resistance region**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a **UJT relaxation oscillator** using a 2N2646 UJT ($\eta = 0.65$, $R_{BB} = 7$ kΩ, $V_D = 0.6$ V) to generate **sawtooth waveforms at 2 kHz** from a $V_S = 18$ V supply. Choose $C = 0.05 \mu F$ and find the value of $R_E$. Also calculate the peak point voltage $V_P$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**V-I Characteristics Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 block diagram. Plot $V_E$ on the X-axis and $I_E$ on the Y-axis with the characteristic S-shaped curve.

**Critical Points (3 marks):**
- **Peak Point (P):** Coordinates $(V_P, I_P)$. Maximum voltage, minimum current in the active region. $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$.
- **Valley Point (V):** Coordinates $(V_V, I_V)$. Minimum voltage in active region, current $I_V$. Marks end of negative resistance.
- **Negative Resistance Region:** The segment **P to V** on the curve, where $dV_E/dI_E < 0$.

**Regions (2 marks):**
- **Cutoff Region (below $I_P$):** UJT is OFF; junction reverse-biased.
- **Negative Resistance Region (P to V):** UJT is transitioning; conductivity modulation active.
- **Saturation Region (beyond V):** UJT is ON; behaves as a low-impedance forward-biased diode.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the Logarithmic Term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - 0.65}\right) = \ln(2.857) \approx 1.0498
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $R_E$ Using the Frequency Formula:**

$$
f = \frac{1}{R_E C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)}
$$

Solving for $R_E$:

$$
R_E = \frac{1}{f \cdot C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - \eta}\right)}
$$

$$
R_E = \frac{1}{2000 \cdot 0.05 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.0498}
$$

$$
R_E = \frac{1}{2000 \cdot 0.05 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.0498} = \frac{1}{1.0498 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
R_E \approx 9525 \;\Omega \approx 9.53 \;\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $V_P$:**

$$
V_P = \eta V_S + V_D = 0.65 \times 18 + 0.6 = 11.7 + 0.6 = 12.3 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Practical Design Note:**

In practice, $R_E$ is implemented using a **fixed resistor in series with a potentiometer** (e.g., 5 kΩ + 10 kΩ pot) to allow fine frequency adjustment. Also, $R_{B1}$ should be chosen such that the load line intersects the V-I curve in the negative resistance region for proper oscillator action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer Summary:** $R_E \approx 9.53$ kΩ, $V_P = 12.3$ V.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — KTU Examiner's Pitfall List:**
> 1. **Formula Confusion (–2 marks):** Students frequently write $V_P = \eta V_{BB}$ **without the $V_D$ term**. The diode drop is essential and adds **0.5 to 0.7 V**. Examiners WILL deduct marks.
> 2. **Misidentifying the Symbol (–1 mark):** Calling the UJT a "transistor" without qualification loses marks. Always clarify: *"It is a single-junction device, not a BJT/FET."*
> 3. **Wrong Region for Negative Resistance (–1 mark):** Students often say *"the UJT has negative resistance"* without specifying the **P-to-V segment** of the curve. Always state the region explicitly.
> 4. **Unit Errors in Numerical Problems (–1 mark):** Watch out for $\mu$F vs nF vs pF in capacitor values. Convert before substituting.
> 5. **Skipping the Approximation Justification:** When deriving the relaxation oscillator period, you must explicitly state that $V_S \gg V_V$ to justify the simplification. Examiners look for this logical step.
> 6. **Drawing $R_{B1}$ and $R_{B2}$ Equal:** In the equivalent circuit, $R_{B1}}$ must be shown as **variable** (modulated) and $R_{B2}$ as **fixed**. Equal resistance depiction is a common error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — UJT Module 1**

- ✅ **UJT = Single PN Junction + 3 terminals** (E, B1, B2). It is **NOT a transistor** in the conventional amplifier sense.
- ✅ **Intrinsic Stand-off Ratio:** $\eta = R_{B1} / R_{BB}$, range **0.51 to 0.82**, dimensionless.
- ✅ **Peak Voltage:** $V_P = \eta V_{BB} + V_D$ — the firing threshold voltage.
- ✅ **Valley Point:** $V_V \approx 1-3$ V (typical), $I_V \approx 1-25$ mA (typical).
- ✅ **Negative Resistance Region:** Between P and V on the V-I curve. Here, **$V_E$ decreases as $I_E$ increases**.
- ✅ **Conductivity Modulation:** The *physical cause* of negative resistance — hole injection reduces $R_{B1}$.
- ✅ **Relaxation Oscillator Period:** $T = R_E C \cdot \ln(1/(1-\eta))$ — memorize the exact form, not a rough approximation.
- ✅ **Output Pulse:** Sharp **negative-going spike** across $R_{B1}$ at each firing event.
- ✅ **Interbase Resistance:** $R_{BB} = R_{B1} + R_{B2} \approx 4-12$ kΩ; acts as a voltage divider.
- ✅ **Standard Device:** 2N2646 ($\eta = 0.65$, $R_{BB} \approx 7$ kΩ) is the most common KTU-lab exemplar.
- ✅ **Primary Use Case:** Triggering SCRs/TRIACs in **phase-control** AC power circuits.
- ✅ **Cutoff vs Saturation:** Below $V_P$ = OFF (open switch); beyond $V_V$ = ON (closed switch).
- ✅ **Diode Drop $V_D$:** Always include in $V_P$ formula unless the problem explicitly says "neglect $V_D$."
- ✅ **Equivalent Circuit Symbols:** Diode $D$ + variable $R_{B1}$ + fixed $R_{B2}$ + $V_{BB}$ source.
- ✅ **Asymmetric Construction:** Emitter is closer to B2, so initially $R_{B1} > R_{B2}}$ when emitter is unbiased — *correction*: actually $R_{B1} < R_{B2}$ *before* modulation when measured from E, but during operation $R_{B1}$ is the one that varies.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — UJT Notes.** Proceed to the next topic as per your KTU 2024 syllabus sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Overview of Online & Offline UPS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Online & Offline UPS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** is a power conditioning device that provides **continuous, regulated, and instantaneous backup power** to a connected load when the primary mains supply fails, sags, surges, or exhibits frequency distortion. It achieves this by rectifying AC mains into DC, storing energy in a battery bank, and inverting it back to a clean AC waveform through an internal inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> A UPS is an electrical apparatus that provides **emergency power** to a load when the input power source or mains power fails. The on-battery runtime of most uninterruptible power sources is relatively short (a few minutes) but sufficient to start a standby power source or properly shut down protected equipment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine the municipal water supply to your home. Normally, water flows directly from the city pipeline. Now, picture this:

- **The City Pipeline** = Mains AC supply
- **The Overhead Water Tank on your roof** = Battery + Charger (stores energy)
- **The Motor Pump that fills the tank** = The Rectifier/Charger
- **The Tap water reaching your kitchen** = The Inverter-fed output
- **A sudden pipeline cut** = Mains failure

> **Analogy 1 — Offline UPS (Standby / Passive):** The tap is directly connected to the pipeline. The instant the pipeline cuts, an automatic valve switches the tap to the rooftop tank. There is a tiny delay (the **transfer time**) before water starts flowing from the tank. You might not even notice the gap.

> **Analogy 2 — Online UPS (Double Conversion / Active):** The tap is *never* connected to the pipeline directly. Water is always pumped from the pipeline into the tank, and then from the tank to the tap. The tap only ever sees tank water. The moment the pipeline fails, the tank (battery) keeps supplying water without any switchover gap — **zero transfer time**.

This is the heart of the difference: **Offline waits and switches**; **Online always conditions**.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry-Recognized Values)

The following parameters are universally used by KTU examiners to evaluate UPS quality:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Transfer Time** | $T_t$ | **< 4–10 ms (Offline)** / **0 ms (Online)** |
| **Efficiency (Online, full load)** | $\eta_{on}$ | **85% – 95%** |
| **Efficiency (Offline, full load)** | $\eta_{off}$ | **95% – 99%** |
| **Output THD** | $THD_{out}$ | **< 3% (Online)** / **3–5% (Offline)** |
| **Battery Backup Time** | $T_b$ | **5 – 30 minutes** (typical) |
| **Input Power Factor** | $PF_{in}$ | **0.9 – 0.99** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must be able to draw and explain the **block diagram** of both Online and Offline UPS and clearly state the difference in **transfer time**, **efficiency**, and **output waveform purity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of UPS Topologies

KTU recognizes three principal UPS architectures:

1. **Offline UPS** (also called *Standby* or *Passive* UPS)
2. **Line-Interactive UPS** (a hybrid variant)
3. **Online UPS** (also called *Double-Conversion* or *True* UPS)

### 2.2 Offline UPS — Operating Principle (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Normal Mode (Mains Healthy):**
The **Static Transfer Switch (STS)** directly connects the mains to the load. A small trickle charger keeps the battery fully charged. The inverter is in **standby (idle)** mode. Efficiency is very high (≈ **98%**) because no double conversion is happening.

**Step 2 — Mains Fails / Sags / Surges:**
The sensing circuitry detects the abnormality (typically when $V_{in} < 0.9\,V_{nom}$ or $V_{in} > 1.1\,V_{nom}$, or frequency deviates beyond ±3 Hz).

**Step 3 — Transfer to Battery Mode:**
The STS disconnects mains and the inverter is switched ON. Battery DC is converted to AC and fed to the load.

**Step 4 — Mains Restores:**
After a stabilization delay (to avoid repeated transfers during transient mains fluctuations), the load is transferred back to mains and the inverter returns to standby.

> **The "Why":** Offline UPS is simple, cheap, and efficient. But the **transfer time** (typically **2–10 ms**) means sensitive loads (computers, PLCs) may reset.

### 2.3 Online UPS — Operating Principle (Double Conversion)

**Step 1 — Normal Mode (Mains Healthy, But NEVER bypassed to load):**
- Mains → **Rectifier** → DC bus
- DC bus → **Inverter** → Load
- DC bus → **Charger** keeps battery full

**Step 2 — Mains Fails:**
- Rectifier stops drawing from mains
- Battery seamlessly supplies the DC bus
- **Inverter continues to feed the load with ZERO interruption**
- The battery discharge is so smooth that the load never knows mains failed.

**Step 3 — Mains Returns:**
- Rectifier resumes, battery recharges gradually
- Load continues to be fed by the inverter throughout

**Step 4 — Inverter Failure / Overload (Bypass Mode):**
- A **mechanical/static bypass switch** connects mains directly to load to prevent total blackout while the inverter is serviced.

> **The "Why":** Online UPS provides the **cleanest possible power** with **zero transfer time** but consumes more energy (≈ 90% efficient) because power is converted twice (AC→DC→AC).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and form the core numerical toolkit for KTU Board Exam questions on UPS systems.

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $T_b = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{bat}}{P_{load}} \times \eta_{inv}$ | Battery backup time | minutes (or hours) |
| $T_b = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} \times 60$ | Backup time in minutes | minutes |
| $P_{bat} = V_{bat} \times I_{bat}$ | DC power drawn from battery | Watts |
| $\eta_{total} = \eta_{rect} \times \eta_{inv}$ | Online UPS overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | % |
| $C_{req} = \dfrac{P_{load} \times T_b}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv}}$ | Required battery capacity | Ah |
| $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Transformer step ratio (auxiliary) | V |

> **Where:**
> - $C_{Ah}$ = Battery capacity in Ampere-hours
> - $V_{bat}$ = Battery nominal voltage (e.g., 12 V, 24 V, 48 V)
> - $P_{load}$ = Connected AC load in Watts
> - $\eta_{inv}$ = Inverter efficiency (typically 0.85–0.95)
> - $V_n$ = RMS voltage of the $n^{th}$ harmonic
> - $V_1$ = RMS voltage of the fundamental

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospitals**: Online UPS for ICUs, ventilators, and operation theatres — zero transfer time is non-negotiable.
- **Data Centers**: Online UPS for servers — any transfer glitch causes data corruption.
- **Homes / Small Offices**: Offline UPS for PCs, Wi-Fi routers, CCTV — cost-effective.
- **Industrial PLCs & CNC Machines**: Line-Interactive UPS — handles voltage sags well, cheaper than online.
- **Telecom Towers**: Online UPS with extended battery banks — grid power is unreliable in remote sites.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Battery Backup Time

**Problem Setup:** A 600 VA offline UPS has a 12 V, 7 Ah battery and feeds a 300 W load. The inverter efficiency is 90%. Compute the backup time in minutes.

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation.**

The energy stored in the battery is $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \times C_{Ah}$ in Watt-hours.

The energy delivered to the load (after inverter losses) is $E_{load} = \dfrac{P_{load}}{\eta_{inv}}$.

The backup time in hours is the ratio of these two:

$$T_b = \dfrac{V_{bat} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} \quad \text{(in hours)}$$

**Step 2 — Convert hours to minutes.**

$$T_b = \dfrac{V_{bat} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} \times 60 \quad \text{(in minutes)}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$T_b = \dfrac{12 \times 7 \times 0.90}{300} \times 60$$

$$T_b = \dfrac{75.6}{300} \times 60$$

$$T_b = 0.252 \times 60$$

$$\boxed{T_b = 15.12 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation.**

> The battery stores 84 Wh of energy (12 V × 7 Ah). After inverter losses (10%), only 75.6 Wh reaches the 300 W load, sustaining it for about **15 minutes** — long enough to save work and shut down safely.

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Battery Capacity for a Given Backup

**Problem Setup:** A 1 kVA online UPS uses a 48 V battery bank. The load is 800 W, inverter efficiency is 92%, and required backup is 10 minutes. Find the required battery capacity.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the backup time formula to solve for $C_{Ah}$.**

From the master equation:

$$T_b = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times 60}{P_{load}}$$

Solving for capacity:

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{P_{load} \times T_b}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times 60}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{800 \times 10}{48 \times 0.92 \times 60}$$

$$C_{Ah} = \dfrac{8000}{2649.6}$$

$$\boxed{C_{Ah} \approx 3.02 \text{ Ah}}$$

> A standard **48 V / 4 Ah** battery bank would be selected, with a safety margin.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Online vs Offline vs Line-Interactive)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-asked comparison table** in KTU Board Exams. Memorize the structure, not the absolute numbers.

| Parameter | Offline UPS | Line-Interactive UPS | Online UPS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Power Flow (Normal)** | Mains → Load (direct) | Mains → Load (via AVR tap) | Mains → Rectifier → Inverter → Load |
| **Conversion Stages** | Single (only on battery) | Single (only on battery) | **Double (always)** |
| **Transfer Time** | **4 – 10 ms** | **2 – 4 ms** | **0 ms** |
| **Output Voltage Regulation** | Poor (≈ ±10%) | Good (AVR ±5%) | Excellent (±1–2%) |
| **Efficiency @ Full Load** | **95 – 99%** | **93 – 97%** | **85 – 95%** |
| **Output THD** | High (3–5%) | Medium (2–3%) | **Low (< 3%)** |
| **Cost** | **Low** | Medium | **High** |
| **Heat Generation** | Low | Low–Medium | **High** (always working) |
| **Battery Usage** | Only during outage | Only during outage | Continuous trickle charge |
| **Best Suited For** | Home PCs, routers | Small offices, AV systems | **Servers, medical, data centers** |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Online UPS Efficiency

**Problem Setup:** An online UPS has a rectifier efficiency of 93% and an inverter efficiency of 95%. Compute the total efficiency.

**Step 1 — Apply the multiplicative efficiency law.**

$$\eta_{total} = \eta_{rect} \times \eta_{inv}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\eta_{total} = 0.93 \times 0.95 = 0.8835$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{total} \approx 88.35\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** For every 1000 W drawn from the mains in an online UPS, only ~883 W reaches the load — the rest is dissipated as heat in the rectifier and inverter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024, students are expected to draw **functional block diagrams** of both Online and Offline UPS. Since physical circuit drawings are difficult in Mermaid, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flows** capture all the critical signal/power paths and switching actions.

### 4.1 Offline UPS — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MAINS AC SUPPLY] -->|Normal: Direct Path| B[STATIC TRANSFER SWITCH STS]
    B -->|Closed in Normal Mode| C[AC LOAD COMPUTER CCTV]
    A -->|Trickle Charge| D[RECTIFIER CHARGER]
    D -->|Charging Current| E[DC BATTERY BANK 12V]
    E -.->|Battery Mode Only| F[DC TO AC INVERTER]
    F -.->|AC Output on Mains Failure| B
    B -->|Switched to Inverter on Outage| C
    G[SUPERVISORY CONTROL MCU] -->|Senses Voltage Frequency| A
    G -->|Sends Switch Command| B
    G -->|Triggers Inverter| F
    H[BYPASS SWITCH MANUAL] -.->|Maintenance Bypass| C
    styleA fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleE fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleF fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**How to read this diagram (for exam answer):**
- **Solid arrows** = active path during normal (mains healthy) operation.
- **Dotted arrows** = active path only during mains failure (battery mode).
- The **MCU** is the brain that detects mains failure and commands the STS to switch.
- The **battery** is idle during normal operation — it only discharges when called upon.

### 4.2 Online UPS — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Double Conversion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MAINS AC SUPPLY] -->|Step 1 AC to DC| B[RECTIFIER]
    B -->|DC Bus Always Live| C[DC LINK CAPACITOR BUS]
    C -->|Charges in Normal| D[DC BATTERY BANK 48V]
    C -->|Step 2 DC to AC| E[PWM INVERTER IGBT]
    E -->|Filtered Sine Output| F[LC OUTPUT FILTER]
    F -->|Clean AC| G[CRITICAL LOAD SERVER]
    C -.->|DC Bus Maintained| E
    D -.->|Seamless DC Supply on Mains Fail| C
    H[STATIC BYPASS SWITCH] -.->|Inverter Fault or Overload| G
    A -.->|Only on Bypass Mode| H
    I[CONTROL LOGIC DSP FPGA] -->|PWM Gate Pulses| E
    I -->|Charging Control| B
    styleA fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleB fill:#ff9f43,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleD fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleE fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleF fill:#a29bfe,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**How to read this diagram (for exam answer):**
- The **load NEVER directly sees the mains** in an online UPS — every electron passes through the rectifier-then-inverter chain.
- On mains failure, the **battery silently takes over the DC bus** — the inverter keeps running without any switching action at the AC side. This is why **transfer time = 0 ms**.
- The **static bypass** is a safety route used only when the inverter itself fails or during overloads.

### 4.3 Comparative Decision Flow — Which UPS to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Load Criticality Assessment] --> Q1{Is zero transfer time mandatory?}
    Q1 -->|YES| ONL[Choose ONLINE UPS]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{Budget constrained?}
    Q2 -->|YES| OFF[Choose OFFLINE UPS]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Need voltage regulation in normal mode?}
    Q3 -->|YES| LI[Choose LINE INTERACTIVE UPS]
    Q3 -->|NO| OFF
    ONL --> OUT1[Servers Hospitals Data Centers]
    LI --> OUT2[Small Offices AV Systems]
    OFF --> OUT3[Home PC Router CCTV]
    styleONL fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleLI fill:#ffd700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    styleOFF fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define UPS. List any two applications of UPS systems.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** is an electrical device that provides **emergency power** to a load when the main supply fails, by using a battery bank and an inverter.

**Two applications:**
1. Power backup for **personal computers and servers** in offices to prevent data loss.
2. **Hospital equipment** (ventilators, monitors) requiring zero interruption of supply.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for two distinct applications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Online and Offline UPS in terms of transfer time.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Offline UPS | Online UPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Transfer Time** | **4 to 10 milliseconds** (load sees a brief gap) | **0 milliseconds** (no gap, double conversion always active) |

In an **Offline UPS**, the load is normally fed directly from mains; the inverter kicks in only after the mains fails, causing a small but measurable transfer delay. In an **Online UPS**, the load is **always** fed through the rectifier-inverter chain, so the battery seamlessly takes over without any switching at the AC output.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for Offline value, 1 mark for Online value, 1 mark for the underlying reason.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: Each Module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [Choice: Online UPS Focus]

**(a) Draw the block diagram of an Online UPS and explain the operation in both normal and battery modes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Mains AC → [RECTIFIER] → DC Bus → [INVERTER] → [LC FILTER] → Load
                 ↓            ↑
              [CHARGER]    [BATTERY]
```

**Normal Mode Operation (Mains Healthy):**
- The **rectifier** converts the incoming AC mains into regulated DC.
- This DC is split into two paths:
  1. To the **DC bus**, which feeds the **PWM inverter**.
  2. To the **battery charger**, which maintains the battery at full charge.
- The **inverter** converts the DC back into a clean, sinusoidal AC output.
- The **LC filter** smooths the PWM waveform into a pure sine wave.
- The load receives **galvanically isolated, regulated, and clean** AC power — independent of any mains disturbance.

**Battery Mode Operation (Mains Failed):**
- The rectifier stops drawing current from mains.
- The **battery seamlessly feeds the DC bus** — no switching occurs at the AC side.
- The inverter continues to run on battery DC, and the load sees **uninterrupted, clean AC**.
- When mains returns, the rectifier resumes and the battery is gradually recharged.

> **The defining feature: there is NO transfer time because the load is permanently connected to the inverter output.**

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- 2 marks → Block diagram (labeled correctly)
- 2 marks → Normal mode explanation
- 2 marks → Battery mode explanation
- 1 mark → Key feature (zero transfer time) stated

---

**(b) An online UPS has a 48 V battery bank with 10 Ah capacity and feeds a 600 W load. The inverter efficiency is 90%. Calculate the backup time in minutes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
- $V_{bat} = 48$ V
- $C_{Ah} = 10$ Ah
- $P_{load} = 600$ W
- $\eta_{inv} = 0.90$

**Step 2 — Write the backup time formula.**

$$T_b = \dfrac{V_{bat} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} \times 60 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$T_b = \dfrac{48 \times 10 \times 0.90}{600} \times 60$$

$$T_b = \dfrac{432}{600} \times 60$$

$$T_b = 0.72 \times 60$$

$$\boxed{T_b = 43.2 \text{ minutes}}$$

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- 1 mark → Data extraction
- 2 marks → Correct formula
- 2 marks → Substitution
- 1 mark → Final numerical answer
- 1 mark → Unit and physical interpretation

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [Choice: Offline UPS Focus]

**(a) Draw the block diagram of an Offline UPS and explain its working in normal and battery modes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Mains AC ─┬─→ [STS / Transfer Switch] ─→ AC Load
          │         ↑
          │    [INVERTER] (OFF in normal mode)
          │         ↑
          └── [CHARGER] ─→ [BATTERY]
```

**Normal Mode Operation (Mains Healthy):**
- The **Static Transfer Switch (STS)** is in position 1, directly connecting mains to the load.
- The inverter is in **standby (off)** — consuming negligible power.
- The **battery charger** trickle-charges the battery continuously.
- **Efficiency is very high (~98%)** because no AC-DC-AC conversion occurs.

**Battery Mode Operation (Mains Failed):**
- The **MCU detects the outage** (voltage drop or frequency deviation).
- The STS switches to position 2, disconnecting mains and connecting the inverter output to the load.
- The inverter turns ON, draws DC from the battery, and produces AC for the load.
- **Transfer time** is the small interval between the outage and the inverter assuming the load — typically **4 to 10 ms**.
- When mains returns, the STS transfers back, and the inverter returns to standby.

> **Key disadvantage:** the transfer time may cause sensitive digital equipment to reset.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- 2 marks → Block diagram
- 2 marks → Normal mode
- 2 marks → Battery mode
- 1 mark → Transfer time mention

---

**(b) Compare Online and Offline UPS based on: transfer time, efficiency, output waveform quality, and cost. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Online UPS | Offline UPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Transfer Time** | **0 ms** — load always on inverter | **4–10 ms** — switchover delay |
| **Efficiency** | **85–95%** — double conversion loss | **95–99%** — direct mains feed in normal mode |
| **Output Waveform** | **Pure sine, < 3% THD** — regenerated AC | **Mains-shaped, 3–5% THD** — passed through |
| **Cost** | **High** — rectifier, inverter, battery, control all active | **Low** — only charger and standby inverter |

**Justification:**
- Online UPS performs **double conversion always**, hence lower efficiency but perfect output.
- Offline UPS bypasses the conversion chain in normal mode, hence high efficiency but transfer gap.
- **Online is preferred for critical loads** (servers, ICUs); **Offline is preferred for home/office desktops** where cost outweighs the need for zero transfer time.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- 2 marks → Transfer time + efficiency
- 2 marks → Output waveform
- 2 marks → Cost + application preference
- 1 mark → Concluding justification

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the static bypass switch with the static transfer switch** — they are different elements. Bypass is for *inverter failure* in Online UPS; STS is for *mains-to-battery switchover* in Offline UPS.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — writing "43.2" without "minutes" costs a mark.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula** — students often use $T_b = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{bat}}{P_{load}}$ and forget to multiply by $\eta_{inv}$ and 60. The official KTU-recommended form is the one given in Section 2.4.
> 4. **Not labeling the block diagram** — every block must be named. An unlabeled box = 0 marks for that block.
> 5. **Stating efficiency as a single number** — always give the **range** (e.g., "85–95%") to be safe.
> 6. **Missing the "double conversion" phrase** in Online UPS answers — this is the **keyword** examiners look for.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- **UPS** = Uninterruptible Power Supply → bridges mains failure to safe shutdown / generator start.
- **Offline UPS** = *Standby* UPS. Load is on mains directly. Transfer time = **4–10 ms**. Efficiency = **95–99%**. Cheap. Used for PCs, routers.
- **Online UPS** = *Double-Conversion* UPS. Load is **always** on inverter. Transfer time = **0 ms**. Efficiency = **85–95%**. Expensive. Used for servers, ICUs.
- **Line-Interactive UPS** = Middle-ground. Has an **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** tap changer. Transfer time = **2–4 ms**.
- **Block diagram of Offline UPS** → Mains → STS → Load; Battery → Inverter → STS; Charger keeps battery full.
- **Block diagram of Online UPS** → Mains → Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter → LC Filter → Load; Battery on DC bus; Static bypass for faults.
- **Backup time formula** → $T_b = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv}}{P_{load}} \times 60$ minutes.
- **Required capacity formula** → $C_{Ah} = \dfrac{P_{load} \times T_b}{V_{bat} \times \eta_{inv} \times 60}$.
- **Online efficiency** = $\eta_{rect} \times \eta_{inv}$ (multiplicative — two stages).
- **THD** of online output = **< 3%** (pure sine). Offline output tracks mains THD.
- **Static Transfer Switch (STS)** is the key differentiator between Offline and Online UPS.
- **Bypass switch** is a maintenance feature — never the primary power path in Online UPS.
- **Battery bank voltage** is typically **12 V (small UPS) / 24 V / 48 V (larger systems)**.
- **Zero transfer time** is possible **only** in Online UPS — this is the single most important KTU exam keyword.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SMPS Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** SMPS Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Switched Mode Power Supply (SMPS)** is an electronic power supply unit that incorporates a **switching regulator** to convert electrical power with high efficiency. Instead of dissipating excess power as heat (as in a linear regulator), the SMPS rapidly switches a power transistor between saturation (ON) and cutoff (OFF) states at a high switching frequency, typically in the range of **20 kHz to 1 MHz**, and uses energy storage elements (inductors and capacitors) along with a high-frequency transformer to deliver a regulated DC output voltage.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PBMRT304 (Analog Electronics), the SMPS is categorized as a high-frequency power conversion topology, distinct from the conventional series-pass linear regulator.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Pulse-Width Tap":**
> Imagine filling a bucket with water from a tap. A *linear regulator* is like leaving the tap half-open continuously — water flows slowly, but a lot is wasted as splashing and heat. An *SMPS* is like fully opening the tap for a brief moment and then completely closing it, repeating this very fast. By controlling **how long** the tap stays open (the duty cycle), you can precisely fill the bucket to the exact level you want, with almost no waste. The faster you pulse, the smoother the result. This is exactly how an SMPS regulates voltage — it rapidly pulses current and filters it to a smooth DC level.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants & Standards)

- **Switching Frequency** ($f_{sw}$): Typically **20 kHz to 1 MHz** (above audible range to avoid noise).
- **Efficiency** ($\eta$): Typically **70% to 95%** (linear regulators: 30%–50%).
- **Line Regulation**: 0.1% to 0.5% (excellent).
- **Load Regulation**: 0.1% to 1% (excellent).
- **Ripple Voltage**: **50 mV to 100 mV peak-to-peak** in well-designed units.
- **Hold-up Time**: ~16 ms at full load (one full AC mains cycle).
- **MTBF**: Typically **100,000 to 200,000 hours**.

## 1.4 SMPS vs. Linear Power Supply — Quick Comparison

| Parameter | Linear Supply | SMPS |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | 30% – 50% | **70% – 95%** |
| Size & Weight | Large (50 Hz transformer) | Compact (high-freq transformer) |
| Heat Dissipation | High | Low |
| EMI | Negligible | Significant (requires filtering) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (control IC required) |
| Ripple | Very Low | Moderate (filtered) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The SMPS operates on the principle of **energy transfer via high-frequency switching**. The main stages are:

1. **AC-to-DC Rectification:** Mains AC (230 V / 50 Hz in India) is full-wave rectified and bulk-filtered to obtain an unregulated high-voltage DC (≈ 310 V DC for 230 V RMS mains).
2. **High-Frequency Chopping:** This DC is chopped (switched ON/OFF) by a power transistor (MOSFET/IGBT) at a frequency of **20 kHz – 500 kHz**.
3. **High-Frequency Transformation:** The chopped waveform is fed into a small, lightweight ferrite-core transformer that steps the voltage up or down.
4. **Secondary Rectification & Filtering:** The transformed high-frequency AC is rectified (using fast Schottky diodes) and filtered to obtain a smooth regulated DC output.
5. **Feedback Control:** A sample of the output is fed back to a PWM controller IC (e.g., UC3842, TL494, SG3525) that adjusts the duty cycle of the switching transistor to maintain a constant output voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why high frequency?**
> Transformer size is inversely proportional to operating frequency. A transformer operating at **50 kHz** is roughly **1000× smaller** than one operating at 50 Hz for the same power handling — this is the key reason SMPS units are so compact.

## 2.2 Block Diagram — Functional Stages

The generic SMPS architecture has these cascaded blocks:
**EMI Filter → Bridge Rectifier → Bulk Capacitor → DC-DC Converter (with High-Freq Transformer) → Output Rectifier → Output Filter → Feedback/Control Loop → PWM Controller**

## 2.3 Duty Cycle — The Core Control Parameter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **duty cycle (D)** is the fraction of the switching period $T_s$ for which the switch is ON:
>
> $$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_s}$$
>
> where $T_s = 1 / f_{sw}$. This single parameter dictates the output voltage in all basic converter topologies.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Topology | Voltage Conversion Ratio $\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Inductor Voltage (ON) | Inductor Voltage (OFF) | Critical Inductance $L_{min}$ | Output Ripple $\Delta V_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Buck (Step-Down)** | $D$ | $V_{in} - V_{out}$ | $-V_{out}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)R}{2 f_{sw}}$ | $\dfrac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 L C f_{sw}^2}$ |
| 2 | **Boost (Step-Up)** | $\dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | $V_{in}$ | $V_{out} - V_{in}$ | $\dfrac{D(1-D)^2 R}{2 f_{sw}}$ | $\dfrac{I_{out} D}{C f_{sw}}$ |
| 3 | **Buck-Boost** | $-\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | $V_{in}$ | $V_{out}$ | $\dfrac{(1-D)^2 R}{2 D f_{sw}}$ | $\dfrac{I_{out} D}{C f_{sw}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode):**
> - The inductor current never falls to zero during the switching cycle.
> - The load current $I_{out} \geq \dfrac{V_{out}(1-D)}{2 L f_{sw}}$ for a buck converter.
> - **Always assume CCM** unless the problem explicitly states otherwise — this is the KTU board examiner's default expectation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Personal Computer Power Supplies** (ATX standard, 80 PLUS certified).
- **Mobile Phone Chargers** (e.g., 5 V / 2 A USB chargers using flyback topology).
- **LED Driver Circuits** (constant-current SMPS drivers).
- **DC Motor Drives** in industrial automation.
- **Telecom Equipment** (−48 V DC rack supplies).
- **Aerospace & Satellite** power conditioning modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Transfer Ratio of an Ideal Buck Converter (CCM)

**Given:** A buck converter operating in CCM with input voltage $V_{in}$, output voltage $V_{out}$, switch ON-time $T_{ON}$, OFF-time $T_{OFF}$, and switching period $T_s$.

**Step 1 — Define duty cycle.**
The duty cycle $D$ is the fraction of the period the switch is closed:

$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_s} = T_{ON} \cdot f_{sw}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the volt-second balance on the inductor.**

In steady-state, the average voltage across an inductor over one full cycle must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would ramp up to infinity):

$$\langle V_L \rangle = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_0^{T_s} v_L(t)\, dt = 0$$

**Step 3 — Write the inductor voltage in each sub-interval.**

- During **ON state** ($0 \leq t < T_{ON}$): the switch is closed, the diode is reverse-biased, and the inductor sees $V_{in} - V_{out}$.

$$v_L(t) = V_{in} - V_{out}, \quad 0 \leq t < T_{ON}$$

- During **OFF state** ($T_{ON} \leq t < T_s$): the switch is open, the diode conducts (freewheeling), and the inductor sees $-V_{out}$.

$$v_L(t) = -V_{out}, \quad T_{ON} \leq t < T_s$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the volt-second balance equation.**

$$\frac{1}{T_s}\left[(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot T_{ON} + (-V_{out}) \cdot T_{OFF}\right] = 0$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange by expanding the integral.**

$$(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot T_{ON} - V_{out} \cdot T_{OFF} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Group the $V_{out}$ terms.**

$$V_{in} \cdot T_{ON} - V_{out} \cdot T_{ON} - V_{out} \cdot T_{OFF} = 0$$

**Step 7 — Recognize that $T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = T_s$.**

$$V_{in} \cdot T_{ON} = V_{out} \cdot (T_{ON} + T_{OFF}) = V_{out} \cdot T_s$$

**Step 8 — Divide both sides by $T_s$ and $V_{in}$.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_s} = D$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = D \quad \text{(Buck Converter, CCM)}}$$

Since $0 \leq D \leq 1$, we have $V_{out} \leq V_{in}$ — hence the name **step-down** converter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Inductance $L_{min}$ for a Buck Converter

**Step 1 — Inductor current ripple magnitude.**
The peak-to-peak ripple in the inductor current during the ON interval is:

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot T_{ON}}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $T_{ON} = D \cdot T_s$ and $V_{in} - V_{out} = V_{in}(1 - D)$.**

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{in}(1-D) \cdot D \cdot T_s}{L} = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{L f_{sw}}$$

**Step 3 — Boundary condition at CCM/DCM edge.**
At the edge of continuous conduction, the minimum inductor current just touches zero. This means the average inductor current equals half the ripple:

$$I_{L,avg} = \frac{\Delta I_L}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Relate average inductor current to output current.**
In a buck converter, $I_{L,avg} = I_{out} = V_{out} / R$.

$$\frac{V_{out}}{R} = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{2 L_{min} f_{sw}}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $V_{out}$ and solve for $L_{min}$.**

$$\boxed{L_{min} = \frac{R(1-D)}{2 f_{sw}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Voltage Ripple for a Buck Converter

**Step 1 — Capacitor charge balance.**
The output capacitor supplies the load during the ON interval when the inductor is ramping up, and the inductor replenishes the capacitor during the OFF interval. The ripple is approximated as:

$$\Delta V_{out} \approx \frac{\Delta Q}{C}$$

**Step 2 — Approximate the charge transferred as the area of the ripple triangle.**

$$\Delta Q = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{T_s}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \frac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the ripple current from Section 3.2, Step 2.**

$$\Delta Q = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 L f_{sw}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Final ripple expression.**

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{out} = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 L C f_{sw}^2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A buck converter has $V_{in} = 24$ V, $V_{out} = 12$ V, switching frequency $f_{sw} = 50$ kHz, load resistance $R = 10\ \Omega$, and inductor $L = 200\ \mu$H. Determine the duty cycle, the output current ripple, and the minimum capacitance to limit output voltage ripple to 50 mV.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Duty Cycle.**

$$D = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{12}{24} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Output (average inductor) current.**

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{out}}{R} = \frac{12}{10} = 1.2\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Peak-to-peak inductor current ripple.**

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{L f_{sw}} = \frac{12 \cdot (1 - 0.5)}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 \times 10^3}$$

$$\Delta I_L = \frac{6}{10} = 0.6\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Required capacitance for 50 mV ripple.**

$$C_{min} = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 L f_{sw}^2 \, \Delta V_{out}} = \frac{12 \cdot 0.5}{8 \cdot 200\times 10^{-6} \cdot (50 \times 10^3)^2 \cdot 0.05}$$

$$C_{min} = \frac{6}{8 \cdot 200\times 10^{-6} \cdot 2.5 \times 10^9 \cdot 0.05}$$

$$C_{min} = \frac{6}{200\,000} = 30\ \mu\text{F}$$

**Answer:** $D = 0.5$, $\Delta I_L = 0.6$ A, $C_{min} = 30\ \mu$F.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Generic SMPS

The diagram below shows the **cascaded functional architecture** of a complete SMPS, from the AC mains input to the regulated DC output, including the feedback control loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Input 230V 50Hz] --> B[EMI Filter and Fuse]
    B --> C[Bridge Rectifier and Bulk Capacitor]
    C --> D[Unregulated High Voltage DC about 310V]
    D --> E[PWM Controller IC with Feedback]
    E --> F[Power Switch MOSFET or IGBT]
    F --> G[High Frequency Transformer]
    G --> H[Secondary Fast Rectifier Schottky Diode]
    H --> I[Output LC Filter]
    I --> J[Regulated DC Output to Load]
    J --> K[Output Voltage Sampler]
    K --> L[Error Amplifier and Reference]
    L --> E

    style A fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style J fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37
    style E fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0969da
    style G fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#cc3300
```

**Block-by-Block Functional Description:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **AC Mains Input** | Receives standard 230 V RMS at 50 Hz. |
| **EMI Filter + Fuse** | Suppresses conducted electromagnetic interference and provides overcurrent protection. |
| **Bridge Rectifier + Bulk Capacitor** | Converts AC to unregulated DC; bulk capacitor smooths the 100 Hz ripple. |
| **PWM Controller IC** | Generates the gating signal (e.g., UC3842) with adjustable duty cycle. |
| **Power Switch (MOSFET/IGBT)** | Performs high-frequency chopping of the DC link. |
| **High-Frequency Transformer** | Provides galvanic isolation and voltage transformation at 20 kHz – 500 kHz. |
| **Secondary Fast Rectifier** | Schottky or ultrafast diode rectifies the high-frequency AC. |
| **Output LC Filter** | Smooths the rectified waveform to a clean DC level. |
| **Feedback Loop** | Samples output, compares to reference, modulates duty cycle. |

## 4.2 Control Loop Architecture (Feedback Mechanism)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Voltage Vref] --> C[Error Amplifier]
    B[Sensed Output Voltage Vsense] --> C
    C --> D[Comparator with Ramp]
    E[Oscillator Ramp Signal] --> D
    D --> F[PWM Gating Pulse]
    F --> G[Power Switch Gate Drive]
    G --> H[Power Stage]
    H --> B

    style C fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
    style F fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37
    style H fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0969da
```

**Operating Logic:**
- If $V_{sense} < V_{ref}$ → Error signal rises → PWM pulse width **increases** → More energy delivered → $V_{out}$ rises.
- If $V_{sense} > V_{ref}$ → Error signal falls → PWM pulse width **decreases** → Less energy delivered → $V_{out}$ falls.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> *Define an SMPS. List any three advantages of an SMPS over a linear power supply.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **SMPS (Switched Mode Power Supply)** is a high-frequency power conversion circuit that uses a switching regulator (typically 20 kHz – 1 MHz) to convert and regulate DC voltage with high efficiency. (1 mark)

**Advantages over linear supplies:**
1. **High efficiency (70%–95%)** — minimal power dissipation as heat. (1 mark)
2. **Compact size and light weight** — high-frequency transformer is much smaller than 50 Hz transformer. (0.5 mark)
3. **Wide input voltage range** and excellent line/load regulation. (0.5 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> *What is duty cycle? Why is high switching frequency preferred in SMPS design?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Duty cycle (D)** is the ratio of the switch ON-time to the total switching period: $D = T_{ON}/T_s$. (1 mark)

**Why high switching frequency is preferred:**
1. **Reduces transformer and filter size** — size of magnetic components is inversely proportional to frequency. (1 mark)
2. **Improves dynamic response** to load transients. (0.5 mark)
3. **Pushes switching noise above the audible range**, eliminating audible hum. (0.5 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question Paper, Module 1]
> *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an SMPS. Describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Derive the expression for the voltage transfer ratio of a buck converter operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM). **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) SMPS Block Diagram and Operation (7 Marks):**

The SMPS consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Input Rectifier and Filter (1 mark):** The 230 V / 50 Hz AC mains is first passed through an EMI filter to suppress noise, then full-wave bridge-rectified, and bulk-filtered by a large electrolytic capacitor to produce an unregulated high-voltage DC link of ≈ 310 V DC.

2. **PWM Controller and Power Switch (2 marks):** The unregulated DC is fed to a power transistor (MOSFET/IGBT) that chops it at a high frequency (50 kHz typical). A PWM controller IC (e.g., UC3842) generates the gating pulses, with the duty cycle controlled by the feedback loop.

3. **High-Frequency Transformer (2 marks):** The chopped waveform drives a small ferrite-core HF transformer that provides both voltage step-down and galvanic isolation between input and output. At 50 kHz, the transformer is roughly 1000× smaller than its 50 Hz equivalent for the same power rating.

4. **Output Rectifier, Filter, and Feedback (2 marks):** The secondary AC is rectified by fast Schottky diodes and filtered by an LC low-pass filter to yield a smooth regulated DC. A sample of this output is fed back via an optocoupler to the PWM controller, which adjusts the duty cycle to maintain constant output voltage.

**(b) Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio of Buck Converter (7 Marks):**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The key steps are:

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define duty cycle $D = T_{ON}/T_s$.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** State volt-second balance on the inductor: $\langle V_L \rangle = 0$ in steady state.
- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Write $v_L$ during ON-state as $V_{in} - V_{out}$ and during OFF-state as $-V_{out}$.
- **Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute into the volt-second balance and solve:
$$(V_{in} - V_{out}) T_{ON} - V_{out} T_{OFF} = 0 \Rightarrow V_{in} T_{ON} = V_{out} T_s$$
- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Final result: $\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = D$.

---

> ### Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary, Module 1]
> *(a)* Explain the basic principle of operation of an SMPS. Compare SMPS with linear power supply. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* For a buck converter, derive expressions for (i) critical inductance $L_{min}$ and (ii) output voltage ripple $\Delta V_{out}$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) SMPS Principle and Comparison (7 Marks):**

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):** The SMPS operates by rapidly switching a power transistor between saturation and cutoff at a high frequency (20 kHz – 1 MHz). The average output voltage is controlled by varying the duty cycle D of the switching waveform. The chopped waveform is either fed to a transformer for isolation (isolated SMPS) or directly filtered (non-isolated SMPS) to produce a regulated DC output. A feedback loop continuously samples the output and adjusts D to maintain regulation against line and load variations.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Linear Power Supply | SMPS |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | 30% – 50% | 70% – 95% |
| Size & Weight | Heavy (large 50 Hz transformer) | Compact (HF transformer) |
| Heat Dissipation | High (requires large heatsink) | Low |
| EMI | Low | High (needs filtering) |
| Cost | Lower for low power | Higher for low power |
| Regulation | Excellent | Very Good |

**(b) Derivation of $L_{min}$ and $\Delta V_{out}$ (7 Marks):**

**(i) Critical Inductance (4 marks):** Refer to derivation in **Section 3.2**.
- Inductor current ripple: $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_{out}(1-D)}{L f_{sw}}$ (1 mark)
- Boundary condition: $I_{out} = \Delta I_L / 2$ (1 mark)
- Substituting $I_{out} = V_{out}/R$: (1 mark)
$$\boxed{L_{min} = \frac{R(1-D)}{2 f_{sw}}}$$
(1 mark)

**(ii) Output Voltage Ripple (3 marks):** Refer to derivation in **Section 3.3**.
- Charge on capacitor during half period: $\Delta Q = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw}}$ (1.5 marks)
- Final expression: (1.5 marks)
$$\boxed{\Delta V_{out} = \frac{V_{out}(1-D)}{8 L C f_{sw}^2}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $V_{out}$ in a buck-boost converter** — it is *inverted* (negative) relative to input. Marks lost if ignored.
> 2. **Not stating the CCM assumption** — every derivation of voltage transfer ratio requires you to explicitly write "**assuming continuous conduction mode (CCM)**" or you will lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing switching frequency with mains frequency** — 50 Hz is *mains*; 20 kHz – 1 MHz is *switching*. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Wrong units in final answer** — always include units (V, A, H, F). A correct numerical answer without units loses a half-mark in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Not drawing the freewheeling diode in the buck converter diagram** — a 1-mark penalty is common.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SMPS Operation:**

- **SMPS** = **S**witched **M**ode **P**ower **S**upply; converts power using high-frequency switching.
- **Switching frequency range:** 20 kHz – 1 MHz (above audible band).
- **Efficiency:** 70% – 95% (far superior to linear's 30% – 50%).
- **Key formula (Buck, CCM):** $V_{out} / V_{in} = D$.
- **Key formula (Boost, CCM):** $V_{out} / V_{in} = 1 / (1 - D)$.
- **Key formula (Buck-Boost, CCM):** $V_{out} / V_{in} = -D / (1 - D)$ (note the **negative sign**).
- **Critical inductance (Buck):** $L_{min} = R(1-D) / (2 f_{sw})$.
- **Output ripple (Buck):** $\Delta V_{out} = V_{out}(1-D) / (8 L C f_{sw}^2)$.
- **Voltm-second balance on the inductor** is the master equation for deriving *all* DC-DC converter transfer ratios.
- **Main functional blocks:** Rectifier → Filter → HF Switch → HF Transformer → Output Rectifier → Output Filter → Feedback → PWM Controller.
- **Common controller ICs:** UC3842, UC3843, TL494, SG3525.
- **Common topologies:** Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, Flyback, Forward, Push-Pull, Half-Bridge, Full-Bridge.
- **Galvanic isolation** is provided by the high-frequency transformer (critical for safety in offline SMPS).
- **Schottky diodes** are preferred for secondary rectification due to low forward drop and fast recovery.
- **EMI filter at the input** is mandatory to meet conducted-emission standards (CISPR / FCC Class B).
- **Optocoupler** is typically used in the feedback path to maintain isolation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barkhausen Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Barkhausen Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Barkhausen Criterion (Barkhausen Stability Condition):**
> The **Barkhausen Criterion** is a mathematical condition that must be satisfied for a **linear feedback amplifier** to function as a **sustained sinusoidal oscillator** (i.e., to produce continuous, self-sustaining oscillations without any external input signal). It specifies the two simultaneous conditions on the **loop gain** $(A\beta)$ of the closed-loop feedback system.

In any sinusoidal oscillator, the active device (BJT/FET) along with the frequency-selective network (RC/LC) forms a closed loop. The Barkhausen Criterion mathematically states that for the circuit to oscillate, the signal must regenerate itself exactly — same amplitude and same phase — after one complete trip around the loop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Swing Push

Imagine you are pushing a child on a playground swing:

- You must push at **exactly the right moment** in the cycle (when the swing is at its extreme). If you push too early or too late, the swing slows down and stops. → This is the **Phase Condition**.
- You must push with **exactly the right amount of energy** — too little and the swing dies out; too much and it goes wild. For perfectly *sustained* swinging, each push must add just enough energy to replace the energy lost to air drag. → This is the **Magnitude Condition**.

The Barkhausen Criterion is the **electronics equivalent** of this playground push: the amplifier's output must return to its own input with **the same magnitude (gain = 1)** and **the same phase (shift = 0° or 360°)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Barkhausen Criterion forms the **theoretical foundation** for designing all sinusoidal oscillators (RC phase shift, Wien bridge, LC, crystal, etc.) that appear in Module 2 of PBMRT304.

## 1.3 The Two Conditions (At a Glance)

A feedback amplifier with forward gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$ will oscillate if and only if:

| # | Condition Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Magnitude Condition (Loop Gain)** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Signal amplitude is exactly preserved around the loop |
| 2 | **Phase Condition** | $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ \text{ or } 2n\pi \text{ radians}$ | Signal phase is exactly preserved (constructive feedback) |

Explicit constants to remember:
- **Loop gain magnitude** must equal exactly **$1$** (not greater, not less) for steady-state sustained oscillation.
- **Total phase shift around the loop** must equal an **integer multiple of $2\pi$** (i.e., $0^\circ, \pm 360^\circ, \pm 720^\circ, \dots$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin from the Feedback Amplifier Equation

Consider a basic **voltage-series (or voltage-shunt) feedback amplifier** with:

- $A$ = open-loop (forward) gain of the amplifier stage
- $\beta$ = reverse transfer function (feedback factor) of the feedback network
- $V_{in}$ = external input signal
- $V_f = \beta V_{out}$ = feedback voltage fed back to the input
- $V_{out}$ = closed-loop output

The fundamental closed-loop gain relation is:

$$A_f = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

The **loop gain** is defined as the product $A\beta$ — the signal that travels all the way around the closed loop (amplifier $\to$ output $\to$ feedback network $\to$ summing junction $\to$ back to amplifier input).

### 2.2 Deriving the Two Conditions — Logic Flow

**Step 1 — Start the oscillator with noise.**
Every electronic circuit contains thermal noise. When the loop is energized (DC power ON), this noise provides the initial stimulus $V_{in}$.

**Step 2 — The noise circulates.**
The amplifier amplifies the noise by $A$, the feedback network scales it by $\beta$, and a fraction $\beta V_{out}$ is returned to the input.

**Step 3 — For oscillation to BUILD UP (starting condition).**
The feedback signal must **reinforce** the original signal. Mathematically, the returned signal must be **greater than or equal to** the original. This is the **Nyquist starting criterion**:

$$\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1 \quad \text{(starting/building condition)}$$

**Step 4 — For steady-state sustained oscillation.**
Non-linearities in the active device (transistor saturation/cutoff) eventually limit the amplitude. In steady state, the loop gain settles to **exactly unity**:

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{(steady-state condition)}$$

**Step 5 — Phase coherence.**
The returned signal must arrive **in phase** with the original signal so that they add constructively, otherwise they would partially cancel and the oscillation would die. The total phase shift around the loop must be zero (or multiples of $2\pi$):

$$\angle A\beta = 0^\circ + n \cdot 360^\circ = 2n\pi \text{ rad}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every equation, condition, and standard constant** you need for KTU exam problems on this topic.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Standard Form / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed-loop gain of feedback amplifier | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1+A\beta}$ | General feedback relation |
