| 2 | Loop gain (complex quantity) | $T = A\beta = \vert A\beta \vert \angle A\beta$ | Complex number with magnitude & phase |
| 3 | **Barkhausen Magnitude Condition** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Equivalent to $20\log_{10}\vert A\beta \vert = 0$ dB |
| 4 | **Barkhausen Phase Condition** | $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ + 360^\circ n$ | Equivalent to $2n\pi$ rad, $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ |
| 5 | Starting (build-up) condition | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | Ensures oscillations grow from noise |
| 6 | Feedback voltage | $V_f = \beta V_{out}$ | Defines the feedback factor |
| 7 | Oscillation frequency (general) | Set $\text{Im}\{A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega)\} = 0$ | Solves the phase condition |
| 8 | Loop gain in dB at oscillation | $0$ **dB** | Critical examination figure |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Function generators & signal generators:** Every sine-wave oscillator (Wien bridge, RC phase-shift, Colpitts, Hartley, Crystal, Clapp) is designed by first ensuring the Barkhausen Criterion is met at the desired frequency.
- **Communication transmitters & receivers:** Local oscillators in superheterodyne receivers use LC/RC oscillators that rely on the Barkhausen condition.
- **Digital clocks & microcontrollers:** Crystal oscillators that clock every CPU are highly-stable versions of this same principle.
- **Biomedical & instrumentation:** Audio-frequency oscillators for ECG/EEG calibration and audio testing equipment.
- **Radar & RF systems:** Microwave oscillators (Gunn, Cavity) and VCOs depend on loop-gain engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Point:** In real designs, engineers deliberately set $\vert A\beta \vert$ slightly **greater than 1** (e.g., 1.02 to 1.05) at switch-on. The amplitude-limiting non-linearity of the active device automatically brings the loop gain down to exactly 1 in steady state, yielding a clean sine wave with low distortion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Barkhausen Criterion

We start from the **closed-loop feedback equation** and impose the physical requirement that the output should be self-sustaining without any external input.

### Step A — Write the basic feedback equation

For a basic feedback system, the error (difference) signal at the summing junction is:

$$V_{\epsilon} = V_{in} + V_f = V_{in} + \beta V_{out}$$

This error is amplified by the forward amplifier:

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{\epsilon}$$

### Step B — Substitute $V_{\epsilon}$ into the output equation

$$V_{out} = A \cdot \left( V_{in} + \beta V_{out} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in} + A\beta \cdot V_{out}$$

### Step C — Collect $V_{out}$ terms on one side

$$V_{out} - A\beta \cdot V_{out} = A \cdot V_{in}$$

$$V_{out} \cdot \left( 1 - A\beta \right) = A \cdot V_{in}$$

### Step D — Solve for the closed-loop gain

$$A_f = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

*(For negative-feedback convention with a subtracting junction, the sign inside the denominator is $+A\beta$; the derivation is identical and the final criterion is unchanged.)*

### Step E — Impose the oscillator requirement: $V_{in} = 0$

An oscillator must produce a non-zero output $V_{out}$ **without any external input**, i.e., $V_{in} = 0$. Setting $V_{in} = 0$ in the equation $V_{out}(1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$:

$$V_{out} \cdot (1 - A\beta) = 0$$

For $V_{out} \neq 0$ (we want an oscillation!), we must have:

$$1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{A\beta = 1}$$

### Step F — Recognize that $A\beta$ is complex in general

The loop gain $A\beta$ is a **complex quantity** depending on frequency $\omega$. Write it in polar form:

$$A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega) = \vert A\beta \vert \; e^{j\angle A\beta}$$

For the equation $A\beta = 1$ to hold at some frequency $\omega_0$:

$$\vert A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) \vert \cdot e^{j\angle A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0)} = 1 \cdot e^{j \cdot 0}$$

### Step G — Separate real and imaginary parts (Equating magnitudes)

$$\vert A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) \vert = 1 \quad \text{(Magnitude Condition)}$$

### Step H — Equate the phases

$$\angle A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0 + 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \dots \quad \text{(Phase Condition)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The Barkhausen Criterion is simply the requirement that the **loop gain be exactly equal to the complex number $1 + j0$** at the oscillation frequency. This simultaneously fixes (a) the frequency of oscillation via the phase equation, and (b) the minimum required amplifier gain via the magnitude equation.

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix — Reading Barkhausen for Different Oscillator Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> The phase condition **always** gives the **frequency of oscillation** $f_0$. The magnitude condition **always** gives the **minimum amplifier gain** $A_{min}$ needed to sustain oscillations.

| Oscillator Type | Frequency $f_0$ from $\angle A\beta = 0$ | $A_{min}$ from $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ |
|---|---|---|
| RC Phase-Shift (BJT) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $A_{min} = 29$ |
| Wien Bridge (BJT) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $A_{min} = 3$ |
| Colpitts (LC) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2}$ | $A_{min} = \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ |
| Hartley (LC) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{C \cdot L_{eq}}}$ where $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | $A_{min} = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ |
| Crystal (Pierce / Miller) | $f_0 \approx f_s$ (series resonant) | Determined by $C_m / C$ ratio |

## 3.3 Why "Exactly Equal to 1" — And Not Greater?

A common student doubt is: *"If $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ makes the signal grow, isn't that better?"*

- At switch-on: $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ is desired so that thermal noise grows into a usable oscillation.
- In steady state: the active device enters its non-linear region (saturation/cutoff for BJT, or limiting diodes, or automatic gain control circuits). The effective gain $A$ automatically reduces until $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.
- If $\vert A\beta \vert$ stays $>1$ forever: the waveform **clips** and becomes a **distorted square wave**, not a pure sinusoid.
- If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$: oscillations **decay exponentially** and die out (damped oscillation, $e^{-\alpha t} \sin \omega t$ form).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Feedback Oscillator

The diagram below maps the **closed-loop signal flow** of any sinusoidal oscillator that satisfies the Barkhausen Criterion. Each block represents a functional stage, and the loop direction is shown explicitly.

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC["DC Supply + Noise Source Vn"] --> SUM
    SUM["Summing Junction"] --> AMP["Amplifier Block Gain A j omega"]
    AMP --> OUT["Oscillator Output Vout"]
    OUT --> FB["Feedback Network Transfer Function Beta j omega"]
    FB --> VFB["Feedback Signal Vf equals Beta Vout"]
    VFB --> SUM
    OUT -.-> COND1["Magnitude Condition: |A beta| equals 1"]
    OUT -.-> COND2["Phase Condition: angle A beta equals 0 deg"]
    COND1 --> TONE["Sustained Sinusoid at f0"]
    COND2 --> TONE
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow — How a Circuit "Decides" its Oscillation Frequency

This flowchart shows how a real circuit, once powered, locks onto the single frequency that simultaneously satisfies both Barkhausen conditions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Power ON: thermal noise Vn across full spectrum"] --> S2["Noise enters loop and circulates"]
    S2 --> S3["Frequency selective network RC or LC attenuates all frequencies except f0"]
    S3 --> DEC{"At f0 is phase condition satisfied?"}
    DEC -- "Yes" --> PHA["angle of A beta at f0 equals 0 deg or multiples of 360 deg"]
    DEC -- "No" --> DECAY["Signal at that frequency dies out"]
    DECAY --> S3
    PHA --> MAG{"Is magnitude condition met?"}
    MAG -- "Yes A beta greater than 1" --> BUILD["Amplitude builds up exponentially"]
    MAG -- "No A beta less than 1" --> DECAY
    BUILD --> LIM["Active device enters non linear region and clips"]
    LIM --> STEADY["Loop gain automatically reduces to exactly 1"]
    STEADY --> OUT["Sustained clean sinusoid at f0"]
```

## 4.3 Magnitude & Phase Response — Barkhausen Intersection

The Barkhausen Criterion is best visualised as the intersection point of two curves on a Bode plot. The figure below illustrates the conceptual idea (described in text since we cannot plot curves inside Mermaid).

> [!NOTE]
> **Bode Plot Interpretation (Conceptual):**
> - Plot $\vert A\beta \vert_{\text{dB}}$ versus frequency $f$. The locus crosses the **$0$ dB line** at exactly $f_0$.
> - Plot $\angle A\beta$ versus frequency $f$. The locus crosses the **$0^\circ$ line** at exactly the same $f_0$.
> - The unique frequency where **both curves cross zero simultaneously** is the **oscillation frequency $f_0$**.

## 4.4 Why an Oscillator Needs No Input Port — Architecture Insight

Unlike an amplifier which has a clear **input port** and **output port**, an oscillator is a **closed system**:

- The summing junction mixes the feedback signal with whatever residual noise is present.
- The forward amplifier provides the energy (drawn from the DC supply) to compensate for resistive losses in the tank/RC network.
- The feedback network determines the frequency of operation.
- The entire loop is sealed — there is no external $V_{in}$ port; the loop is its own input.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for oscillations. Mention the role of each condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Barkhausen criterion states that a feedback circuit will act as a **sustained sinusoidal oscillator** if the following two conditions are simultaneously satisfied at a frequency $f_0$:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be exactly unity.

$$\vert A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) \vert = 1$$

This ensures that the signal amplitude does not decay (loss) or grow uncontrollably (clipping/distortion) after one complete round-trip around the loop.

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $2\pi$ radians.

$$\angle A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 0 + 2n\pi, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

This ensures that the feedback signal is **in phase** with the original input — i.e., positive/regenerative feedback rather than degenerative.

The **magnitude condition** dictates the **minimum gain** required, while the **phase condition** fixes the **frequency of oscillation** $f_0$.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between the starting condition and the steady-state condition of oscillations. Why is $\vert A\beta \vert$ set slightly greater than 1 at switch-on?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Starting Condition (Build-up) | Steady-State Condition |
|---|---|---|
| Loop gain magnitude | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ |
| Phase | $2n\pi$ rad | $2n\pi$ rad |
| Signal behaviour | Amplitude grows exponentially from noise | Amplitude is constant — pure sinusoid |
| Active device | Operating in linear region | Operating at the edge of non-linearity (limiting) |

At switch-on, the only signal available is **thermal noise** with very small amplitude. If $\vert A\beta \vert$ were exactly 1, the noise would circulate indefinitely without growing into a usable signal. By deliberately setting $\vert A\beta \vert$ slightly greater than $1$ (typically $1.02$ to $1.05$), the noise is **regeneratively amplified** and builds up to a measurable level. As the amplitude grows, the active device's non-linearity (saturation/cutoff) reduces the effective gain $A$ until $\vert A\beta \vert$ settles at exactly $1$, giving a clean steady-state sinusoid.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🎯 Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Barkhausen criterion** for sinusoidal oscillators starting from the closed-loop gain expression of a feedback amplifier. Show clearly how the two conditions (magnitude and phase) emerge from the requirement that the output should be non-zero even when the input is zero.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain how a sinusoidal oscillator differs structurally from a feedback amplifier. What is the role of the frequency-selective network in ensuring the Barkhausen condition is met at exactly one frequency?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1:** The closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier is:

$$A_f = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

**Step 2:** For oscillation, we require the output $V_{out}$ to be non-zero even when the external input $V_{in} = 0$. Substituting $V_{in} = 0$ in $V_{out}(1 - A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$:

$$V_{out}(1 - A\beta) = 0$$

**Step 3:** For $V_{out} \neq 0$, the term in brackets must vanish:

$$1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A\beta = 1$$

**Step 4:** Since $A\beta$ is a complex function of frequency, $A\beta = 1$ actually represents a complex equality. Writing $A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega) = \vert A\beta \vert e^{j\angle A\beta}$:

- Equating **moduli** $\Rightarrow \vert A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) \vert = 1$ **(Magnitude Condition)**
- Equating **phases** $\Rightarrow \angle A(j\omega_0)\beta(j\omega_0) = 2n\pi$ rad **(Phase Condition)**

**Step 5:** The phase condition fixes the **frequency of oscillation** $f_0$, and the magnitude condition fixes the **minimum amplifier gain** required to sustain the oscillation.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Structural differences** between a feedback amplifier and an oscillator:

| Feature | Feedback Amplifier | Sinusoidal Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| External input port | Yes ($V_{in}$ applied) | No ($V_{in} = 0$) |
| Purpose | Amplifies the input signal | Generates the output signal |
| Loop gain design | $\vert 1 + A\beta \vert \gg 1$ (negative feedback) | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (positive feedback) |
| Frequency selectivity | Wideband (no selective element) | Narrow band — RC or LC tank |
| Operating region | Strictly linear | Initial linear → limiting region |

**Role of the frequency-selective network (RC / LC tank):**

- The frequency-selective network introduces a **frequency-dependent phase shift** that varies with $\omega$.
- At one specific frequency $f_0$, the total phase shift through the amplifier + feedback network becomes exactly $0^\circ$ (or $360^\circ$), satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.
- At all other frequencies, the phase condition fails and any noise component at those frequencies dies out due to destructive interference.
- Hence the network forces oscillation at **one single well-defined frequency** $f_0$ with a clean sinusoidal waveform.

---

### 🎯 Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain why a feedback circuit with $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and total phase shift $0^\circ$ will **sustain** oscillations, while the same circuit with $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ will produce only **damped** oscillations. Mathematically show the time-domain behaviour in both cases.

**(b) [7 marks]** A certain RC phase-shift oscillator uses a BJT amplifier with $h_{fe} = 100$ and three identical RC sections each with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \mu\text{F}$. **(i)** Calculate the **frequency of oscillation**. **(ii)** Will the circuit oscillate? Justify by computing whether $h_{fe}$ exceeds $A_{min}$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Let the closed-loop system have a natural response of the form $v(t) = e^{st}$ where $s$ is complex. The characteristic equation from $1 - A\beta = 0$ is satisfied at the Barkhausen point. For small deviations around $f_0$, we can write the loop gain as $A\beta \approx (1 + \Delta A) e^{j\Delta\phi}$ where $\Delta A$ and $\Delta\phi$ are small departures from unity gain and zero phase.

**Case 1 — $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0$ (Barkhausen satisfied):**

The signal after one round-trip is identical to the original. After $n$ trips:

$$v_n(t) = v_0(t) \cdot (1)^n = v_0(t)$$

The amplitude remains **constant in time** — a sustained sinusoid:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$$

**Case 2 — $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ (say $= k < 1$) and phase condition met:**

Each round-trip multiplies amplitude by $k$:

$$v_n(t) = v_0 \cdot k^n = v_0 \cdot e^{n \ln k}$$

Converting discrete index $n$ to continuous time via $n = t/T$ (where $T$ is the round-trip time):

$$v(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau} \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$$

This is a **damped sinusoid** — the amplitude decays exponentially with time constant $\tau = -T/\ln k$. The oscillation dies out completely as $t \to \infty$.

**Conclusion:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is the **critical** boundary between build-up and decay. Only at this precise value (or slightly above with limiting action) is sustained oscillation possible.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Frequency of oscillation:**

For an RC phase-shift oscillator using three identical RC sections (the standard BJT configuration), the frequency of oscillation is given by:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

Substituting $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^4 \Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \mu\text{F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$:

$$RC = 10^4 \times 10^{-8} = 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{6}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.449 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 649.7 \text{ Hz}$$

**Result:** $f_0 \approx 650 \text{ Hz}$.

**(ii) Will the circuit oscillate?**

The minimum required current gain for a three-section RC phase-shift oscillator is:

$$h_{fe,min} = 29$$

The available current gain of the BJT is $h_{fe} = 100$.

Since $h_{fe} = 100 > 29 = h_{fe,min}$, the Barkhausen magnitude condition is satisfied with margin.

**Result:** ✅ **Yes, the circuit will oscillate** at approximately $650$ Hz. (The extra gain margin of $100/29 \approx 3.45$ ensures reliable starting of oscillations.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing starting vs steady-state:** Many students write "$\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ is required for oscillation." The examiner will deduct marks because for **steady-state sustained** oscillation it is **exactly 1**, not greater. Clearly state both starting and steady-state conditions.
> 2. **Sign error in feedback equation:** The sign of $A\beta$ depends on whether summing is + or −. Always derive from scratch using the polar form $A\beta = 1$, not the Cartesian form, to avoid sign confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting the $2n\pi$ term:** Phase condition is $0 + 2n\pi$, not just $0$. Examiners check for the general form.
> 4. **Mixing up $A_{min}$ values:** RC phase-shift $\to 29$, Wien bridge $\to 3$, Colpitts $\to C_2/C_1$, Hartley $\to L_2/L_1$. Memorize carefully.
> 5. **Writing "frequency of oscillation" without units:** Always include **Hz** or **rad/s** in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Barkhausen Criterion**

- 🎯 **Barkhausen Criterion** is the **loop-gain condition** for sustained sinusoidal oscillation in a feedback amplifier with no external input.
- 🎯 **Magnitude condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ (loop gain magnitude exactly unity).
- 🎯 **Phase condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ + 360^\circ n$ (or equivalently $2n\pi$ rad).
- 🎯 **Starting condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ — provides build-up from noise.
- 🎯 **Steady-state condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ — sustained clean sinusoid.
- 🎯 **Phase condition → fixes $f_0$.** Magnitude condition → fixes **minimum gain** $A_{min}$.
- 🎯 Origin: setting $V_{in} = 0$ in $V_{out} = A(V_{in} + \beta V_{out})$ and demanding $V_{out} \neq 0$.
- 🎯 **In dB:** the loop gain at oscillation is exactly **$0$ dB**.
- 🎯 **Common $A_{min}$ values:** RC phase-shift $= 29$, Wien bridge $= 3$, Colpitts $= C_2/C_1$, Hartley $= L_2/L_1$.
- 🎯 **Why loop gain must be slightly $> 1$ at switch-on:** to grow noise into a usable signal; non-linearity of active device brings it back to 1 in steady state.
- 🎯 **Damped vs sustained:** $\vert A\beta \vert < 1 \Rightarrow$ damped (decays); $\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \Rightarrow$ sustained (constant amplitude).
- 🎯 **Frequency-selective network (RC or LC tank)** ensures the phase condition is satisfied at **one and only one** frequency, guaranteeing a clean sinusoid.
- 🎯 **Real-world uses:** function generators, communication transmitters, CPU clocks (crystal oscillators), audio test equipment, RF/microwave oscillators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Difference Between LC & RC Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Difference Between LC & RC Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Oscillator (KTU Syllabus Definition):** An electronic oscillator is a circuit that converts DC energy from the power supply into AC energy at a specific frequency without any external input signal, relying entirely on the principle of **positive feedback** and the **Barkhausen Criterion**.

An oscillator requires two essential elements:
- An **active amplifying device** (BJT, FET, Op-Amp).
- A **frequency-selective feedback network** that determines the frequency of oscillation.

The classification of oscillators is primarily based on the **type of frequency-selective network** used:

| Type | Frequency-Selective Element | Common Names |
|---|---|---|
| **LC Oscillator** | Inductor (L) + Capacitor (C) tank circuit | Harmonic / Tank Oscillator |
| **RC Oscillator** | Resistor (R) + Capacitor (C) network | Relaxation-type / Audio Oscillator |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Swinging Pendulum vs. The Bouncing Spring**

Imagine two playground setups:

🎡 **The LC Oscillator = A Heavy Pendulum on a Swingset**
- The pendulum has a heavy metallic disc (the **inductor**, storing energy in its magnetic field) and a stretched rope (the **capacitor**, storing energy in its electric field).
- Energy sloshes back and forth very quickly between mass and rope, giving a **high-frequency swing**.
- Inductors are heavy, expensive, and hard to carry around — much like a giant metal pendulum.

🎈 **The RC Oscillator = A Water Tap Dripping into a Bucket**
- Water (charge) trickles through a narrow pipe (**resistor**) and fills a tank (**capacitor**).
- When the tank fills, it tips over and empties, then refills. This slow, repetitive cycle is a **low-frequency** oscillation.
- Resistors and capacitors are cheap, light, and easy to carry — perfect for **portable, low-frequency gadgets**.

---

## 1.3 Core Distinction at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important difference:**
> LC oscillators use an **LC tank circuit** to generate **sinusoidal oscillations at high frequencies (RF range)**, while RC oscillators use an **RC phase-shift network** to generate **sinusoidal oscillations at low frequencies (audio range)**.

### ⚙️ Standard Performance Metrics

- **Frequency Range of Operation:** LC → **kHz to GHz**, RC → **1 Hz to 1 MHz**
- **Quality Factor (Q):** LC → **High (typically 50 to 500+)**, RC → **Low (typically < 10)**
- **Frequency Stability:** LC → **Better**, RC → **Moderate**
- **Integration Friendly:** LC → **Poor (inductors resist miniaturization)**, RC → **Excellent**
- **Standard Examples:** LC → **Hartley, Colpitts, Clapp, Crystal**; RC → **RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — LC Oscillator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle:** A parallel LC tank circuit exhibits **resonance** at a specific frequency. At this resonant frequency, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel each other, leaving only a purely resistive impedance — the tank behaves like a very high impedance. Sustained oscillations occur by feeding back a portion of this resonant signal through an amplifier.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **Energy Storage:** The inductor stores energy in its magnetic field (½LI²). The capacitor stores energy in its electric field (½CV²).
2. **Energy Exchange:** Energy oscillates back and forth between L and C with negligible loss in an ideal tank.
3. **Resonance Condition:** The reactances cancel, $X_L = X_C$.
4. **Frequency Selection:** Only the resonant frequency component experiences zero net phase shift through the tank.
5. **Positive Feedback:** The amplifier provides gain to compensate tank losses and feeds back the in-phase signal.

### Common Topologies
- **Hartley Oscillator:** Tank uses a **tapped inductor** (L₁, L₂) and a single C.
- **Colpitts Oscillator:** Tank uses a **capacitive divider** (C₁, C₂) and a single L.
- **Clapp Oscillator:** A modified Colpitts with an **additional series capacitor** for improved stability.
- **Crystal Oscillator:** Uses a **piezoelectric crystal** (equivalent to a high-Q LC tank).

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — RC Oscillator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Working Principle:** RC networks do not resonate in the tank-circuit sense. Instead, they provide the necessary **180° phase shift** (or specific frequency-dependent attenuation) so that the amplifier's 180° inversion (common-emitter or inverting op-amp stage) results in a total loop phase of 0° or 360°.

### Step-by-Step Logic
1. **RC Ladder (Phase Shift Oscillator):** Three cascaded RC sections each contribute ≈ 60° phase shift, totalling **180°** at the desired frequency.
2. **Wien Bridge Oscillator:** A lead-lag RC network provides **zero phase shift** at one specific frequency, requiring a non-inverting amplifier.
3. **Frequency-Dependent Attenuation:** The RC network attenuates all frequencies except one, ensuring clean sinusoidal output.
4. **Amplifier Compensation:** The amplifier's gain (≥ 29 for Wien Bridge, ≥ 29 for RC phase shift) compensates for the network's attenuation.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Preferred Oscillator Type | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **RF Transmitters & Receivers** | LC / Crystal | Need high frequency and high stability for carrier generation. |
| **Mobile Phones (Local Oscillators)** | LC / Crystal (SAW) | Miniaturized inductors and crystals operate in GHz range. |
| **Audio Test Equipment** | RC (Wien Bridge) | Low distortion sine waves in the 20 Hz–20 kHz band. |
| **Function Generators** | RC (Wien Bridge) | Wide low-frequency coverage at low cost. |
| **Heart Rate Monitors / Pacemakers** | RC | Low power, low frequency, integrated design. |
| **Computer Clocks (CPU)** | Crystal (LC type) | Extremely stable, precise frequency reference. |

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly examinable** under the KTU 2024 Scheme. Master them with correct units.

| # | Parameter | LC Oscillator Formula | RC Oscillator Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Resonant / Oscillation Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{eq} C_{eq}}}$ | (Wien Bridge) $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 2 | **Phase Shift Oscillator Frequency** | — | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$ | Hz |
| 3 | **Inductive Reactance** | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | — | Ω |
| 4 | **Capacitive Reactance** | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | — | Ω |
| 5 | **Resonance Condition** | $X_L = X_C$ | — | Ω |
| 6 | **Quality Factor of Tank** | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R_s}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $Q = \dfrac{1}{3}\sqrt{6}$ (Phase Shift) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Hartley Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)C}}$ | — | Hz |
| 8 | **Colpitts Frequency** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}$ | — | Hz |
| 9 | **Barkhausen Criterion (Loop Gain)** | $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$ | $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | **Barkhausen Criterion (Loop Phase)** | $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ | $\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360°$ | Degrees |

### Symbols Used
- $L$ → Inductance in **Henry (H)**
- $C$ → Capacitance in **Farad (F)**
- $R$ → Resistance in **Ohm (Ω)**
- $f_0$ → Frequency of oscillation in **Hertz (Hz)**
- $A$ → Amplifier gain
- $\beta$ → Feedback network gain
- $M$ → Mutual inductance between L₁ and L₂
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Resonant Frequency for LC Oscillator

We start with a **parallel LC tank circuit** and find the frequency at which the imaginary part of its admittance vanishes.

### Step 1: Write the admittance of L and C in parallel
The admittance of an inductor is $\dfrac{1}{j\omega L}$ and that of a capacitor is $j\omega C$.

$$
Y_{tank} = \frac{1}{j\omega L} + j\omega C
$$

### Step 2: Combine into a single fraction
Put both terms on a common denominator.

$$
Y_{tank} = \frac{1 + j\omega L \cdot j\omega C}{j\omega L} = \frac{1 - \omega^2 LC}{j\omega L}
$$

**Logic:** We multiplied the numerator and denominator of the second term by $j\omega L$ to combine.

### Step 3: Identify the resonance condition
Resonance occurs when the **numerator becomes zero** (the imaginary part vanishes, leaving only a real conductance). This requires:

$$
1 - \omega_0^2 LC = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve for angular frequency $\omega_0$
Rearranging the equation from Step 3:

$$
\omega_0^2 LC = 1
$$

$$
\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{LC}
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

### Step 5: Convert from angular frequency to ordinary frequency
Using the relationship $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$:

$$
2\pi f_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** This is the **fundamental frequency of an LC oscillator**. Every LC-based oscillator (Hartley, Colpitts, Clapp, Crystal) uses a variation of this formula with effective L and C values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Frequency for RC Phase Shift Oscillator

The RC phase shift oscillator uses a common-emitter BJT amplifier (180° phase inversion) followed by **three identical RC sections**, each contributing 60° to give a total of 180°.

### Step 1: Network transfer function
Consider a single RC section feeding the next. The voltage transfer ratio of a three-section RC ladder loaded by the amplifier input resistance is given by:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha(1 - 6\alpha^2)}
$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$ is a normalized frequency parameter.

### Step 2: Apply the Barkhausen phase condition
The loop phase must be zero. The phase of $\beta$ is zero when its imaginary part is zero:

$$
\alpha(1 - 6\alpha^2) = 0
$$

Since $\alpha \neq 0$ for finite frequencies:

$$
1 - 6\alpha^2 = 0
$$

$$
\alpha^2 = \frac{1}{6}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute back to find frequency
Recall that $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$, therefore:

$$
\frac{1}{\omega R C} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
\omega R C = \sqrt{6}
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{R C \sqrt{6}}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to ordinary frequency

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For oscillations to start,** the amplifier gain must satisfy $A \geq 29$ (i.e., the gain must overcome the network attenuation of $\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Frequency for Wien Bridge Oscillator

The Wien Bridge uses a **series RC combination** in one arm and a **parallel RC combination** in the adjacent arm. The feedback fraction is:

$$
\beta = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}
$$

### Step 1: Write the impedances
- Series arm: $Z_s = R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega R C}{j\omega C}$
- Parallel arm: $Z_p = \dfrac{R \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}$

### Step 2: Substitute into feedback fraction
$$
\beta = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega R C}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega R C}}
$$

### Step 3: Simplify the denominator
Find a common denominator $(1 + j\omega R C)$:

$$
Z_s + Z_p = \frac{(1 + j\omega R C)^2 + j\omega R C}{j\omega C(1 + j\omega R C)}
$$

### Step 4: Form the ratio
$$
\beta = \frac{R \cdot j\omega C}{(1 + j\omega R C)^2 + j\omega R C}
$$

### Step 5: Apply zero-phase condition
For the loop phase to be zero, the imaginary part of the denominator must vanish. Expanding $(1 + j\omega R C)^2 = 1 + 2j\omega R C - \omega^2 R^2 C^2$:

$$
(1 + 2j\omega R C - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j\omega R C = (1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + j(3\omega R C)
$$

The imaginary part is $3\omega R C$. For phase to be zero, the **real part must vanish** (i.e., we choose the operating point such that the real and imaginary balance appropriately, leading to the standard result):

$$
1 - \omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 0
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{R C}
$$

### Step 6: Final frequency

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **At this frequency,** the feedback fraction is real and equals $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$. The non-inverting amplifier must therefore provide a gain $A = 3$ to satisfy the **Barkhausen magnitude condition** $A\beta = 1$. In practice, $A$ is set slightly greater than 3 (e.g., 3.1) to ensure startup.

---

## 3.4 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Comparison Parameter | LC Oscillator | RC Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Range** | High (kHz to GHz) | Low (Hz to MHz) |
| **Frequency-Determining Element** | L and C tank | R and C network |
| **Operating Principle** | Resonance of LC tank | RC phase shift / frequency-selective attenuation |
| **Quality Factor (Q)** | High (50–500+) | Low (< 10) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Better | Moderate |
| **Output Waveform Purity** | Excellent (especially Crystal) | Good (Wien Bridge has low distortion) |
| **Tunability** | Difficult (large L/C values impractical) | Easy (variable R or C) |
| **Physical Size & Weight** | Bulky, heavy | Compact, lightweight |
| **Cost** | Higher (inductors expensive) | Lower |
| **Suitability for IC Fabrication** | Poor (inductors hard to integrate) | Excellent (R and C are IC-friendly) |
| **Power Consumption** | Generally higher | Generally lower |
| **Examples** | Hartley, Colpitts, Clapp, Crystal, Armstrong | RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge, Twin-T |
| **Application Areas** | RF communication, transmitters, IF stages | Audio oscillators, function generators, biomedical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LC Oscillator

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the signal flow and functional blocks of a generic LC oscillator, illustrating how the tank circuit, amplifier, and feedback path interact.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeDC["DC Power Supply Vcc"] --> nodeAmp["Active Amplifying Device BJT or FET or Op-Amp"]
    nodeAmp --> nodeOut["Sustained Sinusoidal Output Vout at f0"]
    nodeOut --> nodeFeed["Feedback Network Couples Output Back to Input"]
    nodeFeed --> nodeTank["LC Tank Circuit Determines Frequency f0 = 1 over 2 pi sqrt LC"]
    nodeTank --> nodeAmp
    nodeTank --> nodeFreq["Frequency Selection Only Resonant Frequency Survives"]
    nodeFreq --> nodeOut
    nodeAmp --> nodePhase["Loop Phase Equals 0 or 360 Degrees"]
    nodePhase --> nodeBark["Barkhausen Criterion Satisfied A beta is greater than or equal to 1"]
    nodeBark --> nodeStable["Stable Continuous Oscillations Generated"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The amplifier boosts the small noise voltage; the tank circuit acts as a **band-pass filter** that selects only the resonant frequency; the feedback network re-injects this filtered signal in-phase to the input, sustaining oscillation.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RC Phase Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeDC2["DC Power Supply Vcc"] --> nodeAmp2["Inverting Amplifier Common Emitter BJT or Op-Amp 180 Degree Phase Shift"]
    nodeAmp2 --> nodeOut2["Sinusoidal Output Vout at f0"]
    nodeOut2 --> nodeRC1["RC Section 1 Contributes 60 Degree Phase Shift"]
    nodeRC1 --> nodeRC2["RC Section 2 Contributes 60 Degree Phase Shift"]
    nodeRC2 --> nodeRC3["RC Section 3 Contributes 60 Degree Phase Shift"]
    nodeRC3 --> nodeFeed2["Total RC Network Phase Shift is 180 Degrees"]
    nodeFeed2 --> nodeAmp2
    nodeLoop["Total Loop Phase 180 from Amp plus 180 from RC equals 360 Equals 0 Degrees"]
    nodeLoop --> nodeBark2["Barkhausen Criterion Satisfied"]
    nodeBark2 --> nodeGain["Amplifier Gain A is greater than or equal to 29"]
    nodeGain --> nodeStable2["Frequency f0 = 1 over 2 pi RC sqrt 6 Generated"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeDC3["DC Power Supply Vcc"] --> nodeNonInv["Non Inverting Amplifier Gain A equals 3"]
    nodeNonInv --> nodeOut3["Low Distortion Sine Wave Output"]
    nodeOut3 --> nodeSeries["Series RC Arm Zs equals R plus 1 over j omega C"]
    nodeOut3 --> nodeParallel["Parallel RC Arm Zp equals R parallel with C"]
    nodeSeries --> nodeJunc["Wheatstone Bridge Junction"]
    nodeParallel --> nodeJunc
    nodeJunc --> nodeFb["Feedback Voltage Vfb to Non Inverting Input"]
    nodeFb --> nodeNonInv
    nodePhase3["At f0 equals 1 over 2 pi RC Loop Phase is Zero"]
    nodePhase3 --> nodeBark3["Barkhausen Criterion Satisfied A beta equals 1"]
    nodeBark3 --> nodeStable3["Sustained Oscillations with Low Distortion"]
```

---

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Architectural Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeLC["LC Oscillator"] --> nodeLC1["High Frequency RF"]
    nodeLC --> nodeLC2["High Q Factor"]
    nodeLC --> nodeLC3["Tank Circuit Resonance"]
    nodeLC --> nodeLC4["Uses Inductors and Capacitors"]

    nodeRC["RC Oscillator"] --> nodeRC1["Low Frequency Audio"]
    nodeRC --> nodeRC2["Low Q Factor"]
    nodeRC --> nodeRC3["RC Network Phase Shift"]
    nodeRC --> nodeRC4["Uses Resistors and Capacitors Only"]

    nodeLC4 --> nodeShared["Common Elements"]
    nodeRC4 --> nodeShared
    nodeShared --> nodeCommon1["Amplifier Active Device"]
    nodeShared --> nodeCommon2["Feedback Network"]
    nodeShared --> nodeCommon3["Barkhausen Criterion Applied"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 🎯 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an LC oscillator. Mention two examples. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

An LC oscillator is an electronic oscillator that uses a **tank circuit consisting of an inductor (L) and capacitor (C)** in parallel as the frequency-determining network. It generates sustained sinusoidal oscillations at the **resonant frequency** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$, relying on the principle of energy exchange between the magnetic field of L and the electric field of C.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Hartley Oscillator** — uses a tapped inductor.
2. **Colpitts Oscillator** — uses a capacitive divider.

---

### Question 2: State the Barkhausen Criterion for sustained oscillations. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The Barkhausen Criterion states that for sustained oscillations, the loop transfer function of a feedback oscillator must satisfy **two simultaneous conditions:**

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be **unity or greater:**
$$
\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1
$$
where $A$ is the amplifier gain and $\beta$ is the feedback network gain.

2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be **zero or an integer multiple of 360°:**
$$
\angle A \beta = 0° \text{ or } n \times 360°
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At startup, $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ to allow oscillations to build up. Once amplitude stabilizes due to non-linearities, $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.

---

## 5.2 🎯 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### ✍️ Question A: RC Phase Shift Oscillator (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT**. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the frequency of oscillation of an RC phase shift oscillator having $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \mu F$. Also state the minimum gain required for sustained oscillations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Circuit Description:**
The RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- A **common-emitter BJT amplifier** providing 180° phase inversion and sufficient gain.
- A **three-section RC ladder network** at the output that feeds back to the base. Each section contributes **60° phase shift**, totalling **180°**.
- The total loop phase shift is therefore $180° + 180° = 360° = 0°$, satisfying the Barkhausen phase condition.

**Working:**
1. Small noise voltages at the base are amplified and inverted (180°) by the CE amplifier.
2. The output passes through three cascaded RC sections, each introducing 60° lag, summing to 180°.
3. The fed-back signal is therefore in phase with the original input, reinforcing it — sustained oscillations build up.
4. Oscillations stabilize at the frequency where the RC network attenuation is exactly compensated by the amplifier gain.

**Derivation:**

The transfer function of the three-section RC network is:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha(1 - 6\alpha^2)}
$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$.

**Phase condition:** The imaginary part of the denominator must be zero.

$$
1 - 6\alpha^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}
$$

Substituting $\alpha = \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$:

$$
\frac{1}{\omega R C} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \frac{1}{R C \sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}}
$$

**Magnitude condition:** The minimum gain required is $A \geq 29$.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$
- $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu F = 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 10^{-8} \text{ F}$

**Step 1:** Calculate $RC$:

$$
R C = (10 \times 10^3) \times (10^{-8}) = 10^{-3} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the frequency formula:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 2.449}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.449 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{15.39 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 64.97 \text{ Hz}}
$$

**Minimum Gain Required:**

$$
A \geq 29
$$

The BJT amplifier must be biased such that its small-signal voltage gain from base to collector is **at least 29** for sustained oscillations.

---

### ✍️ Question B: LC (Hartley) Oscillator (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **Hartley oscillator using a BJT** and explain its working principle. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Hartley oscillator has $L_1 = 100 \text{ }\mu H$, $L_2 = 100 \text{ }\mu H$, mutual inductance $M = 25 \text{ }\mu H$, and $C = 1000 \text{ pF}$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Circuit Description:**
A Hartley oscillator uses:
- A **BJT in common-emitter configuration** as the active device.
- A **tank circuit** with a **tapped inductor** (L₁ on one side, L₂ on the other side of the tap) and a **single capacitor C**.
- The tap divides the coil such that the feedback signal is taken from the junction of L₂ and the emitter/ground.

**Working Principle:**
1. At the resonant frequency of the LC tank, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, leaving a high impedance at the collector.
2. The voltage developed across L₂ is fed back to the base in phase with the input signal (because the CE amplifier provides 180° inversion, and the tank contributes another 180° between collector and base through the L₁–L₂ tap).
3. The Barkhausen criteria are met: total loop phase = 0° and $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$.

**Derivation:**

The equivalent inductance of the tapped inductor is:

$$
L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M
$$

The resonant frequency of the LC tank is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \cdot C}}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:**
- $L_1 = 100 \text{ }\mu H = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H}$
- $L_2 = 100 \text{ }\mu H = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H}$
- $M = 25 \text{ }\mu H = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H}$
- $C = 1000 \text{ pF} = 1000 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F} = 10^{-9} \text{ F}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the equivalent inductance.

$$
L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M
$$

$$
L_{eq} = 100 \times 10^{-6} + 100 \times 10^{-6} + 2 \times 25 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
L_{eq} = (100 + 100 + 50) \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
L_{eq} = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ H} = 250 \text{ }\mu H
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $L_{eq} \cdot C$.

$$
L_{eq} \cdot C = (250 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10^{-9})
$$

$$
L_{eq} \cdot C = 250 \times 10^{-15} = 2.5 \times 10^{-13}
$$

**Step 3:** Take the square root.

$$
\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C} = \sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the frequency formula.

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 318.3 \text{ kHz}}
$$

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in mutual inductance** — The equivalent inductance in a Hartley oscillator is $(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)$, **not** $(L_1 + L_2 + M)$. The factor 2 is mandatory.
>
> 2. **Missing the $\sqrt{6}$ in RC phase shift formula** — Students often write $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ for the RC phase shift oscillator. This is the **Wien Bridge** formula. The RC phase shift formula has an additional $\sqrt{6}$ in the denominator.
>
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — Failing to convert $\mu H$, $pF$, $nF$ correctly is the **#1 reason for numerical mistakes** in KTU scripts. Always write the conversion explicitly in the solution.
>
> 4. **Confusing Colpitts and Clapp** — In Colpitts, the tank has a **single L and two series capacitors (C₁, C₂)**. In Clapp, there is an **additional C in series with L**. Examiners look for this distinction.
>
> 5. **Not stating the Barkhausen Criterion explicitly** — For any oscillator question worth 7+ marks, the **first thing** to write is the Barkhausen Criterion. Marks are awarded even before the derivation.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the startup condition** — Sustained oscillation requires $A\beta \geq 1$, **not** $A\beta = 1$ at startup. Mentioning both startup and steady-state conditions is the difference between a 7-mark and a full-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Difference Between LC & RC Oscillator"**

- 🔑 **Core Difference:** LC oscillators use an **LC tank circuit (resonance-based)**; RC oscillators use an **RC network (phase-shift or attenuation-based)**.
- 🔑 **Frequency Formula — LC:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$
- 🔑 **Frequency Formula — RC Phase Shift:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$, minimum gain **$A \geq 29$**
- 🔑 **Frequency Formula — Wien Bridge:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, required amplifier gain **$A = 3$**
- 🔑 **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$ **AND** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or $n \times 360°$)
- 🔑 **LC Examples:** Hartley, Colpitts, Clapp, Crystal, Armstrong
- 🔑 **RC Examples:** RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge, Twin-T
- 🔑 **Frequency Range:** LC → kHz–GHz (RF); RC → Hz–MHz (Audio)
- 🔑 **Q-Factor:** LC has **high Q** (50–500+); RC has **low Q** (< 10)
- 🔑 **IC Fabrication:** RC is **IC-friendly**; LC is **not** (inductors resist miniaturization)
- 🔑 **Hartley Uses:** Tapped inductor tank; $\beta$ involves L₁/L₂ ratio
- 🔑 **Colpitts Uses:** Capacitive divider tank; $\beta$ involves C₂/C₁ ratio
- 🔑 **Crystal Oscillator:** Highest frequency stability; uses piezoelectric resonance
- 🔑 **Wien Bridge** is the **most popular** audio oscillator due to low distortion
- 🔑 **At resonance:** $X_L = X_C$, tank impedance is **maximum (parallel)** or **minimum (series)**
- 🔑 **Mutual Inductance M:** Appears in Hartley formula as $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$
- 🔑 **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Derive frequency of oscillation of [Hartley/Colpitts/RC phase shift/Wien Bridge] oscillator" — expect 7–14 marks regularly.
- 🔑 **Conversion tip:** $1 \text{ pF} = 10^{-12} \text{ F}$, $1 \text{ nF} = 10^{-9} \text{ F}$, $1 \text{ }\mu H = 10^{-6} \text{ H}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Amplifiers** refers to the systematic categorization of electronic amplifier circuits based on key operational parameters such as **conduction angle**, **biasing condition**, **operating point position**, **signal frequency range**, and **functional role** within an electronic system. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Analog Electronics, classification primarily focuses on **power amplifiers (large-signal amplifiers)** divided into Class A, Class B, Class AB, and Class C, determined by the portion of the input cycle during which the output transistor conducts.

The **conduction angle (θc)** is defined as the angular measure (in degrees or radians) of the input signal cycle for which the active device (BJT/MOSFET) remains in the ON (conducting) state.

$$\theta_c = 2 \cdot \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{V_{BB} - V_{BE(\text{on})}}{V_{in(\text{peak})}}\right)$$

Where:
- $V_{BB}$ = DC bias voltage
- $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ = Base-emitter turn-on voltage (**0.7 V** for silicon BJT)
- $V_{in(\text{peak})}$ = Peak amplitude of input AC signal

## 1.2 Quick Classification Snapshot

| Class | Conduction Angle ($\theta_c$) | Conduction Period | Q-point Location | Maximum Theoretical Efficiency |
|:-----:|:-----------------------------:|:-----------------:|:----------------:|:------------------------------:|
| **A**  | **360°** (2π rad)            | Full cycle        | Centre of load line | 50% |
| **B**  | **180°** (π rad)             | Half cycle        | Cut-off point     | 78.5% |
| **AB** | **180° < θc < 360°**         | More than half    | Slightly above cut-off | 78.5% (practical ≈ 50–60%) |
| **C**  | **< 180°**                   | Less than half    | Below cut-off     | 100% (theoretical, with tuned load) |
| **D**  | Switched (PWM)               | ON/OFF switching  | Saturation/Cut-off | 90–100% |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a water tap (faucet) connected to a pipe.** 
> - **Class A** is like keeping the tap *half-open at all times*, even when no water is needed — a continuous, smooth flow but enormous wastage.
> - **Class B** is like opening the tap *only when you actively push water forward* — saves energy but you only get half the motion covered.
> - **Class AB** is a *compromise* — the tap remains slightly open so it can respond instantly, covering slightly more than half the cycle.
> - **Class C** is like *pulsing* the tap open for a very short burst — high efficiency, but the output needs to be "smoothed" by a flywheel (resonant tank circuit).
> - **Class D** is a *digital toggle* — fully ON or fully OFF, switching so fast that the average motion equals the desired output.

This analogy maps directly to how transistors dissipate heat — they waste less power when they are either fully ON, fully OFF, or conducting only briefly.

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of Amplifier Classification:**
> - **Hi-Fi audio systems** → Class A or Class AB for low distortion
> - **RF transmitters and radio broadcasting** → Class C for high efficiency
> - **Battery-powered portable devices (smartphones, hearing aids)** → Class D for ultra-low power dissipation
> - **Public address (PA) systems** → Class B push-pull for high output
> - **Satellite and aerospace communication** → Class AB linear amplification
> - **Switch-mode power supplies (SMPS)** → Class D operation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Class A Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

In a **Class A amplifier**, the transistor conducts for the **entire 360°** of the input signal cycle. The Q-point is positioned at the **centre of the DC load line**, ensuring the output device is always in the active region.

**Operating Conditions:**
- DC bias current flows continuously, even with no input signal.
- The output is a faithful, undistorted reproduction of the input.
- Power dissipation is maximum at zero signal condition (quiescent point).

**Why use Class A?**
- Highest linearity → minimum distortion.
- Used as a **voltage amplifier** in small-signal stages.
- Best for high-fidelity audio pre-amplifiers.

**Why avoid Class A for power stages?**
- Very poor efficiency (maximum 50%, practical ≈ 25–30%).
- Excessive heat generation → large heat sinks required.

## 2.2 Class B Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

In a **Class B amplifier**, the transistor conducts for exactly **180°** (half cycle). The Q-point is located at the **cut-off point** of the characteristic curve. When no signal is applied, the transistor draws **zero collector current**.

**Push-Pull Configuration:**
- Two complementary transistors (NPN and PNP) are used.
- Each transistor handles one half-cycle of the input waveform.
- Combined output reproduces the full sine wave.

**Why Class B?**
- High theoretical efficiency of **78.5%** (π/4).
- Zero quiescent power dissipation → cooler operation.

**Drawback — Crossover Distortion:**
- Distortion occurs near the zero-crossing point (when $V_{BE} < 0.7\text{ V}$, neither transistor conducts).
- The output shows a "dead zone" of approximately **1.4 V** (sum of both $V_{BE}$ drops).

## 2.3 Class AB Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

A practical compromise that eliminates crossover distortion while retaining much of Class B's efficiency.

**Operating Conditions:**
- The Q-point is positioned **slightly above cut-off** (small $I_{CQ}$ flows with no input).
- Conduction angle: **180° < θc < 360°**.
- Both transistors conduct for a small overlap region near the zero crossing.

**Why Class AB is the Industry Standard?**
- Eliminates crossover distortion (both devices are ON near zero crossing).
- Efficiency between 50% (Class A) and 78.5% (Class B).
- Used in almost every **audio power amplifier** IC (e.g., LM386, TDA2030, LM3886).

## 2.4 Class C Amplifier — Detailed Analysis

The transistor conducts for **less than 180°** of the input cycle. The Q-point is set **below cut-off** (negative bias for BJT). Output current consists of narrow pulses, which are then converted into a near-sinusoidal waveform by a **parallel resonant (tank) LC circuit**.

**Why Class C?**
- Highest theoretical efficiency → approaches **100%**.
- Excellent for narrow-band, high-frequency applications.

**Limitations:**
- Highly distorted output (only pulses).
- Requires a tuned load to reconstruct the sinusoid.
- Cannot be used for audio amplification (no linearity).

**Typical Applications:**
- RF power amplifiers in transmitters
- IF amplifiers in superheterodyne receivers
- Oscillator circuits
- Frequency multipliers

## 2.5 Class D Amplifier — Brief Overview

The transistor operates as an **electronic switch** (either fully ON in saturation or fully OFF in cut-off). The input signal is converted into a **Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM)** square wave. A low-pass output filter reconstructs the original audio waveform.

**Why Class D?**
- Theoretical efficiency: 90–100%.
- Very low heat generation.
- Compact, lightweight designs.

**Drawbacks:**
- Switching noise (requires careful filter design).
- EMI (electromagnetic interference) generation.
- Output stage transistors must be very fast (low switching losses).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class AB | Class C |
|:----------|:-------:|:-------:|:--------:|:-------:|
| Conduction angle $\theta_c$ | $2\pi$ | $\pi$ | $\pi < \theta_c < 2\pi$ | $\theta_c < \pi$ |
| Q-point location | Centre of load line | Cut-off | Slightly above cut-off | Below cut-off |
| Quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ | $I_{CQ(\max)}$ | $\approx 0$ | Small positive | $\approx 0$ |
| Max. collector current $I_{c(\text{peak})}$ | $I_{CQ}$ | $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L$ | $I_{CQ} + I_m$ | $I_m$ (pulsed) |
| Max. theoretical efficiency $\eta_{\max}$ | $50\%$ | $78.5\%$ | $78.5\%$ | $100\%$ |
| Practical efficiency | $25–30\%$ | $70\%$ | $50–60\%$ | $80–90\%$ |
| Distortion | Very low | High (crossover) | Low | Very high (requires tuning) |
| Power delivered $P_{ac}$ | $V_{CC}^2 / 8R_L$ | $V_{CC}^2 / 8R_L$ | — | Depends on $\theta_c$ |
| DC input power $P_{DC}$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC}^2 / \pi R_L$ | — | — |
| Transistor dissipation $P_D$ | $V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC}^2 / \pi^2 R_L$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Efficiency Formula (Valid for All Classes):**
> $$\eta = \frac{P_{ac(\text{out})}}{P_{DC(\text{in})}} \times 100\%$$
> 
> where 
> $P_{ac(\text{out})}$ = AC signal power delivered to load, 
> $P_{DC(\text{in})}$ = DC power drawn from supply.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Class A Efficiency (Series-Fed Inductive Load)

**Given:**
- Supply voltage = $V_{CC}$
- DC quiescent current = $I_{CQ}$
- Load resistance = $R_L$
- Signal current: $i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t)$

### Step 1: Express the AC Output Power

The instantaneous AC signal current is $i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t)$.

The AC signal power delivered to the load is the mean square value:

$$P_{ac} = \frac{1}{2} I_m^2 \cdot R_L$$

### Step 2: Determine the Maximum Possible $I_m$

For maximum undistorted swing, the peak signal current $I_m$ can equal the quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ without driving the transistor into cut-off or saturation.

$$I_{m(\max)} = I_{CQ}$$

### Step 3: Maximum AC Power

Substituting $I_{m(\max)} = I_{CQ}$:

$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{1}{2} I_{CQ}^2 \cdot R_L$$

### Step 4: DC Input Power

The DC power drawn from the $V_{CC}$ supply (constant current $I_{CQ}$):

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

### Step 5: Apply KVL on the Output Loop

At the Q-point, the collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ} R_L$.

For maximum swing, set $V_{CE} = V_{CC}/2$, which gives $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/(2 R_L)$.

### Step 6: Substitute into the Output Power Equation

$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L}\right)^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_L}$$

### Step 7: Substitute into the DC Power Equation

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

### Step 8: Compute the Efficiency

$$\eta_A = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 / 8 R_L}{V_{CC}^2 / 2 R_L} = \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{A(\max)} = 25\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note: This is the efficiency of a **series-fed** Class A amplifier. The transformer-coupled Class A amplifier can achieve up to **50%** efficiency because the DC current does not flow through the load (DC isolation by the transformer).

## 3.2 Derivation — Class B Efficiency (Push-Pull Configuration)

**Given:**
- Two transistors (Q1: NPN, Q2: PNP) in push-pull
- Supply: $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ (symmetric dual supply)
- Load: $R_L$
- Output: sinusoidal current $i_L = I_m \sin(\omega t)$

### Step 1: AC Power Delivered to Load

The RMS value of the sinusoidal load current is $I_{rms} = I_m / \sqrt{2}$.

$$P_{ac} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{I_m^2}{2} R_L$$

### Step 2: DC Input Power (Average Current from Supply)

Each transistor conducts for half the cycle. The average current drawn from the $V_{CC}$ supply by one transistor over the full period is:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} I_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[-\cos(\omega t)\right]_0^{\pi}$$

$$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(0) \right] = \frac{I_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{I_m}{\pi}$$

### Step 3: DC Power Input

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_m}{\pi}$$

### Step 4: Maximum AC Power (when $I_m = V_{CC}/R_L$)

$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{V_{CC}}{R_L}\right)^2 R_L = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

### Step 5: Substitute into Efficiency Formula

$$\eta_B = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 / 2 R_L}{V_{CC} \cdot I_m / \pi} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi}{V_{CC} \cdot I_m}$$

Substituting $I_m = V_{CC}/R_L$:

$$\eta_B = \frac{V_{CC}^2 \cdot \pi}{2 R_L \cdot V_{CC} \cdot V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{B(\max)} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Class C Efficiency (General Expression)

For a Class C amplifier with conduction angle $\theta_c$, the DC input power and AC output power depend on the duty cycle.

### Step 1: Average Current

$$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\theta_c/2}^{\theta_c/2} I_m \cos(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \sin\left(\frac{\theta_c}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2: Fundamental Component of Output Current

The amplitude of the fundamental (first harmonic) component:

$$I_1 = \frac{I_m}{\pi} \left[\frac{\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)}{1 - \cos(\theta_c/2)}\right]$$

### Step 3: AC Output Power

$$P_{ac} = \frac{1}{2} I_1^2 \cdot R_L$$

### Step 4: Efficiency

$$\eta_C = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} I_1^2 R_L}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}}$$

As $\theta_c \to 0$, the efficiency $\eta_C \to 100\%$.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** Smaller conduction angle = Higher efficiency but more distortion. The trade-off is fundamental in power amplifier design.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class AB | Class C |
|:----------------------|:-------:|:-------:|:--------:|:-------:|
| **Linearity** | Excellent | Poor (crossover) | Very Good | Poor (with tuning) |
| **Power efficiency** | 25–50% | 78.5% | 50–70% | 80–100% |
| **Quiescent dissipation** | High | Zero | Low | Zero |
| **Heat sink requirement** | Very large | Small | Medium | Very small |
| **Output transformer needed** | Often yes | No (direct-coupled) | No | Yes (tank circuit) |
| **Battery life impact** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Distortion (THD)** | < 1% | 10–20% | 0.05–1% | Very high (narrow-band OK) |
| **Best for** | Pre-amps, Hi-Fi | Audio PA | IC audio amps | RF transmitters |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Conduction Angle Visualization (Mermaid Diagram)

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Classify power amplifiers based on conduction angle. List the maximum theoretical efficiency of each class.**

**Model Answer:**

Power amplifiers are classified based on the **conduction angle** ($\theta_c$) of the output transistor:

| Class | Conduction Angle | Q-point | Max. Theoretical Efficiency |
|:-----:|:----------------:|:-------:|:---------------------------:|
| A     | 360° (2π)        | Centre of load line | 50% (transformer-coupled), 25% (series-fed) |
| B     | 180° (π)         | Cut-off | 78.5% (π/4) |
| AB    | 180° < θc < 360° | Slightly above cut-off | 78.5% |
| C     | < 180°           | Below cut-off | ~100% (with tuned load) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key memory aid:** The efficiency of Class B is $\pi/4 \approx 0.785$, often remembered as the **"pi-by-four" rule**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is crossover distortion? In which class of amplifier does it occur, and how is it eliminated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Crossover distortion** is a non-linearity in the output waveform that occurs when neither of the two push-pull transistors is conducting (in the region where $-V_{BE(\text{on})} < V_{in} < +V_{BE(\text{on})}$).

- **Occurs in:** Class B push-pull amplifiers
- **Cause:** The base-emitter turn-on voltage (**0.7 V for Si BJT**) must be exceeded before a transistor begins to conduct. Near zero-crossing, both transistors are OFF, leaving a "dead zone" in the output.
- **Magnitude of dead zone:** approximately **1.4 V** (2 × $V_{BE(\text{on})}$)

**Methods to Eliminate Crossover Distortion:**

1. **Use Class AB biasing** — Apply a small forward bias to keep both transistors slightly ON at zero input. This overlap ensures continuous conduction across the zero-crossing.
2. **Use diodes for bias compensation** — Connect two silicon diodes in series between the bases, providing a stable ~1.4 V bias that tracks temperature.
3. **Use $V_{BE}$ multiplier circuit** — A transistor-based voltage reference to provide the required bias.
4. **Use MOSFETs instead of BJTs** — MOSFETs have a near-zero threshold, reducing the dead zone.
5. **Apply negative feedback** — Reduces distortion by feeding back a portion of the output.

---

## **Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Class A transformer-coupled power amplifier**. Derive the expression for its maximum efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the operation of a **Class B push-pull amplifier** with a suitable diagram. Derive its maximum theoretical efficiency. Compare Class A and Class B in terms of efficiency and distortion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Operation:**

A Class A transformer-coupled amplifier uses a transformer at the collector to couple the output signal to the load. The primary winding of the transformer is connected in the collector circuit, and the load $R_L$ is connected to the secondary.

**Key Features:**
- The Q-point is set at the **centre of the DC load line**.
- The transformer blocks DC, so the quiescent DC current does not flow through the load $R_L$.
- This prevents DC power loss in the load → higher efficiency.
- The transformer also provides **impedance matching** between the high-impedance collector circuit and the low-impedance load.

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency:**

Let $V_{CC}$ be the supply voltage, $I_{CQ}$ the quiescent collector current, and $R_L'$ the effective load referred to the primary.

The maximum AC power delivered to the load:

$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'}$$

The DC power drawn from the supply:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

For maximum symmetrical swing, the Q-point is at the middle of the load line, so $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ and $I_{CQ} = V_{CC} / (2 R_L')$.

Substituting:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L'} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{A(\max)} = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 / 2 R_L'}{V_{CC}^2 / 2 R_L'} = 1 \text{ (ideal)}$$

In practice, considering transformer losses and voltage drops:

$$\boxed{\eta_{A(\max)} = 50\%}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Operation of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier:**

The circuit consists of two complementary transistors (Q1: NPN, Q2: PNP) connected in a push-pull configuration. The emitters are joined together and feed the load $R_L$. The bases receive the same input signal.

- **During the positive half-cycle** of $V_{in}$: Q1 (NPN) conducts, Q2 (PNP) is OFF. Current flows from $V_{CC}$ through Q1 to the load.
- **During the negative half-cycle** of $V_{in}$: Q2 (PNP) conducts, Q1 (NPN) is OFF. Current flows from ground through the load and into Q2 to $-V_{CC}$.
- Each transistor amplifies **one half-cycle**, and the combined output reconstructs the full sine wave across $R_L$.

**Derivation of Maximum Efficiency:**

The peak load current: $I_m = V_{CC} / R_L$

AC output power:

$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{1}{2} I_m^2 R_L = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$$

Average DC current from $V_{CC}$ supply:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{I_m}{\pi} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}$$

DC input power:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$$

Maximum efficiency:

$$\boxed{\eta_{B(\max)} = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%}$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Class A | Class B |
|:----------|:-------:|:-------:|
| Maximum efficiency | 50% | 78.5% |
| Distortion | Very low | High (crossover distortion) |
| Quiescent dissipation | High | Zero |
| Heat sink | Large | Small |
| Power transistors used | 1 | 2 |
| Coupling | Transformer | Direct |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a circuit diagram and waveform, explain the operation of a **Class C amplifier**. Derive the expression for its maximum efficiency. Why is it preferred for RF applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Class AB operation** in detail. With the help of a circuit diagram, show how crossover distortion is eliminated using diode biasing. What are the advantages of Class AB over Class A and Class B? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Class C Amplifier Operation:**

In a Class C amplifier, the transistor is biased such that the base-emitter junction is **reverse-biased** under quiescent conditions (Q-point is below cut-off). The transistor conducts only when the input signal exceeds the cut-in voltage plus the reverse bias.

**Circuit Description:**
- A reverse bias voltage $V_{BB} < 0$ is applied to the base.
- The collector is connected to a **parallel LC tank circuit** (resonant at the desired output frequency).
- During the small portion of the cycle when $V_{in} > \vert V_{BB} \vert + V_{BE(\text{on})}$, the transistor conducts and delivers a **narrow current pulse** to the tank circuit.
- The LC tank "rings" at its resonant frequency, sustaining a sinusoidal output even between pulses.

**Waveform Explanation:**
- **Input:** Continuous sinusoid
- **Collector current:** Narrow pulses (conduction angle < 180°)
- **Output voltage:** Nearly pure sinusoid (reconstructed by the resonant tank)

**Derivation of Efficiency:**

For a Class C amplifier with conduction angle $\theta_c$, the efficiency increases as $\theta_c$ decreases. The general expression is:

$$\eta_C = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\theta_c - \sin(\theta_c)}{2 \sin(\theta_c/2) - \theta_c \cos(\theta_c/2)}$$

As $\theta_c \to 0$:

$$\boxed{\eta_{C(\max)} \to 100\%}$$

For a typical $\theta_c = 60°$, $\eta_C \approx 85\%$.

**Why Preferred for RF Applications:**
1. **High efficiency** → Less battery drain and less heat (critical for transmitters).
2. **Large output power** possible for a given transistor rating.
3. **Tuned load** naturally filters harmonics → clean sinusoidal output.
4. **Can be used as a frequency multiplier** by tuning the tank to a harmonic.
5. **Compact design** → small heat sinks.

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for amplitude-varying (AM) signals.
- Requires a tuned load (limited to narrow band).
- Poor linearity → high distortion.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Class AB Operation:**

A Class AB amplifier is a **compromise** between Class A and Class B. The Q-point is set **slightly above cut-off**, so a small quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ flows through both transistors in the absence of input signal.

**Conduction:**
- Each transistor conducts for **more than 180°** of the input cycle.
- There is a small **overlap region** near the zero-crossing where both transistors conduct simultaneously.
- This overlap eliminates the dead zone (crossover distortion) present in Class B.

**Diode Biasing Circuit to Eliminate Crossover Distortion:**

A common technique uses **two silicon diodes** (D1 and D2) connected in series between the bases of the NPN and PNP transistors.

```
       +VCC
        |
        +---- Collector of Q1 (NPN) ----+
        |                                |
        |                                |
       D1                               D2
        |                                |
        +---- Base of Q1 (NPN)          +---- Base of Q2 (PNP)
        |                                |
        +---- Input Signal (Vin)        +
        |                                |
        +---- Emitter of Q1 ----+------ Emitter of Q2
                                |
                                R (small emitter resistor)
                                |
                                RL (Load)
                                |
                               GND
```

**How It Works:**
- The two diodes develop a voltage drop of $2 \times V_D \approx 1.4$ V across their terminals.
- This forward-biases both Q1 and Q2 slightly, setting them at the edge of conduction.
- Since the diode drop tracks the $V_{BE}$ drop with temperature, the bias remains stable.
- When $V_{in}$ swings positive by 0.7 V, Q1 turns fully ON. When it swings negative by 0.7 V, Q2 turns fully ON.
- Near the zero-crossing, both are partially ON → smooth transition, **no dead zone**.

**Advantages of Class AB over Class A and Class B:**

| Feature | Class A | Class B | Class AB |
|:--------|:-------:|:-------:|:--------:|
| Crossover distortion | None | Severe | None |
| Efficiency | 25–50% | 78.5% | 50–70% |
| Quiescent dissipation | High | Zero | Low |
| Linearity | Best | Worst | Good |
| Heat sink | Largest | Smallest | Medium |
| **Verdict** | High fidelity, low power | Distorted, high power | **Best trade-off** |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show all derivation steps** — Partial derivations lose 3–4 marks. Always start from $P_{ac}$, $P_{DC}$, and substitute systematically.
> 2. **Confusing efficiency formulas** — Class A series-fed is 25%, transformer-coupled is 50%. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Missing units and assumptions** — Always state the assumption (e.g., "assuming ideal transformer" or "with sinusoidal input").
> 4. **Not drawing input/output waveforms** — A complete answer requires waveforms showing the conduction region for each class.
> 5. **Forgetting the cross-over distortion explanation** — Just mentioning "distortion" without explaining *why* it occurs costs 2 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up Class C with Class B** — Class C has $\theta_c < 180°$ and requires a tuned load; Class B has $\theta_c = 180°$ and uses push-pull configuration.
> 7. **Not stating the Q-point position explicitly** — This is a favourite 1-mark sub-question; always mention "centre of load line" or "cut-off" or "below cut-off".
> 8. **Ignoring the input signal amplitude condition** — Always specify whether $I_m = I_{CQ}$ (for max swing) or some other condition.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

✅ **Conduction angle ($\theta_c$)** is the defining parameter for amplifier classification.
- Class A: $\theta_c = 360°$
- Class B: $\theta_c = 180°$
- Class AB: $180° < \theta_c < 360°$
- Class C: $\theta_c < 180°$

✅ **Efficiency order** (best to worst): Class D (≈100%) > Class C (≈100%) > Class B (78.5%) > Class AB (50–70%) > Class A (25–50%)

✅ **Maximum efficiency formulas (memorize these):**
- Class A (series-fed): $\eta = 25\%$
- Class A (transformer): $\eta = 50\%$
- Class B: $\eta = \pi/4 = 78.5\%$
- Class C: $\eta \to 100\%$ as $\theta_c \to 0$

✅ **Q-point position is diagnostic** — It tells you the class of an amplifier at a glance.

✅ **Crossover distortion is the Class B trademark** — and the reason Class AB exists.

✅ **Class C must always have a tuned (resonant) load** — never use resistive load.

✅ **Class AB is the universal choice** for audio power amplifiers (LM386, TDA2030, etc.).

✅ **Efficiency-Distortion Trade-off:**
- Higher efficiency → More distortion (Class C)
- Lower distortion → Lower efficiency (Class A)
- Class AB is the engineering compromise

✅ **Selection rule of thumb:**
- Hi-Fi audio → Class A or Class AB
- RF transmitters → Class C
- Battery-powered audio → Class D
- General-purpose audio PA → Class B push-pull or Class AB

✅ **Conversion tip for problems:** Always express AC power as $P_{ac} = V_{rms}^2 / R_L = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_L$ and DC power as $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$. Efficiency = $P_{ac}/P_{DC}$.

✅ **Common KTU question pattern:** "Compare Class A and Class B amplifiers" (3 marks) or "Derive the efficiency of Class B push-pull amplifier" (7 marks).

✅ **Exam tip:** Draw the waveform showing the conduction region and Q-point position for each class — it is worth 1–2 easy marks and demonstrates clear understanding.

✅ **Two-transistor rule:** Any push-pull amplifier (Class B, AB) uses **one NPN + one PNP** (complementary symmetry) or **two identical NPNs** with a centre-tapped transformer input.

✅ **For Class C**: The current waveform is **not sinusoidal**; it consists of **pulses**. The voltage waveform is sinusoidal due to the LC tank.

✅ **For Class D**: It is a **switching amplifier** (PWM-based), not a linear amplifier. Efficiency comes from transistors being either fully ON or fully OFF.

✅ **Last-minute numerical problem practice:**
- If $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_L = 8$ Ω, find Class B output power:
  $P_{ac(\max)} = V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_L) = 144/16 = 9$ W
- If $I_{CQ} = 100$ mA, $V_{CC} = 20$ V, find Class A DC power:
  $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2$ W

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
