# 📘 Module 2 – Working of Hartley and Colpitts Oscillator (Using BJT)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hartley Oscillator (KTU Definition):**
> A **Hartley Oscillator** is a sinusoidal LC feedback oscillator that employs a **tank circuit consisting of two inductors (L₁ and L₂) in series with a single shunt capacitor (C)**. When built around a **BJT in Common-Emitter (CE) configuration**, the transistor provides 180° phase inversion, and the tapped-inductor tank provides the additional 180° to satisfy the **Barkhausen Criterion**, producing sustained sine-wave oscillations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Colpitts Oscillator (KTU Definition):**
> A **Colpitts Oscillator** is a sinusoidal LC feedback oscillator whose tank circuit uses a **single inductor (L) shunted by a capacitive voltage divider formed by two series capacitors (C₁ and C₂)**. The CE-mode BJT supplies 180° of phase shift, and the tank network contributes the remaining 180°, making the total loop phase shift equal to 0° (or 360°).

---

### 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **swing in a playground**:
- You give it a small push at the *right moment* (this is the **feedback** that is in-phase with the swing's motion).
- The swing's natural pendulum length (analogous to the **LC tank** in an oscillator) sets the **frequency** at which it oscillates back and forth.
- If your push is too weak, the swing eventually dies down — this is the **damped condition** (βA < 1).
- If you push *too hard* at the wrong time, the swing becomes erratic — this represents an **unstable / distorted oscillation**.
- A perfect, rhythmic push that exactly compensates for friction makes the swing swing forever — this is the **Barkhausen Criterion** $\beta A = 1$.

**In a Hartley oscillator**, the swing's "rope" is split into two segments **L₁** and **L₂** (the inductors), and the stiffness comes from the **spring-like capacitor C**. Energy swaps back and forth between the magnetic field of the inductors and the electric field of the capacitor.

**In a Colpitts oscillator**, the rope is one single piece **L**, but the "hand-grip" is split into two **capacitive plates C₁** and **C₂** that share the charge. The same magnetic ↔ electric field exchange happens, but the frequency-setting is dominated by the equivalent series capacitance.

---

### 🔑 Barkhausen Criterion — The Heart of Every Oscillator

For any sinusoidal oscillator to produce **sustained oscillations** at frequency $f_0$, the following two conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Loop-Gain Condition** | $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$ | The amplifier + feedback network must be able to *exactly compensate* the losses of the tank circuit. |
| **Loop-Phase Condition** | $\angle A \beta = 2\pi n$ where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | The total phase shift around the loop must be **0° (or a multiple of 360°)** so that the feedback is **positive (regenerative)**. |

> [!NOTE]
> For a **BJT in CE configuration**, the amplifier introduces a phase inversion of **180°** (because $V_{CE} = -I_C \cdot R_C$). Therefore, the LC tank circuit must contribute **another 180°** to make the total loop phase shift = **360° = 0°**. This is precisely why LC oscillators use *reactive* (lossless at resonance) tank networks.

---

### 📌 Standard Metrics & Constants (used throughout this module)

- **Resonant frequency symbol:** $f_0$ in **Hz** (or Hz, kHz, MHz depending on L, C values)
- **BJT small-signal current gain:** $h_{fe}$ (dimensionless, typically 50 – 400)
- **Tank quality factor:** $Q$ (dimensionless, often **≥ 10** for clean oscillations)
- **Mutual coupling coefficient:** $k$ where $0 \leq k \leq 1$

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Hartley Oscillator Using BJT

The Hartley oscillator uses a **BJT in Common-Emitter mode** as the active amplifying element. The tank circuit consists of **two inductors L₁ and L₂ (magnetically coupled, with mutual inductance M) in series, and a shunt capacitor C**.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Power-on transient:** When DC supply $V_{CC}$ is switched ON, a small noise current appears in the tank circuit. This excites the **natural resonance** of the L-C network at $f_0$.
2. **Tank resonance:** At $f = f_0$, the inductive reactance cancels the capacitive reactance, leaving a **pure resistive impedance** — the tank is now a "tuned resistor" with very high magnitude (ideally infinite for a lossless tank).
3. **Amplification by BJT:** This resonant voltage appears at the **base** of the BJT. The CE amplifier provides voltage gain $\vert A_v \vert$ and **inverts** the phase by 180°.
4. **Feedback through tapped inductor:** The output at the **collector** is coupled back to the tank. Because the inductors L₁ and L₂ form an **autotransformer (tapped inductor)**, the feedback voltage is taken from the junction between L₁ and L₂. The voltage ratio L₂ : L₁ determines the **feedback fraction β**.
5. **Phase shift from tank:** The parallel-tuned tank presents a **resistive load at resonance**, and due to its parallel-resonant nature, the voltage fed back is **180° out of phase** with the output — adding the second 180° to the 180° from the BJT = 360° = 0°.
6. **Sustained oscillation:** Once $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$ is achieved, the oscillation amplitude grows until non-linearity of the BJT saturates it, and steady sinusoidal oscillation is established at $f_0$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why two inductors?** The split inductor provides a *natural voltage divider* that determines the feedback ratio **β = L₂ / L₁** without needing an additional transformer. This makes the circuit compact and cheap.

### Why It Works — The "Why & How" Insight

- The **transistor's role** is to *amplify* the tiny tank voltage to overcome resistive losses.
- The **tank's role** is to *select* the frequency of oscillation and *phase-shift* the feedback by 180°.
- The **feedback network's role** is to *inject* a portion of the output back to the input with the correct phase.

---

## 2.2 Working Principle — Colpitts Oscillator Using BJT

The Colpitts oscillator replaces the split-inductor with a **capacitive voltage divider (C₁ and C₂)**. The tank contains a single inductor L and two capacitors C₁, C₂ in series.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Tank circuit topology:** L is in parallel with the **series combination** of C₁ and C₂. The equivalent capacitance is $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.
2. **Resonance:** At frequency $f_0$, the tank exhibits **maximum impedance** (parallel resonance), and the voltage at the collector builds up.
3. **Phase shift:** At parallel resonance, the tank impedance is purely resistive, contributing **0° (or 180°, depending on topology)** of phase shift. The CE BJT provides 180°, and the capacitive divider provides the necessary 180° through its loading effect.
4. **Feedback ratio:** Voltage across C₂ (fed back to emitter) divided by voltage across C₁ (at output) gives $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$.
5. **Condition for sustained oscillations:** $h_{fe} \geq \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ (for a basic CE BJT configuration).

> [!TIP]
> **Why prefer Colpitts over Hartley?** Capacitors are *cheaper, smaller, and more stable* than inductors. The Colpitts oscillator is therefore easier to integrate and less sensitive to stray magnetic fields.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these formulas. They appear in **every KTU exam** on this topic.

### **Hartley Oscillator**

| Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| **Frequency of Oscillation** (with mutual coupling) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \, C}}$ | $M$ = mutual inductance, $0 \leq k \leq 1$, $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ |
| **Frequency** (no mutual coupling) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2) \, C}}$ | Used when $M \approx 0$ (spatially separated coils) |
| **Feedback factor** | $\beta = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ (or with M: $\beta = \dfrac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}$) | Dimensionless |
| **Condition for sustained oscillations (BJT-CE)** | $h_{fe} \geq \dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{r_e}{R_C} \approx \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | For $R_C = r_e$, simplifies to $h_{fe} \geq L_1 / L_2$ |

### **Colpitts Oscillator**

| Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|----------|---------|------------------|
| **Equivalent Tank Capacitance** | $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | Series combination of C₁ and C₂ |
| **Frequency of Oscillation** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \, C_{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{L \, C_1 C_2}}$ | Most-used form in KTU problems |
| **Feedback factor** | $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| **Condition for sustained oscillations (BJT-CE)** | $h_{fe} \geq \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ | Simplifies with emitter biasing |

### **Common — Barkhausen Criterion**

| Condition | Formula |
|-----------|---------|
| Magnitude | $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ (sustained), $\vert A \beta \vert > 1$ (growing → amplitude-limited) |
| Phase | $\angle A \beta = 0°$ or $360°$ (positive feedback) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Oscillator Type Used | Why? |
|-------------|----------------------|------|
| **AM Broadcast Transmitters** (550 kHz – 1.6 MHz) | Hartley | High power, frequency tunable by varying C |
| **RF Signal Generators** | Colpitts | Stable, low-cost, moderate Q |
| **Crystal-replacement oscillators (Pierce variant)** | Colpitts-derived | Excellent frequency stability |
| **Local Oscillators in Superheterodyne Receivers** | Colpitts | Miniaturization via integrated capacitors |
| **Function generators (sine wave section)** | Both (Wien + LC variants) | Wide frequency range |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading instructions:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. Do not skip transitions. Mermaid diagrams and shortcuts are deliberately avoided in this section to keep the math auditor-friendly for KTU board exams.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Frequency of Oscillation for Hartley Oscillator (BJT-CE)

### Starting Setup

Consider the tank circuit of a Hartley oscillator. Two inductors L₁ and L₂ are connected in series, with mutual coupling M. A capacitor C is connected across the series combination. The resonant frequency of a series-LC circuit is given by $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_{T} C}}$ where $L_T$ is the **total equivalent inductance**.

### Step 1: Total Inductance of Magnetically Coupled Coils

When two coils share mutual inductance M, the **total series inductance** is:

$$
L_T = L_1 + L_2 \pm 2M
$$

The sign of $2M$ depends on whether the flux aids or opposes. In a properly designed Hartley tank, the coils are wound such that fluxes **aid** (aiding coupling), so:

$$
L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M
$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Series Resonant Formula

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_T \cdot C}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \cdot C}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *exact* expression. The mutual inductance M makes the effective inductance **larger**, hence the frequency **lower**.

### Step 3: Derivation of the Feedback Factor (β)

In the tank, L₁ and L₂ act as an **autotransformer**. The output voltage $V_o$ is developed across L₁, and the feedback voltage $V_f$ is developed across L₂. Since the same current $I$ flows through both inductors:

$$
V_o = j \omega (L_1 + M) I
$$

$$
V_f = j \omega (L_2 + M) I
$$

The feedback factor is the **ratio** of feedback voltage to output voltage:

$$
\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{j \omega (L_2 + M) I}{j \omega (L_1 + M) I}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M}
$$

### Step 4: Condition for Sustained Oscillation (Barkhausen Magnitude)

For a BJT in CE configuration, the voltage gain magnitude is:

$$
\vert A_v \vert = \frac{h_{fe} \cdot R_C}{r_e}
$$

where $r_e = \dfrac{V_T}{I_E} \approx \dfrac{26 \,\text{mV}}{I_E}$ is the intrinsic emitter resistance. Applying $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$:

$$
\frac{h_{fe} \cdot R_C}{r_e} \cdot \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M} = 1
$$

$$
h_{fe} = \frac{r_e}{R_C} \cdot \frac{L_1 + M}{L_2 + M}
$$

In the **idealized approximation** $R_C = r_e$ (i.e., tank loaded such that effective load resistance equals intrinsic emitter resistance):

$$
h_{fe} \geq \frac{L_1 + M}{L_2 + M} \approx \frac{L_1}{L_2} \quad (\text{when } M \text{ is small})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** If $L_1$ is much larger than $L_2$, the feedback is weak and the BJT must have a *high* $h_{fe}$ to compensate. Most practical Hartley designs choose $L_1 \approx L_2$ to keep $h_{fe}$ requirements modest (≈ 50).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Frequency of Oscillation for Colpitts Oscillator (BJT-CE)

### Starting Setup

The Colpitts tank has inductor L in parallel with a series combination of C₁ and C₂. The equivalent capacitance is:

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

### Step 1: Apply Parallel Resonance Formula

For a parallel-LC circuit, the resonant frequency is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute $C_{eq}$ and Simplify

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L \cdot C_1 C_2}}
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for Colpitts frequency.

### Step 3: Derive the Feedback Factor (β)

In a Colpitts tank, the **same current** $I$ flows through C₁ and C₂ (they are in series). The voltage across C₁ is the output, and the voltage across C₂ is fed back to the emitter:

$$
V_o = \frac{I}{j \omega C_1}
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{I}{j \omega C_2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{I / (j \omega C_2)}{I / (j \omega C_1)} = \frac{C_1}{C_2}
$$

### Step 4: Condition for Sustained Oscillation

Applying $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ with $A = \dfrac{h_{fe} R_C}{r_e}$:

$$
\frac{h_{fe} R_C}{r_e} \cdot \frac{C_1}{C_2} = 1
$$

$$
h_{fe} = \frac{r_e}{R_C} \cdot \frac{C_2}{C_1}
$$

In the simplified form (when tank loading balances emitter resistance):

$$
h_{fe} \geq \frac{C_2}{C_1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** To make the Colpitts oscillator easy to start, we typically choose $C_1 \ll C_2$, which makes $\beta$ small but requires a BJT with *high* $h_{fe}$. Conversely, $C_1 \approx C_2$ gives $\beta \approx 1$ and works with ordinary BJTs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Colpitts

**Problem:** A Colpitts oscillator uses $C_1 = 1 \,\text{nF}$, $C_2 = 10 \,\text{nF}$, and $L = 100 \,\mu\text{H}$. Find the frequency of oscillation, the feedback factor, and the minimum $h_{fe}$ required.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Capacitance:**

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-9})(10 \times 10^{-9})}{(1 + 10) \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-18}}{11 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{10}{11} \times 10^{-9} \approx 0.909 \,\text{nF}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(100 \times 10^{-6})(0.909 \times 10^{-9})}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{9.09 \times 10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (3.015 \times 10^{-7})}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{1.894 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 527.9 \,\text{kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Factor:**

$$
\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_2} = \frac{1 \,\text{nF}}{10 \,\text{nF}} = 0.1
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum $h_{fe}$:**

$$
h_{fe,\min} = \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{10}{1} = 10
$$

✅ **Result:** $f_0 \approx 528 \,\text{kHz}$, $\beta = 0.1$, and a BJT with $h_{fe} \geq 10$ will sustain oscillations.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, complex analog circuit drawings are rendered as **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** diagrams using Mermaid. The diagrams below trace the *signal path* of the actual Hartley and Colpitts oscillator circuits and preserve all functional blocks (BJT amplifier, LC tank, feedback path).

---

## 4.1 Hartley Oscillator — Functional Architecture (Using BJT-CE)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[DC Supply VCC] --> BIAS[Biasing Network R1 R2 RE]
    BIAS --> BJT[BJT in Common Emitter Configuration]
    BJT -->|Amplified and 180 deg phase shifted| TANK1[Tank Circuit L1 in series with L2 and shunt C]
    TANK1 -->|Resonant frequency f0| TANK1
    TANK1 -->|Tapped feedback voltage Vf| FB1[Feedback Node between L1 and L2]
    FB1 -->|In phase regenerative feedback| BJT
    BJT -->|Sustained sinusoidal output Vout| OUT1[Output Terminal]

    subgraph TANK_BLOCK1["LC TANK NETWORK"]
      L1COIL[Inductor L1] --- L2COIL[Inductor L2 with mutual M]
      L2COIL --- SHUNTC[Shunt Capacitor C]
    end

    TANK1 --- TANK_BLOCK1
```

### Component-Level Signal Flow Explanation

1. **BIAS** sets the Q-point of the BJT in the active region.
2. **BJT** amplifies the small AC base voltage and shifts its phase by 180°.
3. **TANK_BLOCK1** (L₁–L₂–C) sets the oscillation frequency and provides the second 180° phase shift at parallel resonance.
4. **FB1** taps a fraction of the output voltage (β = L₂/L₁) and feeds it back to the base.
5. **OUT1** delivers the sustained sine wave.

---

## 4.2 Colpitts Oscillator — Functional Architecture (Using BJT-CE)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC2[DC Supply VCC] --> BIAS2[Biasing Network R1 R2 RE]
    BIAS2 --> BJT2[BJT in Common Emitter Configuration]
    BJT2 -->|Amplified and 180 deg phase shifted| TANK2[Tank Circuit Inductor L with Capacitive Divider C1 and C2]
    TANK2 -->|Resonant frequency f0| TANK2
    TANK2 -->|Feedback voltage Vf across C2| FB2[Feedback Node at junction of C1 and C2]
    FB2 -->|In phase regenerative feedback to emitter or base| BJT2
    BJT2 -->|Sustained sinusoidal output Vout| OUT2[Output Terminal]

    subgraph TANK_BLOCK2["LC TANK NETWORK"]
      LCOIL[Inductor L] --- CCAP1[Capacitor C1]
      CCAP1 --- CCAP2[Capacitor C2]
      CCAP2 --- LCOIL
    end

    TANK2 --- TANK_BLOCK2
```

### Component-Level Signal Flow Explanation

1. **BIAS2** stabilizes the BJT's operating point.
2. **BJT2** provides amplification and 180° phase inversion.
3. **TANK_BLOCK2** (L–C₁–C₂) sets the resonant frequency via $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$.
4. **FB2** taps the voltage across C₂ (proportional to C₁/C₂) and returns it to the input.
5. **OUT2** delivers the sustained sinusoid.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Hartley vs. Colpitts (KTU High-Yield)

| Feature | Hartley Oscillator | Colpitts Oscillator |
|---------|--------------------|----------------------|
| Tank elements | 2 inductors (L₁, L₂) + 1 capacitor (C) | 1 inductor (L) + 2 capacitors (C₁, C₂) |
| Frequency formula | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2+2M)C}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1+C_2}{L C_1 C_2}}$ |
| Feedback factor | $\beta = L_2 / L_1$ | $\beta = C_1 / C_2$ |
| BJT $h_{fe}$ condition | $h_{fe} \geq L_1 / L_2$ | $h_{fe} \geq C_2 / C_1$ |
| Frequency tuning | By varying **C** (single capacitor) | By varying **both C₁ and C₂** simultaneously (ganged tuning) |
| Stray magnetic field | **Susceptible** (large inductors) | **Less susceptible** (small inductor, dominant capacitance) |
| Stability | Moderate | **Better** (capacitors are more stable than inductors) |
| Typical frequency range | Audio to low RF (≤ 30 MHz) | RF to VHF (1 MHz – 500 MHz) |
| Application | AM transmitters, signal generators | Local oscillators, RF mixers, crystal-replacement designs |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mutual inductance sign confusion** — Students often write $L_T = L_1 + L_2 - 2M$ for Hartley and *lose 2 marks* in the frequency derivation. The correct sign is **+ 2M** for *aiding* coupling (which is the standard configuration).
> 2. **Inverted feedback factor** — Some students write $\beta = L_1/L_2$ for Hartley. The correct ratio is $L_2/L_1$ because the feedback voltage is across L₂ and the output is across L₁.
> 3. **Forgetting C_eq in Colpitts** — Writing $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{L C_1})$ instead of using $C_{eq} = C_1 C_2 / (C_1 + C_2)$ is the #1 cause of wrong numerical answers.
> 4. **Phase condition ignored** — KTU examiners *love* to deduct marks if you do not explicitly state the **Barkhausen phase condition** ($\angle A\beta = 0°$).
> 5. **Units of $h_{fe}$** — $h_{fe}$ is *dimensionless*; never write "Hz" or "ohms" against it.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Mention its two essential conditions.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Barkhausen criterion specifies the two conditions for sustained sinusoidal oscillations in a feedback oscillator:
1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity, i.e., $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$ (for steady-state sustained oscillations). At start-up, $\vert A \beta \vert > 1$ to allow oscillations to build up.
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the feedback loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (i.e., $0°$ or $2\pi n$ radians). This ensures the feedback is *positive* (regenerative).
If both conditions are satisfied simultaneously at some frequency $f_0$, the circuit oscillates at $f_0$ with a constant amplitude.

---

### **Q2. Compare Hartley and Colpitts oscillators on the basis of tank circuit elements, feedback factor, and frequency stability.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hartley | Colpitts |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Tank circuit | 2 inductors + 1 capacitor | 1 inductor + 2 capacitors |
| Feedback factor $\beta$ | $L_2 / L_1$ | $C_1 / C_2$ |
| Frequency stability | Moderate (inductors drift with temperature) | Better (capacitors are thermally more stable) |
| Susceptibility to stray fields | High | Low |

Colpitts is generally preferred for **higher frequency and better stability**, while Hartley is preferred where **easy tuning via a single capacitor** is desired.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**). Students are given an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions on a module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Hartley Oscillator Focus*

#### **(a) With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a Hartley oscillator using BJT. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A Hartley oscillator consists of:
- A BJT in **Common-Emitter (CE)** configuration with biasing resistors $R_1, R_2, R_E$ and emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$.
- A **tank circuit** comprising two series inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$ (with mutual coupling $M$) and a shunt capacitor $C$.
- The collector of the BJT is connected to one end of the tank, and the base is connected to the *tapping point* between $L_1$ and $L_2$.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- When $V_{CC}$ is switched ON, a small noise voltage develops across the tank.
- The tank resonates at $f_0$, producing a large sinusoidal voltage across it.
- The BJT in CE provides voltage gain $A$ and a phase inversion of **180°**.
- The tapped inductor network (L₁–L₂) acts as an *autotransformer* and feeds back a voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$ to the base, where $\beta = L_2/L_1$ (assuming aiding mutual coupling $M$).
- The tank contributes the remaining **180°** at parallel resonance, satisfying $\angle A\beta = 360° = 0°$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Frequency (3 marks):**
The total inductance of the two coupled coils is:

$$
L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M
$$

The resonant frequency of the series LC tank is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_T \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M) \, C}}
$$

**Condition for sustained oscillation:**

$$
h_{fe} \geq \frac{L_1}{L_2} \quad \text{(simplified, no mutual coupling)}
$$

---

#### **(b) A Hartley oscillator is designed with $L_1 = 100 \,\mu\text{H}$, $L_2 = 1 \,\text{mH}$, mutual inductance $M = 50 \,\mu\text{H}$, and $C = 1000 \,\text{pF}$. Determine the frequency of oscillation and the feedback factor. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Jul 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Inductance (2 marks):**

$$
L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M = (100 \times 10^{-6}) + (1 \times 10^{-3}) + 2 \times (50 \times 10^{-6})
$$

$$
L_T = 100 \times 10^{-6} + 1000 \times 10^{-6} + 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1200 \times 10^{-6} = 1.2 \,\text{mH}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of Oscillation (3 marks):**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L_T \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(1.2 \times 10^{-3})(1000 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{1.2 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (1.0954 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{6.881 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 145.3 \,\text{kHz}}
$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Factor (2 marks):**

$$
\beta = \frac{L_2 + M}{L_1 + M} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-3}) + (50 \times 10^{-6})}{(100 \times 10^{-6}) + (50 \times 10^{-6})}
$$

$$
\beta = \frac{1050 \times 10^{-6}}{150 \times 10^{-6}} = 7
$$

**Step 4 — Required $h_{fe}$ (bonus, 1 mark):**

$$
h_{fe,\min} = \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.143
$$

→ Since real BJTs have $h_{fe} \gg 1$, the oscillation will *easily* start.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Colpitts Oscillator Focus*

#### **(a) With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a Colpitts oscillator using BJT. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A Colpitts oscillator consists of:
- A BJT in **CE configuration** with standard biasing.
- A **tank circuit** with inductor $L$ in parallel with a **capacitive voltage divider** $C_1$–$C_2$.
- The collector of the BJT connects to the top of $L$ and the top of $C_1$.
- The base (or emitter) connects to the junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$ for feedback.

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- On power-on, the tank receives a small disturbance and oscillates at $f_0$.
- The BJT (CE) amplifies the tank voltage and provides **180°** phase inversion.
- The capacitive divider $C_1$–$C_2$ provides the feedback voltage with a phase shift of **180°** at parallel resonance.
- Total phase shift = $180° + 180° = 360° = 0°$, satisfying Barkhausen.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Frequency (3 marks):**
The series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$ gives:

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

For a parallel LC circuit, the resonant frequency is:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L \, C_1 C_2}}
$$

**Condition for sustained oscillation:**

$$
h_{fe} \geq \frac{C_2}{C_1}
$$

---

#### **(b) A Colpitts oscillator has $C_1 = 1000 \,\text{pF}$, $C_2 = 5000 \,\text{pF}$, and $L = 100 \,\mu\text{H}$. Find the frequency of oscillation, the feedback factor, and the minimum $h_{fe}$ required. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent Capacitance (2 marks):**

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{(1000 \times 10^{-12})(5000 \times 10^{-12})}{(1000 + 5000) \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-18}}{6 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.8333 \times 10^{-9} = 833.3 \,\text{pF}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency of Oscillation (3 marks):**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(100 \times 10^{-6})(833.3 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{8.333 \times 10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (2.887 \times 10^{-7})} = \frac{1}{1.814 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 \approx 551.3 \,\text{kHz}}
$$

**Step 3 — Feedback Factor (1 mark):**

$$
\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_2} = \frac{1000}{5000} = 0.2
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum $h_{fe}$ (1 mark):**

$$
h_{fe,\min} = \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{5000}{1000} = 5
$$

✅ A BJT with $h_{fe} \geq 5$ (e.g., BC547 with $h_{fe} \approx 100$) will sustain the oscillation comfortably.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Barkhausen Criterion** has two conditions: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or $360°$).
- [x] **Hartley** uses **2 inductors + 1 capacitor**; frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{(L_1 + L_2 + 2M)C}}$.
- [x] **Colpitts** uses **1 inductor + 2 capacitors**; frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{L C_1 C_2}}$.
- [x] **BJT in CE** contributes the *first 180°* of phase shift; the **LC tank** contributes the *second 180°*.
- [x] **Feedback factor** for Hartley = $L_2 / L_1$; for Colpitts = $C_1 / C_2$.
- [x] **Minimum $h_{fe}$** for Hartley ≈ $L_1 / L_2$; for Colpitts ≈ $C_2 / C_1$.
- [x] **Mutual inductance** is **added** ($+2M$) for aiding coupling in standard Hartley designs.
- [x] **Equivalent Colpitts capacitance** is the **series** combination: $C_{eq} = C_1 C_2 / (C_1 + C_2)$.
- [x] **Colpitts is preferred** for higher frequency and better thermal stability.
- [x] **Hartley is preferred** for easy single-capacitor tuning.
- [x] Both oscillators belong to the **LC feedback oscillator** family and use **Barkhausen criterion** as the design foundation.
- [x] Practical designs add **emitter-bypass capacitor** $C_E$ to maximize AC gain.
- [x] The **amplitude stabilization** in real oscillators happens via transistor non-linearity (saturation/cutoff clipping) — Barkhausen's $A\beta = 1$ holds only for the *small-signal linear* regime.
- [x] **Q-factor** of the tank determines the *purity* of the sine wave — higher Q → cleaner output.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator (Using BJT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator (Using BJT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Phase Shift Oscillator using a BJT** is a sinusoidal, low-frequency, audio-range oscillator that employs a Common Emitter (CE) amplifier stage and a passive RC ladder network as the frequency-selective feedback element. The circuit generates sustained sinusoidal oscillations by satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion**, which mandates that the loop gain of the closed-loop system must equal unity in magnitude, and the total phase shift around the loop must be an integer multiple of $360°$ (or $0°$).

In this topology, the inverting CE amplifier provides exactly $180°$ of phase shift, and the RC ladder network (consisting of three cascaded RC sections) provides the additional $180°$ required to satisfy the criterion. Each individual RC section contributes approximately $60°$ of phase shift at the operating frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The RC Phase Shift Oscillator is a **low-frequency oscillator** (typically operating in the range of **1 Hz to 1 MHz**), where the use of LC tanks becomes physically impractical due to the excessively large values of L and C required. The RC network serves as a compact, low-cost frequency-determining element.

### Key Operating Metrics

- **Operating Frequency Range:** $1 \text{ Hz}$ to $1 \text{ MHz}$ (audio and sub-audio range)
- **Typical Frequency of Oscillation ($f_o$):** $\displaystyle f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$
- **Required Magnitude of Voltage Gain ($\vert A_v \vert$):** $\geq 29$ (for the basic configuration)
- **Total Phase Shift around Loop:** $180° + 180° = 360°$ (or $0°$)
- **Phase Shift per RC Section:** $60°$ (at the oscillation frequency)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing. For the swing to maintain a constant amplitude (sustained oscillation), you must push at exactly the right moment (correct phase) and with exactly the right force (correct magnitude). If you push too early, too late, too gently, or too strongly, the swing will either slow down and stop, or swing wildly out of control.

The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** works on this exact principle:

- The **swing** represents the oscillating output signal (the sinusoid).
- **You (the pusher)** represent the **CE amplifier** — it replenishes the energy lost in every cycle.
- **The timing of your push** represents the **phase shift** — you must push at the right instant.
- **The force of your push** represents the **gain** — it must be precisely enough to compensate for losses.

The three RC sections act like three "timing delays" — they delay the signal progressively by $60°$ each. By the time the signal travels through all three sections, it has been delayed by $180°$. The amplifier adds another $180°$ delay (because it's an inverting amplifier), totaling $360°$ — which means the signal returns to the input in **perfect phase alignment** with itself, reinforcing the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The RC Phase Shift Oscillator is often called a **"repetitive function generator"** for audio applications. It is widely used in musical instrument tone generators, function generators, and audio testing equipment due to its simplicity, low cost, and excellent sinusoidal purity when operated slightly above the threshold of oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Steps

The operational mechanism of the BJT-based RC Phase Shift Oscillator can be decomposed into the following structured steps:

### Step 1: The Barkhausen Criterion (Foundation)
For any sinusoidal oscillator, two simultaneous conditions must be met:
- **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain, $\vert A \beta \vert$, must equal **unity (1)**.
- **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop, $\angle(A \beta)$, must equal $0°$ (or an integer multiple of $360°$).

### Step 2: Role of the CE Amplifier
The CE (Common Emitter) BJT amplifier is a naturally **inverting** configuration. It provides a phase shift of exactly $180°$ between its input and output. The voltage gain is given by:
$$A_v = -h_{fe} \cdot \frac{R_C'}{R_{in}}$$
where $R_C'$ is the effective AC load resistance and $R_{in}$ is the input resistance of the BJT stage.

### Step 3: Role of the RC Ladder Network
The feedback network consists of **three identical RC sections** connected in cascade. Each section is a simple high-pass filter (or a phase-lag network). At the oscillation frequency, each section provides a phase shift of $60°$, and the cumulative shift of the three sections is $180°$.

### Step 4: Closure of the Loop
- Phase shift from amplifier: $180°$
- Phase shift from RC network: $180°$
- **Total loop phase shift: $360° \equiv 0°$** ✓ (Phase condition satisfied)
- The magnitude of $\beta$ (feedback fraction) at the oscillation frequency is exactly $1/29$.
- Therefore, the amplifier must provide a gain of at least **29** so that $\vert A \beta \vert = A \cdot (1/29) \geq 1$.

### Step 5: Sustained Oscillations
- If $\vert A_v \vert > 29$: Oscillations grow in amplitude (limited by transistor non-linearity / saturation).
- If $\vert A_v \vert = 29$: Sustained, stable sinusoidal oscillations of constant amplitude.
- If $\vert A_v \vert < 29$: Oscillations decay and die out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three sections?**
> Two sections can only provide a maximum of $180°$ phase shift (theoretically $180°$ at $f \to 0$, but practically less). A single CE amplifier inverts, so we need exactly $180°$ more from the feedback network. Three sections, each providing $60°$, give a clean and stable $180°$ shift at a well-defined frequency.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Unit** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Angular Frequency of Oscillation | $\omega_o$ | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{R C \sqrt{6}}$ | rad/s | Set by the RC network values |
| Frequency of Oscillation | $f_o$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C \sqrt{6}}$ | Hz | Used in all design calculations |
| Feedback Fraction (at $f_o$) | $\vert \beta \vert$ | $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ | Dimensionless | Magnitude only |
| Minimum Voltage Gain Required | $\vert A_v \vert_{min}$ | $\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$ | Dimensionless | Threshold for oscillation |
| Phase Shift per RC Section | $\phi$ | $\phi = 60°$ | Degrees | At oscillation frequency |
| Total RC Network Phase Shift | $\phi_{total}$ | $\phi_{total} = 3 \times 60° = 180°$ | Degrees | Combined effect |
| Total Loop Phase Shift | $\phi_{loop}$ | $\phi_{loop} = 180° + 180° = 360°$ | Degrees | Satisfies Barkhausen |
| BJT Voltage Gain (CE) | $A_v$ | $A_v = -h_{fe} \cdot \dfrac{R_L'}{R_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | Negative sign indicates inversion |
| Modified Minimum $h_{fe}$ | $h_{fe,min}$ | $h_{fe} \geq 29 + \dfrac{23}{\alpha} + \dfrac{4}{h_{fe}}$ | Dimensionless | More accurate (with loading) |
| Simplified $h_{fe}$ Requirement | $h_{fe}$ | $h_{fe} \geq 29$ | Dimensionless | For unloaded approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** When the loading effect of the amplifier on the RC network is considered, the minimum required $h_{fe}$ is **slightly higher than 29**. The more accurate expression accounts for the input impedance $R_{in}$ of the BJT loading the network. For typical design, designers often choose $h_{fe} \geq 30$ to $44$ as a safety margin.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The RC Phase Shift Oscillator is the workhorse of:

1. **Audio Function Generators:** Generating pure sine waves in the 20 Hz to 20 kHz range for testing audio equipment.
2. **Musical Electronics (Tone Generators):** Producing specific musical pitches in electronic organs and synthesizers.
3. **Tone-Detection Circuits:** In FSK (Frequency Shift Keying) modems and telegraph systems.
4. **Laboratory Instrumentation:** Low-cost, portable sine wave sources for student laboratories.
5. **Servo Systems and Control Loops:** As a reference oscillator for calibration.
6. **Inverters and UPS Systems:** In low-power, low-frequency signal generation sections.

The primary **engineering advantage** is that it avoids the bulky, expensive, and physically large inductors required by LC oscillators at low frequencies. A 20 Hz LC oscillator would require impractically large inductor values (on the order of Henries), whereas an RC oscillator achieves the same frequency with **picofarad to microfarad capacitors** and **kilohm resistors** — both readily available and inexpensive.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Circuit Model and Setup

Consider a BJT-based RC Phase Shift Oscillator where:
- A Common Emitter (CE) amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ is used.
- The feedback network consists of **three identical RC sections** connected in cascade.
- Each section has a series resistor of value $R$ and a shunt capacitor of value $C$ to ground.

Let:
- $V_o$ = Output voltage of the amplifier (input to the RC network)
- $V_f$ = Feedback voltage (output of the third RC section, fed back to the amplifier input)
- $V_1, V_2, V_3$ = Voltages at the nodes between successive RC sections

## 3.2 Derivation of the Feedback Factor $\beta$

The transfer function of the three-section RC ladder network (assuming infinite load impedance, i.e., the input impedance of the amplifier does not load the network) is derived using **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at each intermediate node.

### Node Analysis

**At the output node of the RC network** (where $V_f$ is taken), the current entering the network from the amplifier is zero (assuming no loading). KCL at this node gives:
$$V_f \cdot j\omega C + \frac{V_f - V_3}{R} = 0$$
$$V_f \left( j\omega C + \frac{1}{R} \right) = \frac{V_3}{R}$$
$$V_3 = V_f (1 + j\omega RC) \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}$$

**At the second intermediate node** ($V_3$):
$$\frac{V_3 - V_f}{R} + \frac{V_3 - V_2}{R} + j\omega C \cdot V_3 = 0$$
$$V_3 \left( \frac{2}{R} + j\omega C \right) - \frac{V_f + V_2}{R} = 0$$
$$V_2 = V_3 (2 + j\omega RC) - V_f \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}$$

**At the first intermediate node** ($V_2$):
$$\frac{V_2 - V_3}{R} + \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R} + j\omega C \cdot V_2 = 0$$
$$V_2 \left( \frac{2}{R} + j\omega C \right) - \frac{V_3 + V_1}{R} = 0$$
$$V_1 = V_2 (2 + j\omega RC) - V_3 \quad \text{...(Equation 3)}$$

**At the input node** ($V_1 = V_o$, the amplifier output):
$$\frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} + j\omega C \cdot V_1 = 0$$
$$V_1 \left( \frac{1}{R} + j\omega C \right) = \frac{V_2}{R}$$
$$V_2 = V_1 (1 + j\omega RC) = V_o (1 + j\omega RC) \quad \text{...(Equation 4)}$$

### Solving the System

**Step A:** Substitute Equation 1 into Equation 2 to eliminate $V_3$:
$$V_2 = V_f (1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) - V_f$$
$$V_2 = V_f \left[ (1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) - 1 \right]$$

Expanding the product:
$$(1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) = 2 + j\omega RC + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 = 2 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2$$

So:
$$V_2 = V_f \left[ 2 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 - 1 \right] = V_f \left[ 1 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 \right] \quad \text{...(Equation 5)}$$

**Step B:** Use Equation 3 with $V_1 = V_o$. Substitute $V_3$ from Equation 1 and $V_2$ from Equation 5:
$$V_1 = V_2 (2 + j\omega RC) - V_3$$
$$V_o = V_f (1 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2)(2 + j\omega RC) - V_f (1 + j\omega RC)$$

**Step C:** Group and expand terms. Define the substitution $\alpha = \omega RC$ for clarity. Then:
$$V_o = V_f \left[ (1 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2)(2 + j\alpha) - (1 + j\alpha) \right]$$

Let us expand the first product:
$$(1 + 3j\alpha - \alpha^2)(2 + j\alpha) = 2 + j\alpha + 6j\alpha + 3j^2\alpha^2 - 2\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$
$$= 2 + 7j\alpha - 3\alpha^2 - 2\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$
$$= 2 + 7j\alpha - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$

Subtracting $(1 + j\alpha)$:
$$\beta_{denom} = (2 + 7j\alpha - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3) - (1 + j\alpha) = 1 + 6j\alpha - 5\alpha^2 - j\alpha^3$$

Therefore:
$$V_o = V_f \left[ 1 - 5\alpha^2 + j(6\alpha - \alpha^3) \right]$$

### The Feedback Transfer Function

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 + j(6\alpha - \alpha^3)} \quad \text{where } \alpha = \omega RC$$

## 3.3 Condition for Sustained Oscillation — Phase Criterion

For the Barkhausen phase condition, the imaginary part of the denominator must equal **zero**:
$$6\alpha - \alpha^3 = 0$$
$$\alpha (6 - \alpha^2) = 0$$

Since $\alpha \neq 0$ (we are looking for a finite non-zero frequency):
$$\alpha^2 = 6 \implies \alpha = \sqrt{6}$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_o RC = \sqrt{6} \implies \boxed{\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

And the **frequency of oscillation** in Hertz is:
$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}}$$

## 3.4 Condition for Sustained Oscillation — Magnitude Criterion

At $\alpha = \sqrt{6}$, the denominator becomes purely real:
$$1 - 5\alpha^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29$$

Therefore:
$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{-29 + j0} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

The magnitude is:
$$\boxed{\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}}$$

The negative sign indicates a $180°$ phase shift (which matches our requirement).

## 3.5 Required Amplifier Gain

For the Barkhausen magnitude condition, $\vert A_v \beta \vert = 1$:
$$\vert A_v \vert \cdot \frac{1}{29} = 1 \implies \boxed{\vert A_v \vert = 29}$$

The negative sign of $\beta$ ($-1/29$) and the negative gain of the CE amplifier ($-29$) multiply to give a positive loop gain of $+1$ — satisfying the **positive feedback** requirement.

## 3.6 BJT-Specific Design Constraint

For a CE amplifier using a BJT:
$$\vert A_v \vert = h_{fe} \cdot \frac{R_C'}{R_{in}}$$

Setting this equal to 29:
$$h_{fe,min} = \frac{29 \cdot R_{in}}{R_C'}$$

A more accurate expression that accounts for the loading of the RC network by the BJT's input impedance is:
$$h_{fe} \geq 29 + \frac{23}{\alpha_{cb}} + \frac{4}{h_{fe}}$$

where $\alpha_{cb} = h_{fe}/(1+h_{fe})$ is the common-base current gain. For typical silicon BJTs with $h_{fe} \approx 100$, this simplifies to:
$$h_{fe} \geq 29 + \frac{23}{0.99} + \frac{4}{100} \approx 52.3$$

So the practical design rule of thumb is to choose a BJT with $h_{fe} \geq 50$ to $60$ to ensure reliable startup and sustained oscillations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the RC Phase Shift Oscillator

```mermaid
graph LR
    DCInput[DC Bias Supply Vcc Vbb] --> BiasingNetwork[Biasing Network for BJT Q Point]
    BiasingNetwork --> BJTStage[CE Amplifier BJT Stage]
    InSignal[AC Input Noise Trigger] --> BJTStage
    BJTStage -->|Vout with 180 deg phase shift| RCNet[Three Stage RC Ladder Network]
    RCNet -->|Section 1 R and C 60 deg shift| Node1[Node 1]
    Node1 -->|Section 2 R and C 60 deg shift| Node2[Node 2]
    Node2 -->|Section 3 R and C 60 deg shift| Vfb[Feedback Voltage Vfb 180 deg total]
    Vfb -->|Closed loop feedback| BJTStage
    BJTStage -->|Sustained sine wave output| OutTerminal[Sinusoidal Output Terminal]
    OutTerminal -.->|Loads| LoadRL[Load Resistor RL]
    style BJTStage fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style RCNet fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style Vfb fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    style OutTerminal fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic — Equivalent Representation

```mermaid
graph TB
    Vcc[Vcc Supply] --> RC1[Collector Resistor Rc]
    RC1 --> CollectorNode[Collector of BJT Q1]
    CollectorNode --> BJT1[BJT Q1 CE Configuration]
    BJT1 --> EmitterNode[Emitter of BJT Q1]
    EmitterNode --> RE1[Emitter Resistor Re]
    RE1 --> GroundA[Ground]
    BaseNode[Base of BJT Q1] --> R1[Bias Resistor R1]
    R1 --> VbbNode[Vbb Bias Supply]
    BaseNode --> R2[Bias Resistor R2]
    R2 --> GroundB[Ground]
    CollectorNode -->|Vout Output| C1Coupling[Coupling Capacitor Cc1]
    C1Coupling --> Rseries1[Series Resistor R]
    Rseries1 --> N1[Node 1]
    N1 --> Cshunt1[Shunt Capacitor C]
    Cshunt1 --> GroundC[Ground]
    N1 --> Rseries2[Series Resistor R]
    Rseries2 --> N2[Node 2]
    N2 --> Cshunt2[Shunt Capacitor C]
    Cshunt2 --> GroundD[Ground]
    N2 --> Rseries3[Series Resistor R]
    Rseries3 --> N3[Node 3 Feedback Node Vfb]
    N3 --> Cshunt3[Shunt Capacitor C]
    Cshunt3 --> GroundE[Ground]
    N3 --> C2Coupling[Coupling Capacitor Cc2]
    C2Coupling --> BaseNode
    style BJT1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px
    style Rseries1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style Rseries2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style Rseries3 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style Cshunt1 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style Cshunt2 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style Cshunt3 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style N3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow and Phase Shift Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    StartInput[Vfb 0 degrees at Feedback Node] --> AmpBlock[CE Amplifier Stage]
    AmpBlock -->|Phase Shift 180 deg| AfterAmp[Output 180 degrees]
    AfterAmp --> RCStage1[RC Section 1]
    RCStage1 -->|Phase Shift 60 deg| AfterRC1[Node 1 at 240 deg]
    AfterRC1 --> RCStage2[RC Section 2]
    RCStage2 -->|Phase Shift 60 deg| AfterRC2[Node 2 at 300 deg]
    AfterRC2 --> RCStage3[RC Section 3]
    RCStage3 -->|Phase Shift 60 deg| AfterRC3[Vfb at 360 deg equals 0 deg]
    AfterRC3 --> StartInput
    style AmpBlock fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style RCStage1 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style RCStage2 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style RCStage3 fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style AfterRC3 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** When drawing the circuit in your exam, always remember to label the **input node**, **output node**, and **feedback node** clearly. Also mark the **phase shift** at each stage to demonstrate your understanding of the Barkhausen criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition and Operating Principle
**Q: Define an RC Phase Shift Oscillator using a BJT. State the Barkhausen criteria for sustained oscillations.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

An RC Phase Shift Oscillator is a sinusoidal low-frequency oscillator that uses a **BJT in Common Emitter (CE) configuration** as the amplifying element and a **three-section RC ladder network** as the frequency-selective feedback element. It generates sustained sinusoidal waveforms in the audio frequency range (typically 1 Hz to 1 MHz) without the need for bulky inductors.

The **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillations has two parts:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain $\vert A \beta \vert$ must equal **unity (1)**.
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of **$360°$ (or $0°$)**.

In this oscillator, the CE amplifier provides $180°$ phase shift (since it is inverting) and the RC ladder network provides the additional $180°$ (with each of the three sections contributing $60°$), giving a total loop phase shift of $360°$ — thus satisfying the criterion.

---

### Question 2: Frequency and Gain Formula
**Q: For an RC Phase Shift Oscillator using a BJT, derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The frequency of oscillation is given by:
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$$

The minimum magnitude of voltage gain required is:
$$\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$$

These are derived by analyzing the three-section RC ladder network's transfer function, setting the imaginary part of the denominator to zero (for the phase condition) and equating the magnitude of the loop gain to unity (for the magnitude condition).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: Complete Derivation and Analysis
**Q: (a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RC phase shift oscillator uses three identical RC sections, each with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \mu F$. Calculate: (i) the frequency of oscillation, and (ii) the minimum gain required for sustained oscillations. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Working and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 Marks):**

The circuit consists of a **BJT in CE configuration** providing voltage gain $A_v$, and a **feedback network of three identical RC sections** connected between the output and input. Each RC section contains a series resistor $R$ and a shunt capacitor $C$ to ground. The output of the third section is coupled back to the base of the BJT through a coupling capacitor.

**Working (2 Marks):**

When the DC supply is switched on, thermal noise in the BJT provides a small initial disturbance at the base. This is amplified and inverted ($180°$ phase shift) by the CE amplifier. The output passes through the three RC sections, each introducing a $60°$ phase shift (total $180°$). The resultant signal is in phase with the original disturbance. If $\vert A_v \vert \geq 29$, the loop gain equals or exceeds unity, and oscillations build up and are sustained.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

As derived in Section 3, applying KCL at each node of the RC network:
$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{1}{1 - 5(\omega RC)^2 + j[6\omega RC - (\omega RC)^3]}$$

Setting the imaginary part to zero (phase condition):
$$(\omega RC)^2 = 6 \implies \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

$$\therefore f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}} \text{ Hz}$$

At this frequency, $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$. For the magnitude condition ($\vert A \beta \vert = 1$):
$$\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega = 10 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$
- $C = 0.01 \, \mu F = 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \, F = 10^{-8} \, F$

**(i) Frequency of Oscillation (4 Marks):**
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$$
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (10 \times 10^3) \times (10^{-8}) \times \sqrt{6}}$$
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4} \times 2.449}$$
$$f_o = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$\boxed{f_o \approx 649.7 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Gain (3 Marks):**
$$\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$$
$$\boxed{\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29}$$

A BJT with $h_{fe} \geq 30$ (with safety margin) should be selected for the design.

---

### Question B: Alternative — Design and Practical Considerations
**Q: (a)** Compare the RC phase shift oscillator with the LC oscillator. Under what conditions is the RC phase shift oscillator preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT to generate a sinusoidal signal at **$2 \text{ kHz}$**. Assume identical RC sections with $C = 0.1 \, \mu F$. Specify the required $R$ and the minimum $h_{fe}$ of the BJT. **(7 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Supplementary 2023]`

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Comparison (7 Marks):**

| **Parameter** | **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** | **LC Oscillator** |
|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Range** | Low frequency (1 Hz to 1 MHz) | High frequency (> 1 MHz) |
| **Frequency-Determining Element** | Resistors and Capacitors | Inductor and Capacitor (Tank circuit) |
| **Component Size at Low $f$** | Small and compact | Very large inductors required |
| **Cost** | Low | Higher (especially for precision L) |
| **Frequency Stability** | Moderate | High (with quality crystal/quartz) |
| **Output Waveform Purity** | Good (with proper biasing) | Very good |
| **Tunability** | Easy (vary R or C) | Moderate (vary C) |
| **Loading Effect on Frequency** | Significant | Less significant |
| **Typical Applications** | Audio generators, tone generators | RF transmitters, IF stages |

**Preference Conditions (3 Marks):**

The RC phase shift oscillator is preferred when:
1. **Low-frequency oscillations** (audio range, < 1 MHz) are required.
2. **Cost and size** are critical constraints.
3. **Inductors are impractical** (e.g., in fully integrated circuits).
4. **Simplicity of design** is desired (no tank circuit tuning required).

---

**Part (b) — Design (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $f_o = 2 \text{ kHz} = 2000 \text{ Hz}$
- $C = 0.1 \, \mu F = 10^{-7} \, F$

**Find:** $R$ and minimum $h_{fe}$

**Step 1: Calculate $R$ from frequency formula (4 Marks):**
$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$$
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C \sqrt{6}}$$
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10^{-7} \times \sqrt{6}}$$
$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2000 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.449}$$
$$R = \frac{1}{3.077 \times 10^{-3}}$$
$$\boxed{R \approx 325 \, \Omega}$$

(Standard preferred value: **$R = 330 \, \Omega$**)

**Step 2: Determine minimum $h_{fe}$ (3 Marks):**
For the CE amplifier:
$$A_v = h_{fe} \cdot \frac{R_C'}{R_{in}} \geq 29$$

Assuming $R_C' = R_{in}$ (typical design with proper biasing):
$$h_{fe,min} = 29$$

With safety margin and accounting for loading effects:
$$\boxed{h_{fe,min} \approx 50 \text{ to } 60}$$

A BJT such as **BC107, 2N2222, or BC547** (with typical $h_{fe}$ of 100–300) would be suitable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $180°$ phase shift from the CE amplifier:** Many students only consider the $180°$ from the RC network and forget the inverting nature of the CE stage. Always state explicitly: **"CE amplifier provides $180°$ + RC network provides $180°$ = Total $360°$"**.
>
> 2. **Using the wrong value for $\beta$:** Students often write $\beta = 1/29$ without the negative sign. The correct expression is $\beta = -1/29$ (indicating the $180°$ phase shift). This is crucial for showing the sign convention in the loop gain.
>
> 3. **Confusing RC Phase Shift with Wien Bridge:** Both are RC oscillators, but the RC Phase Shift uses **three RC sections** and a **single inverting amplifier**, whereas the Wien Bridge uses a **two-element RC network** and a **non-inverting amplifier**. Don't mix them up.
>
> 4. **Not specifying units in numerical answers:** Always write Hz, $\Omega$, F, etc. A numerical answer without units gets **zero marks** in KTU valuation.
>
> 5. **Incorrect derivation steps:** KTU examiners look for **systematic KCL application at each node**. Skipping intermediate steps or writing only the final answer (without showing the node equations) results in **loss of 2–3 marks** out of 7 in derivation questions.
>
> 6. **Neglecting the $\sqrt{6}$ term:** A common error is writing $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC)$ instead of $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$. This is a **fatal mistake** that will cost full marks on the frequency part.
>
> 7. **Not drawing the circuit diagram:** Even in numerical problems, KTU examiners expect at least a **block diagram** showing the amplifier + three RC sections. Missing this costs **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Phase Shift Oscillator (BJT)**

**Core Concept:**
- ✔ It is a **low-frequency** sinusoidal oscillator using a BJT (CE) amplifier and three RC sections.
- ✔ Uses **RC network** instead of LC tank because LC becomes impractical at low frequencies.

**Barkhausen Criterion:**
- ✔ Magnitude: $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$
- ✔ Phase: Loop phase shift = $0°$ or $360°$
- ✔ CE amplifier: **$180°$** phase shift (inverting)
- ✔ RC network: **$180°$** total phase shift (3 sections × $60°$ each)
- ✔ **Total: $360°$** ✓

**Key Formulas (MUST memorize):**
- ✔ $\displaystyle f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$
- ✔ $\omega_o = \frac{1}{R C \sqrt{6}}$
- ✔ $\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}$
- ✔ $\vert A_v \vert_{min} = 29$
- ✔ $h_{fe} \geq 29$ (basic) or $h_{fe} \geq 50$–$60$ (practical with safety margin)

**Design Parameters:**
- ✔ Choose $C$ first (typically $0.01 \, \mu F$ to $0.1 \, \mu F$ for audio range).
- ✔ Calculate $R$ from the frequency formula.
- ✔ Select BJT with adequate $h_{fe}$ (BC107, BC547, 2N2222 are common choices).
- ✔ Use coupling capacitors (typically $10 \, \mu F$ electrolytic) at input and feedback paths to block DC.

**Real-World Applications:**
- ✔ Audio function generators, tone generators, musical instruments, lab test equipment.

**Comparison Points (for KTU):**
- ✔ RC Phase Shift: Low frequency, compact, low cost, moderate stability.
- ✔ LC Oscillator: High frequency, good stability, larger components, more expensive.

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ✔ Don't forget the $\sqrt{6}$ term in the frequency formula.
- ✔ Always show the negative sign in $\beta = -1/29$ and $A_v = -29$.
- ✔ Always draw a circuit diagram or block diagram.
- ✔ Include units in all numerical answers.

**One-Line KTU Exam Answer (if asked to "Define"):**
> *"An RC Phase Shift Oscillator using a BJT is a low-frequency sinusoidal oscillator that employs a CE amplifier and a three-section RC ladder network to generate sustained oscillations at a frequency $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC\sqrt{6})$, requiring a minimum voltage gain of 29 to satisfy the Barkhausen criterion."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram # 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram — Section 1 ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **555 Timer IC** is a highly stable, monolithic integrated circuit used for generating accurate time delays or oscillations. Designed originally by **Hans Camenzind** in **1971** at Signetics, it is functionally classified as a *mixed-signal analog/digital device* because it internally combines analog voltage-comparator thresholds with a digital bistable (SR) flip-flop. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):** "The 555 is an 8-pin IC that contains two precision comparators, a bistable flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider — used to implement monostable, astable, and bistable multivibrator functions." The numerical designation **"555"** is derived from the internal **three 5 kΩ resistors** that form the voltage-divider reference network, setting the comparator threshold voltages at precise fractions of the supply rail $V_{CC}$. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Automatic Staircase Light" Imagine a staircase in a public building: - A person steps on the **first step** (trigger input falls below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$) → the **light turns ON** (output goes HIGH). - The light stays ON regardless of what the person does on the staircase — it cannot be retriggered. - A **timer inside the switch** counts down. When the elapsed time reaches the pre-set duration (capacitor voltage rises above $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$) → the **light automatically turns OFF** (output goes LOW). - A person on the **next step** (threshold input rises above $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$) can also force the light OFF. This is exactly how the **555 in monostable mode** behaves. The two hidden "trip wires" are the comparator thresholds at $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, and the internal flip-flop "remembers" the ON state until the timing capacitor resets it. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key electrical parameters of the standard bipolar 555 (e.g., NE555, SE555):** > > - **Supply voltage $V_{CC}$:** **+4.5 V to +16 V** (single supply) > - **Maximum output current:** **200 mA** (sink/source) > - **Threshold voltages:** $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (internally fixed) > - **Internal resistor value:** **5 kΩ** (×3, hence the name *555*) > - **Power consumption:** typically **10 mA at $V_{CC}$ = 15 V** > - **Frequency range (astable):** **~0.1 Hz to ~500 kHz** (practical), up to **3 MHz** with CMOS variants (LMC555, TLC555) ## 1.4 Section 1 — Quick Recall - **555** is an 8-pin IC acting as a precision timer/oscillator. - Contains **2 comparators + 1 SR flip-flop + 1 discharge transistor + 3 × 5 kΩ divider**. - Operates in three canonical modes: **Monostable, Astable, Bistable**. - Threshold levels: $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (Trigger) and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (Threshold). - Invented in **1971** by **Hans Camenzind** at Signetics. # 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram — Section 2 ## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture — Component-by-Component Breakdown The 555 IC is internally partitioned into **five distinct functional blocks**. Understanding each block is the *primary* mark-scoring target in KTU Module 2 questions. ### Block A: Resistive Voltage Divider Three equal resistors of **5 kΩ** are connected in series between $V_{CC}$ (Pin 8) and GND (Pin 1). The node voltages are: $$V_{\text{upper}} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC}$$ $$V_{\text{lower}} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$$ These two voltages act as the **reference inputs** for the two comparators. The center node ($\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$) is also accessible externally as **Pin 5 (Control Voltage)**, allowing the user to override the internal reference (e.g., for frequency modulation). ### Block B: Comparator 1 — The "Trigger" Comparator (Lower Comparator) - **Inverting input (–):** Pin 2 (External Trigger) - **Non-inverting input (+):** $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (internal reference) - **Logic:** When $V_{\text{pin2}} < \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ → output goes **HIGH** → this HIGH is fed to the **SET** input of the SR flip-flop. ### Block C: Comparator 2 — The "Threshold" Comparator (Upper Comparator) - **Non-inverting input (+):** Pin 6 (External Threshold) - **Inverting input (–):** $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (internal reference) - **Logic:** When $V_{\text{pin6}} > \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ → output goes **HIGH** → this HIGH is fed to the **RESET** input of the SR flip-flop. ### Block D: SR Flip-Flop (Bistable Latch) The flip-flop is a **Set–Reset NAND/NOR-based latch** with active-HIGH inputs. Its behavior is tabulated below: | $\overline{RESET}$ (Pin 4) | Trigger Comp. (S) | Threshold Comp. (R) | Q (Output) | $\overline{Q}$ (Discharge) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 0 (Forced Reset) | X | X | 0 | 1 | | 1 | 0 | 0 | **Hold (Memory)** | **Hold** | | 1 | 1 | 0 | **1 (SET)** | 0 | | 1 | 0 | 1 | **0 (RESET)** | 1 | | 1 | 1 | 1 | **Disallowed** | **Disallowed** | > [!WARNING] > The KTU board *specifically* tests the truth table of this internal flip-flop. The "both S=R=1" state is forbidden and is generally latched undefined — this is a common spot for losing marks. ### Block E: Discharge Transistor (Open-Collector NPN) - The NPN transistor's **base** is driven by $\overline{Q}$ (the inverted output of the flip-flop). - Its **collector** is connected to **Pin 7 (Discharge)**. - When Q = 1 (output HIGH), the transistor is **OFF** → Pin 7 is *floating* → external capacitor **charges** through the external resistor(s). - When Q = 0 (output LOW), the transistor is **ON (saturated)** → Pin 7 is effectively shorted to GND → external capacitor **discharges**. ### Block F: Output Stage (Totem-Pole / Push-Pull) A complementary push-pull driver delivers the final output to **Pin 3**, capable of sourcing or sinking **up to 200 mA**, which is sufficient to drive a relay, LED, or small speaker directly. ## 2.2 Pin Configuration Summary (8-Pin DIP) | Pin # | Name | Function | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **GND** | Ground reference (0 V) | | 2 | **TRIG** | Trigger input; activates the timer when $V < \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ | | 3 | **OUT** | Output pin (HIGH ≈ $V_{CC}$ or LOW ≈ 0.1 V) | | 4 | **$\overline{RESET}$** | Active-LOW master reset; tie to $V_{CC}$ if unused | | 5 | **CTRL** | Control voltage; bypass to GND with 10 nF to filter noise | | 6 | **THR** | Threshold input; resets the timer when $V > \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | | 7 | **DIS** | Discharge; open-collector of the internal NPN transistor | | 8 | **$V_{CC}$** | Positive supply (+4.5 V to +16 V) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Astable + Monostable) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for solving 555 timer numerical questions in the KTU ESE. | Parameter | Astable Mode (Free-Running Oscillator) | Monostable Mode (One-Shot) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Charging time $t_H$** | $t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | $T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$ | | **Discharging time $t_L$** | $t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C$ | (Not applicable — single pulse) | | **Total period $T$** | $T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C$ | $T = 1.1 R C$ | | **Frequency $f$** | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) \cdot C}$ | $f = \dfrac{0.91}{R \cdot C}$ | | **Duty cycle $D$** | $D = \dfrac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | (Always one pulse) | | **Capacitor voltage swing** | $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC} \to \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (charge)
$\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \to \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (discharge) | $0 \to \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | | **Trigger condition** | Self-triggered (Pin 2 tied to Pin 6) | External negative pulse to Pin 2 | > [!NOTE] > The constant **0.693** comes from the natural logarithm: $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$. It appears in every RC timing equation because the capacitor charges/discharges between two thresholds that have a **2:1 ratio** ($\frac{2}{3}V_{CC} \div \frac{1}{3}V_{CC} = 2$), and $e^{\ln 2} = 2$. > The constant **1.44** is a rounded form of $\frac{1}{0.693}$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Astable:** LED flashers, pulse generators, clock generators for digital logic, PWM motor control, tone generators for alarms. - **Monostable:** Debounce circuits for mechanical switches, missing-pulse detectors, frequency dividers, time-delay relays. - **Bistable:** Latch circuits, Schmitt triggers, touch-activated flip-flops. ## 2.5 Section 2 — Key Takeaways - The internal divider is what gives the "555" its name (3 × 5 kΩ). - Comparator 1 senses the **lower** threshold; Comparator 2 senses the **upper** threshold. - The SR flip-flop is the *decision-making heart* — it determines whether to charge or discharge. - All timing formulas collapse into two constants: **0.693** and **1.1**. # 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram — Section 3 ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Charging of the Timing Capacitor Consider the standard **astable configuration**: a resistor $R_A$ between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7, a resistor $R_B$ between Pin 7 and Pin 6, and a capacitor $C$ from Pin 6 to GND. Pin 2 is tied to Pin 6. ### Step 1: Initial Condition Suppose the discharge transistor is **OFF** (output HIGH). The capacitor charges through the **series combination** $R_A + R_B$ from the supply $V_{CC}$. The general charging equation for an RC network being driven from 0 toward $V_{\text{final}}$ is: $$V_C(t) = V_{\text{final}} - (V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute the Boundary Conditions The capacitor begins charging from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (after the previous discharge cycle ended). It charges toward $V_{\text{final}} = V_{CC}$. The time constant is $\tau_{\text{charge}} = (R_A + R_B) C$. $$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}\right) \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$ $$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Upper Threshold Condition Charging stops when the capacitor reaches $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (the threshold comparator flips and resets the flip-flop): $$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]}$$ $$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]} = V_{CC} - \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$$ $$e^{-t_H/[(R_A + R_B)C]} = \frac{1}{2}$$ ### Step 4: Solve for $t_H$ Taking the natural logarithm on both sides: $$-\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B) C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$ $$\frac{t_H}{(R_A + R_B) C} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$$ $$\boxed{t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_A + R_B) \cdot C}$$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Discharging of the Timing Capacitor When the threshold comparator fires, the discharge transistor turns ON. Now the capacitor discharges **only through $R_B$** (because $R_A$ is blocked by the saturated transistor), into Pin 7 which is now essentially at GND. ### Step 5: Discharging Equation The capacitor starts at $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ and decays toward 0 V with time constant $\tau_{\text{discharge}} = R_B C$: $$V_C(t) = V_{\text{initial}} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{\text{discharge}}} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$ ### Step 6: Apply the Lower Threshold Condition Discharging stops when $V_C = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ (the trigger comparator fires and sets the flip-flop): $$\frac{1}{3} V_{CC} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_L/(R_B C)}$$ $$e^{-t_L/(R_B C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$ ### Step 7: Solve for $t_L$ $$-\frac{t_L}{R_B C} = -\ln 2$$ $$\boxed{t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_B \cdot C}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation — Total Period, Frequency, and Duty Cycle ### Step 8: Total Period $$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 R_B C = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B) C$$ ### Step 9: Frequency $$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$$ ### Step 10: Duty Cycle $$D = \frac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{0.693(R_A + R_B)C}{0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Note carefully: $D > 50\%$ **always** in the standard astable configuration. To obtain a duty cycle of exactly 50%, a **diode-bypass circuit** across $R_B$ is required (outside the KTU Module 2 scope but a good KTU ESE viva point). ## 3.4 Derivation — Monostable Time Period In monostable mode, a single external resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ set the pulse width. The capacitor starts at 0 V, charges through $R$ toward $V_{CC}$, and is interrupted at $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. ### Step 11: Monostable Charging Equation $$V_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/(RC)}\right)$$ ### Step 12: Apply Threshold At $t = T$, $V_C = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$: $$\frac{2}{3} V_{CC} = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-T/(RC)}\right)$$ $$1 - e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{2}{3}$$ $$e^{-T/(RC)} = \frac{1}{3}$$ ### Step 13: Solve for T $$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln 3 \approx -1.0986$$ $$T = RC \cdot \ln 3 \approx 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C$$ $$\boxed{T = 1.1 \, R \, C}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** Design an astable 555 oscillator with $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ and $D = 75\%$. Choose $C = 0.01 \,\mu\text{F}$. **Solution:** $$D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} = 0.75 \implies 4(R_A + R_B) = 3(R_A + 2R_B) \implies R_A = 2R_B$$ $$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B) C} = \frac{1.44}{(2R_B + 2R_B) \cdot 0.01 \times 10^{-6}} = 1000$$ $$4R_B = \frac{1.44}{1000 \times 10^{-8}} = 144000 \,\Omega$$ $$R_B = 36 \,\text{k}\Omega, \quad R_A = 72 \,\text{k}\Omega$$ ## 3.6 Section 3 — Recap of Derived Equations | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $t_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B) C$ | Time capacitor charges from $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ | | 2 | $t_L = 0.693 R_B C$ | Time capacitor discharges from $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ to $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ | | 3 | $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B) C$ | Total period | | 4 | $f = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$ | Oscillation frequency | | 5 | $D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | Duty cycle | | 6 | $T_{\text{mono}} = 1.1 R C$ | Monostable pulse width | # 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram — Section 4 ## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram (Mermaid Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR VCC1[VCC Pin 8] --> R1A[Resistor 5k] R1A --> R2A[Resistor 5k] R2A --> R3A[Resistor 5k] R3A --> GND1[GND Pin 1] R1A -. 2 by 3 VCC .-> nodeA[Node 2 by 3 VCC] R2A -. 1 by 3 VCC .-> nodeB[Node 1 by 3 VCC] nodeA --> CTRL[Pin 5 Control Voltage] nodeA --> C2NEG[Comparator 2 Inverting] nodeB --> C1POS[Comparator 1 Non Inverting] TRIG[Pin 2 Trigger] --> C1NEG[Comparator 1 Inverting] THR[Pin 6 Threshold] --> C2POS[Comparator 2 Non Inverting] C1NEG --> C1OUT[Comparator 1 Output] C2POS --> C2OUT[Comparator 2 Output] C1OUT --> SFF[SR Flip Flop Set] C2OUT --> RFF[SR Flip Flop Reset] RESET[Pin 4 Reset active low] --> RSTFF[SR Flip Flop Master Reset] SFF --> QNODE[Q Output] RFF --> QNODE QNODE --> OUTSTAGE[Output Buffer Stage] OUTSTAGE --> OUTPUT[Pin 3 Output] QNODE --> NPNBASE[Discharge NPN Base] NPNBASE --> NPN[Discharge NPN Transistor] NPN --> DIS[Pin 7 Discharge] ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagram above maps the **functional flow** of signals: from external pins (left and bottom) → comparators (middle) → flip-flop (right-center) → output stage and discharge transistor (right). This corresponds to a top-down traversal of the standard textbook figure. ## 4.2 Pinout Visualization (DIP-8 Top View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DIP8[DIP 8 Package Top View] P1[Pin 1 GND] P8[Pin 8 VCC] P2[Pin 2 TRIG] P7[Pin 7 DIS] P3[Pin 3 OUT] P6[Pin 6 THR] P4[Pin 4 RESET] P5[Pin 5 CTRL] end P1 --- P8 P2 --- P7 P3 --- P6 P4 --- P5 ``` ## 4.3 Signal Flow During One Full Astable Cycle (Mermaid Sequence) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Capacitor at 1 by 3 VCC] --> B[Discharge Transistor OFF] B --> C[Capacitor Charges via RA plus RB] C --> D{Capacitor reaches 2 by 3 VCC} D -- Yes --> E[Threshold Comparator Output HIGH] E --> F[SR Flip Flop RESET] F --> G[Output LOW] G --> H[Discharge Transistor ON] H --> I[Capacitor Discharges via RB] I --> J{Capacitor reaches 1 by 3 VCC} J -- Yes --> K[Trigger Comparator Output HIGH] K --> L[SR Flip Flop SET] L --> M[Output HIGH] M --> H ``` ## 4.4 Section 4 — Diagram Takeaways - The Mermaid block diagrams reveal the **causal chain**: Pin voltage → Comparator → Flip-flop → Output & Discharge transistor. - Pin 5 (Control Voltage) is the *only* node that allows **external modulation** of the $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ threshold. - Pin 4 (Reset) is the *only* signal that **overrides** all internal comparator logic when pulled LOW. # 555 Timer IC: Functional Diagram — Section 5 ## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme — Part A (3-Mark Questions) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] **List the internal blocks of a 555 timer IC.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The 555 timer IC consists of the following five internal blocks: 1. **Resistive voltage divider** — three equal 5 kΩ resistors forming $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ reference voltages. 2. **Comparator 1 (Trigger comparator)** — senses when external pin 2 falls below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$. 3. **Comparator 2 (Threshold comparator)** — senses when external pin 6 rises above $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. 4. **SR flip-flop (bistable latch)** — stores the state and drives the output. 5. **Discharge transistor (NPN open-collector)** — controlled by the inverted output of the flip-flop, used to discharge the external timing capacitor. *(Award 1 mark for naming the divider, 1 mark for the two comparators, 1 mark for the flip-flop + discharge transistor.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] **Why is the IC named "555"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The IC is named "555" because its internal resistive voltage divider is built using **three equal resistors of 5 kΩ each**, connected in series between $V_{CC}$ and GND. These three 5 kΩ resistors generate the two internal reference voltages of $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ that are used by the comparators to define the timing thresholds. The naming convention is purely a reflection of this internal architecture and does not denote the number of pins or functional blocks. --- ## 5.2 KTU 2024 Scheme — Part B (14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of the 555 timer IC, identifying the role of each internal block. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the duty cycle of a 555 timer connected in **astable** mode. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Block Diagram:** (Refers to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1.) **Explanation of each block (1 mark each for any 4 well-explained blocks, 3 marks for the remaining cohesive narrative):** 1. **Voltage Divider (3 × 5 kΩ):** Forms a potential divider that gives fixed reference voltages of $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$ and $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. The midpoint is brought out as Pin 5 (Control Voltage) for external modulation. 2. **Comparator 1 (Trigger):** It compares the external trigger voltage (Pin 2) with the lower reference $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$. If Pin 2 goes below $\frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$, the comparator output goes HIGH, which **SETS** the internal flip-flop. 3. **Comparator 2 (Threshold):** It compares the external threshold voltage (Pin 6) with the upper reference $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. If Pin 6 exceeds $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$, the comparator output goes HIGH, which **RESETS** the internal flip-flop. 4. **SR Flip-Flop:** A bistable latch that stores the timer's state. When SET, its Q output is HIGH (Pin 3 = HIGH, Pin 7 = open). When RESET, Q is LOW (Pin 3 = LOW, Pin 7 = shorted to GND via discharge transistor). 5. **Discharge Transistor (NPN):** A bipolar transistor whose base is driven by $\overline{Q}$ of the flip-flop. It provides a low-impedance path to discharge the external timing capacitor. 6. **Output Buffer (Totem-pole):** A push-pull output stage capable of sourcing/sinking up to 200 mA, making the 555 capable of directly driving relays, LEDs, and buzzers. **Role of Pin 4 (Reset) and Pin 5 (Control Voltage):** Pin 4 is an active-LOW master reset that overrides all comparator inputs; when tied to $V_{CC}$ it is inactive.
