# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "lock range" of a PLL. State the mathematical expression for the lock range of the IC 565 and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The *lock range* (also called the tracking range or hold-in range) is the band of input frequencies centred on the free-running frequency $f_O$ over which the PLL can **maintain** lock once lock has been acquired. It is a one-sided quantity in the IC 565 datasheet, given by

$$\Delta f_{L} \;=\; \frac{8\,f_{O}}{V_{CC}}$$

Its significance lies in two facts: (i) it is *symmetric* about $f_O$, and (ii) it is **wider** than the capture range, so a PLL that has lost lock may still re-acquire it if the input frequency lies within $\Delta f_L$ but outside $\Delta f_C$.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of pins 5 and 6 of the IC 565?**

**Model Answer:**
Pins 5 and 6 are the **VCO timing component pins**. Pin 5 is where the external timing capacitor $C_1$ is connected (its other end to ground), and pin 6 is where the external timing resistor $R_1$ is connected (its other end to $V_{CC}$ or to the internal reference). Together, $R_1$ and $C_1$ set the free-running (center) frequency of the VCO as

$$f_{O} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{R_{1}\,C_{1}}$$

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the functional block diagram of the IC 565 PLL. Label all 14 pins and clearly state the role of pins 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 11, and 12. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the center frequency of the IC 565. An IC 565 has $R_1 = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = 1\,\text{nF}$, supplied from $\pm 6$ V. Determine (i) the free-running frequency, (ii) the lock range, and (iii) the capture range assuming $C_2 = 1\,\mu\text{F}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**
*(The block diagram and pin description are as in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 above. Examiners award the full 7 marks only if a labelled sketch AND a one-line role description for each pin are both provided.)*

| Pin | Role |
|---|---|
| 1 | Positive supply $V_{CC}$ |
| 2 | Output of internal loop amplifier / LPF node |
| 3 | Input to VCO (control voltage node) |
| 4 | VCO square-wave output |
| 5 | External $C_1$ connection |
| 6 | External $R_1$ connection |
| 7 | Decoupling / internal reference |
| 8 | Ground |
| 9 | External signal input to PD |
| 10 | Reference input to PD (VCO feedback) |
| 11 | Demodulated output (audio recovery) |
| 12 | Phase detector raw output |
| 13 | NC |
| 14 | Internal reference / shield |

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The derivation of $f_O = 1.4/(R_1 C_1)$ follows Section 3.1 of these notes.

**(i) Free-running frequency:**

$$f_{O} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{R_{1}\,C_{1}} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{4.7 \times 10^{3} \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{4.7 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_{O} \;\approx\; 2.98 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Hz} \;\approx\; 298\,\text{kHz}$$

**(ii) Lock range:** Total supply $V_{CC} = 6 - (-6) = 12$ V.

$$\Delta f_{L} \;=\; \frac{8\,f_{O}}{V_{CC}} \;=\; \frac{8 \times 298\,000}{12} \;\approx\; 198.7\,\text{kHz}$$

The PLL will hold lock over the band $f_O \pm \Delta f_L = 298 \pm 198.7$ kHz, i.e., from **99.3 kHz to 496.7 kHz**.

**(iii) Capture range:**

$$\Delta f_{C} \;\approx\; \pm\,\sqrt{\frac{\Delta f_{L}}{2\pi\,(3600)\,C_{2}}} \;=\; \pm\,\sqrt{\frac{198\,700}{2\pi \times 3600 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}}$$

$$=\; \pm\,\sqrt{\frac{198\,700}{0.02262}} \;\approx\; \pm\,\sqrt{8.79 \times 10^{6}} \;\approx\; \pm\,2.96\,\text{kHz}$$

So the total capture range is approximately **5.92 kHz**, much smaller than the lock range of 397.4 kHz.

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of the IC 565 as an **FM demodulator**. Discuss the role of $R_2$ and $C_2$ in the circuit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PLL using IC 565 has $R_1 = 15\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ with $\pm 9$ V supply. The low-pass filter uses $C_2 = 0.5\,\mu\text{F}$. Compute the lock range, capture range, and the band of input frequencies over which the loop will acquire lock. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**
Refer to the FM-demodulator hookup in Section 4.3. **Role of $R_2$ and $C_2$:** they form the loop low-pass filter. $C_2$ sets the **capture range** (larger $C_2 \Rightarrow$ narrower capture range, slower lock) and the **demodulated output bandwidth**. $R_2$ in series with $C_2$ damps the loop and prevents overshoot. Together they determine the loop natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping factor $\zeta$ (refer Section 2.3, rows 5 and 6).

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

$$f_{O} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{15 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; \frac{1.4}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 933.3\,\text{Hz}$$

Total supply $V_{CC} = 9 - (-9) = 18$ V.

$$\Delta f_{L} \;=\; \frac{8 \times 933.3}{18} \;\approx\; 414.8\,\text{Hz}$$

$$\Delta f_{C} \;\approx\; \pm\,\sqrt{\frac{414.8}{2\pi \times 3600 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}} \;\approx\; \pm\,\sqrt{\frac{414.8}{0.01131}} \;\approx\; \pm\,191.5\,\text{Hz}$$

**Acquisition band:** $f_O \pm \Delta f_C = 933.3 \pm 191.5$ Hz, i.e., approximately **741.8 Hz to 1124.8 Hz**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Units mismatch** — the most common error is using $R_1$ in $\Omega$ and $C_1$ in $\mu\text{F}$ but forgetting to convert. The factor of $10^6$ lost here wrecks the entire numerical answer. Always convert to base SI (ohms and farads) before substituting.
> 2. **Lock range vs. capture range confusion** — do NOT use $C_2$ in the lock-range formula. The lock range is independent of $C_2$ and depends only on $f_O$ and $V_{CC}$.
> 3. **Half-width vs. full-width** — the formulas given in textbooks are usually *half-width* (one-sided). The KTU question may ask for the *band*, in which case multiply by 2. Read the wording carefully.
> 4. **Pin numbers** — students routinely swap pins 9 and 10. Memorise: **Pin 9 is the external input**, **Pin 10 is the VCO feedback to the PD**.
> 5. **Missing the +V and GND pins** — when asked to "draw the pin diagram," skipping pins 1, 8, 13, 14 loses 1 to 2 marks even if the active pins are correct.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IC 565 PLL**

- The IC 565 is a **monolithic analog PLL** in a **14-pin DIP** with an operating frequency range of **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz**.
- Three internal blocks: **Phase Detector + Loop Amplifier + VCO**. External components: $R_1$, $C_1$ (timing), $R_2$, $C_2$ (loop filter).
- **Center frequency:** $f_{O} = \dfrac{1.4}{R_{1}\,C_{1}}$. This is the free-running VCO frequency.
- **Lock range:** $\Delta f_{L} = \dfrac{8\,f_{O}}{V_{CC}}$. Independent of $C_2$.
- **Capture range:** $\Delta f_{C} \approx \pm\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta f_{L}}{2\pi\,(3600)\,C_{2}}}$. Strongly dependent on $C_2$.
- Always $\Delta f_{C} \le \Delta f_{L}$; in practice $\Delta f_{C} \ll \Delta f_{L}$.
- **Pin 1** is $+V_{CC}$, **Pin 8** is GND, **Pin 13** is NC, **Pin 14** is internal reference.
- **Pin 9** = external signal input; **Pin 10** = VCO feedback to PD.
- **Pin 5** = external $C_1$ to ground; **Pin 6** = external $R_1$ to $V_{CC}$.
- **Pin 2** and **Pin 3** = loop filter terminals ($R_2$, $C_2$ connected between them).
- **Pin 4** = VCO output (square wave, can be taken to pin 10 for closed loop).
- **Pin 11** = demodulated (audio) output — the heart of every FM/FSK application.
- **Pin 12** = raw phase detector output (contains high-frequency ripple).
- Typical supply: **$\pm 6$ V to $\pm 12$ V** dual, or **+12 V** single with pin 8 grounded.
- VCO linearity is better than **1 %**, and VCO temperature stability is about **200 ppm/°C**.
- Major applications: **FM demodulation, FSK decoding, frequency synthesis, tone decoding, signal conditioning, motor speed control**.
- Exam traps: unit-conversion errors (always convert to SI), confusing $\Delta f_L$ with $\Delta f_C$, swapping pins 9/10, omitting supply pins from the diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an electronic oscillator circuit whose **output frequency is linearly (or proportionally) proportional to an externally applied input control voltage** ($V_c$ or $V_{in}$). In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the VCO is most commonly realized as an **Astable Multivibrator** using a 555 timer IC, where the control voltage is applied to the **CONTROL pin (Pin 5)** of the 555 timer to modulate the threshold levels of the internal comparators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "VCO (Analysis)" specifically refers to deriving the **frequency of oscillation ($f_o$)** as a function of the **control voltage ($V_c$)**, and plotting the **Voltage-Frequency Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** curve. Mastering the derivation is essential for the 14-mark ESE questions.

The general transfer equation of an ideal VCO is expressed as:

$$f_o = f_{min} + K_{VCO} \cdot (V_c - V_{min})$$

Where:
- $f_o$ = Instantaneous output frequency
- $K_{VCO}$ = Tuning sensitivity (Hz/Volt)
- $V_c$ = Control voltage
- $f_{min}, V_{min}$ = Minimum frequency and corresponding minimum control voltage

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Pitch Pedal" of a Guitar**
>
> Imagine a guitarist standing on a stage. The guitarist's **fingers on the strings** are like the **resistors/capacitors** of an oscillator (they determine the basic pitch). Now imagine a **foot pedal** (the wah-wah or pitch-shifter pedal) that the guitarist presses with their foot — the harder they press, the higher the pitch. **The pressure on the pedal is the *control voltage* ($V_c$)**, and **the resulting pitch of the guitar string is the *output frequency* ($f_o$)** of the VCO.
>
> - **No pressure (0V control)** → Guitar plays its **lowest base pitch** ($f_{min}$)
> - **More pressure (higher $V_c$)** → Guitar plays a **higher pitch** ($f_o$ increases)
> - **Maximum pressure (max $V_c$)** → Guitar plays its **highest pitch** ($f_{max}$)
>
> In electronics, instead of a guitarist, we have a **555 timer IC** generating square waves, and instead of a foot pedal, we have a **DC voltage applied to Pin 5** that shifts the internal threshold levels and changes the charging/discharging rate of the timing capacitor, thereby shifting the output frequency.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$):** Typical value is **+5V to +15V DC**
- **Control Voltage Range:** $0 \le V_c \le V_{CC}$ (theoretically, the control pin internally sits at $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ when left open)
- **Operating Frequency Range:** Standard astable 555 VCO operates in the range **1 Hz to 500 kHz**
- **Output:** Square wave (typically) at the Pin 3 output
- **Duty Cycle Range:** Variable based on the resistor configuration

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "VCO = Voltage Changes Oscillation." The mnemonic helps recall that voltage is the *control input* and oscillation (frequency) is the *output variable*.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The 555 Timer as a VCO

The 555 timer IC contains **two internal comparators**, a **SR flip-flop**, a **discharge transistor**, and a **resistive voltage divider** (three equal 5k$\Omega$ resistors). The voltage divider sets two critical reference levels:

$$\text{Upper Threshold} = \frac{2}{3} V_{CC} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Lower Threshold} = \frac{1}{3} V_{CC}$$

### 2.1.1 The Role of the Control Voltage Pin (Pin 5)

The **Control Voltage pin (Pin 5)** is directly connected to the junction of the upper two 5k$\Omega$ resistors of the internal voltage divider. By applying an external DC voltage $V_c$ at Pin 5, we **override the internal $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ reference** and force the new threshold levels to become:

$$V_{TH} = V_c \quad \text{(Upper Threshold, applied to Comparator 1)}$$

$$V_{TL} = \frac{V_c}{2} \quad \text{(Lower Threshold, applied to Comparator 2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the lower threshold becomes $V_c/2$:** The internal resistive divider is symmetric (three equal 5k$\Omega$ resistors). When the top of the divider is forced to $V_c$, the middle tap (Pin 5) is $V_c$ and the bottom tap is at ground. The middle comparator therefore sees exactly **half** of $V_c$ at its non-inverting input.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Charging/Discharging Logic

Consider the standard **astable configuration** of the 555 timer with:
- $R_A$ = Resistor between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7 (discharge pin)
- $R_B$ = Resistor between Pin 7 and Pin 6/2 (threshold/trigger pins tied together)
- $C$ = Timing capacitor between Pin 6/2 and ground

### Phase 1: **Charging of Capacitor C** (Output is HIGH)
- The discharge transistor (Pin 7) is **OFF**.
- Capacitor $C$ charges through the **series combination** of $R_A$ and $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$.
- The voltage across the capacitor rises exponentially from $\frac{V_c}{2}$ towards $V_{CC}$.
- Charging continues until $V_C$ reaches the **upper threshold** $V_{TH} = V_c$.

### Phase 2: **Discharging of Capacitor C** (Output is LOW)
- The discharge transistor (Pin 7) turns **ON**, shorting Pin 7 to ground.
- Capacitor $C$ now discharges through **only $R_B$** (since $R_A$ is connected between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7, but Pin 7 is now grounded, no current flows from $V_{CC}$).
- The voltage across the capacitor decays exponentially from $V_c$ towards 0V.
- Discharging continues until $V_C$ reaches the **lower threshold** $V_{TL} = \frac{V_c}{2}$.

This charging-discharging cycle repeats indefinitely, producing a **square wave** at the output (Pin 3) whose frequency depends on how fast the capacitor can swing between $\frac{V_c}{2}$ and $V_c$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---------------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| Upper Threshold | $V_{TH} = V_c$ | Voltage at which charging stops | Volts (V) |
| Lower Threshold | $V_{TL} = \frac{V_c}{2}$ | Voltage at which discharging stops | Volts (V) |
| Charging Time Constant | $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C$ | During charging phase | Seconds (s) |
| Discharging Time Constant | $\tau_d = R_B \cdot C$ | During discharging phase | Seconds (s) |
| Charging Time $T_{HIGH}$ | $T_H = (R_A + R_B) \cdot C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$ | Time output is HIGH | Seconds (s) |
| Discharging Time $T_{LOW}$ | $T_L = R_B \cdot C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 R_B C$ | Time output is LOW | Seconds (s) |
| **Total Time Period $T$** | $T = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | One complete cycle | Seconds (s) |
| **Frequency of Oscillation $f_o$** | $f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$ | Output square wave frequency | Hertz (Hz) |
| Duty Cycle | $D = \frac{R_A + R_B}{R_A + 2R_B} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of time output is HIGH | Percentage (%) |
| VCO Sensitivity | $K_{VCO} = \frac{df_o}{dV_c}$ | Slope of VTC curve | Hz/Volt |
| Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f = K_{VCO} \cdot \Delta V_c$ | Change in frequency per voltage change | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Notice that in the standard 555 astable frequency formula $f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$, the **control voltage $V_c$ does NOT appear directly**. This is because the formula assumes $V_{TH} = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ and $V_{TL} = \frac{1}{3}V_{CC}$. When the control voltage is varied, the $\ln(2)$ factor changes, and the formula becomes frequency-dependent on $V_c$.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

VCOs are foundational building blocks in modern communication and signal processing systems:

- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLL):** The VCO is the heart of a PLL, used in FM demodulators, frequency synthesizers, and clock recovery circuits.
- **Frequency Modulation (FM) Transmitters:** The audio signal modulates the carrier frequency through a VCO.
- **Function Generators:** VCOs enable variable-frequency waveform generation in lab instruments.
- **Tone Dialers (DTMF):** Dual-tone generation in telephone dialing uses VCOs.
- **Radar and Sonar Systems:** Chirp signal generation for ranging applications.
- **DC-DC Converters:** Voltage-to-frequency conversion for feedback control.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: General Charging Equation of Capacitor C

The capacitor $C$ charges from $V_{CC}$ through the series resistors $R_A$ and $R_B$ towards the **upper threshold** $V_c$. The general charging equation for a capacitor charging from an initial voltage $V_i$ to a final voltage $V_f$ through a resistance $R$ is:

$$V_C(t) = V_f - (V_f - V_i) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

In our circuit during charging:
- Initial voltage (just after switching): $V_i = \frac{V_c}{2}$
- Final asymptotic voltage: $V_f = V_{CC}$
- Time constant: $\tau_c = (R_A + R_B)C$

Substituting:

$$V_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}\right) \cdot e^{-t/[(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of Charging Time $T_H$

The charging time $T_H$ is the time taken by the capacitor voltage to rise from $\frac{V_c}{2}$ to $V_c$. Setting $V_C(T_H) = V_c$:

$$V_c = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}\right) \cdot e^{-T_H/[(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

**Row 1 — Rearranging for the exponential term:**

$$V_{CC} - V_c = \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}\right) \cdot e^{-T_H/[(R_A+R_B)C]}$$

**Row 2 — Solving for the exponential:**

$$e^{-T_H/[(R_A+R_B)C]} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_c}{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}$$

**Row 3 — Taking natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$-\frac{T_H}{(R_A+R_B)C} = \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_c}{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}\right)$$

**Row 4 — Solving for $T_H$:**

$$T_H = -(R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_c}{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}\right)$$

**Row 5 — Using the logarithmic identity $-\ln(a/b) = \ln(b/a)$:**

$$T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}{V_{CC} - V_c}\right)$$

**Row 6 — Recognizing the special case $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:**

When $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ (the natural bias):

- Numerator: $V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2} = V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$
- Denominator: $V_{CC} - V_c = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$

$$T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2V_{CC}/3}{V_{CC}/3}\right) = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: General Discharging Equation of Capacitor C

The capacitor $C$ discharges from $V_c$ through **only $R_B$** towards 0V (since the discharge transistor shorts Pin 7 to ground during this phase).

The general discharging equation:

$$V_C(t) = V_i \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

In our circuit during discharging:
- Initial voltage: $V_i = V_c$
- Time constant: $\tau_d = R_B C$

Substituting:

$$V_C(t) = V_c \cdot e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

### Step-by-Step Evaluation of Discharging Time $T_L$

The discharging time $T_L$ is the time taken by the capacitor voltage to fall from $V_c$ to $\frac{V_c}{2}$. Setting $V_C(T_L) = \frac{V_c}{2}$:

$$\frac{V_c}{2} = V_c \cdot e^{-T_L/(R_B C)}$$

**Row 1 — Dividing both sides by $V_c$:**

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-T_L/(R_B C)}$$

**Row 2 — Taking natural logarithm:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\frac{T_L}{R_B C}$$

**Row 3 — Recognizing that $\ln(1/2) = -\ln(2)$:**

$$-\ln(2) = -\frac{T_L}{R_B C}$$

**Row 4 — Solving for $T_L$:**

$$\boxed{T_L = R_B C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 R_B C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** The discharging time $T_L$ is **independent of the control voltage $V_c$**. This is because both the initial voltage and the final voltage scale linearly with $V_c$, and the ratio ($\frac{1}{2}$) is constant. This means the discharging half-period is fixed, while only the charging half-period changes with $V_c$. This produces an **asymmetric duty cycle** as $V_c$ varies — a common KTU question.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Time Period and Frequency

The total time period is the sum of charging and discharging durations:

$$T = T_H + T_L$$

Substituting the generalized expressions:

$$T = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}{V_{CC} - V_c}\right) + R_B C \cdot \ln(2)$$

**Row 1 — Simplifying for the special case $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$:**

$$T = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 R_B C = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$$

**Row 2 — Frequency is the reciprocal of the period:**

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}}$$

**Row 3 — For the generalized case with variable $V_c$:**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{(R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2V_{CC} - V_c}{2V_{CC} - 2V_c}\right) + R_B C \cdot \ln(2)}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: VTC (Voltage-Frequency Transfer Characteristics)

The Voltage-Frequency Transfer Characteristic is a plot of $f_o$ (y-axis) versus $V_c$ (x-axis).

| **Control Voltage $V_c$** | **Upper Threshold** | **Lower Threshold** | **Charging Time $T_H$** | **Total Period $T$** | **Frequency $f_o$** | **Qualitative Behavior** |
|---------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|--------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------------------|
| $V_c \to 0$ | 0 | 0 | Very short (no charging span) | $\approx 0.693 R_B C$ | **High (near max)** | Output almost always LOW |
| $V_c = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | $\frac{V_{CC}}{6}$ | Moderate | $0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | $f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A+2R_B)C}$ | Normal operation |
| $V_c = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ | $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ | $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ | Standard | $0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$ | $f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A+2R_B)C}$ | **Nominal frequency** |
| $V_c \to V_{CC}$ | $V_{CC}$ | $\frac{V_{CC}}{2}$ | Very long (cap cannot reach $V_{CC}$) | Large | **Low (near 0)** | Output almost always HIGH |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Trend for Exam:** As $V_c$ increases, $f_o$ **decreases** (inverse relationship). The VTC curve is a **monotonically decreasing hyperbolic curve** — students often mistakenly draw it as increasing!

## 3.5 Derivation 4: VCO Sensitivity $K_{VCO}$

The VCO tuning sensitivity is the **rate of change of output frequency with respect to control voltage**:

$$K_{VCO} = \frac{df_o}{dV_c}$$

Using the generalized frequency equation and applying calculus (chain rule + quotient rule), the analytical expression is complex. For KTU exam purposes, a **graphical estimation** of $K_{VCO}$ from the VTC plot is acceptable:

$$K_{VCO} = \frac{\Delta f_o}{\Delta V_c} = \frac{f_2 - f_1}{V_{c2} - V_{c1}}$$

The unit is **Hz per Volt** (Hz/V).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Schematic of 555 Timer as a VCO

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional block-level architecture** of the 555 timer configured as a VCO. This representation maps the signal flow and functional interactions rather than attempting a literal physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC[DC Supply VCC] --> RA[Resistor RA]
    VCC --> RB[Resistor RB]
    RA --> pin7[Pin 7 Discharge]
    RB --> pin6[Pin 6 Threshold]
    pin6 --> pin2[Pin 2 Trigger]
    pin6 --> C[Timing Capacitor C]
    pin2 --> C
    C --> GND[Ground]
    pin7 --> C
    VcExt[External Control Voltage Vc] --> pin5[Pin 5 Control Voltage]
    pin5 --> cmp1[Comparator 1 Upper]
    pin5 --> cmp2[Comparator 2 Lower]
    pin6 --> cmp1
    pin2 --> cmp2
    cmp1 --> ff[SR Flip Flop]
    cmp2 --> ff
    ff --> drv[Output Driver]
    drv --> pin3[Pin 3 Output Square Wave]
    ff --> pin7
    pin3 --> out[Frequency fo = function of Vc]
    VcExt --> out
```

**Node Interaction Explanation:**
- The **external control voltage $V_c$** enters via **Pin 5** and sets the **upper threshold** at $V_c$ and **lower threshold** at $V_c/2$.
- The **timing capacitor C** charges through $R_A + R_B$ until it reaches $V_c$ (Comparator 1 trips).
- The **flip-flop** then activates the **discharge transistor** (Pin 7), causing C to discharge through $R_B$ until voltage drops to $V_c/2$ (Comparator 2 trips).
- The cycle repeats, generating a **square wave at Pin 3** whose frequency depends on $V_c$.

## 4.2 Internal Block Diagram of 555 Timer

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC1[VCC] --> div[Resistive Divider 3 x 5kOhm]
    div --> tap1[Tap at 2 by 3 VCC]
    div --> tap2[Tap at 1 by 3 VCC]
    tap1 --> c1[Comparator 1 Non Inverting]
    tap2 --> c2[Comparator 2 Inverting]
    pin5[Pin 5 Control Voltage] --> c1
    pin5 --> c2
    pin6[Pin 6 Threshold] --> c1inv[Comparator 1 Inverting]
    pin2[Pin 2 Trigger] --> c2ni[Comparator 2 Non Inverting]
    c1 --> rsflip[SR Flip Flop R Input]
    c2 --> rsflipS[SR Flip Flop S Input]
    rsflip --> qbar[Q Bar Output]
    qbar --> drvtr[Discharge Transistor Q1]
    qbar --> outdrv[Output Buffer Driver]
    outdrv --> pin3[Pin 3 Output]
    drvtr --> pin7[Pin 7 Discharge Pin]
    rsflipS --> outdrv
    pin4[Pin 4 Reset] --> rsflip
    pin8[Pin 8 VCC] --> vccsupply[Power Supply Rail]
    pin1[Pin 1 Ground] --> groundsym[Ground Reference]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow (Charging-Discharging Cycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Charging
    Charging: Phase 1 Capacitor Charging
    Charging: C charges through RA plus RB
    Charging: Voltage rises from Vc by 2 to Vc
    Charging --> Decision_Check_Upper: Voltage reaches Vc
    Decision_Check_Upper: Threshold reached
    Decision_Check_Upper --> Discharging: Comparator 1 trips
    Discharging: Phase 2 Capacitor Discharging
    Discharging: C discharges through RB only
    Discharging: Voltage falls from Vc to Vc by 2
    Discharging --> Decision_Check_Lower: Voltage reaches Vc by 2
    Decision_Check_Lower: Threshold reached
    Decision_Check_Lower --> Charging: Comparator 2 trips
    Charging --> [*]
    Discharging --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the State Diagram:** The oscillator continuously cycles between the **Charging** and **Discharging** states. The transition between states is governed by the **comparator thresholds**, which are in turn set by the **control voltage $V_c$**.
