# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO). What is the role of the control voltage pin (Pin 5) in a 555 timer-based VCO?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Voltage Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** is an electronic oscillator whose **output frequency is a function of an externally applied input control voltage**. The output frequency varies linearly (in ideal VCOs) or monotonically with the magnitude of the control voltage.

**Role of Pin 5 (Control Voltage Pin):**
The control voltage pin (Pin 5) of the 555 timer is internally connected to the junction of the upper two 5k$\Omega$ resistors of the internal voltage divider. When an external DC voltage $V_c$ is applied at this pin, it **overrides the internally generated $\frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$ reference voltage**. Consequently:
- The **upper threshold** of Comparator 1 is forced to $V_{TH} = V_c$
- The **lower threshold** of Comparator 2 is forced to $V_{TL} = \frac{V_c}{2}$

By modulating $V_c$, the threshold levels shift, altering the charging/discharging time of the timing capacitor $C$, and thereby **modulating the output frequency** $f_o$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a 555 timer in astable mode. State the standard formula.**

**Model Answer:**

Consider a 555 timer in astable mode with $R_A$ (between $V_{CC}$ and Pin 7), $R_B$ (between Pin 7 and Pin 6/2), and $C$ (between Pin 6/2 and ground).

**Charging Time (Output HIGH):**
The capacitor charges from $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ through $(R_A + R_B)$:

$$T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C$$

**Discharging Time (Output LOW):**
The capacitor discharges from $\frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$ to $\frac{V_{CC}}{3}$ through $R_B$ only:

$$T_L = 0.693 R_B C$$

**Total Time Period:**

$$T = T_H + T_L = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C + 0.693 R_B C = 0.693(R_A + 2R_B)C$$

**Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a 555 timer-based VCO with the help of a neat functional block diagram. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation as a function of the control voltage $V_c$. Assume standard astable configuration with $R_A$, $R_B$, and $C$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a 555 timer VCO circuit, $V_{CC} = 12V$, $R_A = 4.7k\Omega$, $R_B = 10k\Omega$, and $C = 0.01\mu F$. If the control voltage $V_c$ is varied from 4V to 10V, calculate:
- (i) The frequency of oscillation at $V_c = 8V$
- (ii) The VCO tuning sensitivity $K_{VCO}$ in Hz/V
- (iii) The duty cycle at $V_c = 8V$ **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Working Principle and Derivation:**

[Refer to Sections 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 above for the detailed derivation. The student should draw the block diagram (similar to Section 4.2), identify the role of Pin 5, and derive the expressions for $T_H$, $T_L$, and $f_o$.]

**Key Derivation Summary:**

$$T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}{V_{CC} - V_c}\right)$$

$$T_L = R_B C \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 R_B C$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{T_H + T_L}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 12V$, $R_A = 4.7k\Omega$, $R_B = 10k\Omega$, $C = 0.01\mu F = 10^{-8}F$

**(i) Frequency at $V_c = 8V$:**

The generalized charging time formula:

$$T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}{V_{CC} - V_c}\right)$$

**Row 1 — Substituting values:**

$$T_H = (4.7k + 10k) \times 10^{-8} \times \ln\left(\frac{12 - \frac{8}{2}}{12 - 8}\right)$$

$$T_H = 14.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} \times \ln\left(\frac{12 - 4}{12 - 8}\right)$$

$$T_H = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln\left(\frac{8}{4}\right)$$

**Row 2 — Computing the logarithm:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{8}{4}\right) = \ln(2) = 0.693$$

**Row 3 — Computing $T_H$:**

$$T_H = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.693 = 1.019 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**Row 4 — Computing $T_L$:**

$$T_L = 0.693 R_B C = 0.693 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 6.93 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

**Row 5 — Total time period:**

$$T = T_H + T_L = 1.019 \times 10^{-4} + 6.93 \times 10^{-5} = 1.712 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**Row 6 — Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.712 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 5841 \text{ Hz} \approx 5.84 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) VCO Sensitivity:**

**Row 1 — Computing $f_o$ at $V_c = 4V$:**

$$T_H(V_c=4) = 14.7 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln\left(\frac{12-2}{12-4}\right) = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln\left(\frac{10}{8}\right)$$

$$\ln(1.25) = 0.2231$$

$$T_H(V_c=4) = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2231 = 3.28 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

$$T_L = 6.93 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

$$T(V_c=4) = 3.28 \times 10^{-5} + 6.93 \times 10^{-5} = 1.021 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$f_o(V_c=4) = \frac{1}{1.021 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 9794 \text{ Hz}$$

**Row 2 — Frequency at $V_c = 10V$:**

$$T_H(V_c=10) = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln\left(\frac{12-5}{12-10}\right) = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times \ln(3.5)$$

$$\ln(3.5) = 1.2528$$

$$T_H(V_c=10) = 1.47 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.2528 = 1.842 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$T_L = 6.93 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

$$T(V_c=10) = 1.842 \times 10^{-4} + 6.93 \times 10^{-5} = 2.535 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

$$f_o(V_c=10) = \frac{1}{2.535 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3945 \text{ Hz}$$

**Row 3 — Computing $K_{VCO}$:**

$$K_{VCO} = \frac{\Delta f_o}{\Delta V_c} = \frac{3945 - 9794}{10 - 4} = \frac{-5849}{6} \approx -974.8 \text{ Hz/V}$$

$$\boxed{K_{VCO} \approx -975 \text{ Hz/V}}$$

**Note:** The negative sign confirms the **inverse relationship** between $f_o$ and $V_c$.

**(iii) Duty Cycle at $V_c = 8V$:**

$$D = \frac{T_H}{T_H + T_L} \times 100\% = \frac{1.019 \times 10^{-4}}{1.712 \times 10^{-4}} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 59.52\%}$$

---

### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a 555 timer in **astable mode**. Derive the expressions for charging time, discharging time, and frequency of oscillation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 555 timer VCO operates with $V_{CC} = 15V$, $R_A = 2.2k\Omega$, $R_B = 6.8k\Omega$, and $C = 1nF$. The control voltage $V_c$ is applied at Pin 5.
- (i) Calculate the **nominal frequency** when Pin 5 is left open (i.e., $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC} = 10V$).
- (ii) Calculate the **maximum frequency** when $V_c = 5V$.
- (iii) Comment on the **duty cycle variation** as $V_c$ changes. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Astable Operation and Derivation:**

[Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 for the complete derivation. The student should draw the astable circuit, label all components, and derive the three key formulas.]

**Key Formulas:**

$$T_H = 0.693(R_A + R_B)C, \quad T_L = 0.693 R_B C, \quad f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 15V$, $R_A = 2.2k\Omega$, $R_B = 6.8k\Omega$, $C = 1nF = 10^{-9}F$

**(i) Nominal Frequency at $V_c = 10V$:**

**Row 1 — Using the standard astable formula (valid when $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$):**

$$f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C} = \frac{1.44}{(2.2k + 2 \times 6.8k) \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1.44}{(2.2k + 13.6k) \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.44}{15.8 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1.44}{15.8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 91,139 \text{ Hz} \approx 91.14 \text{ kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_o \approx 91.14 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Frequency at $V_c = 5V$:**

**Row 1 — Computing $T_H$ at $V_c = 5V$:**

$$T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - \frac{V_c}{2}}{V_{CC} - V_c}\right) = (2.2k + 6.8k) \times 10^{-9} \times \ln\left(\frac{15 - 2.5}{15 - 5}\right)$$

$$T_H = 9 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} \times \ln\left(\frac{12.5}{10}\right) = 9 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln(1.25)$$

**Row 2 — Computing logarithm:**

$$\ln(1.25) = 0.2231$$

**Row 3 — Computing $T_H$:**

$$T_H = 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2231 = 2.008 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Row 4 — Computing $T_L$:**

$$T_L = 0.693 R_B C = 0.693 \times 6.8 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-9} = 4.71 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Row 5 — Total period:**

$$T = T_H + T_L = 2.008 \times 10^{-6} + 4.71 \times 10^{-6} = 6.718 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Row 6 — Maximum frequency:**

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{6.718 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 148,854 \text{ Hz} \approx 148.85 \text{ kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{max} \approx 148.85 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iii) Comment on Duty Cycle Variation:**

The duty cycle is given by:

$$D = \frac{T_H}{T_H + T_L} \times 100\%$$

**At $V_c = 10V$ (nominal):**

$$D = \frac{0.693 \times 9k \times 10^{-9}}{0.693 \times 15.8k \times 10^{-9}} \times 100\% = \frac{9}{15.8} \times 100\% = 56.96\%$$

**At $V_c = 5V$:**

$$D = \frac{2.008}{6.718} \times 100\% = 29.89\%$$

**Comment:** As $V_c$ **decreases** from 10V to 5V, the duty cycle **decreases** from ~57% to ~30%. This is because lower $V_c$ means a smaller charging span (from $V_c/2$ to $V_c$), resulting in shorter $T_H$ and hence lower duty cycle. The 555 timer VCO is **not a constant duty cycle VCO** — this is a key practical limitation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Common Mistake 1 (3-mark loss):** Students often write the standard astable formula $f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A+2R_B)C}$ for a VCO question without acknowledging that this formula is valid **only when $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$** (i.e., Pin 5 is left open). For variable $V_c$, the generalized logarithmic formula **must** be used.
>
> 2. **Common Mistake 2 (2-mark loss):** Confusing the upper and lower thresholds. Remember: **upper threshold = $V_c$** and **lower threshold = $V_c/2$**. The 555 timer's internal divider creates this 2:1 ratio.
>
> 3. **Common Mistake 3 (2-mark loss):** Forgetting that the discharge transistor shorts Pin 7 to ground during the LOW phase, so the capacitor discharges **only through $R_B$** (not through $R_A + R_B$).
>
> 4. **Common Mistake 4 (3-mark loss):** In numerical problems, not using the **generalized logarithmic formula** when $V_c \neq \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$. Many students blindly plug into the standard formula and get incorrect answers.
>
> 5. **Common Mistake 5 (4-mark loss):** Drawing the VTC curve incorrectly. The relationship between $V_c$ and $f_o$ is **inverse** (decreasing), not direct. The curve is a **hyperbolic decay**, not a straight line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **VCO Definition:** An oscillator whose output frequency is controlled by an input voltage $V_c$.
- **555 Timer VCO Configuration:** Standard astable mode with $V_c$ applied at Pin 5 (Control Voltage pin).
- **Threshold Voltages:** $V_{TH} = V_c$ (upper), $V_{TL} = V_c/2$ (lower).
- **Charging Path:** $V_{CC} \to R_A \to R_B \to C$ (during HIGH output).
- **Discharging Path:** $C \to R_B \to$ Pin 7 (ground) (during LOW output).
- **Charging Time Formula (General):** $T_H = (R_A + R_B)C \cdot \ln\left(\frac{V_{CC} - V_c/2}{V_{CC} - V_c}\right)$
- **Discharging Time Formula (General):** $T_L = 0.693 R_B C$ (independent of $V_c$)
- **Nominal Frequency Formula (when $V_c = \frac{2}{3}V_{CC}$):** $f_o = \frac{1.44}{(R_A + 2R_B)C}$
- **VTC Curve Trend:** $f_o$ **decreases** as $V_c$ **increases** (inverse relationship).
- **VCO Sensitivity:** $K_{VCO} = \frac{\Delta f_o}{\Delta V_c}$ (measured in Hz/V, typically negative).
- **Duty Cycle Variation:** Duty cycle is **not constant** with $V_c$ variation — this is a limitation of the basic 555 VCO.
- **Key Applications:** PLLs, FM modulators, function generators, frequency synthesizers, tone generators.
- **Exam Tip:** Always check whether the question specifies Pin 5 open (use standard formula) or Pin 5 driven by $V_c$ (use generalized logarithmic formula).
- **Memory Aid:** "**V**oltage **C**ontrols **O**scillation" — V-C-O = Pin 5 voltage, C is the capacitor that determines timing, O is the output frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capture Range, Lock-In Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capture Range, Lock-In Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Foundational Context: The Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)

Before diving into the ranges, we must establish the system in which they exist. A **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** is a closed-loop feedback control system that automatically adjusts the frequency of a Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) to match the frequency of an input reference signal. It comprises three fundamental building blocks:

1. **Phase Detector (PD)** — Multiplies the input signal with the VCO output to produce an error voltage proportional to the phase difference.
2. **Loop Filter (LF)** — A low-pass filter (typically a passive lead-lag or active PI filter) that removes high-frequency noise and shapes the dynamic response.
3. **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** — Generates an output whose frequency is linearly proportional to an applied control voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Hook:** In Module 2, the PLL is treated as a frequency-selective and tracking subsystem. The *Capture Range* and *Lock-In Range* are the two frequency-window parameters that completely define whether the PLL will work in a given application (e.g., FM demodulation, frequency synthesis, clock recovery).

---

## 1.2 Formal Definition of Lock-In Range ($\Delta\omega_L$)

> **Lock-In Range** (also called *Hold-In Range* or *Tracking Range*) is the maximum range of input frequencies, centered on the VCO's free-running frequency ($\omega_o$), over which the PLL can **maintain** phase lock once lock has already been established.

Mathematically:

$$\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v = \pm (K_d \cdot K_o)$$

where $K_d$ is the phase detector gain (in V/rad) and $K_o$ is the VCO sensitivity (in rad/s/V).

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Capture Range ($\Delta\omega_C$)

> **Capture Range** (also called *Acquisition Range*) is the maximum range of input frequencies, centered on $\omega_o$, over which the PLL can **acquire** lock starting from an *unlocked* condition.

Mathematically, for a second-order PLL with a passive lead-lag filter:

$$\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{\frac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}}$$

where $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are the time constants of the loop filter.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tug of War" Picture

Imagine you and a friend pulling on opposite ends of a rope, but **you can only see how tight the rope is**, not its exact length:

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Lock-In Range** | The range over which you can *hold* the rope steady once you've already matched your friend's pull strength. | Tracking range when already in lock. |
| **Capture Range** | The range over which you can *first* find and grab the rope when you are wildly out of sync. | Acquisition range from free-running state. |
| **VCO free-running freq. $\omega_o$** | Your natural resting pull-strength. | The frequency at which the VCO sits with zero control voltage. |
| **Pull-in time** | How long it takes you to stop slipping and firmly grab the rope. | Transient time before lock is achieved. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Invariant:** In **every** PLL configuration, the inequality $\boxed{\Delta\omega_C \le \Delta\omega_L}$ holds true. The capture range is always a *subset* of the lock-in range.

---

## 1.5 Why Are These Two Ranges Different?

The asymmetry arises because:

- **Once locked**, the loop's behaviour is governed by the **DC loop gain** ($K_v = K_d K_o$), since the low-frequency error signal passes through the filter unattenuated. This gives the wide **Lock-In Range**.

- **Before lock**, the VCO and input are beating at a difference frequency, producing a low-frequency *beat note* envelope on the PD output. This envelope **must pass through the loop filter** to slew the VCO frequency. The filter's finite bandwidth *attenuates* the beat note, narrowing the usable frequency range. This gives the narrower **Capture Range**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Exam Fact:** In a **first-order PLL** (no filter, unity gain loop), $\Delta\omega_C = \Delta\omega_L = K_v$. The two ranges collapse into one because there is no filter to attenuate the beat note.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

Let us trace what happens physically when an input frequency $\omega_i$ is applied to a PLL whose VCO is free-running at $\omega_o$:

### Step 1: Frequency Offset Detection
The phase detector multiplies $v_i(t)$ with $v_o(t)$, producing a DC error term plus a sum-frequency term and a **difference-frequency (beat) component** at $\vert \omega_i - \omega_o \vert$.

### Step 2: Beat Note Filtering
The loop filter (low-pass characteristic) strips the high-frequency sum term and partially passes the low-frequency beat envelope. If $\vert \omega_i - \omega_o \vert$ is *larger than the filter bandwidth*, the beat note is attenuated so heavily that it cannot appreciably shift the VCO frequency — **no lock is acquired**.

### Step 3: Capture Condition
If $\vert \omega_i - \omega_o \vert \le \Delta\omega_C$, the beat note is strong enough to slowly slew the VCO. As the VCO frequency creeps toward $\omega_i$, the beat frequency drops, the filter passes it more efficiently, and a **positive feedback runaway** occurs until the loop snaps into lock.

### Step 4: Steady-State Lock
Once locked, $\omega_o^{\prime} = \omega_i$ exactly. The phase detector produces a clean DC error voltage $v_d = K_d \cdot \phi_e$ where $\phi_e$ is the static phase error.

### Step 5: Tracking Window
The input frequency can now be slowly varied over a much wider band ($\pm \Delta\omega_L$) without losing lock, because the filter's low-frequency gain is unity and the full DC loop gain $K_v$ is available.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Physical "Why" |
|---|---|
| Beat note generation | Multiplication of two sinusoids in the PD is mathematically equivalent to $\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$. |
| Filter attenuation | A real low-pass filter rolls off above its cutoff $\omega_{LF}$, killing the beat note if it sits too high. |
| Runaway pull-in | As $\omega_{VCO}$ approaches $\omega_i$, the beat note frequency $\omega_{VCO} - \omega_i$ drops, filter gain rises, error grows, pull accelerates — a positive-feedback lock acquisition. |
| Asymmetric ranges | Filter attenuation depends on the *AC* beat note, but tracking depends on the *DC* error — hence two different bandwidths. |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are guaranteed 3-to-7 mark questions.**

| Parameter | Symbol | First-Order PLL | Second-Order PLL (Passive Lead-Lag) | Second-Order PLL (Active PI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Loop DC Gain | $K_v$ | $K_d K_o$ | $K_d K_o$ | $K_d K_o$ |
| **Lock-In Range** | $\Delta\omega_L$ | $\pm K_v$ | $\pm K_v$ | $\pm K_v$ |
| **Capture Range** | $\Delta\omega_C$ | $\pm K_v$ | $\pm \sqrt{\dfrac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}}$ | $\pm K_v$ (extended) |
| Natural Frequency | $\omega_n$ | — | $\sqrt{\dfrac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{K_v}{\tau_1}}$ |
| Damping Factor | $\zeta$ | — | $\dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_v}{\tau_1+\tau_2}}\cdot\tau_2$ | $\dfrac{\tau_2}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{K_v}{\tau_1}}$ |
| Pull-In Time | $T_p$ | — | $\approx \dfrac{(\Delta\omega)^2 \tau^2}{K_v^2 \cdot \text{const}}$ | Small |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Note:** Since $K_v$ in rad/s has units of *rad/s* and frequencies are often expressed in *Hz*, the conversion is $f = \omega / (2\pi)$. The same form holds for $\Delta f_L$ and $\Delta f_C$ in Hz.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why These Ranges Matter |
|---|---|
| **FM Stereo Demodulator** | $\Delta\omega_L$ must comfortably exceed the maximum FM deviation (75 kHz) for distortion-free demodulation. |
| **Frequency Synthesizers** | $\Delta\omega_C$ determines how fast the synthesizer can hop between channels during a frequency change. |
| **Clock & Data Recovery (CDR)** | The PLL must *capture* the incoming random bit stream's line rate ($\Delta\omega_C \ge$ tolerance) and *track* jitter over time ($\Delta\omega_L \ge$ wander). |
| **Carrier Recovery in PSK/QAM** | Lock-in range must accommodate Doppler shifts and oscillator drift. |

> [!WARNING]
> A common engineering mistake: designing a PLL with a *too-narrow* $\Delta\omega_C$ for the application. The loop will never lock, even if its tracking range is enormous.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lock-In Range

Consider a PLL in lock. The phase detector output is:

$$v_d(t) = K_d \, \phi_e$$

The VCO output frequency is:

$$\omega_{VCO}(t) = \omega_o + K_o \, v_c(t)$$

In lock, $\omega_{VCO} = \omega_i$, so:

$$\omega_i = \omega_o + K_o \, v_c$$

The control voltage $v_c$ equals the filtered PD output. For a DC (locked) error, the filter passes the signal with **unity DC gain** (assuming standard lead-lag or PI topology with finite DC gain of 1 in passive case, or large DC gain in PI case). In the passive filter case, the DC transfer is unity:

$$v_c = v_d = K_d \, \phi_e$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_i - \omega_o = K_o \cdot K_d \cdot \phi_e = K_v \cdot \phi_e$$

For lock to be maintained, the static phase error $\phi_e$ must satisfy $\vert \phi_e \vert \le \pi/2$ (boundary of the phase detector's linear range for a multiplier-type PD).

Setting $\phi_e = \pm \pi/2$:

$$\Delta\omega_L = \omega_i - \omega_o \bigg\vert_{max} = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} K_v$$

For a sinusoidal phase detector, the effective limit on $\phi_e$ is the same, but if we define the lock range as the frequency at which the loop can no longer hold:

$$\boxed{\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v}$$

> **Note:** Some KTU textbooks include the $\pi/2$ factor, while others use the simplified $\pm K_v$ form assuming the phase detector is normalized (i.e., $K_d$ already absorbs the $\pi/2$ bound). The KTU 2024 scheme standard answer accepts **$\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v$** as the canonical form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Capture Range (Second-Order Passive Lead-Lag)

The phase detector output, when out of lock, contains a beat note:

$$v_d(t) = K_d \sin\!\big[(\omega_i - \omega_o)t + \phi_o\big]$$

The passive lead-lag loop filter has transfer function:

$$F(s) = \frac{s\tau_2 + 1}{s(\tau_1 + \tau_2) + 1}$$

The DC gain is unity and the high-frequency gain is $\tau_2 / (\tau_1 + \tau_2)$.

For a sinusoidal beat at angular frequency $\Omega = \omega_i - \omega_o$, the filter's magnitude response is:

$$\vert F(j\Omega) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \Omega^2 \tau_2^2}}{\sqrt{1 + \Omega^2 (\tau_1 + \tau_2)^2}}$$

As the loop slews, the average (DC) component of the filtered error is:

$$\overline{v_c} = \frac{K_d}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\tau_2}{\tau_1 + \tau_2} \cdot \text{(rms of beat through filter)}$$

The simplified result, derived by balancing the slew rate against the filter's attenuation of the beat note, yields:

$$\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{\frac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}}$$

**Re-arranging** the capture condition $\vert \Omega \vert \le \Delta\omega_C$:

$$\Omega^2 \le \frac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}$$

This is the standard closed-form result found in Salivahanan, Roy Choudhury, and most KTU reference texts.

---

## 3.3 Work-Out Example (Model Problem)

> **Problem:** A second-order PLL uses a phase detector with $K_d = 0.5$ V/rad and a VCO with $K_o = 2\pi \times 20$ kHz/V. The passive lead-lag filter has $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 1$ k$\Omega$, $C = 0.1$ $\mu$F. Calculate (a) Lock-In Range, (b) Capture Range, (c) the natural frequency $\omega_n$.

**Step 1: Compute the time constants**

$$\tau_1 = R_1 C = 10^4 \times 10^{-7} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

$$\tau_2 = R_2 C = 10^3 \times 10^{-7} = 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compute the loop gain $K_v$**

$$K_v = K_d \cdot K_o = 0.5 \times (2\pi \times 20 \times 10^3)$$

$$K_v = 0.5 \times 1.2566 \times 10^5 = 6.283 \times 10^4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3: Lock-In Range**

$$\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v = \pm 6.283 \times 10^4 \text{ rad/s} = \pm 10 \text{ kHz (in Hz)}$$

**Step 4: Capture Range**

$$\tau_1 + \tau_2 = 1.1 \text{ ms} = 1.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{\frac{6.283 \times 10^4}{1.1 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{5.712 \times 10^7} = \pm 7.557 \times 10^3 \text{ rad/s}$$

Converting to Hz:

$$\Delta f_C = \frac{7.557 \times 10^3}{2\pi} \approx \pm 1.20 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 5: Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{6.283 \times 10^4}{1.1 \times 10^{-3}}} \approx 7.557 \text{ krad/s}$$

> **Verification:** Note that $\Delta\omega_C = \omega_n$ for this filter topology — a useful KTU-style sanity check.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PLL Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal vi t] --> B[Phase Detector PD]
    C[VCO Output vo t] --> B
    B --> D[Loop Filter LF]
    D --> E[VCO Voltage Controlled Oscillator]
    E --> C
    E --> F[Locked Output]
```

> The **Phase Detector** compares the input and VCO signals, producing a phase-error voltage. The **Loop Filter** cleans this error voltage, and the cleaned signal steers the **VCO** frequency. Once lock is achieved, the VCO output tracks the input.

## 4.2 Lock vs Capture Range Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OUT["Outside ΔωC"]
        Z1[Input too far from ωo]
    end
    subgraph CAP["Inside Capture Range but Outside Lock Range"]
        Z2[Loop may or may not lock]
    end
    subgraph LOCK["Inside Lock In Range"]
        Z3[Loop will track reliably]
    end
    subgraph PROC["Acquisition Process"]
        P1[VCO free runs at ωo] --> P2[Beat note produced at PD]
        P2 --> P3{Beat within filter BW}
        P3 -- Yes --> P4[VCO slews toward ωi]
        P4 --> P5[Phase lock acquired]
        P3 -- No --> Z1
    end
```

## 4.3 Acquisition vs Tracking — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: VCO free running at ωo] --> S1[Apply input at ωi]
    S1 --> S2{Is ωi within ΔωC?}
    S2 -- No --> SXX[No lock possible. Loop stays unlocked]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Beat note develops at PD output]
    S3 --> S4[Loop filter passes beat envelope]
    S4 --> S5[VCO frequency slews toward ωi]
    S5 --> S6[Beat frequency decreases]
    S6 --> S7[Filter gain increases on beat]
    S7 --> S8[Runaway pull in occurs]
    S8 --> S9[Phase lock achieved]
    S9 --> S10{Is ωi within ΔωL?}
    S10 -- Yes --> S11[Loop tracks. Static phase error φe]
    S10 -- No --> S12[Loop loses lock]
```

## 4.4 Frequency-Domain Map of the Two Ranges

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0["ωo - ΔωL"] --> L1["ωo - ΔωC"]
    L1 --> L2["ωo"]
    L2 --> L3["ωo + ΔωC"]
    L3 --> L4["ωo + ΔωL"]
    L0 -.->|no lock| X1[Unlocked]
    L1 -.->|capture only| X2[Acquisition]
    L2 -.->|locked at center| X3[Zero phase error]
    L3 -.->|capture only| X4[Acquisition]
    L4 -.->|no lock| X5[Unlocked]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Inside $\Delta\omega_C$, lock can be *acquired*. Inside $\Delta\omega_L$ (which extends beyond $\Delta\omega_C$), lock can be *maintained*. The shaded inner band is the **operational sweet-spot** where both acquisition and tracking are guaranteed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Lock-In Range and Capture Range of a PLL. Why is the capture range always smaller than the lock-in range in a second-order PLL?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lock-In Range ($\Delta\omega_L$):** The range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *maintain* phase lock once it is already locked. (1 mark)
- **Capture Range ($\Delta\omega_C$):** The range of input frequencies over which the PLL can *acquire* lock starting from an unlocked condition. (1 mark)
- **Reason for $\Delta\omega_C < \Delta\omega_L$:** Before lock, the PD output is a beat note that must pass through the loop filter; the filter attenuates this beat note if its frequency lies outside the filter bandwidth. After lock, the PD output is a DC error that passes through the filter unattenuated. Since the filter limits acquisition but not tracking, $\Delta\omega_C \le \Delta\omega_L$. (1 mark)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the expressions for Lock-In Range and Capture Range of a second-order PLL using a passive lead-lag filter, in terms of the loop gain and filter time constants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Lock-In Range:** $\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v = \pm K_d K_o$ (1.5 marks)
- **Capture Range:** $\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{\dfrac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}}$ (1.5 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *ESE Module Question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Two parallel questions (A and B) are given below — you answer exactly one.*

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Lock-In Range of a second-order PLL with a passive lead-lag filter. (7 marks)

**(b)** A PLL has $K_d = 0.8$ V/rad, $K_o = 2\pi \times 25$ kHz/V, and a passive lead-lag filter with $\tau_1 = 2$ ms, $\tau_2 = 0.2$ ms. Compute the Lock-In Range, Capture Range, natural frequency, and damping factor. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

In lock, the phase detector output is $v_d = K_d \phi_e$ where $\phi_e$ is the static phase error. After the loop filter (DC gain = 1 for passive lead-lag), the VCO control voltage is $v_c = v_d$. The VCO frequency becomes:

$$\omega_{VCO} = \omega_o + K_o v_c = \omega_o + K_o K_d \phi_e$$

In lock, $\omega_{VCO} = \omega_i$, so:

$$\omega_i - \omega_o = K_v \phi_e$$

The maximum permissible phase error for a sinusoidal PD is $\pm \pi/2$. Hence:

$$\boxed{\Delta\omega_L = \pm \frac{\pi}{2} K_v \quad \text{or in normalized form} \quad \pm K_v}$$

(7 marks distributed: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 2 for boundary condition)

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Step 1: Loop Gain**

$$K_v = K_d K_o = 0.8 \times 2\pi \times 25 \times 10^3 = 0.8 \times 1.5708 \times 10^5 = 1.2566 \times 10^5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2: Lock-In Range**

$$\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v = \pm 1.2566 \times 10^5 \text{ rad/s} = \pm 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Capture Range**

$$\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{\frac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}} = \pm \sqrt{\frac{1.2566 \times 10^5}{2.2 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$= \pm \sqrt{5.711 \times 10^7} = \pm 7.557 \times 10^3 \text{ rad/s} = \pm 1.203 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4: Natural Frequency**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_v}{\tau_1 + \tau_2}} \approx 7.557 \text{ krad/s}$$

**Step 5: Damping Factor**

$$\zeta = \frac{\tau_2}{2} \sqrt{\frac{K_v}{\tau_1(\tau_1 + \tau_2)}} = \frac{0.2 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \sqrt{\frac{1.2566 \times 10^5}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.2 \times 10^{-3}}}$$

$$= 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2.857 \times 10^{10}} = 10^{-4} \times 1.69 \times 10^5 \approx 16.9$$

(7 marks distributed: 2 for $K_v$, 2 for $\Delta\omega_L$ + $\Delta\omega_C$, 1.5 for $\omega_n$, 1.5 for $\zeta$)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a PLL and clearly mark the points where Lock-In Range and Capture Range are determined. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare first-order, second-order (passive lead-lag) and second-order (active PI) PLLs in terms of capture range, lock-in range, acquisition time, and noise performance. State one application where each is most suitable. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Block Diagram** (also given in Section 4.1 above — student must redraw during exam). (3 marks)

**Working Principle:**
- The **Phase Detector** multiplies input $v_i$ with VCO output $v_o$, producing an error voltage containing a DC component (proportional to phase difference) and a sum-frequency component.
- The **Loop Filter** removes the sum-frequency term and the high-frequency components of the error.
- The **VCO** adjusts its output frequency in proportion to the filtered control voltage.
- In lock, $\omega_{VCO} = \omega_i$ with a finite static phase error. (2 marks)

**Range Determination:**
- **Lock-In Range** is determined at the *steady-state* lock point — depends on the DC loop gain $K_v$ and the phase detector's linear range. (1 mark)
- **Capture Range** is determined *before* lock — depends on the loop filter's bandwidth and how much of the AC beat note is passed to slew the VCO. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

| Parameter | First-Order | Second-Order (Passive) | Second-Order (Active PI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Delta\omega_L$ | $\pm K_v$ | $\pm K_v$ | $\pm K_v$ |
| $\Delta\omega_C$ | $\pm K_v$ | $\pm \sqrt{K_v/(\tau_1+\tau_2)}$ | $\pm K_v$ (extended) |
| Acquisition Time | Fastest | Slowest | Medium |
| Noise Performance | Poor (no filtering) | Better (LP filtering) | Best (extra integrator) |
| Typical Use | Simple tone decoding | General-purpose FM demod | High-precision synthesizers |

(7 marks: 4 for table content, 3 for applications)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on PLL range questions:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing $K_v$ in Hz instead of rad/s. Always verify $K_o$ is in rad/s/V, not Hz/V. Multiply by $2\pi$ if needed.
> 2. **Forgetting the filter effect:** Using $\Delta\omega_C = \pm K_v$ for a second-order PLL. This is only correct for a first-order PLL or a PLL with an active PI filter.
> 3. **Mixing $\phi_e$ bounds:** Stating $\phi_e \le \pi$ instead of $\pi/2$ for a multiplier-type PD. The linear range is $\pm \pi/2$ (within which $\sin \phi_e \approx \phi_e$).
> 4. **Time constant error:** Using $\tau_1$ alone instead of $\tau_1 + \tau_2$ in the capture-range formula. The filter's *effective* time constant is the **sum** for a passive lead-lag network.
> 5. **No mention of inequality:** Failing to explicitly state $\Delta\omega_C \le \Delta\omega_L$ — a 1-mark question that students routinely skip.
> 6. **Sign convention:** Always give both $\pm$ signs; PLLs are symmetric about $\omega_o$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Lock-In Range** = tracking window when already locked: $\Delta\omega_L = \pm K_v$
- **Capture Range** = acquisition window from unlocked: $\Delta\omega_C = \pm \sqrt{K_v / (\tau_1 + \tau_2)}$ for passive lead-lag PLL
- **Universal Inequality:** $\Delta\omega_C \le \Delta\omega_L$ always.
- **First-Order PLL:** $\Delta\omega_C = \Delta\omega_L = K_v$ (no filter to attenuate beat).
- **Active PI Filter PLL:** $\Delta\omega_C \approx \Delta\omega_L = K_v$ (extended capture range).
- **Loop Gain:** $K_v = K_d \cdot K_o$, with $K_d$ in V/rad and $K_o$ in rad/s/V.
- **Conversion:** $f = \omega / 2\pi$ when answering in Hz.
- **Pull-In Time** depends on $\Delta\omega^2 / K_v^2$ and increases sharply as $\omega_i$ approaches $\Delta\omega_C$.
- **Active filters** are preferred when **wide capture range + good noise rejection** are both required.
- **Application Key Words:** FM demodulator → second-order passive; Frequency synthesizer → active PI; Tone decoder → first-order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of PLL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of PLL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Phase Locked Loop (PLL)** is a negative feedback control system that automatically adjusts the phase of a locally generated signal (from a VCO) to match the phase of an input reference signal. The **Applications of PLL** refer to the wide range of signal-processing tasks that exploit this phase-locking property, including **frequency synthesis, frequency multiplication/division, modulation/demodulation, signal conditioning, and clock recovery**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the principal applications studied are:

1. **Frequency Multiplier** (with frequency divider in feedback)
2. **Frequency Divider** (with frequency multiplier in feedback)
3. **Frequency Synthesizer**
4. **AM Demodulator (Synchronous Detector)**
5. **FM Demodulator**
6. **FSK (Frequency Shift Keying) Demodulator**
7. **Tracking Filter**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Of the applications listed above, **Frequency Multiplier, Frequency Divider, AM Demodulator, FM Demodulator, and FSK Demodulator** carry the highest weightage in university examination questions. Questions of up to **14 marks** are routinely framed around these.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Phase Locking:** A state in which the **difference in phase** between the input signal and the VCO output is held **constant** with respect to time. Under lock, the frequencies of the two signals are **exactly equal**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Blind Piano Tuner"

Imagine a piano tuner who is **blindfolded** and must tune a piano string to a perfect pitch given by a tuning fork. The tuner does the following:

- **Listens** to the *beat frequency* (the "phasing" between the fork and the string).
- **Turns the tuning peg** a tiny amount to reduce the beat frequency.
- Keeps doing this until the beat frequency is **zero** — at that instant, the string is in perfect tune (frequency-locked and phase-locked).

A PLL behaves identically:
- The **Phase Detector (PD)** "listens" to the phase difference between the input and VCO.
- The **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** averages this difference into a smooth DC control voltage.
- The **VCO** "turns the tuning peg" by changing its frequency to match the input.

Once the phase difference is constant, the loop is **locked**, and the VCO control voltage becomes a **faithful replica** of the modulating information.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The PLL is essentially a **smart, automatic tuner**. Once locked, the **control voltage of the VCO** carries the demodulated information. This is the key insight behind all demodulator applications.

## 1.3 Standard Block Recap (Carried from Previous Module)

The basic PLL has three building blocks:

- **Phase Detector (PD):** Multiplies the input signal with the VCO output to produce an error voltage proportional to the **phase difference** $\phi_{e}$.
- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Removes the high-frequency sum term; passes only the slowly varying error.
- **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO):** Generates an output whose frequency deviates from its free-running value $f_{o}$ in proportion to the control voltage $v_{c}(t)$.

In **all applications**, the basic PLL is **augmented** with an additional block inside or outside the feedback path.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Application 1 — Frequency Multiplier

### 2.1.1 Concept
A frequency multiplier produces an output frequency that is an **integer multiple (N)** of the input frequency. The PLL achieves this by inserting a **frequency divider** ($\div N$) in the feedback path between the VCO and the Phase Detector.

### 2.1.2 Operational Logic
1. The input frequency is $f_{in}$.
2. The VCO produces a frequency $f_{out}$ that is fed back to the PD **after** division by $N$.
3. In lock condition: the frequency at the PD inputs must be equal.
4. Therefore, $f_{out} / N = f_{in}$ — leading to $f_{out} = N f_{in}$.

### 2.1.3 Why This Works
The PLL is a negative feedback system. It automatically forces the **divided VCO frequency** to track the input. Whatever input is given, the VCO shifts itself to be exactly $N$ times higher. The multiplication factor $N$ is set digitally by the divider, making the system **programmable**.

## 2.2 Application 2 — Frequency Divider

### 2.2.1 Concept
A frequency divider produces an output that is an **integer sub-multiple** of the input. The PLL achieves this by inserting a **frequency multiplier ($\times N$)** in the feedback path.

### 2.2.2 Operational Logic
1. The input frequency is $f_{in}$.
2. The VCO produces a frequency $f_{out}$ that is fed back **after multiplication by $N$**.
3. In lock: $N \cdot f_{out} = f_{in}$ — therefore $f_{out} = f_{in} / N$.

## 2.3 Application 3 — Frequency Synthesizer

### 2.3.1 Concept
A frequency synthesizer generates a **range of stable, precise frequencies** from a single, highly-stable reference (usually a crystal oscillator). It uses a **variable division ratio $N$** in the feedback path so that the output frequency can be **digitally selected**.

### 2.3.2 Operational Logic
1. A **crystal reference** of frequency $f_{ref}$ is applied at the input.
2. A **programmable divider** ($\div N$) is in the feedback path.
3. The VCO produces $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$.
4. By varying $N$, the output frequency can be changed in **discrete steps of $f_{ref}$**.

This is the basis of every modern radio tuner, mobile phone, and digital communication transceiver.

## 2.4 Application 4 — FM Demodulator

### 2.4.1 Concept
A PLL is an excellent **FM demodulator** because, in lock, the VCO control voltage $v_{c}(t)$ is **directly proportional to the instantaneous frequency deviation** of the input FM signal.

### 2.4.2 Operational Logic
1. The input is an FM wave: $f_{i}(t) = f_{c} + k_{f} m(t)$.
2. The VCO output is locked to this input — the VCO frequency exactly tracks $f_{i}(t)$.
3. Since $f_{vco} = f_{o} + K_{v} v_{c}(t)$, the control voltage $v_{c}(t)$ must vary in such a way as to make $f_{vco}$ equal to $f_{i}(t)$ at every instant.
4. Therefore: $v_{c}(t) \propto m(t)$ — the original modulating signal is recovered directly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** A PLL FM demodulator provides a **linear demodulation characteristic** over a much wider range of input frequency deviation than a conventional **Foster-Seeley** or **Ratio Detector** discriminator. This is why high-fidelity communication receivers use PLLs.

## 2.5 Application 5 — AM Demodulator (Synchronous Detector)

### 2.5.1 Concept
A PLL can be used as a **synchronous (coherent) AM detector** by locking onto the carrier component of the AM signal. The recovered VCO output serves as a **local carrier** that is **exactly synchronous** with the incoming carrier.

### 2.5.2 Operational Logic
1. The input AM signal has a carrier $f_{c}$ and sidebands.
2. The PLL locks onto the carrier component.
3. The VCO output is multiplied with the input AM signal in a separate multiplier.
4. Low-pass filtering recovers the modulating signal $m(t)$.

The big advantage over a simple envelope detector is that the PLL synchronous detector works even for **suppressed-carrier AM (DSB-SC)**, where the carrier is missing.

## 2.6 Application 6 — FSK Demodulator

### 2.6.1 Concept
**FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)** uses two discrete frequencies — a **mark (logic 1)** frequency $f_{1}$ and a **space (logic 0)** frequency $f_{2}$ — to transmit binary data.

### 2.6.2 Operational Logic
1. A PLL is set up so its **lock range** straddles both $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$.
2. When the input switches between $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$, the VCO control voltage $v_{c}(t)$ shifts between two distinct DC levels.
3. A **voltage comparator** (Schmitt trigger) at the LPF output converts these two levels into logic 0 and logic 1.
4. The original binary data stream is recovered.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** The `vert` and `mid` symbols are used in place of the standard pipe character to prevent any markdown-table syntax conflicts.

| Application | Configuration | Output Frequency Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Multiplier** | Divide-by-$N$ in feedback | $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$ | $N$ is integer $\ge 2$ |
| **Frequency Divider** | Multiply-by-$N$ in feedback | $f_{out} = f_{in} / N$ | $N$ is integer $\ge 2$ |
| **Frequency Synthesizer** | Variable divide-by-$N$ in feedback | $f_{out} = N \cdot f_{ref}$ | $f_{ref}$ from crystal |
| **FM Demodulator** | Basic PLL | $v_{c}(t) \propto m(t)$ | Linear characteristic |
| **AM Demodulator** | PLL + multiplier + LPF | Recovers $m(t)$ | Works for DSB-SC |
| **FSK Demodulator** | PLL + Schmitt trigger | Logic 0 / 1 output | $f_{1}, f_{2}$ in lock range |

### 2.8 Universal PLL Performance Formulas

| Parameter | Formula (General) | Typical IC Value (e.g., NE565) |
|---|---|---|
| **Free-running frequency** | $f_{o} \approx \dfrac{0.3}{R_{1} C_{1}}$ | $\approx 0.3$ Hz to 500 kHz |
| **Lock range** ($\Delta f_{L}$) | $\Delta f_{L} = f_{L max} - f_{L min}$ | Proportional to $K_{v} K_{\phi}$ |
| **Capture range** ($\Delta f_{C}$) | $\Delta f_{C} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta f_{L}}{R_{1} C_{1}}}$ | Always $\le \Delta f_{L}$ |
| **Hold-in range** | $\Delta f_{H} = \dfrac{8 f_{o} V_{cc}}{V_{cc} - V_{c \, saturate}}$ | For 565 IC |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Frequency synthesizers based on PLLs are the heart of every **smartphone baseband, Wi-Fi router, GPS receiver, satellite tuner, and software-defined radio**. The PLL allows a stable crystal reference to generate thousands of channel frequencies with sub-Hz precision.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Frequency of PLL Frequency Multiplier

We start from the principle of **frequency lock**: when a PLL is in lock, the instantaneous frequency of the input signal **exactly equals** the instantaneous frequency of the signal arriving at the **other input of the phase detector**.

Let:
- $f_{in}$ = input signal frequency
- $f_{out}$ = VCO output frequency
- $N$ = division ratio of the feedback divider

**Step 1:** The feedback path contains a divide-by-$N$ circuit, so the frequency reaching the second PD input is:

$$f_{fb} = \frac{f_{out}}{N}$$

**Step 2:** Under lock condition, the PD requires both its inputs to have the **same frequency**. Thus:

$$f_{in} = f_{fb}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the expression from Step 1 into Step 2:

$$f_{in} = \frac{f_{out}}{N}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $f_{out}$ by multiplying both sides by $N$:

$$f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$$

**Result:** The output is exactly $N$ times the input — i.e., frequency multiplication by $N$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Output Frequency of PLL Frequency Divider

For the divider, a **multiply-by-$N$** block is placed in the feedback path.

**Step 1:** The feedback frequency reaching the PD is:

$$f_{fb} = N \cdot f_{out}$$

**Step 2:** Under lock:

$$f_{in} = f_{fb}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$f_{in} = N \cdot f_{out}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for $f_{out}$:

$$f_{out} = \frac{f_{in}}{N}$$

**Result:** The output is the input divided by $N$ — i.e., frequency division by $N$.

## 3.3 Derivation: PLL FM Demodulator Output

The instantaneous input frequency for an FM signal is:

$$f_{i}(t) = f_{c} + k_{f} \cdot m(t)$$

The VCO output frequency in response to control voltage $v_{c}(t)$ is:

$$f_{vco}(t) = f_{o} + K_{v} \cdot v_{c}(t)$$

**Step 1:** Under lock, the VCO frequency must equal the input frequency:

$$f_{o} + K_{v} \cdot v_{c}(t) = f_{c} + k_{f} \cdot m(t)$$

**Step 2:** The VCO is designed such that its free-running frequency equals the carrier frequency: $f_{o} = f_{c}$. Hence those terms cancel:

$$K_{v} \cdot v_{c}(t) = k_{f} \cdot m(t)$$

**Step 3:** Solving for the control voltage:

$$v_{c}(t) = \frac{k_{f}}{K_{v}} \cdot m(t)$$

**Result:** The VCO control voltage is a **scaled, faithful reproduction** of the original modulating signal. The constant $k_{f} / K_{v}$ is the demodulator gain, and is **frequency-independent** — giving the PLL FM demodulator its **wide, flat demodulation response**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Capture Range vs Lock Range (General)

Let:
- $K_{\phi}$ = Phase detector gain (V/rad)
- $K_{v}$ = VCO gain (rad/s/V)
- $\tau_{1}, \tau_{2}$ = LPF time constants
- $f_{o}$ = free-running VCO frequency

**Lock Range** (the band of frequencies over which the PLL **remains** locked once lock is achieved):

$$\Delta f_{L} = K_{\phi} \cdot K_{v}$$

**Capture Range** (the band of frequencies over which the PLL **acquires** lock from an unlocked state):

$$\Delta f_{C} \approx \sqrt{\Delta f_{L} \cdot f_{LPF \, cutoff}}$$

Specifically, for a first-order LPF of cutoff $f_{c \, LPF}$:

$$\Delta f_{C} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta f_{L}}{\pi \cdot R_{1} C_{1}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Capture range is always **smaller** than lock range because the LPF attenuates the high-frequency sum term; only when the input is close enough to $f_{o}$ for the beat to pass through the LPF will lock be acquired. Once locked, the strong negative feedback maintains lock over a wider band.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operation Summary

| Application | Extra Block | Key Equation | Recovered Output | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Multiplier | $\div N$ in feedback | $f_{out} = N f_{in}$ | High-frequency tone | Tone generators, mixers |
| Frequency Divider | $\times N$ in feedback | $f_{out} = f_{in} / N$ | Sub-harmonic | Digital clocks |
| Synthesizer | Programmable $\div N$ | $f_{out} = N f_{ref}$ | Discrete $f$ steps | Radios, mobiles |
| FM Demodulator | None | $v_{c} \propto m(t)$ | Audio signal | Broadcast FM receivers |
| AM Demodulator | Multiplier + LPF | $v_{o} \propto m(t)$ | Audio signal | DSB-SC receivers |
| FSK Demodulator | Schmitt trigger | $v_{o} = 0$ or $1$ | Digital bits | Modems |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — PLL as Frequency Multiplier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal fin] --> B[Phase Detector]
    C[VCO Output fout] --> D[Divide by N Block]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Low Pass Filter]
    E --> F[VCO]
    F --> C
    F --> G[Output fout = N times fin]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** The VCO generates a high frequency, which is **divided down** by $N$ before being compared to the input. The PLL forces these two to be equal, automatically making the VCO $N$ times the input.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PLL as Frequency Divider

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal fin] --> B[Phase Detector]
    C[VCO Output fout] --> D[Multiply by N Block]
    D --> B
    B --> E[Low Pass Filter]
    E --> F[VCO]
    F --> C
    F --> G[Output fout = fin divided by N]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PLL as FM Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FM Input Signal fi of t] --> B[Phase Detector]
    F[VCO Output] --> B
    B --> E[Low Pass Filter]
    E --> G[Demodulated Audio vc of t]
    E --> F
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** The basic PLL itself is the FM demodulator. The VCO control voltage is **directly** the recovered audio.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — PLL as AM (Synchronous) Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[AM Input Signal] --> B1[Multiplier X1]
    F[VCO Output Locked to Carrier] --> B1
    B1 --> E1[Low Pass Filter 1]
    F --> C[PLL Feedback Path]
    A --> C
    E1 --> G[Demodulated Audio Output]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** The PLL recovers a clean, synchronous replica of the carrier. Multiplying the AM signal with this synchronous carrier shifts the spectrum to baseband. The LPF then extracts the original audio.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — PLL as FSK Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FSK Input f1 or f2] --> B[Phase Detector]
    F[VCO Output] --> B
    B --> E[Low Pass Filter]
    E --> H[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    H --> G[Recovered Binary Data 0 or 1]
    E --> F
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** The LPF produces a **two-level DC voltage** corresponding to the two input frequencies. The Schmitt trigger snaps this into clean logic levels.

## 4.6 Functional Flow — All Applications Compared

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Basic PLL Core PD, LPF, VCO] --> Q1{Application?}
    Q1 -->|Multiply output freq| P1[Insert divide by N in feedback]
    Q1 -->|Divide output freq| P2[Insert multiply by N in feedback]
    Q1 -->|Synthesize discrete freqs| P3[Insert programmable divide by N]
    Q1 -->|Demodulate FM| P4[Use VCO control voltage directly]
    Q1 -->|Demodulate AM| P5[Multiply input with VCO output, then LPF]
    Q1 -->|Demodulate FSK| P6[Use Schmitt trigger on LPF output]
    P1 --> Out1[fo equals N times fin]
    P2 --> Out2[fo equals fin divided by N]
    P3 --> Out3[fo equals N times fref, programmable]
    P4 --> Out4[vc of t proportional to m of t]
    P5 --> Out5[Recovered m of t from DSB or DSB SC]
    P6 --> Out6[Recovered binary stream]
    style Start fill:#a9cce3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Q1 fill:#f5b041,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P4 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P5 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style P6 fill:#d5dbdb,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Out1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Out2 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Out3 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000

    style Out5 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style Out6 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain how a PLL can be used as a frequency multiplier. Derive the expression for the output frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A PLL frequency multiplier is constructed by inserting a **frequency divider ($\div N$)** in the feedback path between the VCO output and the Phase Detector input. Under lock condition, the two inputs to the phase detector must have the **same frequency**. Therefore:

$$\frac{f_{out}}{N} = f_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{out} = N \cdot f_{in}$$

The output frequency is an integer multiple of the input. By selecting different division ratios, the multiplication factor can be made programmable.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**List any three applications of PLL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Frequency Multiplier/Divider/Synthesizer** — for generating stable frequencies from a reference.
2. **FM Demodulator** — the VCO control voltage directly gives back the modulating signal.
3. **FSK Demodulator** — by combining the PLL with a Schmitt trigger at the LPF output.

(Other valid applications: AM synchronous detector, tracking filter, motor speed control, clock recovery.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Do not write $f_{out} = f_{in}$ for a frequency multiplier** — students routinely forget the role of the feedback divider. Always explicitly state the **block inserted** in the feedback path and show the **division or multiplication step-by-step**.
> 2. **Do not confuse capture range with lock range** — capture range is **always smaller** than lock range. Many students mark them equal and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **In FM demodulation, the demodulated output is $v_{c}(t)$, NOT $v_{o}(t)$** of any extra stage** — the control voltage of the VCO itself is the output.
> 4. **Always draw the block diagram with the PD, LPF, VCO labeled in the correct order** — the PD output goes to LPF, LPF output goes to VCO, and VCO output loops back to the PD.

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PLL as a frequency multiplier and frequency divider. Derive the expression for the output frequency in each case. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain how a PLL can be used as an FM demodulator. Derive the expression for the demodulated output voltage. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Frequency Multiplier and Divider (7 marks):**

**Frequency Multiplier:**

- **Block Diagram:** PD $\rightarrow$ LPF $\rightarrow$ VCO $\rightarrow$ **Divider ($\div N$)** $\rightarrow$ back to PD.
- **Operation:** Under lock, frequency at PD inputs must match. The divider reduces the VCO output by $N$. So the PLL automatically pushes the VCO to a frequency $N$ times higher than the input.
- **Derivation:** $\dfrac{f_{out}}{N} = f_{in}$ $\Rightarrow$ $f_{out} = N f_{in}$. **(2 marks for derivation)**

**Frequency Divider:**

- **Block Diagram:** PD $\rightarrow$ LPF $\rightarrow$ VCO $\rightarrow$ **Multiplier ($\times N$)** $\rightarrow$ back to PD.
- **Operation:** The multiplier raises the VCO output by $N$ before comparison. The PLL therefore forces the VCO to a frequency $N$ times **lower** than the input.
- **Derivation:** $N \cdot f_{out} = f_{in}$ $\Rightarrow$ $f_{out} = f_{in} / N$. **(2 marks for derivation)**

**(Total 7 marks: 1 mark each for block diagram, 1 mark for operation, 2 marks for derivation, both applications)**

---

**Part (b) — PLL as FM Demodulator (7 marks):**

The basic PLL itself acts as an FM demodulator. The input FM signal has instantaneous frequency:

$$f_{i}(t) = f_{c} + k_{f} m(t)$$

The VCO output frequency in response to control voltage $v_{c}(t)$ is:

$$f_{vco}(t) = f_{o} + K_{v} v_{c}(t)$$

**Step 1:** Under lock, $f_{vco} = f_{i}$, hence:

$$f_{o} + K_{v} v_{c}(t) = f_{c} + k_{f} m(t)$$

**Step 2:** Since VCO free-running frequency equals carrier frequency $f_{o} = f_{c}$:

$$K_{v} v_{c}(t) = k_{f} m(t)$$

**Step 3:** Solving for control voltage:

$$v_{c}(t) = \frac{k_{f}}{K_{v}} m(t)$$

The VCO control voltage is a **scaled, linear reproduction** of the original message signal. The constant $k_{f}/K_{v}$ is the demodulator gain.

**Advantages over conventional discriminators:**
- Wide linear demodulation range.
- Low distortion.
- Simple to implement using a single IC (e.g., NE565).

**(7 marks: 2 for concept, 3 for derivation, 2 for advantages)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a PLL as a frequency synthesizer. Mention its use in modern communication systems. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain how a PLL is used as an FSK demodulator. Draw the block diagram and the output waveform. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — PLL as Frequency Synthesizer (7 marks):**

- **Definition:** A frequency synthesizer generates a set of stable, precise frequencies from a single highly-stable reference.
- **Block Diagram:** Reference oscillator (crystal) $\rightarrow$ PD $\rightarrow$ LPF $\rightarrow$ VCO $\rightarrow$ **Programmable divider ($\div N$)** $\rightarrow$ back to PD.
- **Operation:** The VCO output is divided by $N$ and compared to the crystal reference. Under lock, $f_{out} / N = f_{ref}$ $\Rightarrow$ $f_{out} = N f_{ref}$. By varying $N$, the output frequency can be switched in **discrete steps of $f_{ref}$**.
- **Key features:** The output inherits the **long-term stability of the crystal**, but offers a wide range of programmable output frequencies.

**Applications in modern systems:**
- Cellular phones (channel selection)
- Satellite transponders
- Wi-Fi and Bluetooth transceivers
- GPS receivers
- Software-defined radios

**(7 marks: 2 for block + operation, 2 for derivation, 3 for applications)**

---

**Part (b) — PLL as FSK Demodulator (7 marks):**

**FSK Signal:**
Binary data is sent as two frequencies:
- $f_{1}$ for **mark** (logic 1)
- $f_{2}$ for **space** (logic 0)

**Block Diagram:** FSK input $\rightarrow$ PD $\rightarrow$ LPF $\rightarrow$ **Schmitt Trigger** $\rightarrow$ Binary data output. LPF output also drives the VCO (closing the loop).

**Operation:**
1. The PLL's **lock range** is designed to enclose both $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ (with $f_{o}$ centered between them).
2. When input is $f_{1}$, the LPF output sits at one DC level $V_{1}$.
3. When input is $f_{2}$, the LPF output sits at another DC level $V_{2}$.
4. The Schmitt trigger converts $V_{1}$ to logic 1 and $V_{2}$ to logic 0 (or vice versa).
5. The original binary stream is recovered.

**Output waveform:** Square wave matching the original binary pattern.

**(7 marks: 2 for block diagram, 3 for operation, 2 for output description)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- A **PLL** is a negative-feedback system that forces the VCO output to track the input in both **frequency and phase**.
- **Frequency Multiplier:** Insert a **$\div N$** block in the feedback path. Output: $f_{out} = N f_{in}$.
- **Frequency Divider:** Insert a **$\times N$** block in the feedback path. Output: $f_{out} = f_{in} / N$.
- **Frequency Synthesizer:** Use a **programmable $\div N$** with a **crystal reference**. Output: $f_{out} = N f_{ref}$. Switches in steps of $f_{ref}$.
- **FM Demodulator:** The VCO control voltage $v_{c}(t) = (k_{f}/K_{v}) \cdot m(t)$ directly recovers the message.
- **AM Demodulator (Synchronous):** PLL tracks the carrier, then a multiplier + LPF recovers the message. Works for **suppressed-carrier AM** (DSB-SC).
- **FSK Demodulator:** LPF gives two-level DC; **Schmitt trigger** converts to clean binary bits.
- **Lock range** $\ge$ **Capture range** — always.
- **Lock range** $\Delta f_{L} = K_{\phi} K_{v}$ (product of PD and VCO gains).
- **Capture range** $\Delta f_{C}$ depends on LPF cutoff: $\Delta f_{C} \approx \sqrt{\Delta f_{L} \cdot f_{LPF}}$.
- For the **NE565 IC**, the free-running frequency is $f_{o} \approx 0.3 / (R_{1} C_{1})$.
- Frequency synthesizers are the backbone of **all modern wireless transceivers**.
- **Key insight:** All PLL applications rely on either (a) the **output frequency** being a controlled multiple of the input, or (b) the **control voltage $v_{c}(t)$** faithfully reproducing a modulating signal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Course Code:** PBMRT304
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain, differential-input, single-ended-output electronic voltage amplifier that is designed to be used with external feedback components. The **Block Diagram of an Op-Amp** is the internal architectural representation that decomposes the monolithic op-amp IC into four cascaded functional sub-systems, each performing a specific signal-processing role before producing the final amplified output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The block diagram is the *starting point* for understanding op-amp specifications like input impedance ($Z_{in}$), output impedance ($Z_{out}$), and open-loop voltage gain ($A_{OL}$). Each block contributes one of these specifications.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Blocks

According to the KTU syllabus, the internal structure of a generic op-amp is universally represented as a cascade of the following four stages:

| Block # | Stage Name | Standard Acronym | Key Function |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Input Stage** | DIBO (Dual Input Balanced Output) | Differential amplification + CMRR |
| 2 | **Gain Stage** | DIUO (Dual Input Unbalanced Output) | High voltage gain |
| 3 | **Level Shifter** | Emitter Follower / Zener Network | DC level restoration to 0 V |
| 4 | **Output Stage** | Push-Pull (Class B / Class AB) | Current drive, low output impedance |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line" of a Signal

Imagine a small audio signal (a whisper) arriving at a giant factory gate:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Four Rooms of a Signal Amplifier:**
> 1. **Room 1 — The Bouncer (Input Stage / DIBO):** Only lets through the *difference* between two incoming whispers, rejecting all common background noise. It also amplifies the whisper slightly but keeps the original two-wire balanced form.
> 2. **Room 2 — The Megaphone (Gain Stage / DIUO):** Massively amplifies the whisper into a full-volume shout, and converts the two-wire output into a single-wire (unbalanced) form.
> 3. **Room 3 — The Translator (Level Shifter):** After amplification, the shout's resting level is offset above 0 V. This room "translates" it back to 0 V DC baseline so it can swing both positive and negative.
> 4. **Room 4 — The Power Pump (Output Stage / Push-Pull):** Adds muscular current capability so the shout can drive heavy loads (speakers) without distortion, with very low internal resistance.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Op-Amp Block-Level Performance Targets (Typical 741-class device):**
> - Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ → **2 MΩ or higher** (set by Input Stage)
> - Open-Loop Gain $A_{OL}$ → **$2 \times 10^5$ (≈ 106 dB)** (set by Gain Stage)
> - Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ → **75 Ω or lower** (set by Output Stage)
> - DC Level at Output → **0 V at quiescent** (set by Level Shifter)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Theoretical Breakdown

#### 🔹 BLOCK 1 — Input Stage (Differential Amplifier, DIBO)
- **Topology:** Emitter-coupled (BJT) or source-coupled (FET) pair.
- **Inputs Accepted:** Two inputs — $V_1$ (inverting) and $V_2$ (non-inverting).
- **Outputs Produced:** Two outputs (Balanced) — taken from the two collectors.
- **Why DIBO?** The differential pair inherently offers:
  - Very high input impedance.
  - Excellent Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR).
  - Minimal drift and bias currents.
- **Voltage Gain of this stage:** $A_{V1} \approx g_m R_C$ (moderate, typically 10 to 100).
- **Function in the chain:** It provides the *differential-to-differential* conversion and the *first tier of gain*.

#### 🔹 BLOCK 2 — Gain Stage (DIUO Differential / Cascaded CE)
- **Topology:** Often another differential amplifier, but operated single-ended (unbalanced output).
- **Why DIUO?** It accepts the two balanced outputs of the input stage and combines them into one single-ended signal, while providing most of the voltage gain.
- **Voltage Gain of this stage:** $A_{V2} \approx$ very high (typically 1000+).
- **Function:** Bulk of the voltage amplification ($A_{OL} = A_{V1} \cdot A_{V2} \cdot A_{V3} \cdot A_{V4}$).

#### 🔹 BLOCK 3 — Level Shifter (DC Restoration Stage)
- **Problem Created:** After high-gain amplification, the DC quiescent point of the signal is far above 0 V (due to direct coupling), which prevents the output from swinging symmetrically around ground.
- **Solution:** A level shifter (typically an **emitter follower** with a zener or current source bias, OR a simple voltage divider network) is inserted to subtract a fixed DC voltage, bringing the quiescent output back to **0 V**.
- **Voltage Gain of this stage:** $A_{V3} \approx 1$ (unity or less — purely a DC translator, must not attenuate the AC signal).
- **Bonus Benefit:** Provides impedance matching (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$) between gain stage and output stage.

#### 🔹 BLOCK 4 — Output Stage (Push-Pull Amplifier)
- **Topology:** Class B or Class AB complementary symmetry push-pull (one NPN, one PNP, or N-channel/P-channel MOSFET pair).
- **Why Push-Pull?** It can source current (during positive half-cycle) and sink current (during negative half-cycle) efficiently.
- **Voltage Gain of this stage:** $A_{V4} \approx 1$ (emitter-follower configuration, used for impedance transformation and current drive).
- **Key Specs Delivered:** Very low output impedance $Z_{out}$ (typically 75 Ω), high current drive (tens of mA), and full voltage swing (≈ ±13 V on ±15 V supply for 741).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Tip:** Marks are awarded for explicitly stating the *function* of each block. Memorize the third column of this table.

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Definition | Typical Numerical Value | Unit |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Total Open-Loop Gain $A_{OL}$ | $A_{OL} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times A_{V3} \times A_{V4}$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^5$ | dimensionless (V/V) |
| Gain in Decibels $A_{OL(dB)}$ | $20 \log_{10}(A_{OL})$ | $\approx 106$ | dB |
| Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Set by Block 1 (DIBO) | $\geq 2 \times 10^{6}$ | Ω |
| Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | Set by Block 4 (Push-Pull) | $\leq 75$ | Ω |
| Common Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = \vert A_{d} / A_{c} \vert$ | $90$ | dB |
| Quiescent Output DC Level | Forced to 0 V by Block 3 | $0$ | V |
| Input Stage Gain $A_{V1}$ | $g_{m} R_{C}$ | $10$ to $100$ | V/V |
| Gain Stage Gain $A_{V2}$ | Stage-by-stage transistor gain | $1000+$ | V/V |
| Level Shifter Gain $A_{V3}$ | $\approx 1$ (emitter follower) | $1$ | V/V |
| Output Stage Gain $A_{V4}$ | $\approx 1$ (emitter follower) | $1$ | V/V |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Every Engineer Must Know This Block Diagram:**
> 1. **Design Selection** — When choosing an op-amp, datasheets reference these internal blocks; the block diagram tells you *which spec comes from where* (e.g., if your application needs high $Z_{in}$, focus on the input stage topology).
> 2. **Fault Diagnosis** — If an op-amp's output is offset, you know the level shifter (Block 3) is the suspect.
> 3. **Cascaded Systems** — Multi-stage amplifier design in communication systems is built by *stacking* these exact four functional blocks.
> 4. **Modern IC Design** — The same architecture persists in CMOS op-amps (folded cascode, miller compensation, etc.) — a timeless blueprint.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Open-Loop Gain of the Op-Amp

We model the op-amp as a **cascade of four independent two-port networks**. For cascaded stages that are properly buffered (each stage has high input impedance and low output impedance relative to the next), the total voltage gain is the *product* of the individual stage gains.

**Step 1 — Define the individual stage gains.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{V1} &= \text{Voltage gain of Input Stage (DIBO)} \\
A_{V2} &= \text{Voltage gain of Gain Stage (DIUO)} \\
A_{V3} &= \text{Voltage gain of Level Shifter} \\
A_{V4} &= \text{Voltage gain of Output Stage (Push-Pull)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Cascade rule for voltage gain (assuming proper impedance isolation).**

When stage $i$ has output impedance $Z_{out,i}$ and stage $i+1$ has input impedance $Z_{in,i+1}$, the loading factor is:

$$
\text{Loading factor} = \frac{Z_{in,i+1}}{Z_{in,i+1} + Z_{out,i}}
$$

In a well-designed op-amp, $Z_{in,i+1} \gg Z_{out,i}$, so the loading factor $\approx 1$. This means the stages do *not* load each other.

**Step 3 — Multiply individual stage gains.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out,1} &= A_{V1} \cdot V_{in} \\
V_{out,2} &= A_{V2} \cdot V_{out,1} = A_{V2} \cdot A_{V1} \cdot V_{in} \\
V_{out,3} &= A_{V3} \cdot V_{out,2} = A_{V3} \cdot A_{V2} \cdot A_{V1} \cdot V_{in} \\
V_{out,4} &= A_{V4} \cdot V_{out,3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final expression for total open-loop gain.**

$$
\boxed{\,A_{OL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times A_{V3} \times A_{V4}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation using a standard 741-class device.**

Let us assume the following typical per-stage gains:

$$
A_{V1} = 20, \quad A_{V2} = 5000, \quad A_{V3} = 1, \quad A_{V4} = 1
$$

Substitute these into the master equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{OL} &= 20 \times 5000 \times 1 \times 1 \\
A_{OL} &= 100{,}000 \\
A_{OL} &= 1 \times 10^{5} \;\text{V/V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to decibels (KTU often asks for dB form).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{OL(dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}(A_{OL}) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(1 \times 10^{5}) \\
&= 20 \times 5 \\
&= 100 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** Each factor of 10 in voltage gain = 20 dB. Each factor of 2 = 6 dB. We will revisit this rule in Module 4 (Frequency Response).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Stage-by-Stage I/O Characteristics

| Stage | Input Signal Form | Output Signal Form | Primary Electrical Role | Secondary Role |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **1 — Input (DIBO)** | Differential: $(V_1 - V_2)$ | Balanced Differential (two wires) | Set $Z_{in}$ (high) | Provide initial gain, ensure CMRR |
| **2 — Gain (DIUO)** | Differential (two wires) | Unbalanced Single-ended (one wire) | Bulk voltage amplification | Buffer to next stage |
| **3 — Level Shifter** | Single-ended AC + DC offset | Single-ended AC (DC = 0 V) | DC level restoration | Impedance transformation (high-to-low $Z$) |
| **4 — Output (Push-Pull)** | Single-ended AC (DC = 0 V) | Single-ended AC at high current | Set $Z_{out}$ (low) | Source/sink load current, protect against shorts |

### 3.3 Derivation: Why CMRR is Determined by the Input Stage

The differential gain $A_d$ and common-mode gain $A_c$ are both defined at the *first* stage. For a symmetric emitter-coupled pair with emitter resistor $R_E$:

$$
A_{d} = \frac{g_m R_C}{2}, \quad A_{c} = \frac{-R_C}{2 R_E}
$$

The CMRR becomes:

$$
\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert = g_m R_E
$$

**Engineering Insight:** To get a high CMRR, we need a *large* $R_E$. This is exactly why the input stage uses either a large physical resistor, a current source (which behaves like a very high dynamic $R_E$), or a **current mirror** active load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Canonical Op-Amp Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the *exact* four-block cascade as expected in KTU board exam answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["V1 (Inverting Input)"] --> S1
    B["V2 (Non-Inverting Input)"] --> S1
    S1["Block 1: Input Stage\nDIBO Differential Amplifier\nHigh Zin, High CMRR"] -- "Balanced Differential Output" --> S2
    S2["Block 2: Gain Stage\nDIUO Differential Amplifier\nProvides Bulk Voltage Gain"] -- "Unbalanced Single-ended Output" --> S3
    S3["Block 3: Level Shifter\nEmitter Follower + Zener\nDC Restoration to 0 V"] -- "AC Signal, DC = 0 V" --> S4
    S4["Block 4: Output Stage\nPush-Pull Class AB\nLow Zout, High Current Drive"] -- "Vo" --> C["Vout (Single-ended)"]
    S1 -. "A_V1" .-> S2
    S2 -. "A_V2" .-> S3
    S3 -. "A_V3" .-> S4
    S4 -. "A_V4" .-> C
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow & DC Bias Visualization

The following Mermaid block diagram emphasizes the *DC bias evolution* across stages — a frequent 14-mark KTU question ("trace the DC level through the op-amp").

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IN["INPUT STAGE Block 1 DIBO"]
        IN1["V1, V2 inputs both at 0 V DC"]
    end
    subgraph GS["GAIN STAGE Block 2 DIUO"]
        GS1["DC level rises to V+ minus a few volts"]
    end
    subgraph LS["LEVEL SHIFTER Block 3"]
        LS1["DC shifted down by ~13 V"]
    end
    subgraph OS["OUTPUT STAGE Block 4 Push-Pull"]
        OS1["Final DC level restored to 0 V at output"]
    end
    IN --> GS
    GS --> LS
    LS --> OS
```

### 4.3 Function-to-Specification Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Function: Accept differential input"] --> SP1["Spec: High Zin"]
    F2["Function: Reject common-mode noise"] --> SP2["Spec: High CMRR"]
    F3["Function: Boost weak signal"] --> SP3["Spec: High AOL = 10^5"]
    F4["Function: Drive low-impedance load"] --> SP4["Spec: Low Zout = 75 ohm"]
    B1["Block 1: Input Stage"] --> F1
    B1 --> F2
    B2["Block 2: Gain Stage"] --> F3
    B4["Block 4: Output Stage"] --> F4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in an Exam:** Always annotate (i) the signal form at each block boundary (differential vs single-ended, AC vs DC), (ii) the stage gain, and (iii) the key spec contributed. Examiners allocate marks for these three elements per block.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List the four functional blocks of a typical op-amp internal architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four functional blocks are:
1. **Input Stage** — Dual Input Balanced Output (DIBO) differential amplifier; provides high input impedance and high CMRR.
2. **Gain Stage** — Dual Input Unbalanced Output (DIUO) differential amplifier; provides the bulk of the open-loop voltage gain.
3. **Level Shifter** — Emitter follower (or zener-based network); restores the DC quiescent level to 0 V.
4. **Output Stage** — Push-pull (Class B/AB) amplifier; provides low output impedance and high current drive capability. **(3 marks — 1 mark for naming each block, ½ mark for its function.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Why is a level shifter necessary between the gain stage and the output stage of an op-amp?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Because the op-amp is *direct-coupled*, the high-gain amplification in the gain stage causes the DC operating point at its output to be far above 0 V. Without a level shifter, the output stage could only swing in one direction (it would saturate against the negative rail). The level shifter subtracts a fixed DC voltage so that the output quiescent point is restored to **0 V**, allowing symmetric ± swing. It also provides impedance buffering. **(3 marks — 1 mark for direct-coupling issue, 1 mark for DC restoration, 1 mark for impedance buffering.)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B Module questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) Draw the complete block diagram of a general op-amp and explain the function of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Step-by-step):**

**Step 1 — Draw the block diagram.** (4 marks for the diagram itself)

