```
   V1 ──┐                    ┌──── A_V1 ────┐
        ├──── [Block 1] ─────┤                │
   V2 ──┘   DIBO (Diff)      │  Balanced Out  │
                              └──── A_V1 ────┘
                                     │
                              ┌──── A_V2 ────┐
                              │  Unbalanced  │
                              │  [Block 2]   │── A_V2
                              │  Gain Stage  │
                              └──── A_V2 ────┘
                                     │
                              ┌──── A_V3 ────┐
                              │  [Block 3]   │── A_V3 (≈1)
                              │  Level Shift │
                              └──── A_V3 ────┘
                                     │
                              ┌──── A_V4 ────┐
                              │  [Block 4]   │── A_V4
                              │  Push-Pull   │
                              │  Output      │
                              └──── A_V4 ────┘
                                     │
                                    Vout
```

**Step 2 — Explain each block.** (3 marks — distributed as ¾ mark each)

- **Block 1 — Input Stage (DIBO):** Differential amplifier with two inputs ($V_1$, $V_2$) and two outputs. Sets the high input impedance and provides excellent CMRR. (¾ mark)
- **Block 2 — Gain Stage (DIUO):** Converts the balanced signal to a single-ended one and provides the bulk of the voltage gain. (¾ mark)
- **Block 3 — Level Shifter:** DC level restoration to 0 V using an emitter follower; provides impedance matching. (¾ mark)
- **Block 4 — Output Stage:** Push-pull amplifier providing low output impedance and high current drive. (¾ mark)

---

**(b) Derive the expression for the total open-loop gain of the op-amp. If the individual stage gains are $A_{V1} = 10$, $A_{V2} = 5000$, $A_{V3} = 0.95$, $A_{V4} = 0.95$, calculate the total open-loop gain in dB. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the cascade gain formula.** (1 mark)

$$
A_{OL} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times A_{V3} \times A_{V4}
$$

**Step 2 — Justify why multiplication is valid (proper impedance buffering).** (1 mark)

Because the output impedance of each stage is much smaller than the input impedance of the next, the loading factor is approximately 1, allowing simple multiplication of gains.

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.** (1 mark)

$$
A_{OL} = 10 \times 5000 \times 0.95 \times 0.95
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.** (1 mark)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{OL} &= 10 \times 5000 \times 0.9025 \\
&= 50{,}000 \times 0.9025 \\
&= 45{,}125
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels.** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{OL(dB)} &= 20 \log_{10}(45{,}125) \\
&= 20 \times \log_{10}(4.5125 \times 10^{4}) \\
&= 20 \times [4 + \log_{10}(4.5125)] \\
&= 20 \times [4 + 0.6545] \\
&= 20 \times 4.6545 \\
&\approx 93.09 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclude with the answer.** (1 mark)

$$
\boxed{\,A_{OL} = 45{,}125 \;\text{V/V} \approx 93.1 \;\text{dB}\,}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) Explain in detail the role of the input stage and the output stage in determining the input impedance and output impedance of an op-amp. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Input Stage and $Z_{in}$.** (3.5 marks)

The input stage is a **DIBO differential amplifier** (emitter-coupled pair). Its input impedance is set by the input transistor base resistance and the biasing network. By using Darlington pairs, FET inputs, or Wilson current mirrors, the input impedance can be pushed into the MΩ to TΩ range. A high $Z_{in}$ ensures the op-amp draws negligible current from the source, preserving signal integrity. For a 741 op-amp, $Z_{in} \approx 2\;\text{M}\Omega$.

**Step 2 — Output Stage and $Z_{out}$.** (3.5 marks)

The output stage is a **push-pull complementary symmetry amplifier** (Class AB). It is configured as an emitter follower, which inherently has a low output impedance. The low $Z_{out}$ ensures the op-amp can deliver a stable output voltage even into low-impedance or varying loads, with minimal signal attenuation. For a 741 op-amp, $Z_{out} \approx 75\;\Omega$. The push-pull topology also provides the *current drive capability* needed to source/sink tens of milliamps.

---

**(b) Why is the level shifter (DC restoration stage) considered critical in the op-amp architecture? What would happen if it were omitted? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the role.** (2 marks)

The op-amp is direct-coupled (no coupling capacitors) to allow DC amplification. After the gain stage amplifies the signal, the DC quiescent point is shifted far from 0 V (typically to a high positive voltage), preventing symmetric output swing.

**Step 2 — Function of the level shifter.** (2 marks)

The level shifter (emitter follower with zener reference, or voltage-divider current-source bias) subtracts a precise DC voltage so the output sits at 0 V DC at quiescence. It also provides impedance matching (high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$) and must have unity AC gain so the signal is not attenuated.

**Step 3 — Consequence of omission.** (2 marks)

If omitted, the output would sit at a high positive DC voltage (e.g., +10 V) when input is zero. The output would then be unable to swing in the negative direction without saturating, destroying the op-amp's ability to amplify bipolar (AC) signals symmetrically. The op-amp would essentially act as a "DC offset amplifier" with no useful AC behavior.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** (1 mark)

Hence, the level shifter is the *unsung hero* of op-amp architecture — invisible in gain calculations but indispensable for proper operation.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Block Diagram Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label the input/output signal form at each block boundary.** Examiners expect "Differential → Balanced → Single-ended" annotations. Skipping these costs **2 to 3 marks** per block diagram question.
> 2. **Confusing DIBO and DIUO.** DIBO = Dual Input **Balanced** Output. DIUO = Dual Input **Unbalanced** Output. Mixing these up is the #1 blunder.
> 3. **Forgetting the DC restoration role of the level shifter.** Many students describe it as "an amplifier," losing 1 mark. The correct answer is "DC level restoration" or "impedance buffering."
> 4. **Not converting $A_{OL}$ to dB.** KTU frequently asks for the decibel form. Use $20 \log_{10}(A_{OL})$, not $10 \log_{10}$.
> 5. **Omitting the role of the level shifter in impedance matching.** It does TWO things: (i) DC restore, (ii) impedance buffer. Mention both.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your wall before ESE):**
> - ✅ The op-amp is built as a **cascade of 4 functional blocks**: Input (DIBO) → Gain (DIUO) → Level Shifter → Output (Push-Pull).
> - ✅ **DIBO** = Dual Input **Balanced** Output. **DIUO** = Dual Input **Unbalanced** Output. Never confuse them.
> - ✅ **Input Stage** decides: $Z_{in}$ (high) and CMRR (high).
> - ✅ **Gain Stage** decides: Most of the open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL}$.
> - ✅ **Level Shifter** decides: DC quiescent output = 0 V; also acts as impedance buffer.
> - ✅ **Output Stage** decides: $Z_{out}$ (low) and current drive capability.
> - ✅ **Total Gain Formula:** $A_{OL} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times A_{V3} \times A_{V4}$ (valid only if impedance buffering prevents loading).
> - ✅ **Decibel Conversion:** $A_{OL(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(A_{OL})$.
> - ✅ Direct-coupling is what necessitates the level shifter — coupling capacitors would block DC.
> - ✅ Typical 741 numbers: $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5 \approx 106 \text{ dB}$, $Z_{in} \approx 2\;\text{M}\Omega$, $Z_{out} \approx 75\;\Omega$.
> - ✅ In exam answers, **always annotate the signal form (differential / single-ended)** at each block boundary — it carries up to **30% of the marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ideal & Practical Op-Amp Parameters - Frequency Response - Voltage Transfer Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ideal & Practical Op-Amp Parameters - Frequency Response - Voltage Transfer Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp) — Formal Definition

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output, designed to be used with external feedback components to perform a wide variety of linear and non-linear operations. The **IC 741** is the most widely studied general-purpose op-amp in the KTU 2024 Analog Electronics syllabus.

In the context of KTU Module 3, we transition from circuit applications (Modules 1 & 2) to the **internal electrical characteristics** that govern an op-amp's real-world behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> The module covers *ideal and practical op-amp parameters*, the *frequency response* of the open-loop and closed-loop configurations, and the *voltage transfer characteristics* of basic amplifier configurations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Perfect vs. The Real Microphone"

Imagine a public address (PA) system microphone:

- **The Ideal Op-Amp** is a *fictional* "perfect" microphone that:
  - Listens to the singer's voice with **infinite sensitivity** (infinite gain).
  - Picks up sound from the singer with **zero physical resistance** (infinite input impedance).
  - Drives the speaker with **no internal resistance** (zero output impedance).
  - Works equally well for a whisper or a rock concert (infinite bandwidth).

- **The Practical Op-Amp (e.g., µA741)** is a *real* microphone that:
  - Has **finite sensitivity** (gain ≈ 2 × 10⁵).
  - Presents a finite input resistance (≈ **2 MΩ**).
  - Has a small output resistance (≈ **75 Ω**).
  - Cannot amplify very high frequencies (band-limited to ≈ **1 MHz**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study the difference?**
> The *ideal* op-amp equations (e.g., $V_o = -(R_f/R_i)V_i$) form the foundation of circuit analysis. The *practical* parameters tell us the **limits** within which these equations remain valid — this is the key to KTU numerical problems and design questions.

### 1.3 Ideal Op-Amp Parameters — The Five Golden Rules

The **ideal op-amp model** is governed by five mathematically limiting parameters:

| # | Parameter | Ideal Value | Physical Meaning |
|---|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$) | **$\infty$** | Differential gain from input to output |
| 2 | Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) | **$\infty$** | Draws no current from the source |
| 3 | Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) | **0** | Delivers any current without voltage drop |
| 4 | Bandwidth (BW) | **$\infty$** | Amplifies DC to infinite frequency |
| 5 | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) | **$\infty$** | Ignores common-mode signals completely |

Additional idealities include: **zero output** for zero differential input, **zero noise**, **zero input offset voltage/current**, and **infinite slew rate**.

### 1.4 Practical Op-Amp Parameters — The Real IC 741

The **µA741** (and equivalent KTU-recommended devices) is a real device with measurable non-idealities. These parameters define the **boundaries of operation** and are heavily tested in KTU examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Terminology — "DC Parameters" vs "AC Parameters"**
> - **DC parameters** are measured with constant (slowly varying) signals: input bias current, input offset voltage, output voltage swing.
> - **AC parameters** are measured with sinusoidal signals at a specific frequency: small-signal bandwidth, slew rate, CMRR (at a given frequency).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Practical Parameter Analysis

#### (a) Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$ or $A_{d}$)

The differential gain from the inverting/non-inverting input to the output with **no feedback** applied.

- **Typical value (741):** $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ (≈ **106 dB**)
- **Definition:** $A_{OL} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_d} = \dfrac{V_o}{V_{+} - V_{-}}$

> [!NOTE]
> $A_{OL}$ is **frequency-dependent** and decreases by 20 dB/decade after the dominant pole.

#### (b) Input Bias Current ($I_B$)

The **average** of the two DC currents flowing into the inverting and non-inverting input terminals.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

- **Typical value (741):** $I_B \approx 80 \text{ nA}$ (max ≈ 500 nA)

#### (c) Input Offset Current ($I_{os}$)

The **difference** between the two input bias currents.

$$I_{os} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

- **Typical value (741):** $I_{os} \approx 20 \text{ nA}$ (max ≈ 200 nA)

#### (d) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{os}$)

The **small DC voltage** that must be applied between the two inputs to force the output to zero. Caused by mismatch in the input transistor pair.

- **Typical value (741):** $V_{os} \approx 2 \text{ mV}$ (max ≈ 6 mV)

#### (e) Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

The ability of the op-amp to **reject signals common to both inputs** (e.g., noise, interference).

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} \quad \text{(ratio form)}$$

$$\text{CMRR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

- **Typical value (741):** CMRR = 90 dB (i.e., $A_d / A_c = 31{,}623$)

#### (f) Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)

The ability to **reject variations** in the power supply voltage (also called Supply Sensitivity).

- **Typical value (741):** PSRR = 30 µV/V (or ≈ 90 dB)

#### (g) Slew Rate (SR)

The **maximum rate of change** of the output voltage with respect to time, under large-signal conditions.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max}$$

- **Typical value (741):** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/µs}$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Slew Rate Limitation:**
> For a sinusoidal output $V_o(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the maximum rate of change is $2\pi f V_p$. Slew-rate-limited distortion occurs when:
> $$2\pi f V_p \leq SR \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$
> This is a **favorite KTU numerical problem**.

#### (h) Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)

The product of the closed-loop gain and the corresponding -3 dB bandwidth. It is a **constant** for a given op-amp and equals the unity-gain frequency ($f_T$).

$$\text{GBW} = A_{CL} \times f_c = f_T = \text{constant}$$

- **Typical value (741):** GBW ≈ **1 MHz**

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Frequency Response

The **open-loop gain** of a practical op-amp (like the 741) exhibits a single dominant-pole response:

$$A(jf) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right)}$$

The **magnitude** form:

$$\vert A(jf) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right)^2}}$$

| Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|----------|---------|------|-------|
| Open-loop gain (magnitude) | $\vert A \vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_1)^2}}$ | V/V | $A_0$ is DC gain |
| -3 dB bandwidth (open-loop) | $f_1$ (or $f_H$) | Hz | Frequency where gain drops to $A_0/\sqrt{2}$ |
| Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBW} = A_{CL} \cdot f_c$ | Hz | **Constant** for a given op-amp |
| Closed-loop bandwidth | $f_c = \dfrac{\text{GBW}}{A_{CL}}$ | Hz | Increases as gain decreases |
| Maximum undistorted sine freq. | $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$ | Hz | Slew-rate limited |
| 20 dB/decade roll-off | $-20$ dB/decade | — | Above dominant pole $f_1$ |
| CMRR (dB) | $20\log_{10}(A_d / A_c)$ | dB | Higher is better |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Problems:**
> When feedback is applied, the **bandwidth increases** proportionally as the **gain decreases** — this is the famous gain-bandwidth trade-off. This single concept is the source of multiple KTU Part B questions.

### 2.3 Voltage Transfer Curve — Closed-Loop Behavior

When an op-amp is configured with negative feedback, its **closed-loop voltage transfer curve** becomes a straight line whose slope equals the ideal closed-loop gain.

| Configuration | Closed-Loop Gain ($A_{CL}$) | Transfer Equation |
|---------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | $V_o = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}\right) V_{in}$ |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | $V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}\right) V_{in}$ |
| Voltage Follower | $A_{CL} = 1$ | $V_o = V_{in}$ |

**Output Saturation Limit:**
The output cannot exceed the power supply rails. The output saturates at approximately:

$$V_{o(\text{sat})} = \pm V_{sat} \approx \pm(V_{CC} - 2\text{V})$$

For a typical 741 with $\pm 15\text{V}$ supplies: $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13\text{V}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> The voltage transfer curve is the design engineer's "**cheat sheet**" — it tells you at a glance the linear dynamic range, the saturation limits, and the gain. In instrumentation (e.g., ECG amplifiers, signal conditioning in PLCs), the designer uses this curve to ensure the signal stays within the linear region.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Open-Loop Frequency Response with Dominant Pole

The internal architecture of the 741 introduces a **dominant low-frequency pole** at $f_1$ (≈ 5 Hz). For frequencies above this pole, the gain rolls off at -20 dB/decade.

**Starting from the single-pole transfer function:**

$$A(jf) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right)}$$

**Step 1 — Separate the denominator into real and imaginary parts:**

$$A(jf) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\dfrac{f}{f_1}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the magnitude squared by multiplying by the complex conjugate:**

$$\vert A(jf) \vert^2 = A(jf) \cdot A^*(jf) = \frac{A_0^2}{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root to get the magnitude:**

$$\vert A(jf) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the -3 dB frequency:**

At $f = f_1$:

$$\vert A(jf_1) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \cdot A_0$$

In decibels, $20\log_{10}(0.707) = -3 \text{ dB}$. Hence $f_1$ is the **-3 dB bandwidth**.

**Step 5 — For $f \gg f_1$ (high-frequency asymptote):**

$$\vert A(jf) \vert \approx \frac{A_0}{f / f_1} = A_0 \cdot \frac{f_1}{f}$$

Taking $\log$ of both sides (in dB):

$$20\log_{10}\vert A \vert = 20\log_{10}(A_0 f_1) - 20\log_{10}(f)$$

The slope is **-20 dB/decade** — a decade increase in frequency produces a 20 dB drop in gain.

**Step 6 — Find the unity-gain frequency $f_T$:**

At unity gain, $\vert A \vert = 1$, so:

$$1 = \frac{A_0 f_1}{f_T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_T = A_0 \cdot f_1$$

This is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Gain-Bandwidth Product for Closed-Loop Operation

When negative feedback is applied, the closed-loop gain is $A_{CL}$, and the new -3 dB bandwidth becomes $f_c$.

**Using the concept of feedback and the open-loop response:**

For $f \gg f_1$ in the open-loop case:

$$\vert A_{OL} \vert \cdot f = A_0 \cdot f_1 = f_T$$

The closed-loop bandwidth $f_c$ occurs where the open-loop gain curve intersects the desired closed-loop gain line on a Bode plot. At that intersection:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f_c/f_1)^2}}$$

For $f_c \gg f_1$:

$$A_{CL} \approx \frac{A_0 f_1}{f_c} = \frac{f_T}{f_c}$$

Rearranging:

$$f_c = \frac{f_T}{A_{CL}} = \frac{\text{GBW}}{A_{CL}}$$

This proves that **GBW is invariant** for any closed-loop configuration.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example: Slew-Rate Limited Frequency

**Problem (KTU-style):**
An op-amp has a slew rate of 0.5 V/µs. Find the maximum frequency at which it can produce an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude 5 V.

**Given:** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/µs} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}$, $V_p = 5 \text{ V}$

**Step 1 — Write the output sinusoid:**

$$V_o(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to find the maximum rate of change:**

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_p \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum value:**

$$\left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} = 2\pi f V_p$$

**Step 4 — Apply the slew rate constraint:**

$$2\pi f_{max} V_p \leq SR$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $f_{max}$:**

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 5}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{500{,}000}{31.416}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 15{,}915 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}$$

**Result:** $f_{max} \approx 15.9 \text{ kHz}$ for an undistorted 5 V peak sine wave.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Practical Parameters of IC 741

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (µA741) | Unit | Test Condition (KTU standard) |
|-----------|--------|-----------------------|------|-------------------------------|
| Open-loop voltage gain | $A_{OL}$ | $2 \times 10^5$ | V/V | DC, $R_L \geq 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Input resistance | $R_{in}$ | $2$ | M$\Omega$ | — |
| Output resistance | $R_{out}$ | $75$ | $\Omega$ | — |
| Input bias current | $I_B$ | $80$ | nA | — |
| Input offset current | $I_{os}$ | $20$ | nA | — |
| Input offset voltage | $V_{os}$ | $2$ | mV | $R_S \leq 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| CMRR | — | $90$ | dB | $f \leq 1 \text{ kHz}$ |
| Slew rate | $SR$ | $0.5$ | V/µs | $R_L \geq 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Gain-Bandwidth product | GBW | $1$ | MHz | — |
| Output voltage swing | $V_{opp}$ | $\pm 13$ | V | $R_L \geq 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ |
| Power supply voltage | $V_{CC}, V_{EE}$ | $\pm 15$ | V | Typical |
| Power consumption | $P_D$ | $50$ | mW | Quiescent |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Op-Amp Symbol & Terminal Identification

The op-amp has **five essential terminals**: inverting input, non-inverting input, output, $V_{CC}$ (+V), and $V_{EE}$ (-V). The block-level functional architecture is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1["V+ NON INVERTING INPUT"] -->|signal path| DIFF["DIFFERENTIAL GAIN STAGE Ad"]
    V2["V- INVERTING INPUT"] -->|signal path| DIFF
    DIFF --> GAIN["HIGH GAIN STAGE Aol"]
    GAIN --> OUTPUT["OUTPUT STAGE Vout"]
    OUTPUT --> ZOUT["OUTPUT IMPEDANCE Zout"]
    ZOUT --> RL["LOAD RL"]
    VCC["VCC POSITIVE SUPPLY"] --> BIAS["BIAS NETWORK"]
    VEE["VEE NEGATIVE SUPPLY"] --> BIAS
    BIAS --> DIFF
    BIAS --> GAIN
    BIAS --> OUTPUT
    V1 --> ZIN["INPUT IMPEDANCE Zin"]
    V2 --> ZIN
    ZIN --> DIFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The two inputs feed a **differential gain stage** (which inherently provides CMRR).
> - A high-gain intermediate stage boosts the signal.
> - The output stage provides **current drive capability** to the load $R_L$.
> - The bias network powers the internal transistors from $V_{CC}$ and $V_{EE}$.

### 4.2 Bode Plot — Open-Loop Frequency Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FREQUENCY AXIS LOG SCALE"] --> B["f1 DOMINANT POLE 5 HZ"]
    B --> C["FLAT GAIN REGION 20 LOG A0 DB DC TO f1"]
    C --> D["ROLLOFF REGION SLOPE MINUS 20 DB PER DECADE"]
    D --> E["fT UNITY GAIN FREQUENCY 1 MHZ"]
    E --> F["GBW CONSTANT FOR ALL CLOSED LOOP GAINS"]

    G["GAIN AXIS DB SCALE"] --> H["0 DB UNITY GAIN LINE"]
    H --> I["INTERSECT OPEN LOOP GAIN AT fT"]
    I --> J["AOL CL LINE FOR CLOSED LOOP GAIN 20"]
    J --> K["NEW BANDWIDTH fc EQUALS GBW DIVIDED BY ACL"]
    K --> L["HIGHER ACL MEANS LOWER fc"]
```

### 4.3 Voltage Transfer Curve — Inverting vs Non-Inverting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INV["INVERTING AMPLIFIER"]
        A1["Vout"] -->|slope equals minus Rf over Ri| A2["LINEAR REGION"]
        A2 -->|upper limit| A3["PLUS VSAT APPROX 13V"]
        A2 -->|lower limit| A4["MINUS VSAT APPROX MINUS 13V"]
        A5["Vin negative"] -->|produces| A6["Vout positive"]
        A7["Vin positive"] -->|produces| A8["Vout negative"]
    end

    subgraph NONINV["NON INVERTING AMPLIFIER"]
        B1["Vout"] -->|slope equals 1 plus Rf over Ri| B2["LINEAR REGION"]
        B2 -->|upper limit| B3["PLUS VSAT APPROX 13V"]
        B2 -->|lower limit| B4["MINUS VSAT APPROX MINUS 13V"]
        B5["Vin positive"] -->|produces| B6["Vout positive"]
        B7["Vin negative"] -->|produces| B8["Vout negative"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["PRACTICAL OP AMP GBW EQUALS 1 MHZ"] --> Q1["CLOSE LOOP GAIN 1 VOLTAGE FOLLOWER"]
    P --> Q2["CLOSE LOOP GAIN 10"]
    P --> Q3["CLOSE LOOP GAIN 100"]
    P --> Q4["CLOSE LOOP GAIN 1000"]

    Q1 --> R1["BANDWIDTH 1 MHZ WIDEST"]
    Q2 --> R2["BANDWIDTH 100 KHZ"]
    Q3 --> R3["BANDWIDTH 10 KHZ"]
    Q4 --> R4["BANDWIDTH 1 KHZ NARROWEST"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from the Diagram:**
> As you increase the closed-loop gain by a factor of 10, the bandwidth decreases by a factor of 10. This is the **fundamental trade-off** that every KTU design problem revolves around.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**List any six ideal characteristics of an op-amp.**

**Model Answer:**

The six ideal characteristics of an op-amp are:

1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain** ($A_{OL} = \infty$).
2. **Infinite input impedance** ($Z_{in} = \infty$) — draws no current from the source.
3. **Zero output impedance** ($Z_{out} = 0$) — delivers any load current without voltage drop.
4. **Infinite bandwidth** — amplifies signals from DC to infinity.
5. **Infinite CMRR** — perfectly rejects common-mode signals.
6. **Zero output for zero differential input** ($V_o = 0$ when $V_+ = V_-$).

*(Any six valid points — ½ mark each = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**Define slew rate. What is its significance in op-amp performance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Slew rate (SR) is the maximum rate of change of the output voltage with respect to time, under large-signal conditions.

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} \quad \text{(unit: V/µs)}$$

**Significance:**

- It limits the **maximum rate** at which the output can swing, regardless of input amplitude.
- For a sinusoidal output $V_o = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the slew-rate-limited maximum frequency is:
$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$$
- It causes **slew-induced distortion** (triangular waveform) when the input frequency is too high or the amplitude is too large.
- For IC 741, $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/µs}$ — a common KTU exam value.

*(Definition 1 mark + significance 2 marks = 3 marks)*

---

### 📗 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

## ❓ CHOICE A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 3 Internal Choice]

### **Question A (a)** [7 Marks]
**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the voltage transfer characteristics of an inverting and a non-inverting amplifier using an op-amp. Show the linear region and saturation levels clearly.**

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Inverting Amplifier Circuit:**

The inverting amplifier has input $V_{in}$ applied through $R_i$ to the inverting terminal, with $R_f$ as the feedback resistor from output to inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Closed-loop gain:**
$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_i}$$

**Transfer equation:**
$$V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_i} V_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Non-Inverting Amplifier Circuit:**

The input $V_{in}$ is applied to the non-inverting terminal. $R_f$ and $R_i$ form a voltage divider at the inverting input.

**Closed-loop gain:**
$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}$$

**Transfer equation:**
$$V_o = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}\right) V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Linear Region:**

In the linear region, the op-amp operates with negative feedback, and the output faithfully follows the transfer equation. The slope of the transfer curve equals $A_{CL}$.

- Inverting: **negative slope** (output inverts input).
- Non-inverting: **positive slope** (output follows input).

**Step 4 — Saturation Region:**

When the magnitude of the theoretical output exceeds the supply-dependent saturation voltage:

$$V_{o(\text{sat})} = \pm V_{sat} \approx \pm(V_{CC} - 2\text{V})$$

For a 741 with $\pm 15$ V supplies: $V_{sat} \approx \pm 13$ V.

The transfer curve **flattens** (clips) at these levels. Beyond the saturation limits, the op-amp acts as a comparator.

**Step 5 — Drawing the Transfer Curve:**

For example, an inverting amplifier with $R_f/R_i = 2$:
- Linear region: slope = -2, extending from -13 V to +13 V on the output axis.
- Saturation: at $V_{in} = +6.5$ V, output saturates at -13 V.
- At $V_{in} = -6.5$ V, output saturates at +13 V.

**Diagram (as text):**
```
        V_o
         |         /  (Non-inv, slope = +3)
   +13V -|--------/    <- Saturation
         |      /  /
         |    /  / (Linear region, slope = +3)
         |  /  /
   ------+/--+-------- V_in
         | /|
         |/ |  (Linear region, slope = -2)
   -13V -+--|-------- <- Saturation
              \  (Inv, slope = -2)
```

*(Circuit + gain equations = 3 marks, linear region = 2 marks, saturation analysis = 2 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

### **Question A (b)** [7 Marks]
**A practical op-amp has an open-loop gain of $2 \times 10^5$ and a dominant pole at 5 Hz. Calculate: (i) the gain at 1 kHz, (ii) the gain-bandwidth product, and (iii) the closed-loop bandwidth if used in a non-inverting amplifier with $R_f = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_i = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$.**

### **Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A_0 = 2 \times 10^5$
- $f_1 = 5 \text{ Hz}$
- $R_f = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_i = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$

**Part (i): Gain at 1 kHz**

Using the magnitude formula:

$$\vert A(f) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_1}\right)^2}}$$

Substitute $f = 1000$ Hz, $f_1 = 5$ Hz:

$$\frac{f}{f_1} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200$$

$$\vert A(1000) \vert = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{\sqrt{1 + (200)^2}} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{\sqrt{40001}} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{200.0025}$$

$$\vert A(1000) \vert \approx 999.99 \approx 1000$$

**Gain at 1 kHz ≈ 1000 V/V (or 60 dB).**

**Part (ii): Gain-Bandwidth Product**

$$f_T = A_0 \times f_1 = 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**GBW = 1 MHz.**

**Part (iii): Closed-Loop Bandwidth**

Closed-loop gain of the non-inverting amplifier:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i} = 1 + \frac{99}{1} = 100$$

Closed-loop bandwidth:

$$f_c = \frac{GBW}{A_{CL}} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{100} = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

**Closed-loop bandwidth = 10 kHz.**

*(Part i: 3 marks, Part ii: 2 marks, Part iii: 2 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

## ❓ CHOICE B: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 3 Internal Choice]

### **Question B (a)** [7 Marks]
**Define and explain the following practical op-amp parameters: (i) Input bias current, (ii) Input offset current, (iii) Input offset voltage, (iv) CMRR. Mention their typical values for IC 741.**

### **Model Solution:**

**(i) Input Bias Current ($I_B$):**

The input bias current is the **average** of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals of the op-amp.

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

**Physical cause:** The base currents of the input differential pair (BJT-based op-amp). For a BJT input stage, $I_B$ depends on $\beta$ and the emitter current.

**Typical value (741):** $I_B \approx 80$ nA (max ≈ 500 nA).

**(ii) Input Offset Current ($I_{os}$):**

The input offset current is the **difference** between the two input bias currents.

$$I_{os} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$$

**Physical cause:** Mismatch in the $\beta$ of the two input transistors.

**Typical value (741):** $I_{os} \approx 20$ nA (max ≈ 200 nA).

**Significance:** $I_{os}$ produces an output offset voltage even when $V_{os} = 0$, and is **temperature-dependent** — a critical concern in DC amplifier design.

**(iii) Input Offset Voltage ($V_{os}$):**

The small DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals to **force the output to zero**.

**Physical cause:** Mismatch in the $V_{BE}$ of the input differential pair transistors.

**Typical value (741):** $V_{os} \approx 2$ mV (max ≈ 6 mV).

**Effect:** Produces an output offset $V_{o(\text{offset})} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{os}$ that can saturate high-gain DC amplifiers. Nulling circuit is required to zero this offset.

**(iv) Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):**

The ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_c$.

$$\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_c} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{CMRR (dB)} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$$

**Physical meaning:** A high CMRR means the op-amp amplifies only the **difference** between the two inputs and rejects any signal common to both (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum, thermal noise).

**Typical value (741):** CMRR ≈ 90 dB (i.e., $A_d/A_c = 31{,}623$).

**Significance:** Critical in instrumentation amplifiers and transducer signal conditioning.

*(Each parameter: ~1.5 marks for definition + typical value + physical cause = 7 marks total)*

---

### **Question B (b)** [7 Marks]
**For an op-amp with slew rate of 0.5 V/µs, find: (i) the maximum frequency of an undistorted sine wave of peak amplitude 10 V, and (ii) the maximum peak amplitude for an undistorted 50 kHz sinusoidal output. If the closed-loop gain of the amplifier is 20, determine the input amplitude limit for the 50 kHz signal.**

### **Model Solution:**

**Given:** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/µs} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}$

**Part (i): Maximum frequency for 10 V peak**

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_p} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 10}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{500{,}000}{62.832}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 7958 \text{ Hz} \approx 7.96 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (ii): Maximum peak amplitude for 50 kHz output**

$$V_{p(\text{max})} = \frac{SR}{2\pi f} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6}{2\pi \times 50{,}000}$$

$$V_{p(\text{max})} = \frac{500{,}000}{314{,}159.27}$$

$$V_{p(\text{max})} \approx 1.59 \text{ V}$$

**Part (iii): Input amplitude limit for 50 kHz with $A_{CL} = 20$**

$$V_{in(\text{max})} = \frac{V_{p(\text{max})}}{A_{CL}} = \frac{1.59}{20}$$

$$V_{in(\text{max})} \approx 0.0796 \text{ V} \approx 79.6 \text{ mV}$$

*(Part i: 2.5 marks, Part ii: 2.5 marks, Part iii: 2 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert slew rate units** — $SR$ must be in V/s (not V/µs) when computing $f_{max}$.
> 2. **Using the wrong closed-loop gain formula** — Inverting: $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_i$ (negative sign is **mandatory**); Non-inverting: $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_i$.
> 3. **Ignoring saturation** — The output cannot exceed $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 2$V; students often write the linear equation even when $V_{in}$ is large.
> 4. **Confusing GBW with bandwidth** — GBW is a **constant** (≈ 1 MHz for 741); closed-loop bandwidth $f_c = \text{GBW}/A_{CL}$ depends on the gain.
> 5. **Forgetting dB conversion for CMRR** — KTU explicitly asks for CMRR **in dB** in most problems; the numerical value alone loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up $I_B$ and $I_{os}$** — $I_B$ is the **average**; $I_{os}$ is the **difference**.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five Ideal Rules:** $A_{OL} = \infty$, $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$, BW $= \infty$, CMRR $= \infty$.
- **741 Open-Loop Gain:** $A_0 \approx 2 \times 10^5$ (≈ 106 dB); dominant pole at $f_1 \approx 5$ Hz.
- **Dominant Pole Roll-off:** Gain drops at **-20 dB/decade** for $f > f_1$.
- **GBW Product:** $\text{GBW} = A_{CL} \cdot f_c = A_0 \cdot f_1 = f_T$ = **constant**.
- **Closed-Loop Bandwidth:** $f_c = \dfrac{\text{GBW}}{A_{CL}}$ — higher gain ⇒ lower bandwidth.
- **Slew Rate (741):** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/µs}$ — limits the rate of output change.
- **Slew-Rate Limited Frequency:** $f_{max} = \dfrac{SR}{2\pi V_p}$.
- **Input Bias Current ($I_B$):** Average of the two input currents; ≈ 80 nA for 741.
- **Input Offset Current ($I_{os}$):** Difference; ≈ 20 nA for 741; temperature sensitive.
- **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{os}$):** ≈ 2 mV for 741; nulled using offset-null pins.
- **CMRR:** 90 dB for 741; higher is better; degrades at high frequency.
- **Inverting Amplifier:** $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i} V_{in}$ — **negative** gain, virtual ground at $V_-$.
- **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** $V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}\right) V_{in}$ — **positive** gain, high input impedance.
- **Saturation Voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx \pm(V_{CC} - 2\text{V}) \approx \pm 13\text{V}$ for $\pm 15$V supply.
- **Voltage Transfer Curve:** Slope equals $A_{CL}$; flat regions (clipping) outside saturation.
- **Key Engineering Insight:** Gain-bandwidth trade-off is the designer's fundamental constraint; choose the lowest $A_{CL}$ that meets the signal-amplitude requirement to maximize bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverting Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inverting Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverting Operational Amplifier** is a closed-loop amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied to the inverting terminal (−) of the op-amp through an input resistor $R_1$, while the non-inverting terminal (+) is grounded. A feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected between the output and the inverting input terminal, establishing negative feedback. The resulting output voltage is an **amplified, phase-inverted (180° out-of-phase)** replica of the applied input voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The inverting amplifier is the most fundamental op-amp topology. Mastery of its derivation using the **Virtual Short** and **Virtual Ground** concepts is mandatory, as it forms the basis for integrators, differentiators, summer circuits, and current-to-voltage converters.

The closed-loop voltage gain is governed by the simple negative ratio:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

The negative sign is the mathematical signature of the **inverting** action.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the op-amp as a seesaw balance controlled by a strict, obsessive referee.**

Imagine a seesaw. The inverting input (−) is the **left seat**, and the non-inverting input (+) is the **right seat** which is firmly planted on the ground. The op-amp's internal "referee" has only one obsession: **the voltage on the left seat must always equal the voltage on the right seat**.

Now, when you push the left seat UP by feeding a **positive** input voltage, the referee (in a desperate attempt to keep balance equal to the grounded right seat at 0 V) will yank the **output downward** as hard as needed. The output will swing **negative**. 

Conversely, if you push the left seat DOWN with a **negative** input, the referee pushes the output **UP positive**. This is the essence of phase inversion — the output "mirrors" the input in amplitude but flips its polarity.

The resistors $R_1$ and $R_f$ act like **gears of different sizes**. If $R_f$ is much larger than $R_1$ (big gear driving a small gear), the output swings much more than the input — this is **amplification**. The ratio of these two "gears" precisely determines the gain.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Ideal Closed-Loop Gain:** $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$ (dimensionless)
- **Input Impedance:** $Z_{in} = R_1$ (set by external resistor)
- **Output Impedance:** $Z_{out} \approx 0\ \Omega$ (ideal, due to voltage-series feedback)
- **Phase Shift:** **180°** between input and output sinusoids
- **Virtual Ground Voltage:** $V^- = V^+ = 0\ V$ (since non-inverting input is grounded)
- **Virtual Short Current:** $I^- = I^+ = 0\ A$ (due to ideal infinite input impedance)
- **Bandwidth × Gain = Constant** (Gain-Bandwidth Product of the op-amp)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

The inverting amplifier's behaviour rests on **two golden assumptions** derived from the ideal op-amp model:

1. **Virtual Short Concept:** Because the open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ is infinite (ideally), and the output is finite, the differential input voltage must approach zero. Hence, $V^- \approx V^+$.

2. **Virtual Open Concept:** The op-amp's input terminals draw no current because the input impedance is infinite. Hence, $I^- = I^+ = 0\ A$.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

- **Step 1 — Reference Anchor:** The non-inverting input is tied to ground, so $V^+ = 0\ V$.

- **Step 2 — Virtual Ground Application:** By the virtual short principle, $V^- = V^+ = 0\ V$. Note that the inverting node is *not physically* connected to ground, but it *behaves* as if it is at ground potential. Hence, it is called a **virtual ground**.

- **Step 3 — Current Conservation at Inverting Node:** Since no current flows into the op-amp's inverting input ($I^- = 0$), the entire current flowing through $R_1$ from the source must continue through $R_f$ into the output node (Kirchhoff's Current Law).

- **Step 4 — Sign and Magnitude Calculation:** The output must assume whatever voltage is needed to push exactly that current through $R_f$. A negative output voltage is required when the input is positive, because current must flow *out* of the inverting node (which is at 0 V) through $R_f$ toward the (negative) output.

- **Step 5 — Gain Determination:** The ratio of $R_f$ to $R_1$ scales how much the output must swing for a given input.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Conditions / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Closed-Loop Voltage Gain | $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$ | Valid when $A_{OL} \cdot \beta \gg 1$ (deep negative feedback) |
| Input Impedance | $Z_{in} = R_1$ | **Low** compared to non-inverting; set by external $R_1$ |
| Output Impedance | $Z_{out} \approx 0$ | Ideal; reduced by feedback factor $\beta$ |
| Feedback Factor | $\beta = R_1 / (R_1 + R_f)$ | Voltage divider ratio feeding back to inverting node |
| Virtual Ground Voltage | $V^- = 0\ V$ | Only when non-inverting input is grounded |
| Closed-Loop Bandwidth | $f_B = GBW / \vert A_{CL} \vert$ | GBW = Gain-Bandwidth Product of op-amp |
| Current through $R_1$ | $I_1 = (V_{in} - V^-) / R_1 = V_{in} / R_1$ | Because $V^- = 0$ |
| Current through $R_f$ | $I_f = (V^- - V_{out}) / R_f = -V_{out} / R_f$ | Because $V^- = 0$ |
| Output Voltage | $V_{out} = - (R_f / R_1) \cdot V_{in}$ | The fundamental transfer function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The inverting topology is the workhorse of **active filters**, **analog summers**, **instrumentation front-ends**, **D/A converter output stages**, and **phase-shift oscillators**. Its predictable gain set by passive components makes it indispensable in PCB analog design.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Sensor Signal Conditioning:** Strain gauges and photodiodes (current sources) easily interface with the low-impedance virtual ground, eliminating loading errors.
- **Audio Mixers:** Multiple inverting summers can linearly combine microphone signals without interaction (due to virtual ground isolation).
- **Function Generators:** Forms the basis of the integrator and differentiator modules used in waveform generation.
- **Active Filters:** Sallen-Key and Multiple-Feedback (MFB) topologies build upon the inverting gain block.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain

We will derive $V_{out}/V_{in}$ rigorously using **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node, applying the ideal op-amp assumptions.

### 3.1.1 Circuit Setup and Node Identification

Consider the inverting amplifier circuit:

- Input voltage source $V_{in}$ is connected to the inverting input (−) through resistor $R_1$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output $V_{out}$ to the inverting input.
- Non-inverting input (+) is connected to ground (0 V).

Let $V^-$ denote the voltage at the inverting node, and $V^+$ denote the voltage at the non-inverting node.

### 3.1.2 Applying Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions

**Assumption 1 — Infinite Open-Loop Gain (Virtual Short):**

The op-amp output is:

$$V_{out} = A_{OL} \cdot (V^+ - V^-)$$

Since the output $V_{out}$ is a finite, real-world voltage and $A_{OL} \to \infty$ for an ideal op-amp, the differential input must vanish:

$$V^+ - V^- = \frac{V_{out}}{A_{OL}} \to 0$$

Therefore:

$$V^- = V^+$$

**Assumption 2 — Infinite Input Impedance (Virtual Open):**

No current flows into either op-amp input terminal:

$$I^- = I^+ = 0\ A$$

### 3.1.3 Determining $V^+$ and $V^-$

Since the non-inverting terminal is directly grounded:

$$V^+ = 0\ V$$

By the virtual short principle (Assumption 1):

$$V^- = V^+ = 0\ V$$

> This is the **virtual ground** — the inverting node sits at 0 V even though it is not physically tied to ground.

### 3.1.4 Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the Inverting Node

The sum of all currents entering the inverting node must equal zero. There are only two branches: the $R_1$ branch and the $R_f$ branch.

**Current through $R_1$ (from $V_{in}$ to the inverting node):**

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1}$$

**Current through $R_f$ (from the inverting node to $V_{out}$):**

$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

By KCL (no current enters the op-amp):

$$I_1 = I_f$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

### 3.1.5 Substituting $V^- = 0$

Replace $V^-$ with 0 V:

$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

### 3.1.6 Solving for the Gain

Cross-multiplying:

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = -V_{out} \cdot R_1$$

Rearranging for $V_{out}$:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$$

Therefore, the **closed-loop voltage gain** is:

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

The negative sign confirms the **inverting** (180° phase shift) action.

## 3.2 Numerical Example — Worked Analytical Problem

**Problem Statement:** An inverting amplifier uses $R_1 = 10\ k\Omega$ and $R_f = 100\ k\Omega$. The input is a sine wave $V_{in}(t) = 0.05 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000\ t)$ V. Determine (a) the closed-loop gain, (b) the output voltage expression, (c) the input impedance seen by the source, and (d) the new −3 dB bandwidth if the op-amp's GBW = 1 MHz.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Closed-Loop Gain:**

$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{100\ k\Omega}{10\ k\Omega} = -10$$

**Part (b) — Output Voltage Expression:**

$$V_{out}(t) = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in}(t) = -10 \cdot 0.05 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000\ t)$$

$$V_{out}(t) = -0.5 \sin(2\pi \cdot 1000\ t)\ V$$

The negative sign indicates a 180° phase inversion.

**Part (c) — Input Impedance:**

$$Z_{in} = R_1 = 10\ k\Omega$$

The source sees a $10\ k\Omega$ load — this is the **major drawback** of the inverting topology compared to the non-inverting configuration.

**Part (d) — Bandwidth:**

$$\vert A_{CL} \vert = 10$$

$$f_B = \frac{GBW}{\vert A_{CL} \vert} = \frac{1\ MHz}{10} = 100\ kHz$$

Since the input signal is at 1 kHz, it lies well within the 100 kHz passband, so the output will be undistorted.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Component Variations

| **Design Change** | **Effect on Gain** | **Effect on $Z_{in}$** | **Effect on Bandwidth** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Double $R_f$ | Gain magnitude doubles | No change | Bandwidth halves (GBW constant) |
| Double $R_1$ | Gain magnitude halves | Doubles | Bandwidth doubles |
| Both $R_1$ and $R_f$ doubled | No change to gain | Doubles | Doubles |
| Reduce both by half | No change to gain | Halves | Halves |
| Add $R_{load}$ at output | No change to ideal gain | No change | Slight reduction due to loading |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Circuit Topology Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Vin Source"] -- R1 --> nodeB["Inverting Node V_minus"]
    nodeB -- Rf --> nodeC["Vout Output Node"]
    nodeA -- "Virtual Open I_in equals 0" --> nodeB
    nodeB -- "Virtual Ground V_minus equals 0V" --> nodeG["Ground Node V_plus equals 0V"]
    nodeG -- "Direct Connection" --> nodeD["Non Inverting Plus Input"]
    nodeC -- "Drives Load" --> nodeE["Output Terminal"]
    nodeD -- "Inside Op Amp Boundary" --> nodeF["Ideal Op Amp A_OL approaches infinity"]
    nodeF -- "Internal Gain Stage" --> nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeH["Source Resistance R_s"]
    nodeE --- nodeI["Load R_L"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Signal-Flow / Block Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **functional block architecture** and signal flow of the inverting amplifier, which is the recommended KTU-friendly approach when a full SPICE-level schematic is impractical.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inA["Input Signal Vin"] --> bA["Input Resistor R1 Block"]
    bA --> sumA{"Summing Junction at Inverting Node"}
    fbA["Feedback Resistor Rf Block"] --> sumA
    sumA --> diffA["Virtual Ground Concept: V_minus equals 0V"]
    diffA --> gndA["Non Inverting Plus Grounded at 0V"]
    diffA --> intA["Ideal Op Amp Open Loop Gain A_OL Infinite"]
    intA --> outA["Output Node Vout"]
    outA --> loadA["Load R_L Connection"]
    outA --> fbA
    sumA -. "KCL: I_R1 equals I_Rf" .- fbA
    outA -. "Inverted Polarity 180 degree Phase Shift" .- inA
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Explanation

The architecture map above traces the following critical paths:

- **Forward Path:** $V_{in} \rightarrow R_1 \rightarrow$ Summing Junction $\rightarrow$ Op-Amp Internal Gain Stage $\rightarrow V_{out}$.
- **Feedback Path:** $V_{out} \rightarrow R_f \rightarrow$ Summing Junction (closes the negative feedback loop).
- **Reference Path:** Ground (0 V) is fed directly to the non-inverting (+) input, anchoring the virtual ground reference at the inverting node.
- **Phase Indicator:** The dotted feedback line annotates the 180° phase reversal that occurs between input and output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** When drawing the inverting amplifier in your answer script, always **explicitly label** the inverting (−) and non-inverting (+) terminals, mark the **virtual ground** at the inverting node with a dashed line to ground, and indicate the direction of current flow through $R_1$ and $R_f$. These labels carry marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define virtual ground. Why is the inverting node of an inverting amplifier considered to be at virtual ground?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Virtual ground** is a concept in ideal op-amp analysis where a node that is not physically connected to ground is treated as being at **0 V (ground potential)**.

In the inverting amplifier configuration, the non-inverting input (+) is directly connected to ground, so $V^+ = 0\ V$. Due to the **virtual short** property of an ideal op-amp (infinite open-loop gain), the voltage at the inverting input must equal the voltage at the non-inverting input. Therefore, $V^- = V^+ = 0\ V$. **(2 marks)**

The node is termed "virtual" because although it sits at 0 V, it is **not physically** tied to ground — no current actually flows to the actual ground from this node. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of an inverting amplifier. What is its input impedance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The closed-loop voltage gain of the inverting amplifier is:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

where $R_1$ is the input resistor and $R_f$ is the feedback resistor. The negative sign indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output. **(2 marks)**

The input impedance of the inverting amplifier is:

$$Z_{in} = R_1$$

This is one of the **limitations** of the inverting topology — the input impedance cannot be made very high without compromising the gain (since increasing $R_1$ reduces $\vert A_{CL} \vert$). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of an inverting amplifier using an ideal op-amp. State clearly the assumptions made.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Assumptions of an Ideal Op-Amp (2 Marks):**
- Infinite open-loop gain: $A_{OL} \to \infty$
- Infinite input impedance: $Z_{in} \to \infty$, hence $I^- = I^+ = 0\ A$
- Zero output impedance: $Z_{out} = 0\ \Omega$
- Infinite bandwidth

**Step 2 — Identify the Node Voltages (1 Mark):**
Since the non-inverting input is grounded: $V^+ = 0\ V$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Virtual Short Concept (1 Mark):**
By virtual short, $V^- = V^+ = 0\ V$ (virtual ground).

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node (2 Marks):**
$$\frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Gain (1 Mark):**
$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** For an inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 2\ k\Omega$ and $R_f = 50\ k\Omega$, calculate (i) the closed-loop gain, (ii) the output voltage when $V_{in} = 20\ mV$, (iii) the input impedance, and (iv) the −3 dB bandwidth if the op-amp GBW is 0.5 MHz.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Closed-Loop Gain (2 Marks):**
$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} = -\frac{50\ k\Omega}{2\ k\Omega} = -25$$

**(ii) Output Voltage (2 Marks):**
$$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in} = -25 \times 20\ mV = -500\ mV = -0.5\ V$$

**(iii) Input Impedance (1 Mark):**
$$Z_{in} = R_1 = 2\ k\Omega$$

**(iv) Bandwidth (2 Marks):**
$$f_B = \frac{GBW}{\vert A_{CL} \vert} = \frac{0.5\ MHz}{25} = 20\ kHz$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an inverting amplifier using an op-amp. Derive its voltage gain.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 Marks):**
The inverting amplifier consists of:
- An op-amp with inverting input (−), non-inverting input (+), and output terminal.
- Input resistor $R_1$ between the source $V_{in}$ and the inverting input.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between the output and the inverting input.
- The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Operation Explanation (2 Marks):**
- When $V_{in}$ is positive, current $I_1 = V_{in}/R_1$ flows through $R_1$ into the virtual ground.
- This same current flows through $R_f$ toward the output, requiring $V_{out}$ to be **negative** to sink the current. This produces the 180° phase inversion.
- The op-amp's high open-loop gain combined with negative feedback forces the inverting node to follow the grounded non-inverting input (virtual ground).

**Gain Derivation (3 Marks):**
Applying KCL at the inverting node:
$$\frac{V_{in} - V^-}{R_1} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f}$$

With $V^- = 0$:
$$A_{CL} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** An inverting amplifier has a gain of −40. If the input signal is $V_{in}(t) = 0.1 \sin(2000\pi t)$ V, determine the peak-to-peak output voltage and the −3 dB frequency, given that the op-amp's gain-bandwidth product is 2 MHz. Also, find suitable values of $R_1$ and $R_f$ if a **current of 100 μA** must flow through $R_1$ when the input is at its peak positive value.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Peak-to-Peak Output Voltage (2 Marks):**
$$V_{out,peak} = \vert A_{CL} \vert \cdot V_{in,peak} = 40 \times 0.1 = 4\ V$$
$$V_{out,pp} = 2 \times V_{out,peak} = 8\ V_{pp}$$

**−3 dB Bandwidth (1 Mark):**
$$f_B = \frac{GBW}{\vert A_{CL} \vert} = \frac{2\ MHz}{40} = 50\ kHz$$

**Choosing $R_1$ (2 Marks):**
At peak input $V_{in} = +0.1\ V$, current through $R_1$ is:
$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = 100\ \mu A$$
$$R_1 = \frac{V_{in}}{I_1} = \frac{0.1\ V}{100\ \mu A} = 1\ k\Omega$$

**Choosing $R_f$ (2 Marks):**
$$\vert A_{CL} \vert = \frac{R_f}{R_1} \Rightarrow R_f = 40 \times 1\ k\Omega = 40\ k\Omega$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the gain expression — partial credit lost if the polarity is omitted.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_1$ and $R_f$** — always remember $R_1$ is the input resistor (connected to source) and $R_f$ is the feedback resistor (connected to output).
> 3. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** — KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for listing the ideal op-amp assumptions before derivation.
> 4. **Confusing input impedance formulas** — for the inverting amplifier, $Z_{in} = R_1$, NOT the op-amp's intrinsic input impedance.
> 5. **Sign errors in KCL** — when writing the current through $R_f$, remember it flows from the inverting node (0 V) toward $V_{out}$, so the expression is $(V^- - V_{out})/R_f$. A sign flip here cascades into a wrong gain.
> 6. **Skipping units** in numerical answers — always specify V, kΩ, kHz, etc. Failure to do so often results in a half-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **inverting amplifier** flips the input signal by 180° and scales it by the factor $R_f / R_1$.
- The **closed-loop gain** is $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$, derived using **virtual short** and **virtual open** principles.
- The **virtual ground** at the inverting node is the cornerstone of the analysis — it is at 0 V but not physically grounded.
- **Input impedance** equals $R_1$ — a key limitation compared to the non-inverting topology.
- **Output impedance** is ideally zero due to voltage-series negative feedback.
- The **gain-bandwidth product** is constant: increasing gain reduces bandwidth proportionally.
- The inverting amplifier forms the **building block** for summers, integrators, differentiators, and active filters.
- Always **list ideal op-amp assumptions** before any derivation in the KTU exam.
- **Phase inversion** is mathematically encoded in the **negative sign** of the gain expression.
- **Sign conventions in KCL** must be strictly observed; even a small sign error invalidates the entire derivation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Inverting Op-Amp - Voltage Follower
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Non-Inverting Op-Amp - Voltage Follower

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Non-Inverting Amplifier** is a closed-loop operational amplifier configuration in which the input signal is applied directly to the **non-inverting (+)** terminal of the op-amp, while a portion of the output voltage is fed back to the **inverting (−)** terminal through a resistive feedback network. The output voltage is **in phase** with the applied input voltage, and the closed-loop voltage gain is governed entirely by the external feedback components, not the op-amp's intrinsic open-loop gain $A_{OL}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A non-inverting amplifier produces an output voltage that is amplified and **non-inverted** (i.e., same polarity) with respect to the input, with voltage gain $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ for an ideal op-amp.

A **Voltage Follower** (also called a **Unity-Gain Buffer** or **Unity-Gain Amplifier**) is a degenerate special case of the non-inverting amplifier in which the entire output is fed back to the inverting input. This results in a closed-loop voltage gain of **exactly unity**, meaning the output voltage precisely tracks the input voltage: $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The voltage follower has $A_v = 1$ (or $0\,\text{dB}$), $R_f = 0\,\Omega$ (direct feedback wire), and $R_1 = \infty$ (open circuit). It is **not** a "do-nothing" circuit — it provides impedance transformation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

### Non-Inverting Amplifier — "The Megaphone Analogy"
Imagine you whisper into a **megaphone**. The megaphone takes your quiet voice and produces a louder version of the **exact same message** — no reversing, no echo, just amplification. The non-inverting amplifier works the same way: whatever voltage (signal) you put in at the (+) terminal, you get a bigger version of the **same polarity** at the output. The ratio between the loud output and quiet input is set by two physical resistors ($R_f$ and $R_1$).

### Voltage Follower — "The Relay Runner with a Cushioned Tray"
Imagine a waiter carrying a tray of soup from a noisy kitchen to a calm dining hall. The waiter doesn't change the soup (output = input), but the tray has rubber feet that absorb vibrations. A voltage follower is identical: the input signal (soup) is delivered unchanged to the output (customer), but the op-amp's high input impedance and low output impedance act like the rubber feet — they **isolate** the source from the load, preventing loading effects. The soup is the same; only the "delivery quality" improves.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Non-Inverting = **No** sign flip. Inverting = **I**nverted sign. Voltage Follower = **Follows** the input (1:1 copy with muscle).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal closed-loop input impedance | $Z_{in}$ | $\infty$ (theoretical) |
| Ideal closed-loop output impedance | $Z_{out}$ | $0$ (theoretical) |
| Open-loop gain of op-amp | $A_{OL}$ | $\approx 2 \times 10^{5}$ |
| Unity-gain bandwidth | $GBW$ | **1 MHz** (typical 741) |
| Slew rate | $SR$ | **0.5 V/µs** (typical 741) |

> [!NOTE]
> All real-world metrics above are **bolded** per KTU board expectations. The ideal values ($\infty$ and $0$) are the **asymptotic limits** used in hand derivations, while the **bolded numerical values** (e.g., **1 MHz**, **0.5 V/µs**) are the standard datasheet values for the $\mu$A741 op-amp family commonly used in KTU lab examinations.

---

## 1.4 Circuit Symbols & Topology at a Glance

| Element | Connection |
|---|---|
| $V_{in}$ | Applied directly to the **(+)** non-inverting input |
| $R_1$ | Connected between the inverting input and **GND** |
| $R_f$ | Connected between the output and the inverting input |
| $V_{out}$ | Taken directly from the op-amp output pin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero current flows into the op-amp inputs** (ideal case). This single fact is the cornerstone of *every* op-amp analysis and will be used repeatedly in Section 3 derivations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The non-inverting amplifier's behavior is dictated by two golden rules of the **ideal op-amp** (Negative feedback assumption):

1. **Virtual Short:** $V^+ = V^-$ (the two input terminals are at the same potential due to infinite open-loop gain).
2. **Infinite Input Impedance:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ (no current flows into either input terminal).

### Why does the output NOT saturate? (The Negative Feedback Loop)

- If $V_{in}$ rises slightly → $V^+$ rises → $V_{out}$ tries to rise (open-loop behavior).
- $V_{out}$ rising means more voltage is fed back through $R_f$ to the inverting terminal.
- This pulls $V^-$ upward, counteracting the increase at $V^+$.
- Equilibrium is reached when $V^+ = V^-$, and the op-amp settles to a stable $V_{out}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Non-Inverting"?** Because the output moves in the **same direction** as the input. If $V_{in}$ goes from +1 V to +2 V, $V_{out}$ also goes UP. Compare this to the inverting amplifier where $V_{out}$ moves OPPOSITE to $V_{in}$.

---

## 2.2 The Voltage Follower — Boundary Case Analysis

A voltage follower is obtained by applying two boundary conditions to the non-inverting formula:

- $R_f \rightarrow 0\,\Omega$ (a direct wire from output to inverting input)
- $R_1 \rightarrow \infty$ (remove $R_1$ entirely / open circuit)

Substituting these limits:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1 + 0 = 1$$

The output therefore equals the input: $V_{out} = V_{in}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The voltage follower's primary engineering purpose is **NOT** amplification (gain = 1), but **impedance buffering** — it presents a near-infinite impedance to the source and a near-zero impedance to the load. This is why it is also called a **buffer amplifier**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **every formula** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the non-inverting amplifier and voltage follower. Pipes `|` are deliberately avoided in formulas per formatting rules.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Boundary / Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Closed-loop voltage gain (Non-Inverting) | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $R_f, R_1 > 0$ | dimensionless (V/V) |
| 2 | Gain in decibels | $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$ | Always $\geq 0\,\text{dB}$ | decibels (dB) |
| 3 | Output voltage (Non-Inverting) | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) V_{in}$ | Linear region only | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Closed-loop input impedance | $Z_{in} \approx (1 + A_{OL} \beta) \cdot Z_{in,\text{open}}$ | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | Closed-loop output impedance | $Z_{out} \approx \dfrac{Z_{out,\text{open}}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta}$ | Very small | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | Voltage Follower Gain | $A_v = 1$ | $R_f = 0,\; R_1 = \infty$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Voltage Follower Output | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | Ideal op-amp | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Feedback factor | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ | $0 < \beta \leq 1$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Loop gain | $T = A_{OL} \beta$ | $\gg 1$ for deep feedback | dimensionless |
| 10 | Bandwidth (GBP limit) | $BW_{cl} = \dfrac{GBW}{A_v}$ | GBW = constant | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Voltage followers are routinely used in **DAC output buffers**, **ADC input drivers**, **sensor signal conditioning** (e.g., piezoelectric and instrumentation sources), and **long-cable driver applications** where loading must be prevented.

---

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Gain-setting resistors** are physically chosen using standard E12/E24 values, making design predictable.
- The **$1 + R_f/R_1$** relationship is a **direct proportionality** between component choice and amplification — a key concept KTU examiners test numerically.
- The **GBW product** being constant means higher gain ⇒ lower bandwidth (gain-bandwidth tradeoff), which is a frequent 7-mark derivation question in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Non-Inverting Amplifier Closed-Loop Gain

We start from the **ideal op-amp** assumptions:

**Step 1 — Apply the virtual short concept.**

Since the op-amp is in negative feedback and $A_{OL} \to \infty$:

$$V^+ = V^-$$

**Step 2 — Identify the voltage at the non-inverting input.**

The non-inverting input is connected directly to the source:

$$V^+ = V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore, the inverting input is also at $V_{in}$.**

By substituting Step 2 into Step 1:

$$V^- = V_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the inverting node.**

The inverting node has two branches: one to ground via $R_1$ and one to the output via $R_f$. Since no current enters the op-amp itself ($I^- = 0$), the current through $R_1$ must equal the current through $R_f$:

$$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V^- = V_{in}$ into the KCL equation.**

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-multiply to eliminate denominators.**

$$V_{in} \cdot R_f = R_1 \cdot (V_{out} - V_{in})$$

**Step 7 — Expand the right-hand side.**

$$V_{in} R_f = R_1 V_{out} - R_1 V_{in}$$

**Step 8 — Collect $V_{out}$ terms on the left side.**

$$R_1 V_{out} = R_1 V_{in} + V_{in} R_f$$

**Step 9 — Factor out $V_{in}$.**

$$R_1 V_{out} = V_{in} (R_1 + R_f)$$

**Step 10 — Divide both sides by $R_1$.**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1}$$

**Step 11 — Separate the fraction.**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 12 — Define the closed-loop voltage gain.**

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Voltage Follower Gain

**Step 1 — Start from the general non-inverting gain equation.**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage-follower boundary condition $R_f = 0$.**

The numerator becomes zero:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{0}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the fraction.**

$$A_v = 1 + 0 = 1$$

**Step 4 — Apply the second boundary condition $R_1 = \infty$ for verification (L'Hôpital-style cross-check).**

$$\lim_{R_1 \to \infty} \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = 1 + 0 = 1$$

**Step 5 — Write the input-output relation.**

$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = 1 \cdot V_{in} = V_{in}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = V_{in}}$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Input Impedance with Feedback

The closed-loop input impedance of the non-inverting amplifier is multiplied by the **loop gain factor** $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$:

$$Z_{in,\text{closed}} = Z_{in,\text{open}} \cdot (1 + A_{OL} \beta)$$

**Step 1 — Identify the feedback factor for the non-inverting configuration.**

From the resistor divider, the fraction of $V_{out}$ appearing at $V^-$ is:

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for a typical design.**

Let $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_f = 9\,\text{k}\Omega$, $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, and $Z_{in,\text{open}} = 2\,\text{M}\Omega$:

$$\beta = \frac{1\,\text{k}\Omega}{1\,\text{k}\Omega + 9\,\text{k}\Omega} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

**Step 3 — Compute the loop gain.**

$$A_{OL} \beta = (2 \times 10^5)(0.1) = 2 \times 10^4$$

**Step 4 — Compute the feedback enhancement factor.**

$$1 + A_{OL}\beta = 1 + 2 \times 10^4 \approx 2 \times 10^4$$

**Step 5 — Compute the closed-loop input impedance.**

$$Z_{in,\text{closed}} = (2 \times 10^6) \cdot (2 \times 10^4) = 4 \times 10^{10}\,\Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a $40\,\text{G}\Omega$ input impedance — effectively infinite for all practical purposes. This is **why** the non-inverting amplifier (and especially the voltage follower) is preferred when interfacing with high-impedance sensors.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: KTU-Style Calculation

> **Problem:** Design a non-inverting amplifier with a gain of 11. If $R_1 = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, find $R_f$ and $V_{out}$ when $V_{in} = 0.5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Set up the gain equation.**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 11$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_f$.**

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 11 - 1 = 10$$

$$R_f = 10 \cdot R_1 = 10 \cdot 2\,\text{k}\Omega = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = 11 \cdot 0.5\,\text{V} = 5.5\,\text{V}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** Using the unsimplified form: $V_{out} = V_{in}(1 + R_f/R_1) = 0.5 \cdot (1 + 20/2) = 0.5 \cdot 11 = 5.5\,\text{V}$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Non-Inverting Amplifier — Circuit Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level functional architecture** of the non-inverting amplifier. Per protocol, all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes, and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sourceNode1[Vin Source] --> inputNode2[Non Inverting Plus Input]
    gndNodeA[Ground GND] --> r1Node3[R1 Resistor to GND]
    outNode5[Vout Output Terminal]
    r1Node3 --> invNode4[Inverting Minus Input]
    outNode5 --> rfNode6[Rf Feedback Resistor]
    rfNode6 --> invNode4
    inputNode2 --> opampNode7[Op Amp Triangle]
    invNode4 --> opampNode7
    opampNode7 --> outNode5
    style sourceNode1 fill:#cce5ff
    style inputNode2 fill:#d4edda
    style invNode4 fill:#f8d7da
    style opampNode7 fill:#fff3cd
    style outNode5 fill:#d1ecf1
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **Blue node** = Input voltage source
- **Green node** = Non-inverting input terminal
- **Red node** = Inverting input terminal (feedback summing junction)
- **Yellow node** = The op-amp active element
- **Cyan node** = Final output node

---

## 4.2 Voltage Follower — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    inSrcNodeA[Vin Input Signal] --> inPosNodeB[Plus Input Plus]
    inPosNodeB --> opampCoreNodeC[Ideal Op Amp A OL Infinity]
    directWireNodeD[Direct Wire Feedback] --> inNegNodeE[Minus Input Minus]
    opampCoreNodeC --> outTermNodeF[Vout Output]
    outTermNodeF --> directWireNodeD
    inNegNodeE --> opampCoreNodeC
    inPosNodeB --> virtualShortNodeG[Virtual Short Vplus Equals Vminus]
    inNegNodeE --> virtualShortNodeG
    style inSrcNodeA fill:#cce5ff
    style inPosNodeB fill:#d4edda
    style inNegNodeE fill:#f8d7da
    style opampCoreNodeC fill:#fff3cd
    style outTermNodeF fill:#d1ecf1
    style virtualShortNodeG fill:#e2d4f0
```

**Architecture Flow Explanation:**

1. **Vin** enters the **(+)** terminal.
2. The op-amp amplifies the **virtual-short differential** (which is zero internally).
3. The output $V_{out}$ is fed back **100%** via a direct wire to the **(-)** terminal.
4. The op-amp adjusts $V_{out}$ until $V^- = V^+ = V_{in}$.
5. Result: $V_{out} = V_{in}$ with the input impedance boosted by the full loop gain.

---

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Comparison: Non-Inverting vs. Voltage Follower

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph NonInvertingBlock
        NI_In1[Vin] --> NI_Gain2[Gain Av Equals 1 plus Rf over R1]
        NI_Gain2 --> NI_Out3[Vout Same Polarity]
    end
    subgraph VoltageFollowerBlock
        VF_In4[Vin] --> VF_Gain5[Gain Av Equals 1]
        VF_Gain5 --> VF_Out6[Vout Equals Vin]
    end
    style NI_Gain2 fill:#fff3cd
    style VF_Gain5 fill:#d4edda
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **voltage follower** is the special-case of the **non-inverting amplifier** when the gain collapses to unity. The Mermaid diagrams above show the same topology family, differing only in the **feedback resistor arrangement**.

---

## 4.4 Impedance Transformation Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcNodeA[High Z Source] -->|Hi Z loading risk| bufferNodeB[Voltage Follower Op Amp]
    bufferNodeB -->|Low Z drives easily| loadNodeC[Low Z Load]
    bufferNodeA2[Non Inverting Buffer] --> bufferNodeB
    style srcNodeA fill:#f8d7da
    style bufferNodeB fill:#fff3cd
    style loadNodeC fill:#d4edda
    style bufferNodeA2 fill:#e2d4f0
```

**Engineering Interpretation:** A high-impedance source (e.g., a piezoelectric sensor with $\sim 10\,\text{M}\Omega$ output impedance) cannot directly drive a low-impedance load (e.g., an $8\,\Omega$ speaker) without losing signal. The voltage follower in the middle **buffers** the impedance mismatch, allowing maximum power transfer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define a voltage follower. Mention its voltage gain and one practical application.**

**Model Answer:**

A voltage follower is an op-amp circuit in which the output is connected directly back to the inverting input, with the input signal applied to the non-inverting terminal. It is also called a **unity-gain buffer** because the closed-loop voltage gain is:

$$A_v = 1 \quad \text{(or 0 dB)}$$

**Practical Application:** It is widely used as a **buffer amplifier** between a high-impedance source (such as a sensor or function generator) and a low-impedance load, preventing **loading effects** and providing impedance isolation.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect the equation $A_v = 1$ explicitly. Students who only write "$V_{out} = V_{in}$" without the gain value often lose 1 mark.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Write the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier. State the polarity relationship between input and output.**

**Model Answer:**

The closed-loop voltage gain of a non-inverting amplifier is:

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**Polarity Relationship:** The output is **in phase** (non-inverted) with the input. That is, if $V_{in}$ is positive, $V_{out}$ is also positive. There is **no 180° phase shift** between input and output — this is the key distinguishing feature from the inverting amplifier configuration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the closed-loop voltage gain of a **non-inverting amplifier** using an ideal op-amp. Show that the input impedance is extremely high and the output impedance is very low. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a non-inverting amplifier with $R_1 = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 45\,\text{k}\Omega$, calculate **(i)** the voltage gain, **(ii)** the output voltage for $V_{in} = 0.2\,\text{V}$, and **(iii)** the gain in decibels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Using the ideal op-amp virtual short and infinite input impedance rules:

1. $V^+ = V^- = V_{in}$ (since $V^+$ is directly tied to $V_{in}$ and the virtual short holds).
2. Apply KCL at the inverting node:

$$\frac{V^- - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V^-}{R_f}$$

3. Substituting $V^- = V_{in}$:

$$\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_f}$$

4. Solving algebraically:

$$V_{in} R_f = R_1 V_{out} - R_1 V_{in}$$

$$R_1 V_{out} = V_{in}(R_1 + R_f)$$

$$A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

**High input impedance:** The closed-loop input impedance is $Z_{in,cl} = Z_{in,open} \cdot (1 + A_{OL}\beta)$, which is enhanced by the large loop gain factor $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$, making it practically **infinite**.

**Low output impedance:** The closed-loop output impedance is $Z_{out,cl} = \dfrac{Z_{out,open}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$, which is divided by the same large factor, making it practically **zero**.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Voltage Gain:**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{45\,\text{k}\Omega}{5\,\text{k}\Omega} = 1 + 9 = 10$$

**(ii) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out} = A_v \cdot V_{in} = 10 \cdot 0.2\,\text{V} = 2\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Gain in Decibels:**

$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \cdot 1 = 20\,\text{dB}$$

---

> ### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **voltage follower**. Derive its voltage gain and discuss its use as a buffer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A non-inverting amplifier uses $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_f = 99\,\text{k}\Omega$. The input is a sine wave of $50\,\text{mV}$ peak. Calculate **(i)** the gain, **(ii)** the peak output voltage, and **(iii)** the minimum slew-rate-required op-amp if the signal frequency is $20\,\text{kHz}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

In a voltage follower, the output is connected **directly** to the inverting input (i.e., $R_f = 0$), and there is no resistor from the inverting input to ground (i.e., $R_1$ is open). The input is applied to the non-inverting (+) terminal.

**Derivation:**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{0}{\infty} = 1$$

$$V_{out} = V_{in}$$

**Buffer Action:** Although the gain is unity, the op-amp provides **impedance transformation**. The input sees the very high input impedance of the op-amp's (+) terminal (ideally infinite), while the output can drive low-impedance loads due to the very low output impedance. This **isolates** the source from the load, preventing signal loss due to loading.
