**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**(i) Voltage Gain:**

$$A_v = 1 + \frac{99\,\text{k}\Omega}{1\,\text{k}\Omega} = 1 + 99 = 100$$

**(ii) Peak Output Voltage:**

$$V_{out,\text{peak}} = A_v \cdot V_{in,\text{peak}} = 100 \cdot 50\,\text{mV} = 5000\,\text{mV} = 5\,\text{V (peak)}$$

**(iii) Minimum Required Slew Rate:**

The slew-rate requirement for a sine wave of peak voltage $V_p$ and frequency $f$ is:

$$SR_{\min} = 2 \pi f V_p$$

$$SR_{\min} = 2 \pi \cdot (20 \times 10^3\,\text{Hz}) \cdot (5\,\text{V})$$

$$SR_{\min} = 2 \pi \cdot 10^5\,\text{V/s} = 6.283 \times 10^5\,\text{V/s}$$

$$SR_{\min} = 0.628\,\text{V/µs}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Slew-rate unit trap:** Students frequently write $SR = 0.628$ without units, or write the answer in V/s instead of V/µs. The KTU answer-key convention expects **V/µs** for op-amp slew-rate problems. Always convert: $1\,\text{V/µs} = 10^6\,\text{V/s}$.
> 2. **Virtual short misuse:** In a non-inverting amplifier, students sometimes wrongly apply $V^- = 0$ (as in the inverting case). Always remember: $V^- = V_{in}$ (NOT zero).
> 3. **Phase mistake:** Writing "the output is 180° out of phase" for a non-inverting circuit costs full marks on any 7-mark question. The non-inverting output is **in phase** with the input.
> 4. **Decibel formula confusion:** Use $20 \log_{10}(A_v)$ for voltage gain. Using $10 \log_{10}$ is for power, not voltage, and will lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Revision — KTU Module 3: Non-Inverting Op-Amp & Voltage Follower**

- [x] **Gain Formula:** $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ — memorize without hesitation.
- [x] **Output Equation:** $V_{out} = V_{in}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$ — applied for **DC and AC** signals alike.
- [x] **Voltage Follower Special Case:** $R_f = 0\,\Omega$, $R_1 = \infty \Rightarrow A_v = 1$, $V_{out} = V_{in}$.
- [x] **Phase Relationship:** Output is **in phase** with the input (no inversion). This is the key distinguisher from the inverting amplifier.
- [x] **Input Impedance:** Enhanced by $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$ — effectively **infinite** for the voltage follower.
- [x] **Output Impedance:** Reduced by $(1 + A_{OL}\beta)$ — effectively **zero** for the voltage follower.
- [x] **Virtual Short Rule:** $V^+ = V^-$ — only valid under **negative feedback** with finite output (not in saturation).
- [x] **No Current into Inputs:** $I^+ = I^- = 0$ — used to apply **KCL** at the inverting node during derivations.
- [x] **Feedback Factor:** $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_f}$ — appears in all advanced gain/bandwidth calculations.
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product:** $BW_{cl} = \dfrac{GBW}{A_v}$ — higher gain means lower bandwidth; **GBP is constant**.
- [x] **Slew Rate Limit:** $SR_{\min} = 2\pi f V_p$ — must be satisfied to avoid output distortion at high frequencies.
- [x] **Typical 741 Specs to Memorize:** $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $GBW = \mathbf{1\,MHz}$, $SR = \mathbf{0.5\,V/µs}$.
- [x] **Buffer Application:** Used to **isolate** high-Z sources from low-Z loads, preventing loading effects.
- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** The voltage follower has unity gain but is **not useless** — its value lies in impedance transformation, not amplification.
- [x] **Decibel Conversion:** $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(A_v)$ for voltage ratios (use 10 for power ratios).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** When asked to "derive," always start with the **two ideal op-amp assumptions** (virtual short and zero input current), then apply **KCL at the inverting node**. This single framework unlocks ~80% of all op-amp derivation questions in the KTU analog electronics paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Op-Amp Integrator**: A linear analog electronic circuit built around an operational amplifier that produces an output voltage waveform which is the **mathematical time-integral** (with a sign inversion) of the input voltage waveform applied at its input terminal.

The circuit is constructed by placing a **capacitor $C$** in the **feedback path** between the output and the inverting input of the op-amp, while a **resistor $R$** is connected between the input signal source and the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

The governing transfer equation is:
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)$$

where the term $V_{out}(0)$ represents the initial condition (output voltage at $t=0$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology Note**: The integrator is classified under *Linear Applications of Op-Amps* and is often paired in examination questions with the **differentiator** to test the student's understanding of time-domain mathematical operations performed using active analog circuits.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a **water reservoir** that is filling up at a rate controlled by an inflow tap. Your job is to record the **total volume of water** collected at every instant.

- The **rate at which water flows in** is analogous to the **input voltage $V_{in}$** (it tells you how fast something is changing).
- The **total volume accumulated in the tank** is analogous to the **output voltage $V_{out}$** (it is the running total of all the past input).
- The act of "summing up" the inflow over time is the operation of **integration**.

In an electrical integrator, the **resistor $R$** converts the input voltage into a proportional current (just as the inflow tap converts pressure into flow rate), and the **capacitor $C$** accumulates this charge over time (just as the reservoir accumulates water). The op-amp ensures the process is precise, linear, and isolated from the load.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook**: *"Current IN = Capacitor fills UP"* → A capacitor's voltage represents the **accumulation (integral)** of all current that has flowed into it. The integrator circuit forces a specific current (set by $V_{in}/R$) to flow into the capacitor, so its voltage automatically becomes the integral of $V_{in}$.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Design Metrics (Bolded)

The following standard quantities govern integrator behaviour and are routinely tested in KTU examinations:

- **Time Constant $\tau = RC$**: The characteristic time scale of integration (in **seconds**).
- **Integrator Gain $K_i = -1/(RC)$**: The proportionality constant between the output and the integral of the input (in **V/s per V** = **s⁻¹**).
- **Cut-off Frequency $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$**: The frequency below which the circuit behaves as an integrator (in **Hz**).
- **Virtual Ground**: Both op-amp inputs sit at **0 V** because of negative feedback and the ideal op-amp assumption (infinite open-loop gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

The integrator works on a beautiful exploitation of two fundamental op-amp properties and one fundamental capacitor property:

1. **Virtual Short / Virtual Ground** (Why?): Because the op-amp is in a negative feedback loop with very high open-loop gain $A_{OL}$, the differential input voltage $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ is forced to zero. Since $V_+$ is grounded ($V_+ = 0$), the inverting input is also at $0\,\text{V}$. This is *not* a real short circuit — it is a *virtual* one because no current actually flows into the op-amp's input terminals.

2. **Current Continuity at the Inverting Node** (Why?): Since the ideal op-amp draws **zero** input current, all the current flowing through $R$ *must* flow through $C$ (Kirchhoff's Current Law). Thus $I_R = I_C$.

3. **Capacitor I-V Law** (How?): A capacitor's current is proportional to the **rate of change of voltage across it**:
   $$I_C = C\frac{dV_C}{dt}$$
   Integrating both sides gives:
   $$V_C(t) = \frac{1}{C}\int I_C(t)\,dt$$
   This means the capacitor voltage is the **integral of the current** that has flowed into it.

4. **Sign Inversion** (Why?): The voltage across the capacitor is $V_C = V_- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$. Hence the negative sign in the final output equation.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on the topic of the Op-Amp Integrator. Pay special attention to sign conventions and the boundary between integrator vs. low-pass filter behaviour.

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning / Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitor Current | $I_C = C\, \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$ | Amperes (A) | Fundamental constitutive law of a capacitor |
| Inverting Node Current | $I_R = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ | Amperes (A) | Ohm's law applied at the virtual ground |
| Output Voltage (Time Domain) | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt$ | Volts (V) | **Core equation** — output is the inverted integral of input |
| Transfer Function (s-domain) | $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ | Dimensionless (V/V) | Reveals pole at origin; integrator has infinite DC gain |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ | V/V | Falls at **−20 dB/decade**; doubles the roll-off vs. RC filter |
| Phase Response | $\angle H(j\omega) = -90^\circ$ | Degrees (°) | Constant −90° phase shift at all frequencies (true integrator) |
| Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ | Seconds (s) | Sets the integration time scale |
| Integrator Unity-Gain Frequency | $f_{unity} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = 1$ |
| Practical Integrator DC Gain | $A_0 = -\dfrac{R_f}{R}$ | V/V | Finite gain set by feedback resistor $R_f$ (prevents saturation) |
| Practical Integrator Corner Frequency | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ | Hertz (Hz) | Below this, circuit acts as an inverting amplifier; above, as integrator |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The op-amp integrator is far from a purely academic exercise — it is the workhorse of countless engineering systems:

- **Analog Computers (Historical)**: Before digital signal processing became dominant, integrators (along with summers and multipliers) were the building blocks used to solve ordinary differential equations in real time.
- **Function Generators**: A square wave fed to an integrator produces a **triangular wave**, and a triangular wave produces a **parabolic waveform** — the basis of many waveform synthesis circuits.
- **Active Filters**: When combined with other op-amp stages, integrators form second-order low-pass filters (e.g., **Sallen-Key**, **state-variable** filters) used in audio equalizers, anti-aliasing, and control systems.
- **Control Systems (PI Controllers)**: The integral term in a Proportional-Integral (PI) controller is implemented using an integrator op-amp stage to eliminate steady-state error.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (Dual-Slope ADCs)**: The dual-slope ADC integrates the input voltage for a fixed time and then de-integrates using a reference — the integrator is the heart of this topology.
- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs)**: The loop filter in many PLL designs uses an integrator to track frequency with zero steady-state error.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Note**: This topic is mapped to **CO3 (Apply)** — students are expected to *design* and *analyse* linear op-amp circuits including the integrator. Expect numerical design problems where component values are derived from desired time constants or gain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Integrator Output

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most important analytical question** on integrators in KTU board exams. You must be able to reproduce every line below without referring to any source.

**Step 1 — Draw the circuit and label voltages/currents.**

The inverting input node of the op-amp is called the **summing junction** or **virtual ground node**. We label:
- $V_{in}$ : input voltage applied to the left terminal of $R$
- $V_-$ : voltage at the inverting input
- $V_+$ : voltage at the non-inverting input (= $0\,\text{V}$ since it is grounded)
- $V_{out}$ : output voltage at the op-amp output pin
- $I_R$ : current flowing from $V_{in}$ to $V_-$ through $R$
- $I_C$ : current flowing from $V_-$ to $V_{out}$ through $C$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal op-amp assumptions.**

For an ideal op-amp:
- Open-loop gain $A_{OL} \to \infty$
- Input impedance $Z_{in} \to \infty$ (so **no current flows into the op-amp inputs**)
- Output impedance $Z_{out} = 0$

From the first two assumptions, the **virtual short** principle gives:

$$
V_- = V_+ = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the inverting node.**

Since the op-amp input draws no current, the only paths for current are through $R$ and $C$. By KCL:

$$
I_R = I_C
$$

**Step 4 — Express $I_R$ using Ohm's Law.**

The voltage across $R$ is $V_{in} - V_- = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$. Therefore:

$$
I_R = \frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Express $I_C$ using the capacitor constitutive equation.**

The voltage across $C$ is $V_- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$. The current through a capacitor is $I_C = C\,dV_C/dt$. Hence:

$$
I_C = C\frac{d}{dt}(V_- - V_{out}) = C\frac{d}{dt}(0 - V_{out}) = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

**Step 6 — Equate $I_R$ and $I_C$ and rearrange.**

Setting $I_R = I_C$:

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

Rearranging to isolate the derivative of $V_{out}$:

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC}
$$

**Step 7 — Integrate both sides with respect to time.**

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dV_{out}}{dt'}\,dt' = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t')\,dt'
$$

$$
V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t')\,dt'
$$

**Step 8 — Final output equation.**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)
$$

This is the **canonical integrator equation**. The negative sign confirms the inverting nature, and the constant $V_{out}(0)$ is the initial charge on the capacitor at $t=0$.

---

### 3.2 Frequency-Domain Transfer Function Derivation (Laplace Domain)

**Step 1 — Replace the capacitor by its s-domain impedance.**

The impedance of a capacitor in the s-domain is $Z_C = 1/(sC)$. The impedance of a resistor is $Z_R = R$.

**Step 2 — Apply the standard inverting amplifier gain formula.**

For any inverting configuration, $V_{out}/V_{in} = -Z_f/Z_{in}$, where $Z_f$ is the feedback impedance and $Z_{in}$ is the input impedance. With $Z_f = 1/(sC)$ and $Z_{in} = R$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}} = -\frac{1/(sC)}{R} = -\frac{1}{sRC}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ to obtain the frequency response.**

$$
H(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j$:

$$
H(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC} = \frac{-j}{\omega RC} = \frac{1}{\omega RC}\angle(-90^\circ)
$$

**Step 4 — Extract magnitude and phase.**

$$
|H(j\omega)| = \frac{1}{\omega RC}
$$

$$
\angle H(j\omega) = -90^\circ
$$

The magnitude falls at **−20 dB/decade** (i.e., a factor of 10 in magnitude for every factor of 10 increase in frequency) and the phase is a constant **−90°** — the unmistakable signature of an ideal integrator.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**: An ideal op-amp integrator has $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. The input is a square wave that alternates between $+5\,\text{V}$ and $-5\,\text{V}$ with a period of $T = 4\,\text{ms}$ and the output is initialized to $0\,\text{V}$ at $t=0$. Sketch the output waveform for one full period.

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant.**

$$
\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the integrator gain factor.**

$$
\frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\,\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Analyze the first half-cycle ($0 \le t < 2\,\text{ms}$).**

Here $V_{in} = +5\,\text{V}$, so the output slopes negatively:

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t 5\,dt' = -5000\,t
$$

At $t = 2\,\text{ms}$:

$$
V_{out}(2\,\text{ms}) = -5000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = -10\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Analyze the second half-cycle ($2\,\text{ms} \le t < 4\,\text{ms}$).**

Here $V_{in} = -5\,\text{V}$, so the output slopes positively:

$$
V_{out}(t) = -10 - 5000 \times (t - 2\times 10^{-3}) = -10 + 5000(t - 2\times 10^{-3})
$$

At $t = 4\,\text{ms}$:

$$
V_{out}(4\,\text{ms}) = -10 + 5000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 0\,\text{V}
$$

**Result**: The output is a **triangular wave** with peak amplitude of $\pm 10\,\text{V}$ and the same period as the input — a square-to-triangular converter, which is a classical application.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal flow architecture** of an ideal op-amp integrator, tracing the path of the input signal through the resistor, the virtual ground node, the feedback capacitor, and finally to the output. This complements the schematic by emphasizing the *functional* role of each node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Input Voltage Source Vin] -->|provides Vin| resB[Resistor R]
    resB -->|current IR equals Vin divided by R| nodeC[Inverting Node Virtual Ground V- equals 0]
    capD[Feedback Capacitor C] -->|current IC equals C dVout by dt| nodeC
    nodeC -->|feeds into plus input of op-amp| opE[Ideal Op-Amp A OL to infinity]
    opE -->|amplifies the differential input| outF[Output Voltage Vout]
    outF -->|feeds back voltage across capacitor| capD
    gndG[Ground] -->|ties plus input to 0 V| opE

    style srcA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style resB fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style capD fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style opE fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
    style outF fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    style gndG fill:#212529,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Ideal vs. Practical Integrator — Comparative Mermaid View

This second diagram contrasts the **ideal integrator** (pure capacitor in feedback) with the **practical integrator** (resistor $R_f$ in parallel with the capacitor), highlighting the additional component that prevents DC saturation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph idealPath[Ideal Integrator Configuration]
        idealVin[Vin] --> idealR[Input Resistor R]
        idealR --> idealNode[Summing Junction V- equals 0]
        idealNode --> idealC[Feedback Capacitor C only]
        idealC --> idealOut[Vout equals minus 1 by RC times integral of Vin]
    end

    subgraph practicalPath[Practical Integrator Configuration]
        pracVin[Vin] --> pracR[Input Resistor R]
        pracR --> pracNode[Summing Junction V- equals 0]
        pracNode --> pracParallel[Parallel combination of Rf and C]
        pracParallel --> pracOut[Vout equals bounded output no saturation]
        pracNote[Limits DC gain to minus Rf over R and provides stable operation] -.-> pracParallel
    end

    style idealPath fill:#f0f8ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style practicalPath fill:#fff8dc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style idealVin fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style idealR fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    style idealNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style idealC fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style idealOut fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    style pracVin fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    style pracR fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000
    style pracNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    style pracParallel fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    style pracOut fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    style pracNote fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#842029,color:#000
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic of the Ideal Op-Amp Integrator

For quick reference during revision, the canonical schematic is reproduced below using standard ASCII notation:

```
        R
  Vin ----/\/\/------+
                     |
                     |
                    \|
                     \____
                    |    |
                    | -- |   <-- Op-Amp
                    |____|
                     |
                     |  +
                     === C
                     |
                     |
                    Vout
```

> **Reading the schematic**: The input $V_{in}$ is fed through resistor $R$ to the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded. The feedback element is capacitor $C$ connected between the output and the inverting input. The output $V_{out}$ is the negative integral of $V_{in}$ scaled by $1/RC$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**  
Define an ideal op-amp integrator. Write its output voltage equation and draw the circuit diagram.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **ideal op-amp integrator** is a linear analog circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the time integral of the input voltage, using an op-amp with a capacitor in the feedback path and a resistor at the input.

**Circuit Diagram**: (Draw a neat circuit with op-amp, $R$ at the inverting input from $V_{in}$, capacitor $C$ as feedback from output to inverting input, non-inverting input grounded.)

**Output Equation**:

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)
$$

The negative sign indicates inversion, and $V_{out}(0)$ is the initial condition.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
Why is a **practical integrator** preferred over an **ideal integrator**? Mention the role of the feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The ideal integrator has an **infinite DC gain** because its transfer function $H(s) = -1/(sRC)$ has a pole at the origin ($\omega = 0$). As a result, even a tiny DC offset at the input (or the op-amp's input bias current) gets integrated continuously, driving the output to **saturation** against the supply rails.

A **practical integrator** includes a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with the capacitor $C$. This resistor:
1. **Limits the DC gain** to a finite value $A_0 = -R_f/R$, preventing runaway saturation.
2. **Provides a path for the bias current** to flow, reducing the effect of input bias currents.
3. Acts as an integrator only for frequencies **$f \gg 1/(2\pi R_f C)$**; below this corner frequency, the circuit behaves as a simple inverting amplifier.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of an op-amp integrator in terms of the input voltage and the $RC$ time constant. State the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 Mark)**:
1. Op-amp is ideal: $A_{OL} \to \infty$, $Z_{in} \to \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$.
2. The circuit is in negative feedback, so the virtual short applies: $V_- = V_+ = 0$.

**Derivation (6 Marks)**:

Applying KCL at the inverting node (current through $R$ equals current through $C$):

$$
\frac{V_{in} - V_-}{R} = C\frac{d(V_- - V_{out})}{dt}
$$

Substituting $V_- = 0$:

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R} = C\frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC}
$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$
V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t')\,dt'
$$

Final result:

$$
\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)}
$$

---

**(b)** For an integrator with $R = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.05\,\mu\text{F}$, find (i) the time constant, (ii) the integrator gain factor, and (iii) the output voltage at $t = 5\,\text{ms}$ if the input is a constant $+4\,\text{V}$ (initial output is zero). **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Time Constant (2 Marks)**:

$$
\tau = RC = (20 \times 10^3) \times (0.05 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}
$$

**(ii) Integrator Gain Factor (2 Marks)**:

$$
K_i = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 1000\,\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**(iii) Output Voltage at $t = 5\,\text{ms}$ (3 Marks)**:

With $V_{in} = 4\,\text{V}$ (constant) and $V_{out}(0) = 0$:

$$
V_{out}(5\,\text{ms}) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^{5\times 10^{-3}} 4\,dt' = -1000 \times 4 \times 5\times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
V_{out}(5\,\text{ms}) = -1000 \times 0.02 = -20\,\text{V}
$$

**Conclusion**: The output reaches $-20\,\text{V}$, which would in practice saturate a typical op-amp powered by $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ rails. This numerical result demonstrates *why* a practical integrator is required in real designs.

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of an **ideal op-amp integrator**. Derive the expression for the output voltage and transfer function. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (1.5 Marks)**: The integrator consists of an op-amp with a resistor $R$ between the input source and the inverting input, and a capacitor $C$ in the feedback path between the output and the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Working Principle (1.5 Marks)**: The input voltage $V_{in}$ is converted to a current $I_R = V_{in}/R$ by the resistor. Because of the virtual ground, this entire current flows into the capacitor. The capacitor integrates this current over time, and the resulting voltage across it is amplified by the op-amp to produce the output. Since the capacitor voltage is $V_C = -V_{out}$ (because $V_- = 0$), the output is the negative integral of the input.

**Derivation (4 Marks)**:

Applying KCL at the inverting node:

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

Integrating:

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)
$$

For the transfer function, using $Z_f = 1/(sC)$ and $Z_{in} = R$:

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = -\frac{1}{sRC}
$$

---

**(b)** Sketch and explain the **frequency response** (magnitude and phase Bode plots) of the ideal integrator. Compare it with the **practical integrator**. **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Ideal Integrator Frequency Response (3 Marks)**:

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$
H(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC}
$$

Magnitude:

$$
|H(j\omega)| = \frac{1}{\omega RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi f RC}
$$

Phase:

$$
\angle H(j\omega) = -90^\circ \text{ (constant at all frequencies)}
$$

**Bode Plot Description**:
- The magnitude plot is a straight line with a slope of **−20 dB/decade** passing through $0\,\text{dB}$ at the **unity-gain frequency** $f_a = 1/(2\pi RC)$.
- The phase plot is a horizontal line at **−90°** for all frequencies from $0$ to $\infty$.

**Practical Integrator Frequency Response (2 Marks)**:

For the practical integrator with $R_f \parallel C$ in feedback:

$$
H(s) = -\frac{R_f}{R} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sR_fC}
$$

- For $f \ll f_a = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$: acts as **inverting amplifier** with gain $-R_f/R$.
- For $f \gg f_a$: rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** with **−90° phase**, behaving as an integrator.

**Comparison Table (2 Marks)**:

| **Feature** | **Ideal Integrator** | **Practical Integrator** |
|---|---|---|
| DC gain | Infinite (pole at origin) | Finite $= -R_f/R$ |
| Saturation tendency | Highly prone | Controlled by $R_f$ |
| Component count | 2 (R, C) | 3 (R, $R_f$, C) |
| Band of integration | All frequencies | $f > f_a$ only |
| Stability | Marginal | Stable |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**:
> 1. **Sign Error**: Forgetting the **negative sign** in $V_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$ is the most frequent mistake. Always remember the integrator is an *inverting* configuration — the output has opposite polarity to what a casual reading might suggest.
> 2. **Initial Condition Omission**: Failing to include the $V_{out}(0)$ term when the question specifies a non-zero initial condition leads to loss of 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Unit Confusion**: Mixing up seconds and milliseconds in the time constant calculation. Always convert to SI base units ($R$ in ohms, $C$ in farads) before computing $\tau = RC$.
> 4. **Confusing Integrator with Differentiator**: In the **differentiator**, $R$ and $C$ positions are **swapped** (R in feedback, C at input). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Saturation Oversight**: In numerical problems, students often forget to check whether the computed output exceeds the supply rails. Always mention that the op-amp will saturate in practice.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Integrator (Module 3, PBMRT304)**

- ☐ The op-amp integrator has **$R$ at the input and $C$ in the feedback path** of the inverting configuration.
- ☐ The **negative sign** in the output equation is non-negotiable — it is the single most-tested feature.
- ☐ The **time constant $\tau = RC$** governs the speed of integration; smaller $\tau$ means faster integration.
- ☐ The **transfer function** $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ has a **pole at the origin**, which is why the ideal integrator has infinite DC gain.
- ☐ **Magnitude** rolls off at **−20 dB/decade**; **phase** is constant at **−90°**.
- ☐ The **square-to-triangular wave** conversion is the canonical input-output behaviour used as a KTU exam verification problem.
- ☐ The **practical integrator** uses a feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ to limit the DC gain to $-R_f/R$ and prevent saturation.
- ☐ Practical integrators act as integrators only for **$f \gg 1/(2\pi R_f C)$**; below this corner they behave as inverting amplifiers.
- ☐ Key applications: **analog computers, waveform generators, PI controllers, dual-slope ADCs, PLL loop filters, active filters**.
- ☐ The integrator is the **time-domain dual** of the differentiator — swapping $R$ and $C$ converts one into the other.
- ☐ Always **draw the circuit diagram first** in long-answer questions — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for the diagram alone.
- ☐ Always include the **assumptions** (ideal op-amp, virtual short, zero input current) at the start of your derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
