<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — DIFFERENTIATOR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBMRT304 / Module 3):**
> An **op-amp differentiator** is a linear analog computing circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the **time derivative** of the input voltage, i.e.,
> $$V_{out}(t) = -K \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$
> where $K$ is a scaling constant (in **seconds**) determined by the RC time constant of the circuit.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Speedometer" View

Imagine the input signal $V_{in}(t)$ is a **car's position on a highway** measured every second. A differentiator is exactly like the **speedometer** in the car — it does not tell you *where* the car is, but *how fast the position is changing*. Mathematically, velocity is the derivative of position.

| Signal Type at Input | What the Differentiator Outputs |
|---|---|
| A **constant DC** (e.g., 5 V) | **0 V** (no change → no output) |
| A **linearly rising ramp** | A **fixed DC level** (constant slope) |
| A **sine wave** $\sin(\omega t)$ | A **cosine wave** of amplitude $\omega$ times the original |
| A **square wave** | **Sharp positive & negative spikes** at every edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The differentiator is the **dual** (mathematical complement) of the integrator. Wherever integration smooths and remembers, differentiation **reacts** to sudden changes and emphasizes sharp edges.

### Circuit-Level One-Line Summary

In the differentiator topology, the **input element is a capacitor** $C$ and the **feedback element is a resistor** $R_f$ — this is the *exact opposite* of the integrator. The capacitor "resists" changes in voltage, and the op-amp converts that reactive current into a proportional output voltage.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

- **Time constant** of the differentiator: $\tau = R_f C$ (expressed in **seconds**)
- **Gain at any angular frequency** $\omega$: $A(\omega) = \omega R_f C$ (dimensionless, but plotted in **dB**)
- **Standard gain slope** in the ideal region: **+20 dB/decade** (rising with frequency)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ideal Differentiator — Operational Logic

The **ideal** differentiator is built by simply swapping the input and feedback impedances of the standard inverting amplifier, *without* any limiting components.

### Step-by-Step Working Principle

- The op-amp is in the **inverting configuration** → the inverting input is at **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$ V).
- The non-inverting input is grounded for **bias-current compensation** through a resistor $R_{comp} = R_f$.
- The current through the input capacitor $C_1$ depends on **how fast the input voltage is changing**:
  $$i_C = C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$
- Because the inverting node is a **virtual ground**, this entire current $i_C$ is forced to flow through the feedback resistor $R_f$ (KCL at the summing junction).
- The output voltage is the **voltage drop across $R_f$** (with a sign inversion, since the current flows *from* the virtual ground *into* the output):
  $$V_{out} = -i_C \cdot R_f = -R_f C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$
- Hence, the output is the **scaled, inverted time-derivative** of the input.

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign simply indicates **180° phase inversion** between input and output, which is the standard behavior of any inverting op-amp topology.

## 2.2 The Three Critical Limitations of the Ideal Differentiator

These are **favourite KTU exam points** and must be memorized.

1. **Infinite gain at high frequencies** — Because $|A(j\omega)| = \omega R_f C$, as $\omega \to \infty$, the gain theoretically becomes infinite. Any tiny high-frequency noise gets **massively amplified**, swamping the desired output.
2. **Stability problem** — The op-amp's open-loop gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade; the differentiator's gain *rises* at $+20$ dB/decade. The **net loop gain** therefore stays at unity for an extended frequency range, pushing the circuit toward **oscillation** (poor phase margin).
3. **Practical capacitor imperfections** — Real capacitors have Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR) and leakage, which distort the pure derivative action.

## 2.3 The Practical Differentiator — The Engineering Fix

To overcome the limitations, two components are added:

- An **input resistor** $R_1$ in **series** with $C_1$
- A **feedback capacitor** $C_f$ in **parallel** with $R_f$

The resulting circuit behaves as a **true differentiator only in a limited band** $[f_a,\ f_b]$ and is a stable amplifier outside that band.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable in PBMRT304 ESE.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Engineering Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal differentiator output | $V_{out} = -R_f C_1 \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | Pure derivative, **volts** |
| 2 | Differentiator time constant | $\tau_d = R_f C_1$ | **seconds** — sets the gain |
| 3 | High-frequency limit freq $f_a$ | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ | Below $f_a$ → differentiator behaves correctly |
| 4 | Low-frequency limit freq $f_b$ | $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ | Above $f_b$ → differentiator behaves correctly |
| 5 | High-frequency gain ceiling | $A_{HF} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Limits noise amplification, **dimensionless** |
| 6 | Useful differentiation band | $f_a \le f \le f_b$ | Practical operating range, **Hz** |
| 7 | Component matching condition | $R_f C_1 = R_1 C_f$ | Ensures $f_a = f_b$ (symmetric response) |
| 8 | For stable design (KTU rule) | $R_f C_1 \ge R_1 C_f$ | Keeps high-freq gain flat until $\omega \to \infty$ |
| 9 | Magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ ideal | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \omega R_f C_1$ | Rises at **+20 dB/decade** |
| 10 | Output for $V_{in}=V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | $V_{out} = -\omega R_f C_1 V_m \cos(\omega t)$ | Cosine output, amplitude $\propto \omega$ |
| 11 | Phase shift of output w.r.t. input | $-90^\circ$ | Pure differentiator introduces $-90^\circ$ lag |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the magnitude bars around $H(j\omega)$ are written using `\vert ... \vert` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.4 Frequency Response Behavior — Three Regions

- **Region 1: $f < f_a$** — Capacitor $C_f$ is effectively *open* (high impedance). The circuit acts as an **inverting amplifier** with gain $A = -R_f / R_1$ (flat response, 0 dB/decade slope).
- **Region 2: $f_a < f < f_b$** — $C_f$ is dominant, $C_1$ behaves like a virtual ground. The circuit is the **true differentiator** — gain rises at **+20 dB/decade**.
- **Region 3: $f > f_b$** — $C_1$ is effectively *short* (low impedance). The circuit degenerates back into the **inverting amplifier** with gain $-R_f / R_1$, and the high-frequency noise is therefore **clamped**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> Differentiators are used wherever a **rate-of-change** signal is needed:
> - **PID controllers** (the "D" term detects how fast error is changing)
> - **Edge detectors** in pulse and digital circuits
> - **FM demodulators** (since FM information is in the frequency deviation, not amplitude)
> - **Radar & SONAR** signal processing for velocity extraction
> - **Analog computers** for solving differential equations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Differentiator Output Expression

> **Objective:** Show that $V_{out} = -R_f C_1 \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Assume ideal op-amp conditions.**
The inverting input is at virtual ground:
$$V^- = 0\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the i-v relationship of the input capacitor.**
The current through capacitor $C_1$ (from $V_{in}$ toward the virtual ground) is:
$$i_C = C_1 \cdot \frac{d(V_{in} - V^-)}{dt} = C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$
*(Here we used $V^- = 0$, so the voltage across $C_1$ is simply $V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$.)*

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the inverting node.**
Because the op-amp draws no input current, the capacitor current must flow entirely through $R_f$:
$$i_C = i_{R_f}$$

**Step 4 — Express the output in terms of this current.**
The voltage across $R_f$ (with the polarity such that current flows from the virtual ground node to the output node) is:
$$V^- - V_{out} = i_{R_f} \cdot R_f$$
$$0 - V_{out} = i_{R_f} \cdot R_f$$
$$V_{out} = -i_{R_f} \cdot R_f$$

**Step 5 — Combine Step 2 and Step 4.**
Substituting $i_{R_f} = i_C = C_1 \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$:
$$V_{out} = -R_f \cdot C_1 \cdot \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$
$$\boxed{\,V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}\,}$$

> The scaling constant $R_f C_1$ has units of **seconds**, and the product has the dimensions required so that $V_{out}$ remains in **volts**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transfer Function of the Practical Differentiator

> **Objective:** Find $H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)}$ for the circuit with $Z_{in} = R_1 + \dfrac{1}{sC_1}$ and $Z_f = R_f \Vert \dfrac{1}{sC_f}$.

**Step 1 — Write the input impedance $Z_1$ (series combination of $R_1$ and $C_1$).**
$$Z_1(s) = R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1} = \frac{1 + sR_1 C_1}{sC_1}$$

**Step 2 — Write the feedback impedance $Z_f$ (parallel combination of $R_f$ and $C_f$).**
$$Z_f(s) = R_f \Vert \frac{1}{sC_f} = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{sC_f}}{R_f + \frac{1}{sC_f}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C_f}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the standard inverting-amplifier transfer function.**
For any inverting op-amp topology:
$$H(s) = -\frac{Z_f(s)}{Z_1(s)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Z_f(s)$ and $Z_1(s)$.**
$$H(s) = -\frac{\dfrac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C_f}}{\dfrac{1 + sR_1 C_1}{sC_1}}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-multiply and simplify.**
$$H(s) = -\frac{R_f \cdot sC_1}{(1 + sR_f C_f)(1 + sR_1 C_1)}$$

**Step 6 — Separate out the differentiator action explicitly.**
$$H(s) = -\frac{sR_f C_1}{(1 + sR_f C_f)(1 + sR_1 C_1)}$$

This is the **complete, exact transfer function** of the practical differentiator.

**Step 7 — Identify the two break frequencies.**
- The first break (numerator & first denominator term combined) gives:
  $$f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f} \quad \text{(high-frequency pole)}$$
- The second break gives:
  $$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} \quad \text{(low-frequency pole)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule (highly examinable):**
> $$f_a \ll f_b \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad R_f C_1 \gg R_1 C_f$$
> This ensures a wide flat band where the differentiator behaves as a true differentiator.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output for a Sinusoidal Input

> **Objective:** Show that the differentiator shifts the input sine wave by $-90^\circ$ and amplifies it by $\omega R_f C_1$.

**Step 1 — Let the input be a sinusoid.**
$$V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Take the time derivative.**
$$\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $-R_f C_1$.**
$$V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \cdot \omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite using the identity $-\cos(\theta) = \cos(\theta + 180^\circ)$.**
$$V_{out}(t) = \omega R_f C_1 V_m \sin\left(\omega t - 90^\circ\right)$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**
$$\boxed{\,V_{out}(t) = \omega R_f C_1 \cdot V_m \cdot \sin(\omega t - 90^\circ)\,}$$

> The output is a cosine (with a sign flip → sine) leading/lagging the input by exactly $-90^\circ$, and the amplitude is multiplied by the **dimensionless factor** $\omega R_f C_1$, which **grows linearly with frequency** — this is the root cause of high-frequency noise amplification.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Differentiator vs Integrator)

> [!NOTE]
> This side-by-side comparison is a recurring **2-mark short-answer** item in KTU exams.

| Feature | **Differentiator** | **Integrator** |
|---|---|---|
| Input element | **Capacitor** $C$ | **Resistor** $R$ |
| Feedback element | **Resistor** $R_f$ | **Capacitor** $C_f$ |
| Output equation | $V_{out} = -R_f C \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}\, dt$ |
| Gain slope vs frequency | **+20 dB/decade** (rising) | **$-20$ dB/decade** (falling) |
| Phase shift | $-90^\circ$ | $-90^\circ$ (at the integrator region) |
| Acts on | **Rate of change** of input | **Area under** the input curve |
| High-freq behaviour | Gain **increases** (noise problem) | Gain **decreases** (good noise rejection) |
| Typical application | Edge detector, FM demodulator | Ramp generator, ADC smoother |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!TIP]
> Electronic components (resistors, capacitors, op-amp pins) cannot be drawn directly in Mermaid. We therefore use **block-level functional architecture** diagrams that map the **signal flow** with the same engineering precision as a circuit schematic.

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Ideal Differentiator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin["Vin INPUT SOURCE"] --> Cnode["INPUT CAPACITOR C1"]
    Cnode --> Sumnode["SUMMING JUNCTION V NEGATIVE  V PLUS EQUALS ZERO VIRTUAL GROUND"]
    Sumnode --> Rfnode["FEEDBACK RESISTOR RF"]
    Rfnode --> Outnode["Vout OUTPUT NODE"]
    GndA["GROUND"] -.-> Sumnode
    GndB["GROUND"] -.-> Ineg["OPAMP NON INVERTING INPUT PLUS PIN"]
    Ineg --> Opamp["IDEAL OPAMP INFINITE GAIN"]
    Opamp --> Outnode
    classDef io fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F3A93,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFF1CC,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Vin,Outnode io
    class Cnode,Sumnode,Rfnode,Opamp core
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Input voltage drives a **capacitor** $C_1$ (current $\propto dV_{in}/dt$).
- This current is **forced into the virtual ground** of the inverting op-amp input.
- The op-amp output **inverts and amplifies** the current through $R_f$, producing a voltage $\propto -dV_{in}/dt$.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Practical Differentiator (with Stabilizing Components)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Vin["Vin INPUT SIGNAL"] --> R1node["R1 INPUT RESISTOR STABILITY PLUS NOISE LIMIT"]
    R1node --> C1node["C1 INPUT CAPACITOR DIFFERENTIATING ELEMENT"]
    C1node --> Sumnode["INVERTING NODE V PLUS EQUALS ZERO VIRTUAL GROUND"]
    Sumnode --> Rfnode["RF FEEDBACK RESISTOR DC PATH FOR BIAS"]
    Sumnode --> Cfnode["CF FEEDBACK CAPACITOR HIGH FREQUENCY NOISE LIMITER"]
    Rfnode --> Outnode["Vout OUTPUT NODE"]
    Cfnode --> Outnode
    Gnd["GROUND REFERENCE"] -.-> Sumnode
    Gnd -.-> PlusIn["OPAMP NON INVERTING INPUT V PLUS"]
    PlusIn --> Opamp["OPAMP GAIN BLOCK A OL"]
    Opamp --> Outnode
    classDef io fill:#E8F1FF,stroke:#1F3A93,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef fix fill:#E0F7E0,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#FFF1CC,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class Vin,Outnode io
    class R1node,Cfnode fix
    class C1node,Sumnode,Rfnode,Opamp core
```

**Why two extra components?**
- **$R_1$** in series with $C_1$ raises the input impedance at very high frequencies, **clamping the gain** to $R_f / R_1$ and eliminating the noise explosion.
- **$C_f$** in parallel with $R_f$ rolls off the high-frequency noise by providing a low-impedance path at high $\omega$.

---

## 4.3 Bode-Magnitude Sketch (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LF["LOW FREQUENCY REGION  f LESS THAN fa  INVERTING AMPLIFIER GAIN  RF DIVIDED BY R1  0 DB PER DECADE"] --> MF["MID FREQUENCY REGION  fa LESS THAN f LESS THAN fb  TRUE DIFFERENTIATOR  GAIN RISES  PLUS 20 DB PER DECADE"]
    MF --> HF["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION  f GREATER THAN fb  INVERTING AMPLIFIER AGAIN  GAIN CLAMPED AT RF DIVIDED BY R1  0 DB PER DECADE"]
    classDef lf fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mf fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#00688B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hf fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class LF lf
    class MF mf
    class HF hf
```

**Engineering reading:** The mid-band region is the **useful differentiator band**. The slopes outside this band are flat (0 dB/decade) because the additional components dominate, **stabilizing the amplifier** and **limiting noise**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting the **negative sign** in $V_{out} = -R_f C \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ (1 mark lost in nearly every paper).
> 2. Confusing the **input and feedback elements** with the integrator (it is the *capacitor at the input* — not at the feedback).
> 3. Writing the magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ as $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ with **pipe characters** inside markdown — instead use `\\vert H(j\\omega) \\vert` in LaTeX.
> 4. Missing the **virtual-ground assumption** while deriving — examiners explicitly test this in 2-mark sub-parts.
> 5. Not mentioning the **two break frequencies** $f_a$ and $f_b$ in practical differentiator answers.

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Define an ideal op-amp differentiator. Derive the expression for its output voltage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An op-amp differentiator is a circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the time derivative of the input voltage.
- **Circuit description (1 mark):** Consists of an op-amp in inverting configuration with a capacitor $C_1$ at the input and a resistor $R_f$ in the feedback path.
- **Derivation (1 mark):** Using virtual ground at the inverting node and KCL,
  $$V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \cdot \frac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**State any three limitations of the ideal differentiator. How does the practical differentiator overcome them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct pair):**

| # | Limitation of Ideal Differentiation | Remedy in Practical Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gain increases without bound at high frequencies → noise amplification | Input resistor $R_1$ limits high-frequency gain to $R_f / R_1$ |
| 2 | Stability problem / tendency to oscillate | Feedback capacitor $C_f$ provides phase lead compensation |
| 3 | Noisy output due to high-frequency pickup | Combined $R_1$–$C_f$ network clamps the bandwidth |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of an **ideal op-amp differentiator**. Draw its frequency response. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (1 mark):**
- The inverting input has $C_1$ to $V_{in}$ and $R_f$ to $V_{out}$.
- The non-inverting input is grounded.
- Compensation resistor $R_{comp} = R_f$ is connected between non-inverting input and ground (for bias current compensation).

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual-Ground Concept (1 mark):**
$$V^- = V^+ = 0\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor Current (1 mark):**
$$i_C = C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL (1 mark):**
$$i_C = - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5 — Equate and Solve (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f C_1 \frac{dV_{in}}{dt}}$$

**Step 6 — Frequency Response (1 mark):**
For $V_{in} = V_m \sin(\omega t)$,
$$V_{out} = -\omega R_f C_1 V_m \cos(\omega t)$$
Magnitude rises linearly with $\omega$ at **+20 dB/decade**.

**Step 7 — Sketch (1 mark):** Gain magnitude plot rising as a straight line on log-log scale, slope +20 dB/decade, passing through 0 dB at $\omega = 1 / (R_f C_1)$.

---

**(b)** Explain the **practical differentiator** with input resistor $R_1$ and feedback capacitor $C_f$. Derive its transfer function and explain how the components are chosen for stable operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Draw the Practical Circuit (1 mark):**
- Series $R_1$–$C_1$ at input.
- Parallel $R_f$–$C_f$ in feedback.
- Non-inverting input grounded via $R_{comp} = R_f \Vert X_{C_f}$ (matched compensation).

**Step 2 — Write the Impedances (1 mark):**
$$Z_1 = R_1 + \frac{1}{sC_1}, \quad Z_f = R_f \Vert \frac{1}{sC_f}$$

**Step 3 — Transfer Function Derivation (2 marks):**
$$H(s) = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_1} = -\frac{s R_f C_1}{(1 + sR_f C_f)(1 + sR_1 C_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Identify Break Frequencies (1 mark):**
$$f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}, \quad f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$$

**Step 5 — Component Selection Criterion (1 mark):**
- For stable operation: $f_a \ll f_b$, i.e., $R_f C_1 \gg R_1 C_f$.
- High-frequency gain is then capped at $A_{HF} = R_f / R_1$.

**Step 6 — Three-Region Frequency Response (1 mark):**
- $f < f_a$: inverting amplifier with gain $-R_f / R_1$.
- $f_a < f < f_b$: true differentiator (+20 dB/decade slope).
- $f > f_b$: inverting amplifier again, gain capped at $-R_f / R_1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Compare the op-amp **differentiator** and **integrator** in terms of circuit topology, output equation, frequency response, and applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Table — 1.75 marks per row, total 7 marks):**

| Parameter | **Differentiator** | **Integrator** |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit topology | $C_1$ at input, $R_f$ in feedback | $R$ at input, $C_f$ in feedback |
| Output equation | $V_{out} = -R_f C_1 \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{R C_f}\displaystyle\int V_{in}\, dt$ |
| Frequency response (gain magnitude) | Rises at **+20 dB/decade** | Falls at **$-20$ dB/decade** |
| Output phase | $-90^\circ$ w.r.t. input | $-90^\circ$ w.r.t. input |
| Acts on | Rate of change of input | Area under the input curve |
| Response to high-freq input | **Amplified** (noise problem) | **Attenuated** (good noise rejection) |
| Typical applications | Edge detector, FM demodulator, PID "D" term | Ramp generator, ADC smoothing, wave-shaping |

---

**(b)** Design a **practical differentiator** to differentiate an input signal whose highest frequency component is **$f_{max} = 1$ kHz**. Assume $R_f C_1 = 0.1$ s and choose $R_1 C_f$ to be 100 times smaller. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Design Rule (1 mark):**
Pick $R_f C_1 = 0.1$ s as given. Choose $R_1 C_f = R_f C_1 / 100 = 10^{-3}$ s to satisfy $R_f C_1 \gg R_1 C_f$.

**Step 2 — Choose $R_f$ and $C_1$ (1 mark):**
- Pick $C_1 = 0.1\ \mu F = 10^{-7}$ F
- Then $R_f = \dfrac{0.1}{10^{-7}} = 10^6\ \Omega = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$

**Step 3 — Calculate $f_a$ (1 mark):**
The required high-frequency break must satisfy $f_a \ge f_{max} = 1$ kHz so that differentiation holds up to the signal's highest frequency.
$$f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f} \ge 1\ \text{kHz}$$
Choose $f_a = 10 \cdot f_{max} = 10$ kHz for safety.

**Step 4 — Solve for $C_f$ (1 mark):**
$$C_f = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f f_a} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 10^4} \approx 15.9\ \text{pF}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $R_1$ (1 mark):**
From $R_1 C_f = 10^{-3}$ s:
$$R_1 = \frac{10^{-3}}{15.9 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 62.9\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Verify $f_b$ (1 mark):**
$$f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 62.9 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 25.3\ \text{Hz}$$
Since $f_a = 10$ kHz $\gg f_b = 25.3$ Hz, the condition $f_a \ll f_b$ is **inverted correctly** (i.e., $f_a > f_b$ which is exactly what we want for the differentiator band to be wide).

**Step 7 — Summary (1 mark):**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| $C_1$ | $0.1\ \mu F$ |
| $R_f$ | $1\ M\Omega$ |
| $C_f$ | $\approx 15.9\ pF$ |
| $R_1$ | $\approx 62.9\ k\Omega$ |
| Useful differentiator band | $25.3\ \text{Hz} \le f \le 10\ \text{kHz}$ |

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following 10 points before the ESE — they cover ~90% of the marks.**

1. **Core equation:** $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C_1 \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}$ — always carries a **negative sign** (inverting topology).
2. **Topology mnemonic:** Differentiator = **C at input, R at feedback** (Integrator is the *opposite*).
3. **Virtual ground assumption** at $V^-$ is the starting point of every derivation.
4. **Ideal differentiator limitations**: (i) infinite gain at high $\omega$, (ii) noise amplification, (iii) poor stability / oscillation tendency.
5. **Practical differentiator fix**: add $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ AND $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$.
6. **Transfer function** (practical): $H(s) = -\dfrac{s R_f C_1}{(1 + sR_f C_f)(1 + sR_1 C_1)}$ — must be reproduced from memory in 14-mark answers.
7. **Two break frequencies**:
   - $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ — high-frequency roll-off
   - $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$ — low-frequency roll-off
8. **Design rule**: $R_f C_1 \gg R_1 C_f$ ⇒ $f_a \ll f_b$ gives a wide differentiator band.
9. **High-frequency gain ceiling**: $A_{HF} = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ — this is what *limits* the noise.
10. **Output for sine input** is a **cosine** lagging the input by **$-90^\circ$** with amplitude $\omega R_f C_1 V_m$.

> **Golden takeaway for ESE:** The *practical* differentiator is essentially an inverting amplifier in the **outer** frequency regions and a true differentiator **only in the middle band** $[f_a, f_b]$. Always mention both break frequencies and the design rule $R_f C_1 \ge R_1 C_f$ in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Comparator ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparator (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A comparator is an electronic circuit that compares the magnitude of two input voltages (or a single input voltage with a reference voltage) and produces a binary output — switching between two saturated states ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$) — to indicate which input is larger. In Analog Electronics, an **operational amplifier (op-amp) used in open-loop configuration** behaves as a comparator because its open-loop voltage gain $A_{OL}$ is extremely high (typically $10^5$ to $10^6$). Any minute difference between the two input voltages drives the output into saturation. **Key Operating Principle:** $$V_o = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$$ When $(V_+ - V_-) > 0$, then $V_o = +V_{sat}$ When $(V_+ - V_-) < 0$, then $V_o = -V_{sat}$ ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Tilt-Scale Balance"** > Imagine a balance scale with two pans — a fixed reference weight (V_ref) on the left pan, and an unknown weight (V_in) on the right pan. The comparator is like a digital indicator attached to the scale: > - If V_in > V_ref, the indicator flips to **"HIGH"** ($+V_{sat}$) > - If V_in < V_ref, the indicator flips to **"LOW"** ($-V_{sat}$) > - The scale is infinitely sensitive — even a feather's weight difference triggers a clean digital flip. ### 1.3 Types of Op-Amp Comparators (KTU Module Coverage) 1. **Basic Inverting Comparator** — Input to non-inverting terminal, reference to inverting terminal 2. **Basic Non-Inverting Comparator** — Input to inverting terminal, reference to non-inverting terminal 3. **Zero-Crossing Detector** — $V_{ref} = 0\,V$ (grounded) 4. **Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator)** — Comparator with positive feedback to introduce **hysteresis** 5. **Window Comparator** — Detects whether input lies within a defined voltage window ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Standard Saturation Voltage:** $V_{sat} \approx \pm 12\,V$ to $\pm 14\,V$ (for $\pm 15\,V$ supply op-amps) > - **Open-Loop Gain:** $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ (typical for $\mu$A741) > - **Output Stage:** Operates in **non-linear (saturated) region** — not in linear region > - **Response Time:** Typically in the order of **microseconds** ($\mu s$) > - **Reference Symbol:** $V_{ref}$ (in Volts, **bold** for emphasis: **V_ref**) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Theory of a Basic Comparator A comparator uses an op-amp **without negative feedback** (open-loop). The op-amp's differential input is: $$\epsilon = V_+ - V_-$$ The output is governed by the open-loop transfer characteristic: $$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot \epsilon = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$$ Since $A_{OL}$ is enormous, the output saturates for any non-zero $\epsilon$: - If $\epsilon > 0$ (even a few $\mu V$), then $V_o = +V_{sat}$ - If $\epsilon < 0$, then $V_o = -V_{sat}$ - The transition occurs at the **threshold point** where $V_+ = V_-$ ### 2.2 Inverting Comparator — Step-by-Step Logic - **Inverting Comparator Configuration:** $V_{in}$ applied to the **non-inverting** terminal ($V_+$), and $V_{ref}$ applied to the **inverting** terminal ($V_-$). - When $V_{in} > V_{ref}$: $V_+ > V_-$, so $V_o = +V_{sat}$ - When $V_{in} < V_{ref}$: $V_+ < V_-$, so $V_o = -V_{sat}$ - **Threshold Voltage:** $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ > [!NOTE] > **Naming Convention:** Despite being called an "inverting comparator," the name refers to the location of the signal input (at the inverting input in some textbooks) or the phase-inverting nature of the output when the input crosses the reference. ### 2.3 Non-Inverting Comparator — Step-by-Step Logic - **Non-Inverting Comparator Configuration:** $V_{in}$ applied to the **inverting** terminal ($V_-$), and $V_{ref}$ applied to the **non-inverting** terminal ($V_+$). - When $V_{in} > V_{ref}$: $V_- > V_+$, so $V_o = -V_{sat}$ - When $V_{in} < V_{ref}$: $V_- < V_+$, so $V_o = +V_{sat}$ - **Threshold Voltage:** $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ ### 2.4 Zero-Crossing Detector A special case where $V_{ref} = 0\,V$ (inverting input is grounded). The output switches polarity every time the input crosses zero. ### 2.5 Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator) A Schmitt trigger uses **positive feedback** to create two distinct threshold voltages, eliminating false triggering due to noise. This introduces **hysteresis**. - **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UT}$):** Point at which output switches from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ - **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LT}$):** Point at which output switches from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ - **Hysteresis Width ($V_H$):** $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$ The feedback fraction $\beta$ for an inverting Schmitt trigger: $$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ Threshold voltages: $$V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}, \quad V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$ Hysteresis voltage: $$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$ ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **master reference** for all comparator problems. Master it before any derivation or design question. | # | Parameter / Configuration | Governing Equation | Condition / Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Op-Amp Open-Loop Output | $V_o = A_{OL}(V_+ - V_-)$ | Linear (ideal) — V | | 2 | Output Saturation (High) | $V_o = +V_{sat}$ | When $V_+ > V_-$ | | 3 | Output Saturation (Low) | $V_o = -V_{sat}$ | When $V_+ < V_-$ | | 4 | Inverting Comparator Threshold | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ | V | | 5 | Non-Inverting Comparator Threshold | $V_{th} = V_{ref}$ | V | | 6 | Feedback Fraction (Schmitt) | $\beta = R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Upper Threshold (Schmitt) | $V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | V | | 8 | Lower Threshold (Schmitt) | $V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | V | | 9 | Hysteresis Width | $V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | V | | 10 | Zero-Crossing Threshold | $V_{th} = 0\,V$ | V | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs):** Comparators form the core of flash ADCs. > - **Oscilloscope Triggering Circuits:** Schmitt triggers provide clean triggering in the presence of noise. > - **Over-Voltage / Under-Voltage Protection:** Window comparators monitor supply rails. > - **Signal Conditioning in Sensors:** Convert analog sensor outputs to digital levels for microcontrollers. > - **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM) Generation:** Comparators compare a triangle wave with a control voltage. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Voltage of a Basic Inverting Comparator **Given Configuration:** - $V_{in}$ at non-inverting terminal ($V_+$) - $V_{ref}$ at inverting terminal ($V_-$) **Step 1:** The differential input voltage is: $$\epsilon = V_+ - V_- = V_{in} - V_{ref}$$ **Step 2:** The op-amp output in open loop is: $$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot \epsilon = A_{OL}(V_{in} - V_{ref})$$ **Step 3:** As $A_{OL} \to \infty$ (ideal op-amp), the output saturates. The transition point occurs precisely when: $$V_{in} - V_{ref} = 0 \implies V_{in} = V_{ref}$$ **Step 4:** Therefore, the **threshold voltage** of the inverting comparator is: $$\boxed{V_{th} = V_{ref}}$$ **Interpretation:** - For $V_{in} > V_{th}$: $V_o = +V_{sat}$ - For $V_{in} < V_{th}$: $V_o = -V_{sat}$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Threshold Voltages of an Inverting Schmitt Trigger **Given Configuration:** - $V_{in}$ at inverting terminal ($V_-$) through $R_1$ - Output $V_o$ fed back to non-inverting terminal ($V_+$) through resistor $R_2$ from $V_o$, with $R_3$ from $V_+$ to ground - For analysis, we use a single voltage divider: $\beta = R_3 / (R_2 + R_3)$ **Step 1 — Apply KCL at the Non-Inverting Node ($V_+$):** Assuming ideal op-amp input current is zero, the voltage at $V_+$ is determined by the voltage divider formed by $R_2$ and $R_3$ from $V_o$: $$V_+ = V_o \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \beta \cdot V_o$$ **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node ($V_-$):** With $V_{in}$ connected through $R_1$ and no other current path (ideal op-amp): $$V_- = V_{in}$$ (assuming no other resistors at the inverting node; otherwise apply superposition) **Step 3 — Switching Condition:** The output switches state when $V_+ = V_-$: $$\beta \cdot V_o = V_{in}$$ **Step 4 — Assume the Output is at $+V_{sat}$ (Previous State):** If $V_o = +V_{sat}$, then: $$V_+ = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat})$$ The output will switch from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$ when $V_{in}$ rises to equal $V_+$: $$\boxed{V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}}$$ **Step 5 — Assume the Output is at $-V_{sat}$ (Previous State):** If $V_o = -V_{sat}$, then: $$V_+ = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat})$$ The output will switch from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$ when $V_{in}$ falls to equal $V_+$: $$\boxed{V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}}$$ **Step 6 — Calculate Hysteresis Width:** $$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \beta V_{sat} - (-\beta V_{sat})$$ $$V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$ $$\boxed{V_H = \frac{2 R_3 \cdot V_{sat}}{R_2 + R_3}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Resistor Design for a Schmitt Trigger (Worked Example) **Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):** > Design an inverting Schmitt trigger with $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,V$ and an upper threshold voltage $V_{UT} = +4\,V$. Calculate the hysteresis voltage and the lower threshold. Choose $R_3 = 10\,k\Omega$. **Step 1 — Compute Feedback Fraction $\beta$:** From $V_{UT} = \beta \cdot V_{sat}$: $$\beta = \frac{V_{UT}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{4\,V}{12\,V} = \frac{1}{3}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $R_2$:** $$\beta = \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \implies \frac{1}{3} = \frac{10\,k\Omega}{R_2 + 10\,k\Omega}$$ $$R_2 + 10\,k\Omega = 30\,k\Omega \implies R_2 = 20\,k\Omega$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Lower Threshold:** $$V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\frac{1}{3} \times 12\,V = -4\,V$$ **Step 4 — Calculate Hysteresis Width:** $$V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat} = 2 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 12\,V = 8\,V$$ **Final Design Parameters:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $R_2$ (Feedback Resistor) | $20\,k\Omega$ | | $R_3$ (Ground Resistor) | $10\,k\Omega$ | | $\beta$ | $1/3$ | | $V_{UT}$ | $+4\,V$ | | $V_{LT}$ | $-4\,V$ | | $V_H$ | $8\,V$ | --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Comparator Types > [!NOTE] > This analytical matrix is frequently tested in KTU Module 3 examinations. Memorize the key differentiators. | Feature | Basic Comparator | Schmitt Trigger | Window Comparator | |---|---|---|---| | Feedback Type | None (Open Loop) | Positive Feedback | Dual Comparators | | Number of Thresholds | One | Two ($V_{UT}$, $V_{LT}$) | Two ($V_{TH}$, $V_{TL}$) | | Hysteresis | Absent | Present ($V_H$) | Absent | | Noise Immunity | Poor | Excellent | Moderate | | Output States | 2 | 2 | 1 (active) or 0 | | Typical Application | Threshold detection | Waveform squaring | Range detection | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Basic Op-Amp Comparator Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nA["V_in Input Signal"] --> nB["Op-Amp in
Open-Loop Mode
A_OL is very high"] nC["V_ref Reference Voltage"] --> nB nB --> nD{"Compare V_in
with V_ref"} nD -->|"V_in greater than V_ref"| nE["Output: +V_sat
(HIGH state)"] nD -->|"V_in less than V_ref"| nF["Output: -V_sat
(LOW state)"] nE --> nG["Decision Output"] nF --> nG["Decision Output"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Schmitt Trigger Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD sA["Start: V_in applied at inverting input"] --> sB{"Current output
state?"} sB -->|"+V_sat"| sC{"V_in exceeds V_UT?"} sB -->|"-V_sat"| sD{"V_in falls below V_LT?"} sC -->|"Yes"| sE["Output switches to
-V_sat"] sC -->|"No"| sF["Output remains
at +V_sat"] sD -->|"Yes"| sG["Output switches to
+V_sat"] sD -->|"No"| sH["Output remains
at -V_sat"] sE --> sI["End of transition"] sF --> sI["End of transition"] sG --> sI["End of transition"] sH --> sI["End of transition"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Inverting Schmitt Trigger Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR fA["V_in Signal"] -->|R1| fB["Summing Node
at V_minus"] fC["V_o Output"] -->|R2| fD["Summing Node
at V_plus"] fE["Ground Reference"] -->|R3| fD fB --> fF["Op-Amp Core
A_OL multiplication"] fD --> fF fF --> fG["V_o Output
Saturated at plus or minus V_sat"] fG -->|Positive Feedback Loop| fC ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Window Comparator Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR wA["V_in Input"] --> wB["Comparator 1
V_ref_H reference"] wA --> wC["Comparator 2
V_ref_L reference"] wB --> wD["AND Logic Gate"] wC --> wD wD -->|"V_LT less than V_in less than V_UT"| wE["Output: HIGH
Window active"] wD -->|"V_in outside window"| wF["Output: LOW
Window inactive"] ``` ### 4.5 ASCII Schematic: Transfer Characteristics Summary **Inverting Comparator Transfer Characteristic:** ``` V_o | +V_sat------+---------------- | | | (Slope = infinity, ideal) | | -------+----+---------- V_in V_ref | | | -V_sat------+---------------- | | ``` **Schmitt Trigger Transfer Characteristic (showing hysteresis loop):** ``` V_o | +V_sat----->(1)----------+ (Path A: V_in increasing) | | | | | +-------+ | | | V_UT V_LT | | | | +-----+ | | | | (Path B: V_in decreasing) -V_sat------+----------<-(2) | v V_in ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define a comparator. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a zero-crossing detector using an op-amp. Draw its input and output waveforms.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A comparator is an electronic circuit that compares two input voltages and produces a digital output indicating which input is larger. In its basic form, an op-amp is operated in open-loop mode (no feedback) so that its enormous open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ drives the output into saturation for any input difference. **Zero-Crossing Detector Circuit (1 Mark):** ``` V_in o------|\ | >-------o V_o (saturated output) GND o------|-/ Op-Amp ``` The non-inverting input ($V_+$) is grounded, and the input signal $V_{in}$ is applied to the inverting input ($V_-$). **Operation (1 Mark):** When $V_{in} > 0\,V$, then $V_- > V_+$, so $V_o = -V_{sat}$. When $V_{in} < 0\,V$, then $V_- < V_+$, so $V_o = +V_{sat}$. The output therefore switches polarity precisely at every zero-crossing of the input — converting a sine wave (for example) into a square wave. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is hysteresis? Why is hysteresis introduced in a Schmitt trigger comparator? Mention any two applications.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Hysteresis Definition (1 Mark):** Hysteresis is a phenomenon in which the output of a system depends not only on the current input but also on the **past history** of the input. In a Schmitt trigger, this means the switching threshold for an increasing input is different from the switching threshold for a decreasing input. **Why Hysteresis is Needed (1 Mark):** In a basic comparator, if the input signal contains noise near the threshold, the output will "chatter" or oscillate rapidly between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$, causing false triggering. Hysteresis provides a **dead-band** or **noise margin** between $V_{LT}$ and $V_{UT}$, ensuring the output switches cleanly only when the input crosses one of the two distinct thresholds. **Two Applications (1 Mark):** 1. **Square-wave and pulse generation** (function generators, astable multivibrators) 2. **Noise-free signal conditioning** in digital input circuits (e.g., debouncing mechanical switches) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- ## **Question A (14 Marks)** ### Part (a) — 7 Marks > **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** *With a neat circuit diagram and transfer characteristic, explain the operation of a **non-inverting comparator** with reference voltage $V_{ref} = 2\,V$ and $V_{sat} = \pm 13\,V$. Also draw the input-output waveform for a sinusoidal input of amplitude $5\,V$.* **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Circuit Diagram (1 Mark):** ``` GND o------|\ | >-------o V_o V_in o------|-/ Op-Amp (V_ref = 2V at V+) ``` Wait — for a **non-inverting comparator**, $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting** terminal, and $V_{ref}$ to the **non-inverting** terminal: ``` V_ref = 2V ---|+\ | >---+---o V_o V_in --------+|-/ | Op-Amp ``` **Step 1 — Threshold Identification (1 Mark):** The threshold is at $V_{in} = V_{ref} = 2\,V$. **Step 2 — Output Logic (2 Marks):** - When $V_{in} > V_{ref} = 2\,V$: $V_- > V_+$, so $V_o = -V_{sat} = -13\,V$ - When $V_{in} < V_{ref} = 2\,V$: $V_- < V_+$, so $V_o = +V_{sat} = +13\,V$ **Step 3 — Transfer Characteristic (1 Mark):** ``` V_o | +13V----+----------+ | | | | (steep, almost vertical) | | ------+----+-----+---- V_in (V) 0 2V | | | | | | -13V----+----------+ ``` **Step 4 — Input-Output Waveform (2 Marks):** For a $5\,V$ amplitude sine wave $V_{in}(t) = 5\sin(\omega t)$: - The input crosses $2\,V$ at angles $\theta = \sin^{-1}(2/5) = 23.6°$ and $\theta = 180° - 23.6° = 156.4°$ - Output is $-13\,V$ between these crossing points (input > 2V) - Output is $+13\,V$ elsewhere (input < 2V) ``` V_in | ___________ | / \ __+--/-------------\----+-- 2V (threshold) | / \ /| |/ \/ | +------------------\-+----- | \____ V_o | __________ | | | +13V------+ +----- __+ | | +---- t | | | | | | |_____| |____ -13V ``` --- ### Part (b) — 7 Marks > **Design an inverting Schmitt trigger** with $V_{sat} = \pm 12\,V$. The required upper threshold voltage is $V_{UT} = +4\,V$. Calculate the feedback fraction $\beta$, choose $R_3 = 10\,k\Omega$, and determine $R_2$. Also compute the lower threshold and hysteresis width. Draw the hysteresis loop. **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Circuit Identification (1 Mark):** In an inverting Schmitt trigger, $V_{in}$ is applied at the inverting input, and the output is fed back through $R_2$ to the non-inverting input with $R_3$ to ground. **Step 2 — Feedback Fraction Calculation (1 Mark):** $$\beta = \frac{V_{UT}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{4\,V}{12\,V} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$ **Step 3 — Resistor $R_2$ Determination (1 Mark):** $$\beta = \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$ $$\frac{1}{3} = \frac{10\,k\Omega}{R_2 + 10\,k\Omega}$$ $$R_2 + 10 = 30 \implies R_2 = 20\,k\Omega$$ **Step 4 — Lower Threshold Calculation (1 Mark):** $$V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat} = -\frac{1}{3} \times 12 = -4\,V$$ **Step 5 — Hysteresis Voltage (1 Mark):** $$V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat} = 2 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = 8\,V$$ **Step 6 — Final Design Summary (1 Mark):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | $R_2$ | $20\,k\Omega$ | | $R_3$ | $10\,k\Omega$ | | $\beta$ | $1/3$ | | $V_{UT}$ | $+4\,V$ | | $V_{LT}$ | $-4\,V$ | | $V_H$ | $8\,V$ | **Step 7 — Hysteresis Loop Diagram (1 Mark):** ``` V_o | +12V----->(1)----+ | | | | (Path A: V_in increasing) | | 0 +-----+----+-+ -4V 0 +4V V_in 0 +-----+----+-+ | | | | (Path B: V_in decreasing) | | -12V-----+------(2) ``` --- ## **Question B (14 Marks)** ### Part (a) — 7 Marks > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]** *Derive the expressions for the **upper and lower threshold voltages** of an inverting Schmitt trigger using an op-amp. Show that the hysteresis voltage is given by $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ where $\beta$ is the feedback fraction.* **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Circuit Setup (1 Mark):** Consider the standard inverting Schmitt trigger with: - $V_{in}$ at inverting terminal - $R_2$ from $V_o$ to non-inverting terminal - $R_3$ from non-inverting terminal to ground - Op-amp powered by $\pm V_{CC}$ with saturation $\pm V_{sat}$ **Step 2 — Apply Voltage Divider at $V_+$ (1 Mark):** Since the op-amp draws no input current: $$V_+ = V_o \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \beta \cdot V_o$$ where the feedback fraction is: $$\beta = \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$$ **Step 3 — Switching Condition (1 Mark):** The output changes state when $V_+ = V_-$. Since $V_- = V_{in}$ (no current at the inverting input): $$\beta \cdot V_o = V_{in}$$ **Step 4 — Upper Threshold Derivation (2 Marks):** Assume the previous output state is $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (HIGH). The output will switch LOW only when $V_{in}$ rises above $V_+$: $$V_{in} = \beta \cdot V_o = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat})$$ $$\boxed{V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}}$$ **Step 5 — Lower Threshold Derivation (1 Mark):** Assume the previous output state is $V_o = -V_{sat}$ (LOW). The output will switch HIGH only when $V_{in}$ falls below $V_+$: $$V_{in} = \beta \cdot V_o = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat})$$ $$\boxed{V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}}$$ **Step 6 — Hysteresis Voltage (1 Mark):** $$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \beta V_{sat} - (-\beta V_{sat}) = 2\beta V_{sat}$$ $$\boxed{V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}}$$ --- ### Part (b) — 7 Marks > *With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **window comparator**. Draw its transfer characteristic. Mention two applications.* **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Concept and Circuit (2 Marks):** A window comparator detects whether the input voltage $V_{in}$ lies within a defined voltage "window" bounded by an upper threshold $V_{UT}$ and a lower threshold $V_{LT}$. It uses two op-amp comparators: ``` V_UT (e.g., +4V) | +----+----+ | | V_in ---+-----|+\ | | | >------+ | |-/ | | | | | +---------+ | | | | |+\ | | | >------+ | |-/ | | | | +---------+ | V_LT (e.g., +1V) | GND ``` Comparator 1: $V_{ref,1} = V_{UT}$ at $V_+$, $V_{in}$ at $V_-$ Comparator 2: $V_{in}$ at $V_+$, $V_{ref,2} = V_{LT}$ at $V_-$ **Step 2 — Operation Logic (2 Marks):** - **When $V_{in} > V_{UT}$:** Comparator 1 output is HIGH - **When $V_{in} < V_{LT}$:** Comparator 2 output is HIGH - **When $V_{LT} < V_{in} < V_{UT}$:** Both comparators are in the appropriate state, and the AND gate output is HIGH (window active) The output is HIGH **only when $V_{in}$ is inside the window**. **Step 3 — Transfer Characteristic (2 Marks):** ``` V_o | 1 +-----+----------+----- (Logic HIGH inside window) | | | | | | | | | 0 +-----+----------+----- (Logic LOW outside window) | V_LT V_UT V_in | | | ``` **Step 4 — Applications (1 Mark):** 1. **Battery Voltage Monitoring:** Detect if battery voltage lies within a safe operating range 2. **Signal Level Detection in Instrumentation:** Verify analog signals are within ADC input range --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Inverting vs. Non-Inverting Comparator:** Students frequently mix up which input terminal carries $V_{in}$ and which carries $V_{ref}$. Remember: > - **Inverting comparator** → $V_{in}$ at $V_+$ terminal (input side); output switches to $-V_{sat}$ when input exceeds reference > - **Non-inverting comparator** → $V_{in}$ at $V_-$ terminal; output switches to $+V_{sat}$ when input is below reference > > 2. **Sign Error in Hysteresis Calculation:** $V_H$ is always **positive** and equal to $V_{UT} - V_{LT}$. Do not write $V_H = \beta V_{sat}$ (this is only $V_{UT}$). > > 3. **Forgetting to Specify Saturation Voltages:** Always state $V_{sat}$ values in numerical problems — examiners award a mark for this. > > 4. **Missing the Hysteresis Loop Direction:** When drawing the transfer characteristic, clearly indicate the direction of traversal (clockwise for the hysteresis loop). A simple vertical line at $V_{th}$ is **wrong** for a Schmitt trigger. > > 5. **Units and Significant Figures:** Always include units (V, kΩ) in final numerical answers. > > 6. **Confusion with Astable Multivibrator:** A Schmitt trigger oscillator is built using a Schmitt trigger + RC network, but they are not the same. Do not interchange their circuit diagrams. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Comparator (Module 3)** - [ ] A **comparator** operates an op-amp in **open-loop** (no negative feedback) mode. - [ ] The output of a comparator is always **saturated** at $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$ — never in the linear region. - [ ] The **threshold voltage** of a basic comparator is $V_{th} = V_{ref}$. - [ ] In an **inverting comparator**, $V_{in} > V_{ref}$ gives $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (when $V_{in}$ is at $V_+$). - [ ] In a **non-inverting comparator**, $V_{in} < V_{ref}$ gives $V_o = +V_{sat}$ (when $V_{in}$ is at $V_-$). - [ ] A **zero-crossing detector** is a comparator with $V_{ref} = 0\,V$. - [ ] A **Schmitt trigger** uses **positive feedback** to introduce **hysteresis**. - [ ] **Feedback fraction:** $\beta = R_3 / (R_2 + R_3)$ (for inverting Schmitt trigger). - [ ] **Upper threshold:** $V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ - [ ] **Lower threshold:** $V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ - [ ] **Hysteresis width:** $V_H = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ - [ ] **Hysteresis** eliminates false triggering due to **noise** in the input signal. - [ ] A **window comparator** uses two comparators to detect if $V_{in}$ lies within $[V_{LT}, V_{UT}]$. - [ ] Common applications: **ADC front-ends, zero-crossing detectors, Schmitt triggers, signal squaring, level detectors, oscillators.** - [ ] Standard $V_{sat}$ values: $\pm 12\,V$ or $\pm 13\,V$ or $\pm 14\,V$ (depending on op-amp and supply). - [ ] For design problems: always state the chosen $R_3$ (typically $10\,k\Omega$) and compute $R_2$. > [!NOTE] > **End of Module 3 Notes — Comparator** > *These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBMRT304 - Analog Electronics. Practice the worked derivations and design problems to ensure full marks in the End Semester Examination.* ---

topics.title: Schmitt Trigger - S/H - V to I & I to V Converter - Instrumentation Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Schmitt Trigger - S/H - V to I & I to V Converter - Instrumentation Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Specialized Analog Building Blocks

Welcome to one of the most practically relevant modules in your Analog Electronics syllabus. The circuits covered here — **Schmitt Triggers**, **Sample-and-Hold amplifiers**, **Voltage-to-Current (V-I) / Current-to-Voltage (I-V) Converters**, and the **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)** — form the silent backbone of nearly every signal-conditioning front-end you will ever design, whether it is inside a biomedical ECG machine, an industrial process controller, or the ADC input stage of a microcontroller board.

Let us build intuition before we dive into the mathematics.

## 1.1 Schmitt Trigger

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **Schmitt Trigger** is a comparator-based regenerative circuit with **positive feedback** that converts a slowly varying or noisy input signal into a clean, sharply defined two-level (binary) output. Because of the regenerative feedback, the switching thresholds (called **Upper Threshold Voltage, $V_{UT}$** and **Lower Threshold Voltage, $V_{LT}$**) are *different* for the rising and falling edges of the input — a property known as **Hysteresis**.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a light switch that is "hesitant" — a regular switch might flicker on and off when your hand hovers around the middle position. A Schmitt trigger is like a switch with a *deliberate dead zone*. You have to push the lever well past the center to turn the light ON, and you have to pull it well past the center (in the other direction) to turn it OFF. This deliberate gap in the "switching zone" is called **hysteresis** and it kills noise.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- The difference $V_{UT} - V_{LT}$ is called the **Hysteresis Width**, $V_H$.
- Saturation voltages of the op-amp: typically $\pm V_{sat} \approx \pm \mathbf{13}\,\text{V}$ for a $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supply op-amp, or $\pm \mathbf{12}\,\text{V}$ in practice.
- Threshold ratio $\beta$ is a *dimensionless feedback fraction* ($0 < \beta < 1$).

## 1.2 Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit is an analog memory element that captures (samples) the instantaneous amplitude of a continuously varying input signal during a defined sampling interval and then *holds* that captured value constant at its output for the remainder of the sampling period, allowing downstream circuits (especially ADCs) sufficient time to perform an accurate conversion.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **camera with a very fast shutter** taking a snapshot of a moving scene — the "sample" is the click, the "hold" is the photograph itself. The picture stays still long enough for you to examine it carefully.

## 1.3 V-to-I and I-to-V Converters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **V-to-I Converter (Voltage-Controlled Current Source, VCCS):** A circuit that accepts an input voltage $V_{in}$ and forces a load current $I_L$ that is *linearly proportional* to $V_{in}$ and *independent* of the load resistance $R_L$. Used to drive sensors over long cables without signal loss.
>
> **I-to-V Converter (Transimpedance Amplifier, TIA):** A circuit that accepts an input current $I_{in}$ and converts it to a proportional output voltage $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$. Used as the front-end of photodiode amplifiers, current-output DACs, and many sensors.

## 1.4 Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:**
> An **Instrumentation Amplifier** is a precision, closed-loop, differential amplifier that provides a *gain adjustable through a single external resistor*, with extremely high input impedance on both inputs, very high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (**CMRR**), and very low DC offset and drift. It is the workhorse of biomedical and industrial instrumentation.

### Conceptual Analogy
A standard difference amplifier is like a **basic kitchen scale** — it works but is sensitive to where you place the object. An INA is like a **professional laboratory balance** that ignores the tray weight (common-mode), the slight tilt of the table (noise), and only reports the precise difference in mass (true signal).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Schmitt Trigger — Inverting Configuration

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology
The inverting Schmitt trigger uses an op-amp with the input applied to the **inverting (−) terminal** and a **positive feedback** network (a resistive divider $R_1$–$R_2$) feeding a fraction of the output back to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.

### 2.1.2 Operating Logic
- The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is:

$$V_{+} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

where $\beta$ is the **feedback factor**:

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

- The output saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$.
- The op-amp output switches state when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{+}$ (the threshold).

### 2.1.3 Threshold Voltages (Assuming $V_{out} = \pm V_{sat}$)

**Upper Threshold Voltage (input rising, output switches from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$):**

$$V_{UT} = +\beta \, V_{sat}$$

**Lower Threshold Voltage (input falling, output switches from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$):**

$$V_{LT} = -\beta \, V_{sat}$$

**Hysteresis Width:**

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta \, V_{sat}$$

### 2.1.4 The "Why" Behind the Hysteresis
Because $\beta$ is positive, the non-inverting input is *always dragged toward the output polarity*. The input must therefore rise *higher* to flip a positive output to negative (it has to overcome $+V_{+}$), and must fall *lower* to flip a negative output to positive. This is *exactly* the dead-zone behavior of our light-switch analogy.

## 2.2 Schmitt Trigger — Non-Inverting Configuration

Here the input is applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal** and the feedback is to the **inverting (−) terminal** through a divider. The threshold voltages become:

$$V_{UT} = \frac{R_f}{R_i} V_{sat} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{LT} = -\frac{R_f}{R_i} V_{sat}$$

These versions are less common in KTU problems but appear in waveform-shaping applications.

## 2.3 Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit

### 2.3.1 Building Blocks
1. **Analog Switch** (typically a JFET or MOSFET acting as a transmission gate) controlled by a digital control signal.
2. **Hold Capacitor** $C_H$ — stores the sampled voltage.
3. **Buffer Amplifier** (a high-input-impedance, low-output-impedance voltage follower) that presents the held voltage to the load *without discharging $C_H$*.

### 2.3.2 Two Operating Modes
- **Sample Mode** — Switch is **closed (ON)**. The capacitor $C_H$ charges rapidly to the instantaneous value of $V_{in}$ through the switch's low on-resistance $R_{on}$.
- **Hold Mode** — Switch is **open (OFF)**. The capacitor is *isolated* and ideally *retains* its voltage. The unity-gain buffer supplies this voltage to the load.

### 2.3.3 Key Performance Parameters
- **Acquisition Time, $t_{acq}$** — time for the capacitor to charge to within a specified error band (e.g., 0.1 %) of the final value.
- **Aperture Time / Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter)** — small delay between the hold command and the actual moment the switch opens.
- **Hold Droop** — the unwanted slow discharge of $C_H$ during hold mode, due to leakage currents.
- **Feedthrough** — capacitive coupling of input variations into the output during hold mode.

## 2.4 V-to-I Converter (Howland & Floating-Load Variants)

### 2.4.1 Floating-Load V-to-I Converter (Most Common KTU Form)
Using a single op-amp, the load $R_L$ is placed in the feedback loop. The analysis gives:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{in}}{R_s}$$

This current is **independent of $R_L$** as long as the op-amp remains in its linear region (output not saturated).

### 2.4.2 Grounded-Load V-to-I Converter (Improved Howland)
The basic Howland current pump uses an op-amp with both inverting and non-inverting paths matched through a resistor network. For matched resistors ($R_1 = R_3$, $R_2 = R_4$):

$$I_L = \frac{V_{in}}{R_s}$$

A grounded-load version uses an additional op-amp to invert $V_{in}$, allowing the load to be referenced to ground.

## 2.5 I-to-V Converter (Transimpedance Amplifier)

A single op-amp with the input current source connected to the inverting node and a feedback resistor $R_f$ from output to the inverting node:

$$V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$$

This is the **standard transimpedance configuration** used universally in photodiode amplifiers, photomultiplier tubes, and current-output sensors. The non-inverting input is grounded, and the inverting input is held at **virtual ground**, ensuring zero voltage across the source.

## 2.6 Instrumentation Amplifier (3-Op-Amp INA)

### 2.6.1 Topology
Two input-buffer op-amps with a gain-setting resistor $R_G$ between them, feeding a unity-gain difference amplifier as the output stage.

### 2.6.2 Working Through the Stages
- **Stage 1 (Buffer pair):** Each op-amp output equals its inverting input. A current $I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$ flows through $R_G$. This same current flows through the two feedback resistors $R_f$, producing:
  - $V_{o1} = V_1 + I R_f = V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$
  - $V_{o2} = V_2 - I R_f = V_2 - \frac{R_f}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$

- **Stage 2 (Difference amp):** The standard difference amplifier (with $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4$) gives:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

### 2.6.3 Defining Characteristics
- **Differential Gain:** $A_d = 1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}$
- **CMRR:** Extremely high (typically $\mathbf{100\,dB}$ or more) because the input buffers present matched impedances to both inputs and the difference amplifier has a precisely matched resistor network.
- **Input Impedance:** Essentially infinite at both $V_1$ and $V_2$ (no current flows into op-amp inputs).
- **Gain Adjustment:** A *single* resistor $R_G$ adjusts the gain — without disturbing the carefully balanced input symmetry.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> INAs are the *first stage* in virtually every medical ECG/EEG/EMG machine, strain-gauge weighing scales, pressure transducer signal conditioning, and thermocouple amplifier. They are also available as monolithic ICs (e.g., INA128, AD620, AD8421) and account for a multi-hundred-million-dollar global market.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feedback factor (Inverting Schmitt) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless, $0 < \beta < 1$ |
| 2 | Upper Threshold Voltage | $V_{UT} = +\beta \, V_{sat}$ | Volts |
| 3 | Lower Threshold Voltage | $V_{LT} = -\beta \, V_{sat}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Hysteresis Width | $V_H = 2\beta \, V_{sat}$ | Volts |
| 5 | S/H Acquisition Time | $t_{acq} \approx (R_{on}) C_H \cdot \ln(\text{resolution})$ | Seconds |
| 6 | S/H Hold Droop Rate | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{I_{leak}}{C_H}$ | V/s |
| 7 | V-to-I Output Current (floating) | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{in}}{R_s}$ | Amps |
| 8 | I-to-V Output Voltage (TIA) | $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$ | Volts |
| 9 | INA Differential Gain | $A_d = 1 + \dfrac{2R_f}{R_G}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | INA CMRR (ideal) | $\text{CMRR} = \infty$ when resistors perfectly matched | dB |
| 11 | Non-Inv. Schmitt thresholds | $V_{T} = \pm \dfrac{R_f}{R_i} V_{sat}$ | Volts |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Inverting Schmitt Trigger Thresholds

We will derive $V_{UT}$, $V_{LT}$, and $V_H$ from first principles, *without* skipping any step.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Circuit
- Op-amp supply rails: $\pm V_{CC}$, output saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$.
- Input $V_{in}$ enters the **inverting (−) terminal**.
- $R_1$ connects $V_{out}$ to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.
- $R_2$ connects the **non-inverting (+) terminal** to **ground**.

### 3.1.2 Voltage at the Non-Inverting Terminal
The non-inverting terminal sees a simple resistive divider. Since *no current flows into the op-amp input* (ideal op-amp assumption), $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a voltage divider between $V_{out}$ and ground:

$$V_{+} = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

> **Note on notation:** The KTU textbook writes the feedback factor as $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ when the divider is taken between output and ground with the tap going to $V_{+}$. Both notations are equivalent if you stay consistent. We will use the textbook convention $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$, giving $V_{+} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$ when $R_2$ is the resistor to ground and $R_1$ is from $V_{out}$ to $V_{+}$. *(This is the KTU convention used in the standard reference.)*

### 3.1.3 The Switching Criterion
The op-amp output $V_{out}$ depends on the sign of $(V_{+} - V_{in})$:
- If $V_{in} > V_{+}$ → output swings to $-V_{sat}$
- If $V_{in} < V_{+}$ → output swings to $+V_{sat}$

The output *switches state* exactly when $V_{in} = V_{+}$ (the comparison point).

### 3.1.4 Deriving $V_{UT}$
Assume the output is currently at $+V_{sat}$ (which means $V_{in}$ has been *below* $V_{+}$). As $V_{in}$ rises and crosses $V_{+} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat})$, the output flips to $-V_{sat}$. The threshold at which this happens is:

$$\boxed{V_{UT} = +\beta \, V_{sat}}$$

### 3.1.5 Deriving $V_{LT}$
Now assume the output is at $-V_{sat}$. As $V_{in}$ falls and crosses $V_{+} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat})$, the output flips back to $+V_{sat}$:

$$\boxed{V_{LT} = -\beta \, V_{sat}}$$

### 3.1.6 Deriving $V_H$

$$V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$$

$$V_H = (+\beta V_{sat}) - (-\beta V_{sat})$$

$$\boxed{V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}}$$

### 3.1.7 Worked Numerical Example
Suppose $R_1 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 90\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{sat} = 13\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1:** Compute $\beta$.

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10}{10 + 90} = 0.1$$

**Step 2:** Compute $V_{UT}$.

$$V_{UT} = 0.1 \times 13 = 1.3\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $V_{LT}$.

$$V_{LT} = -0.1 \times 13 = -1.3\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Compute $V_H$.

$$V_H = 2 \times 0.1 \times 13 = 2.6\,\text{V}$$

> A noise spike of magnitude less than $\pm 1.3\,\text{V}$ superimposed on a slowly varying input will *not* cause unwanted switching. The circuit is now **noise-immune up to 1.3 V of input noise**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Instrumentation Amplifier Gain

### 3.2.1 Stage 1 Analysis
Let the two input voltages be $V_1$ (at the non-inverting input of $A_1$) and $V_2$ (at the non-inverting input of $A_2$). Each op-amp is in a non-inverting configuration.

**Key observation:** The voltage across $R_G$ is exactly $(V_1 - V_2)$ because both op-amp inputs are at $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively (virtual short) and $R_G$ is connected between the two inverting inputs.

**Step 1:** Current through $R_G$:

$$I = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_G}$$

**Step 2:** The two feedback resistors (one at each op-amp, both labeled $R_f$) carry the *same* current $I$ (Kirchhoff's current law at the inverting nodes, since op-amp inputs draw no current).

**Step 3:** Output of $A_1$:

$$V_{o1} = V_1 + I R_f = V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 4:** Output of $A_2$:

$$V_{o2} = V_2 - I R_f = V_2 - \frac{R_f}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Step 5:** Difference (input to Stage 2):

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}(V_1 - V_2)$$

$$V_{o1} - V_{o2} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

### 3.2.2 Stage 2 Analysis (Unity-Gain Difference Amplifier)
The Stage 2 difference amplifier with $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$ gives:

$$V_{out} = \frac{R_4}{R_3}\left(V_{o1} - V_{o2}\right) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1}$$

For matched resistors ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4$):

$$V_{out} = V_{o1} - V_{o2}$$

### 3.2.3 Final INA Equation

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)}$$

### 3.2.4 Numerical Example
Given $R_f = 25\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_1 = 2.05\,\text{V}$, $V_2 = 2.00\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1:** Compute gain.

$$A_d = 1 + \frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{5{,}000} = 1 + 10 = 11$$

**Step 2:** Compute input difference.

$$V_1 - V_2 = 0.05\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3:** Compute output.

$$V_{out} = 11 \times 0.05 = 0.55\,\text{V}$$

> A tiny **50 mV differential signal** sitting on top of a **2 V common-mode** is faithfully amplified to **550 mV**, and the **2 V common-mode is completely rejected** (in the ideal case).

## 3.3 V-to-I Converter — Floating-Load Derivation

Consider a single op-amp with $V_{in}$ at the non-inverting input, $R_s$ between $V_{in}$ and the inverting input, and load $R_L$ between the op-amp output and the inverting input.

**Step 1:** Virtual short: $V_{-} = V_{+} = V_{in}$.

**Step 2:** Current through $R_s$:

$$I_{R_s} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{-}}{R_s} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{in}}{R_s} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the *only* path for current at the inverting node is through $R_L$. By KCL, this current equals the load current $I_L$:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{out} - V_{-}}{R_L} = \frac{V_{out} - V_{in}}{R_L}$$

**Step 4:** The op-amp adjusts $V_{out}$ such that $V_{-} = V_{in}$. The feedback forces $V_{out}$ to whatever value is needed:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} + I_L R_L$$

**Step 5:** But this seems to suggest $I_L$ depends on $R_L$. The *key insight* is that the op-amp must generate exactly the right $V_{out}$ to push $I_L$ through $R_L$, and that $I_L$ is in fact determined by the *input* source, not by $R_L$:

$$\boxed{I_L = \frac{V_{in}}{R_s}}$$

> The load resistance $R_L$ drops out of the final expression — the load current is **solely** a function of $V_{in}$ and $R_s$. The op-amp automatically increases $V_{out}$ to compensate for any increase in $R_L$.

## 3.4 I-to-V Converter — Transimpedance Amplifier Derivation

A current source $I_{in}$ is injected into the inverting node, with $R_f$ from the output back to the inverting node, and the non-inverting input grounded.

**Step 1:** Virtual ground: $V_{-} = V_{+} = 0\,\text{V}$.

**Step 2:** The current $I_{in}$ cannot flow into the op-amp (input impedance is infinite), so *all* of it must flow through $R_f$:

$$I_{R_f} = I_{in}$$

**Step 3:** Voltage across $R_f$ (from inverting node to output):

$$V_{R_f} = V_{-} - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$$

**Step 4:** By Ohm's law on $R_f$:

$$I_{R_f} = \frac{V_{-} - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 5:** Equate and solve:

$$I_{in} = \frac{-V_{out}}{R_f}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f}$$

> The output voltage is **negative** when $I_{in}$ is positive (conventional current flowing *into* the inverting node). This sign convention is critical for photodiode circuits where the photocurrent flows in a specific direction.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Inverting Schmitt Trigger — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below captures the signal flow, the positive feedback path, and the regeneration mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["V_in"] -->|"to inverting input"| nodeA["Op-Amp A1"]
    node2["R2 to GND"] --> nodeB["V_plus node"]
    node3["R1 from V_out"] --> nodeB["V_plus node"]
    nodeB -->|"positive feedback"| nodeA
    nodeA -->|"V_out = plus or minus V_sat"| node4["Comparator Output"]
    node4 -->|"two-level binary"| node5["Noise-free Square Wave"]
    node6["Beta = R1 over R1 plus R2"] -.-> nodeB
    node7["V_UT = plus Beta V_sat"] -.-> nodeA
    node8["V_LT = minus Beta V_sat"] -.-> nodeA
    node9["V_H = 2 Beta V_sat"] -.-> nodeA
```

> **How to read this:** The input $V_{in}$ is compared against $V_{+}$ (the threshold). $V_{+}$ is *not* constant — it is a fraction $\beta$ of the *output*. This dependency of the threshold on the output is the **physical embodiment of hysteresis**.

## 4.2 Sample-and-Hold Circuit — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["V_in analog input"] --> node2["Analog Switch S1 MOSFET or JFET"]
    node3["Control signal sample or hold"] --> node2
    node2 -->|"sample mode: switch closed"| node4["Hold Capacitor C_H"]
    node4 -->|"high Z node"| node5["Buffer Amplifier A2 voltage follower"]
    node5 -->|"low output impedance"| node6["V_out to ADC or next stage"]
    node7["Leakage currents I_leak"] -.->|"cause droop"| node4
    node8["R_on of switch"] -.->|"limits charging speed"| node2
    node9["t_acq and aperture time"] -.->|"performance metrics"| node2
```

> **How to read this:** $C_H$ is the analog "memory." The buffer's *only* job is to deliver the capacitor's voltage to the load without draining the capacitor.

## 4.3 Instrumentation Amplifier (3-Op-Amp Topology)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["V_1 input"] -->|"non-inv input"| nodeA["A1 input buffer"]
    node2["V_2 input"] -->|"non-inv input"| nodeB["A2 input buffer"]
    node3["R_G gain resistor"] -->|"sets differential gain"| nodeA
    node3 -->|"sets differential gain"| nodeB
    nodeA --> node4["V_o1 output of A1"]
    nodeB --> node5["V_o2 output of A2"]
    node4 -->|"non-inv input of A3"| nodeC["A3 difference amplifier"]
    node5 -->|"inv input of A3 via R1"| nodeC
    nodeC -->|"R2 and R4 matched"| node6["V_out differential output"]
    node7["A_d = 1 plus 2 R_f over R_G"] -.-> nodeC
    node8["CMRR very high matched resistors"] -.-> nodeC
    node9["Infinite input impedance"] -.-> node1
    node9 -.-> node2
```

> **How to read this:** Notice that the gain is *tuned by a single resistor $R_G$* placed between the two buffers, *not* at the output stage. This preserves the symmetry of the input impedance, which is the very feature that gives the INA its superb CMRR.

## 4.4 V-to-I and I-to-V Block Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["V_in source"] --> node2["Op-Amp with R_s and floating R_L"]
    node2 -->|"feedback forces I_L = V_in over R_s"| node3["Load current I_L"]
    node4["Photodiode or current source"] --> node5["TIA with R_f feedback"]
    node5 -->|"V_out = minus I_in R_f"| node6["Output voltage to ADC"]
    node7["V_in drives current through load"] -.-> node2
    node8["Virtual ground at inv input"] -.-> node5
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Schmitt trigger problems, **forgetting the sign of $V_{sat}$** in $V_{LT}$ is the most common mistake. $V_{LT}$ is *negative* for an inverting Schmitt — students frequently write $V_{LT} = +\beta V_{sat}$, costing **2 of 7 marks**.
> - For the INA, students often write $A_d = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_G}$ (missing the factor of 2). The correct expression is $1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}$. This is a **favourite 2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> - In the V-to-I problem, examiners check whether the student accounted for the **direction of current** and the **floating vs grounded load** distinction.
> - In the I-to-V / TIA problem, the **negative sign** in $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$ is mandatory. Omitting it loses a mark.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Schmitt trigger. Explain the concept of hysteresis with respect to the inverting Schmitt trigger circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Schmitt trigger is a comparator circuit with **positive feedback** that converts a slowly varying input into a sharply defined two-level output. The output switches state at two *different* input threshold voltages depending on the direction of the input.

For the inverting Schmitt trigger with feedback factor $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$:
- When $V_{in}$ rises through $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$, the output switches from $+V_{sat}$ to $-V_{sat}$.
- When $V_{in}$ falls through $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$, the output switches from $-V_{sat}$ to $+V_{sat}$.

The difference $V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2\beta V_{sat}$ is called the **hysteresis width**. Hysteresis makes the circuit immune to noise: any input fluctuation smaller than $V_H/2$ around a threshold will not cause false switching.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for threshold equations, 1 mark for explaining noise immunity.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the four key functional blocks of a Sample-and-Hold circuit and state the role of the hold capacitor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four key functional blocks are:
1. **Analog Switch** (JFET/MOSFET) — connects or disconnects the input from the capacitor under digital control.
2. **Hold Capacitor $C_H$** — stores the sampled input voltage as charge $Q = C_H V_{in}$ during the hold mode.
3. **Buffer Amplifier** (voltage follower) — presents a high input impedance (to prevent $C_H$ from discharging) and a low output impedance (to drive the load).
4. **Control Logic** — generates the sample/hold timing signal.

**Role of $C_H$:** It acts as the *analog memory* element. During sample mode, it charges to $V_{in}$. During hold mode, it retains that charge and hence the voltage, allowing the downstream ADC time to perform a stable conversion.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark per correctly named block with role, 1 mark reserved for the explicit role of $C_H$.)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** — Internal Choice Group

#### Part (a) — Schmitt Trigger Design and Waveform Analysis (7 Marks)

**Q.** Design an inverting Schmitt trigger using an op-amp with saturation voltages $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ such that the upper threshold is $+4\,\text{V}$ and the lower threshold is $-4\,\text{V}$.
(i) Calculate the required feedback factor $\beta$ and the hysteresis width $V_H$.
(ii) Choose suitable values for $R_1$ and $R_2$ if $R_1 + R_2 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$.
(iii) Draw the transfer characteristic (output vs input).

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Feedback factor and hysteresis:**

We need $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat} = +4\,\text{V}$ and $V_{LT} = -4\,\text{V}$ with $V_{sat} = 12\,\text{V}$.

$$\beta = \frac{V_{UT}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$V_H = 2\beta V_{sat} = 2 \times \frac{1}{3} \times 12 = 8\,\text{V}$$

**(ii) Resistor values:**

We have two equations:

$$\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{and} \quad R_1 + R_2 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$$

From the first equation, $3R_1 = R_1 + R_2 \Rightarrow 2R_1 = R_2$.

Substituting:

$$R_1 + 2R_1 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$3R_1 = 100\,\text{k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_1 = 33.33\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = 66.67\,\text{k}\Omega$$

> *(In practice, use the nearest standard E12 values: $R_1 = 33\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 68\,\text{k}\Omega$.)*

**(iii) Transfer characteristic:**

The plot is a **rectangular hysteresis loop**:
- For $V_{in} < -4\,\text{V}$: $V_{out} = +12\,\text{V}$
- For $-4\,\text{V} < V_{in} < +4\,\text{V}$: $V_{out}$ holds its previous state (horizontal line — this is the *dead zone*).
- For $V_{in} > +4\,\text{V}$: $V_{out} = -12\,\text{V}$

The arrows on the loop indicate direction of traversal during rising and falling input sweeps.

**Mark Split:** (i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — I-to-V and V-to-I Converter Analysis (7 Marks)

**Q.** (i) Derive the output voltage of a transimpedance amplifier (I-to-V converter) with feedback resistor $R_f = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$ when the input current is $I_{in} = 25\,\mu\text{A}$. (ii) For a floating-load V-to-I converter, find the load current when $V_{in} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $R_s = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$. Verify the result is independent of $R_L = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) I-to-V Converter:**

The standard transimpedance equation is $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$.

$$V_{out} = -25 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{3}$$

$$V_{out} = -2.5\,\text{V}$$

> The negative sign indicates that a *positive* current flowing *into* the inverting node produces a *negative* output voltage.

**(ii) V-to-I Converter:**

The output current is given by:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{in}}{R_s} = \frac{5}{2.2 \times 10^3} = 2.273\,\text{mA}$$

**Verification for $R_L = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$:** The op-amp output must satisfy $V_{out} = V_{in} + I_L R_L = 5 + 2.273 = 7.27\,\text{V}$. Since this is within the supply rails ($\pm 15\,\text{V}$), the op-amp is in its linear region and $I_L$ remains exactly $2.273\,\text{mA}$.

**Verification for $R_L = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$:** The required op-amp output is $V_{out} = 5 + 2.273 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.7 \times 10^{3} = 5 + 10.68 = 15.68\,\text{V}$. This is just slightly *above* the typical saturation of $\pm 13\,\text{V}$ — so in practice, the load current will *not* remain exactly $2.273\,\text{mA}$ once the op-amp saturates.

> **Key insight:** The equation $I_L = V_{in}/R_s$ is valid only as long as the op-amp remains in its linear (unsaturated) region. For larger $R_L$, the op-amp saturates and $I_L$ becomes a function of $R_L$ again.

**Mark Split:** (i) 3 marks (2 for formula, 1 for numerical substitution), (ii) 4 marks (2 for derivation, 2 for verification with both $R_L$ values and discussion of saturation).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** — Internal Choice Group

#### Part (a) — Instrumentation Amplifier Design (7 Marks)
