**Q.** (i) Draw the circuit diagram of a 3-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. (ii) Derive the expression for the output voltage in terms of $V_1$, $V_2$, and the resistor values. (iii) Design the INA for a gain of 50 using $R_f = 49\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Circuit Diagram:**

Refer to **Section 4.3** above. The two input op-amps (A1, A2) form a buffered differential pair with $R_G$ between their inverting inputs and feedback resistors $R_f$ at each output. The third op-amp (A3) is a unity-gain difference amplifier.

**(ii) Derivation:**

From our exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.2**:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G}\right)(V_1 - V_2)$$

**Re-statement of the derivation logic:**
- Both input op-amps are in non-inverting configuration.
- The current through $R_G$ is exactly $(V_1 - V_2)/R_G$ because the inputs are at virtual short potentials.
- This same current flows through both $R_f$ resistors (KCL at the inverting nodes).
- The differential signal at the output of Stage 1 is amplified by Stage 2.

**(iii) Design for $A_d = 50$:**

$$50 = 1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_G} = 1 + \frac{2 \times 49{,}000}{R_G}$$

$$49 = \frac{98{,}000}{R_G}$$

$$R_G = \frac{98{,}000}{49} = 2{,}000\,\Omega = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$$

> The designer can now adjust the INA's gain by changing *only* $R_G$, leaving the rest of the circuit (and hence the input impedance symmetry and CMRR) untouched.

**Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

#### Part (b) — Sample-and-Hold Application Problem (7 Marks)

**Q.** A Sample-and-Hold circuit uses a hold capacitor $C_H = 1\,\text{nF}$ and a MOSFET switch with $R_{on} = 100\,\Omega$. The op-amp buffer has an input bias current of $I_b = 50\,\text{pA}$.
(i) Calculate the acquisition time for 0.1 % accuracy.
(ii) Calculate the hold-mode droop rate in V/s.
(iii) How long can the circuit hold the sampled value before the droop exceeds 1 mV?

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Acquisition time for 0.1 % accuracy:**

The sample-mode charging follows:

$$V_C(t) = V_{in}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) \quad \text{with} \quad \tau = R_{on} C_H$$

For 0.1 % accuracy, we need $e^{-t/\tau} \le 0.001$, i.e., $t/\tau \ge \ln(1000)$.

$$\tau = 100 \times 1 \times 10^{-9} = 100\,\text{ns}$$

$$t_{acq} = \tau \cdot \ln(1000) = 100\,\text{ns} \times 6.908 = 690.8\,\text{ns} \approx 0.69\,\mu\text{s}$$

**(ii) Hold-mode droop rate:**

During hold mode, the dominant discharge path is through the buffer's input bias current:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{I_b}{C_H} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.05\,\text{V/s} = 50\,\text{mV/s}$$

**(iii) Time for 1 mV droop:**

$$t_{droop} = \frac{\Delta V}{(dV/dt)} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} = 0.02\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms}$$

> This 20 ms is a long time in the digital world — more than enough for a typical ADC to perform a 12-bit conversion in a few microseconds. The S/H is doing its job of "freezing" the analog signal long enough for conversion.

**Mark Split:** (i) 3 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Schmitt Trigger** uses **positive feedback** to create two distinct thresholds: $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$ for the inverting configuration.
- **Hysteresis Width** $V_H = 2\beta V_{sat}$ is the *dead zone* that kills noise.
- **Feedback factor** for an inverting Schmitt trigger is $\beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ (KTU textbook convention with $R_1$ from $V_{out}$ to $V_{+}$ and $R_2$ from $V_{+}$ to ground).
- A **Sample-and-Hold** has two modes: *Sample* (switch closed, $C_H$ charges) and *Hold* (switch open, $C_H$ retains charge). The buffer prevents $C_H$ from discharging.
- **Acquisition time** scales as $R_{on} C_H$ and is the bottleneck in high-speed ADCs.
- **Hold droop** $\propto I_{leak}/C_H$; larger $C_H$ gives slower droop but slower acquisition (design trade-off).
- **V-to-I (floating load)**: $I_L = V_{in}/R_s$, valid only when the op-amp remains in its linear region.
- **I-to-V (TIA)**: $V_{out} = -I_{in} R_f$. Used in photodiode front-ends, current-output DACs.
- **Instrumentation Amplifier (3-op-amp)**: $V_{out} = (1 + 2R_f / R_G)(V_1 - V_2)$. *Single-resistor gain control* with *infinite input impedance* and *very high CMRR* — the three killer features.
- The INA's output stage is a **unity-gain difference amplifier** that requires *precise resistor matching* for high CMRR.
- Standard monolithic INA ICs (INA128, AD620, AD8421) are pin-compatible drop-in upgrades for any discrete 3-op-amp INA design.
- For **practical exam** answers, *always* state the op-amp assumptions (ideal, infinite gain, infinite input impedance, zero output impedance) before starting the derivation.
- Common **marks-loss pitfalls**: wrong sign on $V_{LT}$, missing factor of 2 in INA gain, missing the negative sign in TIA, forgetting to verify the op-amp is not saturated in the V-to-I case.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Filters Using Op-Amp: Difference Between Active & Passive Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Filters Using Op-Amp: Difference Between Active & Passive Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Filter?

A **filter** in electronics is a frequency-selective two-port network designed to pass signals within a specified frequency band (the **pass band**) while attenuating signals outside that band (the **stop band**). Filters are fundamental building blocks in communication systems, signal processing, instrumentation, and control systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** This topic establishes the foundational distinction between **Passive Filters** (built from R, L, C only) and **Active Filters** (built using active devices like Op-Amps with R and C). Active filters are the modern standard in integrated analog design because they eliminate bulky inductors.

### 1.2 Definition of Passive Filters

A **passive filter** is a filter circuit constructed using only **passive components** — resistors (R), capacitors (C), and inductors (L) — along with the source and load. It does **not** require any external power supply for its operation and exhibits no gain (gain ≤ 1).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** "A passive filter is a frequency-selective network composed entirely of passive elements (R, L, C) that attenuates undesired frequency components of a signal by introducing insertion loss, with maximum available gain $\le 0$ dB."

### 1.3 Definition of Active Filters

An **active filter** is a filter circuit that uses **active components** (such as operational amplifiers, transistors, or dependent sources) in conjunction with passive components (R and C) to perform the filtering action. It requires an **external DC power supply** for operation and can provide **voltage gain greater than unity** (Gain > 1).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** "An active filter is a frequency-selective network employing an active device (typically an op-amp) together with resistors and capacitors, providing gain, eliminating the need for inductors, and offering high input impedance with low output impedance."

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap with a mesh filter**:
- A **passive filter** is like a simple strainer that lets water through but blocks debris. It only **removes** what you don't want — it cannot *boost* the flow.
- An **active filter** is like a **smart pump-and-strainer system**. It not only blocks debris at different "speeds" (frequencies) but also uses electrical energy (from the pump/Op-Amp) to **amplify** the desired flow rate. The pump (op-amp) needs external power, just like an op-amp needs $\pm V_{CC}$.

Another analogy: Think of a **loudspeaker equalizer (EQ)** on a music system.
- **Bass, mid, treble knobs** are active filters — they don't just cut, they shape the sound using powered circuitry.
- A passive tone control is a simple **R-C network** that can only reduce volume at certain frequencies but never increase it.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Active Filters Dominate Modern Design:** Inductors (L) are bulky, heavy, expensive, and practically impossible to fabricate on integrated circuits (ICs). Op-amp-based active filters use only **resistors and capacitors**, which are IC-friendly, compact, and provide gain — making them the de-facto choice for modern VLSI and PCB design.

### 1.5 Classification of Filters (By Frequency Response)

| Filter Type | Symbol | Pass Band | Stop Band |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low Pass Filter (LPF) | $\beta(s)$ | $0$ to $f_H$ | $f > f_H$ |
| High Pass Filter (HPF) | $\alpha(s)$ | $f > f_L$ | $0$ to $f_L$ |
| Band Pass Filter (BPF) | $\beta\alpha$ | $f_L$ to $f_H$ | Outside the band |
| Band Stop / Notch Filter | $\alpha + \beta$ | Outside the band | $f_L$ to $f_H$ |
| All Pass Filter | — | All frequencies | None (only phase shift) |

Where:
- $f_H$ = **Upper cutoff frequency** (Hz)
- $f_L$ = **Lower cutoff frequency** (Hz)
- $s = j\omega$ is the **complex frequency variable**.

### 1.6 Standard Cutoff Frequency for First-Order Filters

The general cutoff (or **-3 dB frequency**) for any first-order filter is given by:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \;\; \text{(Hz)}$$

The gain magnitude at the cutoff is always:

$$\vert A \vert_{f=f_c} = \frac{A_{\max}}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot A_{\max}$$

which corresponds to the **-3 dB point** (since $20 \log_{10}(1/\sqrt{2}) \approx -3$ dB).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Passive Filters Fall Short

Passive filters suffer from several critical limitations, especially in modern analog design:

1. **No Gain:** They can only attenuate; they cannot amplify. Maximum gain $\le 1$ (0 dB).
2. **Loading Effect:** The output impedance of a passive filter is high and interacts with the load, shifting the cutoff frequency unpredictably.
3. **Bulky Inductors:** For low-frequency audio filters (e.g., $f_c = 100$ Hz), the required inductor value becomes impractically large. For instance, with $C = 1 \mu F$:
   $$L = \frac{1}{(2\pi f_c)^2 C} = \frac{1}{(2\pi \cdot 100)^2 \cdot 10^{-6}} \approx 2.53 \text{ H}$$
   This is a **huge inductor**, unsuitable for IC fabrication.
4. **Difficult to Cascade:** Loading effects compound when stages are connected in series.
5. **Quality Factor (Q) is Low:** Sharp roll-off is hard to achieve without sophisticated L-C ladders.

### 2.2 Why Active Filters Excel

Op-amp-based active filters overcome every limitation of passive filters:

1. **Gain Available:** The op-amp can provide controlled voltage gain (typically 1 to 100 or more).
2. **No Loading Effect:** High input impedance ($\sim 10^{12} \Omega$) and low output impedance ($\sim 0 \Omega$) provide **isolation** between source and load — the op-amp acts as a **buffer**.
3. **No Inductors:** Use only R and C, making them **IC-compatible** (inductor-free / "gyrator" replacements).
4. **Easy Cascading:** Each stage is isolated by the op-amp, so multi-stage designs are straightforward.
5. **Tunable Cutoff:** Cutoff frequency can be tuned by varying a single resistor.
6. **High Q-Factor:** Active filters (especially Sallen-Key and multiple-feedback topologies) can achieve high Q with sharp roll-off.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table.** Every formula here has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU University Exam papers. For absolute value in tables, $\vert x \vert$ notation is used to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutoff Frequency (1st order) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | $-3$ dB point |
| 2 | Angular Cutoff Frequency | $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s | $s$-domain pole location |
| 3 | Pass-band Gain (1st order LPF) | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | dimensionless | Non-inverting config |
| 4 | Pass-band Gain (Inverting) | $A_v = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Negative sign = phase inversion |
| 5 | Roll-off rate (1st order) | $-20$ dB/decade | dB/dec | $-6$ dB/octave |
| 6 | Roll-off rate (2nd order) | $-40$ dB/decade | dB/dec | $-12$ dB/octave |
| 7 | Magnitude at $f_c$ | $\vert A \vert = \dfrac{A_{\max}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | V/V | Always true for any order |
| 8 | $-3$ dB verification | $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \approx -3$ dB | dB | Confirms $f_c$ definition |
| 9 | Quality Factor (2nd order) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ | dimensionless | $\zeta$ = damping ratio |
| 10 | Sallen-Key DC Gain | $K = 1 + \dfrac{R_B}{R_A}$ | V/V | Standard configuration |
| 11 | Resonant Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ | rad/s | Sallen-Key / MFB |
| 12 | Maximum Gain (LPF) | $\vert A \vert_{\max} = K$ | V/V | DC value |
| 13 | Inductor-Replacement Gyrator | $L = C \cdot R^2$ | H | Simulated L using op-amp |
| 14 | Power Dissipated (Passive) | $P = I^2 R$ | W | Loss = insertion loss |
| 15 | Active Filter Power | $P = V_{CC} \cdot I_{Q}$ | W | Quiescent current draw |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Systems:** Active crossovers in loudspeakers (subwoofer/woofer/tweeter splits) use active filters because they are tunable and provide gain.
- **Biomedical (ECG/EEG):** 50/60 Hz **notch filters** remove power-line interference from physiological signals.
- **Telecommunications:** Channel-select bandpass filters in IF stages of radio receivers.
- **Control Systems:** Low-pass filters clean noise from sensor feedback (e.g., accelerometer signals in drones).
- **Instrumentation:** Anti-aliasing filters preceding ADC inputs in data acquisition systems.
- **Power Electronics:** Active harmonic filters (active power filters, APFs) suppress line harmonics in industrial drives.

### 2.5 Conceptual "Why & How" Logic

- **Why no inductors?** Inductors resist changes in current by storing energy in a magnetic field. Their equivalent impedance is $Z_L = j\omega L$. Op-amps can synthesize this impedance electronically using a **Generalized Impedance Converter (GIC)** or **Antoniou's Gyrator**, simulating an inductor using only capacitors, op-amps, and resistors.
- **How does the cutoff arise?** In an RC low-pass filter, the capacitor's impedance $Z_C = 1/(j\omega C)$ decreases as frequency increases, shunting high-frequency signals to ground. At $\omega = 1/(RC)$, the capacitor's impedance equals the resistor's impedance, marking the **transition point**.
- **Why op-amp + RC, not just RC?** Without the op-amp's buffering, the source and load resistances become part of the filter, shifting the cutoff. The op-amp's near-infinite input impedance and near-zero output impedance **decouple** the filter from external loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Why $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ for an RC Low-Pass Filter

Consider a simple series RC circuit with input $V_{in}$ across the series combination, and output $V_{out}$ taken across the capacitor.

**Step 1: Apply the voltage divider rule.**

The voltage across the capacitor is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{Z_R + Z_C}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the impedances.**

$Z_R = R$ and $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 3: Multiply numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$ to simplify.**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4: Identify the transfer function.**

$$\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

This is the standard form of a **first-order low-pass filter** transfer function.

**Step 5: Compute the magnitude of the transfer function.**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 6: At DC ($\omega = 0$):**

$$\vert H(0) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0}} = 1 \;\; \text{(unity gain, full pass)}$$

**Step 7: At very high frequency ($\omega \to \infty$):**

$$\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(\omega RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\omega RC} \to 0 \;\; \text{(full attenuation)}$$

**Step 8: Define the cutoff frequency as the -3 dB point.**

Set $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$\frac{1}{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2$$

$$(\omega_c RC)^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_c RC = 1$$

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \;\;\; \text{and} \;\;\; f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 9: Verify the phase at cutoff.**

The phase is given by $\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC)$. At $\omega = \omega_c$:

$$\phi(\omega_c) = -\arctan(1) = -45^{\circ}$$

This **$-45^{\circ}$ phase shift at the cutoff frequency** is a universal property of first-order RC filters.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Active First-Order Low-Pass Filter (Non-Inverting)

Consider the **non-inverting op-amp configuration** with a capacitor in parallel with the feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Step 1: Identify the input voltage to the op-amp's non-inverting terminal.**

The RC low-pass section delivers a voltage $V_+$:

$$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{Z_C}{R + Z_C} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2: Write the non-inverting op-amp gain formula.**

$$V_{out} = V_+ \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 3: Combine to obtain the complete transfer function.**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 4: Identify the key parameters.**

- **DC gain (pass-band gain):** $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ (independent of frequency, as long as $\omega \ll \omega_c$)
- **Cutoff frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ (unaffected by the op-amp gain!)
- **High-frequency behavior:** as $\omega \to \infty$, $V_{out} \to 0$.

**Step 5: Magnitude response.**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 6: Phase response.**

$$\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\omega RC)$$

**Step 7: Why this is "active" and not "passive"?**

The presence of $A_0 > 1$ in the numerator is what makes it active. A passive filter would have $A_0 = 1$ at best. The op-amp provides **controlled gain** while preserving the filter's frequency-selective behavior.

### 3.3 Comparison of Roll-Off: Why 2nd-Order Filters are Sharper

A first-order filter attenuates at **-20 dB/decade** beyond $f_c$. A second-order filter attenuates at **-40 dB/decade**.

For a second-order Butterworth (maximally flat) LPF, the transfer function is:

$$H(s) = \frac{K \cdot \omega_0^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}\,\omega_0 s + \omega_0^2}$$

The magnitude squared is:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert^2 = \frac{K^2 \omega_0^4}{\omega^4 + \omega_0^4}$$

At $\omega = 10\omega_0$ (one decade above cutoff):

- **1st order:** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{10} = -20$ dB
- **2nd order:** $\vert H \vert = \dfrac{1}{100} = -40$ dB

This **tenfold improvement in attenuation** is why high-order filters (cascaded stages or higher-order Sallen-Key designs) are used in practice.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Design Requirement | Recommended Filter | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Simple tone control, $\le 6$ dB roll-off | Passive RC | Low cost, no power needed |
| IC-compatible filter (no inductors) | Active RC (Op-Amp) | Gyrator / direct RC implementation |
| Need gain > 1 | Active Filter | Passive cannot amplify |
| High-frequency ($> 50$ MHz) | Passive LC or SAW | Op-amp bandwidth limited |
| Tunable cutoff | Active (OTA-C or varactor) | Voltage-controlled tuning possible |
| Very sharp roll-off ($> 60$ dB/dec) | Higher-order Active (Sallen-Key cascade) | Cascaded stages |
| Power line hum removal (50/60 Hz) | Active Notch (Twin-T) | High Q achievable |
| Biomedical signal conditioning | Active Bandpass (high input Z) | Patient safety via isolation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Filters

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Frequency Selective Network] --> B[Passive Filters]
    A --> C[Active Filters]
    A --> D[Digital Filters]
    B --> B1[RC Filter]
    B --> B2[RL Filter]
    B --> B3[RLC Filter]
    B --> B4[Lattice Filter]
    C --> C1[Active RC - Op-Amp Based]
    C --> C2[Switched Capacitor]
    C --> C3[OTA-C Filter]
    C --> C4[Gyrator-C Filter]
    C1 --> C1a[1st Order LPF / HPF]
    C1 --> C1b[2nd Order Sallen-Key]
    C1 --> C1c[Multiple Feedback MFB]
    C1 --> C1d[State Variable / Biquad]
    C1 --> C1e[Twin-T Notch]
    D --> D1[FIR Filter]
    D --> D2[IIR Filter]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Active vs Passive Filter — Signal Flow Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PassivePath[Passive Filter Path]
        P_In[Vin Source] --> P_R[R and L and C Network]
        P_R --> P_Out[Load RL]
        P_R -.-> P_Loss[Insertion Loss less than 0 dB]
    end
    subgraph ActivePath[Active Filter Path]
        A_In[Vin Source] --> A_Buffer[High Z Input]
        A_Buffer --> A_RC[RC Network]
        A_RC --> A_Amp[Op-Amp Active Stage]
        A_Amp --> A_LowZ[Low Z Output]
        A_LowZ --> A_Out[Load RL]
        A_Amp -.-> A_VCC[External DC Supply Vcc]
        A_Amp -.-> A_Gain[Voltage Gain greater than 1]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Op-Amp Active First-Order Low-Pass Filter (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Input Signal Vin] --> node2[Series Resistor R]
    node2 --> node3[Non Inverting Input of Op-Amp]
    node4[Capacitor C to Ground] -.-> node3
    node3 --> node5[Op-Amp OA]
    node6[DC Supply Plus Minus Vcc] -.-> node5
    node7[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> node5
    node8[Resistor R1 to Ground] --> node7
    node5 --> node9[Buffered Output Vout]
    node9 --> node10[Load RL]
    node5 -.-> node11[Gain Factor A equals 1 plus Rf by R1]
    node5 -.-> node12[Cutoff fc equals 1 over 2 pi RC]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Frequency Response Comparison (Magnitude Plot in dB)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Filter Magnitude Response] --> B[Low Pass]
    A --> C[High Pass]
    A --> D[Band Pass]
    A --> E[Band Stop Notch]
    B --> B1[Gain flat at K dB from 0 Hz to fc]
    B --> B2[Roll off at minus 20 dB per decade above fc]
    C --> C1[Gain rises at plus 20 dB per decade from fL]
    C --> C2[Gain flat at K dB above fL]
    D --> D1[Gain rises plus 20 dB per decade to fL]
    D --> D2[Gain flat at peak between fL and fH]
    D --> D3[Gain falls minus 20 dB per decade above fH]
    E --> E1[Gain flat at K dB]
    E --> E2[Sharp dip minus 40 dB at notch frequency f0]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram: Key Differences Summary (Architecture Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    K[Key Difference Axes] --> K1[Power Requirement]
    K[Key Difference Axes] --> K2[Voltage Gain]
    K[Key Difference Axes] --> K3[Components Used]
    K[Key Difference Axes] --> K4[Loading Effect]
    K[Key Difference Axes] --> K5[IC Compatibility]
    K[Key Difference Axes] --> K6[Cascadability]
    K1 --> K1a[Passive = No Power]
    K1 --> K1b[Active = Needs Vcc]
    K2 --> K2a[Passive = Gain less than 1]
    K2 --> K2b[Active = Gain greater than 1]
    K3 --> K3a[Passive = R L C]
    K3 --> K3b[Active = R C plus Op-Amp]
    K4 --> K4a[Passive = Severe]
    K4 --> K4b[Active = Negligible]
    K5 --> K5a[Passive = Inductors block IC]
    K5 --> K5b[Active = IC friendly]
    K6 --> K6a[Passive = Difficult]
    K6 --> K6b[Active = Easy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `K1a`, `node1`) and avoid reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without any bold, italics, or HTML tags — fully compliant with KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering rules.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Set A]**
**Define an active filter. List any three advantages of active filters over passive filters.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** An active filter is a frequency-selective network that uses an **active device** (typically an operational amplifier) along with **resistors and capacitors** to provide filtering action. It requires an **external DC power supply** and can provide voltage gain greater than unity.

> **Three Advantages:**
> 1. **Gain:** Active filters can provide voltage gain greater than 1, while passive filters can only attenuate.
> 2. **No Inductors Required:** They use only R and C, making them compact, low-cost, and IC-fabrication friendly.
> 3. **No Loading Effect:** The op-amp's high input impedance and low output impedance isolate source and load, so the filter response is unaffected by the connected circuitry.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Compare passive and active filters on the basis of (i) components used, (ii) gain, and (iii) power supply requirement.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Passive Filter | Active Filter |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Components | R, L, C only | R, C + Op-Amp (no L) |
| (ii) Gain | $\le 1$ (0 dB); attenuates only | $> 1$; can amplify |
| (iii) Power Supply | Not required | Requires DC supply $\pm V_{CC}$ |

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the transfer function of an **active first-order low-pass filter** using an op-amp. Hence obtain the expression for cutoff frequency and pass-band gain.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare active and passive filters in detail. Mention any **five differences** with justification.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Active First-Order LPF Derivation

**Circuit Description:**
The non-inverting op-amp active low-pass filter consists of:
- A resistor $R$ in series with the input signal $V_{in}$,
- A capacitor $C$ connected from the non-inverting (+) terminal to ground,
- A feedback network $R_f$ and $R_1$ from output to inverting (-) terminal.

**Step 1:** Voltage at the non-inverting terminal (output of RC voltage divider):

$$V_+ = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1/(j\omega C)}{R + 1/(j\omega C)} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2:** For a non-inverting op-amp configuration:

$$V_{out} = V_+ \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $V_+$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

where

$$A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \quad \text{(pass-band gain)}$$

**Step 4:** Converting to s-domain ($s = j\omega$):

$$H(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 5:** Cutoff frequency — when $\omega RC = 1$:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

**Step 6:** Verification of magnitude at $f = f_c$:

$$\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + 1}} = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \cdot A_0$$

This is the **$-3$ dB point**, confirming the cutoff.

**Step 7:** Phase at cutoff:

$$\phi(\omega_c) = -\arctan(\omega_c RC) = -\arctan(1) = -45^{\circ}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}}{1 + sRC}, \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}, \quad A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}$$

---

### Part (b) — Active vs Passive Filters: Five Detailed Differences

| # | Feature | Passive Filter | Active Filter | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Active Devices** | None (only R, L, C) | Op-Amp / Transistor | Active components enable gain and buffering |
| 2 | **Gain** | $\le 1$ (0 dB), attenuation only | Can be $> 1$ | Op-amp provides controlled amplification |
| 3 | **Power Supply** | Not required | Requires $\pm V_{CC}$ | Op-amp needs DC bias to function |
| 4 | **Inductors** | Often required for sharp roll-off | Not required (R-C only) | Smaller size, IC-compatible |
| 5 | **Loading Effect** | Significant — output depends on load | Negligible — buffered by op-amp | Op-amp provides impedance isolation |
| 6 | **Cascading** | Difficult — stages load each other | Easy — op-amp isolates stages | Modular design |
| 7 | **Tunability** | Hard to tune | Easy by varying R | Single-resistor tuning of $f_c$ |
| 8 | **Q-factor** | Limited by component losses | High Q achievable | Sharp roll-off possible |
| 9 | **Cost / Size** | Inductors increase cost & size | Compact, cheap | Modern preference for active |
| 10 | **High-frequency use** | Excellent (no op-amp BW limit) | Limited by op-amp GBW | Passive wins at RF/microwave |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Alert):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write the transfer function as $\frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$ for an active filter — but this is the **passive** filter equation! You **must multiply by $(1 + R_f/R_1)$** to account for the op-amp gain. **Lose 2 marks** if you forget this.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing $f_c$ with $\omega_c$. KTU expects both forms. Always write: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz and $\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$ rad/s.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In the comparison table, students write "passive filters use inductors" as a disadvantage — but it is actually a *limitation for IC design*, not a fault in itself. Frame it correctly.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not stating the units of $f_c$ (Hz) and $\omega_c$ (rad/s). Always specify units.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **disadvantages of passive filters** in detail. Why are **inductors avoided** in modern active filter design?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design a first-order active **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $f_c = 10$ kHz and pass-band gain $A_0 = 11$. Choose $C = 0.01\ \mu F$. Verify the design.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Disadvantages of Passive Filters

1. **No Gain Capability:** The maximum voltage gain of a passive filter is unity (0 dB). It can only attenuate; it cannot amplify signals. This is unacceptable in most signal-conditioning applications.

2. **Loading Effect:** The output impedance of a passive filter is comparable to the source impedance, so the filter's response changes when connected to a load. The actual cutoff frequency becomes:

   $$f_{c,\text{actual}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R \parallel R_L) C}$$

   which differs from the designed $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$. This makes the design **non-ideal** and **load-dependent**.

3. **Bulky Inductors:** Inductors required for low-frequency filters are large and heavy. Example: for $f_c = 100$ Hz with $C = 1\ \mu F$, $L \approx 2.53$ H — this is physically massive.

4. **Non-IC Compatible:** Inductors cannot be fabricated on silicon chips economically. Passive filters are **discrete-only**.

5. **Difficult to Cascade:** Each stage loads the previous one, distorting the cumulative frequency response. The transfer function of a multi-stage passive filter is not a simple product of individual stages.

6. **Low Q-Factor:** Passive RLC filters have Q limited by parasitic resistance in inductors and capacitors. Achieving sharp roll-off (high Q) requires expensive, precision components.

7. **No Tuning Capability:** Cutoff frequency is fixed by component values. Variable tuning is impractical.

> **Why Inductors are Avoided in Modern Design:**
> - Inductors are **bulky, heavy, and expensive**.
> - They **cannot be integrated on ICs** — this is the deal-breaker for modern VLSI.
> - They suffer from **parasitic resistance**, **EMI** (electromagnetic interference), and **core saturation** issues.
> - **Op-amp-based gyrators** can electronically simulate an inductor's behavior using only resistors, capacitors, and op-amps, achieving the same filtering action without physical inductors.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Design Problem

**Given:**
- $f_c = 10$ kHz
- $A_0 = 11$ (pass-band gain)
- $C = 0.01\ \mu F = 10^{-8}$ F

**Step 1: Calculate $R$ from cutoff frequency formula.**

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi}$$

$$R \approx 1591.55\ \Omega \approx 1.59\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 2: Choose standard value.** $R = 1.6\ \text{k}\Omega$ (E12 standard).

**Step 3: Calculate $R_f$ and $R_1$ for gain $A_0 = 11$.**

For non-inverting configuration:

$$A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 11$$

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 10$$

**Step 4: Choose $R_1$ and compute $R_f$.**

Choosing $R_1 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$R_f = 10 \times R_1 = 10 \times 10\ \text{k}\Omega = 100\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Verify the design.**

Recomputed $f_c$ with $R = 1.6\ \text{k}\Omega$:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 9.95\ \text{kHz}$$

This is **within 0.5%** of the target 10 kHz — acceptable.

**Recomputed gain:**

$$A_0 = 1 + \frac{100\ \text{k}\Omega}{10\ \text{k}\Omega} = 1 + 10 = 11 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Magnitude at cutoff.**

$$\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \frac{11}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 7.78\ \text{V/V}$$

In dB: $20 \log_{10}(7.78) \approx 17.82$ dB, which is $20 \log_{10}(11) - 3 = 20.83 - 3 = 17.83$ dB. ✓

**Final Design Summary:**

| Component | Value | Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| $R$ (series) | $1.6\ \text{k}\Omega$ | E12 / 1% |
| $C$ (to ground) | $0.01\ \mu F$ | ±5% |
| $R_f$ (feedback) | $100\ \text{k}\Omega$ | ±1% |
| $R_1$ (to ground) | $10\ \text{k}\Omega$ | ±1% |

**Specifications achieved:**
- $f_c \approx 9.95$ kHz ✓
- $A_0 = 11$ ✓
- Roll-off: $-20$ dB/decade above $f_c$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall for Numerical Problems:**
> - **Always show units** in every line. Writing "R = 1591.55" without "$\Omega$" loses marks.
> - **Choose standard E12/E24 values** in the final design, not theoretical values. Examiners want practical design.
> - **Verify the design** at the end — KTU rewards the "verify" step with 1–2 extra marks.
> - For inverting filters, **remember the negative sign** in gain: $A_v = -R_f / R_{in}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Active vs Passive Filters (Module 3)**

- ✅ **Passive Filter:** R, L, C only. No gain. No power supply. **Insertion loss always present.**
- ✅ **Active Filter:** Op-Amp + R + C. **Gain > 1 possible.** Requires $\pm V_{CC}$.
- ✅ **Cutoff frequency (1st order):** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz — universal formula.
- ✅ **Angular cutoff:** $\omega_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ rad/s.
- ✅ **$-3$ dB point:** $\vert A \vert = A_{\max} / \sqrt{2}$ at $f = f_c$.
- ✅ **Phase at cutoff:** $-45^{\circ}$ (1st-order) or $-90^{\circ}$ (2nd-order).
- ✅ **Roll-off rate:** $-20$ dB/decade per order of filter.
- ✅ **Op-amp advantages:** high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$, no loading effect, easy cascading.
- ✅ **Inductor-avoidance rationale:** bulk, cost, EMI, non-IC-friendly.
- ✅ **Sallen-Key 2nd order:** uses two RC sections + op-amp buffer; $K = 1 + R_B/R_A$.
- ✅ **MFB (Multiple Feedback):** inverting topology, used for high-Q bandpass filters.
- ✅ **Twin-T Notch:** standard topology for 50/60 Hz line-frequency rejection.
- ✅ **Standard formula to memorize:** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ for Sallen-Key.
- ✅ **Attenuation rule:** every decade above $f_c$ → 20 dB additional attenuation (1st order).
- ✅ **For 2nd-order:** every decade above $f_c$ → 40 dB additional attenuation.
- ✅ **Active filter limitation:** op-amp **GBW** (gain-bandwidth product) limits high-frequency operation.
- ✅ **Power consumption:** active filters draw quiescent current even with no input signal.
- ✅ **KTU-favorite question pattern:** "Compare active and passive filters" — **always include** the table format with at least 8 rows.
- ✅ **Numericals pattern:** "Design a 1st-order LPF with $f_c = $ ___ and $A_0 = $ ___" — remember the order: **first $R$ from $f_c$, then $R_f$/$R_1$ from $A_0$**.
- ✅ **Last-minute trick:** if the question asks for "frequency response" — write the transfer function in s-domain first, then the magnitude in dB form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active Filters: Analysis of First Order HPF & LPF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Active Filters: Analysis of First Order HPF & LPF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Active Filter?

An **Active Filter** is a frequency-selective electronic circuit that uses one or more **active components** (typically an operational amplifier) combined with **passive components** (resistors and capacitors) to achieve a desired magnitude and phase response over a specific band of frequencies. Unlike passive filters, active filters can provide **voltage gain greater than unity**, offer **high input impedance**, **low output impedance**, and do not require bulky inductors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** *An active filter is a signal-conditioning network employing an op-amp together with RC components, providing controlled passband gain, sharp roll-off, and isolation between source and load.*

## 1.2 First-Order vs Higher-Order Filters

The **order** of a filter is determined by the number of energy-storing elements (capacitors/inductors) in the network. A **first-order filter** contains exactly one reactive element (here, one capacitor) and produces a roll-off rate of **±20 dB/decade** beyond the cutoff frequency. Higher-order filters (2nd, 3rd, ...) are built by cascading first-order sections and achieve steeper roll-off rates of **−40 dB/decade, −60 dB/decade**, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** In Module 3, the focus is strictly on **first-order** configurations built around a single op-amp. Mastery of this section is the gateway to understanding Sallen-Key, Multiple-Feedback (MFB), and state-variable filters in higher semesters.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a **water tap with a flexible rubber diaphragm** inside it.

- A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** behaves like a tap fitted with a **heavy sponge at the outlet**: low-pressure, slow changes (low frequencies) pass through easily, but sudden bursts (high frequencies) get absorbed/dampened.
- A **High Pass Filter (HPF)** behaves like a tap with a **spring-loaded valve**: it blocks the slow, steady trickle (DC / low frequencies) but lets through the rapid, pulsating bursts (high frequencies).
- The **op-amp** is the *amplifier-pump* that boosts the weak output back to a useful level and prevents the sponge/spring from being "pushed back" by whatever is connected downstream (loading effect).

## 1.4 Key Physical Parameters to Remember

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (KTU problems) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cutoff / Corner Frequency | $f_c$ | **1 kHz – 100 kHz** | Hz |
| Angular Cutoff Frequency | $\omega_c$ | $2\pi f_c$ | rad/s |
| Passband Gain | $A_0$ | **1 to 10** (often unity or 2) | dimensionless |
| Roll-off Slope | $\pm 20$ dB/decade | First-order | dB/dec |
| Phase Shift at $f_c$ | $\pm 45°$ | LPF: −45°, HPF: +45° | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** The passband gain $A_0$ of a first-order active filter is set by the op-amp's feedback resistors, **independent** of the cutoff frequency $f_c$. This **decoupling of gain and cutoff** is the single biggest advantage of active filters over passive RC filters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture (Non-Inverting Topology)

The non-inverting active filter is the most commonly examined configuration in KTU 2024 because it offers:
- High input impedance (no loading of source)
- Easy setting of passband gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$
- Stable closed-loop operation

**General Working Logic:**

1. The **RC network** (at the non-inverting input) acts as a **frequency-dependent voltage divider**.
2. The **op-amp**, configured in non-inverting mode, multiplies this divided voltage by a **constant gain** $A_0$.
3. For an **LPF**: the resistor $R$ is in **series** and the capacitor $C$ is to **ground** — at high frequencies, the capacitor's impedance $\to 0$, short-circuiting the signal to ground.
4. For an **HPF**: the capacitor $C$ is in **series** and the resistor $R$ is to **ground** — at low frequencies, the capacitor's impedance $\to \infty$, blocking the signal.

## 2.2 The Two Critical Frequencies

- **Cutoff Frequency $f_c$:** The frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (or **−3 dB**) of its passband value. Equivalently, the frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$.
- **Bandwidth (BW):** For an LPF, BW = $f_c$. For an HPF, BW = $\infty$ (theoretically).

## 2.3 Why Active Filters are Preferred (Real-World Utility)

- **Audio Equipment:** Crossover networks in speakers separate bass (LPF) and treble (HPF) signals.
- **Biomedical Instrumentation:** ECG/EEG machines use HPFs to remove DC electrode offset and LPFs to remove 50 Hz mains interference (with notch filters).
- **Communication Receivers:** LPF selects the desired channel bandwidth; HPF removes low-frequency rumble.
- **Control Systems:** Anti-aliasing LPF before an ADC and a reconstruction HPF after a DAC.
- **Why op-amps are mandatory:** A passive RC LPF loaded by the next stage would shift $f_c$ unpredictably. The op-amp's near-infinite input impedance **isolates** the RC network, making $f_c$ depend **only** on the chosen $R$ and $C$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — it covers ~80% of all numerical questions in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic.**

| # | Quantity | First-Order LPF | First-Order HPF |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transfer Function $H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)}$ | $A_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | $A_0 \cdot \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ |
| 2 | Magnitude Response $\vert H(j\omega)\vert$ | $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{A_0 \cdot \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| 3 | Phase Response $\angle H(j\omega)$ | $-\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | $+90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ |
| 4 | Cutoff Frequency $f_c$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| 5 | Passband Gain $A_0$ | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ |
| 6 | Gain at $f \ll f_c$ | $A_0$ (flat) | $0$ (rising at +20 dB/dec) |
| 7 | Gain at $f \gg f_c$ | $0$ (falling at −20 dB/dec) | $A_0$ (flat) |
| 8 | Phase at $f = f_c$ | $-45°$ | $+45°$ |
| 9 | Magnitude at $f = f_c$ | $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,A_0$ | $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,A_0$ |
| 10 | Roll-off Slope (beyond $f_c$) | $-20$ dB/decade | $+20$ dB/decade (below $f_c$) |
| 11 | Half-Power Condition | $P_{out} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2}$ when $f = f_c$ | $P_{out} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2}$ when $f = f_c$ |
| 12 | Quality Factor $Q$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ (max for 1st order) | $\dfrac{1}{2}$ (max for 1st order) |

> **Notation used above:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes magnitude; the symbol is used *outside* the markdown table to avoid breaking the table's vertical-pipe delimiter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Low Pass Filter (LPF)

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description
A non-inverting op-amp with a series resistor $R$ feeding the non-inverting input and a capacitor $C$ from the non-inverting input to ground. Feedback resistors $R_f$ and $R_1$ set the gain.

### 3.1.2 Detailed Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the voltage at the non-inverting input ($V_+$) using the voltage divider rule:**

The RC network forms a divider with $R$ in series and $C$ to ground. The capacitor's impedance in the s-domain is $\dfrac{1}{sC}$.

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{Z_C}{R + Z_C}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$:**

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$ to clear the fraction:**

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the non-inverting amplifier gain formula:**

For a non-inverting op-amp configuration, the closed-loop gain is:

$$
A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot V_+(s)
$$

**Step 5 — Combine Step 3 and Step 4 to obtain the Transfer Function:**

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = A_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to frequency response by substituting $s = j\omega$:**

$$
H(j\omega) = A_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 7 — Magnitude and phase response:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|H(j\omega)| &= \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \\[6pt]
\angle H(j\omega) &= -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Identify the cutoff frequency:**

At cutoff, $\omega_c RC = 1$, which gives:

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

At this frequency, $\vert H(j\omega_c)\vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\angle H(j\omega_c) = -45°$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: First-Order High Pass Filter (HPF)

### 3.2.1 Circuit Description
A non-inverting op-amp with a **series capacitor** $C$ feeding the non-inverting input and a **resistor** $R$ from the non-inverting input to ground.

### 3.2.2 Detailed Derivation

**Step 1 — Voltage divider with $C$ in series and $R$ to ground:**

The capacitor's impedance is $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$ and the resistor is $R$.

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $sC$:**

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by the op-amp gain $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$:**

$$
V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot V_+(s) = A_0 \cdot V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Transfer Function:**

$$
H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = A_0 \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ to obtain the frequency response:**

$$
H(j\omega) = A_0 \cdot \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude and phase response:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
|H(j\omega)| &= \frac{A_0 \cdot \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \\[6pt]
\angle H(j\omega) &= +90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify the low-frequency and high-frequency limits:**

- As $\omega \to 0$ (low frequency): $\vert H(j\omega)\vert \to 0$ and $\angle H \to +90°$.
- As $\omega \to \infty$ (high frequency): $\vert H(j\omega)\vert \to A_0$ and $\angle H \to 0°$.
- At $\omega = \omega_c$ (where $\omega_c RC = 1$): $\vert H(j\omega_c)\vert = \dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$ and $\angle H = +45°$.

**Step 8 — Cutoff frequency is identical to the LPF case:**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | First-Order LPF | First-Order HPF |
|---|---|---|
| $H(s)$ form | $A_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ (zero-free) | $A_0 \cdot \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ (one zero at origin) |
| DC Gain | $A_0$ | $0$ |
| Infinite-freq Gain | $0$ | $A_0$ |
| Mid-band Gain | $A_0$ (below $f_c$) | $A_0$ (above $f_c$) |
| Pole Location | $s = -\dfrac{1}{RC}$ (LHP) | $s = -\dfrac{1}{RC}$ (LHP) |
| Zero Location | None | $s = 0$ (origin) |
| Phase Range | $0°$ to $-90°$ | $+90°$ to $0°$ |
| Stopband Attenuation | Improves by 20 dB/dec | Improves by 20 dB/dec |
| Realization | $R$ in series, $C$ to ground | $C$ in series, $R$ to ground |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Active Filter System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal flow architecture** of a generic first-order active filter, identifying the three functional blocks and the control parameters of each.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vin Signal Source] --> B[RC Frequency Selective Network]
    B -->|Vplus| C[Op-Amp Non-Inverting Amplifier]
    C --> D[Vout Buffered Output]
    R[Resistor R] --> B
    Cap[Capacitor C] --> B
    Rf[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> C
    R1[Ground Resistor R1] --> C
    Fc[Cutoff Frequency fc = 1 over 2 pi RC] -.sets.-> B
    Ao[Passband Gain Ao = 1 plus Rf by R1] -.sets.-> C
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **RC Network** sets the cutoff frequency $f_c$ — its value is *independent* of the gain.
- The **Op-Amp Block** sets the passband gain $A_0$ — its value is *independent* of $f_c$.
- The two blocks can therefore be **designed separately**, a key advantage of active filters.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Selection Logic — LPF vs HPF Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{What frequency band to pass?}
    Q -->|Low Frequencies Only| L[Configure RC Network: R in Series, C to Ground]
    Q -->|High Frequencies Only| H[Configure RC Network: C in Series, R to Ground]
    L --> Lout[First Order LPF: H of s equals A0 over 1 plus sRC]
    H --> Hout[First Order HPF: H of s equals A0 times sRC over 1 plus sRC]
    Lout --> Lchar[Roll off minus 20 dB per decade above fc]
    Hout --> Hchar[Roll off minus 20 dB per decade below fc]
```

---

## 4.3 Bode Plot Conceptual Map (Asymptotic Behaviour)

The exact Bode magnitude plot cannot be drawn in Mermaid, but the following state-diagram captures the **asymptotic behaviour** of both filters in the three frequency regions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> LFRegion: Low Frequency f less than fc
    [*] --> MFRegion: Cutoff Region near f equals fc
    [*] --> HFRegion: High Frequency f greater than fc

    LFRegion --> LPF_Mag: LPF Gain equals A0 flat
    LFRegion --> HPF_Mag: HPF Gain rises at plus 20 dB per decade
    MFRegion --> Transition: Gain equals A0 over root 2 minus 3 dB point
    HFRegion --> LPF_Mag2: LPF Gain falls at minus 20 dB per decade
    HFRegion --> HPF_Mag2: HPF Gain equals A0 flat
```

**Reading guide for the Bode plot:**

| Region | LPF Behaviour | HPF Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| $f \ll f_c$ | Flat at $A_0$ (passband) | Rising at +20 dB/decade |
| $f = f_c$ | Drop by **−3 dB** to $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Rise to $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}}$ (still rising) |
| $f \gg f_c$ | Falling at −20 dB/decade | Flat at $A_0$ (passband) |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **cutoff frequency** of a first-order active filter. What is its value in terms of $R$ and $C$ for both LPF and HPF?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cutoff frequency $f_c$ is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., −3 dB) of its passband value. It is also called the **−3 dB frequency** or **half-power frequency**. For a first-order RC active filter,

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

The expression is **identical** for both LPF and HPF since both are governed by the same RC time constant. The numerical value depends only on the choice of $R$ and $C$, not on the op-amp gain.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Q2.** State **two** advantages of an active filter over a passive filter.

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Gain Greater than Unity:** Active filters can provide passband voltage gain $A_0 > 1$ (set by feedback resistors), whereas passive RC filters always have $\vert H(j\omega)\vert \leq 1$ — they can only attenuate.
2. **No Loading Effect:** The op-amp's near-infinite input impedance prevents the load from disturbing the RC network's frequency response, ensuring $f_c$ depends only on $R$ and $C$.

*(Optional third point for full credit: Active filters do not require inductors, making them smaller, cheaper, and easier to integrate on ICs.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

### **Question A**

> **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order **active low pass filter** using a non-inverting op-amp configuration. (7 marks)
> **(b)** For a filter with $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $R_f = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, calculate the cutoff frequency, passband gain, and voltage gain at $f = 2 f_c$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

The circuit consists of $R$ in series and $C$ to ground at the non-inverting input of the op-amp. The voltage at the non-inverting terminal, by the voltage divider rule:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{\frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC$:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$

The op-amp in non-inverting configuration provides:

$$
V_{out}(s) = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_+(s)
$$

Hence the transfer function:

$$
\boxed{H(s) = \frac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = A_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1 + sRC}, \quad \text{where } A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}}
$$

The frequency response:

$$
H(j\omega) = \frac{A_0}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Passband Gain:**

$$
A_0 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 2 — Cutoff Frequency:**

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Gain at $f = 2 f_c$:**

The magnitude response:

$$
|H(j\omega)| = \frac{A_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}
$$

At $f = 2 f_c$, we have $\omega = 2 \cdot 2\pi f_c$, so $\omega RC = 2 \cdot (2\pi f_c RC) = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$.

$$
|H(j \cdot 2\omega_c)| = \frac{2}{\sqrt{1 + 2^2}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{2.236} \approx 0.894
$$

**Result:** At $f = 2 f_c$, the voltage gain is approximately **0.894** (compared to passband gain of 2), confirming the expected attenuation beyond cutoff.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

> **(a)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order **active high pass filter**. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Compare the first-order LPF and HPF in terms of (i) transfer function, (ii) phase response, and (iii) magnitude response, with neat sketches. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

For an HPF, the series element is the capacitor $C$ and the shunt element is the resistor $R$. The voltage at the non-inverting input is:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{sC}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC$:

$$
V_+(s) = V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

Multiplying by the op-amp gain:

$$
V_{out}(s) = A_0 \cdot V_+(s) = A_0 \cdot V_{in}(s) \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{H(s) = A_0 \cdot \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}}
$$

This is a **high-pass** response because as $s \to 0$ (low frequency), $H \to 0$, and as $s \to \infty$ (high frequency), $H \to A_0$.

**Part (b) — Comparison (7 marks):**

| Property | LPF | HPF |
|---|---|---|
| Transfer Function | $A_0 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | $A_0 \cdot \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ |
| DC Gain ($s=0$) | $A_0$ | $0$ |
| High-freq Gain ($s \to \infty$) | $0$ | $A_0$ |
| Phase | $0°$ to $-90°$ | $+90°$ to $0°$ |
| Phase at $f_c$ | $-45°$ | $+45°$ |

**Sketch description (expected in answer script):**
- LPF: Magnitude plot is flat at $A_0$ (in dB) from $f = 0$ to $f_c$, then drops linearly with slope **−20 dB/decade**.
- HPF: Magnitude plot rises with slope **+20 dB/decade** from $f = 0$ to $f_c$, then flattens at $A_0$ for $f > f_c$.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the op-amp gain factor $A_0$:** Many students derive the RC divider alone and write $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1+sRC}$, missing the $\left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right)$ term. This loses **3 out of 7 marks** in Part (a) derivations.
> 2. **Wrong cutoff frequency formula:** Writing $f_c = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$. The factor of $2\pi$ is worth **1 full mark** in numericals.
> 3. **Confusing HPF and LPF element placement:** Writing $R$ in series for an HPF (incorrect — must be $C$ in series) — examiners will deduct at least **2 marks** in the sketch/description.
> 4. **Phase at $f_c$:** LPF phase is $-45°$ and HPF phase is $+45°$. Mixing these up costs 1 mark in Part A conceptual questions.
> 5. **Not mentioning −3 dB / half-power significance** in Part A answers — the 3-mark question on "cutoff frequency" explicitly expects this.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- **First-order active filter = op-amp + one RC section.**
- **Cutoff frequency** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ is **identical** for both LPF and HPF.
- **Passband gain** $A_0 = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ is **identical** for both LPF and HPF.
- **LPF element arrangement:** $R$ in series, $C$ to ground.
- **HPF element arrangement:** $C$ in series, $R$ to ground.
- **LPF Transfer Function:** $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + sRC}$ — passband at low frequencies.
- **HPF Transfer Function:** $H(s) = \dfrac{A_0 \cdot sRC}{1 + sRC}$ — passband at high frequencies.
- **Roll-off rate:** $\pm 20$ dB/decade for both.
- **Phase at $f_c$:** LPF $\to -45°$, HPF $\to +45°$.
- **At $f \to 0$:** LPF gain $= A_0$, HPF gain $= 0$.
- **At $f \to \infty$:** LPF gain $= 0$, HPF gain $= A_0$.
- **Magnitude at $f = f_c$:** $\dfrac{A_0}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707\,A_0$ (i.e., −3 dB below passband).
- **Quality factor $Q = \dfrac{1}{2}$** for any first-order filter — maximum achievable at this order.
- **Decoupling advantage:** $f_c$ and $A_0$ can be designed **independently** — change $R_f$ or $R_1$ to alter gain without affecting $f_c$.
- **Loading immunity:** Op-amp's high input impedance $\Rightarrow$ the cutoff is set **only** by $R$ and $C$, not by the load.
- **HPF cannot pass DC** — a fundamental consequence of the zero at $s=0$ in its transfer function.
- **Use decibel form for sketches:** $20\log_{10}\vert H(j\omega)\vert$ gives a linear plot with a clear corner at $f_c$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working of Band Pass and Band Stop Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working of Band Pass and Band Stop Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Band Pass Filter (BPF) — Formal Definition

A **Band Pass Filter (BPF)** is a frequency-selective two-port network that allows signals within a specific band of frequencies (bounded by a lower cutoff frequency $f_L$ and an upper cutoff frequency $f_H$) to pass through with minimal attenuation, while strongly attenuating all frequencies lying **outside** this band.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A BPF is realized by cascading a **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** and a **High Pass Filter (HPF)** in series, subject to the condition $f_L \neq f_H$ and the LPF cutoff frequency must be **greater than** the HPF cutoff frequency ($f_H \neq f_L$ with $f_H > f_L$). The resulting passband is the *intersection* of the two individual passbands.

**Key Performance Parameters (bold for emphasis):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Lower Cutoff Frequency | $f_L$ | $-3\text{ dB}$ frequency of the HPF section |
| Upper Cutoff Frequency | $f_H$ | $-3\text{ dB}$ frequency of the LPF section |
| **Center Frequency** | $f_0$ | Geometric mean of $f_L$ and $f_H$ |
| **Bandwidth** | $BW$ | Difference between $f_H$ and $f_L$ |
| Quality Factor | $Q$ | Selectivity of the filter |

### 1.2 Band Stop Filter (BSF) — Formal Definition

A **Band Stop Filter (BSF)**, also called a **Band Reject Filter (BRF)** or **Band Elimination Filter**, is a frequency-selective two-port network that **attenuates** signals within a specific band of frequencies (between $f_L$ and $f_H$) while allowing frequencies **outside** this band to pass through with minimal attenuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A BSF is realized by connecting an LPF and an HPF in **parallel** (additive combination at the output), where the LPF allows frequencies above $f_L$ and the HPF allows frequencies below $f_H$. The resulting response is the *union* of the two individual passbands. When the bandwidth becomes very small, the BSF is called a **Notch Filter**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**BPF Analogy — The "Whistle Detector":**
Imagine you are at a noisy railway station trying to hear a specific train's whistle that vibrates at a single, fixed frequency (say, $2\text{ kHz}$). Your ear (acting as a filter) *tunes into* the $2\text{ kHz}$ band and ignores the rumble of wheels (low frequencies) and the chatter of people (high frequencies). This is exactly what a BPF does — it is an electronic "frequency tuner" that listens to one specific band.

**BSF Analogy — The "Noise Canceller":**
Now imagine you are recording a music lecture, and a constant $50\text{ Hz}$ mains hum is ruining the audio. You apply a BSF (notch filter) centered at $50\text{ Hz}$ that *removes only that hum* while preserving the lecturer's voice (above $50\text{ Hz}$) and the high-frequency consonants (above $3\text{ kHz}$). The BSF is essentially a "frequency eraser."

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Applications of BPF:** Audio equalizers, radio/TV tuners (to select a station), wireless communication channel selection, biomedical signal extraction (ECG, EEG band extraction).
>
> **Real-World Applications of BSF:** $50\text{ Hz}/60\text{ Hz}$ mains hum removal, suppression of specific interfering carriers in communication, removal of DC offset or low-frequency drift in instrumentation amplifiers.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard $-3\text{ dB}$ Cutoff Definition:** The cutoff frequencies $f_L$ and $f_H$ are universally defined as the points where the magnitude response drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (approximately $0.707$) of its maximum passband value, corresponding to a power ratio of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ (i.e., $-3\text{ dB}$). The **bandwidth** is universally measured between these two $-3\text{ dB}$ points.

The **Quality Factor** $Q$ characterizes the *selectivity* or *sharpness* of the filter:

$$Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW} = \dfrac{f_0}{f_H - f_L}$$

A **high $Q$** indicates a *narrow*, *selective* filter (sharp peak/notch); a **low $Q$** indicates a *wide* filter (gentle response).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Building Blocks

Both BPF and BSF are **second-order (or higher) composite filters** synthesized from first-order LPF and HPF stages. The way these stages are interconnected determines whether the result is a BPF or a BSF.

#### 2.1.1 First-Order RC Low Pass Filter (LPF)

$$\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} = \dfrac{1}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

where the **upper cutoff frequency** is:

$$f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

At $f = 0$ (DC): Gain = $1$ (passes); At $f \to \infty$: Gain = $0$ (blocks).

#### 2.1.2 First-Order RC High Pass Filter (HPF)

$$\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC} = \dfrac{j(f/f_L)}{1 + j(f/f_L)}$$

where the **lower cutoff frequency** is:

$$f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$$

At $f = 0$ (DC): Gain = $0$ (blocks); At $f \to \infty$: Gain = $1$ (passes).

### 2.2 How a Band Pass Filter Works (Cascade Topology)

A BPF is constructed by **cascading (series connection)** of an HPF followed by an LPF:

$$V_{in} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{HPF}(f_L)} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{LPF}(f_H)} \longrightarrow V_{out}$$

- The **HPF** removes all low-frequency components below $f_L$.
- The **LPF** removes all high-frequency components above $f_H$.
- The intermediate band $f_L < f < f_H$ is the only region where both filters pass the signal — hence the **band pass** behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Condition for BPF Operation:** $f_H > f_L$. If $f_H \leq f_L$, the two filter regions do not overlap, and **no signal passes** (output is identically zero). This is the most common student error in KTU exam answers.

#### 2.2.1 Wide-Band BPF Transfer Function (First-Order Cascade)

When the two cutoff frequencies are far apart ($f_H \gg f_L$), the stages do not load each other, and the overall transfer function is the product:

$$H_{BPF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) \cdot H_{LPF}(s) = \left[\dfrac{s/\omega_L}{1 + s/\omega_L}\right] \cdot \left[\dfrac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}\right]$$

Simplifying (assuming $s \ll \omega_L$ does not apply since we are in the passband):

$$H_{BPF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$$

**Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H_{BPF}(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{(f/f_L)}{\sqrt{\left[1 + (f/f_L)^2\right]\left[1 + (f/f_H)^2\right]}}$$

**Passband characteristics:**
- For $f_L \ll f \ll f_H$: numerator $\approx f/f_L$, both denominators $\approx 1$, so $\vert H \vert \approx f/f_L$ (rising at $+20\text{ dB/decade}$).
- At $f = f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$: $\vert H \vert$ attains a maximum less than unity (this is the **center frequency gain**).
- For $f \gg f_H$: $\vert H \vert$ rolls off at $-20\text{ dB/decade}$.

### 2.3 How a Band Stop Filter Works (Parallel Topology)

A BSF is constructed by **parallel connection** of an HPF and an LPF, with their outputs **summed** at a common node:

$$V_{in} \begin{cases} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{HPF}(f_L)} \longrightarrow V_1 \\ \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{LPF}(f_H)} \longrightarrow V_2 \end{cases} \Bigg\} V_{out} = V_1 + V_2$$

- The **HPF** passes all frequencies **above** $f_L$.
- The **LPF** passes all frequencies **below** $f_H$.
- The output is the **sum**, so any frequency that passes through *at least one* of the filters reaches the output.
- Only the band $f_L < f < f_H$ is **rejected** (neither filter passes it).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Condition for BSF Operation:** $f_L < f_H$. The HPF cutoff must be **less than** the LPF cutoff so that the rejected bands overlap. If $f_L \geq f_H$, all frequencies pass (no rejection) — output is the original signal.

#### 2.3.1 Wide-Band BSF Transfer Function (First-Order Parallel)

$$H_{BSF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) + H_{LPF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L}{1 + s/\omega_L} + \dfrac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

Finding a common denominator:

$$H_{BSF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L (1 + s/\omega_H) + (1 + s/\omega_L)}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$H_{BSF}(s) = \dfrac{1 + 2s/\omega_L + s^2/(\omega_L \omega_H)}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$$

(assuming $\omega_L = \omega_H = \omega_0$ for symmetric design, this simplifies dramatically).

**Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H_{BSF}(j\omega) \vert \approx \begin{cases} 1 & f \ll f_L \quad \text{(passes low)} \\ 0 & f_L < f < f_H \quad \text{(rejected)} \\ 1 & f \gg f_H \quad \text{(passes high)} \end{cases}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. All exam problems reduce to applying these equations.**

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Condition / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Center Frequency $f_0$ | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Bandwidth $BW$ | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hz |
| 3 | Quality Factor $Q$ | $Q = f_0 / BW$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | BPF Topology | Cascade of HPF + LPF | $f_H > f_L$ |
| 5 | BSF Topology | Parallel (sum) of HPF + LPF | $f_L < f_H$ |
| 6 | First-order RC LPF cutoff | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 7 | First-order RC HPF cutoff | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Hz |
| 8 | BPF max gain location | At $f = f_0$ (not at unity) | — |
| 9 | BSF rejected band | $f_L$ to $f_H$ | — |
| 10 | Notch Filter special case | BSF with very small BW (high $Q$) | $Q > 10$ typically |
| 11 | Rolloff rate (1st-order BPF) | $+20\text{ dB/dec}$ rising, $-20\text{ dB/dec}$ falling | — |
| 12 | Rolloff rate (1st-order BSF) | $-20\text{ dB/dec}$ and $+20\text{ dB/dec}$ from edges | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Engineering:** Graphic equalizers use a *bank* of BPFs to extract specific frequency bands (bass, mid, treble).
- **Telecommunications:** A superheterodyne receiver uses a BPF (IF filter) at $455\text{ kHz}$ (AM) or $10.7\text{ MHz}$ (FM) to select one channel.
- **Biomedical:** EEG signal acquisition uses BPFs to isolate alpha waves ($8\text{–}13\text{ Hz}$) from the broader EEG spectrum.
- **Power Systems:** BSF notch filters at $50\text{ Hz}/60\text{ Hz}$ are mandatory in ECG recorders to remove mains interference.
- **Instrumentation:** BPFs in lock-in amplifiers extract signals of known reference frequency buried in noise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: BPF Transfer Function from Cascaded LPF and HPF

**Given:**
- First-order RC LPF with transfer function $H_{LPF}(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + s R_2 C_2}$ and cutoff $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_2 C_2}$.
- First-order RC HPF with transfer function $H_{HPF}(s) = \dfrac{s R_1 C_1}{1 + s R_1 C_1}$ and cutoff $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C_1}$.

**Step 1 — Write individual transfer functions in terms of $\omega_L$ and $\omega_H$:**

Define $\omega_L = 2\pi f_L$ and $\omega_H = 2\pi f_H$. Then:

$$H_{HPF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L}{1 + s/\omega_L}, \quad H_{LPF}(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

**Step 2 — Cascade them (multiply):**

$$H_{BPF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) \cdot H_{LPF}(s)$$

$$H_{BPF}(s) = \left(\dfrac{s/\omega_L}{1 + s/\omega_L}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Combine the denominators:**

$$H_{BPF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$$

This is the **open-circuit transfer function** of a wide-band BPF assuming the two stages do not load each other (i.e., the input impedance of the second stage is much larger than the output impedance of the first).

**Step 4 — Magnitude Response in the Frequency Domain:**

Substituting $s = j\omega = j2\pi f$:

$$\vert H_{BPF}(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\omega/\omega_L}{\sqrt{\left[1 + (\omega/\omega_L)^2\right]\left[1 + (\omega/\omega_H)^2\right]}}$$

**Step 5 — Find the Center Frequency $f_0$:**

The center frequency is defined as the frequency where $\vert H \vert$ is maximum. To find it, differentiate $\vert H \vert^2$ with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero. After algebraic simplification (assuming $f_H \gg f_L$):

$$f_0 = \sqrt{f_L \cdot f_H}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Gain at $f_0$:**

$$\vert H_{BPF}(j\omega_0) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\left[1 + (f_L/f_H)\right]\left[1 + (f_H/f_L)\right]}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2 + (f_L/f_H) + (f_H/f_L)}}$$

For widely separated cutoffs ($f_H \gg f_L$), this approaches $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2 + f_H/f_L}} \ll 1$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: BSF Transfer Function from Parallel LPF and HPF

**Given:** Same first-order LPF and HPF as above, but their outputs are summed at a common node (e.g., via an op-amp summing amplifier or directly at a high-impedance node).

**Step 1 — Write the summed transfer function:**

$$H_{BSF}(s) = H_{HPF}(s) + H_{LPF}(s)$$

$$H_{BSF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L}{1 + s/\omega_L} + \dfrac{1}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

**Step 2 — Find a common denominator:**

$$H_{BSF}(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L \cdot (1 + s/\omega_H) + (1 + s/\omega_L)}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the numerator:**

$$N(s) = \dfrac{s}{\omega_L} + \dfrac{s^2}{\omega_L \omega_H} + 1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_L}$$

$$N(s) = 1 + \dfrac{2s}{\omega_L} + \dfrac{s^2}{\omega_L \omega_H}$$

**Step 4 — Express in symmetric form (assuming $\omega_L = \omega_0 = \omega_H$ for the symmetric case):**

When $\omega_L = \omega_H = \omega_0$:

$$H_{BSF}(s) = \dfrac{1 + 2s/\omega_0 + s^2/\omega_0^2}{(1 + s/\omega_0)^2} = \dfrac{(1 + s/\omega_0)^2}{(1 + s/\omega_0)^2} = 1$$

This degenerate result tells us the parallel sum trivially gives unity in the perfectly symmetric case — meaning the *output equals the input* and the filter has no rejection. This is because the symmetric case is the boundary condition $f_L = f_H$, which is not a valid filter.

**Step 5 — General asymmetric case $\omega_L \neq \omega_H$:**

$$H_{BSF}(s) = \dfrac{1 + (2/\omega_L) s + (1/(\omega_L \omega_H)) s^2}{1 + (1/\omega_L + 1/\omega_H) s + (1/(\omega_L \omega_H)) s^2}$$

The numerator has a *zero* of transmission at:

$$s = -\omega_L \cdot \dfrac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - \omega_L/\omega_H}}{1} \quad \text{(for } \omega_H > \omega_L\text{, roots are complex)}$$

This complex zero pair produces the **notch** (deep attenuation) in the magnitude response.

**Step 6 — Verify DC and high-frequency limits:**

At $s = 0$ (DC): $H_{BSF}(0) = \dfrac{1 + 0 + 0}{1 + 0 + 0} = 1$ ✓ (passes DC)

As $s \to \infty$: $H_{BSF}(\infty) = \dfrac{s^2/(\omega_L \omega_H)}{s^2/(\omega_L \omega_H)} = 1$ ✓ (passes high frequencies)

This confirms the band-stop behavior.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quality Factor $Q$ for BPF/BSF

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$Q = \dfrac{f_0}{BW} = \dfrac{\sqrt{f_L f_H}}{f_H - f_L}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of design parameters:**

For a second-order BPF with transfer function:

$$H(s) = \dfrac{K \cdot s \cdot \omega_0 / Q}{s^2 + s \cdot \omega_0 / Q + \omega_0^2}$$

where $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ is the natural resonant frequency, the standard form comparison gives:

$$Q = \dfrac{\omega_0}{BW_{\text{rad}}} = \dfrac{f_0}{BW}$$

**Step 3 — Special case for $Q$:**

When $f_L$ and $f_H$ are close (narrow band), $f_0 \approx f_L \approx f_H$ and:

$$Q \approx \dfrac{f_0}{f_H - f_L} \gg 1$$

When $f_H \gg f_L$ (wide band), $Q \to \dfrac{\sqrt{f_L f_H}}{f_H} = \sqrt{f_L / f_H} \to 0$, meaning the filter is *non-selective*.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — BPF vs. BSF Comparison

| Property | Band Pass Filter (BPF) | Band Stop Filter (BSF) |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Cascade (HPF $\to$ LPF) | Parallel (HPF $+$ LPF, summed) |
| **Passes** | $f_L < f < f_H$ | $f < f_L$ and $f > f_H$ |
| **Rejects** | $f < f_L$ and $f > f_H$ | $f_L < f < f_H$ |
| **Center Frequency** | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ | $f_0 = \sqrt{f_L f_H}$ |
| **Bandwidth** | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | $BW = f_H - f_L$ |
| **Quality Factor** | $Q = f_0 / BW$ | $Q = f_0 / BW$ |
| **Wide-Band TF** | $H(s) = \dfrac{s/\omega_L}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$ | $H(s) = \dfrac{1 + 2s/\omega_L + s^2/(\omega_L \omega_H)}{(1 + s/\omega_L)(1 + s/\omega_H)}$ |
| **Roll-off at low end** | $+20\text{ dB/dec}$ | $-20\text{ dB/dec}$ (into stop band) |
| **Roll-off at high end** | $-20\text{ dB/dec}$ | $+20\text{ dB/dec}$ (out of stop band) |
| **Special case** | Tuned RF amplifier | Notch filter (narrow BSF) |
| **Narrow-band realization** | Multiple-feedback (MFB) active BPF | Twin-T notch filter |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: BPF Realization

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **block-level signal flow** of a wide-band Band Pass Filter constructed by cascading first-order HPF and LPF sections.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Vin Input Signal"] --> hpfBlock["Stage 1: First-Order RC HPF"]
    hpfBlock -->|"Attenuates f < fL"| midNode["Cascade Node"]
    midNode --> lpfBlock["Stage 2: First-Order RC LPF"]
    lpfBlock -->|"Attenuates f > fH"| outNode["Vout Band-Pass Output"]
    subgraph Cutoffs["Cutoff Definitions"]
        cfL["fL = 1 divided by 2 pi R1 C1"]
        cfH["fH = 1 divided by 2 pi R2 C2"]
    end
    hpfBlock -. "HPF cutoff" .-> cfL
    lpfBlock -. "LPF cutoff" .-> cfH
    classDef passRegion fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#2a8a2a,color:#0a3d0a
    classDef rejectRegion fill:#fcd5d5,stroke:#a02020,color:#5a0a0a
    class midNode passRegion
    class outNode passRegion
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The input signal first encounters the HPF, which blocks DC and low frequencies below $f_L$.
- The mid-band signal then enters the LPF, which blocks high frequencies above $f_H$.
- Only the band $f_L < f < f_H$ reaches the output, producing the band-pass response.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: BSF Realization

The following diagram shows the **parallel sum topology** of a wide-band Band Stop Filter.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src["Vin Input Signal"] --> splitJunction{"Signal Splitter"}
    splitJunction -->|"Path 1"| pathHPF["HPF Branch: First-Order RC High Pass, cutoff fL"]
    splitJunction -->|"Path 2"| pathLPF["LPF Branch: First-Order RC Low Pass, cutoff fH"]
    pathHPF --> sumAmp["Summing Junction Op-Amp Adder"]
    pathLPF --> sumAmp
    sumAmp --> vout["Vout Band-Stop Output"]
    notePass["Passes: f less than fL and f greater than fH"]:::passStyle
    noteReject["Rejects: fL less than f less than fH"]:::rejectStyle
    vout --- notePass
    sumAmp --- noteReject
    classDef passStyle fill:#d4f7d4,stroke:#2a8a2a,color:#0a3d0a
    classDef rejectStyle fill:#fcd5d5,stroke:#a02020,color:#5a0a0a
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The input is split into two parallel paths.
- The HPF path passes high frequencies; the LPF path passes low frequencies.
- At the summing junction, the two filtered signals are added.
- The result passes the union of the two passbands (low + high) and rejects the overlap (the band $f_L < f < f_H$).

### 4.3 Frequency Response Schematic (Conceptual Plot)

The following Mermaid plot schematically represents the magnitude response of a BPF (left) and a BSF (right) on a logarithmic frequency axis.

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Magnitude Response: BPF vs BSF (Conceptual)"
    x-axis "Frequency (log scale)" [fL, f0, fH]
    y-axis "|H| in dB" 0 --> 10
    line [0, 10, 0]
    line [10, 0, 10]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the plot above:**
> - The **first line** (peaks at $f_0$) represents the **BPF** — maximum gain in the middle, attenuated at both ends.
> - The **second line** (valley at $f_0$) represents the **BSF** — maximum gain at low and high frequencies, attenuated in the middle.
> - The $-3\text{ dB}$ points define $f_L$ and $f_H$ for both filters.

### 4.4 Narrow-Band BPF (Multiple-Feedback Topology) — Block Diagram

For high-$Q$ selective applications, the simple cascade is inadequate. An **active multiple-feedback (MFB) BPF** is used:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inNode["Vin"] --> r1["R1 Input Resistor"]
    r1 --> invInput["Inverting Input of Op-Amp"]
    invInput --> c1["C1 Feedback to Ground"]
    invInput --> r2["R2 Feedback Resistor"]
    r2 --> c2["C2 Series in Feedback"]
    c2 --> outNode["Vout"]
    outNode --> nonInv["Non-Inverting Input Tied via R3 and R4"]
    invInput --- c1
    outNode --- r2
    c2 --- r2
    noteActive["Active filter, Q greater than 10, narrow bandpass at f0"]:::activeStyle
    outNode --- noteActive
    classDef activeStyle fill:#fff3c4,stroke:#a08000,color:#3d2c00
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Design equations for MFB BPF (KTU frequently asked):**
>
> $$f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$$
>
> $$Q = \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{R_2 C_2}{R_1 C_1}}$$
>
> The MFB topology allows independent tuning of $f_0$ and $Q$, which is why it is preferred for narrow-band applications like tone decoding (e.g., the classic LM567 PLL tone decoder).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Define a Band Pass Filter. Explain how it can be realized using a first-order low pass and a first-order high pass filter.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
