# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q1.** State and explain the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillations. List the two conditions.

**Model Answer (Expected for full 3 marks):**

> The **Barkhausen Criterion** specifies the conditions under which a feedback amplifier will operate as a **sinusoidal oscillator** (i.e., produce sustained oscillations without any external input signal).
>
> The two conditions are:
>
> 1. **Loop-gain Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain $|A\beta|$ must be **unity** (equal to 1).
>
> $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$
>
> 2. **Loop-phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be **zero or an integer multiple of 360°**.
>
> $$\angle A\beta = 0° \text{ or } 360° \text{ or } 2n\pi \text{ radians}$$
>
> In practice, $|A\beta|$ is kept **slightly greater than 1** at switch-on to allow the oscillation amplitude to build up from thermal noise, and a **gain-control mechanism** reduces it to exactly 1 at steady state.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q2.** Mention the **frequency of oscillation** and **gain requirement** for a Wien bridge oscillator. What type of feedback network is used?

**Model Answer (Expected for full 3 marks):**

> The Wien bridge oscillator uses a **Wien bridge network** (a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm) as the **frequency-selective positive feedback network**.
>
> - **Frequency of oscillation:**
>
> $$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$
>
> - **Feedback factor at $f_o$:**
>
> $$\beta = \frac{1}{3}$$
>
> - **Gain required from the non-inverting op-amp:**
>
> $$A_v = 3 \quad \text{(so that } A_v \cdot \beta = 1\text{)}$$
>
> This is achieved by setting $R_f = 2 R_1$ in the negative feedback path.
>
> (Note: The op-amp is used in the **non-inverting** configuration so that it introduces **0° phase shift**, while the Wien network provides 0° phase shift only at $f_o$.)

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks) — KTU Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 scheme, Part B questions carry **14 marks each** with internal choice. Each is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. The model answers below are exhaustive and follow the KTU board examiner's valuation key.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **Q (a).** With a neat **circuit diagram**, explain the operation of an **RC phase shift oscillator** using a BJT. Derive the expression for its **frequency of oscillation** and the **minimum gain** required. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b).** Design an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT ($h_{fe} = 150$) to generate a **5 kHz** sine wave. Choose appropriate values of $R$, $C$, and $R_C$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Circuit Description (1.5 marks)**

The RC phase shift oscillator consists of:
- A **BJT in Common-Emitter (CE) configuration** acting as the **inverting amplifier** (gain $|A|$, phase shift 180°).
- A **3-stage RC ladder network** (also called a phase-shift network) connected between the collector (output) and the base (input).
- Each RC section has identical components: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$.

The three RC sections are connected in **cascade**, and the network provides a **frequency-dependent phase shift** that equals **180° at one specific frequency** $f_o$.

**Step 2: Operation (1 mark)**

- At the **oscillation frequency** $f_o$, the RC network provides a phase shift of **180°**.
- The CE amplifier also provides a phase shift of **180°**.
- Total phase shift around the loop = $180° + 180° = 360° = 0°$ (Barkhausen phase condition satisfied).
- For sustained oscillation, the magnitude condition $|A\beta| = 1$ must also be met.

**Step 3: Frequency of Oscillation Derivation (3.5 marks)**

Each RC section (assuming non-interacting) has transfer function:

$$H_1(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

Three sections in cascade:

$$H(s) = \left(\frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}\right)^3$$

Let $s = j\omega_o$ and $x = \omega_o RC$:

$$H(jx) = \frac{(jx)^3}{(1 + jx)^3} = \frac{-jx^3}{(1 - 3x^2) + j(3x - x^3)}$$

For $H$ to be a **purely real negative** number (so that the phase shift is exactly 180°), the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$3x - x^3 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x(3 - x^2) = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad x^2 = 3$$

So $\omega_o RC = \sqrt{3}$, giving:

$$f_o = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\pi RC} \quad \text{(non-interacting sections)}$$

Accounting for the **loading effect** of the BJT's $h_{ie}$ and $r_o$ on the RC sections, the **exact result** is:

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}}$$

**Step 4: Minimum Gain (1 mark)**

The magnitude of $H$ at $f_o$ is:

$$\vert H(f_o) \vert = \frac{1}{29}$$

So the amplifier must provide a gain of:

$$\boxed{\vert A \vert \geq 29}$$

For a BJT CE amplifier, $\vert A \vert = h_{fe} R_C / R'$, so $R_C / R' \geq 29/h_{fe}$.

#### Model Solution for Q(b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $f_o = 5$ kHz, $h_{fe} = 150$.

**Step 1: Find the RC time constant (2 marks).**

$$\frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6}\,RC} = 5000 \quad\Rightarrow\quad RC = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{6} \times 5000}$$

$$\sqrt{6} \approx 2.4495, \quad 2\pi\sqrt{6} \approx 15.394$$

$$RC = \frac{1}{15.394 \times 5000} = \frac{1}{76\,970} \approx 1.299 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2: Choose a practical value for C and compute R (2 marks).**

Choose $C = 0.001\,\mu F = 10^{-9}\,F$ (small, easily available).

$$R = \frac{1.299 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-9}} = 12\,990\,\Omega \approx 13\,k\Omega$$

**Standard value:** $R = 12\,k\Omega$ (preferred E12 series value) gives $f_o \approx 5.4$ kHz; for precise 5 kHz, use a 15 kΩ pot.

**Step 3: Compute the collector resistor (2 marks).**

For a **safety margin**, choose $\vert A \vert = 30$ (slightly > 29).

$$R_C = \frac{\vert A \vert \cdot R'}{h_{fe}}$$

Where $R' \approx R$ (the effective resistance seen at the collector, including bias network). Taking $R' \approx R = 12\,k\Omega$:

$$R_C = \frac{30 \times 12\,k}{150} = 2.4\,k\Omega$$

**Use $R_C = 2.4\,k\Omega$ (or 2.2 kΩ standard value).**

**Step 4: Verify and tabulate (1 mark).**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $R$ (each RC section) | 12 kΩ |
| $C$ (each RC section) | 1 nF |
| $R_C$ (collector) | 2.4 kΩ |
| $h_{fe}$ (BJT) | 150 |
| $f_o$ | 5 kHz (approx) |
| $\vert A \vert$ | 30 |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **Q (a).** Explain the operation of a **Wien bridge oscillator** using an **op-amp** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for its **frequency of oscillation**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q (b).** Explain the need for **amplitude stabilization** in a Wien bridge oscillator. Describe any **one method** of amplitude stabilization. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q(a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Block Diagram & Description (1.5 marks)**

A Wien bridge oscillator uses:
- An **op-amp in non-inverting configuration** (gain $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1$, phase shift = 0°).
- A **Wien bridge** as the **positive feedback** network, comprising:
  - **Series arm:** $Z_s = R + 1/(j\omega C)$ (a resistor $R$ in series with a capacitor $C$).
  - **Parallel arm:** $Z_p = R \parallel (1/j\omega C)$ (a resistor $R$ in parallel with a capacitor $C$).
- A **resistive divider** $R_f$ and $R_1$ in the **negative feedback** path.

**Step 2: Operation (1 mark)**

- The Wien network passes a fraction $\beta$ of the output back to the non-inverting input.
- $\beta$ is **frequency-dependent**: at one specific frequency $f_o$, it is purely real and equals 1/3.
- Since the op-amp has 0° phase shift, the loop phase at $f_o$ is 0° (Barkhausen phase condition).
- The op-amp gain is set to **3** so that $A_v \cdot \beta = 3 \cdot (1/3) = 1$ (Barkhausen magnitude condition).

**Step 3: Derivation of Frequency (3.5 marks)**

**Series arm impedance:**

$$Z_s = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C}$$

**Parallel arm impedance:**

$$Z_p = \frac{R \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Feedback factor:**

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p} = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}{\dfrac{1 + j\omega RC}{j\omega C} + \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}}$$

After simplification (multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C (1 + j\omega RC)$):

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2) + 3j\omega RC}$$

**Zero-phase condition:** the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero relative to the real part — equivalent to setting the real part of the denominator to zero:

$$1 - \omega_o^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}$$

$$\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Magnitude at $f_o$:** substitute $\omega_o = 1/RC$:

- Numerator: $j\omega_o RC = j(1) = j$
- Denominator: $0 + j3\omega_o RC = j3$

$$\beta = \frac{j}{j3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

So $|A_v| = 1/|\beta| = 3$, which means $R_f = 2 R_1$ in the negative feedback path. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution for Q(b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Need for Amplitude Stabilization (2 marks)**

In a Wien bridge oscillator:
- If the loop gain $|A_v\beta| < 1$ (i.e., $A_v < 3$), oscillations **die out** — the circuit acts as a damped oscillator.
- If the loop gain $|A_v\beta| > 1$ (i.e., $A_v > 3$), the amplitude **grows without bound** until the op-amp **saturates/clipping** occurs, producing a **distorted (non-sinusoidal) output** with high harmonic content.
- For **distortion-free sustained oscillation**, the loop gain must be **exactly 1** at steady state.

The challenge is that the loop gain depends on temperature, supply voltage, and component aging. Therefore, an **automatic gain control (AGC)** mechanism is needed to keep the gain *slightly above 3* during start-up and *exactly 3* during steady state.

**Step 2: Method 1 — JFET-based AGC (3.5 marks)**

A **JFET** is used as a **voltage-controlled resistor (VCR)** in the negative feedback path:

- The JFET is connected in **parallel** with $R_f$ (or in series — both topologies exist).
- The **JFET's drain-source resistance $R_{DS}$** depends on the **gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$**:
  - $V_{GS} = 0$ (at start-up) → $R_{DS}$ is **low** (JFET fully ON) → effective feedback resistance is **small** → $A_v$ is **higher than 3** → oscillations build up.
  - As amplitude grows, a **peak detector / rectifier** produces a negative DC voltage at the JFET gate, pinching off the channel → $R_{DS}$ **increases** → effective feedback resistance **increases** → $A_v$ **decreases** toward 3.
  - At steady state, the AGC loop stabilizes $A_v = 3$ exactly.

**Step 3: Method 2 — Thermistor-based AGC (1.5 marks — mention as alternative)**

A **thermistor** (negative temperature coefficient) is placed in the feedback path:
- At low amplitudes, the thermistor is **cool**, so its resistance is **high**, making the gain $A_v$ high → oscillations build up.
- As amplitude grows, the thermistor **heats up** (due to RMS current), resistance **drops**, $A_v$ reduces toward 3.
- At steady state, equilibrium is reached with $A_v = 3$ and low-distortion output.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> - **Forgetting the $\sqrt{6}$ factor** in the phase-shift oscillator frequency formula is a **2-mark** deduction. Always write $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6}\,RC}$.
> - **Confusing $A_v = 3$ and $A_v = 1/3$** in the Wien bridge (these are the amplifier gain and feedback factor respectively) is a common **1-mark** error. Memorize: $A_v = 3$ and $\beta = 1/3$.
> - **Omitting the AGC discussion** in any Wien bridge question worth ≥ 7 marks costs **2–3 marks**. AGC is a **favorite KTU question element**.
> - **Not mentioning the Barkhausen criterion** in derivation questions results in **1-mark** loss even if the rest of the derivation is correct. Always *begin* with: "According to the Barkhausen criterion, ..."

---

## 5.3 KTU Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- ⭐ **Barkhausen Criterion**: $|A\beta| = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or $360°$).
- ⭐ **RC Phase Shift Oscillator**:
  - Uses BJT CE or inverting op-amp.
  - Frequency: $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{6}\,RC}$.
  - Required gain: $\vert A \vert \geq 29$.
  - Three RC sections each contribute ~60° phase shift; total = 180°.
  - **No built-in AGC** → poor amplitude stability and higher distortion.
- ⭐ **Wien Bridge Oscillator**:
  - Uses non-inverting op-amp.
  - Frequency: $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$.
  - Required gain: $A_v = 3$ (so $R_f = 2 R_1$).
  - Wien network feedback factor at $f_o$: $\beta = 1/3$.
  - **Built-in AGC** (JFET, thermistor, lamp) → low distortion, high stability.
- ⭐ **Impedance formulas to memorize**:
  - Series RC: $Z_s = R + 1/(j\omega C) = (1 + j\omega RC)/(j\omega C)$.
  - Parallel RC: $Z_p = R/(1 + j\omega RC)$.
- ⭐ **Wien network derivation mantra**: Substitute $s = j\omega$ in $\beta = sRC / (s^2 R^2 C^2 + 3sRC + 1)$; set the **real part of the denominator to zero** to find $f_o$.
- ⭐ **Design rule of thumb**: For audio-band oscillators, choose $C$ in the range $0.001\,\mu F$ to $0.1\,\mu F$; compute $R$ from the desired $f_o$.
- ⭐ **Comparison one-liner for KTU**: *Wien bridge = better stability, lower distortion, gain of 3*; *Phase shift = simpler design, gain of 29, no AGC*.
- ⭐ **Why three RC sections?**: One section max = 90°; need 180°; one or two can't provide 180° in a realizable way; three is the *minimum* cascade to get exactly 180°.
- ⭐ **Non-inverting op-amp gain formula**: $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1$. Set $R_f = 2 R_1$ to get $A_v = 3$.
- ⭐ **KTU favorite trick question**: "What happens if the Wien bridge uses $R_1 \neq R_2$ or $C_1 \neq C_2$?" — Answer: the formula $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC)$ no longer holds; the more general $f_o = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2})$ applies.
- ⭐ **Amplitude stabilization methods** (must-know list): JFET as VCR, thermistor (NTC), incandescent lamp, back-to-back Zener diodes, automatic level control (ALC) using a rectifier + integrator.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triangular and Sawtooth Waveform Generators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Triangular and Sawtooth Waveform Generators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Triangular Waveform Generator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triangular Waveform Generator (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A triangular waveform generator is a non-sinusoidal oscillator that produces a periodic output voltage whose amplitude varies linearly with time between two equal but opposite peak values, forming a symmetric triangular shape. It is constructed using a **Schmitt trigger** (regenerative comparator) cascaded with an **op-amp integrator**, forming a closed-loop function generator.

The triangular waveform is characterized by:
- **Equal rise time ($T_r$) and fall time ($T_f$)** — making the waveform symmetric.
- **Constant slope** in both halves of the cycle.
- **Peak-to-peak amplitude** controlled by the feedback network.

## 1.2 Sawtooth Waveform Generator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sawtooth Waveform Generator (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A sawtooth (or ramp) waveform generator produces a periodic waveform in which the voltage rises (or falls) linearly to a peak value and then drops abruptly back to the starting level. The **rise time ($T_r$)** is much greater than the **fall time ($T_f$)**, or vice versa. It is realized by modifying the triangular generator such that the charging and discharging paths of the integrator use unequal resistors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Triangular and Sawtooth waveform generators are typically implemented using the **op-amp function generator block** — a combination of a Schmitt trigger (square wave generator) and an integrator.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

Imagine a **bath tub with two taps**:
- **Tap A (Hot water)** fills the tub at a **constant rate** until water reaches an **upper mark** — then a **floating ball sensor** triggers a **valve to switch to cold water**.
- **Tap B (Cold water)** drains the tub at a **constant rate** until water reaches a **lower mark** — the sensor flips the valve again.

The water level plotted against time is a **triangular wave**:
- **Rising edge (fill)** = positive slope of the triangle.
- **Falling edge (drain)** = negative slope of the triangle.
- **Sensor action** = role of the **Schmitt trigger** comparator.
- **Tub with constant inflow/outflow** = role of the **op-amp integrator**.

For a **sawtooth**, imagine a tank that **fills slowly** (like a rain barrel) and then **drains instantly** when full — that sharp drop is the "tooth" of the sawtooth.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Op-amp supply rails | $\pm V_{CC}$ | $\pm 12\text{ V},\ \pm 15\text{ V}$ |
| Saturation voltage | $V_{sat}$ | $\pm (V_{CC} - 2\text{ V})$ |
| Standard op-amp IC | — | **$\mu$A741, LM358** |
| Time period range | $T$ | **$\mu$s to ms** |
| Frequency range | $f$ | **Hz to kHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The op-amp is assumed to operate in the **saturation region** for the Schmitt trigger section and in the **linear region** for the integrator section. This dual mode of operation is fundamental to the entire function generator.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Architecture of the Function Generator

The triangular/sawtooth waveform generator is built from **two op-amp stages** in a closed feedback loop:

$$\text{Square wave (Schmitt)} \xrightarrow{\text{Integrates}} \text{Triangular wave} \xrightarrow{\text{Feedback}} \text{Schmitt input}$$

### Stage 1 — Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator / Astable Multivibrator)

The Schmitt trigger is a comparator with **positive feedback** that produces a **square wave** $V_{sq}$ at its output. It has two threshold voltages:

$$V_{TH} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat} \qquad V_{TL} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

where the **feedback factor** $\beta$ depends on the resistor network:

$$\beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

### Stage 2 — Op-Amp Integrator (Miller Integrator)

The integrator produces a **linear ramp** output. When the input is a constant $V_{sq} = \pm V_{sat}$, the output is:

$$V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{R_3 C}\int V_{sq}\, dt = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C}\, t$$

**Why these two stages?**
- The Schmitt trigger's output (square wave) is fed as input to the integrator.
- The integrator's triangular output is fed back to the non-inverting input of the Schmitt trigger.
- The two thresholds ($V_{TH}$ and $V_{TL}$) of the Schmitt trigger correspond to the **peak amplitudes** of the triangular wave.
- This creates a self-sustaining oscillation.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Phase 1 — Positive saturation of Schmitt trigger:**
- Schmitt output $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$.
- Integrator input is positive → integrator output $V_{tri}$ ramps **downward** (negative slope).
- $V_{tri}$ falls linearly until it reaches $V_{TL} = -\beta V_{sat}$.

**Phase 2 — Negative saturation of Schmitt trigger:**
- As soon as $V_{tri} \leq V_{TL}$, the Schmitt trigger flips to $V_{sq} = -V_{sat}$.
- Integrator input is now negative → $V_{tri}$ ramps **upward** (positive slope).
- $V_{tri}$ rises linearly until it reaches $V_{TH} = +\beta V_{sat}$.

**Phase 3 — Cycle repeats indefinitely**, generating the triangular wave.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **board-exam essential**. Memorize the symbols and the final expressions.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feedback factor | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless, $0 < \beta < 1$ |
| 2 | Upper threshold voltage | $V_{TH} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Lower threshold voltage | $V_{TL} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Peak-to-peak triangular amplitude | $V_{opp} = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Integrator slope magnitude | $\left\vert \dfrac{dV_{tri}}{dt}\right\vert = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C}$ | V/s |
| 6 | Half-period | $\dfrac{T}{2} = 2 \beta R_3 C$ | seconds (s) |
| 7 | **Time period (full cycle)** | $T = 4 \beta R_3 C = \dfrac{4 R_2 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2}$ | seconds (s) |
| 8 | **Frequency of triangular wave** | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_2 R_3 C}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | Sawtooth frequency (unidirectional) | $f_{saw} = \dfrac{1}{T_{rise} + T_{fall}}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 10 | Sawtooth with unequal resistors | $f_{saw} = \dfrac{1}{2\beta R_3 C \left(\dfrac{R_3}{R_{3a}} + \dfrac{R_3}{R_{3b}}\right)}$ | Hertz (Hz) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical constraint on table syntax:** All absolute-value or "divides" notations in formulas use `\vert` or `\mid` (not the bare pipe `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)** — Time-base generators use sawtooth waveforms for horizontal beam sweep.
- **Function generators** in laboratories — produce sine, square, triangular, and sawtooth outputs.
- **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** circuits in power electronics.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs)** — ramp-type ADCs use sawtooth comparison.
- **TV deflection circuits** — sawtooth drives horizontal/vertical scanning.
- **Music synthesizers** — triangular and sawtooth are fundamental audio waveforms (rich in odd/even harmonics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Threshold Voltages (Schmitt Trigger Analysis)

**Given:** An inverting Schmitt trigger with resistors $R_1$ (input) and $R_2$ (feedback from output to non-inverting terminal). The op-amp saturates at $\pm V_{sat}$.

**Step 1:** Apply superposition at the non-inverting input $V^+$ of op-amp A1.

$$V^+ = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sq} + R_2 \cdot V_{tri}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2:** At the switching instant, the comparator's two inputs are equal: $V^+ = V^- = V_{tri}$ (since the inverting input is directly connected to the triangular output in the standard function generator configuration).

$$V_{tri} = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sq} + R_2 \cdot V_{tri}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $V_{tri}$ — this is the threshold value at which switching occurs.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tri}(R_1 + R_2) &= R_1 \cdot V_{sq} + R_2 \cdot V_{tri} \\
V_{tri} \cdot R_1 + V_{tri} \cdot R_2 &= R_1 \cdot V_{sq} + R_2 \cdot V_{tri} \\
V_{tri} \cdot R_1 &= R_1 \cdot V_{sq} \\
V_{tri} &= V_{sq}
\end{aligned}
$$

This result is for the *non-inverting* configuration. For the **standard inverting Schmitt** used in the function generator (where the **triangular wave goes to the non-inverting input** through $R_1$ and the output $V_{sq}$ also feeds the same node through $R_2$):

$$V^+ = \frac{R_1 \cdot V_{sq} + R_2 \cdot V_{tri}}{R_1 + R_2}$$

At the switching moment, the inverting input is at the **same potential** as the non-inverting input (high-gain comparator saturates), so the threshold is reached when $V_{tri} = \pm \beta \cdot V_{sat}$:

$$V_{TH} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

$$V_{TL} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time Period of the Triangular Wave

**Given:** The integrator output is a linear ramp. When $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$, the output decreases with slope:

$$\frac{dV_{tri}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C}$$

**Step 1:** The triangular wave must traverse from $V_{TH}$ to $V_{TL}$ during the first half-period.

$$\Delta V = V_{TL} - V_{TH} = -\beta V_{sat} - (+\beta V_{sat}) = -2\beta V_{sat}$$

**Step 2:** Time taken for the ramp:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T}{2} &= \frac{\Delta V}{\text{slope}} = \frac{-2\beta V_{sat}}{-V_{sat}/(R_3 C)} \\
\frac{T}{2} &= \frac{2\beta V_{sat} \cdot R_3 C}{V_{sat}} \\
\frac{T}{2} &= 2\beta R_3 C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Full time period:

$$T = 4\beta R_3 C = \frac{4 R_2 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4:** Frequency of triangular oscillation:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_2 R_3 C}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sawtooth Waveform Period

For a sawtooth, the integrator uses **two separate input resistors** $R_{3a}$ and $R_{3b}$ (one for charging via a diode during positive half, and one for discharging during negative half). The two half-periods are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rise} &= 2\beta R_{3a} C \\
T_{fall} &= 2\beta R_{3b} C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total period:**

$$T_{saw} = T_{rise} + T_{fall} = 2\beta C (R_{3a} + R_{3b})$$

**Sawtooth frequency:**

$$\boxed{f_{saw} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2 R_2 C (R_{3a} + R_{3b})}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary

| Feature | Triangular Wave Generator | Sawtooth Wave Generator |
|---|---|---|
| **Waveform shape** | Symmetric (equal rise & fall) | Asymmetric (one direction is much faster) |
| **Triggering element** | Single Schmitt + symmetric integrator | Schmitt + integrator with **unequal input resistors** |
| **Rise time vs Fall time** | $T_r = T_f$ | $T_r \neq T_f$ |
| **Integrator resistors** | Single $R_3$ | Two: $R_{3a}$ and $R_{3b}$ (with diodes) |
| **Frequency formula** | $f = \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{4R_2 R_3 C}$ | $f_{saw} = \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{2R_2 C(R_{3a}+R_{3b})}$ |
| **Primary application** | Function generators, test equipment | CRO time-base, TV sweep, PWM |
| **Amplitude control** | $V_{opp} = 2\beta V_{sat}$ | $V_{opp} = 2\beta V_{sat}$ (same) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

> **Problem:** A triangular wave generator uses $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.01\ \mu\text{F}$. The op-amp saturates at $\pm 13\text{ V}$. Find (a) the peak-to-peak amplitude of the triangular wave, and (b) the frequency of oscillation.

**Solution (a) — Amplitude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{20}{10 + 20} = \frac{2}{3} \\
V_{TH} &= +\beta V_{sat} = +\frac{2}{3} \times 13 = +8.67\text{ V} \\
V_{TL} &= -\beta V_{sat} = -\frac{2}{3} \times 13 = -8.67\text{ V} \\
V_{opp} &= V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 2 \times 8.67 = 17.33\text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b) — Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 4\beta R_3 C = 4 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 5 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} \\
T &= \frac{8}{3} \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 13.33 \times 10^{-5}\text{ s} = 133.3\ \mu\text{s} \\
f &= \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{1.333 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 7.5\text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Function Generator (Triangular Waveform)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **closed-loop interaction** between the Schmitt trigger, integrator, and feedback path:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["OP-AMP A1 SCHMITT TRIGGER COMPARATOR"] -->|Square Wave Vsq| nodeB["OP-AMP A2 MILLER INTEGRATOR R3 C"]
    nodeB -->|Triangular Wave Vtri| nodeC["OUTPUT TERMINAL"]
    nodeC -->|Feedback| nodeD["RESISTOR R2"]
    nodeD -->|To Non-Inverting Input| nodeA
    nodeE["DC OFFSET Vref"] -->|Optional Input| nodeB
    nodeF["RESISTOR R1"] -->|From Vtri to Non-Inverting Input| nodeA
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **Schmitt Trigger** (Op-Amp A1) produces a **square wave** $V_{sq}$ at its output.
- The square wave is fed into the **Integrator** (Op-Amp A2 with $R_3$, $C$).
- The integrator integrates the square wave, producing a **triangular wave** $V_{tri}$.
- A portion of $V_{tri}$ is fed back to the non-inverting input of A1 through $R_2$, controlling the switching thresholds.
- A small DC offset (optional) can be added at the integrator input for symmetry adjustment.

## 4.2 Circuit Schematic — Triangular Wave Generator (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1["STAGE 1 SCHMITT TRIGGER"]
        opa1["OP-AMP 741 A1"]
        r1a["R1 10K INPUT"]
        r2a["R2 20K FEEDBACK"]
        r1a --> opa1
        r2a --> opa1
    end
    subgraph Stage2["STAGE 2 INTEGRATOR"]
        opa2["OP-AMP 741 A2"]
        r3a["R3 5K INPUT"]
        ca["C 0.01 uF FEEDBACK"]
        r3a --> opa2
        ca --> opa2
    end
    opa1 -->|"Vsq square wave"| r3a
    opa2 -->|"Vtri triangular output"| triOut["OUTPUT Vtri"]
    triOut -->|"Feedback to A1"| r2a
    triOut -->|"Feedback to A1"| r1a
```

## 4.3 Waveform Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ws["TIME AXIS"] --> p1["T = 0"]
    p1 --> p2["T = T rise square high Vtri falls linearly"]
    p2 --> p3["T = T over 2 square low Vtri rises linearly"]
    p3 --> p4["T = T full cycle repeats"]
```

**Waveform Description:**
- **Interval $0$ to $T/2$:** $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$ → $V_{tri}$ ramps down from $+V_{TH}$ to $-V_{TL}$.
- **Interval $T/2$ to $T$:** $V_{sq} = -V_{sat}$ → $V_{tri}$ ramps up from $-V_{TL}$ to $+V_{TH}$.

## 4.4 Sawtooth Variant — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["SCHMITT TRIGGER A1"] -->|Vsq| nodeS2["DIODE D1 ANODE TO A2 INPUT"]
    nodeS2 -->|Forward biased during Vsq positive| nodeS3["R3a CHARGING RESISTOR"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeI["INTEGRATOR A2 WITH C"]
    nodeS4["DIODE D2 FOR DISCHARGE PATH"] --> nodeS5["R3b DISCHARGE RESISTOR"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeOut["Vtri SAWTOOTH OUTPUT"]
    nodeOut -->|"Feedback"| nodeS1
```

**Key Difference from Triangular Generator:**
- **Diode D1** conducts when $V_{sq} > 0$ → charging through $R_{3a}$ (slow ramp).
- **Diode D2** conducts when $V_{sq} < 0$ → discharging through $R_{3b}$ (fast ramp, or vice versa).
- The asymmetry $R_{3a} \gg R_{3b}$ (or vice versa) produces the sawtooth shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`nodeA`, `opa1`, `r1a`, etc.) — none of the reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) are used as standalone identifiers. All node labels are quoted as **plain uppercase text** with no embedded markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
>
> **Q1.** Define a triangular waveform generator. List the two op-amp stages used to construct it.

**Model Answer:**
A triangular waveform generator is a non-sinusoidal oscillator that produces a periodic output voltage whose amplitude varies linearly with time between two equal and opposite peak values. It is constructed using:
1. **Schmitt Trigger (Regenerative Comparator)** — produces the square wave.
2. **Op-amp Integrator (Miller Integrator)** — integrates the square wave to produce the triangular waveform.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
>
> **Q2.** What is the role of positive feedback in the Schmitt trigger used inside a triangular wave generator?

**Model Answer:**
Positive feedback in the Schmitt trigger provides **hysteresis**, producing two distinct threshold voltages ($V_{TH}$ and $V_{TL}$). This ensures the comparator output switches rapidly between $+\!V_{sat}$ and $-\!V_{sat}$ depending on whether the triangular input is above or below the thresholds. Without positive feedback, the comparator would behave as a linear amplifier and the system would not oscillate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a triangular waveform generator using op-amps. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A triangular wave generator uses $R_1 = 15\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 30\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_3 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$. If the op-amp saturation voltage is $\pm 14\text{ V}$, calculate (i) the peak-to-peak amplitude of the triangular wave, and (ii) the frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4.1)

**Operation:**
The function generator consists of two op-amp stages:
- **Op-Amp A1** acts as a **Schmitt trigger** (inverting comparator with positive feedback). It produces a **square wave** $V_{sq}$ at its output that alternates between $+V_{sat}$ and $-V_{sat}$.
- **Op-Amp A2** is configured as a **Miller integrator**. It integrates the square wave $V_{sq}$ to produce a **triangular wave** $V_{tri}$.
- The triangular wave $V_{tri}$ is fed back to the non-inverting input of A1 through resistor $R_2$. This feedback controls the switching thresholds of the Schmitt trigger.

**Switching Thresholds:**

$$V_{TH} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat} \qquad V_{TL} = -\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$$

**Working:** When $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$, the integrator output ramps downward at a rate of $V_{sat}/(R_3 C)$. When $V_{tri}$ reaches $V_{TL}$, the Schmitt trigger switches to $V_{sq} = -V_{sat}$, and the integrator ramps upward. The cycle repeats, producing a continuous triangular wave.

**Frequency Derivation:** (As shown in SECTION 3.2)

$$\boxed{f = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_2 R_3 C}}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Peak-to-peak amplitude:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{30}{15 + 30} = \frac{30}{45} = \frac{2}{3} \\
V_{TH} &= +\beta V_{sat} = \frac{2}{3} \times 14 = 9.33\text{ V} \\
V_{TL} &= -\beta V_{sat} = -9.33\text{ V} \\
V_{opp} &= V_{TH} - V_{TL} = 18.67\text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Frequency of oscillation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 4\beta R_3 C = 4 \times \frac{2}{3} \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \\
T &= \frac{8}{3} \times 10^{-3} = 2.667\text{ ms} \\
f &= \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2.667 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 375\text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme Pattern]
>
> **(a)** Explain the operation of a sawtooth waveform generator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the triangular waveform generator and sawtooth waveform generator with respect to waveform shape, integrator configuration, and frequency formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Operation:**
A sawtooth waveform generator is a modification of the triangular wave generator. The basic op-amp function generator (Schmitt + integrator) is altered so that the integrator charging and discharging paths have **unequal time constants**.

**Configuration:**
- Op-Amp A1 is the **Schmitt trigger** producing the square wave.
- Op-Amp A2 is the **integrator** but with **two input resistors** $R_{3a}$ and $R_{3b}$ in parallel, separated by **two diodes** D1 and D2.
- When $V_{sq} = +V_{sat}$, diode D1 conducts and the integrator charges through $R_{3a}$ (slow ramp, large $R_{3a}$).
- When $V_{sq} = -V_{sat}$, diode D2 conducts and the integrator discharges through $R_{3b}$ (fast ramp, small $R_{3b}$), or vice versa.

**Frequency Derivation:**

The two half-periods are:

$$T_1 = \frac{2 R_2 R_{3a} C}{R_1 + R_2} \qquad T_2 = \frac{2 R_2 R_{3b} C}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Total period:**

$$T_{saw} = T_1 + T_2 = \frac{2 R_2 C (R_{3a} + R_{3b})}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Frequency:**

$$\boxed{f_{saw} = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{2 R_2 C (R_{3a} + R_{3b})}}$$

If $R_{3b} \ll R_{3a}$, the discharge time $T_2$ is negligible, and the output closely resembles a true sawtooth (slow rise, abrupt fall).

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | Triangular Generator | Sawtooth Generator |
|---|---|---|
| **Waveform shape** | Symmetric: equal rise and fall times | Asymmetric: one half is much faster than the other |
| **Integrator configuration** | Single input resistor $R_3$ and feedback capacitor $C$ | Two input resistors $R_{3a}$ and $R_{3b}$ separated by diodes |
| **Slope symmetry** | Both slopes have equal magnitude $\vert V_{sat}/(R_3 C)\vert$ | Slopes differ: $V_{sat}/(R_{3a} C)$ and $V_{sat}/(R_{3b} C)$ |
| **Frequency formula** | $f = \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{4R_2 R_3 C}$ | $f_{saw} = \dfrac{R_1+R_2}{2 R_2 C(R_{3a}+R_{3b})}$ |
| **Common application** | Function generators, audio synthesis | CRO time-base, TV sweep, PWM modulation |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to substitute $\beta$ in the time period formula** — Students often write $T = 4 R_3 C$ directly, missing the feedback factor $\beta = R_2/(R_1 + R_2)$. **This costs 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing the sign convention** — The triangular wave's *upper* threshold is $+\beta V_{sat}$, **not** $-V_{sat}$. Many students mistakenly write the amplitude as $V_{opp} = 2V_{sat}$.
> 3. **Saturation voltage not given** — When the problem says "op-amp supply is $\pm 15$ V", students often write $V_{sat} = 15$ V directly. The board expects $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 2$ V (i.e., $\approx 13$ V for $\mu$A741), **or** an explicit statement that $V_{sat} = V_{CC}$ is assumed.
> 4. **Units not carried in numerical substitution** — Always convert $C$ from $\mu\text{F}$ to $\text{F}$ (e.g., $0.01\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}\text{ F}$) before computing $T$.
> 5. **Sawtooth formula mix-up** — Students sometimes use $f = R_1/(4 R_2 R_3 C)$ (missing the "+") or write the sawtooth period as $T = 4\beta R_3 C$ (same as triangular) instead of $T = 2\beta C(R_{3a} + R_{3b})$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Triangular & Sawtooth Waveform Generators**

- A **triangular waveform generator** = **Schmitt trigger + op-amp integrator** in a closed loop.
- The **Schmitt trigger** (Op-Amp A1) generates a **square wave** by regenerative positive feedback.
- The **integrator** (Op-Amp A2) integrates the square wave to produce a **triangular wave**.
- The triangular output is fed back to the non-inverting input of the Schmitt trigger to **control the switching thresholds**.
- **Feedback factor** is $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- **Upper threshold:** $V_{TH} = +\beta V_{sat}$; **Lower threshold:** $V_{TL} = -\beta V_{sat}$.
- **Peak-to-peak amplitude** of triangular wave: $V_{opp} = 2\beta V_{sat}$.
- **Time period:** $T = 4\beta R_3 C = \dfrac{4 R_2 R_3 C}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- **Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{4 R_2 R_3 C}$ — *board-favorite formula*.
- **Sawtooth waveform generator** is obtained by using **unequal input resistors** ($R_{3a}$, $R_{3b}$) in the integrator stage, separated by **diodes**.
- **Sawtooth frequency:** $f_{saw} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{2 R_2 C (R_{3a} + R_{3b})}$.
- **Applications of sawtooth:** CRO time-base generator, TV deflection circuits, ramp-type ADC, PWM modulators.
- **Applications of triangular:** Function/arbitrary waveform generators, audio synthesizers, V-F converters.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis:** Be prepared to draw the block diagram, derive the frequency formula, and solve numerical problems involving $\beta$, $V_{sat}$, $T$, and $f$.
- **Key assumption:** Op-amp A1 operates in the **saturation region** (Schmitt trigger), and Op-amp A2 operates in the **linear region** (integrator). Both assumptions are *non-negotiable* for the derivations to be valid.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Multivibrator?

A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic switching circuit that uses two active devices (BJTs, FETs, or op-amps) cross-coupled by resistors and/or capacitors to produce a non-sinusoidal output waveform (square, rectangular, or pulse).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):** A multivibrator is a regenerative switching circuit whose active devices (transistors) operate alternately between cutoff and saturation, producing sharp-edged waveforms. It relies on positive feedback to achieve bistable, monostable, or astable behavior.

### 1.2 The Three Families of Multivibrators

| Type | Stable States | Trigger Required? | Typical Output |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Bistable** (Flip-Flop) | 2 stable | Yes (Set / Reset) | Latched DC level |
| **Monostable** (One-Shot) | 1 stable + 1 quasi-stable | Yes (single trigger) | Single pulse |
| **Astable** (Free-Running) | 0 stable + 2 quasi-stable | **No** | Continuous square wave |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two Buckets" Picture

Imagine two buckets connected by siphons that pour into each other. The astable multivibrator is like a **seesaw**: no matter where it starts, it cannot rest — it flips endlessly. The monostable is like a **spring-loaded door**: it sits comfortably in one closed position (stable), but a tap (trigger) kicks it open briefly, after which it automatically swings shut again.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The word *"vibrator"* is historical — it does NOT produce mechanical vibration. It simply means the output voltage alternates (vibrates) between two levels.

### 1.4 The Role of the RC Time Constant

The **time constant τ = RC** controls how fast the base voltage of the OFF transistor can climb from a deep negative value back up to the turn-ON threshold (≈ 0 V for a BJT with V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V). This single mechanism defines the **pulse width** in monostable and **half-period** in astable circuits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Collector-Coupled Astable Multivibrator — Operating Principle

**Circuit topology:** Two identical NPN transistor stages (Q1 and Q2) with:
- Collector resistor **R_C** on each collector.
- Base resistor **R** at each base (tied to V_CC).
- Cross-coupling **capacitors C1, C2** (collector of one → base of the other).

> [!NOTE]
> **No DC path exists between the collector of one transistor and the base of the other.** The coupling is purely capacitive, which is why the base can swing *below* 0 V during transitions.

### 2.2 Two Quasi-Stable States (Astable)

#### State A: Q1 OFF, Q2 ON
- V_C1 = V_CC, V_C2 = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0
- V_B2 = V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V
- V_B1 < 0 V (held below cutoff by charge on C1)
- C1 is charging through R; C2 holds the ON-state of Q2.

#### State B: Q1 ON, Q2 OFF
- V_C2 = V_CC, V_C1 = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0
- V_B1 = V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V
- V_B2 < 0 V (held below cutoff by charge on C2)
- C2 is charging through R; C1 holds the ON-state of Q1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The state is **quasi-stable** because the moment V_B reaches the turn-ON threshold, the positive feedback forces the OPPOSITE transistor to switch, and the cycle repeats.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Half-period T₁ (Q1 OFF) | $T_1 = R_1 C_1 \ln 2 = 0.693\,R_1 C_1$ | seconds; R in Ω, C in F |
| 2 | Half-period T₂ (Q2 OFF) | $T_2 = R_2 C_2 \ln 2 = 0.693\,R_2 C_2$ | seconds |
| 3 | Total period (astable) | $T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693\,(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$ | seconds |
| 4 | Frequency of oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{R_1C_1 + R_2C_2}$ | Hertz |
| 5 | Symmetric astable (R₁ = R₂ = R, C₁ = C₂ = C) | $T = 1.386\,RC, \quad f = \dfrac{0.722}{RC}$ | — |
| 6 | Duty cycle | $D = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2} \times 100\,\%$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | Monostable pulse width | $T_W = R_B\,C\,\ln 2 = 0.693\,R_B C$ | seconds |
| 8 | Monostable recovery time | $T_r \approx 5\,R_C\,C$ | seconds (full reset) |
| 9 | Peak base reverse voltage | $V_{BB(\text{peak})} \approx -V_{CC}$ | volts (≈ supply) |
| 10 | Trigger pulse width | $t_{tr} \ll T_W$ | to avoid retriggering |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the magic number 0.693?** It is simply $\ln 2 = 0.6931...$, arising from the exponential charging of an RC network that must recover from $-V_{CC}$ to $0$ (a 2:1 voltage ratio), because $e^{-0.693} = 0.5$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Astable** → Clock generators for digital logic, ring counters, LED flashers, PWM modulators, tone-burst generators in instrumentation, relaxation oscillator front-end.
- **Monostable** → Pulse-stretching in radar, debouncing mechanical switches, missing-pulse detectors, fixed-width sampling gates, time-delay relays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Frequency of Astable Multivibrator

We will derive the time T₁ during which Q1 is OFF and Q2 is ON (quasi-stable state A).

**Step 1 — Identify the state at the instant of switching.**
At t = 0, Q1 just turned OFF, Q2 just turned ON.

- V_C2 has just dropped from V_CC to V_CE(sat) ≈ 0 V.
- V_B1 was at V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V just before, but the **capacitor C1 cannot change its voltage instantaneously**, so V_B1 falls by the same step that occurred at V_C2.
- Step magnitude = V_CC − V_CE(sat) ≈ V_CC.
- Therefore, immediately after switching: V_B1(0⁺) = V_BE(sat) − V_CC ≈ −V_CC.

**Step 2 — Charging equation of V_B1.**
V_B1 is the voltage across the base, but the capacitor C1 is charging through R₁ from −V_CC toward +V_CC. The Thevenin equivalent seen at the base is V_CC through R₁ (since the other end of R₁ is tied to V_CC and the other plate of C1 is at V_C2 ≈ 0 V which is now constant).

The general charging law from initial value V_i to final value V_f is:

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_f - (V_f - V_i)\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

Here, V_f = V_CC, V_i = −V_CC, τ = R₁C₁.

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (-V_{CC}))\,e^{-t/(R_1C_1)}$$

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\,e^{-t/(R_1C_1)}$$

**Step 3 — Define the switching condition.**
Q1 turns ON when V_B1 reaches the turn-ON threshold. For a silicon BJT this is approximately V_BE(cut-in) ≈ 0 V (often taken as 0 for algebraic simplicity in KTU problems, since V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V but the capacitor jumps by V_CC, so the very next turn-on is at 0 V crossing).

Setting V_B1(T₁) = 0:

$$0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\,e^{-T_1/(R_1C_1)}$$

$$2V_{CC}\,e^{-T_1/(R_1C_1)} = V_{CC}$$

$$e^{-T_1/(R_1C_1)} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\dfrac{T_1}{R_1 C_1} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$

$$\boxed{\,T_1 = R_1 C_1 \ln 2 = 0.693\,R_1 C_1\,}$$

**Step 4 — By symmetry, the other half-period is:**

$$T_2 = R_2 C_2 \ln 2 = 0.693\,R_2 C_2$$

**Step 5 — Total period and frequency.**

$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693\,(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$$

$$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{0.693\,(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)} = \dfrac{1.44}{R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **1.44** is memorizable for KTU exams: $f \approx \dfrac{1.44}{R_1C_1 + R_2C_2}$. For a symmetric design it reduces to $f = \dfrac{0.722}{RC}$.

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Pulse Width of Monostable Multivibrator

**Step 1 — Stable state identification.**
Assume Q1 is normally ON (saturated) and Q2 is normally OFF. This is the **stable state** that the circuit returns to automatically.

- V_C1 = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V (taken as 0 for simplicity in algebraic work)
- V_B1 = V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V (taken as 0 for simplicity)
- V_C2 = V_CC (Q2 is OFF, no collector current)
- V_B2 < 0 V (held below cutoff by reverse bias from −V_BB)

The capacitor C (between collector of Q2 and base of Q1) is fully charged, with the voltage across it being:

$$V_C = V_{C2} - V_{B1} = V_{CC} - V_{BE(\text{sat})} \approx V_{CC}$$

**Step 2 — Apply a negative trigger pulse to Q2's base.**
Q2 turns ON. Its collector voltage falls from V_CC to V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.

**Step 3 — Capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously.**
The base of Q1 is the other plate of C. Since V_C across C must remain ≈ V_CC, the drop in V_C2 pulls V_B1 down by the same amount:

$$V_{B1}(0^+) = V_{BE(\text{sat})} - V_{CC} \approx -V_{CC}$$

This drives Q1 into **deep cutoff** — Q1 is now OFF, V_C1 = V_CC. The circuit is in its **quasi-stable state**.

**Step 4 — Recovery of V_B1.**
V_B1 now charges through R_B (the base resistor of Q1) from −V_CC toward +V_CC. The time constant is τ = R_B C.

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (-V_{CC}))\,e^{-t/(R_B C)} = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\,e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

**Step 5 — Q1 turns back ON.**
The quasi-stable state ends when V_B1 reaches the turn-ON threshold (≈ 0 V):

$$0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\,e^{-T_W/(R_B C)}$$

$$e^{-T_W/(R_B C)} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_W = R_B C \ln 2 = 0.693\,R_B C\,}$$

**Step 6 — Recovery (reset) time.**
After Q1 turns back ON, V_C1 falls from V_CC to V_CE(sat) ≈ 0. The capacitor must recharge through R_C (the collector resistor of Q2's stage that connects to the base of Q1 — careful: it is the collector resistor of the transistor whose collector is connected to the capacitor). Standard KTU result:

$$T_r \approx 5\,R_C C \quad \text{(for 99.3\% full recovery)}$$

---

### 3.3 KTU Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Astable | Monostable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stable states | 0 | 1 |
| Quasi-stable states | 2 | 1 |
| Trigger needed? | No | Yes (negative pulse) |
| Output waveform | Continuous square wave | Single pulse of width T_W |
| Period / Width | $T = 0.693(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$ | $T_W = 0.693\,R_B C$ |
| Frequency / Rate | $f = 1.44/(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$ | One pulse per trigger |
| Used as | Oscillator, clock | Delay, pulse-stretcher |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Astable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC["DC SUPPLY VCC"]
    RC1["COLLECTOR RESISTOR RC1"]:::res
    RC2["COLLECTOR RESISTOR RC2"]:::res
    R1["BASE RESISTOR R1"]:::res
    R2["BASE RESISTOR R2"]:::res
    Q1["TRANSISTOR Q1"]:::tr
    Q2["TRANSISTOR Q2"]:::tr
    C1["COUPLING CAPACITOR C1"]:::cap
    C2["COUPLING CAPACITOR C2"]:::cap
    OUT1["OUTPUT V C1"]:::out
    OUT2["OUTPUT V C2"]:::out

    VCC --- RC1
    VCC --- RC2
    VCC --- R1
    VCC --- R2

    RC1 --- Q1
    Q1 --- OUT1
    OUT1 --- C1
    C1 --- R1

    RC2 --- Q2
    Q2 --- OUT2
    OUT2 --- C2
    C2 --- R2

    Q2 -.->|Collector| C1
    Q1 -.->|Collector| C2

    classDef res fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a87c00,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000
    classDef tr fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c9184a,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Astable Waveform Timing Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CYCLE START"] --> B["Q1 OFF  Q2 ON"]
    B -->|"Duration T1 = 0.693 R1 C1"| C["Q1 ON  Q2 OFF"]
    C -->|"Duration T2 = 0.693 R2 C2"| B
    B --> D["OUTPUT V C1 RECTANGULAR WAVE"]
    C --> E["OUTPUT V C2 INVERTED RECTANGULAR WAVE"]
    D --> F["REPEATS CONTINUOUSLY  F = 1.44  R1C1 PLUS R2C2"]
    E --> F
```

### 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    VS["VCC SUPPLY"]:::res
    VBB["NEGATIVE BIAS -VBB"]:::res
    RQ1["RC1 COLLECTOR RESISTOR"]:::res
    RQ2["RC2 COLLECTOR RESISTOR"]:::res
    RB1["R BASE1 BIAS TO Q1"]:::res
    RB2["R BASE2 BIAS TO Q2"]:::res
    QR["DC COUPLING RESISTOR R"]:::res
    TR1["TRANSISTOR Q1 NORMALLY ON"]:::tr
    TR2["TRANSISTOR Q2 NORMALLY OFF"]:::tr
    CAP["TIMING CAPACITOR C"]:::cap
    TRG["NEGATIVE TRIGGER PULSE"]:::inp
    OUTM["OUTPUT PULSE WIDTH 0.693 RB C"]:::out

    VS --- RQ1
    VS --- RQ2
    VS --- RB1
    VBB --- RB2
    VS --- RB1

    RQ1 --- TR1
    TR1 --- CAP
    CAP --- TR2
    RQ2 --- TR2

    RB1 --- TR1
    TR1 --- QR
    QR --- TR2
    RB2 --- TR2

    TR1 --> OUTM
    TRG --> TR2

    classDef res fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a87c00,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#0077b6,color:#000
    classDef tr fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c9184a,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000
    classDef inp fill:#e0aaff,stroke:#5a189a,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Monostable Waveform State Transition

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["STABLE STATE  Q1 ON  Q2 OFF"] -->|"TRIGGER APPLIED"| S2
    S2["QUASI STABLE STATE  Q1 OFF  Q2 ON  DURATION 0.693 RB C"] -->|"CAPACITOR CHARGES BACK"| S3
    S3["V B1 CROSSES 0 V  Q1 TURNS ON"] -->|"RECOVERY TIME APPROX 5 RC C"| S1
    S1 --> O1["OUTPUT V C1 NORMALLY LOW  RISES TO VCC DURING PULSE"]
    S2 --> O2["OUTPUT V C2 NORMALLY VCC  FALLS TO 0 DURING PULSE"]
    S3 --> O3["SINGLE OUTPUT PULSE PRODUCED"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the timing diagram:** In an astable, V_C1 and V_C2 are **180° out of phase** square waves. In a monostable, V_C2 produces a single negative-going pulse whose width is set by the RC product, while V_C1 produces a single positive-going pulse of the same width.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define a multivibrator. List the three basic types of multivibrators and state one application of each.

**Model Answer:**
A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative switching circuit that employs two active devices with positive feedback, producing non-sinusoidal waveforms (square, rectangular, or pulse).

The three basic types are:
1. **Bistable Multivibrator** — has two stable states. Application: binary memory element (flip-flop), storage register.
2. **Monostable Multivibrator** — has one stable state and one quasi-stable state. Application: pulse-stretcher, missing-pulse detector.
3. **Astable Multivibrator** — has two quasi-stable states and no stable state. Application: square-wave clock generator, relaxation oscillator.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for list, 1 for applications)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is an astable multivibrator called a "free-running" multivibrator? What determines its frequency of oscillation?

**Model Answer:**
An astable multivibrator is called **free-running** because it has **no stable state** — it switches continuously and automatically between its two quasi-stable states without requiring any external trigger pulse. Once switched on, it oscillates indefinitely.

The **frequency of oscillation** is determined by the RC time constants of the two base circuits. For an asymmetric circuit:

$$f = \dfrac{1.44}{R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2}$$

For a symmetric design (R₁ = R₂ = R, C₁ = C₂ = C):

$$f = \dfrac{0.722}{RC}$$

The factor $\ln 2 = 0.693$ appears because the base voltage must recover from $-V_{CC}$ to $0$ (a 2:1 voltage ratio).

*(3 marks — 1 for free-running reason, 2 for frequency formula with explanation)*

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q(a).** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a collector-coupled astable multivibrator. Derive the expression for its frequency of oscillation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** Two NPN transistors Q1 and Q2 cross-coupled via capacitors C1 and C2. Each collector is tied to V_CC through R_C, and each base is tied to V_CC through R. There is **no external triggering** — the circuit is self-oscillating.

**Working — Quasi-stable states:**

**State I (0 < t < T₁): Q1 OFF, Q2 ON**
- V_C1 = V_CC, V_C2 = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0
- V_B2 = V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V
- V_B1 is held at a negative value by the charge on C1, and it exponentially charges toward V_CC through R₁.
- When V_B1 reaches ~0 V, Q1 turns ON, and the positive feedback drives Q2 OFF.

**State II (T₁ < t < T₂): Q1 ON, Q2 OFF**
- V_C2 = V_CC, V_C1 = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0
- V_B1 = V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V
- V_B2 is now held negative and charges through R₂.
- When V_B2 reaches ~0 V, the cycle reverses.

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation.)

During State I, V_B1 charges from −V_CC toward V_CC through R₁C₁:

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\,e^{-t/(R_1 C_1)}$$

Setting V_B1 = 0 at t = T₁:

$$T_1 = 0.693\,R_1 C_1$$

By symmetry:

$$T_2 = 0.693\,R_2 C_2$$

Total period:

$$T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693\,(R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2)$$

Frequency:

$$\boxed{\,f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{R_1 C_1 + R_2 C_2}\,}$$

*(7 marks: 2 circuit + working, 4 derivation, 1 final expression)*

---

**Q(b).** Design an astable multivibrator using BJT to generate a square wave of frequency **f = 5 kHz** with a **duty cycle of 60%**. Assume V_CC = 12 V, V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V, V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the formulas:**

$$T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{5000} = 0.2\text{ ms} = 200\,\mu s$$

Duty cycle D = T₁/T = 0.60 → T₁ = 120 μs (Q1 OFF), T₂ = 80 μs (Q2 OFF).

**Step 2 — Apply the half-period formulas:**

$$T_1 = 0.693\,R_1 C_1 \Rightarrow R_1 C_1 = \dfrac{120\,\mu s}{0.693} = 173.16\,\mu s$$

$$T_2 = 0.693\,R_2 C_2 \Rightarrow R_2 C_2 = \dfrac{80\,\mu s}{0.693} = 115.44\,\mu s$$

**Step 3 — Choose convenient capacitor values:**

Let C₁ = C₂ = C = 0.01 μF = 10 nF.

$$R_1 = \dfrac{173.16\,\mu s}{0.01\,\mu F} = 17.32\text{ k}\Omega \approx 17.3\text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_2 = \dfrac{115.44\,\mu s}{0.01\,\mu F} = 11.54\text{ k}\Omega \approx 11.5\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Choose collector resistors (saturated design rule):**
A safe value is R_C ≤ β·R/(10) to ensure saturation. For β = 50, choose R_C = 1 kΩ.

**Final Design Values:**
- R_C1 = R_C2 = 1 kΩ
- R₁ = 17.3 kΩ, C₁ = 0.01 μF
- R₂ = 11.5 kΩ, C₂ = 0.01 μF
- V_CC = 12 V

**Verification:** $f = 1.44 / (R_1C_1 + R_2C_2) = 1.44 / (173 + 115)\,\mu s = 1.44 / 288\,\mu s = 5000$ Hz ✓

*(7 marks: 1 for T calculation, 2 for RC product, 2 for component choice, 1 for verification, 1 for collector resistor selection)*

---

#### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q(a).** Explain the working of a collector-coupled monostable multivibrator with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the pulse width. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** Q1 has DC base bias (R_B to V_CC) so it normally operates in saturation. Q2 has its base reverse-biased through R_B2 connected to −V_BB, so it is normally in cutoff. The collector of Q2 is AC-coupled to the base of Q1 via timing capacitor C. The collector of Q1 is DC-coupled to the base of Q2 through resistor R.

**Working:**

**Stable State (Q1 ON, Q2 OFF):**
- V_C1 = V_CE(sat) ≈ 0
- V_B1 = V_BE(sat) ≈ 0.7 V
- V_C2 = V_CC
- V_B2 ≈ 0 V (pulled down by V_C1 through R) → Q2 remains OFF
- Capacitor C charges to V_C ≈ V_CC.

**Triggering (negative pulse at Q2 base):** Q2 turns ON; V_C2 drops from V_CC to ≈ 0. Since V_C across C cannot change instantaneously, V_B1 falls from +0.7 V to ≈ −V_CC, driving Q1 into cutoff.

**Quasi-stable State (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON):**
- V_C1 = V_CC, V_C2 ≈ 0
- V_B1 charges from −V_CC toward V_CC through R_B with time constant R_B C.
- When V_B1 reaches ≈ 0 V, Q1 turns ON, regenerative action drives Q2 back OFF — circuit returns to stable state.

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.2 for full derivation.)

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\,e^{-t/(R_B C)}$$

Setting V_B1 = 0 at t = T_W:

$$\boxed{\,T_W = R_B C \ln 2 = 0.693\,R_B C\,}$$

*(7 marks: 2 circuit, 2 stable-state explanation, 2 derivation, 1 final expression)*

---

**Q(b).** Compare astable and monostable multivibrators on the basis of: (i) number of stable states, (ii) trigger requirement, (iii) output waveform, (iv) expression for period/pulse width, (v) applications. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Astable | Monostable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Stable states | **Zero** — only quasi-stable | **One** stable + one quasi-stable |
| (ii) Trigger | **Not required** — self-oscillating | **Required** — single trigger pulse initiates one cycle |
| (iii) Output | Continuous rectangular/square wave (no DC component zero) | Single pulse of fixed width per trigger |
| (iv) Timing formula | $T = 0.693\,(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$ | $T_W = 0.693\,R_B C$ |
| (v) Applications | Clock generator, square-wave oscillator, VCO, LED flasher, PWM | Pulse-stretcher, missing-pulse detector, time-delay generator, debouncing |
| Recovery | Automatic, instantaneous at switch | Needs $T_r \approx 5\,R_C C$ to recharge capacitor |
| Coupling | Both sides use capacitors | One side capacitor, one side resistor |

*(7 marks — 1 mark per meaningful comparison row, 2 marks for diagram sketches of typical waveforms if drawn)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing V_BE(sat) with the switching threshold.** KTU problems often tell you to *take the switching threshold as 0 V* for simplicity, even though V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V. Mixing the two gives the wrong factor (0.693 becomes something else). Read the question's assumption line carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the recovery.** Many students write $V_B(t) = V_{CC} - V_{CC} e^{-t/RC}$, but the initial value is $-V_{CC}$, not $0$, so the difference is $2V_{CC}$. Missing this gives a wrong time constant interpretation.
> 3. **Astable: do not write f = 1/RC.** The correct formula has 0.693 inside. Memorize: $T = 0.693(R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$ and $f = 1.44 / (R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$.
> 4. **Monostable recovery time is NOT the pulse width.** Recovery is when the capacitor recharges through R_C *after* the pulse ends. Writing $T_W = 5R_C C$ is a common mistake.
> 5. **Drawing the base waveform upside-down.** The base voltage goes *negative* during the OFF half-cycle, not positive. Many students draw it as a positive ramp.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Astable = Free-running oscillator** with no stable state; emits continuous square wave at $f = 1.44 / (R_1C_1 + R_2C_2)$.
- ✅ **Monostable = One-shot** with one stable state; emits a single pulse of width $T_W = 0.693 R_B C$ on receiving a trigger.
- ✅ The magic number **0.693** comes from $\ln 2$, which arises because base voltage must recover from $-V_{CC}$ to $0$ (a 2:1 ratio).
- ✅ Cross-coupling in astable is **purely capacitive** (no DC path) — this is what allows the base to swing negative.
- ✅ Monostable uses **one capacitive coupling and one resistive coupling** — this asymmetry is what gives it one stable state.
- ✅ Recovery time of monostable $\approx 5 R_C C$ (time for capacitor to fully recharge through collector resistor).
- ✅ Duty cycle of astable = $T_1/(T_1+T_2)$; symmetric design = 50% duty cycle.
- ✅ Both circuits rely on **positive feedback through cross-coupling** and the **saturation/cutoff switching** of BJTs.
- ✅ KTU exam tip: When designing an astable, always choose R_C such that $R_C \le \beta_{min} \cdot R / 10$ to guarantee saturation.
- ✅ In KTU numerical problems, if the question says "neglect V_BE(sat)," use the 0-V threshold; if it specifies V_BE(sat) = 0.7 V, the threshold is 0.7 V and the initial jump is $V_{CC} - 0.7$ V (a small change but may matter for precision).
- ✅ Astable is also called a **relaxation oscillator**; monostable is also called a **univibrator** or **one-shot multivibrator**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Convertors: DAC - Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Convertors: DAC - Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of DAC Specifications

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a mixed-signal device that reconstructs a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog output proportional to an applied digital input code. The **specifications of a DAC** constitute the quantitative and qualitative metrics that characterize the *fidelity*, *speed*, *precision*, and *robustness* of the conversion process. These specifications allow the designer to select, compare, and qualify a DAC for a given signal-chain application.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBMRT304 – Analog Electronics)**, the specifications of a DAC define the boundary between the *ideal* mathematical model and the *practical* behavior of a real monolithic converter. They encompass three primary families:

1. **Static (DC) Specifications** – accuracy, linearity, offset, gain, and monotonicity.
2. **Dynamic (AC) Specifications** – settling time, slew rate, glitch impulse, SNR, SFDR, and ENOB.
3. **System-Level Specifications** – resolution, reference tolerance, power dissipation, and interface format.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 4):**
> A student is expected to *define, derive, and quantitatively evaluate* the static and dynamic specifications of a DAC, and to relate them to underlying converter architectures (Binary-Weighted, R-2R Ladder, Thermometer, and Sigma-Delta).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Digital Water Tap

Imagine a **digital water tap** mounted on a wall. The tap handle is marked with discrete integer positions from `0` (fully closed) to `15` (fully open) for a 4-bit tap. Now consider these real-world features:

| Real-World Feature | Equivalent DAC Specification |
|---|---|
| Number of distinct openings (`0` to `15`) | **Resolution (N bits)** |
| How accurately the *full* opening delivers the promised flow | **Full-Scale Error / Gain Error** |
| The faucet dripping slightly even when at `0` | **Offset Error** |
| Each step (`n` to `n+1`) adding the *same* amount of water | **Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)** |
| Total water delivered matching the dial reading | **Integral Non-Linearity (INL)** |
| The tap never delivering *less* water when turned further | **Monotonicity** |
| Time taken to settle after a sudden change | **Settling Time** |
| Sudden splash when flipping between `0111` and `1000` | **Glitch Impulse** |
| Audible hissing when water flows | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** |

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy reinforces the central idea: every DAC specification is a *deviation from the ideal linear staircase transfer curve* $V_{out} = V_{FS} \cdot \frac{D}{2^N}$.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

The most commonly evaluated static and dynamic metrics are:

- **Resolution (N)**: The number of distinguishable output levels, equal to $2^N$ discrete steps.
- **Step Size (LSB)**: $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1}$ (unipolar, end-point scaling).
- **Full-Scale Range ($V_{FS}$)**: The nominal analog span between the minimum and maximum output codes.
- **Sampling/Update Rate ($f_s$)**: The rate at which the DAC accepts a new digital word.
- **Quantization Noise (RMS)**: $\sigma_q = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Value:** A canonical converter reference is **2.500 V** or **5.000 V**, but DACs commonly support $V_{REF}$ in the range **1.8 V – 36 V** depending on architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static (DC) Specifications – Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Resolution
Resolution is the **smallest change in the analog output** that corresponds to a one-LSB change in the digital input code. It is determined entirely by the bit-width and the reference span.

$$\text{Resolution} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N} = 1 \text{ LSB}$$

For an N-bit DAC, the input word ranges from `0` to `(2^N - 1)`, giving $2^N$ distinct analog levels.

### 2.1.2 Quantization Error
Quantization is the inherent rounding between the ideal continuous input and its discrete staircase reconstruction. The maximum quantization error is bounded by $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB}$.

$$e_q(n) = V_{out}(n) - V_{ideal}(n), \quad \vert e_q \vert \leq \frac{\Delta V}{2}$$

### 2.1.3 Offset Error
The offset error is the **deviation of the actual output at code `0` from the ideal output at code `0`**. It is typically expressed in LSBs or as a percentage of Full-Scale Range (FSR).

$$V_{out}(0)_{actual} = V_{offset}, \quad \text{Offset Error} = \frac{V_{offset}}{\Delta V} \text{ LSB}$$

### 2.1.4 Gain (Full-Scale) Error
The gain error is the **deviation of the actual full-scale output from the ideal full-scale output**, measured at code `2^N − 1`, *after* the offset error has been trimmed to zero. It is usually expressed as a percentage of FSR.

$$\text{Gain Error (\%FSR)} = \frac{V_{out,FS}^{actual} - V_{out,FS}^{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.5 Integral Non-Linearity (INL)
INL is the **maximum deviation of the actual DAC transfer function from the *best-fit straight line*** (end-point or least-squares) over the full input code range.

$$\text{INL}(k) = \frac{V_{out}(k) - V_{ideal}(k)}{\Delta V} \text{ LSB}$$

$$\text{INL}_{max} = \max_{k} \vert \text{INL}(k) \vert$$

### 2.1.6 Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)
DNL measures the **deviation of each actual step size from the ideal 1-LSB step**. It is the local linearity metric.

$$\text{DNL}(k) = \frac{\Delta V_{actual}(k) - \Delta V_{ideal}}{\Delta V_{ideal}} \text{ LSB}$$

- A **negative DNL** at any code implies **non-monotonicity**.
- $\text{DNL} \geq -1 \text{ LSB}$ is the **monotonicity threshold**.

### 2.1.7 Monotonicity
A DAC is **monotonic** if, and only if, its output *never decreases* as the input code increases. This requires $\text{DNL}(k) \geq -1$ LSB for every code $k$.

## 2.2 Dynamic (AC) Specifications

### 2.2.1 Settling Time ($t_s$)
The time required for the output to settle within a specified error band (typically $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB or $\pm 1$ LSB) of the final value after a full-scale step input change.

### 2.2.2 Slew Rate (SR)
The maximum rate of change of the analog output, usually expressed in V/µs.

$$\text{SR} = \left. \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} \right|_{max}$$

### 2.2.3 Glitch Impulse
A transient deviation from the monotonic transition between codes, often worst at the major carry transition (`0111…1` → `1000…0`). Quantified in **volt-seconds (V·s)** or nV·s.

### 2.2.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
For an ideal N-bit DAC processing a full-scale sinusoid:

$$\text{SNR}_{ideal} = 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

### 2.2.5 Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)
The actual achievable resolution after noise and distortion are accounted for:

$$\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$$

### 2.2.6 Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR)
The ratio, in dB, of the RMS amplitude of the fundamental output to the RMS amplitude of the *largest* spurious component in the spectrum (harmonic or intermodulation).

$$\text{SFDR (dB)} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{fundamental,rms}}{V_{spur,max,rms}} \right)$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Specification** | **Symbol** | **Ideal Expression** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution (Step Size) | $\Delta V$ | $\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$ | Volts | 1 LSB |
| Number of Levels | $L$ | $2^N$ | – | Discrete steps |
| Quantization Noise (RMS) | $\sigma_q$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts | Uniform assumption |
| Max Quantization Error | $e_{q,max}$ | $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB | LSB | – |
| Ideal SNR | $\text{SNR}$ | $6.02N + 1.76$ | dB | Full-scale sine |
| SINAD | $\text{SINAD}$ | $10 \log \left( \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise} + P_{distortion}} \right)$ | dB | – |
| ENOB | $\text{ENOB}$ | $\dfrac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | Bits | Real-world resolution |
| Settling Time | $t_s$ | $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB band | seconds | Full-scale step |
| DNL | $\text{DNL}(k)$ | $\dfrac{\Delta V_{actual}(k) - \Delta V}{\Delta V}$ | LSB | Local linearity |
| INL | $\text{INL}(k)$ | $\dfrac{V_{out}(k) - V_{ideal}(k)}{\Delta V}$ | LSB | Global linearity |
| Monotonicity Criterion | – | $\text{DNL}(k) \geq -1$ | LSB | Sufficient condition |
| Gain Error | $E_G$ | $\dfrac{V_{FS,actual} - V_{FS,ideal}}{V_{FS}}$ | % FSR | After offset trim |
| Slew Rate | $\text{SR}$ | $\left( \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} \right)_{max}$ | V/µs | – |
| Glitch Impulse | $Q_g$ | $\int \vert \Delta V_{glitch} \vert \, dt$ | V·s | Major carry |
| SFDR | $\text{SFDR}$ | $20 \log \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V_{spur,max}} \right)$ | dBc | – |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These specifications govern the suitability of a DAC in:

- **Audio Engineering (Hi-Fi)**: ENOB ≥ 16 bits, SFDR > 90 dB, ultra-low glitch.
- **Process Control / Instrumentation**: INL ≤ $\pm 1$ LSB, monotonic, $t_s$ < 10 µs.
- **Communications / Direct-Digital Synthesis (DDS)**: High SFDR, high update rate ($f_s$ > 100 MSPS), low jitter.
- **Automotive & Industrial Sensors**: Wide temperature range (−40 °C to +125 °C), high PSRR, monotonic by design.
- **Medical Imaging & Beamforming**: 12–16 bit, low noise, fast settling, and excellent channel-to-channel matching.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal SNR of an N-bit DAC

### Step 1: Model the Quantization Error
The quantization error $e_q$ is modeled as a uniformly distributed random variable on the interval $\left[ -\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}, +\dfrac{\Delta V}{2} \right]$, with probability density function:

$$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{\Delta V}, \quad \text{for } \vert e_q \vert \leq \frac{\Delta V}{2}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Mean-Square Value of Quantization Noise
The mean-square value of $e_q$ is the variance (mean is zero):

$$\sigma_q^2 = \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{+\Delta V/2} e_q^2 \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q^2 &= \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{+\Delta V/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta V} \, de_q \\
&= \frac{1}{\Delta V} \left[ \frac{e_q^3}{3} \right]_{-\Delta V/2}^{+\Delta V/2} \\
&= \frac{1}{\Delta V} \left( \frac{(\Delta V/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta V/2)^3}{3} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{\Delta V} \cdot \frac{2 (\Delta V)^3}{24} \\
&= \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the RMS quantization noise voltage is:

$$\sigma_q = \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}}$$

### Step 3: Compute the RMS Value of a Full-Scale Sinusoid
For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $A_{peak}$ equal to the full-scale range $\dfrac{2^N \cdot \Delta V}{2} = 2^{N-1} \Delta V$:

$$V_{signal,rms} = \frac{A_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2^{N-1} \Delta V}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 4: Form the SNR Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{signal,rms}}{\sigma_q} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{2^{N-1} \Delta V / \sqrt{2}}{\Delta V / \sqrt{12}} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{2^{N-1} \sqrt{12}}{\sqrt{2}} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10} \left( 2^{N-1} \cdot \sqrt{6} \right) \\
&= 20 \left[ (N-1) \log_{10}(2) + \tfrac{1}{2} \log_{10}(6) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$ and $\log_{10}(6) \approx 0.77815$:

$$\text{SNR} = 20(0.30103)(N-1) + 20(0.5)(0.77815)$$

$$\text{SNR} = 6.0206(N-1) + 7.7815$$

$$\boxed{\text{SNR}_{dB} \approx 6.02N + 1.76}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The classical 1.76 dB term arises from the 3 dB headroom of a full-scale sine (versus FS-DC) and the $\sqrt{6}$ factor from $\sqrt{12}/\sqrt{2}$. This is the *ideal* N-bit DAC limit; real devices add thermal noise, distortion, and reference noise.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Monotonicity Condition from DNL

Let the actual step size between code $k$ and code $k-1$ be $S(k) = \Delta V \cdot [1 + \text{DNL}(k)]$. For monotonicity we require:

$$V_{out}(k) = V_{out}(k-1) + S(k) \geq V_{out}(k-1)$$

This is satisfied if, and only if, $S(k) \geq 0$ for every $k$:

$$1 + \text{DNL}(k) \geq 0 \implies \text{DNL}(k) \geq -1 \text{ LSB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Therefore, **monotonicity** $\iff$ $\text{DNL}(k) \geq -1$ LSB for all $k \in [1, 2^N - 1]$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (KTU-style):** An 8-bit DAC has a full-scale output of $V_{FS} = 9.96$ V. Determine (a) the ideal LSB step size, (b) the ideal RMS quantization noise, and (c) the ideal SNR.

**Given:** $N = 8$, $V_{FS} = 9.96$ V (end-point full-scale: $V_{FS} = (2^8 - 1) \cdot \Delta V$).

### (a) Step Size

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N - 1} = \frac{9.96}{255} \\
&= 0.03906 \text{ V} = 39.06 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (b) RMS Quantization Noise

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q &= \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{39.06 \text{ mV}}{3.4641} \\
&\approx 11.28 \text{ mV}_{rms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) Ideal SNR

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= 6.02(8) + 1.76 \\
&= 48.16 + 1.76 \\
&= 49.92 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check via signal-to-noise ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{peak} &= 2^{N-1} \cdot \Delta V = 128 \times 39.06 \text{ mV} = 5.000 \text{ V} \\
V_{rms} &= \frac{5.000}{\sqrt{2}} = 3.5355 \text{ V} \\
\text{SNR} &= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{3.5355}{0.01128} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(313.4) \\
&\approx 49.92 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix – Choosing a DAC Specification

| **Application** | **Min Resolution (bits)** | **Max INL (LSB)** | **Settling Time (typ.)** | **SFDR (dBc)** | **Recommended Architecture** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DC Reference / Bias | 8 – 10 | $\pm 1$ | 1 – 10 µs | 60 | R-2R / PWM |
| Process Control (4–20 mA) | 12 | $\pm 1$ | < 5 µs | 70 | R-2R Ladder |
| Audio (16-bit Hi-Fi) | 16 | $\pm 1$ | < 2 µs | 90 | Sigma-Delta |
| Video (HD, 10-bit) | 10 | $\pm 1$ | < 35 ns | 70 | Current-Steering |
| DDS / Waveform Gen. | 12 – 14 | $\pm 1$ | < 10 ns | 80 | Current-Steering |
| Radar / Beamforming | 14 – 16 | $\pm 1$ | < 5 ns | 85 | Current-Steering |
