# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the term *Resolution* of a DAC. A 10-bit DAC has a full-scale output of 5.00 V. Calculate the size of one LSB.**

**Model Answer:**

Resolution is the smallest incremental change in the analog output that results from a 1-LSB change in the digital input code. For an N-bit DAC with full-scale output $V_{FS}$:

$$\Delta V = \text{1 LSB} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$

For $N = 10$ and $V_{FS} = 5.00$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{5.00}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.00}{1024} \\
&\approx 4.88 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, **1 LSB ≈ 4.88 mV**.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: What is *Monotonicity* in a DAC? State the DNL condition for a DAC to be monotonic.**

**Model Answer:**

A DAC is **monotonic** if its analog output never decreases as the digital input code increments. This is a critical specification for control-loop and signal-reconstruction applications.

**Condition:** A DAC is monotonic **if and only if** its differential non-linearity satisfies:

$$\text{DNL}(k) \geq -1 \text{ LSB} \quad \forall \, k \in [1, 2^N - 1]$$

If at any code the DNL drops below −1 LSB, the step size becomes negative and the output *decreases*, violating monotonicity.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the static specifications of a DAC: *Resolution, Offset Error, Gain Error, INL,* and *DNL*. Define monotonicity and state the relationship between DNL and monotonicity.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 12-bit DAC with $V_{REF} = 5.12$ V is ideal. Compute (i) the 1-LSB step size, (ii) the RMS quantization noise voltage, (iii) the ideal SNR in dB, and (iv) the maximum quantization error in mV.

#### Model Solution to (a):

A DAC converts an N-bit digital code $D$ into an analog voltage $V_{out}$. The **static (DC) specifications** describe the deviations of the actual output from the ideal staircase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out}^{ideal}(k) &= \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N} \cdot k \\
\text{where} \quad k &\in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^N - 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Resolution (N bits):** The number of distinct output levels = $2^N$.
- **Offset Error:** $V_{out}(0)$ deviation from zero, in LSB.
- **Gain Error:** Deviation of $V_{out}(2^N-1)$ from $V_{FS}$ after offset trim, in %FSR.
- **INL(k):** Maximum deviation of the *best-fit straight line* from $V_{out}^{ideal}(k)$, in LSB.
- **DNL(k):** Deviation of the *actual step* $V_{out}(k) - V_{out}(k-1)$ from 1 LSB.

**Monotonicity condition:**

$$\text{DNL}(k) \geq -1 \text{ LSB} \quad \forall \, k$$

If at any code $k_0$, $\text{DNL}(k_0) < -1$ LSB, the step becomes negative, violating monotonicity.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $N = 12$, $V_{REF} = 5.12$ V.

**(i) Step Size (1 LSB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= \frac{V_{REF}}{2^N} = \frac{5.12}{4096} \\
&= 1.25 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) RMS Quantization Noise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q &= \frac{\Delta V}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{1.25 \text{ mV}}{3.4641} \\
&\approx 0.3608 \text{ mV}_{rms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Ideal SNR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \\
&= 72.24 + 1.76 \\
&= 74.00 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{q,max} &= \pm \frac{1}{2} \text{ LSB} = \pm 0.625 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the *ideal signal-to-noise ratio* of an N-bit DAC processing a full-scale sine wave. Hence, calculate the SNR for $N = 16$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the dynamic specifications of a DAC: *Settling Time, Slew Rate, Glitch Impulse, SFDR,* and *ENOB*. A 14-bit DAC is measured to have SINAD = 78 dB. Compute its ENOB.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Derivation of Ideal SNR of an N-bit DAC:**

The quantization error $e_q$ is modeled as a uniformly distributed random variable over $\left[ -\dfrac{\Delta V}{2}, \dfrac{\Delta V}{2} \right]$ where $\Delta V = V_{FS}/2^N$. The variance (mean-square noise) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_q^2 &= \int_{-\Delta V/2}^{\Delta V/2} e_q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta V} \, de_q = \frac{(\Delta V)^2}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

The RMS signal for a full-scale sine of peak $A_{peak} = 2^{N-1} \Delta V$ is:

$$V_{signal,rms} = \frac{2^{N-1} \Delta V}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{signal,rms}}{\sigma_q} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{2^{N-1} \Delta V / \sqrt{2}}{\Delta V / \sqrt{12}} \right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10} \left( 2^{N-1} \cdot \sqrt{6} \right) \\
&= 6.02N + 1.76 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For N = 16:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR} &= 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 \\
&= 96.32 + 1.76 \\
&= 98.08 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Dynamic Specifications of a DAC:**

- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time required for the output to settle and remain within a specified error band (typically $\pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB) of the final value after a full-scale code transition.
- **Slew Rate (SR):** The maximum rate of change of the output, expressed in V/µs. For a sinusoid of peak $V_p$ at frequency $f$: $\text{SR} \geq 2\pi f V_p$.
- **Glitch Impulse ($Q_g$):** The unwanted transient area (V·s) at a code transition, worst at the *major carry* (`0111…1` to `1000…0`) where many bits switch simultaneously.
- **SFDR (Spurious-Free Dynamic Range):** The dB ratio of the fundamental output to the *largest spurious tone* in the spectrum, defining the converter's spectral purity.
- **ENOB (Effective Number of Bits):** The equivalent resolution of the converter after noise and distortion are accounted for, computed from SINAD.

**ENOB Calculation:**

Given $\text{SINAD} = 78$ dB, $N = 14$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ENOB} &= \frac{\text{SINAD} - 1.76}{6.02} \\
&= \frac{78 - 1.76}{6.02} \\
&= \frac{76.24}{6.02} \\
&\approx 12.66 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

The DAC effectively performs as a **12.66-bit** converter in real-world conditions, having lost approximately 1.34 bits of effective resolution to noise and distortion.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LSB and FSR units** – Always report INL/DNL in *LSB*, not in Volts, when comparing linearity across different reference voltages.
> 2. **Using $2^N$ instead of $(2^N - 1)$ in end-point scaling** – When $V_{FS}$ is defined as the output at code $(2^N - 1)$, divide by 255 for 8-bit, not 256.
> 3. **Misapplying the SNR formula** – The $6.02N + 1.76$ dB expression is valid *only* for an *ideal* DAC driving a *full-scale sine*. For triangular or DC signals, the formula changes.
> 4. **Forgetting the sign of DNL** – A DNL of $-0.5$ LSB is acceptable, but a DNL of $-1.2$ LSB violates monotonicity. Always compare against the $-1$ LSB threshold.
> 5. **Ignoring major-carry glitch** – The transition at mid-scale (`1000…0` for an N-bit DAC) is *always* the worst-case glitch, and students frequently overlook its impact on reconstruction-filter design.
> 6. **Mixing up INL and DNL** – INL is a *cumulative* (global) error, DNL is an *incremental* (local) error. They are related by $\text{INL}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{DNL}(i)$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Resolution N bits → $2^N$ levels → 1 LSB = $V_{REF}/2^N$**
- [ ] **Monotonicity ⇔ DNL ≥ −1 LSB at every code**
- [ ] **INL is *global*, DNL is *local*; INL is the running sum of DNL**
- [ ] **Offset Error: deviation at code 0 (after trimming offset)**
- [ ] **Gain Error: deviation at code (2^N − 1) after offset trim, in % FSR**
- [ ] **Quantization Error bounded by ±½ LSB; RMS noise = $\Delta V/\sqrt{12}$**
- [ ] **Ideal SNR of N-bit DAC = $6.02N + 1.76$ dB (full-scale sine)**
- [ ] **SINAD includes noise + distortion; ENOB = (SINAD − 1.76)/6.02**
- [ ] **SFDR is the dBc distance from fundamental to largest spur**
- [ ] **Settling Time: time to within ±½ LSB of final value after a full-scale step**
- [ ] **Slew Rate: max $dV_{out}/dt$; glitch impulse worst at major carry**
- [ ] **Update rate $f_s$ sets the Nyquist bandwidth: $f_{signal} < f_s/2$**
- [ ] **Practical 12-bit DACs typically achieve ENOB ≈ 11.0 – 11.7 bits**
- [ ] **For every extra 1 bit of ENOB, SNR improves by ≈ 6 dB**
- [ ] **Architectures (R-2R, Weighted, Thermometer, Current-Steering) trade off monotonicity vs. speed vs. INL**
- [ ] **KTU favorite question types: definition, formula derivation (SNR), and numerical evaluation of LSB, RMS noise, and ENOB**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAC: Weighted Resistor Type, R-2R Ladder Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DAC: Weighted Resistor Type, R-2R Ladder Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a discrete digital (binary) input word of *n* bits into a continuous analog output voltage (or current) whose magnitude is directly proportional to the value of the digital input code. The output is given in general form as:

$$V_o = K \times D$$

where **K** is an analog scaling constant (set by a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ and the resistor network) and **D** is the decimal equivalent of the binary input word.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 4, the two architectures focused on are (i) **Weighted-Resistor (Binary-Weighted) DAC** and (ii) **R-2R Ladder DAC**. Both belong to the family of *current-summing, op-amp based* DACs, where each bit controls an electronic switch that either feeds a reference voltage or grounds a precision resistor.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Volume Mixer"

Imagine an audio mixing console with **n sliding faders**, each adding water into a common bucket:

- The **MSB (Most Significant Bit)** fader is *wide* and pours a *large* amount of water per unit slide.
- The **LSB (Least Significant Bit)** fader is *narrow* and pours a *tiny* amount.
- The bucket's water level is the **analog output voltage**.
- A switch on every fader is either **UP (bit = 1, pours water)** or **DOWN (bit = 0, dry)**.

The **weighted-resistor DAC** literally sizes each fader's opening by powers of 2. The **R-2R ladder DAC** uses only *two pipe sizes* (R and 2R) but cleverly divides the flow at junctions so the same weighting effect is achieved. This is why R-2R is the **industry favourite** for IC fabrication.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Resolution (n)**: number of bits in the input word → produces $2^n$ distinct analog output levels.
- **Step Size (LSB voltage, $V_{LSB}$)**: smallest change in output, equal to $V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$.
- **Full-Scale Output ($V_{FS}$)**: maximum analog output when all bits are HIGH.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$)**: time for output to settle within $\pm \tfrac{1}{2} V_{LSB}$ of its final value.
- **Reference Voltage ($V_{ref}$)**: stable DC source that defines the output scale.

> [!NOTE]
> A 4-bit DAC has $2^4 = 16$ levels. An 8-bit DAC has $256$ levels. A 12-bit DAC has $4096$ levels — used in precision audio and instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Functional Concept

Every DAC follows the same conceptual pipeline:

1. A **digital input latch** holds the n-bit word.
2. **n electronic switches** (one per bit) connect each precision resistor to either $V_{ref}$ or ground.
3. A **resistor summing network** combines the individual contributions.
4. An **op-amp (inverting summer)** converts the summed current into a low-impedance output voltage.

### 2.2 Why "Weighted" Resistors?

For an n-bit binary word $b_1 b_2 b_3 \dots b_n$ (where $b_1$ is the MSB and $b_n$ is the LSB), the bit weights in decimal are $2^{n-1}, 2^{n-2}, \dots, 2^0$. To make the **current contribution of each bit proportional to its binary weight**, the resistor connected to bit $b_i$ must be **inversely proportional** to $2^{n-i}$. The binary-weighted resistor choice is therefore:

$$R_i = 2^{i-1} \cdot R \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

This is the *fundamental idea* of the weighted-resistor DAC.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Weighted-Resistor DAC** | **R-2R Ladder DAC** |
|---|---|---|
| Resistor values needed | $R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$ (n different values) | Only **R** and **2R** (two values) |
| Switch current (bit $b_i = 1$) | $I_i = V_{ref} / (2^{i-1}R)$ | $I_i = V_{ref} / (2R \cdot 2^{n-i})$ (halved at each rung) |
| Total summing current | $I = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$ | $I = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2R} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{b_i}{2^{n-i}}$ |
| Output (with $R_f = R$) | $V_o = -V_{ref} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{b_i}{2^{n-i}}$ |
| Output in compact binary form | $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$ |
| Step size $\vert \Delta V_o \vert$ | $V_{ref} / 2^{n-1}$ | $V_{ref} / 2^{n}$ |
| Full-scale output $\vert V_{FS} \vert$ | $V_{ref} (2 - 2^{1-n}) \approx 2 V_{ref}$ | $V_{ref} (1 - 2^{-n}) \approx V_{ref}$ |
| Required resistor spread ratio | $2^{n-1} : 1$ (very large for n>8) | $2 : 1$ (constant) |
| Suitability for IC fabrication | Poor for n $> 8$ | Excellent for n $\geq 8$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The two architectures differ by **exactly a factor of 2** in their output range — a direct consequence of how the MSB current enters the summing junction (full value vs. halved by current division).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio playback (CD players, MP3 DACs)** uses R-2R or segmented architectures for high linearity.
- **Microcontroller waveform generation** (function generators, DDS chips) embed R-2R ladders.
- **Industrial process control** uses 12-/16-bit DACs for valve and motor positioning.
- **Communication systems** (QAM modulators) use DACs at the transmitter to synthesize IF/RF waveforms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Weighted-Resistor DAC — Complete Derivation

#### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

The weighted-resistor DAC is built around an **inverting summing amplifier**. Each bit $b_i$ drives an SPDT switch. When $b_i = 1$, the switch routes $V_{ref}$ to resistor $R_i = 2^{i-1}R$. When $b_i = 0$, the switch grounds the resistor. All $R_i$ converge at the inverting input of the op-amp, which sits at **virtual ground**. The non-inverting input is grounded. Feedback resistor is $R_f = R$.

#### 3.1.2 Derivation of Output Voltage

Because the inverting node is a virtual ground, the current through each resistor (when its bit = 1) is independent of the other bits — **superposition applies directly**.

**Step 1:** Write the current contributed by bit $b_i$ into the summing node:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref} - 0}{R_i} = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2^{i-1} R}$$

**Step 2:** Sum all individual currents (KCL at virtual ground — all currents flow into the op-amp):

$$I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i = \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the inverting amplifier gain equation $V_o = -R_f \cdot I_{total}$:

$$V_o = -R_f \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$$

**Step 4:** Set $R_f = R$ (standard choice) for a unity-gain configuration:

$$V_o = -V_{ref} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$$

**Step 5 (Full-Scale Verification):** When all bits = 1, sum is a geometric series:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2^{i-1}} = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \dots + \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} = 2 \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^n}\right) = 2 - \frac{1}{2^{n-1}}$$

Therefore the full-scale output is:

$$V_{FS} = -V_{ref} \left( 2 - \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \right) \approx -2 V_{ref} \quad \text{for large } n$$

**Step 6 (Resolution/Step size):** Changing the LSB from 0 to 1 changes the sum by exactly $\tfrac{1}{2^{n-1}}$:

$$|V_{LSB}| = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}}$$

#### 3.1.3 Worked Example — 4-bit Weighted-Resistor DAC

Given $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$ and digital input $b_1 b_2 b_3 b_4 = 1\,0\,1\,1$ (= 11 decimal), calculate $V_o$.

**Step A:** Identify the bit weights:
- $b_1 = 1$ → contribution $= 1 \times V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$
- $b_2 = 0$ → contribution $= 0$
- $b_3 = 1$ → contribution $= V_{ref}/4 = 2.5\ \text{V}$
- $b_4 = 1$ → contribution $= V_{ref}/8 = 1.25\ \text{V}$

**Step B:** Sum the contributions:

$$V_o = -(10 + 0 + 2.5 + 1.25) = -13.75\ \text{V}$$

**Step C (Sanity check):** The analog ratio should equal digital ratio $(11/15)$ of the full-scale $|V_{FS}| \approx 20\ \text{V}$:

$$\frac{11}{15} \times 20 = 14.67\ \text{V} \quad (\text{rounded ideal } V_{FS} \text{ gives } 13.75\ \text{V — exact!})$$

> [!NOTE]
> The exact value matches because we used the *exact* full-scale expression $2 - 1/8 = 15/8$, giving $|V_{FS}| = 10 \times 15/8 = 18.75\ \text{V}$, and $(11/15) \times 18.75 = 13.75\ \text{V}$. ✓

---

### 3.2 R-2R Ladder DAC — Complete Derivation

#### 3.2.1 Circuit Description

The R-2R ladder has **only two resistor values, R and 2R**, arranged in a repetitive "rung" structure. The horizontal (series) arms are always R; the vertical (shunt) arms to the switches are always 2R. Each 2R arm is terminated by a SPDT switch that connects to either $V_{ref}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0). The right-most end is terminated by a 2R resistor to ground (the LSB equivalent). The output of the ladder is taken at the left-most node, fed into the inverting input of an op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f = R$.

#### 3.2.2 Thevenin Reduction (Ladder Analysis from LSB to MSB)

The key insight of the R-2R ladder is the **constant equivalent resistance** of $R$ when looking into any internal node toward the LSB end. We prove this by recursive Thevenin reduction.

**Step 1 — Rightmost node (LSB end):** The right-most 2R (terminator) is in parallel with the 2R switch of the LSB. Looking into them, we see an equivalent resistance of $2R \parallel 2R = R$. Add the horizontal R in series, total to the *next* node (left) is $R + R = 2R$.

**Step 2 — Inductive generalisation:** At any internal node, looking *rightward* (toward LSB), we have a vertical 2R (switch) in parallel with the Thevenin-equivalent of the right-side ladder, which by induction is $2R$. So:

$$R_{eq,right} = 2R \parallel 2R = R$$

Adding the series horizontal R for the next node:

$$R_{eq, left\ of\ node} = R + R = 2R$$

This confirms that **at every node, the resistance looking to the right is $R$**, and to the left of any node is $2R$ (excluding the switch's 2R).

**Step 3 — Current from the MSB node:** The MSB switch (bit $b_1$) connects $V_{ref}$ to the leftmost 2R. The total resistance from $V_{ref}$ to the virtual ground (op-amp inverting input) is $2R + R = 3R$ — but a more useful viewpoint: the 2R vertical branch and the 2R rightward equivalent form a current divider. The current supplied by $V_{ref}$ into the 2R MSB branch when $b_1 = 1$ is:

$$I_{source,1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R}$$

**Step 4 — Current division at the MSB node:** This current $I_{source,1}$ splits at the MSB node between (a) the 2R vertical branch and (b) the 2R equivalent looking right. Since both are equal (2R each), the current splits **equally**. The half flowing *rightward* is the input to the next stage, while the other half flows *downward* into the op-amp summing junction.

**Step 5 — Cascading halving:** At *each* subsequent node (moving from MSB toward LSB), the incoming current splits **50/50** again. By the time the current reaches the contribution of bit $b_i$, it has been halved $(i-1)$ times.

**Step 6 — Current at the op-amp summing junction:** Summing all contributions:

$$I_{total} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \dots + \frac{b_n}{2^{n-1}} \right) = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$$

**Step 7 — Op-amp output:** With feedback resistor $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -R_f \cdot I_{total} = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{b_i}{2^{i-1}}$$

**Step 8 (Compact form):** Re-index the sum to put $b_1$ in the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ position:

$$V_o = -V_{ref} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \dots + \frac{b_n}{2^n} \right) = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$$

**Step 9 (Full-Scale Output):** All bits = 1:

$$|V_{FS}| = V_{ref} \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2^n} \right) \approx V_{ref}$$

**Step 10 (Step Size):**

$$|V_{LSB}| = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

#### 3.2.3 Worked Example — 4-bit R-2R Ladder DAC

Given $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$ and input $b_1 b_2 b_3 b_4 = 1\,0\,1\,1$ (= 11 decimal):

**Step A:** Apply the formula:

$$V_o = -10 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right) = -10 \left( 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 + 0.0625 \right) = -6.875\ \text{V}$$

**Step B (Sanity check):** Full scale is $10 \times 15/16 = 9.375\ \text{V}$. Expected output = $(11/15) \times 9.375 = 6.875\ \text{V}$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **factor-of-2 difference** in $V_{FS}$: the R-2R ladder produces a maximum of $\approx V_{ref}$, while the weighted-resistor DAC produces $\approx 2V_{ref}$. This is a *direct consequence* of the MSB current being halved by the current-divider action at the leftmost node of the ladder.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Parameter** | **Weighted-Resistor DAC** | **R-2R Ladder DAC** | **Winner** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor count for n bits | n (values: R, 2R, 4R, …) | 2n+1 (only R, 2R) | R-2R |
| Max : Min resistor ratio | $2^{n-1} : 1$ | $2 : 1$ | R-2R (especially for n>8) |
| Output full-scale | $\approx 2V_{ref}$ | $\approx V_{ref}$ | Depends on design |
| Sensitivity to switch $R_{on}$ | High (small LSB resistor) | Low (constant 2R at all rungs) | R-2R |
| Monotonicity guarantee | No (resistor mismatch on MSB) | Better (matched ladder) | R-2R |
| Speed / Settling time | Slower (parasitics on big R) | Faster | R-2R |
| Manufacturing ease | Poor for high n | Excellent | R-2R |
| Typical application | Low-bit teaching circuits | High-precision ICs (8–16 bit) | R-2R |

flowchart TB

    B --> C1["HALF GOES TO OP AMP SUMMING NODE\nI EQUALS VREF OVER 4R"]

    D --> E2["QUARTER PROCEEDS RIGHT\nI EQUALS VREF OVER 8R"]

    F --> G1["EIGHTH TO OP AMP\nI EQUALS VREF OVER 16R"]
    F --> G2["EIGHTH PROCEEDS RIGHT\nI EQUALS VREF OVER 16R"]

### 4.5 Transfer Characteristics — Ideal Staircase

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["0000\n0V"] --> S1["0001\nSTEP UP"] --> S2["0010\nSTEP UP"] --> S3["0011\nSTEP UP"] --> S4["0100\nSTEP UP"] --> S5["0101\nSTEP UP"] --> S6["0110\nSTEP UP"] --> S7["0111\nSTEP UP"] --> S8["1000\nSTEP UP"] --> S9["1001\nSTEP UP"] --> S10["1010\nSTEP UP"] --> S11["1011\nSTEP UP"] --> S12["1100\nSTEP UP"] --> S13["1101\nSTEP UP"] --> S14["1110\nSTEP UP"] --> S15["1111\nFS APPROX VREF"]
    style S0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style S15 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The staircase is **monotonically increasing** for an ideal R-2R DAC. In a real weighted-resistor DAC, mismatch in the largest resistor ($R$ for MSB) can produce a **non-monotonic step** at the $1000 \to 0111$ transition, which is a critical design flaw.
