## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4]**
*List any **three** drawbacks of a binary-weighted-resistor DAC that are overcome by an R-2R ladder DAC.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Wide resistor spread:** The weighted-resistor DAC requires resistor values from $R$ to $2^{n-1}R$, a ratio of $2^{n-1}:1$. For an 8-bit DAC this is 128 : 1, which is extremely difficult to fabricate with matched tolerances. The R-2R ladder needs only a 2 : 1 ratio.
2. **Switch ON-resistance sensitivity:** The MSB switch (in series with the smallest resistor, $R$) sees the entire MSB current. Any non-zero $R_{on}$ of the switch becomes a significant fraction of $R$ and introduces non-linearity. In the R-2R ladder, every switch is in series with a 2R resistor, so $R_{on}$ is a small, *constant* fraction at every bit — the errors track uniformly.
3. **Monotonicity risk:** A mismatch in the MSB resistor (largest weight, smallest value) can cause the $011\dots1 \to 100\dots0$ transition to step *downward* instead of up, breaking monotonicity. The R-2R ladder, with its matched R/2R structure and the averaging effect of current division, guarantees near-perfect monotonicity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Define the term **resolution** of a DAC. A 6-bit DAC uses a reference voltage of $10\ \text{V}$. Find its (i) percentage resolution, (ii) step size, and (iii) full-scale output, assuming an R-2R architecture.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Resolution of a DAC is the smallest change in the analog output that can be distinguished, expressed either as the number of bits $n$ or as the size of one LSB step in volts.

**Given:** $n = 6$, $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$ (R-2R ladder, $R_f = R$).

**(i) Percentage resolution:**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{1}{64} \times 100 = 1.5625\ \%$$

**(ii) Step size (for R-2R):**

$$|V_{LSB}| = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{64} = 0.15625\ \text{V}$$

**(iii) Full-scale output (R-2R, all bits HIGH):**

$$|V_{FS}| = V_{ref} \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^n}\right) = 10 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{64}\right) = 10 \times \frac{63}{64} = 9.84375\ \text{V}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B offers **internal choice**. Below are **two fully independent alternative questions (A and B)** for this topic. Each contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Weighted-Resistor DAC [KTU University Exam — May 2023, ESE Main]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **4-bit binary-weighted-resistor DAC** using an op-amp summing amplifier. Derive an expression for its analog output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (3 marks):**
The 4-bit weighted-resistor DAC consists of:

- A precision reference voltage $V_{ref}$.
- Four SPDT switches $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ controlled by input bits $b_1, b_2, b_3, b_4$ respectively. When $b_i = 1$, the switch connects $V_{ref}$ to the resistor; when $b_i = 0$, it grounds the resistor.
- Four precision resistors of values $R, 2R, 4R, 8R$ respectively, where the MSB ($b_1$) is paired with the smallest resistor $R$ and the LSB ($b_4$) with the largest $8R$. This weighting makes each bit's current contribution proportional to its binary weight.
- An inverting op-amp (e.g., $\mu$A741) configured as a summing amplifier with feedback resistor $R_f = R$.
- The non-inverting input is grounded; the inverting input is a virtual ground (0 V).

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Current from each branch into the virtual ground:

$$I_1 = \frac{b_1 V_{ref}}{R}, \quad I_2 = \frac{b_2 V_{ref}}{2R}, \quad I_3 = \frac{b_3 V_{ref}}{4R}, \quad I_4 = \frac{b_4 V_{ref}}{8R}$$

By KCL at the virtual ground, all currents flow into the op-amp feedback path:

$$I_f = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + I_4 = \frac{V_{ref}}{R}\left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \frac{b_4}{8} \right)$$

Applying the inverting amplifier equation $V_o = -R_f \cdot I_f$ with $R_f = R$:

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -V_{ref} \left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \frac{b_4}{8} \right)\,}$$

This can be written compactly as:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}$$

For maximum input (1111), the output is approximately $-2V_{ref}$.

---

**(b)** For the 4-bit weighted-resistor DAC of part (a), $V_{ref} = 8\ \text{V}$ and $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. Determine the analog output for the digital input **1010** and the maximum full-scale output. What is the percentage resolution? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decode the input:** $b_1 b_2 b_3 b_4 = 1010_2 = 10_{10}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the output equation:**

$$V_o = -8 \left(1 + \frac{0}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{0}{8}\right) = -8 \left( 1 + 0 + 0.25 + 0 \right) = -8 \times 1.25 = -10\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum full-scale output (all bits = 1):**

$$|V_{FS}| = V_{ref} \left( 2 - \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \right) = 8 \left( 2 - \frac{1}{8} \right) = 8 \times \frac{15}{8} = 15\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage resolution (n = 4):**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^n} \times 100 = \frac{1}{16} \times 100 = 6.25\ \%$$

**Step 5 — Step size:**

$$|V_{LSB}| = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{8}{8} = 1\ \text{V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write the resolution formula as $\tfrac{1}{2^n - 1}$ for FSO, or confuse the step size of weighted-resistor ($V_{ref}/2^{n-1}$) with that of R-2R ($V_{ref}/2^n$). **You will lose 1 mark** for each such slip. Memorise: **weighted-resistor step = $V_{ref}/2^{n-1}$**, **R-2R step = $V_{ref}/2^n$**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — R-2R Ladder DAC [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, ESE Main]**

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **4-bit R-2R ladder DAC** and explain its operation. Using the Thevenin-equivalent method, derive the expression for the analog output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit (2 marks):**
The 4-bit R-2R ladder consists of horizontal (series) resistors of value $R$ and vertical (shunt) resistors of value $2R$. The leftmost end is fed to the inverting input of the op-amp; the rightmost end is terminated by a 2R resistor to ground. Each vertical 2R is connected via an SPDT switch to either $V_{ref}$ (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0). The op-amp uses $R_f = R$ as feedback.

**Thevenin Reduction (3 marks):**

Starting from the rightmost node (LSB end) and moving left:
- At any node, the vertical 2R is in parallel with the Thevenin-equivalent of the right-side ladder. By induction, this right-side equivalent is always $2R$.
- Therefore the equivalent resistance looking *rightward* from any node is $2R \parallel 2R = R$.
- Adding the horizontal R to move to the next node gives $2R$ to the left of any node.

At the **MSB node**, $V_{ref}$ drives a 2R vertical branch (when $b_1 = 1$) and the equivalent of the rest of the ladder is $2R$. These are equal, so the current supplied by the source splits **50/50**. The half flowing into the op-amp summing junction is:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{V_{ref}}{4R}$$

At the **next node**, the current is again halved before reaching the op-amp:

$$I_2 = \frac{V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{V_{ref}}{8R}$$

Continuing this halving, the contribution of bit $b_i$ is:

$$I_i = \frac{b_i \cdot V_{ref}}{2R} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{i-1}}$$

**Final Output Equation (2 marks):**

Summing all currents and applying $V_o = -R_f \cdot I_{total}$ with $R_f = R$:

$$V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2} \left( b_1 + \frac{b_2}{2} + \frac{b_3}{4} + \frac{b_4}{8} \right) = -V_{ref} \left( \frac{b_1}{2} + \frac{b_2}{4} + \frac{b_3}{8} + \frac{b_4}{16} \right)$$

In compact form:

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i \cdot 2^{n-i}\,}$$

For all bits = 1, $|V_{FS}| \approx V_{ref}$.

---

**(b)** For a 4-bit R-2R ladder DAC with $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$, calculate the analog output for the input word **1101**. Also compute the percentage resolution and the step size. If this DAC must be redesigned as a weighted-resistor DAC producing the same step size, what is the value of the smallest resistor, assuming $V_{ref}$ is unchanged? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute output for 1101:**

$$V_o = -10 \left( \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{0}{8} + \frac{1}{16} \right) = -10 \left( 0.5 + 0.25 + 0 + 0.0625 \right) = -10 \times 0.8125 = -8.125\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Percentage resolution (n = 4):**

$$\% \text{Resolution} = \frac{1}{2^4} \times 100 = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25\ \%$$

**Step 3 — Step size (R-2R):**

$$|V_{LSB}| = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Weighted-resistor redesign with same step size:**

For a weighted-resistor DAC:

$$|V_{LSB}|_{WR} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n-1}} = \frac{10}{2^3} = \frac{10}{8} = 1.25\ \text{V}$$

This is **not equal** to the R-2R step size. To force equality, the smallest resistor in the weighted-resistor DAC must satisfy:

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{R_1}$$

Setting $V_{LSB} = 0.625\ \text{V}$, $V_{ref} = 10\ \text{V}$, $R_f = R$ (smallest resistor value):

$$0.625 = \frac{10 \cdot R}{R_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 = \frac{10 R}{0.625} = 16 R$$

**Conclusion:** The MSB (smallest) resistor must be $R_1 = 16R$. The other resistors will be $32R, 64R, 128R$, with the LSB resistor as large as $128R$ — illustrating the wide spread problem of weighted-resistor DACs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake is writing the R-2R formula with the factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ in the wrong place. Many students write $V_o = -V_{ref}(b_1 + b_2/2 + \dots)$ for the R-2R DAC — this is the **weighted-resistor** formula! Always remember: **R-2R has a leading 1/2** because the MSB current is halved by the current divider.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **DAC** converts a digital binary word $D$ into an analog voltage $V_o = -K \cdot D$, where $K$ is set by $V_{ref}$ and the resistor network.
- [x] **Resolution** $= n$ bits → $2^n$ distinct output levels.
- [x] **Step size** is the smallest analog increment corresponding to a 1-LSB change.
- [x] **Weighted-Resistor DAC** uses resistors $R, 2R, 4R, \dots, 2^{n-1}R$.
- [x] **Weighted-Resistor Output:** $V_o = -V_{ref}\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i/2^{i-1}$ (with $R_f = R$).
- [x] **Weighted-Resistor Full-Scale Output:** $\approx 2V_{ref}$.
- [x] **Weighted-Resistor Step Size:** $V_{ref}/2^{n-1}$.
- [x] **Weighted-Resistor Drawback:** Resistor spread ratio is $2^{n-1}:1$ — impractical for $n > 8$.
- [x] **R-2R Ladder DAC** uses only **two resistor values, R and 2R**, in a repetitive rung structure.
- [x] **R-2R Ladder Output:** $V_o = -\frac{V_{ref}}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i/2^{i-1} = -V_{ref}\sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i/2^i$.
- [x] **R-2R Ladder Full-Scale Output:** $\approx V_{ref}$ (exactly $V_{ref}(1 - 2^{-n})$).
- [x] **R-2R Ladder Step Size:** $V_{ref}/2^n$ (exactly *half* the weighted-resistor step).
- [x] **Key property of the R-2R ladder:** Equivalent resistance looking into any node toward the LSB end is *always* $R$ (recursively proved by Thevenin reduction).
- [x] **Current-division principle:** At each rung, the current splits 50/50, halving the contribution of every successive lower bit.
- [x] **R-2R advantages:** Easy IC fabrication, matched resistors, monotonicity, low switch-$R_{on}$ sensitivity.
- [x] **KTU exam trap:** Always specify feedback resistor value ($R_f = R$ assumed unless stated) and confirm whether the formula expects the leading $\frac{1}{2}$ or not.
- [x] **Practical ICs using R-2R or scaled variants:** AD7524, DAC0808, AD9708, MAX5217.
- [x] **Higher-end alternatives** (beyond Module 4 scope): multiplying DACs, current-steering DACs, sigma-delta DACs, thermometer-coded DACs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC: Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** ADC: Specifications # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of ADC Specifications ## 1.1 What is an ADC? An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is a mixed-signal integrated circuit that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code (typically binary). The conversion is governed by three fundamental operations: **sampling**, **quantization**, and **encoding**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > An ADC is a device that translates real-world analog signals (voltage/current) into a discrete digital representation suitable for microcontrollers, DSPs, and digital communication systems. The quality of this translation is judged entirely by the **specification set** discussed in this module. ## 1.2 Why Do We Need Specifications? Every ADC is imperfect. Specifications act as a **standardized language of imperfections** — they quantify *how far* a practical ADC deviates from an ideal mathematical one. When selecting an ADC for an application (audio, instrumentation, radar, biomedical), the engineer must match these specifications to the system requirement. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Ruler Analogy 🎯** > Imagine measuring the length of a pencil using two rulers: > - **Ruler A** has markings every **1 mm** (high resolution) but is slightly stretched near the end (poor **linearity**). > - **Ruler B** has markings every **1 cm** (low resolution) but is uniformly accurate (good linearity). > > ADC specifications tell you **which ruler your ADC behaves like** — and how much it *lies* about the measurement. ## 1.3 The Two Broad Classes of ADC Specifications | Class | Purpose | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Static (DC) Specifications** | Describe behavior when input is a **slow or constant** signal | Resolution, Offset Error, Gain Error, INL, DNL | | **Dynamic (AC) Specifications** | Describe behavior when input is a **fast-changing** signal | SNR, SINAD, ENOB, THD, SFDR, Aperture Uncertainty | > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When a question says *"with a static input of 2.5 V"*, expect **Static specs**. When it says *"with a 1 kHz sine wave sampled at 100 kSPS"*, expect **Dynamic specs**. The distinction is the **#1 discriminator** in valuation. ## 1.4 Block Diagram — The ADC as a Black Box An ADC is fundamentally characterized by these externally observable quantities: 1. **Analog Input** $V_{IN}$ (continuous voltage) 2. **Reference Voltage** $V_{REF}$ (defines the full-scale range) 3. **Digital Output** $D$ (an N-bit binary word) 4. **Clock** $f_{CLK}$ (governs conversion timing) 5. **Control Lines** (Start, EOC, CS, RD) The mathematical ideal is: $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{IN}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{IN} \cdot 2^{N}}{V_{REF}} \right\rfloor$$ where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is the floor function (quantization). ## 1.5 Resolution — The Most Fundamental Specification **Resolution** is the smallest change in the analog input that can be distinguished at the output. It is determined by the number of bits $N$ and the reference voltage. $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Resolution is NOT the same as Accuracy.** > Resolution = *finest step size* (theoretical limit). > Accuracy = *how close the measured value is to the true value* (real-world). > A **12-bit ADC** can have resolution of ~$1\,\text{mV}$ but accuracy of only ±$5\,\text{mV}$ due to non-idealities. --- # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Static (DC) Specifications — Detailed Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Resolution ($V_{LSB}$) The **Least Significant Bit (LSB) voltage** is the quantum step of the ADC. $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N}} = \frac{V_{REF(H)} - V_{REF(L)}}{2^{N}}$$ For a unipolar ADC with $V_{REF} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $N = 10$ bits: $$V_{LSB} = \frac{5}{2^{10}} = \frac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88\,\text{mV}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $2^N$ and not $2^{N-1}$?** > A unipolar $N$-bit ADC has $2^N$ discrete output codes, hence $2^N$ equal step intervals from $0$ to $V_{FSR}$. ### 2.1.2 Quantization Error ($e_q$) The inherent error introduced by mapping a continuous value onto discrete levels. The maximum quantization error is $\pm \frac{1}{2}\,V_{LSB}$. $$-\frac{V_{LSB}}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}$$ ### 2.1.3 Offset Error The deviation of the first code transition (from $00\ldots0$ to $00\ldots1$) from the ideal value of $+0.5\,V_{LSB}$. It is **independent of input amplitude** and appears as a vertical shift of the transfer curve. ### 2.1.4 Gain Error The deviation of the last code transition from its ideal value, after offset error has been corrected. It is a **slope error** and is typically expressed as a percentage of FSR. ### 2.1.5 Differential Non-Linearity (DNL) The difference between an actual code width and the ideal $1\,V_{LSB}$ step: $$DNL(k) = \frac{V_{T}(k+1) - V_{T}(k)}{V_{LSB,ideal}} - 1$$ where $V_T(k)$ is the input voltage at the transition to code $k$. > [!WARNING] > If $DNL \leq -1\,LSB$, the ADC has **missing codes** — a serious fault. ### 2.1.6 Integral Non-Linearity (INL) The maximum deviation of the actual transfer characteristic from the best-fit straight line (or endpoint line), measured in LSBs. $$INL(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} DNL(i)$$ ### 2.1.7 Monotonicity An ADC is **monotonic** if increasing the analog input never produces a decrease in the digital output code. A DNL $\geq 0$ for all codes guarantees monotonicity. --- ## 2.2 Dynamic (AC) Specifications — Detailed Breakdown ### 2.2.1 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) For an ideal N-bit ADC, considering only quantization noise, the theoretical SNR is: $$SNR_{ideal} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \quad \text{dB}$$ > [!NOTE] > The derivation of this $6.02\,N$ rule appears in Section 3 of these notes. ### 2.2.2 Signal-to-Noise and Distortion Ratio (SINAD) The ratio of the RMS signal amplitude to the RMS sum of **all noise + harmonic distortion** components. $$SINAD = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise} + P_{distortion}}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$ ### 2.2.3 Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) The actual usable resolution under real (noisy) conditions: $$ENOB = \frac{SINAD - 1.76}{6.02} \quad \text{bits}$$ ### 2.2.4 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) $$THD = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{harmonics}}{P_{fundamental}}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$ ### 2.2.5 Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR) The ratio of the RMS amplitude of the fundamental to the RMS amplitude of the **largest spurious component** (harmonic or otherwise). ### 2.2.6 Aperture Time and Aperture Uncertainty (Aperture Jitter) - **Aperture Time** = the (small) time window during which the sample-and-hold acquires the input. - **Aperture Uncertainty ($t_j$)** = the random variation in sampling instant due to clock jitter. The jitter-limited SNR is: $$SNR_{jitter} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f_{in} t_j}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$ where $f_{in}$ is the analog input frequency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** As input frequency rises, $SNR_{jitter}$ falls at **20 dB/decade**. This is why high-frequency ADCs (e.g., oscilloscope front-ends) demand ultra-low-jitter clocks. ### 2.2.7 Conversion Time ($t_{conv}$) The time required to complete one A/D conversion. It sets the **maximum conversion rate**: $$f_{s,max} = \frac{1}{t_{conv}}$$ --- ## 2.3 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table. It covers ~80% of numerical problems. | # | Specification | Formula | Unit / Boundary | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Step Size (Resolution) | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N}}$ | Volts (V) | | 2 | Number of Discrete Levels | $L = 2^{N}$ | Levels | | 3 | Max Quantization Error | $e_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ | Volts (V) | | 4 | Ideal SNR | $SNR = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB | | 5 | ENOB | $ENOB = \dfrac{SINAD - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | | 6 | RMS Quantization Noise | $V_{q,rms} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | Volts (V) | | 7 | Jitter-Limited SNR | $SNR_j = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2\pi f_{in} t_j}\right)$ | dB | | 8 | Dynamic Range | $DR = 20 \log_{10}(2^{N}) = 6.02\,N$ | dB | | 9 | Total Levels for N bits | $L = 2^{N}$ | Codes | | 10 | Power (dBW) | $P_{dBW} = 10 \log_{10}(P / 1\,W)$ | dBW | | 11 | Conversion Rate | $f_{s,max} = 1 / t_{conv}$ | Samples/s | | 12 | Aperture Error (peak) | $\Delta V = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j$ | Volts (V) | | 13 | Power Supply Rejection | $PSRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta V_{PS}}{\Delta V_{OUT}}\right)$ | dB | | 14 | Common-Mode Rejection | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{DM}}{A_{CM}}\right)$ | dB | | 15 | DNL (at code k) | $DNL(k) = \dfrac{V_{T}(k+1) - V_{T}(k)}{V_{LSB}} - 1$ | LSB | > [!TIP] > **Engineering Utility:** These specifications govern every high-speed design from **5G base-station ADCs** (jitter-limited, ENOB-driven) to **medical ECG ADCs** (resolution and CMRR-driven). --- # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS Value of Quantization Noise **Premise:** Quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed between $-V_{LSB}/2$ and $+V_{LSB}/2$ for a properly designed ADC (mid-tread or mid-rise). **Step 1 — Define the Probability Density Function (PDF):** $$p(e_q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{V_{LSB}} & \text{for } -\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2} \\[4pt] 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — RMS Definition:** $$V_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e_q^{2}\, p(e_q)\, de_q}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the PDF over the bounded range:** $$V_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\int_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2} e_q^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{V_{LSB}}\, de_q}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:** $$V_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{V_{LSB}} \left[\frac{e_q^{3}}{3}\right]_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2}}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute the limits:** $$V_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{V_{LSB}} \cdot \frac{1}{3}\left[\left(\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(-\frac{V_{LSB}}{2}\right)^{3}\right]}$$ **Step 6 — Simplify the cubic difference (odd function):** $$V_{q,rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3\,V_{LSB}} \cdot \frac{2\,V_{LSB}^{3}}{8}} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{LSB}^{2}}{12}}$$ **Step 7 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} \approx 0.2887\, V_{LSB}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ideal SNR = $6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB **Premise:** The full-scale sinusoid has peak-to-peak swing equal to $2^{N}\,V_{LSB}$ (i.e., $V_{FSR}$). The signal power is computed as the RMS power of a sine wave of amplitude $V_{FSR}/2$. **Step 1 — Signal RMS Voltage:** For a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_p = V_{FSR}/2 = 2^{N-1} V_{LSB}$: $$V_{signal,rms} = \frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2^{N-1}\,V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 2 — Signal Power (assuming 1 Ω load):** $$P_{signal} = V_{signal,rms}^{2} = \frac{2^{2N-2}\,V_{LSB}^{2}}{2} = \frac{2^{2N-1}\,V_{LSB}^{2}}{2} = 2^{2N-3}\,V_{LSB}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Noise Power:** $$P_{noise} = V_{q,rms}^{2} = \frac{V_{LSB}^{2}}{12}$$ **Step 4 — Form the Ratio:** $$SNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{2^{2N-3}\,V_{LSB}^{2}}{V_{LSB}^{2}/12} = 12 \cdot 2^{2N-3} = \frac{12 \cdot 2^{2N}}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to dB (10 log₁₀):** $$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.5 \cdot 2^{2N}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2)$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate the constants:** - $10 \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 1.7609 \approx 1.76$ dB - $20 \log_{10}(2) \approx 6.0206 \approx 6.02$ dB **Step 7 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{SNR_{ideal} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \quad \text{dB}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aperture Uncertainty (Jitter) SNR Limit **Premise:** If the sampling clock has jitter $t_j$, the maximum voltage error for an input sinusoid of frequency $f_{in}$ and full-scale amplitude $V_{FS}$ is: **Step 1 — Slew Rate of Input Sinusoid:** $$\frac{dV}{dt}\bigg|_{max} = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS}$$ **Step 2 — Peak Voltage Error due to Jitter:** $$\Delta V_{max} = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j$$ **Step 3 — Signal RMS (full-scale sine):** $$V_{sig,rms} = \frac{V_{FS}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ **Step 4 — Noise RMS (assuming uniform distribution over $\pm\Delta V_{max}$):** $$V_{noise,rms} = \frac{\Delta V_{max}}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j}{\sqrt{3}}$$ **Step 5 — SNR Ratio:** $$SNR_j = \frac{V_{sig,rms}}{V_{noise,rms}} = \frac{V_{FS}/\sqrt{2}}{2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j / \sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2} \cdot 2\pi f_{in} t_j} = \frac{1}{2\pi f_{in} t_j} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3}{2}}$$ **Step 6 — Drop the $\sqrt{3/2} \approx 1.22 \approx 1.76$ dB constant for the standard approximation:** $$\boxed{SNR_{jitter} \approx 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f_{in} t_j}\right) \quad \text{dB}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This shows the **-20 dB/decade** rolloff with input frequency. If you double $f_{in}$, you lose ~6 dB of SNR. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix (KTU 2024 Style) **Sample Problem:** *A 12-bit ADC with $V_{REF} = 5.12$ V is used to digitize a 2 kHz sine wave of amplitude 2.5 V. Compute: (a) Resolution, (b) Maximum quantization error, (c) Ideal SNR, (d) Dynamic Range.* **Step-by-step solution:** > **Step (a) — Resolution:** > > $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^{N}} = \frac{5.12}{2^{12}} = \frac{5.12}{4096} = 1.25\,\text{mV}$$ > **Step (b) — Maximum Quantization Error:** > > $$e_{q,max} = \pm\frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm 0.625\,\text{mV}$$ > **Step (c) — Ideal SNR (N = 12):** > > $$SNR = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\,\text{dB}$$ > **Step (d) — Dynamic Range:** > > $$DR = 6.02 \times 12 = 72.24\,\text{dB}$$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Jitter-Limited SNR **Problem:** A 16-bit ADC samples a $f_{in} = 50\,\text{MHz}$ input signal with a clock jitter of $t_j = 2\,\text{ps}$. Determine whether quantization noise or jitter dominates. **Step 1 — Ideal SNR (N = 16):** $$SNR_{ideal} = 6.02(16) + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 2 — Jitter-Limited SNR:** $$SNR_j = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 50 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2 \times 10^{-12}}\right)$$ $$SNR_j = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{6.2832 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(1591.55)$$ $$SNR_j \approx 20 \times 3.2019 \approx 64.04\,\text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Compare:** > [!IMPORTANT] > Since $SNR_j \approx 64\,\text{dB} \ll SNR_{ideal} \approx 98\,\text{dB}$, the **jitter dominates**. The effective resolution is: > > $$ENOB_{jitter} = \frac{64.04 - 1.76}{6.02} \approx 10.35\,\text{bits}$$ > > The 16-bit ADC effectively performs as a **~10-bit ADC** for this input frequency. --- # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ADC Specification Hierarchy (Mermaid Mind Map) ```mermaid mindmap root((ADC
Specifications)) Static_DC Resolution V_LSB Quantization_Error Offset_Error Gain_Error DNL INL Monotonicity Dynamic_AC SNR SINAD ENOB THD SFDR Aperture_Jitter Conversion_Time System_Level Reference_Voltage Input_Range Power_Dissipation PSRR CMRR Sampling_Rate ``` ## 4.2 Signal Flow — From Analog Input to Quantized Digital Code ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Analog Input V_IN] --> B[Sample and Hold S or H] B --> C[Quantizer N-bit] C --> D[Encoder Binary] D --> E[Digital Output D] F[Reference V_REF] --> C G[Clock f_CLK] --> B G --> C H[Quantization Noise e_q] -.-> C ``` ## 4.3 Block Architecture of a Typical SAR ADC (showing specification touchpoints) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_FRONT_END A1[Analog Input Pin] --> A2[Input Buffer High CMRR] A2 --> A3[Sample Capacitor C_SH] end subgraph CORE_CONVERSION A3 --> B1[Comparator Aperture Jitter t_j] B1 --> B2[SAR Logic N-bit] B2 --> B3[DAC String V_REF] B3 --> B1 end subgraph OUTPUT_INTERFACE B2 --> C1[Output Register] C1 --> C2[Parallel or Serial Output] end D1[External Clock] --> B1 D2[Bandgap V_REF] --> B3 ``` ## 4.4 Specification Interdependency Map ```mermaid flowchart TD R[Resolution N bits] --> Q[Quantization Error] R --> SNR[SNR] R --> ENOB[ENOB] R --> DR[Dynamic Range] Q --> SNR AJ[Aperture Jitter t_j] --> JSNR[Jitter Limited SNR] FIN[Input Frequency f_in] --> JSNR JSNR --> ENOB NOISE[Thermal Noise] --> SNR DIST[Harmonic Distortion] --> SINAD SINAD --> ENOB ``` ## 4.5 Transfer Characteristic — Ideal vs. Real ADC ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IDEAL_ADC I1[Straight Line 0 to FSR] --> I2[Equal LSB Steps] end subgraph REAL_ADC R1[Offset Shift] --> R2[Gain Slope Error] R2 --> R3[DNL Variations] R3 --> R4[INL Bowing] end IDEAL_ADC -. Compared To .-> REAL_ADC ``` --- # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the term "Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)" of an ADC. Why is ENOB always less than or equal to the nominal resolution $N$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** ENOB is a dynamic specification that quantifies the **actual usable resolution** of an ADC when operated under real-world conditions, including quantization noise, thermal noise, harmonic distortion, and aperture jitter. > > **Mathematical Expression (1 mark):** > > $$ENOB = \frac{SINAD - 1.76}{6.02} \quad \text{bits}$$ > > **Why ENOB ≤ N:** In an ideal ADC, the only noise source is quantization noise, and $SINAD = SNR_{ideal} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$, giving $ENOB = N$. In practice, additional noise and distortion increase the denominator of the SINAD ratio, thereby **reducing ENOB** below the nominal N. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Differentiate between DNL and INL of an ADC. What does a DNL of $-1$ LSB indicate?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | DNL | INL | |---|---|---| | **Full Form** | Differential Non-Linearity | Integral Non-Linearity | | **Measures** | Deviation of a **single code width** from ideal 1 LSB | Cumulative deviation from the **best-fit straight line** | | **Granularity** | Per-code (local) | Whole-transfer-curve (global) | | **Mathematical Link** | $INL(k) = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} DNL(i)$ | — | > **DNL = $-1$ LSB (1 mark):** This indicates a **missing code** — at least one output code in the transfer characteristic will never appear at the output, regardless of the analog input. It is a **catastrophic non-linearity** for control and instrumentation applications. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **🅰️ QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** of an ideal $N$-bit ADC. (7 marks) **(b)** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{REF} = 5.12\,\text{V}$ digitizes a sinusoidal input. Compute the (i) resolution, (ii) maximum quantization error, (iii) ideal SNR, and (iv) RMS quantization noise voltage. (7 marks) --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.A(a) — SNR Derivation [7 marks]** > **Step 1 [1 mark] — Ideal Quantization Error PDF:** > Quantization error is uniformly distributed between $-V_{LSB}/2$ and $+V_{LSB}/2$, giving: > > $$p(e_q) = \frac{1}{V_{LSB}} \text{ for } \vert e_q \vert \leq \frac{V_{LSB}}{2}$$ > **Step 2 [1 mark] — RMS Quantization Noise:** > > $$V_{q,rms}^{2} = \frac{1}{V_{LSB}} \int_{-V_{LSB}/2}^{+V_{LSB}/2} e_q^{2}\, de_q = \frac{V_{LSB}^{2}}{12}$$ > > $$\therefore V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$$ > **Step 3 [1 mark] — Full-Scale Signal Power:** > For a full-scale sinusoid with peak $V_p = 2^{N-1} V_{LSB}$: > > $$P_{signal} = \left(\frac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} = \frac{2^{2N-2}\,V_{LSB}^{2}}{2} = 2^{2N-3}\,V_{LSB}^{2}$$ > **Step 4 [1 mark] — Noise Power:** > > $$P_{noise} = V_{q,rms}^{2} = \frac{V_{LSB}^{2}}{12}$$ > **Step 5 [1 mark] — Form SNR Ratio:** > > $$SNR = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{2^{2N-3}\,V_{LSB}^{2}}{V_{LSB}^{2}/12} = 12 \times 2^{2N-3} = 1.5 \times 2^{2N}$$ > **Step 6 [1 mark] — Convert to dB:** > > $$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20 N \log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02\,N$$ > **Step 7 [1 mark] — Final Result:** > > $$\boxed{SNR_{ideal} = 6.02\,N + 1.76\,\text{dB}}$$ --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.A(b) — Numerical Computations [7 marks]** > **(i) Resolution [2 marks]:** > > $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}} = \frac{5.12}{2^{12}} = \frac{5.12}{4096} = 1.25\,\text{mV}$$ > **(ii) Maximum Quantization Error [1 mark]:** > > $$e_{q,max} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm 0.625\,\text{mV}$$ > **(iii) Ideal SNR [2 marks]:** > > $$SNR = 6.02(12) + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\,\text{dB}$$ > **(iv) RMS Quantization Noise Voltage [2 marks]:** > > $$V_{q,rms} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{1.25\,\text{mV}}{3.464} \approx 0.361\,\text{mV RMS}$$ --- ### **🅱️ QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** Explain the following ADC specifications with relevant equations: (i) **Aperture Time and Aperture Jitter**, (ii) **Conversion Time**, (iii) **Power Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)**. (7 marks) **(b)** A 14-bit ADC samples an analog input of frequency $f_{in} = 10\,\text{MHz}$ using a clock with aperture jitter $t_j = 5\,\text{ps}$. Determine: (i) Jitter-limited SNR, (ii) ENOB under this jitter condition. (7 marks) --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.B(a) — Specification Explanations [7 marks]** > **(i) Aperture Time and Aperture Jitter [3 marks]:** > > **Aperture Time** is the small but finite time window during which the sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit acquires the input voltage before the quantization begins. > > **Aperture Jitter (Uncertainty) $t_j$** is the random, sample-to-sample variation in the exact instant of sampling, caused by clock oscillator noise. It produces a voltage error: > > $$\Delta V_{max} = 2\pi f_{in}\,V_{FS}\,t_j$$ > > The jitter-induced SNR limit is: > > $$SNR_j = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f_{in}\,t_j}\right)\,\text{dB}$$ > **(ii) Conversion Time [2 marks]:** > > Conversion time $t_{conv}$ is the time required for the ADC to complete one full A/D conversion. It governs the maximum sampling rate: > > $$f_{s,max} = \frac{1}{t_{conv}}$$ > > For a successive-approximation ADC, $t_{conv} = N \cdot T_{CLK}$, where $T_{CLK}$ is the clock period and $N$ is the resolution in bits. > **(iii) PSRR [2 marks]:** > > Power Supply Rejection Ratio is a measure of the ADC's immunity to noise on its power supply rails. It is defined as: > > $$PSRR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta V_{supply}}{\Delta V_{out,equivalent}}\right)\,\text{dB}$$ > > High PSRR is critical in mixed-signal systems where digital switching noise can couple into analog power rails. --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION FOR Q.B(b) — Jitter-Limited Analysis [7 marks]** > **(i) Jitter-Limited SNR [4 marks]:** > > $$SNR_j = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f_{in}\,t_j}\right)$$ > > $$2\pi f_{in} t_j = 2\pi \times 10 \times 10^{6} \times 5 \times 10^{-12} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}$$ > > $$SNR_j = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(3183.10)$$ > > $$SNR_j = 20 \times 3.5028 = 70.06\,\text{dB}$$ > **(ii) ENOB under Jitter [3 marks]:** > > $$ENOB = \frac{SNR_j - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{70.06 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{68.30}{6.02} \approx 11.35\,\text{bits}$$ > **Conclusion:** The 14-bit ADC effectively performs as an **~11.35-bit ADC** at 10 MHz input due to the 5 ps clock jitter. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing Resolution with Accuracy [−2 marks]:** Resolution = step size ($V_{LSB}$); Accuracy = closeness to true value. Mixing them is the **#1 valuation trap**. > 2. **Using $2^{N-1}$ instead of $2^N$ in $V_{LSB}$ formula [−1 to −2 marks]:** A unipolar ADC has $2^N$ codes, not $2^{N-1}$. > 3. **Forgetting the $+1.76$ dB constant in SNR formula [−1 mark]:** The 1.76 dB comes from the sine-wave crest factor; it is **not optional** for a sine input. > 4. **Wrong unit for ENOB [−1 mark]:** ENOB is dimensionless (in "bits"), not dB. > 5. **Jitter formula: missing $20\log_{10}$ [−1 mark]:** Jitter SNR is always expressed in dB using $20\log_{10}$, not $10\log_{10}$. > 6. **DNL sign confusion [−1 mark]:** Negative DNL means *narrower* step; DNL = $-1$ LSB means a **missing code**. > 7. **Assuming ENOB = N in a real ADC [−2 marks]:** Real ADCs always have $ENOB < N$ because of thermal noise, distortion, and jitter. --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Revise in under 5 minutes:** > > - ✅ **Resolution:** $V_{LSB} = V_{REF}/2^{N}$ — most fundamental spec. > - ✅ **Quantization Error:** Uniformly distributed in $[-V_{LSB}/2, +V_{LSB}/2]$. > - ✅ **RMS Quantization Noise:** $V_{LSB}/\sqrt{12} \approx 0.2887\,V_{LSB}$. > - ✅ **SNR Ideal:** $6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB — the "**6 dB per bit**" rule. > - ✅ **ENOB:** $(SINAD - 1.76) / 6.02$ — always ≤ N. > - ✅ **Dynamic Range:** $6.02\,N$ dB (no 1.76 correction). > - ✅ **Jitter SNR:** $-20\log_{10}(2\pi f_{in} t_j)$ dB; rolls off at 6 dB/octave of input frequency. > - ✅ **DNL:** Per-code step deviation; $<-1$ LSB = **missing codes**. > - ✅ **INL:** Cumulative DNL; measures max deviation from best-fit line. > - ✅ **Monotonicity:** DNL ≥ 0 for all codes. > - ✅ **Conversion Rate:** $f_{s,max} = 1/t_{conv}$; for SAR, $t_{conv} \approx N \cdot T_{CLK}$. > - ✅ **PSRR / CMRR:** Both expressed in dB, both 20-log ratios. > - ✅ **Aperture Error:** $\Delta V = 2\pi f_{in} V_{FS} t_j$ — rises linearly with $f_{in}$. > - ✅ **KTU 2024 Exam Cue:** Whenever a problem involves **high-frequency inputs**, always check the jitter-limited SNR first; it is often the binding constraint. > - ✅ **KTU 2024 Exam Cue:** For audio-grade ADCs (e.g., 16/24-bit), SNR is the **figure of merit**; for instrumentation, CMRR and INL matter more. > - ✅ **Pitfall to Avoid:** Resolution $\ne$ Accuracy. Resolution = theoretical; Accuracy = real-world after offset/gain/INL errors. ---

topics.title: ADC: Flash Type & Successive Approximation Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBMRT304 - ANALOG ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ADC: Flash Type & Successive Approximation Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** is an electronic circuit that converts a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog input signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital output code (usually binary). The conversion involves three fundamental operations: **Sampling**, **Quantization**, and **Encoding**.

The reverse process — converting digital signals back to analog — is performed by a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)**.

---

## 1.2 Flash Type ADC — Conceptual Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Flash ADC (Parallel Comparator ADC):**
> A Flash ADC is the **fastest** type of ADC that performs conversion in a **single step** (essentially one comparator-propagation delay). It uses a bank of $2^n - 1$ comparators operating in parallel, each comparing the input analog voltage against a unique reference voltage generated by a precision resistor ladder.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Weighing Scale

Imagine you walk into a market with a basket of apples and need to know its weight **instantly** without using a measuring scale. You have **15 friends** standing in a row, each pre-trained to yell "YES" only when the basket weighs more than their personal threshold (say 1 kg, 2 kg, 3 kg ... up to 15 kg). The moment you place the basket, all 15 friends react simultaneously — those whose threshold is lower yell YES, and those whose threshold is higher stay silent. From the pattern of YES/NO yells, an observer instantly decodes the weight.

This is exactly how a **Flash ADC** works: the comparators are the friends, the resistor ladder provides the thresholds, and the priority encoder decodes the pattern instantly!

---

## 1.3 Successive Approximation Type ADC — Conceptual Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC:**
> A SAR ADC uses a **binary search algorithm** implemented by a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) to find the closest digital code corresponding to the analog input. It requires exactly **n clock cycles** for an **n-bit** conversion, making it an excellent trade-off between speed and hardware complexity.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Guessing Game

Picture the classic number-guessing game where someone thinks of a number between 0 and 100, and you must find it using only "higher" or "lower" hints. The smartest strategy is **NOT** to guess sequentially (1, 2, 3, ...). Instead, you guess **50** first — if the answer is higher, you guess **75**; if lower, you guess **25**; and so on, halving the search range each time. In just **7 guesses**, you can identify any number from 0 to 100.

This is precisely the **binary search** strategy of the **SAR ADC** — it tests one bit at a time, starting from the **MSB**, and converges on the correct digital code in exactly **n clock cycles**.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Resolution ($n$):** Number of bits in the digital output (e.g., 8-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit).
> - **Full-Scale Voltage ($V_{FS}$ or $V_{REF}$):** The maximum input voltage the ADC can convert.
> - **Least Significant Bit (LSB):** $LSB = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$, the smallest resolvable voltage step.
> - **Quantization Error:** Theoretical maximum of **±½ LSB**.
> - **Conversion Time ($T_c$):** Time required to produce one valid digital output.
> - **Sampling Rate / Throughput:** Samples per second, the reciprocal of conversion time.

---

## 1.5 Why Are These Two ADCs Important for KTU?

> [!NOTE]
> Flash ADCs are used in **high-speed applications** (oscilloscopes, radar, digital oscilloscopes, high-bandwidth communication).
> SAR ADCs are the **industry workhorse** for medium-to-high resolution data acquisition (microcontrollers, ADCs in SoCs, industrial sensors, audio equipment) because they balance **speed, accuracy, and low power consumption**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Flash Type ADC — Complete Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Building Blocks of a Flash ADC

A Flash ADC consists of **three major blocks**:

1. **Precision Resistor Ladder (Reference Divider):**
   - Contains **$2^n$** equal-value precision resistors connected in series between $V_{REF}$ (top) and ground (bottom).
   - Each tap of the ladder provides a unique reference voltage: $V_{REF_k} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ where $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 2^n - 1$.
   - Note: The bottom-most resistor is grounded; the lowest reference is $\frac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$, not zero.

2. **Comparator Bank:**
   - Consists of **$2^n - 1$** high-speed analog comparators.
   - Each comparator's **non-inverting input** receives the analog input voltage $V_{in}$.
   - Each comparator's **inverting input** is tied to a unique tap on the resistor ladder.
   - When $V_{in} > V_{REF_k}$, the comparator output is logic **HIGH (1)**.
   - When $V_{in} < V_{REF_k}$, the comparator output is logic **LOW (0)**.

3. **Priority Encoder:**
   - Converts the **thermometer code** output from the comparator bank into a standard **binary code**.
   - **Thermometer Code:** A pattern of 1s followed by 0s (e.g., 0001111 means $V_{in}$ exceeds the lowest three thresholds).
   - The encoder identifies the position of the highest-order logic 1 and produces the corresponding binary word.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

- **Step 1:** The analog input $V_{in}$ is broadcast simultaneously to all $2^n - 1$ comparators (this is the "flash" of parallelism).
- **Step 2:** Each comparator decides independently whether $V_{in}$ is greater or lesser than its assigned threshold.
- **Step 3:** The resulting thermometer code (e.g., `0011111100000`) is fed to the priority encoder.
- **Step 4:** The encoder outputs the corresponding binary code in a single propagation delay.
- **Step 5:** Conversion is **complete** — typically within **10–50 ns** for an 8-bit Flash ADC.

### 2.1.3 Why "Flash"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The name "Flash" comes from the **simultaneous** (parallel) comparison that happens in a single "flash" of activity. There is no iterative or sequential operation; all comparators fire at the same instant, making it the **fastest ADC architecture** available.

### 2.1.4 Drawbacks of Flash ADC

- **Exponential Hardware Growth:** For an n-bit ADC, you need $2^n - 1$ comparators and $2^n$ resistors. A 10-bit Flash ADC requires **1023 comparators**!
- **High Power Consumption:** All comparators toggle simultaneously.
- **Large Die Area & Cost:** Limits practical Flash ADCs to **6–8 bits** in most commercial use.
- **Comparator Offset Errors:** Slight mismatches create "sparkle" or "bubble" errors in the thermometer code.

---

## 2.2 Successive Approximation Type ADC — Complete Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Building Blocks of a SAR ADC

A SAR ADC consists of **four major blocks**:

1. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit:**
   - Captures and holds the analog input voltage constant during conversion, so the SAR has a stable value to compare.

2. **Comparator (Single, High-Precision):**
   - Compares the held analog input with the output of the internal DAC.

3. **Internal DAC (Usually R-2R Ladder or Binary-Weighted Resistor Network):**
   - Generates an analog reference voltage corresponding to the current guess stored in the SAR.

4. **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) + Control Logic:**
   - Implements the binary search algorithm, controlling the DAC and recording comparator outputs.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic (Binary Search)

For an **n-bit** SAR ADC, conversion proceeds in **n clock cycles**:

- **Cycle 1 (MSB Test):** Set SAR's MSB = 1, all other bits = 0. DAC produces $V_{DAC} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2}$. If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, keep MSB = 1; else, set MSB = 0.
- **Cycle 2:** Set the next bit (MSB-1) = 1, keep the determined MSB. Compare $V_{in}$ with new $V_{DAC}$. Retain or discard the test bit.
- **Cycle 3 to Cycle n:** Continue halving the search range until the LSB is decided.
- **End of Conversion (EOC):** After n cycles, the SAR holds the final n-bit digital equivalent of $V_{in}$.

### 2.2.3 Why "Successive Approximation"?

> [!NOTE]
> Each clock cycle, the SAR **approximates** the input by halving the remaining uncertainty. The internal digital value "successively" converges toward the true analog input value, ensuring monotonic convergence in exactly $n$ steps.

### 2.2.4 Advantages of SAR ADC

- **Fixed Conversion Time:** Always $n$ clock cycles — predictable and deterministic.
- **Moderate Hardware:** Only **one comparator** needed, regardless of resolution.
- **High Resolution Possible:** Easily scales to 12, 16, 18, or even 20 bits.
- **Low Power:** Ideal for battery-powered and embedded systems.
- **No Resistor-Ladder Precision Issues:** Resolution depends on DAC accuracy, not massive parallel resistor networks.

### 2.2.5 Limitations of SAR ADC

- **Slower than Flash** (n clock cycles vs. one).
- **DAC Linearity Limits Resolution:** Internal DAC accuracy directly affects conversion accuracy.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the HIGH-YIELD formula set for solving all numerical and analytical questions on Flash and SAR ADCs in KTU ESE.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LSB (Step Size) | $LSB = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Quantization Error (Max) | $E_q = \pm\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot LSB = \pm\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | Number of Comparators in Flash ADC | $N_c = 2^n - 1$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Number of Resistors in Ladder | $N_R = 2^n$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Reference Voltage at Tap k | $V_{REF_k} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ | Volts (V), $k = 1$ to $2^n - 1$ |
| 6 | Flash ADC Conversion Time | $T_c^{Flash} = t_{comp} + t_{encoder}$ | Seconds (s); very small (ns range) |
| 7 | SAR ADC Conversion Time | $T_c^{SAR} = n \cdot T_{CLK}$ | Seconds (s) |
| 8 | SAR Throughput | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_c^{SAR}} = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot T_{CLK}}$ | Samples per second (S/s) |
| 9 | Dynamic Range (dB) | $DR = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 10 | Digital Output Code | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{LSB} \right\rfloor$ | Integer (decimal form of binary) |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - Flash ADCs are the heart of **digital storage oscilloscopes (DSOs)**, **radar signal processors**, **high-speed serial link receivers (PCIe, SERDES)**, and **medical imaging equipment (CT scanners)**.
> - SAR ADCs dominate **microcontroller integrated ADCs (STM32, Arduino, ESP32)**, **battery monitoring systems**, **industrial process control**, **audio codecs (up to 24-bit)**, and **precision instrumentation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resolution and Quantization Step Size

The resolution of any ADC is determined by its **bit depth (n)** and **full-scale reference voltage ($V_{FS}$)**.

> **Starting Principle:** A digital code of $n$ bits can represent $2^n$ distinct values (from $0$ to $2^n - 1$). These $2^n$ codes must span the analog input range $0$ to $V_{FS}$.

The smallest analog increment that produces a change of one LSB in the digital output is called the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** or **Step Size**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB} &= \frac{\text{Total Analog Range}}{\text{Total Number of Digital Steps}} \\
\text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{FS} - 0}{2^n - 0} \\
\text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the fundamental unit of quantization. Any analog input between $(k \cdot LSB)$ and $((k+1) \cdot LSB)$ will be assigned the digital code $k$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Quantization Error

> **Principle:** When an analog input falls between two quantization levels, it is rounded to the nearest level. The worst-case rounding error occurs when the input is exactly **halfway** between two adjacent codes.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{q, \max} &= \pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= \pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \\
&= \pm \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Insight:** Each additional bit **halves** the quantization error, which is why higher-bit ADCs are crucial in precision instrumentation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Number of Comparators in a Flash ADC

> **Principle:** For an n-bit Flash ADC, the resistor ladder creates $2^n$ distinct voltage levels. However, the reference for the **lowest** comparator is the **first tap above ground** (not ground itself). This means we need one fewer comparator than the number of levels.

Consider a 2-bit Flash ADC ($n = 2$):
- Resistor ladder: $2^2 = 4$ resistors → 4 voltage levels ($0$, $V_{REF}/4$, $V_{REF}/2$, $3V_{REF}/4$).
- Comparators needed: **3** (one each comparing $V_{in}$ with $V_{REF}/4$, $V_{REF}/2$, $3V_{REF}/4$).

Generalizing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Detailed Step-by-Step Numerical: 4-bit Flash ADC with $V_{REF} = 8$ V

### Given Data
- Resolution: $n = 4$ bits
- Reference Voltage: $V_{REF} = 8$ V
- Analog Input: $V_{in} = 5.7$ V

### Step 1: Compute the LSB

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \\
&= \frac{8}{2^4} \\
&= \frac{8}{16} \\
&= 0.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Number of Comparators and Resistors

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n - 1 = 16 - 1 = 15 \text{ comparators} \\
N_R &= 2^n = 16 \text{ resistors}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Reference Voltages at Each Ladder Tap

Using $V_{REF_k} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 15$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{REF_1} &= 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_2} &= 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_3} &= 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_4} &= 4 \times 0.5 = 2.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_5} &= 5 \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_6} &= 6 \times 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_7} &= 7 \times 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_8} &= 8 \times 0.5 = 4.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_9} &= 9 \times 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_{10}} &= 10 \times 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_{11}} &= 11 \times 0.5 = 5.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_{12}} &= 12 \times 0.5 = 6.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_{13}} &= 13 \times 0.5 = 6.5 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_{14}} &= 14 \times 0.5 = 7.0 \text{ V} \\
V_{REF_{15}} &= 15 \times 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Determine the Thermometer Code for $V_{in} = 5.7$ V

All comparators whose reference is **less than 5.7 V** will output HIGH. Those with reference **greater than 5.7 V** will output LOW.

- Comparators with $V_{REF} \leq 5.5$ V (i.e., $k = 1$ to $11$) → Output = **1**
- Comparators with $V_{REF} > 5.5$ V (i.e., $k = 12$ to $15$) → Output = **0**

Thermometer Code (from highest reference downward):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Code} &= \underbrace{0000}_{k=15 \text{ to } 12} \underbrace{11111111111}_{k=11 \text{ to } 1} \\
&= 0000\,11111111111
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Priority Encoder Output

The encoder detects the highest-order 1 at position $k = 11$. The binary representation of 11 in 4 bits is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
11_{10} &= 1011_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Verification of Quantization

The digital output $1011_2$ corresponds to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out,analog}} &= 1011_2 \times \text{LSB} = 11 \times 0.5 = 5.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The quantization error is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_q &= V_{in} - V_{\text{out,analog}} \\
&= 5.7 - 5.5 = +0.2 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $|E_q| = 0.2$ V $<$ $0.5$ LSB $= 0.25$ V, the result is valid (within ±½ LSB range).

---

## 3.5 Detailed Step-by-Step: 4-bit SAR ADC Conversion of $V_{in} = 5.7$ V

### Given Data
- Resolution: $n = 4$ bits
- Reference Voltage: $V_{REF} = 8$ V
- Analog Input: $V_{in} = 5.7$ V
- LSB = 0.5 V (computed above)

### The Binary Search Algorithm

The SAR tests bits starting from the MSB ($b_3$) to the LSB ($b_0$).

| Cycle | Bit Tested | SAR Trial Code | $V_{DAC}$ (V) | Compare $V_{in}$ vs $V_{DAC}$ | Decision | Final SAR Code |
|:-----:|:----------:|:--------------:|:-------------:|:----------------------------:|:--------:|:--------------:|
| 1 | $b_3$ (MSB) | 1000 | $V_{REF}/2 = 4.0$ | $5.7 > 4.0$ → Keep | $b_3 = 1$ | **1**000 |
| 2 | $b_2$ | 1100 | $4.0 + 2.0 = 6.0$ | $5.7 < 6.0$ → Discard | $b_2 = 0$ | 10**0**0 |
| 3 | $b_1$ | 1010 | $4.0 + 1.0 = 5.0$ | $5.7 > 5.0$ → Keep | $b_1 = 1$ | 101**1** |
| 4 | $b_0$ (LSB) | 1011 | $4.0 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 5.5$ | $5.7 > 5.5$ → Keep | $b_0 = 1$ | 1011 |

### Cycle-by-Cycle Algebraic Detail

#### Cycle 1 — Test MSB ($b_3$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= b_3 \cdot 2^3 \cdot \text{LSB} = 1 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.5 = 4.0 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 5.7 \text{ V} > 4.0 \text{ V} &= V_{DAC}, \text{ keep } b_3 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Cycle 2 — Test $b_2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= (b_3 \cdot 2^3 + b_2 \cdot 2^2) \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= (1 \cdot 8 + 1 \cdot 4) \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 12 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 6.0 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 5.7 \text{ V} < 6.0 \text{ V} &= V_{DAC}, \text{ discard: } b_2 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Cycle 3 — Test $b_1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= (b_3 \cdot 2^3 + b_2 \cdot 2^2 + b_1 \cdot 2^1) \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= (1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2) \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 10 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 5.0 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 5.7 \text{ V} > 5.0 \text{ V} &= V_{DAC}, \text{ keep } b_1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Cycle 4 — Test LSB ($b_0$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= (b_3 \cdot 2^3 + b_2 \cdot 2^2 + b_1 \cdot 2^1 + b_0 \cdot 2^0) \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= (1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1) \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 11 \cdot 0.5 \\
&= 5.5 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 5.7 \text{ V} > 5.5 \text{ V} &= V_{DAC}, \text{ keep } b_0 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Digital Output} &= 1011_2 = 11_{10} \\
V_{\text{out,analog}} &= 11 \times 0.5 = 5.5 \text{ V} \\
E_q &= 5.7 - 5.5 = +0.2 \text{ V} \; (\text{within } \pm 0.25 \text{ V tolerance})
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the Flash ADC result exactly, confirming algorithmic equivalence.

---

## 3.6 Conversion Time Comparison

For the same clock period $T_{CLK} = 1\ \mu\text{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c^{\text{Flash}} &\approx t_{comp} + t_{encoder} \approx 50 \text{ ns} \\
T_c^{\text{SAR}} &= n \cdot T_{CLK} = 4 \cdot 1\ \mu\text{s} = 4\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** For a 4-bit conversion, the Flash ADC is approximately **80 times faster** than the SAR ADC, but at the cost of **15 comparators** vs. **1 comparator** in the SAR ADC.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Conversion Time | 1 clock (very fast) | $n$ clocks |
| Comparators Required | $2^n - 1$ | $1$ |
| Resistors in Ladder | $2^n$ | Depends on DAC type |
| Power Consumption | Very High | Low to Moderate |
| Practical Resolution | Up to 8 bits | Up to 20+ bits |
| Die Area / Cost | Very High | Low |
| Suitability | High-speed, low-resolution | General-purpose, medium-high resolution |
| Throughput Formula | $\dfrac{1}{t_{comp}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{n \cdot T_{CLK}}$ |
| Example Applications | Oscilloscopes, radar | Microcontrollers, sensors, audio |

### Interpretation of Diagram
- The **resistor ladder** divides $V_{REF}$ into $2^n$ equal segments.

- Conversion completes in essentially **one comparator-propagation delay**.

### Interpretation of Diagram
- The S/H circuit holds $V_{in}$ stable during the entire conversion.
- The SAR generates a trial digital code → DAC converts it to analog → comparator checks against $V_{in}$ → result fed back to SAR.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part A questions test **Remember (L1)** and **Understand (L2)** cognitive levels. Answers should be concise (typically 80–120 words) and precise.

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Compare the conversion time and hardware complexity of a Flash ADC and a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Flash ADC | SAR ADC |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Conversion Time | Very fast — completes in **one clock cycle** (limited only by comparator and encoder propagation delay) | Requires **$n$ clock cycles** for an $n$-bit conversion |
| Hardware Complexity | Very high — needs **$2^n - 1$ comparators** and **$2^n$ precision resistors** | Low — requires only **one comparator**, one internal DAC, and a SAR register |

Hence, the Flash ADC is the fastest but hardware-intensive; the SAR ADC trades speed for compact hardware and better scalability to high resolutions.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Why is a Sample-and-Hold (S/H) circuit necessary in a SAR ADC but not strictly required in a Flash ADC?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a **SAR ADC**, conversion takes **$n$ clock cycles**. During this entire period, the analog input must remain constant; otherwise, the comparator decisions taken in cycle 1 may become invalid by cycle $n$. The S/H circuit **freezes** the input voltage during conversion, ensuring a stable reference for comparison.

In a **Flash ADC**, conversion is completed in essentially **one step** (single comparator-propagation delay). The input does not need to be held because the comparator decision and encoding happen before the input can change appreciably at high sampling rates. Hence, a dedicated S/H circuit is not strictly necessary for Flash ADCs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE guidelines**, every Module-based Part B question carries **14 marks**, broken into sub-parts of typically **7 + 7 marks**. Two complete alternatives are provided so the student can practice both.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a 3-bit Flash Type ADC. Derive the expressions for (i) the number of comparators required, (ii) the LSB value, and (iii) the maximum quantization error. If $V_{REF} = 8$ V, determine the digital output for an analog input $V_{in} = 5.2$ V. **\[14 Marks\]****

#### Part (a): Block Diagram & Working of 3-bit Flash ADC **[7 marks]**

**Block Diagram:**
```
                V_REF (8V)
                  |
                [R1]
                  |
   V_in ----+--------+--------+----+--------+----+---  (to comparators)
              |        |        |        |
             [C1]     [C2]     [C3]     [C4]     [C5]     [C6]     [C7]
            0.5V      1V     1.5V      2V       2.5V      3V     3.5V
              |        |        |        |        |        |        |
              +--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--+
                                       |
                            Thermometer Code (7 bits)
                                       |
                                [Priority Encoder]
                                       |
                                  Binary Output
                                    (3 bits)
```

**Working Explanation:**

The 3-bit Flash ADC consists of:

1. **Resistor Ladder:** A series chain of $2^3 = 8$ equal resistors between $V_{REF}$ and ground. The taps provide reference voltages $\frac{V_{REF}}{8}, \frac{2V_{REF}}{8}, \ldots, \frac{7V_{REF}}{8}$ i.e., $1\text{V}, 2\text{V}, 3\text{V}, 4\text{V}, 5\text{V}, 6\text{V}, 7\text{V}$.

2. **Comparator Bank:** $2^3 - 1 = 7$ comparators simultaneously compare $V_{in}$ with each reference. Outputs form a **thermometer code**.

3. **Priority Encoder:** Decodes the thermometer pattern into a 3-bit binary code.

**Conversion Logic:**
- All comparators whose reference is less than $V_{in}$ output **HIGH (1)**.
- All comparators whose reference exceeds $V_{in}$ output **LOW (0)**.
- The encoder identifies the highest-order 1 and outputs the corresponding binary word.

#### Part (b): Derivations and Numerical **[7 marks]**

**(i) Number of Comparators:**

For an $n$-bit Flash ADC, the resistor ladder produces $2^n$ voltage levels. Comparators are connected from the **first tap above ground** to the **top tap**, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

For $n = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_c &= 2^3 - 1 = 7 \text{ comparators}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) LSB Value:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} \\
&= \frac{8}{2^3} \\
&= \frac{8}{8} \\
&= 1 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{q,\max} &= \pm\frac{1}{2} \cdot \text{LSB} = \pm\frac{V_{REF}}{2^{n+1}} \\
&= \pm\frac{8}{2^4} \\
&= \pm\frac{8}{16} \\
&= \pm 0.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical: Digital Output for $V_{in} = 5.2$ V:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Digital code} &= \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\text{LSB}} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor \frac{5.2}{1} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor 5.2 \right\rfloor \\
&= 5_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting 5 to 3-bit binary:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5_{10} &= 101_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $101_2 \times \text{LSB} = 5 \times 1 = 5$ V; Quantization error = $5.2 - 5 = +0.2$ V $\leq 0.5$ V ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the working principle of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC with a block diagram. Describe the binary search algorithm. For a 4-bit SAR ADC with $V_{REF} = 10$ V, determine the digital output when $V_{in} = 6.3$ V. Calculate the conversion time if the clock frequency is 1 MHz. **\[14 Marks\]****

#### Part (a): Working Principle and Binary Search Algorithm **[7 marks]**

**Block Diagram:**
```
        V_in ---> [Sample & Hold] ----+
                                       |
                                       v
                                  [Comparator] <--+
                                       |          |
                                       v          |
                                     (Output)     |
                                       |          |
                                       v          |
   Clock --> [Control Logic] --> [SAR] --> [DAC] -+
                       |
                       v
                 EOC Signal + n-bit Output
```

**Working Principle:**

A SAR ADC performs conversion through a **binary search** (successive approximation) algorithm:

1. **Sample and Hold:** The input $V_{in}$ is sampled and held constant.

2. **MSB Test (Cycle 1):** The SAR sets the MSB = 1 and all other bits = 0. The DAC produces a corresponding analog voltage $V_{DAC} = \frac{V_{REF}}{2}$.
   - If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, the MSB is **kept at 1**.
   - If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$, the MSB is **reset to 0**.

3. **Next Bit Test (Cycles 2 to n-1):** Each subsequent bit is tested by setting it to 1 (with previously decided bits held) and comparing. The decision to keep or discard follows the same rule.

4. **LSB Test (Cycle n):** The final bit is decided similarly.

5. **EOC (End of Conversion):** After exactly $n$ clock cycles, the SAR holds the final digital equivalent.

**Binary Search Concept:** Each iteration halves the search range, ensuring the digital code converges monotonically to $V_{in}$ in $n$ steps — analogous to finding a number between 0 and 100 in 7 guesses.

#### Part (b): Numerical Solution for $V_{in} = 6.3$ V **[7 marks]**

**Given:** $n = 4$, $V_{REF} = 10$ V, $V_{in} = 6.3$ V

**Step 1: Compute LSB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LSB} &= \frac{V_{REF}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{16} = 0.625 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Binary Search (4 cycles):**

**Cycle 1 — Test MSB ($b_3$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= 2^3 \cdot \text{LSB} = 8 \cdot 0.625 = 5.0 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 6.3 \text{ V} > 5.0 \text{ V}, \quad b_3 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cycle 2 — Test $b_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= (b_3 \cdot 8 + b_2 \cdot 4) \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= (8 + 4) \cdot 0.625 \\
&= 12 \cdot 0.625 = 7.5 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 6.3 \text{ V} < 7.5 \text{ V}, \quad b_2 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cycle 3 — Test $b_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= (8 + 0 + b_1 \cdot 2) \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= (8 + 2) \cdot 0.625 \\
&= 10 \cdot 0.625 = 6.25 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 6.3 \text{ V} > 6.25 \text{ V}, \quad b_1 &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cycle 4 — Test LSB ($b_0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DAC} &= (8 + 0 + 2 + b_0 \cdot 1) \cdot \text{LSB} \\
&= (8 + 2 + 1) \cdot 0.625 \\
&= 11 \cdot 0.625 = 6.875 \text{ V} \\
\text{Since } V_{in} = 6.3 \text{ V} < 6.875 \text{ V}, \quad b_0 &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Digital Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAR Output} &= b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 1010_2 = 10_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out,analog}} &= 1010_2 \cdot \text{LSB} = 10 \cdot 0.625 = 6.25 \text{ V} \\
E_q &= 6.3 - 6.25 = +0.05 \text{ V} \quad (\text{within } \pm 0.3125 \text{ V tolerance}) \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CLK} &= \frac{1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{1}{1\ \text{MHz}} = 1\ \mu\text{s} \\
T_c &= n \cdot T_{CLK} = 4 \cdot 1\ \mu\text{s} = 4\ \mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Throughput:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_s &= \frac{1}{T_c} = \frac{1}{4\ \mu\text{s}} = 250{,}000\ \text{samples/second} = 250\ \text{kS/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Comparator Count:** Students often write "**$2^n$** comparators" instead of the correct "**$2^n - 1$**". Remember, the lowest reference is the **first tap above ground**, not ground itself. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 2. **Mis-identifying Reference Voltages:** Some students compute the **top tap voltage as $V_{REF}$** (incorrect). The top tap is $\dfrac{(2^n - 1) \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ = $V_{REF} - \text{LSB}$. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 3. **Confusion Between Flash and SAR Conversion Time:** Writing "$T_c = 1$ clock for SAR" is wrong. Flash = 1 step; SAR = $n$ clocks. **[−2 marks]**
>
> 4. **Skipping the Verification Step:** Always show the **quantization error verification** after finding the digital output. Examiners award marks for confirming the result lies within $\pm\frac{1}{2}$ LSB.
>
> 5. **SAR Algorithm Direction Error:** In some textbook conventions, $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$ means **discard** the bit (start with high guess). Confirm with your course module which convention KTU follows. The standard convention used here is: $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$ ⇒ **KEEP** the bit.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the S/H Circuit Justification:** When asked "why S/H in SAR?", merely saying "to hold the input" is incomplete. State that it prevents the **input from changing during the $n$ clock cycles** of binary search, ensuring comparator decisions remain valid.
>
> 7. **Writing Decimal Instead of Binary in the Final Answer:** Always write the final digital output in **binary with subscript 2**, e.g., $1011_2$, not just "11".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Flash and SAR ADCs**

### Flash ADC Essentials
- ⚡ **Fastest ADC** — completes conversion in essentially **one step** (single propagation delay).
- 🔢 Requires **$2^n - 1$ comparators** and **$2^n$ precision resistors** for $n$-bit resolution.
- 📐 Reference voltages: $V_{REF_k} = \dfrac{k \cdot V_{REF}}{2^n}$ for $k = 1$ to $2^n - 1$.
- 🎯 Output of comparators is a **thermometer code**, converted to binary by a **priority encoder**.
- 💰 **Drawback:** Hardware grows exponentially; practical limit is around **6–8 bits**.
- 🎬 Used in **oscilloscopes, radar, high-speed serial links, medical imaging**.

### SAR ADC Essentials
- 🔄 Performs conversion in **$n$ clock cycles** using a **binary search algorithm**.
- 1️⃣ Requires only **one comparator** + internal DAC + SAR logic + S/H circuit.
- ⏱️ Conversion time: $T_c = n \cdot T_{CLK}$; Throughput: $f_s = \dfrac{1}{n \cdot T_{CLK}}$.
- 🎚️ Tests from **MSB to LSB** — keeping bits when $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$, discarding otherwise.
- ⚖️ Best **trade-off** between speed, resolution (up to 20+ bits), and power.
- 🎛️ Dominates **microcontrollers, sensor interfacing, audio codecs, industrial control**.

### Universal Formulas to Memorize
- $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^n}$
- $E_{q,\max} = \pm\dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{n+1}} = \pm\dfrac{1}{2}\text{LSB}$
- $\text{Digital code} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{\text{LSB}} \right\rfloor$
- Number of Flash comparators = $2^n - 1$
- SAR conversion time = $n \cdot T_{CLK}$
- Dynamic Range (dB) = $6.02n + 1.76$

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing $2^n$ vs. $2^n - 1$ comparators.
- ❌ Writing $V_{REF}$ at the top tap instead of $V_{REF} - \text{LSB}$.
- ❌ Forgetting the **S/H circuit justification** in SAR.
- ❌ Skipping the **quantization error verification** step.
- ❌ Confusing Flash and SAR conversion time formulas.
- ❌ Forgetting to write final answer in binary form ($1011_2$, not just "11").

### Quick Decision Rule for Applications
- Need **GS/s speed, low resolution** → **Flash ADC**
- Need **kS/s to MS/s speed, high resolution** → **SAR ADC**

---
